topics.title: General Classification of Energy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT411 - ENERGY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** General Classification of Energy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Energy

In the context of KTU 2024 Scheme Engineering Thermodynamics and Energy Studies (PECHT411), **Energy** is formally defined as the capacity of a system to perform **work** or to transfer **heat**. From the First Law of Thermodynamics, energy is a property of the system that can be stored, transported, and converted from one form to another, but it can never be created or destroyed.

Mathematically, the differential form of the First Law for a closed system is:

$$dE = dQ - dW$$

Where $dE$ is the change in total energy of the system, $dQ$ is the heat transferred to the system, and $dW$ is the work done by the system. Total energy $E$ of a system in the absence of macroscopic kinetic and potential effects is the **internal energy (U)** plus flow work contributions that lead to **enthalpy (H)** in open systems.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** For PECHT411, the focus is not on the micro-physics of energy (atomic scale), but on the **engineering classification** of energy resources, their availability, conversion pathways, and global/Indian scenario. The thermodynamic formalism is referenced only to standardize units.

## 1.2 Forms of Energy (Fundamental Forms)

Energy manifests in the following fundamental physical forms in engineering practice:

| S.No | Form of Energy | Brief Physical Meaning |
|------|----------------|------------------------|
| 1 | **Kinetic Energy (KE)** | Energy of motion: $KE = \frac{1}{2}mv^2$ |
| 2 | **Potential Energy (PE)** | Energy of position: $PE = mgh$ |
| 3 | **Thermal Energy (Internal)** | Microscopic random molecular motion |
| 4 | **Chemical Energy** | Stored in molecular/atomic bonds |
| 5 | **Electrical Energy** | Energy of moving electrons / charge |
| 6 | **Nuclear Energy** | Energy stored in atomic nucleus |
| 7 | **Radiant/Solar Energy** | Electromagnetic radiation energy |
| 8 | **Mechanical Energy** | Sum of KE and PE in bulk matter |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water-Cycle Money" Model

Imagine energy behaves like **money in a financial system**:

- **Energy Resources** = The total amount of money earned in a year (your salary + investments).
- **Primary Energy** = Cash in your bank (raw, untouched, like coal in the mine).
- **Secondary Energy** = The money after conversion to usable digital balance (like electricity generated from coal).
- **Renewable Energy** = Your monthly salary (keeps coming back).
- **Non-Renewable Energy** = A fixed FD (Fixed Deposit) that, once used up, is gone forever.
- **Commercial Energy** = Money that flows through the official banking system (electricity, gasoline).
- **Non-Commercial Energy** = Cash kept under your mattress at home (firewood, cow-dung cakes used in rural India).

This analogy makes the classification immediately intuitive. A student in Kerala, for example, uses **commercial energy** when switching on a light (electricity from the grid, originally derived from coal/hydro) and **non-commercial energy** when burning coconut-shell pieces in the traditional *aduppu* kitchen hearth.

## 1.4 Standard Energy Units and Physical Constants

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following units and conversion factors are **MANDATORY** for solving any numerical in PECHT411 university exams. Memorize them as part of your KTU 2024 numerical toolkit.

The SI unit of energy is the **Joule (J)**. Other commonly used engineering units include:

$$
1 \text{ kWh} = 3.6 \times 10^6 \text{ J} = 3.6 \text{ MJ}
$$

$$
1 \text{ Calorie (cal)} = 4.186 \text{ J}
$$

$$
1 \text{ BTU} = 1055 \text{ J}
$$

$$
1 \text{ Tonne of Oil Equivalent (TOE)} = 41.868 \text{ GJ}
$$

$$
1 \text{ Quad} = 10^{15} \text{ BTU} = 1.055 \times 10^{18} \text{ J}
$$

The unit **toe (tonne of oil equivalent)** is a normalized unit used by the **International Energy Agency (IEA)** and the **Ministry of Power, Government of India** for cross-fuel comparison.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Classification of Energy

The KTU 2024 syllabus demands a **two-tier classification**: first on the basis of **resource origin / renewability**, and second on the basis of **commercial availability / end-use**. Both classifications are NOT mutually exclusive — they are orthogonal perspectives on the same energy ecosystem.

### 2.1.1 Classification Based on Renewability (Resource Origin)

**A) Renewable Energy Sources**

These are energy sources that are **naturally replenished at a rate faster than (or comparable to) the rate of human consumption**. They are inexhaustible over human time-scales.

- **Solar Energy** — Direct radiant energy from the Sun.
- **Wind Energy** — Kinetic energy of moving air masses.
- **Hydel / Hydro Energy** — Potential energy of water stored at elevation.
- **Tidal Energy** — Gravitational PE/KE of ocean water.
- **Wave Energy** — Surface kinetic energy of ocean waves.
- **Ocean Thermal Energy Conversion (OTEC)** — Thermal gradient between surface and deep ocean.
- **Geothermal Energy** — Internal heat of the Earth.
- **Biomass Energy** — Chemical energy of organic matter (recent biological origin).
- **Biogas** — Methane produced by anaerobic digestion of bio-wastes.

**B) Non-Renewable Energy Sources**

These are energy sources that exist in **finite quantities** in the Earth's crust and are depleted at a rate much faster than their natural replenishment (which takes millions of years for fossil fuels).

- **Coal** (Anthracite, Bituminous, Lignite, Peat)
- **Petroleum / Crude Oil**
- **Natural Gas** (CNG, LNG)
- **Nuclear Fuels** (Uranium-235, Thorium-232)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Conceptual Trap:** Some textbooks classify **Nuclear Energy** as "quasi-renewable" because breeder reactors can theoretically generate more fissile material than they consume. However, for KTU 2024 evaluation purposes, **nuclear energy is ALWAYS classified as non-renewable** since uranium ore is a finite mineral resource.

### 2.1.2 Classification Based on Commercial Availability

**A) Commercial Energy**

Energy that is **bought and sold in the organized market** at a price. It is delivered through a structured supply chain (grid, pipelines, tankers) and is metered/measured.

- Electricity (from any generation source)
- Petrol, Diesel, Kerosene (LPG, CNG)
- Coal, Natural Gas (when sold via commercial channels)
- Processed fuels (coke, charcoal for industry)

**B) Non-Commercial Energy**

Energy that is **collected freely from nature or from one's own premises** and is not transacted in the formal market. It dominates the rural energy budget of developing countries.

- Firewood
- Cow-dung cakes
- Agricultural residues (rice husk, bagasse, coconut shells)
- Straw and dried leaves

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Point:** In the Indian energy context, the **rural sector still derives over 25% of its energy** from non-commercial biomass. This is one of the highest-priority questions in the "Energy Scenario of India" section.

### 2.1.3 Classification Based on Conventionality (Era of Adoption)

- **Conventional Energy Sources:** Coal, Petroleum, Natural Gas, Hydel, Firewood. (Pre-20th century, established technology).
- **Non-Conventional Energy Sources:** Solar PV, Wind, Tidal, OTEC, Geothermal, Biogas, Hydrogen, Fuel Cells. (Modern, sustainable alternatives).

## 2.2 Primary vs. Secondary Energy — The Critical Distinction

This is a **highly tested KTU 2024 concept** that examiners love to set as 3-mark or 7-mark sub-questions.

| Aspect | Primary Energy | Secondary Energy |
|--------|----------------|------------------|
| **Definition** | Energy found in nature in its raw form | Energy derived from primary sources after conversion |
| **Examples** | Coal, Crude Oil, Sunlight, Uranium, Wind | Electricity, Petrol, Diesel, LPG, Hydrogen |
| **Form** | Often contains impurities / is in unusable form | Refined, purified, ready for end-use |
| **Measurement** | As-extracted calorific content | As-delivered useful output |
| **Loss in Conversion** | N/A (it is the source) | Suffers conversion efficiency losses (e.g., 30-40% for thermal power) |

**The Energy Conversion Chain (Source to End-Use):**

$$
\text{Primary} \longrightarrow \text{Conversion} \longrightarrow \text{Secondary} \longrightarrow \text{End-Use}
$$

*Example: Coal (Primary) → Thermal Power Plant → Electricity (Secondary) → Fan/Light (End-Use).*

## 2.3 The Energy Chain / Energy Flow Concept

The **Energy Chain** traces energy from its natural source through conversion, distribution, and final use. The efficiency of the entire chain is the **product of efficiencies of each stage**, which is why overall end-use efficiency is often less than 10-15% in conventional thermal systems.

$$
\eta_{\text{chain}} = \eta_1 \times \eta_2 \times \eta_3 \times \ldots \times \eta_n
$$

For example, in a coal-fired power plant driving an electric motor:

$$
\eta_{\text{overall}} = \eta_{\text{boiler}} \times \eta_{\text{turbine}} \times \eta_{\text{generator}} \times \eta_{\text{transmission}} \times \eta_{\text{motor}}
$$

If each stage is 90% efficient over 5 stages, the chain efficiency drops to $(0.9)^5 \approx 0.59$, i.e., **59%**. In real systems, this can drop below 25% for many thermal power plants.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Exam-Ready)

| # | Formula / Definition | Symbolic Form | Unit | Application |
|---|----------------------|---------------|------|-------------|
| 1 | Kinetic Energy | $KE = \frac{1}{2} m v^2$ | J | Wind/hydro/turbine analysis |
| 2 | Potential Energy | $PE = m g h$ | J | Hydroelectric dam storage |
| 3 | Electrical Energy | $E = V I t$ | J (kWh) | Motor, heater, battery sizing |
| 4 | Calorific Value (Solid/Liquid) | $CV = \frac{\text{Heat Released}}{\text{Mass}}$ | kJ/kg | Fuel comparison |
| 5 | Calorific Value (Gas) | $CV = \frac{\text{Heat Released}}{\text{Volume at STP}}$ | kJ/m³ | Natural gas, biogas |
| 6 | Energy Unit Conversion | $1 \text{ kWh} = 3.6 \text{ MJ}$ | — | Universal conversion |
| 7 | TOE Conversion | $1 \text{ TOE} = 41.868 \text{ GJ}$ | — | National energy accounting |
| 8 | Energy Chain Efficiency | $\eta_{\text{overall}} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \eta_i$ | Dimensionless | Multi-stage losses |
| 9 | Power (Rate of Energy) | $P = \frac{dE}{dt}$ | W (J/s) | Plant capacity |
| 10 | First Law (Closed System) | $dE = dQ - dW$ | J | Thermodynamic cycles |
| 11 | Specific Energy | $e = \frac{E}{m}$ | J/kg | Battery/fuel cell rating |
| 12 | Energy Density | $\rho_E = \frac{E}{V}$ | J/m³ | Storage volume analysis |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The classification of energy is the **foundational step** for every subsequent module in PECHT411. Specifically:

- For **solar energy design**, you must first identify solar as renewable, primary, and non-conventional.
- For **fossil fuel plants**, the energy chain from coal to electricity is the basis for efficiency calculations.
- For **national energy policy**, the ratio of commercial-to-non-commercial energy indicates a country's developmental index. India aims to shift this ratio in favor of commercial energy.
- For **environmental engineering**, the renewable/non-renewable split directly determines $\text{CO}_2$ emission intensity and sustainability metrics.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Energy Unit Conversion from First Principles

This is a derivation that KTU examiners may set as a 7-mark sub-question under the "Energy Units" topic. We derive the conversion between **kilowatt-hour (kWh)** and **Joules (J)** from base SI units.

**Step 1 — Definition of the Watt (Power Unit):**
The Watt is the SI unit of power, defined as one Joule of energy transferred per second.

$$
P = 1 \text{ W} = 1 \text{ J/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Definition of Energy as Power × Time:**
Since energy is power integrated over time, for constant power $P$:

$$
E = P \times t
$$

**Step 3 — Definition of the kilowatt-hour:**
A kilowatt-hour is the energy consumed by a 1 kW device operating for 1 hour.

$$
1 \text{ kWh} = (1 \text{ kW}) \times (1 \text{ hour})
$$

**Step 4 — Convert kiloWatts to Watts:**

$$
1 \text{ kW} = 1000 \text{ W} = 1000 \text{ J/s}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert hours to seconds:**

$$
1 \text{ hour} = 60 \text{ minutes} = 60 \times 60 \text{ seconds} = 3600 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 6 — Multiply the converted values:**

$$
1 \text{ kWh} = 1000 \text{ J/s} \times 3600 \text{ s} = 3,600,000 \text{ J}
$$

**Step 7 — Final result in Megajoules:**

$$
1 \text{ kWh} = 3.6 \times 10^6 \text{ J} = 3.6 \text{ MJ}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The same derivation technique applies to the BTU, calorie, and TOE conversions. Always start from the defining statement and convert to base units step by step. KTU examiners award full marks only when each conversion step is shown explicitly.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Energy Density of Common Fuels

This matrix is critical for fuel-selection numericals. The Higher Heating Value (HHV) is the standard for KTU problems unless specified otherwise.

| Fuel | Physical State | Calorific Value | Energy Density (MJ/m³) | Renewable? |
|------|----------------|-----------------|--------------------------|------------|
| Coal (Bituminous) | Solid | 24-30 MJ/kg | ~30,000 (loose) | No |
| Petrol | Liquid | 46 MJ/kg | ~34,000 | No |
| Diesel | Liquid | 45 MJ/kg | ~38,000 | No |
| LPG (Propane) | Gas (liquid under pressure) | 50 MJ/kg | ~25,000 | No |
| Natural Gas (CNG) | Gas | 55 MJ/kg | ~9 (at STP) | No |
| Firewood (air-dry) | Solid | 16 MJ/kg | ~5,000 | Yes |
| Cow-dung cake | Solid | 8-10 MJ/kg | ~3,000 | Yes |
| Biogas (60% CH₄) | Gas | 20 MJ/m³ | 20 | Yes |
| Hydrogen (compressed) | Gas | 142 MJ/kg | ~4,000 (at 700 bar) | Yes (if from green sources) |
| Uranium-235 | Solid | $8 \times 10^{13}$ J/kg | Extremely high | No |

## 3.3 Worked Numerical — Calorific Value of a Coal Sample (KTU Style)

**Problem Statement (KTU Model):**
A coal sample of mass 2.5 kg is completely combusted in a Bomb Calorimeter. The heat released raises the temperature of 15 litres of water from 22°C to 78°C. Calculate the Higher Calorific Value (HCV) of the coal in kJ/kg. Given: Specific heat of water $c_w = 4.186$ kJ/(kg·K). Density of water = 1 kg/L.

**Step 1 — Identify the principle:**
The Bomb Calorimeter equation: Heat released by fuel = Heat absorbed by water.

$$
Q_{\text{fuel}} = Q_{\text{water}}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the mass of water:**

$$
m_w = V \times \rho = 15 \text{ L} \times 1 \text{ kg/L} = 15 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the temperature rise:**

$$
\Delta T = T_{\text{final}} - T_{\text{initial}} = 78 - 22 = 56 \text{ °C} = 56 \text{ K}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the heat absorbed equation:**

$$
Q_{\text{water}} = m_w \times c_w \times \Delta T
$$

$$
Q_{\text{water}} = 15 \times 4.186 \times 56
$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the product:**

$$
Q_{\text{water}} = 15 \times 4.186 \times 56
$$

Intermediate: $15 \times 4.186 = 62.79$
Then: $62.79 \times 56 = 3516.24$ kJ

$$
Q_{\text{water}} = 3516.24 \text{ kJ}
$$

**Step 6 — Calculate HCV (per kg of fuel):**

$$
\text{HCV} = \frac{Q_{\text{water}}}{m_{\text{fuel}}} = \frac{3516.24 \text{ kJ}}{2.5 \text{ kg}}
$$

$$
\text{HCV} = 1406.5 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

**Step 7 — Verification by dimensional check:**

$$
\frac{\text{kJ}}{\text{kg}} = \text{kJ/kg} \quad \checkmark
$$

This value is unrealistically low (typical coal is 24,000-30,000 kJ/kg), which means the examiner may have used simplified numbers to test the method. The procedure is what earns full marks, not the realistic outcome.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** In a real bomb calorimeter, you also account for the calorimeter's own water equivalent, fuse wire correction, and acid formation correction. For KTU 2024 Module 1, the simplified formula above is the expected answer.

## 3.4 Step-by-Step: Overall Chain Efficiency of a Coal-to-Motor System

**Problem:** A thermal power plant has the following stage efficiencies: Boiler 88%, Turbine 45%, Generator 96%, Transmission line 92%, Motor 90%. Calculate the overall chain efficiency from coal energy input to mechanical shaft output.

**Step 1 — Write the chain efficiency formula:**

$$
\eta_{\text{chain}} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \eta_i
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values:**

$$
\eta_{\text{chain}} = 0.88 \times 0.45 \times 0.96 \times 0.92 \times 0.90
$$

**Step 3 — Multiply sequentially (left to right):**

First: $0.88 \times 0.45 = 0.396$

$$
\eta_{\text{chain}} = 0.396 \times 0.96 \times 0.92 \times 0.90
$$

Next: $0.396 \times 0.96 = 0.38016$

$$
\eta_{\text{chain}} = 0.38016 \times 0.92 \times 0.90
$$

Next: $0.38016 \times 0.92 = 0.3497$

$$
\eta_{\text{chain}} = 0.3497 \times 0.90 = 0.3147
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to percentage:**

$$
\eta_{\text{chain}} \approx 31.47\%
$$

**Step 5 — Engineering interpretation:**
Only about **31%** of the original chemical energy in coal reaches the motor shaft as useful mechanical energy. The remaining 69% is lost as flue gas heat, condenser rejection, transmission I²R loss, and motor heat.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Classification Tree of Energy (Mermaid Block Diagram)

The following Mermaid diagram provides a complete hierarchical visualization of the General Classification of Energy as per the KTU 2024 syllabus. It is structured to be board-exam-friendly and covers all the orthogonal classifications discussed in Sections 1 and 2.

```mermaid
graph TD
    rootA[ENERGY]:::root --> class1[Classification 1: By Renewability]
    rootA --> class2[Classification 2: By Commercial Availability]
    rootA --> class3[Classification 3: By Era Adoption]
    rootA --> class4[Classification 4: By Form]

    class1 --> ren[Renewable]:::green
    class1 --> nren[Non-Renewable]:::red

    ren --> r1[Solar Energy]
    ren --> r2[Wind Energy]
    ren --> r3[Hydel Energy]
    ren --> r4[Biomass and Biogas]
    ren --> r5[Tidal and Wave]
    ren --> r6[Geothermal]
    ren --> r7[Hydrogen Green]

    nren --> n1[Coal]
    nren --> n2[Petroleum Crude Oil]

    class2 --> ncom[Non-Commercial]:::yellow
```

## 4.2 Energy Conversion Chain (From Primary Source to End-Use)

The following diagram shows the **Energy Chain Concept** — a high-yield KTU 2024 topic. The chain illustrates that energy undergoes multiple transformations, each with associated losses, from the natural source to the final useful work output.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Primary Energy Source]:::primary --> B[Conversion Plant]:::convert
    B --> C[Secondary Energy Carrier]:::secondary
    C --> D[Distribution Network]:::distribute
    D --> E[End-Use Device]:::enduse
    E --> F[Useful Work Output]:::output

    A -. Heat Loss .-> X1[Loss Stage 1]:::loss
    B -. Conversion Loss .-> X2[Loss Stage 2]:::loss
    C -. Transport Loss .-> X3[Loss Stage 3]:::loss
    D -. Grid I2R Loss .-> X4[Loss Stage 4]:::loss
    E -. Device Inefficiency .-> X5[Loss Stage 5]:::loss

    class primary fill:#0f766e,color:#ffffff,stroke:#134e4a
    class convert fill:#1d4ed8,color:#ffffff,stroke:#1e3a8a
    class secondary fill:#7c2d12,color:#ffffff,stroke:#431407
    class distribute fill:#9333ea,color:#ffffff,stroke:#581c87
    class enduse fill:#db2777,color:#ffffff,stroke:#831843
    class output fill:#16a34a,color:#ffffff,stroke:#064e3b
    class loss fill:#525252,color:#ffffff,stroke:#262626
```

**Example Annotation for a Coal-to-Light Pathway:**

- **Primary Energy:** Coal in the mine.
- **Conversion Plant:** Thermal power station.
- **Secondary Energy:** Electricity (11 kV / 33 kV).
- **Distribution Network:** Step-down transformers, 220 V domestic lines.
- **End-Use Device:** LED bulb.
- **Useful Work Output:** Visible light + small heat.

Each transition arrow corresponds to a multiplication by an efficiency $\eta_i$ in the chain efficiency equation.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question]

**Q1. Differentiate between primary and secondary energy with two suitable examples for each.**

**Model Answer:**

Primary energy is the energy that exists in nature in its raw form and has not undergone any conversion process. It is extracted directly from natural resources. Examples: (i) Coal (as mined from the earth), (ii) Crude oil (as pumped from wells), (iii) Solar radiation falling on Earth's surface, (iv) Uranium ore.

Secondary energy, on the other hand, is derived from primary energy sources through one or more conversion processes. It is the energy form that is actually delivered to the end-user. Examples: (i) Electricity generated from coal in a thermal power plant, (ii) Petrol and diesel refined from crude oil, (iii) LPG extracted from natural gas processing, (iv) Hydrogen produced by electrolysis of water.

The key difference is that primary energy sources have not been processed, while secondary energy sources are the output of an energy conversion plant. Conversion of primary to secondary always involves efficiency losses. *(3 marks)*

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Question]

**Q2. Classify the following energy sources as Renewable (R) or Non-Renewable (NR), and as Commercial (C) or Non-Commercial (NC): (a) Cow-dung cake, (b) Natural gas, (c) Hydel power, (d) Firewood, (e) LPG, (f) Solar PV.**

**Model Answer:**

| Energy Source | R / NR | C / NC |
|---------------|--------|--------|
| (a) Cow-dung cake | R | NC |
| (b) Natural gas | NR | C |
| (c) Hydel power | R | C |
| (d) Firewood | R | NC |
| (e) LPG | NR | C |
| (f) Solar PV | R | C |

*(3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Part B (14-Mark) Common Mistakes:**
> 1. **Mixing up classifications:** Students often write "solar is non-conventional" but forget to also state it is "renewable, primary, commercial". Always give ALL four classifications.
> 2. **No diagrams:** In a 14-mark question on energy classification, NOT drawing the energy chain or classification tree leads to a deduction of at least 2 marks. Always include a labeled block diagram.
> 3. **Ignoring units:** Numerical answers without explicit units (kJ/kg, MJ, kWh) are penalized heavily.
> 4. **Skipping intermediate steps in chain efficiency:** Writing the final answer without showing multiplication of $\eta_i$ leads to partial credit only.
> 5. **Vague definitions:** Writing "renewable means it is reusable" is incomplete. Define it as "naturally replenished at a rate faster than human consumption" for full credit.

### Question Choice A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question]

**Q(A). (a) Discuss in detail the general classification of energy based on (i) renewability, (ii) commercial availability, and (iii) conventionality. Give at least three examples under each category. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Classification Based on Renewability:**

Renewable energy sources are those that are naturally replenished at a rate comparable to or faster than the rate at which humans consume them. They are inexhaustible over human time-scales. Examples: (1) Solar energy — direct radiant energy from the Sun, replenished continuously. (2) Wind energy — driven by solar heating differential of the Earth's surface, replenished daily. (3) Hydel energy — the water cycle is continuously recharged by evaporation and precipitation. (4) Biomass — biological waste regrows within months to years.

Non-renewable energy sources are those that exist in finite quantities and are depleted at a rate much faster than they can be replenished by natural processes (which take millions of years). Examples: (1) Coal — formed from ancient plant matter over geological timescales. (2) Petroleum and crude oil — formed from marine organisms. (3) Natural gas — same origin as petroleum. (4) Nuclear fuels (Uranium-235) — finite mineral resource.

**(ii) Classification Based on Commercial Availability:**

Commercial energy is bought and sold in the formal market through structured supply networks. Examples: (1) Grid electricity distributed by KSEB in Kerala. (2) Petrol and diesel from IOCL/BPCL/HPCL pumps. (3) LPG cylinders from gas agencies. (4) Pipeline natural gas from GAIL.

Non-commercial energy is collected freely and is not transacted in the formal market; it dominates the rural household sector. Examples: (1) Firewood collected from forests. (2) Cow-dung cakes made at home. (3) Agricultural residues like rice straw. (4) Bagasse from sugar mills (used internally, not sold).

**(iii) Classification Based on Conventionality:**

Conventional energy sources have been in use for over a century with mature technology. Examples: (1) Coal (used since the Industrial Revolution). (2) Petroleum. (3) Natural gas. (4) Large-scale hydel.

Non-conventional energy sources are modern, sustainable alternatives with relatively recent technological adoption. Examples: (1) Solar photovoltaic and solar thermal. (2) Wind turbines. (3) Tidal and wave energy. (4) Biogas plants. (5) Fuel cells and hydrogen energy.

*(7 marks — full marks require labeled block diagram of classifications)*

**(b) A thermal power plant has the following efficiencies: Boiler 86%, Turbine 44%, Generator 95%, Transmission 90%, Motor 88%. If the plant consumes 1000 kg of coal per hour with a calorific value of 25,000 kJ/kg, determine: (i) the overall chain efficiency, (ii) the electrical energy delivered to the motor in kWh per hour, and (iii) the mechanical energy output at the motor shaft. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Overall Chain Efficiency:**

$$
\eta_{\text{chain}} = \eta_{\text{boiler}} \times \eta_{\text{turbine}} \times \eta_{\text{generator}} \times \eta_{\text{transmission}} \times \eta_{\text{motor}}
$$

$$
\eta_{\text{chain}} = 0.86 \times 0.44 \times 0.95 \times 0.90 \times 0.88
$$

Step-by-step:
$0.86 \times 0.44 = 0.3784$
$0.3784 \times 0.95 = 0.3595$
$0.3595 \times 0.90 = 0.3235$
$0.3235 \times 0.88 = 0.2847$

$$
\eta_{\text{chain}} \approx 0.2847 = 28.47\%
$$

**(ii) Electrical Energy Delivered to Motor:**

Total heat input from coal:

$$
Q_{\text{in}} = m_{\text{coal}} \times CV = 1000 \times 25,000 = 2.5 \times 10^7 \text{ kJ/h}
$$

The energy reaching the motor (after boiler, turbine, generator, transmission):

$$
E_{\text{motor,electrical}} = Q_{\text{in}} \times \eta_{\text{boiler}} \times \eta_{\text{turbine}} \times \eta_{\text{generator}} \times \eta_{\text{transmission}}
$$

$$
E_{\text{motor,electrical}} = 2.5 \times 10^7 \times 0.86 \times 0.44 \times 0.95 \times 0.90
$$

Intermediate: $0.86 \times 0.44 \times 0.95 \times 0.90 = 0.3235$

$$
E_{\text{motor,electrical}} = 2.5 \times 10^7 \times 0.3235 = 8.088 \times 10^6 \text{ kJ/h}
$$

Converting to kWh:

$$
E_{\text{motor,electrical}} = \frac{8.088 \times 10^6}{3600} = 2246.6 \text{ kWh/h}
$$

**(iii) Mechanical Energy Output at Shaft:**

$$
E_{\text{shaft}} = E_{\text{motor,electrical}} \times \eta_{\text{motor}} = 8.088 \times 10^6 \times 0.88
$$

$$
E_{\text{shaft}} = 7.117 \times 10^6 \text{ kJ/h}
$$

Converting to kWh:

$$
E_{\text{shaft}} = \frac{7.117 \times 10^6}{3600} = 1977 \text{ kWh/h}
$$

*(7 marks)*

---

### Question Choice B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Question]

**Q(B). (a) Define energy. Explain the energy chain concept with a neat block diagram and discuss why overall chain efficiency is always less than individual stage efficiencies. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:**
Energy is the capacity of a system to perform work or to transfer heat. It is a scalar property of a system that can be stored, transported, and converted between different forms, but is conserved in total quantity (First Law of Thermodynamics).

**Energy Chain Concept:**

The energy chain is the sequence of energy transformations that occurs from the natural primary source to the final end-use. Each stage introduces losses, and the **overall chain efficiency is the product of individual stage efficiencies**.

**Block Diagram (Description):**

The energy chain proceeds as: Primary Source → Extraction/Collection → Conversion Plant → Secondary Energy Carrier → Distribution Network → End-Use Device → Useful Work. At each transition, energy is lost as waste heat, friction, electrical resistance (I²R), or radiation.

**Why Overall Chain Efficiency < Individual Efficiencies:**

Mathematically, since each $\eta_i$ is less than 1 (i.e., $0 < \eta_i < 1$), the product of multiple such numbers is **strictly less than any individual factor**. For example, even if all five stages had an excellent individual efficiency of 90%, the chain efficiency is $0.9^5 = 0.59$, i.e., 59%. This is the fundamental reason why end-to-end energy utilization in real systems is often poor, and why energy conservation efforts focus on improving each stage separately — even small improvements at each stage compound multiplicatively.

The chain efficiency formula:

$$
\eta_{\text{chain}} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \eta_i < \eta_i \quad \forall i
$$

*(7 marks)*

**(b) A 100 W incandescent bulb is replaced by a 12 W LED bulb of equivalent light output. The bulb is used 6 hours per day, 30 days per month. Calculate: (i) the monthly energy savings in kWh, (ii) the monthly cost savings in ₹ at ₹7 per kWh, and (iii) the monthly CO₂ reduction in kg, given that grid emission factor is 0.82 kg CO₂ per kWh. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Monthly Energy Savings:**

Energy consumption of incandescent per month:

$$
E_{\text{inc}} = P_{\text{inc}} \times t = 100 \text{ W} \times (6 \times 30) \text{ h} = 100 \times 180 = 18,000 \text{ Wh} = 18 \text{ kWh}
$$

Energy consumption of LED per month:

$$
E_{\text{LED}} = 12 \text{ W} \times 180 \text{ h} = 2160 \text{ Wh} = 2.16 \text{ kWh}
$$

Monthly energy savings:

$$
\Delta E = E_{\text{inc}} - E_{\text{LED}} = 18 - 2.16 = 15.84 \text{ kWh}
$$

**(ii) Monthly Cost Savings:**

$$
\text{Cost saving} = \Delta E \times \text{Rate} = 15.84 \times 7 = ₹110.88
$$

**(iii) Monthly CO₂ Reduction:**

$$
\text{CO}_2 \text{ reduction} = \Delta E \times \text{Emission Factor} = 15.84 \times 0.82 = 12.99 \text{ kg}
$$

*(7 marks)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use this checklist as your **last 5-minute revision** before the KTU 2024 Energy Engineering exam.

- [ ] **Energy** = capacity to do work or transfer heat (SI unit: Joule).
- [ ] **Primary energy** = raw, in nature (coal, sunlight, uranium, wind).
- [ ] **Secondary energy** = converted from primary (electricity, petrol, LPG, hydrogen).
- [ ] **Renewable** = replenished at rate ≥ human consumption (solar, wind, hydro, biomass, tidal, geothermal).
- [ ] **Non-renewable** = finite, depletes over human timescales (coal, oil, gas, nuclear).
- [ ] **Commercial energy** = sold in market (electricity, petroleum products, piped gas).
- [ ] **Non-commercial energy** = collected freely (firewood, cow-dung, agri-residue).
- [ ] **Conventional** = mature, century-old (coal, oil, gas, large hydro, firewood).
- [ ] **Non-conventional** = modern alternatives (solar, wind, biogas, fuel cells, hydrogen).
- [ ] **Energy chain** = sequence Source → Conversion → Carrier → Distribution → End-use.
- [ ] **Chain efficiency** = product of all stage efficiencies: $\eta_{\text{chain}} = \prod \eta_i$.
- [ ] **Key conversions:** $1 \text{ kWh} = 3.6 \text{ MJ}$; $1 \text{ TOE} = 41.868 \text{ GJ}$; $1 \text{ BTU} = 1055 \text{ J}$.
- [ ] **Calorific value of fuels** (typical): Bituminous coal 24-30 MJ/kg, petrol 46 MJ/kg, natural gas 55 MJ/kg, firewood 16 MJ/kg, cow-dung 8-10 MJ/kg.
- [ ] **Bomb calorimeter formula:** $\text{HCV} = \dfrac{m_w c_w \Delta T}{m_{\text{fuel}}}$ (with $c_w = 4.186$ kJ/kg·K).
- [ ] **First Law (closed system):** $dE = dQ - dW$.
- [ ] **Power = energy / time** ($P = E/t$); SI unit: Watt.
- [ ] **Energy density (volumetric)** matters for transport and storage; **specific energy (gravimetric)** matters for mobile applications.
- [ ] **Nuclear energy** is **non-renewable** for KTU exam purposes (even though breeder reactors exist).
- [ ] **Renewable + Commercial + Non-Conventional = Solar PV / Wind Farm.**
- [ ] **Non-Renewable + Commercial + Conventional = Petrol / Coal.**
- [ ] **Renewable + Non-Commercial + Conventional = Firewood / Cow-dung.**
- [ ] **India's primary energy mix (approx., commercial):** Coal 44%, Oil 24%, Renewables 12%, Large Hydro 10%, Gas 7%, Nuclear 3%.
- [ ] **Final exam mantra:** Always state all four classifications (Renewable/Non-Renewable, Primary/Secondary, Commercial/Non-Commercial, Conventional/Non-Conventional) when describing any energy source for full credit.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: World Energy Resources
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT411 - ENERGY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** World Energy Resources

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Energy Resources** are naturally occurring reserves or stocks of energy-bearing materials (fuels) and natural energy flows (such as solar radiation, wind, and tides) that can be converted into useful forms of energy (heat, mechanical work, or electricity) to satisfy the demands of human civilization.

In strict KTU 2024 terminology, an **energy resource** is differentiated from an **energy reserve** by the economic and technological feasibility of extraction:

- **Resource:** The *total estimated quantity* of an energy-bearing material theoretically existing in the Earth's crust (geological or geographical occurrence).
- **Reserve:** The *portion of the resource* that can be economically and technically recovered *today* using available technology at prevailing market prices.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Bank Account**
> Imagine your lifetime finances as a country's energy portfolio.
> - **Total Wealth (Resource):** Every rupee you will *ever* earn in your entire life, including uncertain future bonuses.
> - **Spendable Balance (Reserve):** The money *currently* sitting safely in your bank account that you can withdraw immediately.
> - **Annual Expense (Production):** How much you spend every year.
> - **Years Remaining (R/P Ratio):** Your bank balance divided by your yearly expense — telling you how many years your current savings will last.
> Just like a person, a nation must balance *what it spends* (energy consumption) with *what it has safely saved* (reserves) and *what it can invest for the future* (renewable resources).

## 1.3 Energy Units — The Common Language

To compare coal, oil, gas, and renewables on a single ruler, energy engineers use standardized units:

| Common Unit | Equivalent Value | Application |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **1 Joule (J)** | 1 N·m | SI base unit of energy |
| **1 BTU** | **1,055 J** (≈ 252 cal) | Imperial/US system |
| **1 kcal** | **4,186.8 J** | Food energy, thermochemistry |
| **1 kWh** | **3.6 MJ** | Electrical energy billing |
| **1 toe** (tonne of oil equivalent) | **41.868 GJ** | International energy statistics |
| **1 Quad** | 10¹⁵ BTU | National energy policy (USA) |
| **1 Mtoe** | **41.868 PJ** | Country-level consumption |

> [!NOTE]
> The **toe (tonne of oil equivalent)** is the *lingua franca* of world energy statistics. When the IEA (International Energy Agency) or BP Statistical Review reports "World primary energy consumption," it is almost always expressed in **Mtoe** or **Gtoe**.

## 1.4 The Three Primary Classification Axes

Energy resources are classified along **three independent axes**:

### Axis 1: Origin (Physical Nature)
- **Primary Energy:** Found in nature *before* any human conversion (coal, crude oil, sunlight, wind, uranium).
- **Secondary Energy:** Derived from primary sources through conversion (electricity, gasoline, hydrogen, refined diesel).

### Axis 2: Renewability (Replenishment Rate)
- **Non-Renewable (Conventional/Fossil):** Stock-limited; formed over geological timescales (coal, oil, natural gas, uranium).
- **Renewable (Non-Conventional):** Flow-limited; replenished within human timescales (solar, wind, hydro, biomass, geothermal, tidal).

### Axis 3: Commercial Viability
- **Commercial Energy:** Traded in organized markets and priced (electricity, LPG, gasoline, coal).
- **Non-Commercial Energy:** Traditionally gathered free of cost (firewood, dung cakes, agricultural waste).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Renewability Spectrum — A Critical Nuance

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners expect students to recognize that *renewability* is not a binary flag. Energy resources exist on a **continuous spectrum** of replenishment time. The deciding factor is the comparison between **replenishment timescale (τ_r)** and the **human planning horizon (τ_h ≈ 100 years)**.
> - If τ_r ≪ τ_h → **Renewable** (solar, wind)
> - If τ_r ≈ τ_h → **Marginally Renewable** (biomass, deep geothermal)
> - If τ_r ≫ τ_h → **Non-Renewable** (fossil fuels, nuclear)

## 2.2 World Primary Energy Mix — Current State

According to global energy balance data, the world primary energy supply (TPES) is dominated by fossil fuels, with renewables (excluding large hydro) still forming a small but rapidly growing share.

> [!NOTE]
> A useful mental model: For every **100 units** of primary energy consumed globally, roughly **82 units** come from fossil fuels, **10 units** from biofuels and waste, **5 units** from hydro, and **3 units** from nuclear + modern renewables combined (these are approximate and vary by year).

## 2.3 Reserve Estimation — The Geological Certainty Pyramid

Resources are subdivided by **geological certainty** using a three-tier system defined by the **United Nations Framework Classification (UNFC)**:

| Tier | UNFC Code | Geological Certainty | Economic Feasibility | Example |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| **Proved Reserves** | 1-1-1 | High (drilled & measured) | Currently economic | Operating oil fields |
| **Probable Reserves** | 1-2-1 | Moderate (seismic inference) | Currently economic | Adjacent extensions |
| **Possible Reserves** | 1-3-1 | Low (geological extrapolation) | Currently economic | Speculative basins |
| **Inferred Resources** | 2-3-2 | Low to moderate | Potentially economic | Frontier fields |
| **Speculative Resources** | 3-4-3 | Very low | Not yet economic | Hypothetical |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU problems, only **Proved (Proven) Reserves** are used unless otherwise stated. Always read the wording of the question carefully — "reserves" vs "resources" is a common trap.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!CAUTION]
> **Table cells use `\vert` instead of `\|` to preserve markdown table integrity.** Do not write `P/x` in plain text; use the LaTeX form below.

| # | Quantity / Concept | Formula / Definition | Standard Units | Notes / Conditions |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | Reserve-to-Production Ratio (R/P) | $R/P \;=\; \dfrac{\text{Proved Reserves}}{\text{Annual Production}}$ | **years** | Static index; ignores demand growth |
| 2 | Growth-adjusted lifetime | $t \;\approx\; \dfrac{\ln(R/P) \cdot \ln(g+1)}{g}$ where $g$ = fractional growth rate | years | Dynamic index |
| 3 | Energy from combustion | $Q \;=\; m \times \text{GCV}$ | kJ, kWh, toe | $m$ = fuel mass, GCV = gross calorific value |
| 4 | Conversion — kWh → MJ | $1 \text{ kWh} \;=\; 3.6 \text{ MJ}$ | exact | $1 \text{ MJ} \;=\; 0.2778 \text{ kWh}$ |
| 5 | Conversion — toe → GJ | $1 \text{ toe} \;=\; 41.868 \text{ GJ}$ | standard | $1 \text{ Mtoe} \;=\; 41.868 \text{ PJ}$ |
| 6 | Conversion — BTU → J | $1 \text{ BTU} \;=\; 1055.06 \text{ J}$ | standard | Used in US/UK statistics |
| 7 | Higher (Gross) vs Lower (Net) Heating Value | $\text{NCV} \;=\; \text{GCV} \;-\; \left( h_{fg} \cdot \dfrac{9H}{100} \right)$ | same as GCV | $h_{fg}$ at 25 °C = **2,442 kJ/kg** water; $H$ = hydrogen mass % |
| 8 | Dulong's formula (coal GCV) | $\text{GCV} \;=\; 33{,}820\,C \;+\; 144{,}248\left(H - \dfrac{O}{8}\right) \;+\; 9{,}270\,S$ | kJ/kg | $C, H, O, S$ on dry-ash-free basis (%) |
| 9 | Energy density (volumetric) | $\rho_E \;=\; \text{GCV} \times \rho_{\text{fuel}}$ | MJ/L | Determines transport/storage economics |
| 10 | Power vs Energy | $E \;=\; P \times t$ | J = W·s | $1 \text{ W} \;=\; 1 \text{ J/s}$ |

## 2.5 Why This Matters in Real Engineering

- **Reserves drive geopolitics** — OPEC nations' bargaining power is set by their *proved reserves*, not by total resources.
- **R/P ratio sets long-term policy** — A 50-year coal R/P is fundamentally different from a 200-year uranium R/P.
- **Calorific value determines plant economics** — A thermal power station's efficiency ranking, fuel blending decisions, and emission control strategies all depend on GCV/NCV.
- **Dulong's formula** is still used for rapid GCV estimation of coal when a bomb calorimeter is unavailable — a routine calculation in plant fuel audits.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The R/P Ratio (Static Lifetime Estimate)

The **Reserve-to-Production Ratio** is the most fundamental metric used by the World Energy Council, BP Statistical Review, and the IEA. Here is its rigorous derivation.

**Statement:** Given proved reserves $R$ and constant annual production rate $P$, find the time $t^*$ at which reserves are exhausted.

$$
\text{Reserve depletion at constant rate } P \;\Rightarrow\; R(t) \;=\; R_0 \;-\; P \cdot t
$$

At the moment of exhaustion, $R(t^*) = 0$. Substituting:

$$
0 \;=\; R_0 \;-\; P \cdot t^*
$$

Solving for the time $t^*$:

$$
t^* \;=\; \dfrac{R_0}{P} \;\equiv\; \text{R/P ratio}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **static** R/P. It assumes that (i) production continues at *today's* level, (ii) no new reserves are discovered, and (iii) consumption is *not* growing. In reality, all three assumptions fail — which is why the IEA also publishes a *dynamic* version.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example 1 — Coal Reserves (India / World)

**Problem:** A coal basin has proved reserves of **108,000 million tonnes** and is producing at the rate of **600 million tonnes per year**. Calculate the static R/P ratio and interpret.

**Step 1 — Identify variables.**

$$
R_0 \;=\; 108{,}000 \text{ Mt}, \qquad P \;=\; 600 \text{ Mt/yr}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the R/P formula.**

$$
t^* \;=\; \dfrac{R_0}{P} \;=\; \dfrac{108{,}000 \text{ Mt}}{600 \text{ Mt/yr}}
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate.**

$$
t^* \;=\; 180 \text{ years}
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> At *constant* production, the coal will last **180 years**. However, India's coal production is *growing* (historically 4–6% per year), so the *true* exhaustion time will be significantly shorter — typically **60–90 years** under realistic growth scenarios. This is the core of why India invests in solar and nuclear.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Dynamic (Growth-Adjusted) Reserve Lifetime

**Statement:** Suppose production grows annually at fractional rate $g$. Find the time $t^{**}$ at which reserves are exhausted.

Annual production in year $n$ is:

$$
P_n \;=\; P_0 (1 + g)^{n}
$$

Cumulative production over $N$ years (geometric series):

$$
\text{Cumulative} \;=\; \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} P_0(1+g)^{n} \;=\; P_0 \cdot \dfrac{(1+g)^{N} - 1}{g}
$$

At exhaustion, cumulative production equals the initial reserve:

$$
R_0 \;=\; P_0 \cdot \dfrac{(1+g)^{N} - 1}{g}
$$

Rearranging:

$$
(1+g)^{N} \;=\; 1 + g \cdot \dfrac{R_0}{P_0} \;=\; 1 + g \cdot (R/P)_{\text{static}}
$$

Taking the natural logarithm:

$$
N \cdot \ln(1+g) \;=\; \ln\!\left(1 + g \cdot (R/P)_{\text{static}}\right)
$$

$$
\boxed{\,t^{**} \;=\; \dfrac{\ln\!\left(1 + g \cdot (R/P)_{\text{static}}\right)}{\ln(1 + g)}\,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> For small $g$, use the approximation $\ln(1+g) \approx g$, and the result reduces to $t^{**} \approx (R/P)_{\text{static}} \cdot (1 - g \cdot R/(2P) + \ldots)$, showing a *shorter* lifetime than the static case.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example 2 — Oil Reserve with Growth

**Problem:** A country has oil reserves $R_0 = 8{,}000$ million tonnes and current production $P_0 = 200$ Mt/yr, growing at **3% per year**. Find (a) the static R/P and (b) the dynamic lifetime.

**Step (a) — Static R/P.**

$$
t^* \;=\; \dfrac{8{,}000}{200} \;=\; 40 \text{ years}
$$

**Step (b) — Dynamic lifetime.**

Substitute $R/P = 40$ and $g = 0.03$:

$$
t^{**} \;=\; \dfrac{\ln(1 + 0.03 \times 40)}{\ln(1.03)} \;=\; \dfrac{\ln(1 + 1.20)}{\ln(1.03)}
$$

$$
t^{**} \;=\; \dfrac{\ln(2.20)}{\ln(1.03)} \;=\; \dfrac{0.7885}{0.02956}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,t^{**} \;\approx\; 26.7 \text{ years}\,}
$$

**Interpretation:** Production growth of 3% per year cuts the *effective* oil lifetime from 40 years down to about **27 years** — a 33% reduction.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example 3 — Heating Value Conversion

**Problem:** A furnace uses **500 kg/day** of furnace oil with GCV = **42 MJ/kg**. Express this daily energy input in **kWh** and in **toe** (annual).

**Step 1 — Daily energy in MJ.**

$$
Q_{\text{day}} \;=\; m \times \text{GCV} \;=\; 500 \text{ kg} \times 42 \text{ MJ/kg} \;=\; 21{,}000 \text{ MJ/day}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert to kWh.** Using $1 \text{ kWh} = 3.6 \text{ MJ}$:

$$
Q_{\text{day}} \;=\; \dfrac{21{,}000}{3.6} \;\approx\; 5{,}833.3 \text{ kWh/day}
$$

**Step 3 — Annual energy in kWh.**

$$
Q_{\text{year}} \;=\; 5{,}833.3 \times 365 \;\approx\; 2.129 \times 10^{6} \text{ kWh/yr}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to toe.** First, annual energy in MJ:

$$
Q_{\text{year}} \;=\; 21{,}000 \times 365 \;=\; 7.665 \times 10^{6} \text{ MJ/yr}
$$

Using $1 \text{ toe} = 41.868 \text{ GJ} = 41{,}868 \text{ MJ}$:

$$
Q_{\text{year}} \;=\; \dfrac{7.665 \times 10^{6}}{41{,}868} \;\approx\; 183.1 \text{ toe/yr}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU answer scripts, **always show the unit-cancellation chain explicitly** to score full marks in the "units & dimensions" step.

---

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example 4 — Dulong's Formula Application

**Problem:** A coal sample has the following ultimate analysis (dry basis): $C = 68\%$, $H = 5\%$, $O = 12\%$, $S = 1.0\%$, $N = 1.2\%$, Ash = 12.8%. Estimate GCV using Dulong's formula.

**Step 1 — Apply Dulong's equation.**

$$
\text{GCV} \;=\; 33{,}820\,C \;+\; 144{,}248\left(H - \dfrac{O}{8}\right) \;+\; 9{,}270\,S
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute term-by-term.**

Carbon term:

$$
33{,}820 \times 0.68 \;=\; 22{,}997.6 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

Hydrogen-oxygen correction:

$$
144{,}248 \times \left(0.05 - \dfrac{0.12}{8}\right) \;=\; 144{,}248 \times (0.05 - 0.015) \;=\; 144{,}248 \times 0.035
$$

$$
=\; 5{,}048.68 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

Sulphur term:

$$
9{,}270 \times 0.01 \;=\; 92.70 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

**Step 3 — Sum.**

$$
\text{GCV} \;=\; 22{,}997.6 \;+\; 5{,}048.68 \;+\; 92.70 \;\approx\; 28{,}139 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,\text{GCV} \;\approx\; 28.14 \text{ MJ/kg}\,}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Dulong's formula is **empirical** and is most accurate for bituminous coals. For lignite and high-ash coals, error of 5–10% is common — examiners may award partial credit for stating this caveat.

---

## 3.7 Conversion: GCV ↔ NCV (Lower Heating Value)

**Problem:** A natural gas has GCV = **52 MJ/kg** and hydrogen content $H = 23\%$. Find NCV.

**Step 1 — Apply conversion formula.**

$$
\text{NCV} \;=\; \text{GCV} \;-\; 2{,}442 \times \dfrac{9H}{100}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute.**

$$
\text{NCV} \;=\; 52{,}000 \;-\; 2{,}442 \times \dfrac{9 \times 23}{100} \;=\; 52{,}000 \;-\; 2{,}442 \times 2.07
$$

$$
\text{NCV} \;=\; 52{,}000 \;-\; 5{,}054.94 \;\approx\; 46{,}945 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,\text{NCV} \;\approx\; 46.95 \text{ MJ/kg}\,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering rule of thumb:** NCV is typically **5–10% lower** than GCV for gaseous fuels and **2–5% lower** for liquid fuels. Coal can show 5–8% gap. Always specify whether the value is GCV or NCV — KTU valuation penalizes ambiguous units.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between energy resource and energy reserve. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Energy Resource | Energy Reserve |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Definition | Total estimated quantity of an energy-bearing material in the Earth's crust (geological occurrence) | Subset of resource that is *technically and economically* recoverable with *current* technology and prices |
| Certainty | Includes identified + undiscovered | Only identified and recoverable |
| Example | Total coal in a sedimentary basin | Proved coal in operating mines |

> Energy Resource = Resource = "potentially there" ; Reserve = "ready to extract today." *(1 mark for distinction, 1 mark for definitions, 1 mark for example pair.)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — June 2023]`
**Define R/P ratio. What is its limitation as a measure of resource longevity?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Definition (1 mark):** The Reserve-to-Production ratio is the static lifetime of a resource at the current rate of production, given by $R/P = R_0 / P$ where $R_0$ is proved reserve and $P$ is annual production, expressed in **years**.
- **Limitations (2 marks):**
  1. Assumes *constant* production — ignores demand growth (in reality consumption rises).
  2. Ignores *new discoveries* that may add to proved reserves.
  3. Ignores *technological progress* that can convert resources into reserves.
  4. Does not account for *extraction economics* (price shocks, environmental costs).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Classify the world energy resources in detail with examples. Discuss the three different bases of classification. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A coal deposit has proved reserves of **1,800 million tonnes** and current annual production of **90 Mt/yr**. Production is expected to grow at **4% per year**. Find:
  - (i) the static R/P ratio,
  - (ii) the dynamic reserve lifetime,
  - (iii) the cumulative coal produced in the first 10 years. *(7 marks)*

#### Solution to (a):

**Basis 1 — Origin (Primary vs Secondary):**
- *Primary:* found in nature before conversion — coal, crude oil, sunlight, uranium, wind.
- *Secondary:* derived after conversion — electricity, gasoline, diesel, hydrogen, refined fuels.
*(2 marks)*

**Basis 2 — Renewability (Renewable vs Non-Renewable):**
- *Non-Renewable:* fossil fuels (coal, oil, gas) and nuclear (uranium) — replenishment time ≫ human timescale.
- *Renewable:* solar, wind, hydro, biomass, geothermal, tidal, wave — replenishment time ≪ human timescale.
*(2 marks)*

**Basis 3 — Commercial Use (Commercial vs Non-Commercial):**
- *Commercial:* traded in markets, have a market price — electricity, LPG, gasoline, coal.
- *Non-Commercial:* gathered without formal transactions — firewood, dung cakes, agricultural residue.
*(2 marks)*

**Conclusion (1 mark):** A single resource (e.g., coal) is classified differently on each axis — *primary*, *non-renewable*, *commercial* — illustrating that the three classifications are *independent*.

#### Solution to (b):

**Given:** $R_0 = 1{,}800$ Mt, $P_0 = 90$ Mt/yr, $g = 0.04$.

**Part (i) — Static R/P ratio:**

$$
t^* \;=\; \dfrac{R_0}{P_0} \;=\; \dfrac{1{,}800}{90} \;=\; \boxed{20 \text{ years}}
$$

**Part (ii) — Dynamic reserve lifetime:**

$$
t^{**} \;=\; \dfrac{\ln(1 + g \cdot R/P)}{\ln(1+g)} \;=\; \dfrac{\ln(1 + 0.04 \times 20)}{\ln(1.04)} \;=\; \dfrac{\ln(1.80)}{\ln(1.04)}
$$

$$
t^{**} \;=\; \dfrac{0.5878}{0.03922} \;\approx\; \boxed{15.0 \text{ years}}
$$

**Part (iii) — Cumulative production in 10 years** (geometric series with $P_0 = 90$):

$$
C_{10} \;=\; P_0 \cdot \dfrac{(1+g)^{10} - 1}{g} \;=\; 90 \cdot \dfrac{(1.04)^{10} - 1}{0.04}
$$

$$
(1.04)^{10} \;\approx\; 1.4802 \;\Rightarrow\; C_{10} \;=\; 90 \cdot \dfrac{0.4802}{0.04} \;=\; 90 \times 12.006 \;\approx\; \boxed{1{,}080.5 \text{ Mt}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> After 10 years, **1,080.5 Mt** out of **1,800 Mt** reserves are exhausted — about **60%** of the total. This visually shows the *accelerating* nature of compound growth.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam — June 2022]` *(Module Internal Choice)*

**(a)** Explain the differences between GCV and NCV of a fuel. A fuel oil has GCV = **45,200 kJ/kg** and contains **12% hydrogen** by mass. Calculate its NCV. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A thermal power station consumes **12,000 tonnes of coal per day** with GCV = **24 MJ/kg**. If the station operates at **35% overall efficiency** and feeds an average electrical load of **500 MW**, calculate the daily energy generated (in MWh), the energy input required, and verify whether the coal supply is sufficient. *(7 marks)*

#### Solution to (a):

**GCV vs NCV (3 marks):**

| Aspect | GCV (Gross / Higher) | NCV (Net / Lower) |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Definition | Heat released assuming water vapour in products is fully condensed and latent heat recovered | Heat released assuming water vapour leaves as vapour (latent heat not recovered) |
| Use | Used in laboratory bomb calorimetry | Used in industrial furnace calculations where flue gas exits hot |
| Typical relation | GCV > NCV | NCV = GCV − latent heat of vaporization of product water |

**Numerical (4 marks):**

Using NCV = GCV − $2{,}442 \times \dfrac{9H}{100}$ with $H = 12$:

$$
\text{NCV} \;=\; 45{,}200 \;-\; 2{,}442 \times \dfrac{9 \times 12}{100} \;=\; 45{,}200 \;-\; 2{,}442 \times 1.08
$$

$$
\text{NCV} \;=\; 45{,}200 \;-\; 2{,}637.36 \;\approx\; \boxed{42{,}563 \text{ kJ/kg}}
$$

#### Solution to (b):

**Step 1 — Daily energy input from coal.**

$$
Q_{\text{in}} \;=\; 12{,}000 \times 10^3 \text{ kg} \times 24 \text{ MJ/kg} \;=\; 2.88 \times 10^{8} \text{ MJ/day}
$$

Convert to MWh using $1 \text{ MWh} = 3{,}600 \text{ MJ}$:

$$
Q_{\text{in}} \;=\; \dfrac{2.88 \times 10^{8}}{3{,}600} \;=\; 80{,}000 \text{ MWh/day}
$$

**Step 2 — Daily electrical energy output** (at 35% efficiency):

$$
E_{\text{out}} \;=\; \eta \times Q_{\text{in}} \;=\; 0.35 \times 80{,}000 \;=\; 28{,}000 \text{ MWh/day}
$$

**Step 3 — Energy required by the load** for one day:

$$
E_{\text{load}} \;=\; 500 \text{ MW} \times 24 \text{ h} \;=\; 12{,}000 \text{ MWh/day}
$$

**Step 4 — Verify sufficiency:**

$$
E_{\text{out}} \;=\; 28{,}000 \text{ MWh/day} \;>\; E_{\text{load}} \;=\; 12{,}000 \text{ MWh/day}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Yes, the coal supply is sufficient.} \text{ Surplus} \;=\; 16{,}000 \text{ MWh/day}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The surplus represents plant availability margin and accounts for auxiliary consumption (mills, fans, pumps) which are *not* subtracted here. In a real valuation, examiners may expect you to *briefly mention* auxiliaries consume 5–8% internally.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls on World Energy Resources Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing "reserves" and "resources"** — Mixing the two in a single answer leads to immediate -2 marks. Always state which you are using.
> 2. **Forgetting units in R/P** — The answer is in *years*, not "tonnes" or "ratio." A bare number without units = 0.5 mark deduction.
> 3. **Static R/P trap** — If the question mentions "demand growth" or "production is increasing," you *must* use the dynamic formula. Using static R/P here loses up to 3 marks.
> 4. **Heating value ambiguity** — Always specify GCV or NCV; if a numerical value is given without label, state your assumption explicitly.
> 5. **Unit conversion slip-ups** — `1 toe = 41.868 GJ` is the *most-asked* conversion. Memorize the chain: **kWh → MJ → GJ → toe → Mtoe**.
> 6. **Geological certainty** — Reserve classification questions often expect the UNFC code (1-1-1, 1-2-1, etc.) — writing only "proved" or "probable" without the code loses 1 mark.
> 7. **Dulong's formula basis** — Always state "dry, ash-free basis" if the problem doesn't provide ash; otherwise use dry basis and note the assumption.
> 8. **Mistaking 1 BTU = 1,055 J as exact** — In KTU, use **1 BTU = 1,055.06 J**, or as an approximation **1 BTU ≈ 1.055 kJ** for energy calculations.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — World Energy Resources**

- **Energy Resource** = total estimated natural occurrence; **Energy Reserve** = currently extractable subset.
- **Three classification axes:** Origin (Primary/Secondary), Renewability (Renewable/Non-Renewable), Commercial use (Commercial/Non-Commercial).
- **Major world primary energy sources:** Coal, Oil, Natural Gas, Nuclear, Hydro, Solar, Wind, Biomass, Geothermal, Tidal.
- **Energy units to memorize cold:**
  - $1 \text{ kWh} = 3.6 \text{ MJ}$
  - $1 \text{ toe} = 41.868 \text{ GJ}$
  - $1 \text{ BTU} = 1{,}055.06 \text{ J}$
  - $1 \text{ Quad} = 10^{15} \text{ BTU}$
- **Static R/P ratio:** $t^* = R_0 / P$ — measures years of supply at *current* production.
- **Dynamic R/P lifetime:** $t^{**} = \ln(1 + g \cdot R/P) / \ln(1+g)$ — accounts for production growth.
- **UNFC reserve tiers** (in increasing uncertainty): Proved 1-1-1 → Probable 1-2-1 → Possible 1-3-1 → Inferred → Speculative.
- **Dulong's formula** for coal GCV (in kJ/kg): $33{,}820\,C + 144{,}248(H - O/8) + 9{,}270\,S$, where $C, H, O, S$ are mass fractions.
- **NCV = GCV − 2,442 × (9H/100)**, where 2,442 kJ/kg is the latent heat of water vaporization at 25 °C and $H$ is mass % of hydrogen.
- **Cumulative production under growth:** $C_N = P_0 \cdot [(1+g)^N - 1]/g$.
- **Current world energy mix (approximate order of dominance):** Oil > Coal > Natural Gas > Biofuels/Waste > Nuclear > Hydro > Other Renewables.
- **India's energy position:** Third-largest primary energy consumer globally, ~70% import dependence for crude oil, vast coal reserves (≈ 4th globally in proved reserves), but low per-capita consumption compared to OECD nations.
- **Renewable energy growth (KTU hot topic):** Solar PV has the fastest cost decline (~80% in the last decade), making it the leading capacity addition in India and worldwide.
- **"Reserve" vs "Resource"** is the single most-tested conceptual distinction in Module 1 — expect it in 70% of past papers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Energy Consumption and Future Perspectives
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT411 - ENERGY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Energy Consumption and Future Perspectives

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is Energy Consumption?

**Energy Consumption** is the amount of energy (measured in Joules, kWh, Mtoe, or GJ) that is utilized by a defined entity — a household, a sector, a nation, or the entire world — over a specific period of time. It represents the *demand side* of the energy economy and is the single most important metric used by energy planners, economists, and policy-makers to design infrastructure, forecast demand, and allocate capital.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (verbatim from PECHT411 syllabus):**
> *Energy consumption refers to the use of energy as a fuel or feedstock by a society, expressed in quantifiable metrics such as Total Primary Energy Supply (TPES), Total Final Consumption (TFC), Per Capita Energy Consumption (PCEC), and Energy Intensity (EI).*

### 1.2 The Energy Hierarchy: Primary → Final → Useful

Energy does not vanish, but it does degrade. Every unit of energy passes through three distinct stages:

1. **Primary Energy** — Energy found in nature *before* any human conversion (coal, crude oil, natural gas, solar radiation, wind, uranium, falling water, biomass).
2. **Secondary / Final Energy** — Energy after conversion into a form delivered to the end user (electricity, gasoline, diesel, LPG, refined fuels, district heat).
3. **Useful Energy** — Energy actually performing the desired service at the point of use (heat in a furnace, mechanical work in a turbine, light from an LED).

> [!NOTE]
> The conversion chain is governed by the **First Law of Thermodynamics (energy conservation)** but degraded by the **Second Law (entropy increase)**, meaning usable energy *monotonically decreases* at every step.

### 1.3 Key Quantitative Metrics

| Metric | Definition | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|
| **TPES (Total Primary Energy Supply)** | All energy produced/imported minus exports/stock changes | Mtoe, EJ |
| **TFC (Total Final Consumption)** | Energy delivered to end-use sectors | Mtoe, GJ |
| **PCEC (Per Capita Energy Consumption)** | Average energy used per person per year | GJ/person/year, toe/capita |
| **Energy Intensity (EI)** | Energy consumed per unit of GDP | MJ/\$ or toe/\$1000 |
| **Energy Elasticity** | Ratio of % change in energy demand to % change in GDP | dimensionless |

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — "The Energy Waterfall"

Imagine energy as **water flowing down a series of rocky waterfalls**:

- The **source river** (mountains) = **Primary energy** (sun, coal, gas).
- The **water reaching the city reservoir** = **Final energy** (electricity, gasoline after refining).
- The **water finally used to drink, irrigate, or run a turbine** = **Useful energy**.
- The **splash, mist, and heat lost to the air** = **Rejected energy / losses** (governed by the 2nd Law).

> [!TIP]
> **Global reference values (2023, IEA data):** World PCEC ≈ **1.6 toe/capita**, USA ≈ 7.0, EU ≈ 3.0, India ≈ **0.6**, Norway ≈ 6.5. The huge gap illustrates the **energy poverty–energy abundance** divide.

### 1.5 Why "Future Perspectives"?

A *snapshot* of consumption is insufficient. Engineers must understand:
- **Demand growth** (population × affluence × efficiency).
- **Resource depletion** (peak oil, gas, coal).
- **Climate constraints** (net-zero targets).
- **Technology disruption** (renewables, storage, hydrogen, fusion).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Future energy perspectives are framed around the **"Energy Trilemma"** — the simultaneous pursuit of **Energy Security, Energy Equity (affordability), and Environmental Sustainability**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Drivers of Energy Consumption

The **IPAT Identity** (Ehrlich–Holdren, 1971) and its modern refinement the **Kaya Identity** capture the four root drivers:

$$I = P \times A \times T$$

Where:
- $I$ = Environmental Impact (here = energy-related emissions)
- $P$ = Population
- $A$ = Affluence (GDP per capita)
- $T$ = Technology (energy intensity inverse)

The **Kaya Identity** (1990) translates this into pure energy terms:

$$CO_2 = P \times \left(\frac{GDP}{P}\right) \times \left(\frac{E}{GDP}\right) \times \left(\frac{CO_2}{E}\right)$$

- $\dfrac{GDP}{P}$ = GDP per capita (affluence)
- $\dfrac{E}{GDP}$ = Energy Intensity
- $\dfrac{CO_2}{E}$ = Carbon Intensity of energy

> [!NOTE]
> The Kaya identity is the *most-tested* analytical tool in KTU energy economics questions. Memorize the four factors and their units.

### 2.2 World Energy Consumption — Historical & Current Snapshot

- **1800 (Industrial Revolution start):** Global energy ≈ 0.5 EJ — almost entirely biomass.
- **1900:** ~ 25 EJ — coal dominant.
- **1950:** ~ 110 EJ — oil rising.
- **2000:** ~ 420 EJ — oil + gas dominant.
- **2023:** ~ 620 EJ — fossil fuels still ≈ 81 %, renewables ≈ 14 %, nuclear ≈ 4 %.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU frequently asks: "**Discuss the global energy mix evolution.**" Anchor your answer to the *fossil-fuels → transition → renewables* arc, citing the 620 EJ benchmark.

### 2.3 Sectoral Energy Consumption

Most national energy balances divide final consumption into five sectors:

| Sector | Typical Share (India) | Typical Share (World) |
|---|---|---|
| **Industrial** | ~ 55 % | ~ 37 % |
| **Transport** | ~ 14 % | ~ 29 % |
| **Residential / Domestic** | ~ 22 % | ~ 22 % |
| **Commercial / Services** | ~ 4 % | ~ 6 % |
| **Agriculture** | ~ 5 % | ~ 2 % |
| **Non-energy (feedstock)** | included in industrial | ~ 4 % |

### 2.4 India's Energy Scenario (Critical for KTU)

- India is the **3rd largest primary energy consumer** globally (after China and USA).
- **~ 55 %** of installed power capacity is coal-based.
- Per capita electricity consumption ≈ **1,300 kWh/yr** (world avg ≈ 3,500 kWh/yr).
- Heavy dependence on **imported crude oil (>85 %)** and **LNG (>50 %)**.
- **Renewable installed capacity > 190 GW** (one of the fastest expansions).
- Target: **Net-zero by 2070**; 50 % non-fossil installed capacity by 2030.

### 2.5 Future Energy Perspectives — Five Strategic Themes

1. **Decarbonization** — shift from fossil to low-carbon energy vectors.
2. **Electrification of everything** — transport, heating, industry powered by electricity.
3. **Hydrogen Economy** — green H₂ as the new "oil" of the 21st century.
4. **Circular Energy Economy** — recycling, demand response, storage.
5. **Energy Justice / Equity** — universal access (SDG 7).

> [!TIP]
> Link each theme to a **UN Sustainable Development Goal (SDG)**: SDG 7 (Affordable & Clean Energy), SDG 13 (Climate Action), SDG 9 (Industry, Innovation, Infrastructure).

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Identity | Expression | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Per Capita Energy Consumption | $PCEC = \dfrac{E_{total}}{N_{pop}}$ | toe/person, GJ/capita | Demand forecasting |
| 2 | Energy Intensity | $EI = \dfrac{E_{total}}{GDP}$ | MJ/\$ or toe/\$1000 | Efficiency comparison |
| 3 | Kaya Identity | $CO_2 = P \cdot \dfrac{GDP}{P} \cdot \dfrac{E}{GDP} \cdot \dfrac{CO_2}{E}$ | Mt CO₂ | Emission decomposition |
| 4 | Energy Demand (CAGR projection) | $E_n = E_0 \cdot (1+r)^n$ | EJ, Mtoe | Year-ahead forecasts |
| 5 | First-Law Efficiency | $\eta_{I} = \dfrac{E_{useful,out}}{E_{in}}$ | dimensionless | Process audit |
| 6 | Cumulative Energy Demand (CED) | $CED = \sum_i E_i^{embodied}$ | MJ | LCA of fuels |
| 7 | Energy Payback Period | $EPP = \dfrac{E_{invested}}{E_{annual,output}}$ | years | Renewables |
| 8 | Energy Conservation Ratio | $E_{saved} = E_{before} - E_{after}$ | GJ, kWh | ECM evaluation |
| 9 | Net Energy Ratio | $EROI = \dfrac{E_{returned}}{E_{invested}}$ | dimensionless | Resource quality |
| 10 | Carbon Intensity | $CI = \dfrac{CO_2}{E}$ | tCO₂/toe | Climate accounting |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical symbols (memorize for KTU):** $E$ = energy, $N$ = population, $r$ = growth rate, $n$ = number of years, $P$ = population, $EI$ = energy intensity, $EROI$ = Energy Return On Investment.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Per Capita Energy Consumption (PCEC)

**Statement:** The average energy available to each inhabitant of a region, assuming equitable distribution.

**Starting principle:** Total energy consumption in a region is the sum of consumptions across all individuals (an additive scalar quantity).

$$
\text{Let } E_{total} = \text{annual total energy consumption of the region} \quad [GJ]
$$

$$
\text{Let } N_{pop} = \text{resident population of the region} \quad [\text{persons}]
$$

**Step 1:** Define PCEC as the *extensive* (total) energy divided by the *intensive* (per-person) base:

$$
PCEC = \frac{E_{total}}{N_{pop}}
$$

**Step 2:** Insert standard units. Multiply by $\dfrac{1}{10^6}$ to convert GJ → toe ($1 \text{ toe} = 41.868 \text{ GJ}$):

$$
PCEC \big[toe/capita\big] = \frac{E_{total}\,[GJ]}{N_{pop} \times 41.868}
$$

**Step 3 (numerical example for KTU):** If a state has $E_{total} = 1.255 \times 10^7$ GJ and $N_{pop} = 5 \times 10^6$:

$$
PCEC = \frac{1.255 \times 10^7}{5 \times 10^6} = 2.51 \ \text{GJ/capita}
$$

Convert to toe/capita:

$$
PCEC = \frac{2.51}{41.868} = 0.060 \ \text{toe/capita}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For a **14-mark question**, you must (a) define PCEC, (b) derive the expression, (c) work a numerical example, and (d) discuss its *limitations* (ignores distribution inequality, ignores energy quality, ignores end-use).

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Energy Intensity (EI)

**Statement:** The amount of energy required to produce one unit of economic output.

$$
\text{Define } E_{total} = \text{annual primary or final energy consumption} \quad [MJ]
$$

$$
\text{Define } GDP = \text{Gross Domestic Product in monetary terms} \quad [\$ \text{ or } ₹]
$$

**Step 1:** Energy Intensity is a ratio of two extensive quantities yielding an *intensive* metric:

$$
EI = \frac{E_{total}}{GDP}
$$

**Step 2:** Common KTU unit — **MJ per constant 2005 US\$** (IEA convention):

$$
EI \left[\frac{MJ}{\$}\right] = \frac{E_{total}\,[MJ]}{GDP\,[\$]}
$$

**Step 3 (numerical example):** A country's energy use is $5 \times 10^8$ GJ and GDP is \$ 2.5 × 10¹¹:

$$
EI = \frac{5 \times 10^{8} \times 10^3 \ MJ}{2.5 \times 10^{11} \ \$} = 2.0 \ \frac{MJ}{\$}
$$

**Step 4 (interpolation):** A *lower* EI = a more *energy-efficient* economy. World average ≈ 5.2 MJ/\$; Japan ≈ 4.0; India ≈ 7.5; Singapore ≈ 3.2 (lowest).

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Compound Annual Growth Rate (CAGR) of Energy Demand

Forecasting future demand is a KTU favourite. Assume *exponential* growth (standard assumption over long horizons):

**Step 1:** Let the demand at time $0$ be $E_0$ and at time $n$ be $E_n$. For constant annual growth rate $r$:

$$
E_n = E_0 \cdot (1+r)^n
$$

**Step 2:** Solve for $r$:

$$
(1+r)^n = \frac{E_n}{E_0}
$$

$$
1+r = \left(\frac{E_n}{E_0}\right)^{1/n}
$$

$$
r = \left(\frac{E_n}{E_0}\right)^{1/n} - 1
$$

**Step 3 (numerical):** India's primary energy grew from 750 Mtoe (2010) to 1200 Mtoe (2022). Find CAGR over 12 years:

$$
r = \left(\frac{1200}{750}\right)^{1/12} - 1 = (1.6)^{0.0833} - 1
$$

$$
\ln(1.6) = 0.4700 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 0.4700 \times 0.0833 = 0.0392
$$

$$
r = e^{0.0392} - 1 = 0.0400 = 4.0 \ \% \text{ per year}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The $e^{x}$ approximation $e^{0.04} \approx 1.0408$ is acceptable to 2 decimal places. **Always retain 3 significant figures** in KTU derivations.

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Energy Payback Period (EPP) for a Renewable Plant

**Definition:** The time required for a system to generate the *embodied energy* it consumed during its manufacture, installation, and decommissioning.

**Step 1:** Define the system:

- $E_{embodied}$ — total energy consumed in raw-material extraction, manufacturing, transport, construction, and end-of-life dismantling of the plant **[GJ]**.
- $E_{annual}$ — net annual energy delivered by the plant (after parasitic losses) **[GJ/yr]**.

**Step 2:** Form the ratio (a time, in years):

$$
EPP = \frac{E_{embodied}}{E_{annual}}
$$

**Step 3 (numerical):** A 2 MW solar PV plant has $E_{embodied} = 80{,}000$ GJ and produces $E_{annual} = 22{,}000$ GJ/yr:

$$
EPP = \frac{80{,}000}{22{,}000} = 3.64 \ \text{years}
$$

For KTU: *Always state the typical EPP* (PV ≈ 1–4 yr, wind ≈ 0.5–2 yr, nuclear ≈ 5–17 yr, coal plant ≈ 0.5 yr — but coal's *carbon* payback is centuries).

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — "What Determines a Country's Energy Future?"

| Lever | Equation Form | Direction of Effect | Time Horizon |
|---|---|---|---|
| Population growth | $P(t) = P_0(1+g_p)^t$ | ↑P ⇒ ↑E (positive) | Decades |
| Affluence (GDP/capita) | $A = GDP/P$ | ↑A ⇒ ↑E (positive) | Decades |
| Energy intensity | $EI = E/GDP$ | ↑EI ⇒ ↑E (positive) | Years–decades |
| Decarbonization policy | $CI = CO_2/E$ | ↓CI ⇒ ↓ fossil share | Years |
| Technology (efficiency) | $\eta = W_out/Q_in$ | ↑η ⇒ ↓E for same service | Years |
| Energy substitution | substitution elasticity | Mixed | Years–decades |

> [!TIP]
> The Kaya identity integrates *all* of these levers. **Practice rewriting any country-specific energy projection in Kaya form** — examiners reward this.

### 3.6 Sankey-Style Numerical Walkthrough — Energy Flow in a Coal-Fired Plant

**Given:** A 500 MW coal plant, calorific value $CV = 25$ MJ/kg, plant efficiency $\eta_{gross} = 38$ %.

**Step 1:** Annual fuel input at 80 % plant load factor (PLF):

$$
E_{in} = 500 \times 10^3 \times 8760 \times 0.80 = 3.504 \times 10^9 \ \text{kWh} / \text{yr} = 12.61 \times 10^6 \ \text{GJ/yr}
$$

**Step 2:** Annual electrical output:

$$
E_{out} = E_{in} \times \eta = 12.61 \times 10^6 \times 0.38 = 4.79 \times 10^6 \ \text{GJ/yr}
$$

**Step 3:** Annual losses (heat + auxiliaries):

$$
E_{loss} = 12.61 \times 10^6 - 4.79 \times 10^6 = 7.82 \times 10^6 \ \text{GJ/yr} \ (62\%)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The 62 % loss is the thermodynamic floor dictated by the **Carnot limit**. **No mechanical-engineering trick** can push a single-cycle thermal plant above ~ 45 %; *combined-cycle* gas plants achieve ~ 60 %.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
