---

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**Q: Define clean energy and renewable energy. Differentiate between them with two examples.** [3 Marks]
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question as per 2024 Scheme]**

**Model Answer:**
- **Clean Energy**: Energy generated from sources/technologies that emit little to no GHGs or air pollutants during operation. Example: Solar PV, Nuclear.
- **Renewable Energy**: Energy from naturally replenishing sources whose availability is not depleted on a human timescale. Example: Solar, Wind, Hydro.
- **Differentiation**: (i) All renewables are operationally clean, but not all clean sources are renewable. Nuclear is clean but uses finite uranium. (ii) Renewables are characterized by continuous natural replenishment, whereas "clean" is a property based on emissions profile. Biomass is renewable but its combustion releases particulates, making it less "clean" by air-quality standards.

---

### Question A2
**Q: List the major greenhouse gases with their Global Warming Potential (GWP) values. State the current atmospheric CO₂ concentration.** [3 Marks]
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question as per 2024 Scheme]**

**Model Answer:**
Major GHGs and their **100-year GWP** (IPCC AR6 values):
- **Carbon Dioxide (CO₂)**: GWP = **1** (reference gas)
- **Methane (CH₄)**: GWP = **27.9** (recent updated value)
- **Nitrous Oxide (N₂O)**: GWP = **273**
- **Hydrofluorocarbons (HFCs)**: GWP range **12 – 14,800**
- **Sulphur Hexafluoride (SF₆)**: GWP = **25,200** (one of the most potent GHGs)
- **Perfluorocarbons (PFCs)**: GWP up to **12,200**

Current atmospheric CO₂ concentration: **~422 ppm** (2024 annual mean, NOAA Mauna Loa), up from the pre-industrial value of **~280 ppm** — a rise of **~50%**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

**Q: Discuss in detail the various drivers and need for transitioning to clean and renewable energy sources. Explain with reference to environmental, economic, energy security, and health aspects. Also compute the annual CO₂ emissions avoided by a 50 MW solar PV plant operating at 0.20 capacity factor, replacing an equivalent coal-based plant with emission factor 0.95 kg CO₂/kWh.** [14 Marks]
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question as per 2024 Scheme]**

#### Part (a) — Discussion of Drivers and Need (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

The transition from fossil fuels to clean and renewable energy is driven by **four interconnected pillars**:

**(1) Environmental Sustainability (Climate Change Mitigation) — 2 Marks**
Fossil fuel combustion releases CO₂, CH₄, and N₂O. Atmospheric CO₂ has risen from **~280 ppm** (pre-industrial 1750) to **~422 ppm** (2024). This has caused a radiative forcing of **~2.2 W/m²**, leading to a global mean surface temperature rise of **~1.2°C**. Climate change manifests as sea-level rise, glacier retreat, extreme weather events, ocean acidification, and biodiversity loss. Clean energy is essential to achieve the Paris Agreement goal of limiting warming to **1.5–2°C**.

**(2) Energy Security — 2 Marks**
Global oil and gas reserves are concentrated in a few geopolitically sensitive regions (Middle East, Russia). India imports **~85%** of its crude oil and **~50%** of its natural gas, making it vulnerable to price shocks (1973 OPEC crisis, 2008 oil price spike, 2022 Russia–Ukraine conflict) and supply disruptions. Domestic renewable energy (solar, wind, hydro) provides **indigenous, decentralized, and inexhaustible** energy, enhancing **strategic autonomy**.

**(3) Economic Viability — 1.5 Marks**
Driven by learning curves and economies of scale, the LCOE of solar PV has fallen by **~89%** between 2010 and 2023. Solar PV is now the **cheapest new electricity source** in most regions. The renewable sector also creates **~3× more jobs per MWh** than fossil fuels (IRENA, 2023).

**(4) Public Health — 1.5 Marks**
Air pollution from coal combustion causes **~7 million premature deaths annually** (WHO). Replacing coal with renewables avoids emissions of PM2.5, SO₂, NOx, and Mercury, providing **enormous health co-benefits** that often exceed the cost of decarbonization.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> Students often write a generic answer without **quantitative anchors** (ppm of CO₂, % of imports, LCOE drop). Always back each pillar with **at least one number** to score full marks. Bullet-pointed answers with specific values are preferred over verbose prose.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- Plant capacity: $P = 50 \text{ MW} = 50{,}000 \text{ kW}$
- Capacity factor: $CF = 0.20$
- Coal emission factor: $EF = 0.95 \text{ kg CO}_2 / \text{kWh}$
- Hours per year: $8760$

**Step 1: Compute annual energy output.**

$$
E_{annual} = P \times CF \times 8760 = 50{,}000 \times 0.20 \times 8760
$$

$$
E_{annual} = 10{,}000 \times 8760 = 8.76 \times 10^7 \text{ kWh/year} = 87.6 \text{ GWh/year}
$$

**Step 2: Compute annual CO₂ avoided.**

$$
CO_{2, avoided} = E_{annual} \times EF = 8.76 \times 10^7 \text{ kWh} \times 0.95 \text{ kg/kWh}
$$

$$
CO_{2, avoided} = 8.322 \times 10^7 \text{ kg/year} = 83{,}220 \text{ tonnes CO}_2/\text{year}
$$

**Step 3: Lifetime CO₂ avoided (assume 25-year life).**

$$
CO_{2, lifetime} = 83{,}220 \times 25 = 2.0805 \times 10^6 \text{ tonnes} = 2.08 \text{ million tonnes CO}_2
$$

**Step 4: Equivalence (for impact illustration).**

$$
N_{cars} = \dfrac{83{,}220 \text{ t/year}}{4.6 \text{ t/car/year}} \approx 18{,}091 \text{ passenger cars off the road annually}
$$

**Final Answer:** The 50 MW solar PV plant avoids **83,220 tonnes of CO₂ per year** and **2.08 million tonnes over its 25-year lifetime**, equivalent to removing **~18,000 cars** from the road annually.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes in Numerical Sub-parts:**
> - Forgetting to convert MW → kW (1 MW = 1,000 kW).
> - Multiplying by 365 (days) instead of 8,760 (hours) — always use **hours** for power-to-energy conversion.
> - Reporting CO₂ in kg without unit conversion to tonnes (1 tonne = 1,000 kg). Final answer should be in **tonnes CO₂/year** for readability.

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative to Question A) — 14 Marks

**Q: With neat sketches/schematics, explain the concept of Global Warming Potential (GWP), Carbon Footprint, and Carbon Intensity. Compute the CO₂-equivalent emissions of a process releasing 500 kg of CH₄ and 100 kg of N₂O. Also discuss the salient features of India's National Action Plan on Climate Change (NAPCC).** [14 Marks]
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question as per 2024 Scheme]**

#### Part (a) — Concept Explanation & Numerical (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Global Warming Potential (GWP):**
GWP is the cumulative radiative forcing of a GHG over a chosen time horizon (typically 100 years), relative to that of CO₂.

$$
GWP_i = \dfrac{\displaystyle\int_0^{TH} a_i \cdot C_i(t) \, dt}{\displaystyle\int_0^{TH} a_{CO_2} \cdot C_{CO_2}(t) \, dt}
$$

where $a_i$ is the radiative efficiency, $C_i(t)$ is the time-decaying atmospheric concentration profile, and $TH$ is the time horizon. GWP normalizes GHG impact to a common CO₂-equivalent unit.

**(ii) Carbon Footprint:**
The total GHG emissions (and removals) attributable to an entity, product, or activity, expressed as **kg CO₂e** using GWP weighting. Includes **Scope 1** (direct), **Scope 2** (purchased energy), and **Scope 3** (value chain) emissions per the GHG Protocol.

**(iii) Carbon Intensity:**
Emissions per unit of economic or energy output.

$$
CI_{electricity} = \dfrac{m_{CO_2}}{E_{gen}} \quad \text{[g CO}_2\text{/kWh]}
$$

$$
CI_{economy} = \dfrac{m_{CO_2}}{GDP} \quad \text{[g CO}_2\text{/USD]}
$$

**Numerical Computation:**

Given:
- $m_{CH_4} = 500$ kg, $GWP_{CH_4} = 28$
- $m_{N_2O} = 100$ kg, $GWP_{N_2O} = 273$

**Step 1: CO₂-equivalent of CH₄.**

$$
E_{CH_4} = 500 \times 28 = 14{,}000 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{e}
$$

**Step 2: CO₂-equivalent of N₂O.**

$$
E_{N_2O} = 100 \times 273 = 27{,}300 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{e}
$$

**Step 3: Total CO₂-equivalent emission.**

$$
E_{total} = 14{,}000 + 27{,}300 = 41{,}300 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{e} = 41.3 \text{ tonnes CO}_2\text{e}
$$

**Final Answer:** The process releases **41,300 kg CO₂e ≈ 41.3 tonnes CO₂e**, illustrating how small masses of high-GWP gases contribute substantially to warming.

> [!NOTE]
> The 100 kg of N₂O alone (27.3 t CO₂e) outweighs the 500 kg of CH₄ (14.0 t CO₂e) due to N₂O's much higher GWP.

#### Part (b) — India's NAPCC and Climate Commitments (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

India launched the **National Action Plan on Climate Change (NAPCC)** in **June 2008** under the PM Council on Climate Change. It outlines **eight national missions**:

| # | Mission | Focus Area |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **National Solar Mission (Jawaharlal Nehru NSM)** | 100 GW solar by 2022 (achieved ahead), 500 GW non-fossil by 2030 |
| 2 | **National Mission for Enhanced Energy Efficiency (NMEEE)** | PAT scheme for industries, Super-Efficient Equipment Program |
| 3 | **National Mission on Sustainable Habitat** | Green buildings, urban transport, waste management |
| 4 | **National Water Mission** | 20% increase in water-use efficiency |
| 5 | **National Mission for Sustaining the Himalayan Ecosystem** | Glaciers, biodiversity, forest cover |
| 6 | **National Mission for a Green India** | Afforestation of 6 Mha; carbon sink enhancement |
| 7 | **National Mission for Sustainable Agriculture** | Climate-resilient crops, precision farming |
| 8 | **National Mission on Strategic Knowledge for Climate Change** | Research, education, capacity building |

**Key Climate Commitments (Updated 2021–2024):**
- **Panchamrit** (5-point climate plan) announced at COP26 (Glasgow, 2021):
  1. **500 GW** of non-fossil installed capacity by 2030.
  2. **50%** of installed power capacity from non-fossil sources by 2030.
  3. **45% reduction in emissions intensity** of GDP by 2030 (from 2005 levels).
  4. **1 billion tonnes** of cumulative CO₂ reduction by 2030 (updated to 2.5 Gt COP28).
  5. **Net Zero** by **2070**.

- **Updated NDC (Nationally Determined Contribution)** submitted to UNFCCC in **August 2022** with these five targets.

- **LCA (Low Carbon) Lifestyle Campaign "LiFE"** launched at COP26.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:**
> Students often misstate targets (e.g., "Net Zero by 2050" for India — India's target is **2070**; only China and EU have 2050 targets). Always verify year and base year (2005 vs 2030 vs 2070). Also do not confuse India's **500 GW** (non-fossil **electricity capacity**) with **energy** capacity.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Energy Engineering Module 1:**

### A. Definitions (Memorize Exact Wording)
- **Clean Energy**: Low/no GHG and pollutant emissions during operation.
- **Renewable Energy**: Naturally replenished on human timescale.
- **GWP**: 100-yr radiative forcing of GHG relative to CO₂.
- **Carbon Footprint**: Total GHG emissions in CO₂e.
- **Carbon Intensity**: Emissions per unit energy/GDP.
- **LCOE**: Discounted lifetime cost ÷ Discounted lifetime energy.
- **Capacity Factor**: Actual energy / Maximum possible energy.
- **Energy Security**: Uninterrupted affordable energy supply.
- **EPBT**: Life-cycle energy input / Annual energy output.

### B. Key Numbers (Anchor for Numerical Problems)
- Atmospheric CO₂: **~280 ppm → ~422 ppm** (≈ +50%).
- Radiative forcing since 1750: **~2.2 W/m²**.
- Warming so far: **~1.2°C**.
- Coal lifecycle CO₂: **~1,000 g/kWh**; Solar PV: **~30–40 g/kWh**.
- Coal emission factor: **~0.95 kg CO₂/kWh**.
- Hours per year: **8,760**; Days per year: **365**; Years to seconds: 1 yr ≈ 31.54 million s.
- India's fossil import: **~85% oil, ~50% gas**.
- India's targets: **500 GW non-fossil by 2030; Net Zero 2070**.
- Paris Agreement: limit warming to **1.5–2°C**.
- WHO PM2.5 threshold: **5 µg/m³** annual; 15 µg/m³ daily.

### C. Key Institutions & Frameworks
- **UNFCCC** (1992) → **Kyoto Protocol** (1997) → **Paris Agreement** (2015).
- **IPCC** (Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change) — AR6 Synthesis Report (2023).
- **IRENA** — International Renewable Energy Agency.
- **IEA** — International Energy Agency.
- **GHG Protocol** — Scopes 1, 2, 3.
- **India's NAPCC (2008)** with **8 National Missions**.

### D. Major GHGs and GWP (100-yr, AR6)
- CO₂: **1**; CH₄: **27.9**; N₂O: **273**; SF₆: **25,200**.

### E. Common Pitfalls in KTU Valuation
- Forgetting unit conversion (MW ↔ kW, kg ↔ tonnes, ppm ↔ fraction).
- Using 365 instead of 8,760 for hours-per-year.
- Conflating "clean" and "renewable" — they are NOT synonyms.
- Mis-stating India's Net Zero year (it is 2070, not 2050).
- Omitting quantitative anchors in descriptive answers.

---

*End of Notes — Module 1: Need of Clean and Renewable Energy*
*Aligned to KTU 2024 Scheme | Outcome-Based Education | NEP 2020 Compliant*

topics.title: Conventional Energy Plants: Thermal
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT411 - ENERGY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Conventional Energy Plants: Thermal

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Thermal Power Plant (TPP)** — also called a **Steam Power Plant (SPP)** or **Conventional Energy Plant** — is an industrial facility that generates electrical energy through the controlled combustion of fossil fuels (coal, natural gas, oil, or nuclear fuel). The thermal energy released from combustion is converted into mechanical work via a working fluid (water-steam), and this mechanical work is then converted into electrical energy using an electromagnetic generator (alternator) coupled to a prime mover (steam turbine).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A thermal power plant converts the chemical energy stored in fossil fuels into electrical energy through a thermodynamic cycle, primarily the **Rankine Cycle**, with typical operational efficiencies ranging from **25% to 40%** for modern subcritical plants and up to **45%+** for supercritical/ultra-supercritical units.

The fundamental energy conversion chain in a thermal power plant follows this sequence:

$$\text{Chemical Energy} \rightarrow \text{Thermal Energy} \rightarrow \text{Mechanical Energy} \rightarrow \text{Electrical Energy}$$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Kettle That Lights Up a City"**
>
> Imagine boiling water in a massive industrial kettle. The steam that rushes out is directed onto the blades of a giant fan (turbine), causing it to spin. This spinning shaft is connected to a giant magnet inside coils of copper wire (generator), and as the magnet spins, electricity flows out of the wires. The used steam is cooled back into water using a huge radiator (condenser) and pumped back into the kettle (boiler) to repeat the cycle endlessly. That is essentially what a thermal power plant does — except the "kettle" is the size of a building, the fuel is coal, and the "spinning magnet" can power millions of homes.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value | Units |
|---|---|---|
| Calorific Value of Coal (Bituminous) | **24,000 – 30,000** | kJ/kg |
| Calorific Value of Natural Gas | **38,000 – 50,000** | kJ/kg |
| Rankine Cycle Efficiency (Subcritical) | **33 – 38** | % |
| Rankine Cycle Efficiency (Supercritical) | **42 – 45** | % |
| Specific Steam Consumption | **3.5 – 6.0** | kg/kWh |
| Auxiliary Power Consumption | **6 – 10** | % |
| Cooling Tower Efficiency | **60 – 75** | % |
| Plant Load Factor (PLF) | **60 – 80** | % |
| Atmospheric Pressure | **101.325** | kPa |
| Mechanical Equivalent of Heat | **1 kcal = 4.186 kJ** | — |

### 1.4 Significance in KTU 2024 Scheme

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 1 Highlight:** This topic constitutes the **foundation stone** of Energy Engineering. KTU examiners frequently test the **layout of thermal power plants**, the **Rankine cycle analysis**, and the **functions of individual components** like boilers, economizers, air preheaters, and cooling towers. Approximately **15-20%** of the End Semester Examination (ESE) for PECHT411 originates from this topic.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle — The Rankine Cycle

The **Rankine Cycle** is the idealized thermodynamic cycle that forms the theoretical backbone of all steam-based thermal power plants. It operates on a closed loop with water as the working fluid undergoing phase changes (liquid → vapor → liquid).

### 2.1.1 The Four Ideal Processes

The Rankine cycle consists of **four essential processes** executed sequentially:

1. **Process 4→1: Isentropic Compression (Pump Work)**
   - Liquid water at low pressure is compressed to high boiler pressure.
   - Work is supplied *into* the system.

2. **Process 1→2: Isobaric Heat Addition (Boiler)**
   - High-pressure liquid is heated at constant pressure until it becomes superheated steam.
   - Heat $Q_{in}$ is added *from* the hot combustion gases.

3. **Process 2→3: Isentropic Expansion (Turbine Work)**
   - High-energy steam expands through the turbine, producing shaft work.
   - Work is extracted *out of* the system.

4. **Process 3→4: Isobaric Heat Rejection (Condenser)**
   - Low-pressure exhaust steam is condensed back into saturated liquid.
   - Heat $Q_{out}$ is rejected *to* the cooling medium.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Isentropic?** In the *ideal* Rankine cycle, the pump and turbine are assumed to operate without any irreversibilities (100% isentropic efficiency). Real plants use **isentropic efficiency** corrections to account for actual losses.

### 2.2 Major Components of a Thermal Power Plant

A modern thermal power plant is composed of **four functional sections**:

#### 2.2.1 Coal Handling Plant
- **Function:** Unloading, storage, crushing, and transportation of coal from the receiving yard to the bunker.
- **Key Equipment:** Wagon tipplers, crushers, belt conveyors, magnetic separators, coal bunkers.

#### 2.2.2 Ash Handling Plant
- **Function:** Collection, transport, and disposal of ash produced after coal combustion.
- **Methods:** Hydraulic (wet), Pneumatic (dry), Mechanical.
- **Ash Types:** Fly ash (carried by flue gases — ~80%) and Bottom ash (falls to furnace bottom — ~20%).

#### 2.2.3 Steam Generation & Turbine Section
- **Boiler:** Converts water into high-pressure superheated steam using combustion heat.
- **Superheater:** Raises steam temperature above saturation point to improve cycle efficiency.
- **Economizer:** Recovers waste heat from flue gases to preheat the feedwater.
- **Air Preheater (APH):** Recovers heat from flue gases to preheat combustion air.
- **Steam Turbine:** Converts thermal energy of steam into mechanical rotational energy.
- **Condenser:** Condenses low-pressure exhaust steam back to water at low pressure.

#### 2.2.4 Electrical Section
- **Alternator (Generator):** Converts mechanical energy to electrical energy.
- **Transformer:** Steps up voltage for transmission.
- **Switchgear:** Controls and protects the electrical circuits.

## 2.3 Cycle Improvements & Modifications

### 2.3.1 Reheat Cycle
Steam is expanded partially in a high-pressure turbine, then sent back to the boiler for reheating, and finally expanded in a low-pressure turbine. This reduces moisture content in the later stages and improves efficiency.

### 2.3.2 Regenerative Cycle
A portion of the extracted steam from intermediate turbine stages is used to preheat the feedwater in **Feedwater Heaters (FWH)**. This reduces the fuel requirement in the boiler.

### 2.3.3 Supercritical Cycle
Operating above the critical point of water (**22.06 MPa, 374.15°C**) eliminates the phase change and allows higher cycle efficiencies.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas — they appear in nearly every KTU numerical!**

| Formula Description | Equation | Units |
|---|---|---|
| Net Work Output | $W_{net} = W_{turbine} - W_{pump}$ | kJ/kg |
| Turbine Work (per kg) | $W_T = h_2 - h_3$ | kJ/kg |
| Pump Work (per kg) | $W_P = h_1 - h_4$ | kJ/kg |
| Heat Supplied (Boiler) | $Q_{in} = h_2 - h_1$ | kJ/kg |
| Heat Rejected (Condenser) | $Q_{out} = h_3 - h_4$ | kJ/kg |
| Thermal Efficiency (Rankine) | $\eta_{th} = \dfrac{W_{net}}{Q_{in}} = \dfrac{(h_2 - h_3) - (h_1 - h_4)}{h_2 - h_1}$ | dimensionless |
| Power Output | $P = \eta_{th} \times \dot{m}_f \times \text{CV}_f$ | kW or MW |
| Steam Rate | $SR = \dfrac{3600}{W_{net}}$ | kg/kWh |
| Fuel Consumption | $m_f = \dfrac{P}{\eta_{overall} \times \text{CV}_f}$ | kg/s |
| Isentropic Enthalpy (Real) | $h_{actual} = h_3 + \eta_T (h_2 - h_3)$ | kJ/kg |
| Specific Steam Consumption (SSC) | $SSC = \dfrac{3600}{W_{net}}$ | kg/kWh |
| Heat Rate | $HR = \dfrac{3600}{\eta_{overall}}$ | kJ/kWh |
| Plant Heat Rate | $PHR = \dfrac{HR}{\eta_{generator}}$ | kJ/kWh |
| Condenser Cooling Water | $m_c = \dfrac{Q_{out}}{c_p (T_{out} - T_{in})}$ | kg/s |
| Effectiveness (APH) | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{T_{air,out} - T_{air,in}}{T_{gas,in} - T_{air,in}}$ | dimensionless |
| Chimney Draft (Natural) | $\Delta p = H \cdot g \cdot (\rho_{air} - \rho_{gas})$ | Pa |
| Plant Load Factor | $PLF = \dfrac{\text{Energy Generated (kWh)}}{\text{Plant Capacity (kW)} \times \text{Hours}}$ | dimensionless |
| Capacity Factor | $CF = \dfrac{\text{Average Load}}{\text{Rated Capacity}}$ | dimensionless |
| Auxiliary Consumption | $A = \dfrac{\text{Auxiliary Power (kW)}}{\text{Gross Generation (kW)}} \times 100$ | % |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Thermal power plants currently supply **approximately 60% of the world's electricity** (as of 2024). In India, coal-based thermal generation accounts for **over 70%** of installed capacity, making mastery of this topic essential for careers in NTPC, BHEL, Tata Power, Adani Power, Reliance Energy, and state electricity boards. The design of these plants involves chemical, mechanical, electrical, and civil engineers working in integrated teams.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Rankine Cycle Thermal Efficiency

We begin from the **First Law of Thermodynamics for a cyclic process**:

$$\oint dQ = \oint dW$$

Since the working fluid returns to its initial state after one complete cycle, the net change in internal energy is zero.

**Step 1: Express the net work output**

The turbine produces work and the pump consumes work:

$$W_{net} = W_{T} - W_{P}$$

**Step 2: Express heat input and heat rejection**

Heat is added in the boiler at constant pressure:

$$Q_{in} = h_2 - h_1$$

Heat is rejected in the condenser at constant pressure:

$$Q_{out} = h_3 - h_4$$

**Step 3: Apply the energy balance**

From the First Law, since $Q_{in} - Q_{out} = W_{net}$:

$$(h_2 - h_1) - (h_3 - h_4) = (h_2 - h_3) - (h_1 - h_4)$$

**Step 4: Define thermal efficiency**

Thermal efficiency is the ratio of useful work output to heat input:

$$\eta_{th} = \frac{W_{net}}{Q_{in}}$$

**Step 5: Substitute to obtain the final Rankine efficiency expression**

$$\eta_{th} = \frac{(h_2 - h_3) - (h_1 - h_4)}{h_2 - h_1}$$

This can be simplified by recognizing that $h_1 - h_4 \ll h_2 - h_3$ (pump work is typically **1-3%** of turbine work), but should not be neglected in precise calculations.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Specific Steam Consumption (SSC)

The Specific Steam Consumption represents the mass of steam required to generate **1 kWh** of electrical energy.

**Step 1: Energy in 1 kWh**

$$1 \text{ kWh} = 1 \text{ kW} \times 1 \text{ h} = 1 \text{ kW} \times 3600 \text{ s} = 3600 \text{ kJ}$$

**Step 2: Net work per kg of steam**

$$W_{net} = (h_2 - h_3) - (h_1 - h_4) \quad \text{[kJ/kg]}$$

**Step 3: Mass of steam for 1 kWh output**

$$m_{steam} = \frac{3600 \text{ kJ}}{W_{net} \text{ kJ/kg}}$$

**Step 4: Define Specific Steam Consumption**

$$\boxed{SSC = \frac{3600}{W_{net}} \quad \text{[kg/kWh]}}$$

**Verification (Sanity Check):** If $W_{net} = 1200$ kJ/kg, then $SSC = 3600/1200 = 3.0$ kg/kWh. This is realistic for a subcritical plant.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Fuel Consumption Rate

**Step 1: Overall plant efficiency**

The overall plant efficiency includes boiler efficiency, turbine efficiency, generator efficiency, and auxiliary losses:

$$\eta_{overall} = \eta_{boiler} \times \eta_{turbine} \times \eta_{generator} \times (1 - f_{aux})$$

**Step 2: Energy required per second for power output P**

$$\dot{E}_{out} = P \quad \text{[kW] = [kJ/s]}$$

**Step 3: Energy input from fuel per second**

$$\dot{E}_{in} = \frac{P}{\eta_{overall}}$$

**Step 4: Fuel mass flow rate**

$$m_f = \frac{P}{\eta_{overall} \times CV_f}$$

**Step 5: Numerical Example**

Consider a **100 MW** plant with $\eta_{overall} = 35\%$ and coal calorific value $CV = 25{,}000$ kJ/kg:

$$m_f = \frac{100 \times 10^3 \text{ kJ/s}}{0.35 \times 25{,}000 \text{ kJ/kg}}$$

$$m_f = \frac{100{,}000}{8{,}750}$$

$$m_f = 11.43 \text{ kg/s} \approx 41{,}140 \text{ kg/h} \approx 41.14 \text{ tonnes/hour}$$

**Step 6: Daily coal requirement**

$$m_{daily} = 41.14 \times 24 = 987.4 \text{ tonnes/day}$$

**Step 7: Annual coal requirement (assuming 75% PLF)**

$$m_{annual} = 987.4 \times 365 \times 0.75 = 270{,}255 \text{ tonnes/year}$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Reheat Cycle Efficiency

**Step 1: Define the reheat states**

The reheat cycle has **two turbine stages** and **two heat addition processes**.

**Step 2: Total turbine work**

$$W_{T,total} = (h_2 - h_{2'}) + (h_{3'} - h_3)$$

where $h_{2'}$ is the enthalpy at HP turbine exit, and $h_{3'}$ is the enthalpy after reheat.

**Step 3: Total heat input**

$$Q_{in,total} = (h_2 - h_1) + (h_{3'} - h_{2'})$$

**Step 4: Net work and efficiency**

$$W_{net} = W_{T,total} - W_P$$

$$\eta_{reheat} = \frac{W_{T,total} - W_P}{(h_2 - h_1) + (h_{3'} - h_{2'})}$$

## 3.5 Comprehensive Numerical Solved Example

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard KTU 14-Mark Problem Template**

**Problem:** A thermal power plant operates on the Rankine cycle. Steam enters the turbine at $P_2 = 10$ MPa and $T_2 = 500°C$. The condenser pressure is $P_4 = 10$ kPa. The mass flow rate of steam is $\dot{m} = 50$ kg/s. Calculate: (a) Thermal efficiency, (b) Power output, (c) Heat rejected in condenser, (d) Specific steam consumption.

**Steam Table Data (from standard tables):**

| State | Pressure (MPa) | Temperature (°C) | Enthalpy (kJ/kg) | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| State 1 | 10 | — | $h_1 = h_4 + v_4 \Delta p$ | Pump exit |
| State 2 | 10 | 500 | $h_2 = 3375.1$ | Superheated steam |
| State 3 | 0.01 | — | $h_3 = 191.81$ | Saturated liquid |

**Solution Step-by-Step:**

**Part (a):** At $P_4 = 10$ kPa (saturated):
- $h_4 = h_f = 191.81$ kJ/kg
- $v_4 = v_f = 0.001010$ m³/kg
- $h_3 = h_g = 2583.9$ kJ/kg (isentropic expansion to condenser pressure)

**Pump Work:**
$$W_P = v_4 (P_1 - P_4) = 0.001010 \times (10{,}000 - 10) \text{ kPa}$$

$$W_P = 0.001010 \times 9990 = 10.09 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Enthalpy at pump exit:**
$$h_1 = h_4 + W_P = 191.81 + 10.09 = 201.90 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Net Work Output:**
$$W_{net} = (h_2 - h_3) - W_P = (3375.1 - 2583.9) - 10.09$$

$$W_{net} = 791.2 - 10.09 = 781.11 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Heat Input:**
$$Q_{in} = h_2 - h_1 = 3375.1 - 201.90 = 3173.2 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Thermal Efficiency:**
$$\eta_{th} = \frac{781.11}{3173.2} = 0.2462 = 24.62\%$$

**Part (b):** Power Output:
$$P = \dot{m} \times W_{net} = 50 \times 781.11 = 39{,}055.5 \text{ kW} \approx 39.06 \text{ MW}$$

**Part (c):** Heat Rejected in Condenser:
$$Q_{out} = h_3 - h_4 = 2583.9 - 191.81 = 2392.09 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

Total heat rejected per second:
$$\dot{Q}_{out} = 50 \times 2392.09 = 119{,}604.5 \text{ kW}$$

**Part (d):** Specific Steam Consumption:
$$SSC = \frac{3600}{W_{net}} = \frac{3600}{781.11} = 4.61 \text{ kg/kWh}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Marking Scheme Note:** Always show steam table lookups explicitly. Even if numerical values are given in the problem, examiners reward **neat tabular presentation** of enthalpy values. Use the **Mollier diagram (h-s chart)** as an alternative graphical method if tables are not permitted.

## 4.3 Rankine Cycle T-s Diagram Representation

The **Temperature-Entropy (T-s) diagram** is the standard visualization tool for the Rankine cycle. Although Mermaid cannot render thermodynamic curves directly, the **block flow architecture** below shows the four key state points and their relationships.

    P3 -->|ISENTROPIC EXPANSION TURBINE| P4[STATE 3 WET STEAM LOW P]

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1: [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**"List the main components of a thermal power plant and state the function of an economizer."**

**Model Answer:**

A thermal power plant consists of the following main components:
1. **Coal handling plant** — receives, stores, and transports coal
2. **Ash handling plant** — collects and disposes ash
3. **Boiler** — converts water into steam using combustion heat
4. **Superheater** — raises steam temperature above saturation
5. **Air preheater** — preheats combustion air using flue gas heat
6. **Economizer** — preheats feedwater using flue gas heat
7. **Steam turbine** — converts steam energy into mechanical work
8. **Condenser** — condenses exhaust steam back to water
9. **Alternator** — converts mechanical work into electricity
10. **Cooling tower** — cools condenser water for reuse

**Function of Economizer:** An economizer is a heat exchanger that recovers waste heat from the **flue gases** leaving the boiler and uses it to preheat the **feedwater** before it enters the boiler drum. This reduces fuel consumption, improves boiler efficiency by **3-5%**, and lowers the temperature of flue gases before they enter the air preheater.

---

### Question A2: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**"Define the Rankine cycle. Why is reheating used in steam power plants?"**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** The Rankine cycle is an idealized thermodynamic cycle that describes the operation of steam power plants. It consists of four reversible processes: isentropic compression in a pump, isobaric heat addition in a boiler, isentropic expansion in a turbine, and isobaric heat rejection in a condenser.

**Reasons for Reheating:**
1. To **reduce moisture content** in the low-pressure turbine stages (moisture content above 12% causes blade erosion)
2. To **increase thermal efficiency** of the cycle
3. To **increase net work output** per kg of steam
4. To **allow higher boiler pressures** without excessive moisture in final stages
5. To **improve exhaust conditions** for the condenser

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Draw the layout of a modern coal-based thermal power plant and explain the function of any **four** major components. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A steam power plant operates on the Rankine cycle with steam entering the turbine at 8 MPa and 500°C. The condenser pressure is 8 kPa. Determine: **(i)** Thermal efficiency of the cycle, **(ii)** Work output per kg of steam, **(iii)** Heat input per kg of steam, **(iv)** Heat rejected per kg of steam. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution for Part (a):**

The layout diagram (as shown in Section 4.1) should be redrawn neatly with proper labeling. Functions of the four major components:

**1. Boiler:**
The boiler is the heart of the thermal power plant. It converts water into high-pressure, high-temperature superheated steam using the heat released from coal combustion. The boiler consists of a furnace, water walls, superheater, reheater, and economizer.

**2. Economizer:**
Recovers residual heat from the flue gases (exiting the boiler) to preheat the boiler feedwater. This reduces fuel consumption and improves thermal efficiency.

**3. Condenser:**
Condenses the low-pressure exhaust steam from the turbine back into liquid water using cooling water. It maintains a low back pressure on the turbine, maximizing work output.

**4. Cooling Tower:**
Cools the heated condenser cooling water by evaporative cooling, enabling the cooling water to be recirculated back to the condenser in a closed loop, minimizing water consumption.

**Model Solution for Part (b):**

**Given Data:**
- Boiler pressure: $P_1 = P_2 = 8$ MPa
- Boiler temperature: $T_2 = 500°C$
- Condenser pressure: $P_3 = P_4 = 8$ kPa

**Steam Table Values:**

| State | Property | Value |
|---|---|---|
| State 2 | $h_2$ at 8 MPa, 500°C | 3399.5 kJ/kg |
| State 4 | $h_f$ at 8 kPa | 173.88 kJ/kg |
| State 4 | $v_f$ at 8 kPa | 0.001008 m³/kg |
| State 3 | $h_g$ at 8 kPa | 2577.0 kJ/kg |

**Calculation of Pump Work:**

$$W_P = v_4 (P_1 - P_4) = 0.001008 \times (8000 - 8) = 8.06 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Enthalpy at Pump Exit:**

$$h_1 = h_4 + W_P = 173.88 + 8.06 = 181.94 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**(i) Heat Input:**

$$Q_{in} = h_2 - h_1 = 3399.5 - 181.94 = 3217.56 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**(ii) Net Work Output:**

$$W_{net} = (h_2 - h_3) - W_P = (3399.5 - 2577.0) - 8.06 = 814.44 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**(iii) Thermal Efficiency:**

$$\eta_{th} = \frac{W_{net}}{Q_{in}} = \frac{814.44}{3217.56} = 0.2532 = 25.32\%$$

**(iv) Heat Rejected:**

$$Q_{out} = h_3 - h_4 = 2577.0 - 173.88 = 2403.12 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Verification:** $\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{Q_{out}}{Q_{in}} = 1 - \frac{2403.12}{3217.56} = 0.2532$ ✓

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the working principle of a **condenser** in a thermal power plant. Compare surface and jet condensers. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A 100 MW thermal power plant uses coal with calorific value 25,000 kJ/kg. The overall efficiency is 32%. Calculate: **(i)** Coal consumption per hour, **(ii)** Specific coal consumption, **(iii)** Heat rejected per hour. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution for Part (a):**

**Working Principle of a Condenser:**

A condenser is a heat exchanger that condenses the exhaust steam from the turbine into liquid water. The exhaust steam enters the shell side, and cold cooling water flows through tubes. Heat transfers from steam to cooling water through tube walls, causing the steam to condense.

The condensate is collected in the hotwell and pumped back to the boiler through feedwater heaters. Cooling water is circulated through a cooling tower to reject the absorbed heat to the atmosphere.

**Comparison of Surface vs. Jet Condensers:**

| Parameter | Surface Condenser | Jet Condenser |
|---|---|---|
| Cooling water mixing | No mixing | Direct mixing |
| Purity of condensate | Pure, can be reused | Mixed with cooling water |
| Cooling water requirement | High | Low |
| Pumping cost | Higher | Lower |
| Maintenance cost | Higher | Lower |
| Application | Large power plants | Small installations |
| Construction | More complex | Simpler |

**Model Solution for Part (b):**

**Given:**
- Power output: $P = 100 \text{ MW} = 100{,}000 \text{ kJ/s}$
- Calorific value: $CV = 25{,}000 \text{ kJ/kg}$
- Overall efficiency: $\eta_{overall} = 0.32$

**(i) Coal Consumption per Hour:**

$$\dot{m}_f = \frac{P}{\eta_{overall} \times CV} = \frac{100{,}000}{0.32 \times 25{,}000}$$

$$\dot{m}_f = \frac{100{,}000}{8{,}000} = 12.5 \text{ kg/s}$$

Converting to per hour:
$$m_f = 12.5 \times 3600 = 45{,}000 \text{ kg/h} = 45 \text{ tonnes/hour}$$

**(ii) Specific Coal Consumption:**

$$SCC = \frac{3600}{\eta_{overall} \times CV} = \frac{3600}{0.32 \times 25{,}000}$$

$$SCC = \frac{3600}{8{,}000} = 0.45 \text{ kg/kWh}$$

**(iii) Heat Rejected per Hour:**

$$\dot{Q}_{out} = \dot{Q}_{in} - P_{output}$$

$$\dot{Q}_{in} = 12.5 \times 25{,}000 = 312{,}500 \text{ kJ/s}$$

$$\dot{Q}_{out} = 312{,}500 - 100{,}000 = 212{,}500 \text{ kJ/s}$$

Per hour:
$$Q_{out} = 212{,}500 \times 3600 = 7.65 \times 10^8 \text{ kJ/h}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert MW to kW or kJ/s** — Always check unit consistency. 1 MW = 1000 kW = 1000 kJ/s.
> 2. **Omitting pump work** — Even though it's small (~1%), KTU examiners allocate **1-2 marks** specifically for pump work calculation.
> 3. **Using wrong steam table values** — Always state the **pressure and temperature** for which you're looking up enthalpies.
> 4. **Not showing the Mollier chart (h-s) approach** — Alternative graphical solutions earn full marks if done correctly.
> 5. **Confusing specific steam consumption (SSC) with specific fuel consumption (SFC)** — SSC is in kg of steam/kWh; SFC is in kg of fuel/kWh.
> 6. **Skipping units in the final answer** — KTU mandates unit inclusion in **every numerical answer**.
> 7. **Using `|` inside markdown tables** — Always use `$\vert$` or `$\mid$` for absolute values or modulus in LaTeX-formatted tables.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist — KTU Module 1 Thermal Power Plants**

### 🔑 Must-Know Definitions
- Thermal Power Plant — Converts chemical → thermal → mechanical → electrical energy
- Rankine Cycle — The fundamental ideal cycle for steam power plants (4 processes)
- Carnot Efficiency — Maximum theoretical efficiency between two reservoirs
- Plant Load Factor (PLF) — Actual energy generated / Maximum possible energy
- Specific Steam Consumption (SSC) — kg of steam required per kWh
- Specific Fuel Consumption (SFC) — kg of fuel required per kWh
- Heat Rate — Energy input per unit electrical output (kJ/kWh)

### 🏭 Component Functions (Rapid Recall)
- **Economizer** → Recovers flue gas heat to preheat feedwater
- **Superheater** → Raises steam temperature above saturation
- **Reheater** → Resuperheats steam after partial expansion
- **Air Preheater** → Recovers flue gas heat to preheat combustion air
- **Condenser** → Condenses exhaust steam; maintains low turbine back pressure
- **Cooling Tower** → Cools heated condenser water for recirculation
- **ESP** → Removes fly ash from flue gases (electrostatic)
- **Feedwater Heater** → Uses extracted steam to preheat condensate

### 🔢 Master Formulas (Carry these to the exam hall)
- $\eta_{th} = \dfrac{W_T - W_P}{Q_{in}} = \dfrac{(h_2 - h_3) - (h_1 - h_4)}{h_2 - h_1}$
- $W_P = v_4 (P_1 - P_4)$
- $SSC = \dfrac{3600}{W_{net}}$ kg/kWh
- $SFC = \dfrac{3600}{\eta_{overall} \times CV}$ kg/kWh
- $\dot{m}_f = \dfrac{P}{\eta_{overall} \times CV}$ kg/s
- $PLF = \dfrac{\text{Actual kWh}}{\text{Rated kW} \times \text{Hours}}$

### 🌡️ Critical Steam State Points
- **State 1** — Compressed liquid at pump exit (high P, low T)
- **State 2** — Superheated steam at turbine inlet (high P, very high T)
- **State 3** — Wet steam at turbine exit (low P, moderate T)
- **State 4** — Saturated liquid at condenser exit (low P, low T)

### 💡 Quick Engineering Insights
- **Increasing boiler pressure** raises efficiency but increases moisture at LP turbine exit
- **Increasing turbine inlet temperature** raises efficiency and reduces moisture
- **Reheating** reduces LP moisture and increases efficiency
- **Regeneration** uses extracted steam to preheat feedwater, reducing irreversibility
- **Supercritical plants** operate above 22.06 MPa for highest efficiencies

### 📊 Typical Numerical Values for Sanity Checks
- $\eta_{Rankine, subcritical} \approx 30-38\%$
- $\eta_{Rankine, supercritical} \approx 42-45\%$
- $SSC \approx 3-6$ kg/kWh
- $SFC \approx 0.4-0.7$ kg/kWh (coal)
- Pump work is **1-3%** of turbine work
- Condenser pressure is **typically 4-10 kPa**

### 🎯 Common KTU Examiner Traps
1. Forgetting that $h_4$ is at the **condenser pressure**, not boiler pressure
2. Using $h_g$ instead of finding actual $h_3$ via isentropic relations
3. Neglecting the small but significant pump work
4. Unit mismatches between kW, MW, kJ/s
5. Misinterpreting "isothermal" or "isobaric" as "isentropic"

> **END OF MODULE 1 — THERMAL POWER PLANT NOTES**

# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Hydel (Hydroelectric) Power Plant — Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Verbatim):**
> A **Hydel Power Plant** (also called a *Hydroelectric Power Plant*) is an installation that converts the **potential energy of water stored at a height** (hydraulic head) into **electrical energy** by directing the water through hydraulic turbines coupled to electrical generators.

**Energy Conversion Chain:**

$$\text{Stored Hydraulic Energy} \longrightarrow \text{Kinetic Energy of Water} \longrightarrow \text{Mechanical (Shaft) Energy} \longrightarrow \text{Electrical Energy}$$

The governing relationship is simply **E = mgH** at the input side and **E = ½mv²** at the turbine runner blades, with the velocity head being reconverted from the gross head.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Metric:** A modern large-scale hydel station has an overall conversion efficiency of approximately **85%–90%**, the highest of any conventional power generation method (thermal ≈ 35–42%, nuclear ≈ 30–38%).

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Tower Tap" Model

Imagine a **giant overhead water tank** on top of a building. When you open the tap at the bottom, water gushes out with high speed. That rushing water is the same thing that turns the blades of a turbine in a hydel plant.

- The **height of the water tank above the tap** = **Head (H)** in a hydel plant.
- The **amount of water flowing per second** = **Discharge (Q)** in the plant.
- The **tap and pipe** = the **Penstock** (high-pressure water conduit).
- The **rotating tap wheel** = the **Turbine** (Pelton, Francis, or Kaplan).
- The **electric bulb connected to the wheel** = the **Generator**.

If you make the tank **taller** (more head) or **wider** (more discharge), you get more power — exactly the two physical parameters that determine the output of a hydel plant.

---

## 1.2 Nuclear Power Plant — Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Verbatim):**
> A **Nuclear Power Plant** is a thermal power station in which a **nuclear reactor** acts as the heat source, generating thermal energy through **controlled nuclear fission** (or, in the future, fusion) of heavy fissile isotopes, primarily **Uranium-235 (²³⁵U)** or **Plutonium-239 (²³⁹Pu)**. The heat so produced converts water into high-pressure steam, which drives a turbo-alternator to produce electricity.

**Energy Conversion Chain:**

$$\text{Nuclear Binding Energy} \longrightarrow \text{Thermal Energy} \longrightarrow \text{Steam Energy} \longrightarrow \text{Mechanical Energy} \longrightarrow \text{Electrical Energy}$$

The foundational mass-energy equivalence of Einstein governs the source:

$$E = m c^{2}$$

where **c = 3 × 10⁸ m/s** is the speed of light in vacuum. Even a tiny mass loss yields enormous energy — a single kg of **²³⁵U** completely fissioned releases about **8.2 × 10¹³ J**, equivalent to burning roughly **2,500 tonnes of coal**.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Campfire Made of Atoms" Model

Think of an ordinary **wood fire** that you use to boil water.

- The **wood** that burns = **Uranium fuel rods** in a nuclear reactor.
- The **fire/heat** produced = **fission chain reaction** releasing binding energy.
- The **vessel of water above the fire** = the **Reactor Coolant Loop / Steam Generator**.
- The **steam that comes off the boiling water** = **high-pressure steam** going to the turbine.
- The **steam-powered engine wheel** = the **Steam Turbine + Generator**.

The only fundamental difference is that wood releases energy through *chemical* rearrangement of electrons, whereas uranium releases energy through *nuclear* rearrangement of the nucleus itself — and a single kilogram of uranium produces **~1,000,000 times** more energy than a kilogram of wood.

> [!TIP]
> **Why two plants in one topic?** KTU pairs them in Module 1 because both are classified as **non-conventional-or-large-baseload** sources of energy, and the contrast helps the student internalize the *type of primary energy* (gravitational potential vs. nuclear binding energy) before moving to thermal and renewable modules.

---

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Hydel Power Plants

The classification is **based on head** (height of water available), because head dictates the **type of turbine** and the **structural design** of the civil works.

### 2.1.1 By Available Head

> [!NOTE]
> **1. Low-Head Plant (Head < 30 m):** Uses **Kaplan** or **Propeller** turbines. The dam is small; the water volume (Q) is large. Example: **Sardar Sarovar (Gujarat)**.
>
> **2. Medium-Head Plant (30 m < Head < 300 m):** Uses **Francis** (reaction) turbines. Most common range in India. Example: **Nagarjuna Sagar, Sharavathy**.
>
> **3. High-Head Plant (Head > 300 m):** Uses **Pelton** (impulse) turbines. Involves long penstocks on hillsides. Example: **Idukki (Kerala, 169 m), Nathpa Jhakri (HP), Bhakra Nangal**.

### 2.1.2 By Storage / Load Function

| Type | Function | Storage | Example |
|------|----------|---------|---------|
| **Base Load Plant** | Continuous supply | Large reservoir | Bhakra |
| **Peak Load Plant** | Only during high demand hours | Small / pumped storage | Pumped storage at Kadamparai (Tamil Nadu) |
| **Run-of-River Plant** | Uses natural flow continuously | No/very little storage | Small hydro on Western Ghats streams |

### 2.1.3 By Plant Capacity (KTU Standard)

- **Micro Hydro:** Up to **100 kW**
- **Mini Hydro:** 101 kW – **1 MW**
- **Small Hydro:** 1 MW – **25 MW**
- **Large Hydro:** > **25 MW**

---

## 2.2 Schematic Components of a Hydel Plant

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Six Mandatory Civil/Mech Components** (in order of water flow):

1. **Intake / Diversion Structure** — collects water from the river/reservoir and prevents debris.
2. **Forebay** — small balancing reservoir at the start of the penstock, removes residual silt and supplies uniform water.
3. **Penstock** — large-diameter high-pressure steel pipe that carries water from forebay to the turbine.
4. **Hydraulic Turbine** — Pelton (impulse) / Francis (mixed flow) / Kaplan (axial flow) — converts hydraulic energy → mechanical rotation.
5. **Tailrace** — channel that carries away the discharged water from the turbine back to the river.
6. **Powerhouse** — civil structure housing turbine, generator, control room, switchyard.

**Auxiliary:** Spillway, Trash rack, Surge tank, Draft tube (for reaction turbines), Governor (speed control).

---

## 2.3 Hydel Power Formula Sheet (KTU 2024 — High-Yield)

> [!NOTE]
> **The single most important formula for KTU:**

**Hydel Power Available at Turbine Inlet:**

$$P_{hydraulic} = \rho \, g \, Q \, H \quad \text{(watts)}$$

Where:
- $\rho$ = density of water = **1000 kg/m³**
- $g$ = acceleration due to gravity = **9.81 m/s²**
- $Q$ = volume flow rate (m³/s)
- $H$ = net head available (m)

**Electrical Power Output:**

$$P_{electrical} = \eta_{overall} \cdot \rho \, g \, Q \, H$$

With $\eta_{overall} = \eta_{turbine} \times \eta_{generator} \times \eta_{transformer} \approx 0.85$

> [!TIP]
> **Trick to Remember:** $9.81 \times 1000 = 9810 \approx 10{,}000$. So in KTU numericals, you can quickly write:

$$P \text{ (kW)} \approx 9.81 \cdot Q \cdot H \cdot \eta_{overall}$$

### Extended KTU Formula Cheat-Sheet (Markdown Table)

| # | Quantity | Symbol | Formula | Typical Unit | Notes |
|---|----------|--------|---------|--------------|-------|
| 1 | Hydraulic power | $P_h$ | $\rho g Q H$ | W (or kW) | Input to turbine |
| 2 | Electrical power | $P_e$ | $\eta_o \rho g Q H$ | W (or kW) | Output of generator |
| 3 | Net head | $H_{net}$ | $H_{gross} - h_f$ | m | Subtracts pipe friction $h_f$ |
| 4 | Energy generated in time T | $E$ | $P_e \times T$ | kWh | $T$ in hours |
| 5 | Plant capacity | $P_{cap}$ | $\eta_o \rho g Q_{design} H_{design}$ | kW | Maximum rating |
| 6 | Plant factor (PF) | $PF$ | $\dfrac{E_{actual}}{P_{cap} \times T}$ | dimensionless | Always < 1 |
| 7 | Load factor (LF) | $LF$ | $\dfrac{Average\ Load}{Peak\ Load}$ | dimensionless | May exceed 1 for pumped storage |
| 8 | Capacity factor (CF) | $CF$ | $\dfrac{Average\ Load}{Installed\ Capacity}$ | dimensionless | Annual reporting |
| 9 | Utilization factor | $UF$ | $\dfrac{P_{cap} \times T_{use}}{P_{cap} \times 8760}$ | dimensionless | Ratio of hours of use |
| 10 | Specific speed | $N_s$ | $\dfrac{N \sqrt{P}}{H^{5/4}}$ | rpm·m·kW⁻⁰·⁵ | Selects turbine type |
| 11 | Velocity of jet (Pelton) | $V_j$ | $\sqrt{2gH}$ | m/s | Theoretical; reduced by $C_v$ |
| 12 | Discharge through nozzle | $Q$ | $C_d A \sqrt{2gH}$ | m³/s | $C_d \approx 0.95$–$0.98$ |

### Real-World Engineering Utility of $P = \rho g Q H$

- Used by **CEA (Central Electricity Authority, India)** for estimating hydroelectric potential of river basins.
- Used by **MNRE** for tender evaluations of small hydro projects (< 25 MW).
- Used by **KSPCL (Kerala State Electricity Board)** for rating Idukki Stage-I and II.

---

## 2.4 Classification of Nuclear Power Plants

> [!NOTE]
> **Based on the type of nuclear reaction (in KTU scope):**

1. **Fission Reactor Plant** — Splits heavy nuclei (²³⁵U, ²³⁹Pu, ²³³U). Commercial reality.
2. **Fusion Reactor Plant** — Merges light nuclei (D + T → He). Experimental (ITER project).

**Further classification of Fission Reactors (KTU-relevant):**

| Reactor Type | Moderator | Coolant | Fuel | KTU Use |
|--------------|-----------|---------|------|---------|
| Pressurized Water Reactor (**PWR**) | Light water (H₂O) | Pressurized H₂O | Enriched UO₂ | Most common worldwide |
| Boiling Water Reactor (**BWR**) | Light water | Boiling H₂O (steam itself) | Enriched UO₂ | Used in Japan, USA |
| Pressurized Heavy Water Reactor (**PHWR**) | Heavy water (D₂O) | Heavy water | Natural UO₂ | Indian standard (Tarapur, Kaiga, Kudankulam) |
| Gas-Cooled Reactor (**GCR/Magnox**) | Graphite | CO₂ | Natural U | UK old design |
| Fast Breeder Reactor (**FBR**) | None (fast neutrons) | Liquid Na | PuO₂–UO₂ mix | **PFBR, Kalpakkam** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **India follows the three-stage nuclear programme of Homi Bhabha:** Stage 1 — PHWR (natural uranium); Stage 2 — FBR (Pu + U-238); Stage 3 — Thorium-based (U-233) reactors. This is a **favourite KTU short-question** topic.

---

## 2.5 Schematic Components of a Nuclear Power Plant

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Seven Mandatory Sub-Systems** (matching the energy flow path):

1. **Nuclear Reactor Core** — heart, contains fuel rods (UO₂), control rods (B₄C, Cd), moderator, coolant.
2. **Moderator** — slows fast neutrons to thermal energies (H₂O, D₂O, graphite).
3. **Control Rods** — neutron-absorbing rods (Boron, Cadmium) that regulate the chain reaction.
4. **Primary Coolant Loop** — transfers heat from core to steam generator (pressurized water in PWR, sodium in FBR).
5. **Steam Generator / Heat Exchanger** — converts primary loop water (or generates steam directly in BWR).
6. **Turbine + Generator** — same as in a thermal plant — Rankine cycle.
7. **Condenser + Cooling Tower** — rejects waste heat to the environment.
8. *(Plus)* **Containment Building** — thick reinforced-concrete dome for radiation shielding and accident containment.

---

## 2.6 Nuclear Engineering Formula Sheet (KTU 2024 — High-Yield)

| # | Quantity | Symbol | Formula | Typical Unit | Notes |
|---|----------|--------|---------|--------------|-------|
| 1 | Mass-energy equivalence | $E$ | $E = m c^{2}$ | Joules | $c = 3\times 10^{8}$ m/s |
| 2 | Energy per fission (²³⁵U) | $E_f$ | $\approx 200$ | MeV | Total recoverable |
| 3 | Energy per kg ²³⁵U | $E_{kg}$ | $\dfrac{N_A \cdot E_f}{235}$ | J/kg | $= 8.2 \times 10^{13}$ J/kg |
| 4 | Mass-energy in eV | — | $1$ eV = $1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ J | — | Conversion constant |
| 5 | Mass defect | $\Delta m$ | $Z m_p + (A-Z)m_n - m_{nucleus}$ | u (atomic mass units) | $1 u = 931.5$ MeV/$c^2$ |
| 6 | Binding energy per nucleon | $BE/A$ | $\dfrac{\Delta m \cdot 931.5}{A}$ | MeV | Peaks at Fe-56 |
| 7 | Multiplication factor | $k$ | $\dfrac{N_{gen}}{N_{abs}}$ | dimensionless | $k<1$ subcritical, $k=1$ critical |
| 8 | Four-factor formula | $k_\infty$ | $\eta \varepsilon p f$ | dimensionless | $k_\infty = \eta \cdot \varepsilon \cdot p \cdot f$ |
| 9 | Fermi age equation (approx) | $k_{eff}$ | $k_\infty \cdot P_{NL} \cdot P_{th}$ | dimensionless | With non-leakage probabilities |
| 10 | Thermal power of reactor | $P_{th}$ | $6.25 \times 10^{18} \cdot f$ | W | $f$ = fissions/sec; 1 W ≈ 3.1×10¹⁰ fissions/s |
| 11 | Electrical power output | $P_e$ | $\eta_{th} \cdot P_{th}$ | W | $\eta_{th} \approx 30\%$ |
| 12 | Burn-up of fuel | $BU$ | $E_{thermal}/m_{U\ in\ core}$ | MWd/tonne | Typical 45,000 MWd/t |
| 13 | Moderating ratio | $MR$ | $\xi \Sigma_s / \Sigma_a$ | dimensionless | Higher = better moderator |

### Real-World Engineering Utility of $E = mc^2$

- Determines **core sizing** of a reactor (how much U-235 to achieve criticality).
- Used for **burn-up calculations** in fuel management.
- Used by **AERB (Atomic Energy Regulatory Board, India)** for licensing.
- Foundational to the **Bhabha Atomic Research Centre (BARC)** reactor design code.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Electrical Power Output of a Hydel Plant (from First Principles)

> [!TIP]
> **Why derive this?** Because KTU frequently asks "Derive an expression for the power developed by a hydroelectric power plant" for 7 marks in Part B.

**Given:** A volume $V$ of water at head $H$ above the turbine runner.

**Step 1 — Express the mass of water considered.**

The mass of water $m$ that is available to fall through head $H$ in time $t$ is given by:

$$m = \rho \cdot V = \rho \cdot Q \cdot t$$

Here, $\rho$ is the density of water (kg/m³), $Q$ is the volumetric flow rate (m³/s), and $t$ is the time duration (s).

**Step 2 — Potential energy of that water at elevation H.**

The gravitational potential energy of mass $m$ at height $H$ above a reference datum is:

$$E_{potential} = m \cdot g \cdot H$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the mass expression into the energy equation.**

Substituting $m = \rho Q t$:

$$E_{potential} = (\rho \cdot Q \cdot t) \cdot g \cdot H$$

**Step 4 — Define Power as energy per unit time.**

Power is the rate of doing work. Differentiating (or simply dividing by $t$):

$$P_{hydraulic} = \frac{E_{potential}}{t} = \rho \, g \, Q \, H \quad \text{(in Watts)}$$

**Step 5 — Account for conversion (turbine, generator, transformer) efficiencies.**

The water power is multiplied by the turbine efficiency $\eta_t$, generator efficiency $\eta_g$, and transformer efficiency $\eta_{tr}$:

$$\eta_{overall} = \eta_t \cdot \eta_g \cdot \eta_{tr}$$

**Step 6 — Final electrical output at the bus-bar.**

$$P_{electrical} = \eta_{overall} \cdot \rho \, g \, Q \, H$$

For typical KTU numericals: $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s², so:

$$\boxed{\,P_{electrical} \ (\text{kW}) = 9.81 \cdot \eta_{overall} \cdot Q \,(\text{m}^{3}\!/\text{s}) \cdot H \,(\text{m})\,}$$

**Step 7 — Annual energy generated.**

If the plant runs for a period $T$ hours per year:

$$E_{annual} \ (\text{kWh}) = P_{electrical} \times T = 9.81 \cdot \eta_{overall} \cdot Q \cdot H \cdot T$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical (KTU-Style) — Hydel Plant Sizing

> [!EXAMPLE]
> **Problem:** A hydroelectric plant has a reservoir at an elevation of **200 m** above the tailrace. The head loss in the penstock is **8 m** and the velocity-head loss is **2 m**. The flow rate through the turbine is **30 m³/s**. The turbine efficiency is **88%** and generator efficiency is **95%**. Calculate: (a) Net head, (b) Hydraulic power, (c) Electrical power output.

**Solution:**

**Part (a) — Net head available at the turbine runner inlet.**

$$H_{net} = H_{gross} - h_{friction} - h_{velocity\ loss}$$

$$H_{net} = 200 - 8 - 2 = 190 \ \text{m}$$

**Part (b) — Hydraulic power entering the turbine runner.**

$$P_{hydraulic} = \rho g Q H_{net}$$

$$P_{hydraulic} = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 30 \times 190 \ \text{W}$$

$$P_{hydraulic} = 5.5917 \times 10^{7} \ \text{W} = 55{,}917 \ \text{kW} \approx 55.92 \ \text{MW}$$

**Part (c) — Electrical power output (shaft efficiency × generator efficiency).**

$$P_{electrical} = \eta_{turbine} \cdot \eta_{generator} \cdot \rho g Q H_{net}$$

$$P_{electrical} = 0.88 \times 0.95 \times 55{,}917 \ \text{kW}$$

$$P_{electrical} = 0.836 \times 55{,}917 = 46{,}746.6 \ \text{kW} \approx 46.75 \ \text{MW}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Marks Distribution Tip (KTU Examiner Style):** The 7-mark question is split as: (a) 2 marks for $H_{net}$ derivation, (b) 2 marks for $P_{hydraulic}$, (c) 3 marks for $\eta_{overall}$ multiplication and final answer with unit.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Energy Released per Fission of U-235

**Step 1 — Identify the fission reaction.**

The most common thermal-neutron-induced fission of ²³⁵U is:

$$^{1}_{0}n + ^{235}_{92}U \longrightarrow ^{141}_{56}Ba + ^{92}_{36}Kr + 3\,^{1}_{0}n + E$$

**Step 2 — Apply the law of conservation of mass-energy.**

Total initial rest mass = Total final rest mass + Released energy ($E$).

$$\Big( m_n + m_{^{235}U} \Big) c^{2} = \Big( m_{^{141}Ba} + m_{^{92}Kr} + 3 m_n \Big) c^{2} + E$$

**Step 3 — Define the mass defect.**

The mass defect $\Delta m$ is the difference between LHS mass and RHS mass:

$$\Delta m = m_n + m_{^{235}U} - \Big( m_{^{141}Ba} + m_{^{92}Kr} + 3 m_n \Big)$$

**Step 4 — Apply $E = \Delta m \, c^{2}$.**

$$E_{fission} = \Delta m \cdot c^{2}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute the standard atomic masses.**

Using atomic mass units (u): $1\,u = 931.5$ MeV/$c^2$. Known values: $m_{^{235}U} = 235.0439\,u$, $m_n = 1.0087\,u$, $m_{^{141}Ba} = 140.9144\,u$, $m_{^{92}Kr} = 91.9262\,u$.

$$\Delta m = \big( 1.0087 + 235.0439 \big) - \big( 140.9144 + 91.9262 + 3 \times 1.0087 \big)$$

$$\Delta m = 236.0526 - 235.8667 = 0.1859 \ u$$

**Step 6 — Compute energy in MeV.**

$$E_{fission} = 0.1859 \times 931.5 \ \text{MeV}$$

$$E_{fission} \approx 173.2 \ \text{MeV}$$

This is the **total nuclear energy**. Approximately **200 MeV** is the conventionally used figure in KTU textbooks because it includes the kinetic energy of fission neutrons, beta, gamma, and neutrino contributions.

**Step 7 — Express per kg of U-235.**

Number of atoms per kg of ²³⁵U = $\dfrac{N_A}{235} = \dfrac{6.022 \times 10^{23}}{235} = 2.563 \times 10^{21}$ atoms/kg.

$$E_{per\ kg} = 2.563 \times 10^{21} \times 200 \times 1.602 \times 10^{-13} \ \text{J}$$

$$E_{per\ kg} = 2.563 \times 10^{21} \times 3.204 \times 10^{-11} \ \text{J} = 8.21 \times 10^{10} \ \text{J/kg}$$

$$\boxed{\,E_{per\ kg\ ^{235}U} \approx 8.2 \times 10^{10} \ \text{J/kg} \approx 82 \ \text{TJ/kg}\,}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Comparative Insight:** $8.2 \times 10^{10}$ J/kg from ²³⁵U is roughly **1,000,000×** the energy per kg of coal (24 MJ/kg). This single fact justifies the KTU emphasis on nuclear energy in the energy-engineering curriculum.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical (KTU-Style) — Energy from 1 kg of U-235

> [!EXAMPLE]
> **Problem:** Calculate (a) the energy released by the complete fission of **1 kg of U-235**, and (b) the equivalent mass of coal (calorific value = **25 MJ/kg**) that would release the same energy. Take energy per fission = 200 MeV.

**Solution:**

**Part (a) — Energy from 1 kg.**

Number of atoms in 1 kg of ²³⁵U:

$$N = \frac{6.022 \times 10^{23}}{235} = 2.563 \times 10^{21} \ \text{atoms}$$

Energy per fission in joules:

$$E_f = 200 \times 1.602 \times 10^{-13} = 3.204 \times 10^{-11} \ \text{J}$$

Total energy:

$$E_{total} = 2.563 \times 10^{21} \times 3.204 \times 10^{-11} = 8.21 \times 10^{10} \ \text{J}$$

**Part (b) — Equivalent mass of coal.**

$$m_{coal} = \frac{E_{total}}{CV_{coal}} = \frac{8.21 \times 10^{10}}{25 \times 10^{6}} = 3{,}284 \ \text{kg} \approx 3.28 \ \text{tonnes}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** 1 kg of U-235 ≈ 3.28 tonnes of coal. This is a **highly probable KTU numerical** (asked in 2018, 2021, 2023 ESE papers).

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Hydel vs. Nuclear (KTU Board-Examiner Favourite)

> [!NOTE]
> **Often asked as a 7-mark "Compare" question.**

| Parameter | Hydel Power Plant | Nuclear Power Plant |
|-----------|------------------|---------------------|
| Source of energy | Potential energy of water (gravitational) | Binding energy of atomic nucleus |
| Initial fuel stock required | None (water is renewable) | Heavy (U-235 mining, enrichment) |
| Site flexibility | Restricted to rivers/heads | Anywhere with cooling water |
| Capacity factor | 30%–60% | 85%–95% (highest) |
| Plant factor (India avg.) | 35%–50% | 70%–80% |
| Start-up time | Minutes (pumped storage) | Days (thermal cycling) |
| Capital cost | High (civil works) | Very high (containment, safety) |
| Operating cost | Very low | Low (high fuel cost offset by capacity) |
| CO₂ emissions | ~3 g/kWh (life-cycle, per IPCC) | ~12 g/kWh |
| CO₂ emissions (coal for ref.) | 820 g/kWh | — |
| Major hazard | Dam failure, submergence | Radioactive release |
| Waste | None operational | Spent fuel (long-lived) |
| Land footprint (per MW) | Large (reservoir) | Small |
| Lifetime | 50–100 years (dam) | 40–60 years (nuclear licence) |
| Refurbishing possibility | Yes (turbines, generators) | Limited (concrete ageing) |
| Examples in India | Idukki, Bhakra, Tehri | Tarapur, Kaiga, Kudankulam, Kalpakkam (PFBR) |

---

## 3.6 Site Selection Criteria (Quick Reference — KTU High-Yield)

### For a Hydel Plant:

> [!NOTE]
> 1. **Availability of water** (annual rainfall, run-off, river flow data ≥ 30 years)
> 2. **Head available** (higher head → smaller Q → cheaper civil works)
> 3. **Geology of the site** (rock foundation, absence of fissures)
> 4. **Accessibility** (transportation of turbine/generator parts)
> 5. **Proximity to load centre** (transmission cost)
> 6. **Submergence** (population displacement, environmental cost)
> 7. **Sediment load** of the river (silt reduces reservoir life)

### For a Nuclear Plant:

> [!NOTE]
> 1. **Distance from densely populated areas** (≥ 1.5 km exclusion zone, AERB rule)
> 2. **Availability of abundant cooling water** (sea coast, large river — minimum 50 m³/s per 1000 MW)
> 3. **Geologically stable bedrock** (seismic zone ≤ IV, no active faults)
> 4. **Low seismic & flood risk**
> 5. **Distance from airports / military zones** (AERB guidelines)
> 6. **Grid connectivity** for evacuating ~1000 MW unit power
> 7. **Proximity to uranium transport** (heavy shielding needed during transport)
