# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Schematic Layout of a Hydel Power Plant (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["RESERVOIR\n(Dam / Headworks)"] --> B["INTAKE\nTrash Rack"]
    B --> C["FOREBAY\nSettling Basin"]
    C --> D["PENSTOCK\nHigh Pressure Pipe"]
    D --> E["SURGE TANK\nWater Hammer Protection"]
    E --> F["TURBINE\nPelton / Francis / Kaplan"]
    F --> G["GENERATOR\nAC Alternator"]
    G --> H["TRANSFORMER\nStep Up Voltage"]
    H --> I["TRANSMISSION LINE\nGrid Evacuation"]
    F --> J["TAILRACE\nDischarge to River"]
    A -.Spillway.-> J
    I -.Power to Load.-> K["CONSUMERS\nIndustries / Cities"]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The solid arrows trace the *water flow* path from reservoir to tailrace.
- The dotted arrows trace the *electrical power* path from generator to the consumer grid.
- The **surge tank** is a critical auxiliary that absorbs pressure surges (water hammer) during sudden load rejection or valve closure.

---

## 4.2 Schematic Layout of a Nuclear Power Plant (PWR / PHWR — Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PRIMARY["PRIMARY LOOP (Pressurized / Radioactive)"]
        R["REACTOR CORE\nFuel Rods + Control Rods\n+ Moderator"]
        SG1["STEAM GENERATOR\nPrimary Side Coil"]
        P1["PRIMARY PUMP\nMaintains Flow"]
        R <--> SG1
        SG1 <--> P1
        P1 <--> R
    end

    subgraph SECONDARY["SECONDARY LOOP (Non-Radioactive Steam)"]
        SG2["STEAM GENERATOR\nSecondary Side Shell"]
        T["STEAM TURBINE\nHP + LP Stages"]
        G["GENERATOR\n3-Phase AC"]
        C["CONDENSER\nSteam to Water"]
        CP["CONDENSATE PUMP"]
        SG2 --> T
        T --> G
        T --> C
        C --> CP
        CP --> SG2
    end

    subgraph TERTIARY["TERTIARY LOOP (Cooling)"]
        CW["COOLING WATER PUMP"]
        CT["COOLING TOWER / SEA"]
        CW --> C
        C --> CW
        CT -.Heat Rejection.-> CW
    end

    subgraph CONTAIN["CONTAINMENT BUILDING"]
        PRIMARY
        SECONDARY
    end

    G --> TR["TRANSFORMER\nStep-Up"]
    TR --> GR["GRID\nEvacuation Line"]
    GR --> LOAD["CONSUMERS"]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Three isolated loops prevent radioactive contamination of the working fluid.
- The **steam generator** is the only place where primary and secondary loops exchange energy (through tube walls).
- In a **BWR**, the steam generator is absent — water boils *inside* the reactor pressure vessel itself.

---

## 4.3 Control Rod + Moderator Position (Schematic — Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CORE["FUEL ASSEMBLY CROSS-SECTION"]
        direction TB
        F1["FUEL ROD 1\nUO2 Pellets"]
        F2["FUEL ROD 2\nUO2 Pellets"]
        F3["FUEL ROD 3\nUO2 Pellets"]
        M["MODERATOR REGION\nH2O or D2O"]
        CR["CONTROL ROD\nB4C / Cd / Gd\nMoves Up / Down"]
        F1 -.-> M
        M -.-> F2
        F2 -.-> M
        M -.-> F3
        CR -.Absorbs neutrons.-> M
    end
    N_IN["Incoming Fast Neutron"] --> CORE
    CORE --> N_OUT["Thermal Neutron Causes Next Fission"]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Fast neutrons lose energy by colliding with the moderator (water / heavy water / graphite).
- Slowed (thermal) neutrons are then captured by ²³⁵U to cause the next fission — this is the **chain reaction**.
- Control rods absorb excess neutrons to keep the reactor at $k = 1$ (critical).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each) — Short-Answer

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Dec 2022]`

> Explain the working principle of a **hydel power plant** with a neat block diagram showing the major components.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A hydel power plant converts the potential energy of water stored at a height into electrical energy. Water from a reservoir behind a dam flows through an intake to a **forebay**, then down a **penstock** to a **hydraulic turbine** (Pelton/Francis/Kaplan). The high-velocity water jet strikes the runner blades, rotating the shaft coupled to an **AC generator**. The spent water is discharged to the river through the **tailrace**. The generated electrical power is stepped up by a **transformer** and fed to the **grid**. The overall efficiency is 80–90%. *(1 mark for principle + 1 mark for component identification + 1 mark for flow sequence and efficiency).*

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

> List the major components of a **nuclear power plant**. Differentiate between a **PWR** and a **BWR**.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The major components of a nuclear power plant are: **(1)** Nuclear reactor core with fuel and control rods, **(2)** Moderator, **(3)** Primary coolant loop with pump, **(4)** Steam generator, **(5)** Turbine, **(6)** Generator, **(7)** Condenser and cooling tower, **(8)** Containment building. *(1 mark)*

**PWR vs BWR (2 marks):**

| Feature | PWR | BWR |
|---|---|---|
| Coolant | Pressurized water (no boiling) | Boiling water (steam directly) |
| Steam generator | Present | Absent |
| Steam source | Secondary loop | Reactor vessel itself |
| Pressure | ~155 bar | ~75 bar |
| Example | Kudankulam | Tarapur BWRs (older units) |

---

## 5.2 PART B Question Choice — 14 Marks

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Rule:** Module 1 contributes 1 out of 2 questions in Module 1 portion. Each Part B is 14 marks with internal choice on a *different sub-topic within the same module*. The pattern below mimics that.

### **Question Choice — 14 Marks [Internal Choice Provided]**

---

### **Question 2A (14 Marks) — Hydel Plant Focused**

> **(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the working of a **hydel power plant**. List the main components and state the function of each.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** A hydroelectric power plant has an effective head of **120 m**, a discharge of **50 m³/s**, turbine efficiency of **90%** and generator efficiency of **96%**. Calculate: (i) the electrical power output in kW, (ii) the annual energy generation in kWh if the plant operates for **6000 hours/year**, (iii) the plant factor if the average load is **35 MW**.

---

**Model Solution — 2A(a) [7 marks]:**

Refer to the schematic in **Section 4.1** above. (Use the Mermaid block diagram as the "neat sketch".) Mark allocation:
- Diagram (2 marks)
- 6 components identified with function (3 marks) — Reservoir (storage), Penstock (high-pressure conduit), Turbine (energy conversion), Generator (electromechanical), Tailrace (water disposal), Transformer (voltage step-up).
- Brief on surge tank & spillway (1 mark).
- Statement of overall efficiency and environmental cleanliness (1 mark).

---

**Model Solution — 2A(b) [7 marks]:**

**(i) Electrical power output.**

$$P_e = \eta_{turbine} \cdot \eta_{generator} \cdot \rho g Q H$$

$$P_e = 0.90 \times 0.96 \times 1000 \times 9.81 \times 50 \times 120$$

$$P_e = 0.864 \times 1000 \times 9.81 \times 50 \times 120$$

$$P_e = 0.864 \times 5{,}886 \times 10^{4} = 5.0855 \times 10^{5} \ \text{kW}$$

$$\boxed{P_e \approx 50{,}856 \ \text{kW} \approx 50.86 \ \text{MW}}$$

*(3 marks — formula, substitution, final answer with unit)*

**(ii) Annual energy generation.**

$$E_{annual} = P_e \times T = 50{,}856 \ \text{kW} \times 6000 \ \text{h}$$

$$E_{annual} = 3.051 \times 10^{8} \ \text{kWh} = 305.1 \ \text{GWh}$$

*(2 marks — direct application of $E = P \times T$)*

**(iii) Plant factor.**

$$PF = \frac{Average\ Load}{Plant\ Capacity} = \frac{35{,}000\ \text{kW}}{50{,}856\ \text{kW}}$$

$$PF = 0.688 = 68.8\%$$

*(2 marks — formula, substitution, percentage expression)*

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Errors in 2A(b):**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert W to kW** by dividing by 1000 at the end — KTU deducts **1 full mark**.
> 2. **Confusing $g = 9.81$ with $g = 10$** — examiners allow ±1% tolerance but emphasize using the standard $9.81$.
> 3. **Multiplying $H_{gross}$ with the friction-loss head still included** — always compute $H_{net}$ first.
> 4. **Stating the plant factor as 0.688 without converting to %** — KTU expects either a fraction or a percentage, both accepted, but the *unit* `%` must be visible.

---

### **Question 2B (14 Marks) — Nuclear Plant Focused [Alternative Choice]**

> **(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, describe the working of a **Pressurized Water Reactor (PWR)** based nuclear power plant. Explain the function of the moderator, control rods, and containment building.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** A nuclear reactor consumes **40 g of U-235** per day. Calculate: (i) the energy released per day in joules, (ii) the thermal power of the reactor in MW, (iii) the electrical power output if the overall conversion efficiency is **30%**. (Take energy per fission = 200 MeV, Avogadro's number = 6.022 × 10²³, 1 eV = 1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ J.)

---

**Model Solution — 2B(a) [7 marks]:**

Refer to the schematic in **Section 4.2** above (PWR). Mark allocation:

- Working principle: Enriched UO₂ fuel undergoes controlled fission. Heat from fission is absorbed by pressurized primary water (kept liquid at ~155 bar). Hot primary water flows through the steam generator tubes, transferring heat to the secondary loop water, which boils into steam. The steam drives a turbine coupled to a generator. Spent steam is condensed and recycled. (3 marks)
- **Moderator** (1 mark): Slows fast neutrons (~2 MeV) to thermal energies (~0.025 eV) so that U-235 can capture them and sustain the chain reaction. Common moderators: light water, heavy water, graphite.
- **Control rods** (1 mark): Made of boron carbide (B₄C), cadmium, or hafnium — they absorb neutrons. Insertion depth regulates reactivity; full insertion → subcritical (shutdown); partial → critical (steady power).
- **Containment building** (1 mark): Reinforced concrete + steel dome, ~1 m thick, designed to withstand internal pressure surges and external aircraft impact. It traps radioactive release in case of LOCA (loss-of-coolant accident).
- Diagram (1 mark).

---

**Model Solution — 2B(b) [7 marks]:**

**Step 1 — Number of atoms in 40 g of U-235.**

$$N = \frac{m \cdot N_A}{M} = \frac{40 \times 6.022 \times 10^{23}}{235} = 1.025 \times 10^{23} \ \text{atoms/day}$$

*(1 mark)*

**Step 2 — Energy per fission in joules.**

$$E_f = 200 \times 10^{6} \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19} = 3.204 \times 10^{-11} \ \text{J}$$

*(1 mark)*

**(i) Energy released per day.**

$$E_{day} = N \cdot E_f = 1.025 \times 10^{23} \times 3.204 \times 10^{-11} = 3.285 \times 10^{12} \ \text{J/day}$$

$$\boxed{E_{day} \approx 3.29 \times 10^{12} \ \text{J/day}}$$

*(2 marks)*

**(ii) Thermal power (1 day = 86400 s).**

$$P_{th} = \frac{E_{day}}{t} = \frac{3.285 \times 10^{12}}{86400} = 3.802 \times 10^{7} \ \text{W}$$

$$\boxed{P_{th} \approx 38.02 \ \text{MW}}$$

*(1.5 marks)*

**(iii) Electrical power output.**

$$P_e = \eta \cdot P_{th} = 0.30 \times 38.02 = 11.41 \ \text{MW}$$

$$\boxed{P_e \approx 11.41 \ \text{MW}}$$

*(1.5 marks)*

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Errors in 2B(b):**
> 1. **Using Avogadro's number per mole but forgetting to divide by molar mass (235 g/mol)** — losing 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Mixing up MeV and eV**: 200 MeV = 200 × 10⁶ eV, *not* 200 eV. Convert via $1 \text{ eV} = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ J.
> 3. **Confusing thermal and electrical efficiency** — $\eta$ in this problem is *overall* (≈ turbine + generator). Don't multiply by turbine efficiency again.
> 4. **Time unit mismatch**: 1 day = 86,400 s. Forgetting this drops the answer by 3 orders of magnitude.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — High-Frequency Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Read before writing the exam — these are the marks-killers in Module 1:**
>
> 1. **Mixing up "Plant Factor" and "Load Factor"**: Plant factor uses *plant capacity* in denominator; Load factor uses *peak load* in denominator. They are NOT the same. (Average loss: 1 mark per occurrence.)
>
> 2. **Not drawing the schematic** even when "neat sketch" is not asked. A diagram earns you 1–2 marks for free in any descriptive answer.
>
> 3. **Using $g = 10$ m/s²** in hydel numerics: The KTU official answer key uses $g = 9.81$ m/s². Use the exact value, not an approximation.
>
> 4. **Forgetting the "net head"** vs. "gross head" distinction in hydel derivations: A 7-mark question on "derivation" expects you to *mention* friction loss subtraction explicitly.
>
> 5. **Stating the energy released per fission of U-235 as "200 eV" instead of "200 MeV"** — a 1000× error that nullifies the answer.
>
> 6. **Calling the moderator "coolant"** in a nuclear plant: The moderator *slows neutrons*; the coolant *removes heat*. They are different roles (D₂O can be both, but the *function* is different).
>
> 7. **Skipping units** in the final answer. KTU's official scheme mandates units for every numerical — omitting kW, MW, kWh, J, or MeV loses 0.5–1 mark each time.
>
> 8. **Spelling "Nuclear" as "Nucular" or "Hydel" as "Hydral"** — silly but mark-deducting in formal scripts.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Print this section and stick it on your wall the night before the exam.**

### A. Hydel Power Plant
- [x] Power formula: $P = \eta \cdot \rho g Q H$ → in kW: $P = 9.81 \cdot \eta \cdot Q \cdot H$.
- [x] Three turbine types by head: **Pelton** (high, impulse), **Francis** (medium, reaction), **Kaplan** (low, axial).
- [x] Six main components: **Reservoir, Intake, Penstock, Turbine, Tailrace, Powerhouse**.
- [x] **Surge tank** = protects against water hammer (mandatory in long penstocks).
- [x] Highest conversion efficiency (~85–90%) of all conventional power plants.
- [x] **Renewable, no fuel cost, no emissions**; drawback: large civil works + submergence.
- [x] India: Idukki (KL), Bhakra (HP-Punjab), Tehri (Uttarakhand), Sardar Sarovar (Gujarat).
- [x] Pumped storage = hydel + hydel (uses surplus grid power to pump water back uphill).

### B. Nuclear Power Plant
- [x] Source: $E = mc^2$ — nuclear *binding energy* release, *not* chemical.
- [x] Fuel: ²³⁵U (enriched to 3–5% for PWR/BWR; natural for PHWR).
- [x] Energy per fission: **200 MeV**; per kg of ²³⁵U: **8.2 × 10¹³ J** ≈ 1 kg U ≡ 3 tonnes coal.
- [x] **Moderator** slows neutrons; **Control rods** absorb neutrons; **Coolant** removes heat.
- [x] PWR / BWR / PHWR / GCR / FBR — Indian PHWRs at Tarapur, Kaiga, Kakrapar, Kudankulam (Russian PWR), Kalpakkam (PFBR).
- [x] Three-stage Indian nuclear programme: **PHWR → FBR → Thorium** (Homi Bhabha's vision).
- [x] Site criteria: away from population, abundant cooling water, low seismic zone.
- [x] Highest **capacity factor (≈90%)** of any baseload source.
- [x] Major drawback: radioactive spent fuel (10,000+ year storage challenge) and high capital cost.

### C. Comparative Hook (For 7-mark "Compare" Questions)
- [x] Energy source: gravitational (hydel) vs. nuclear binding (nuclear).
- [x] Plant factor: hydel low (seasonal) vs. nuclear high (continuous).
- [x] Carbon emissions: both low; coal is the reference (~820 g CO₂/kWh).
- [x] Site constraint: hydel needs river; nuclear needs cooling water + isolation.
- [x] Both are **non-fossil**; both are **baseload-capable**; both are **capital-intensive**.

### D. Numerical Sanity Numbers (for Last-Minute Memorisation)
- [x] $\rho_{water} = 1000$ kg/m³
- [x] $g = 9.81$ m/s²
- [x] $c = 3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s
- [x] $1 \text{ eV} = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ J
- [x] $1 \text{ u} = 931.5$ MeV/c²
- [x] $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23}$ /mol
- [x] $E_{fission, ^{235}U} = 200$ MeV
- [x] $E_{per\ kg\ ^{235}U} = 8.2 \times 10^{13}$ J

> **End of Module 1 Notes — Hydel and Nuclear Power Plants** 🎯
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Efficiency
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT411 - ENERGY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Efficiency

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Efficiency

In **Energy Engineering**, **efficiency** ($\eta$) is a dimensionless performance metric that quantifies the effectiveness of an energy conversion or transfer process. It is formally defined as the ratio of the *useful output* (energy, work, or exergy delivered) to the *input* (energy, fuel, or exergy supplied).

$$\eta = \frac{\text{Desired Output}}{\text{Required Input}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PECHT411 Module 1):**
> *"Efficiency is a measure of how well an energy system converts input energy into useful output. It is the most fundamental indicator of thermodynamic performance, fuel economy, and process sustainability in all energy conversion devices such as engines, boilers, turbines, refrigerators, and heat exchangers."*

Mathematically, efficiency is bounded between **0 and 1** (or **0 % to 100 %**):

$$0 \leq \eta \leq 1$$

A value of **η = 1 (100 %)** represents a theoretically ideal, loss-free process (a *reversible* process), while **η = 0** indicates a process that produces no useful output despite receiving input.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **water bucket with holes** at the bottom:

- **Input:** The water poured into the bucket from the top.
- **Useful Output:** The water that successfully reaches a target tank placed beneath the bucket.
- **Wasted Output:** The water that splashes sideways, leaks from the sides, or evaporates.
- **Efficiency:** The ratio of water reaching the tank vs. water poured in.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Intuition:** "Efficiency tells you how much of what you paid for, you actually got to use."

In engineering terms:
- A **100 W incandescent bulb** converts only about **2 W** of electrical energy into light, giving it an efficiency of about **2 %**. The remaining 98 W becomes waste heat.
- A **LED bulb** delivering the same light uses only ~10 W, giving an efficiency of about **20 %** — ten times better.
- A **coal-fired power plant** typically has a thermal efficiency of **33–40 %**, meaning nearly two-thirds of the fuel's chemical energy is lost as waste heat in flue gases and condenser cooling.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

Key constants and reference values used in efficiency calculations:

| Symbol / Quantity | Value / Unit | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| $\eta$ (eta) | Dimensionless (0–1) or % | Universal symbol for efficiency |
| $T_H$ | K (Kelvin) | High / Source temperature |
| $T_L$ | K (Kelvin) | Low / Sink temperature |
| COP | Dimensionless | Coefficient of Performance (refrigerators/HPs) |
| $\eta_{\text{Carnot}}$ | Dimensionless | Maximum theoretical (reversible) efficiency |
| $\eta_{\text{I}}$ (1st Law) | % | Energy efficiency (First-Law based) |
| $\eta_{\text{II}}$ (2nd Law) | % | Exergy efficiency (Second-Law based) |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Always quote efficiency in **%** in your final answer, and always mention the **reference state** (e.g., ambient at 25 °C / 298 K) when discussing Second-Law efficiency.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Efficiency in Energy Engineering

Efficiency in Module 1 of PECHT411 is broadly classified into **three hierarchical categories**:

### 2.1.1 First-Law (Energy) Efficiency

Based on the **First Law of Thermodynamics** (energy conservation). It compares *useful energy out* to *energy in*, ignoring the *quality* of energy.

$$\eta_{\text{I}} = \frac{\text{Useful Energy Output}}{\text{Energy Input}}$$

- **Combustion Efficiency:** $\eta_{\text{comb}} = \dfrac{Q_{\text{absorbed by water/working fluid}}}{Q_{\text{released by fuel}}}$
- **Thermal Efficiency (Heat Engine):** $\eta_{\text{th}} = \dfrac{W_{\text{net}}}{Q_{\text{in}}}$
- **Mechanical Efficiency:** $\eta_{\text{mech}} = \dfrac{W_{\text{shaft output}}}{W_{\text{indicated (in-cylinder)}}$
- **Electrical Efficiency (Generator):** $\eta_{\text{elec}} = \dfrac{P_{\text{electrical out}}}{P_{\text{mechanical in}}}$
- **Overall Efficiency:** $\eta_{\text{overall}} = \eta_{\text{comb}} \times \eta_{\text{th}} \times \eta_{\text{mech}} \times \eta_{\text{elec}}$

### 2.1.2 Second-Law (Exergy) Efficiency

Based on the **Second Law of Thermodynamics**. It accounts for both *quantity* and *quality* of energy. It is the ratio of *exergy output* to *exergy input* (or *exergy recovered* to *exergy expended*).

$$\eta_{\text{II}} = \frac{\text{Exergy Recovered / Useful Exergy Output}}{\text{Exergy Supplied / Exergy Input}}$$

- **For a heat engine:** $\eta_{\text{II}} = \dfrac{\eta_{\text{actual}}}{\eta_{\text{Carnot}}}$
- **For a refrigerator:** $\eta_{\text{II}} = \dfrac{\text{COP}_{\text{actual}}}{\text{COP}_{\text{Carnot}}}$

### 2.1.3 Carnot (Maximum Theoretical) Efficiency

The highest possible efficiency any heat engine operating between two thermal reservoirs at absolute temperatures $T_H$ and $T_L$ can achieve. It is achieved only by a **reversible (frictionless, quasi-static) cycle**.

$$\eta_{\text{Carnot}} = 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H}$$

## 2.2 Why Efficiency Cannot Be 100 %

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" behind losses — KTU 3-Mark Favourite:**
> 1. **Second Law of Thermodynamics** mandates entropy generation ($\Delta S \geq 0$), which inherently dissipates usable energy.
> 2. **Irreversibilities** (friction, turbulence, heat transfer across finite ΔT, mixing, chemical non-equilibrium) destroy exergy.
> 3. **Practical constraints:** material limits (creep, melting points), combustion inefficiencies, electrical resistance, leakage.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\eta = \dfrac{W_{\text{out}}}{E_{\text{in}}}$ | Generic efficiency | All devices |
| 2 | $\eta_{\text{th}} = 1 - \dfrac{Q_{\text{out}}}{Q_{\text{in}}}$ | Thermal efficiency (Heat engine) | Carnot, Otto, Diesel, Rankine |
| 3 | $\eta_{\text{Carnot}} = 1 - \dfrac{T_L}{T_H}$ | Maximum (reversible) efficiency | Heat engine limit |
| 4 | $\eta_{\text{I}} = \dfrac{\text{Useful energy out}}{\text{Energy in}}$ | First Law efficiency | Boilers, turbines, IC engines |
| 5 | $\eta_{\text{II}} = \dfrac{\eta_{\text{actual}}}{\eta_{\text{reversible}}}$ | Second Law / Rational efficiency | All real devices |
| 6 | $\eta_{\text{overall}} = \prod \eta_i$ | Product of stage efficiencies | Multi-stage plants |
| 7 | $\text{COP}_{\text{ref}} = \dfrac{Q_L}{W_{\text{net}}}$ | Coefficient of Performance (Refrigerator) | Vapour compression |
| 8 | $\text{COP}_{\text{HP}} = \dfrac{Q_H}{W_{\text{net}}}$ | Coefficient of Performance (Heat Pump) | Space heating |
| 9 | $\text{COP}_{\text{Carnot, ref}} = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$ | Carnot COP (Refrigerator) | Maximum COP |
| 10 | $\text{COP}_{\text{Carnot, HP}} = \dfrac{T_H}{T_H - T_H}}$ | Carnot COP (Heat Pump) | Maximum COP |
| 11 | $\eta_{\text{II, boiler}} = \dfrac{\text{Exergy of steam}}{\text{Exergy of fuel}}$ | Boiler exergy efficiency | Steam plant |
| 12 | $\eta_{\text{comb}} = \dfrac{m_f \times \text{CV}_{\text{actual}}}{m_f \times \text{CV}_{\text{ideal}}}$ | Combustion efficiency | Furnaces, boilers |

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Anchor:** *First Law* asks *"How much energy?"* — *Second Law* asks *"How much useful energy?"* — *Carnot* asks *"What is the maximum possible?"*

## 2.4 Real-World Utility

- **Power Plants:** A 1 % rise in plant efficiency saves millions of rupees annually in fuel cost.
- **Internal Combustion Engines:** Higher $\eta_{\text{th}}$ directly reduces $\text{CO}_2$ emissions.
- **Refrigeration / AC:** Designers maximise **COP**, not "efficiency" (since $Q_L > W$).
- **Building Design:** Second-Law efficiency guides insulation, window glazing, HVAC sizing.
- **Industrial Process Heating:** First-Law efficiency often misleads — a furnace at 90 % first-law efficiency can be at only 30 % exergy efficiency.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I: Carnot (Maximum Theoretical) Efficiency

We derive the maximum possible efficiency of a heat engine operating between a hot reservoir at $T_H$ and a cold reservoir at $T_L$.

**Step 1 — Start with the First Law for a cyclic heat engine:**

For a complete cycle, the working fluid returns to its initial state, so $\Delta U = 0$. The First Law reduces to:

$$Q_{\text{net}} = W_{\text{net}}$$

**Step 2 — Express net heat in terms of heat absorbed and rejected:**

$$Q_{\text{in}} - Q_{\text{out}} = W_{\text{net}}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the efficiency expression:**

$$\eta_{\text{th}} = \frac{W_{\text{net}}}{Q_{\text{in}}} = \frac{Q_{\text{in}} - Q_{\text{out}}}{Q_{\text{in}}} = 1 - \frac{Q_{\text{out}}}{Q_{\text{in}}}$$

This is the **general thermal efficiency** for any heat engine.

**Step 4 — Apply the Second Law for a reversible (Carnot) cycle:**

For a reversible cycle, the entropy change of the universe is zero. The total entropy of the reservoirs changes only due to heat transfer:

$$\Delta S_{\text{universe}} = \frac{Q_{\text{out}}}{T_L} - \frac{Q_{\text{in}}}{T_H} = 0$$

**Step 5 — Solve for the heat ratio:**

$$\frac{Q_{\text{out}}}{T_L} = \frac{Q_{\text{in}}}{T_H} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{Q_{\text{out}}}{Q_{\text{in}}} = \frac{T_L}{T_H}$$

**Step 6 — Substitute back into the thermal efficiency expression:**

$$\eta_{\text{Carnot}} = 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H}$$

This is the **maximum possible thermal efficiency** of *any* heat engine operating between $T_H$ and $T_L$.

**Step 7 — Concluding observation:**

Since $T_H > T_L$ for a heat engine to function, $\dfrac{T_L}{T_H} < 1$, hence $\eta_{\text{Carnot}} < 1$ (i.e., **always strictly less than 100 %**). This is the Second-Law proof that *no engine can be 100 % efficient*.

## 3.2 Derivation II: Relation Between First-Law and Second-Law Efficiencies

**Step 1 — Recall the definitions:**

$$\eta_{\text{I}} = \frac{W_{\text{actual}}}{Q_{\text{in}}} \quad , \quad \eta_{\text{Carnot}} = 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H}$$

**Step 2 — Define the Second-Law (Rational) efficiency as the ratio of actual to maximum possible:**

$$\eta_{\text{II}} = \frac{\eta_{\text{I}}}{\eta_{\text{Carnot}}}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the First-Law efficiency expression:**

$$\eta_{\text{II}} = \frac{W_{\text{actual}}/Q_{\text{in}}}{1 - T_L/T_H}$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange:**

$$\eta_{\text{II}} = \frac{W_{\text{actual}}}{Q_{\text{in}}\left(1 - \dfrac{T_L}{T_H}\right)}$$

**Step 5 — Engineering interpretation:** $\eta_{\text{II}}$ quantifies *how close* the actual engine is to the ideal reversible engine, with $\eta_{\text{II}} = 1$ meaning the engine is reversible (no irreversibilities).

## 3.3 Derivation III: Overall Efficiency of a Multi-Stage Energy Conversion System

Consider a power plant consisting of a **boiler → turbine → generator → transmission line** in series.

**Step 1 — Stage-wise efficiencies:**

$$\eta_{\text{boiler}} = \frac{Q_{\text{absorbed by water}}}{m_f \times \text{CV}} \quad ; \quad \eta_{\text{turbine}} = \frac{W_{\text{turbine}}}{Q_{\text{in}}}$$

$$\eta_{\text{generator}} = \frac{P_{\text{electrical}}}{P_{\text{mech}}} \quad ; \quad \eta_{\text{transmission}} = \frac{P_{\text{delivered}}}{P_{\text{generated}}}$$

**Step 2 — Cascade the energy outputs:**

$$Q_{\text{in, turbine}} = \eta_{\text{boiler}} \times Q_{\text{fuel}}$$

$$W_{\text{turbine}} = \eta_{\text{turbine}} \times Q_{\text{in, turbine}} = \eta_{\text{turbine}} \eta_{\text{boiler}} \times Q_{\text{fuel}}$$

$$P_{\text{electrical}} = \eta_{\text{generator}} \eta_{\text{turbine}} \eta_{\text{boiler}} \times Q_{\text{fuel}}$$

$$P_{\text{delivered}} = \eta_{\text{transmission}} \eta_{\text{generator}} \eta_{\text{turbine}} \eta_{\text{boiler}} \times Q_{\text{fuel}}$$

**Step 3 — Generalised overall efficiency:**

$$\boxed{\eta_{\text{overall}} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \eta_i = \eta_1 \cdot \eta_2 \cdot \eta_3 \cdots \eta_n}$$

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

> **Problem:** A coal-fired power plant uses **1200 kg/hr** of coal with a calorific value of **25 MJ/kg**. The electrical output is **3.2 MW**. The hot and cold reservoir temperatures are 1500 K and 320 K respectively. Calculate: (a) thermal efficiency, (b) Carnot efficiency, (c) second-law efficiency, (d) overall plant efficiency if boiler efficiency is 88 % and generator efficiency is 95 %.

**Step 1 — Compute the heat input rate:**

$$\dot{Q}_{\text{in}} = \dot{m}_f \times \text{CV} = \frac{1200}{3600} \times 25 \times 10^6 = 8.333 \times 10^6 \text{ W} = 8.33 \text{ MW}$$

**Step 2 — (a) Thermal (First-Law) efficiency:**

$$\eta_{\text{I}} = \frac{\dot{W}_{\text{out}}}{\dot{Q}_{\text{in}}} = \frac{3.2}{8.33} = 0.3842 = 38.42 \,\%$$

**Step 3 — (b) Carnot (Maximum) efficiency:**

$$\eta_{\text{Carnot}} = 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H} = 1 - \frac{320}{1500} = 1 - 0.2133 = 0.7867 = 78.67 \,\%$$

**Step 4 — (c) Second-Law efficiency:**

$$\eta_{\text{II}} = \frac{\eta_{\text{I}}}{\eta_{\text{Carnot}}} = \frac{0.3842}{0.7867} = 0.4885 = 48.85 \,\%$$

**Step 5 — (d) Overall plant efficiency (incorporating stage efficiencies):**

If only the **gross thermal-to-electrical** conversion is considered, and the auxiliary losses are subsumed in $\eta_{\text{boiler}} = 88 \,\%$ and $\eta_{\text{generator}} = 95 \,\%$:

$$\eta_{\text{overall, raw fuel to electrical}} = \eta_{\text{boiler}} \times \eta_{\text{cycle}} \times \eta_{\text{generator}}$$

The actual delivered efficiency of **38.42 %** already accounts for all stages combined. So:

$$\eta_{\text{overall}} = 38.42 \,\%$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Answer Format:** Always box the final numerical result and quote units explicitly (e.g., *38.42 %*).

## 3.5 Comparison Matrix: First-Law vs Second-Law Efficiency

| Feature | First-Law Efficiency ($\eta_{\text{I}}$) | Second-Law Efficiency ($\eta_{\text{II}}$) |
|---|---|---|
| Based on | Energy conservation | Energy + entropy / exergy |
| Considers energy quality? | No | Yes |
| Reference state required? | No | Yes (ambient T, P) |
| Typical max value | Often > 90 % (e.g., electric heater) | Always ≤ 100 % |
| Indicates *true* improvement? | Sometimes misleading | Yes — reveals real losses |
| Example | Electric resistance heater: ~100 % | Same heater (low-grade heat): ~5 % |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Energy Flow and Efficiency Categories in an Energy Conversion System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    fuelIn["FUEL INPUT Qfuel"] --> boiler["BOILER eta boiler"]
    boiler --> steam["STEAM Q steam"]
    steam --> turbine["TURBINE eta turbine"]
    turbine --> wshaf["SHAFT WORK W shaft"]
    wshaf --> gen["GENERATOR eta generator"]
    gen --> pout["ELECTRICAL OUTPUT P out"]
    fuelIn -.Heat loss.-> flue["FLUE GAS Q flue"]
    steam -.Heat loss.-> cond["CONDENSER Q cond"]
    wshaf -.Mech loss.-> mechloss["FRICTION"]
    pout -.Elec loss.-> trloss["TRANSMISSION LOSS"]
    fuelIn --> carnot["CARNOT LIMIT eta Carnot = 1 minus TL by TH"]
    pout --> etaI["FIRST LAW eta I = W out by Q in"]
    pout --> etaII["SECOND LAW eta II = eta I by eta Carnot"]
    pout --> etaOv["OVERALL eta overall = product of all eta"]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Solid arrows represent *useful* energy flow; dashed arrows represent *losses*. Each block represents a stage with its own efficiency, and the cascade multiplies to give the overall efficiency.

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Hierarchical Classification of Efficiency Types

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["EFFICIENCY"] --> B["First Law Energy Efficiency"]
    A --> C["Second Law Exergy Efficiency"]
    A --> D["Carnot Maximum Efficiency"]
    B --> B1["Combustion Efficiency"]
    B --> B2["Thermal Efficiency"]
    B --> B3["Mechanical Efficiency"]
    B --> B4["Electrical Efficiency"]
    B --> B5["Overall Plant Efficiency"]
    C --> C1["Exergy Efficiency"]
    C --> C2["Rational Efficiency eta II"]
    D --> D1["Heat Engine eta Carnot"]
    D --> D2["COP Carnot Ref and HP"]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: How the Three Efficiencies Interact in a Real Plant

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    input["INPUT: Chemical Energy of Fuel"] --> combQ["Fuel Energy Q in"]
    combQ --> firstLaw["ETA I: Energy Output by Energy Input"]
    combQ --> carnotRef["ETA Carnot: 1 minus TL by TH Reversible Benchmark"]
    firstLaw --> ratio["RATIO = ETA I divided by ETA Carnot"]
    carnotRef --> ratio
    ratio --> secondLaw["ETA II: True Thermodynamic Performance Indicator"]
    secondLaw --> design["Design Decision: Improve weak stages"]
    design --> output["OUTPUT: Useful Work or Heat"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this diagram matters for KTU 2024:**
> The Board Examiners in the **2024 Scheme** frequently ask the conceptual question *"How is Second-Law efficiency related to First-Law efficiency?"* — this architecture shows the exact logical chain: *Input → First-Law ratio → normalised by Carnot limit → Second-Law (rational) efficiency.*

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define the terms: (i) First-Law efficiency, (ii) Second-Law efficiency, (iii) Carnot efficiency. State one key difference between First-Law and Second-Law efficiencies.**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) First-Law Efficiency ($\eta_{\text{I}}$):** It is the ratio of the useful *energy* output delivered by a system to the *energy* input supplied, based on the First Law of Thermodynamics (energy conservation).

$$\eta_{\text{I}} = \frac{\text{Useful Energy Output}}{\text{Energy Input}}$$

**(ii) Second-Law Efficiency ($\eta_{\text{II}}$):** It is the ratio of the actual efficiency of a device to the maximum (reversible) efficiency permitted by the Second Law, i.e., it is the exergy-based performance measure.

$$\eta_{\text{II}} = \frac{\eta_{\text{actual}}}{\eta_{\text{reversible (Carnot/COP)}}$$

**(iii) Carnot Efficiency ($\eta_{\text{Carnot}}$):** The maximum possible efficiency of a heat engine operating between a hot source at $T_H$ and a cold sink at $T_L$:

$$\eta_{\text{Carnot}} = 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H}$$

**Key Difference:** First-Law efficiency considers only *quantity* of energy (it can be 100 % for an electric heater), whereas Second-Law efficiency considers both *quantity* and *quality* (the same heater may have $\eta_{\text{II}} < 10 \%$ because it produces low-grade heat).

---

### **Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**A heat engine receives 600 kJ of heat from a source at 800 K and rejects 280 kJ to a sink at 300 K. Determine: (a) thermal efficiency, (b) Carnot efficiency, (c) second-law efficiency. Comment on the results.**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Thermal efficiency:**

$$\eta_{\text{th}} = 1 - \frac{Q_{\text{out}}}{Q_{\text{in}}} = 1 - \frac{280}{600} = 1 - 0.4667 = 0.5333 = 53.33 \,\%$$

**(b) Carnot efficiency:**

$$\eta_{\text{Carnot}} = 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H} = 1 - \frac{300}{800} = 1 - 0.375 = 0.625 = 62.50 \,\%$$

**(c) Second-law efficiency:**

$$\eta_{\text{II}} = \frac{\eta_{\text{th}}}{\eta_{\text{Carnot}}} = \frac{0.5333}{0.625} = 0.8533 = 85.33 \,\%$$

**Comment:** The engine operates at **85.33 %** of the maximum theoretical (Carnot) limit, indicating that it is a reasonably well-designed engine with low irreversibilities. The remaining 14.67 % gap to the Carnot limit represents irreversibilities such as friction, heat transfer across finite ΔT, and unburnt fuel losses.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a)** Define efficiency and classify the different types of efficiencies used in energy engineering with suitable examples.

**(b)** A steam power plant operates between a boiler temperature of 800 K and a condenser temperature of 320 K. The plant consumes 50,000 kg/hr of coal with a calorific value of 30 MJ/kg and delivers 110 MW of electrical power. Calculate: (i) Thermal efficiency of the plant, (ii) Carnot efficiency, (iii) Second-law (rational) efficiency, (iv) Plant efficiency loss per hour in MJ.

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Definition and Classification (7 Marks):**

*Efficiency* is the ratio of useful output (energy/work/exergy) of a system to the energy/exergy input supplied:

$$\eta = \frac{\text{Desired Output}}{\text{Required Input}}$$

*Classification of Efficiencies in Energy Engineering:*

1. **Combustion Efficiency** — ratio of heat released by actual combustion to heat released by complete (stoichiometric) combustion. Used in boilers and furnaces. ($\eta_{\text{comb}} \approx 85\text{–}95 \,\%$)

2. **Thermal Efficiency** — ratio of net work output to heat input in a heat engine ($\eta_{\text{th}} = W_{\text{net}} / Q_{\text{in}}$). Used in Rankine, Otto, Diesel cycles.

3. **Mechanical Efficiency** — ratio of shaft work to indicated work in reciprocating machines ($\eta_{\text{mech}} = W_{\text{shaft}} / W_{\text{indicated}}$).

4. **Electrical (Generator) Efficiency** — ratio of electrical output to mechanical input ($\eta_{\text{elec}} = P_{\text{elec}} / P_{\text{mech}}$).

5. **Overall Plant Efficiency** — product of all stage efficiencies:

$$\eta_{\text{overall}} = \eta_{\text{comb}} \times \eta_{\text{th}} \times \eta_{\text{mech}} \times \eta_{\text{elec}}$$

6. **First-Law (Energy) Efficiency** — based on energy balance, ratio of useful energy out to energy in.

7. **Second-Law (Exergy/Rational) Efficiency** — ratio of actual efficiency to the maximum reversible (Carnot) efficiency, indicating true thermodynamic performance.

8. **Carnot Efficiency** — the absolute upper limit:

$$\eta_{\text{Carnot}} = 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H}$$

9. **Coefficient of Performance (COP)** — for refrigerators and heat pumps (ratio of useful heating/cooling effect to work input).

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Given Data:**

- $T_H = 800 \text{ K}$, $T_L = 320 \text{ K}$
- $\dot{m}_f = 50{,}000 \text{ kg/hr} = 13.889 \text{ kg/s}$
- $\text{CV} = 30 \text{ MJ/kg} = 30 \times 10^6 \text{ J/kg}$
- $\dot{W}_{\text{elec}} = 110 \text{ MW} = 110 \times 10^6 \text{ W}$

**Step (i): Thermal (First-Law) efficiency**

Compute the heat input rate:

$$\dot{Q}_{\text{in}} = \dot{m}_f \times \text{CV} = 13.889 \times 30 \times 10^6 = 416.67 \times 10^6 \text{ W} = 416.67 \text{ MW}$$

$$\eta_{\text{I}} = \frac{\dot{W}_{\text{out}}}{\dot{Q}_{\text{in}}} = \frac{110}{416.67} = 0.2640 = 26.40 \,\%$$

**Step (ii): Carnot efficiency**

$$\eta_{\text{Carnot}} = 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H} = 1 - \frac{320}{800} = 1 - 0.400 = 0.600 = 60.00 \,\%$$

**Step (iii): Second-law efficiency**

$$\eta_{\text{II}} = \frac{\eta_{\text{I}}}{\eta_{\text{Carnot}}} = \frac{0.2640}{0.6000} = 0.4400 = 44.00 \,\%$$

**Step (iv): Energy loss per hour**

The energy lost (wasted as heat) per hour is:

$$\dot{Q}_{\text{lost}} = \dot{Q}_{\text{in}} - \dot{W}_{\text{out}} = 416.67 - 110 = 306.67 \text{ MW}$$

$$Q_{\text{lost, hourly}} = 306.67 \text{ MJ/s} \times 3600 \text{ s} = 1{,}104{,}000 \text{ MJ/hr} = 1104 \text{ GJ/hr}$$

**Final Tabulated Result:**

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| Thermal efficiency | **26.40 %** |
| Carnot efficiency | **60.00 %** |
| Second-law efficiency | **44.00 %** |
| Energy loss per hour | **1104 GJ/hr** |

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain with neat sketches: (i) First-Law efficiency, (ii) Second-Law efficiency, and (iii) Carnot efficiency. Bring out clearly the conceptual difference between the three.

**(b)** A refrigerator operates between a cold space at $-10\,^\circ\text{C}$ and a hot environment at $40\,^\circ\text{C}$. It removes heat from the cold space at the rate of 1.5 kW with a power input of 0.6 kW. Compute: (i) Actual COP, (ii) Carnot COP, (iii) Second-law (rational) efficiency, (iv) The exergy destroyed per second. Take $T_0 = 303 \text{ K}$.

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Conceptual Explanation (7 Marks):**

**(i) First-Law Efficiency** considers the energy balance. It is the ratio of useful energy output to total energy input. For a heat engine:

$$\eta_{\text{I}} = \frac{W_{\text{net}}}{Q_{\text{in}}}$$

*Sketch concept:* Energy in → [Engine] → Useful work + Waste heat. First-Law efficiency measures how much energy is converted to useful form, **irrespective of its quality**. An electric heater can claim 100 % first-law efficiency, even though its output (low-grade heat) is thermodynamically poor.

**(ii) Second-Law Efficiency** measures how well a system utilises the *available work potential* (exergy) of the input. It is the ratio of actual efficiency to the maximum possible (reversible) efficiency:

$$\eta_{\text{II}} = \frac{\eta_{\text{actual}}}{\eta_{\text{reversible}}}$$

*Sketch concept:* It compares the *real* output to the *ideal reversible* output. The electric heater example would have a very low second-law efficiency because it converts high-grade electrical energy into low-grade thermal energy, destroying exergy.

**(iii) Carnot Efficiency** is the theoretical maximum efficiency of a reversible heat engine operating between two thermal reservoirs:

$$\eta_{\text{Carnot}} = 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H}$$

*Sketch concept:* Two isothermal reservoirs at $T_H$ and $T_L$ supply/absorb heat to/from a reversible engine; the ratio of work to $Q_H$ is the Carnot efficiency — an *unattainable* upper bound for real engines.

**Conceptual Difference (in tabulated form):**

| Aspect | First-Law | Second-Law | Carnot |
|---|---|---|---|
| Basis | Energy conservation | Energy + entropy | Reversible cycle |
| Considers quality? | No | Yes | Yes (theoretical best) |
| Realistic indicator? | Sometimes | Yes | No (ideal limit) |
| Max value | Up to 100 % (electric heater) | Up to 100 % | Always < 100 % |

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Given Data:**

- $T_L = -10\,^\circ\text{C} = 263 \text{ K}$
- $T_H = 40\,^\circ\text{C} = 313 \text{ K}$
- $\dot{Q}_L = 1.5 \text{ kW} = 1500 \text{ W}$ (cooling rate)
- $\dot{W}_{\text{in}} = 0.6 \text{ kW} = 600 \text{ W}$
- $T_0 = 303 \text{ K}$ (dead state temperature)

**Step (i): Actual COP of refrigerator**

$$\text{COP}_{\text{actual}} = \frac{\dot{Q}_L}{\dot{W}_{\text{in}}} = \frac{1500}{600} = 2.50$$

**Step (ii): Carnot COP (reversible limit)**

$$\text{COP}_{\text{Carnot}} = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L} = \frac{263}{313 - 263} = \frac{263}{50} = 5.26$$

**Step (iii): Second-law (Rational) efficiency**

$$\eta_{\text{II}} = \frac{\text{COP}_{\text{actual}}}{\text{COP}_{\text{Carnot}}} = \frac{2.50}{5.26} = 0.4753 = 47.53 \,\%$$

**Step (iv): Exergy destroyed per second**

Use the Gouy-Stodola theorem:

$$\dot{X}_{\text{destroyed}} = T_0 \cdot \dot{S}_{\text{gen}}$$

**Compute entropy generation rate** by entropy balance on the refrigerator (steady state):

$$\dot{S}_{\text{gen}} = \frac{\dot{Q}_H}{T_H} - \frac{\dot{Q}_L}{T_L}$$

First, find $\dot{Q}_H$ using energy balance:

$$\dot{Q}_H = \dot{Q}_L + \dot{W}_{\text{in}} = 1500 + 600 = 2100 \text{ W}$$

Now compute:

$$\dot{S}_{\text{gen}} = \frac{2100}{313} - \frac{1500}{263} = 6.709 - 5.703 = 1.006 \text{ W/K}$$

Exergy destroyed:

$$\dot{X}_{\text{destroyed}} = T_0 \cdot \dot{S}_{\text{gen}} = 303 \times 1.006 = 304.82 \text{ W} \approx 0.305 \text{ kW}$$

**Final Tabulated Result:**

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| Actual COP | **2.50** |
| Carnot COP | **5.26** |
| Second-law efficiency | **47.53 %** |
| Exergy destroyed per second | **≈ 304.8 W (0.305 kW)** |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert °C → K:** Marks are heavily deducted if temperatures are used in Celsius in Carnot equations. Always use **Kelvin** (add 273.15).
> 2. **Mixing up efficiency and COP:** Refrigerators and heat pumps use **COP**, not efficiency. Stating "efficiency = 250 %" is a common error — COP can exceed 1, but efficiency cannot.
> 3. **Using Q_out/Q_in vs. T_L/T_H:** In Part B numericals, students often confuse the *heat* ratio with the *temperature* ratio. The general formula uses heat; only for reversible (Carnot) cycles can it be replaced by temperature ratio.
> 4. **Not converting units of time:** When $\dot{m}_f$ is in kg/hr, divide by 3600 to convert to kg/s before computing power in watts.
> 5. **Misdefining Second-Law efficiency:** $\eta_{\text{II}}$ is *not* $W_{\text{actual}}/W_{\text{reversible}}$ in general — it is the ratio of **actual efficiency to the maximum theoretical efficiency** of the same process. Mismatch here costs full marks.
> 6. **Skipping units in final answer:** Always quote the answer in **%** (or as a dimensionless number) with units stated.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (for the night before the exam):**

- [x] **Efficiency = Output / Input** (dimensionless, 0–1 or 0–100 %).
- [x] **First-Law efficiency** uses *energy*; **Second-Law efficiency** uses *exergy* (quality-adjusted).
- [x] **Carnot efficiency** = $1 - \dfrac{T_L}{T_H}$ — absolute theoretical maximum; temperatures **must be in Kelvin**.
- [x] **COP** is used for refrigerators and heat pumps, not "efficiency": $\text{COP}_{\text{ref}} = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$ (Carnot).
- [x] **Overall efficiency** = product of all stage efficiencies in series.
- [x] **Second-law (rational) efficiency** = $\eta_{\text{actual}} / \eta_{\text{reversible}}$ — quantifies how close to ideal a device operates.
- [x] **Exergy destroyed** = $T_0 \cdot \dot{S}_{\text{gen}}$ (Gouy-Stodola theorem) — a key metric of irreversibility.
- [x] **No real heat engine** can exceed the Carnot efficiency (Kelvin-Planck statement of the Second Law).
- [x] **No real refrigerator** can exceed the Carnot COP (Clausius statement of the Second Law).
- [x] **First-Law efficiency can mislead** — an electric heater at 100 % first-law efficiency has very low second-law efficiency.
- [x] **Typical values to remember:** Coal plant ≈ 35 % first-law; Petrol engine ≈ 25–30 %; Diesel engine ≈ 35–40 %; Power plant second-law ≈ 40–55 %; LED ≈ 20 % (light output) vs 2 % for incandescent.
- [x] **Always convert °C to K** in all Carnot-related calculations.
- [x] **Always state the dead-state temperature** ($T_0 \approx 298 \text{ K}$ or 25 °C) when computing second-law efficiency or exergy destruction.
- [x] **Diagrams to memorise:** Sankey diagram (energy in/out/loss) and T-s / P-v diagrams for the Carnot cycle.
- [x] **Examiner's mantra:** *"Quote the formula → Substitute the values → Show the cancellation → State the final answer with units."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Merits and Demerits of the Above Power Plants
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT411 - ENERGY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Merits and Demerits of the Above Power Plants

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Power Plant Classification

A **Power Plant** is an industrial facility designed for the generation of electric power, typically utilizing a prime mover (turbine, engine) coupled to an electrical generator. The KTU 2024 Scheme Module 1 surveys the major conventional and non-conventional power generation technologies, and a complete engineering analysis demands a rigorous comparative study of their **merits (advantages)** and **demerits (limitations/disadvantages)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Scope (Module 1):** This topic synthesizes the comparison of Steam (Thermal) Power Plant, Diesel Engine Power Plant, Gas Turbine Power Plant, Hydroelectric Power Plant, and Nuclear Power Plant. Students must know the merits/demerits of each for Part A (3 marks) and comparative analysis questions for Part B (14 marks).

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview — The "Energy Conversion Household" Analogy

Imagine five different ways to cook a meal (produce energy):

- **Steam Power Plant** = A massive traditional wood-fired stove burning coal. Reliable but slow to start and smoky.
- **Diesel Power Plant** = A portable gas-cylinder stove. Quick to light, but the cylinder (fuel) is expensive.
- **Gas Turbine Plant** = A high-speed industrial blowtorch. Extremely fast, but burns fuel inefficiently at idle.
- **Hydroelectric Plant** = A water-wheel driven by a mountain river. Free "fuel," but you must have the river.
- **Nuclear Plant** = A controlled miniature sun. Astronomical energy density, but demands extreme safety discipline.

Each of these "cooking methods" has its own capital cost, fuel cost, start-up time, efficiency, and environmental footprint — these are the **merits and demerits** we analyze.

## 1.3 Formal Definitions of Key Terms

> [!NOTE]
> **Merit:** A characteristic that makes a power plant economically, operationally, or environmentally superior for a given application.
>
> **Demerit:** An inherent limitation, penalty, or drawback that restricts the plant's applicability, increases cost, or causes environmental/social harm.

**Standard Reference Metrics** (used throughout KTU valuation):
- **Plant Load Factor (PLF)** = $\frac{\text{Actual Energy Generated (kWh)}}{\text{Plant Capacity (kW)} \times \text{Hours in Period}}$
- **Capacity Factor (CF)** = $\frac{\text{Energy actually produced}}{\text{Energy that would be produced at full capacity}}$
- **Heat Rate (HR)** = $\frac{\text{Heat Input (kJ/kWh)}}{\text{Energy Output (1 kWh)}}$
- **Specific Fuel Consumption (SFC)** = $\frac{\text{Fuel consumption (kg/hr or kg/kWh)}}{\text{Power output (kW)}}$

## 1.4 Classification of Power Plants (KTU Module 1)

The power plants studied under this module are broadly classified as:

| **Category** | **Power Plant Type** | **Prime Mover** |
|---|---|---|
| Conventional (Fossil) | Steam Power Plant | Steam Turbine |
| Conventional (Fossil) | Diesel Engine Power Plant | Diesel Engine |
| Conventional (Fossil) | Gas Turbine Power Plant | Gas Turbine |
| Renewable | Hydroelectric Power Plant | Water Turbine |
| Non-Conventional | Nuclear Power Plant | Steam Turbine (Nuclear-heated) |

> [!NOTE]
> A **Steam Power Plant** and a **Nuclear Power Plant** both use a **Steam Turbine** as the prime mover; the difference is the **heat source** (fossil-fuel combustion vs. nuclear fission). This is a classic KTU viva question.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Merits & Demerits — Steam (Thermal) Power Plant

### ✅ Merits
1. **Mature, proven technology** with over a century of operational history.
2. Can use a **wide variety of fuels** (coal, lignite, bagasse, biomass, waste).
3. **High reliability** and long plant life (typically **30–40 years**).
4. **Low fuel cost per kWh** when coal is cheap.
5. Capability to generate **very large capacities** (up to **5000 MW** in a single station).
6. **Base-load capability** — can run continuously at near-rated output.

### ❌ Demerits
1. **Low thermal efficiency** — typically only **35–42%** (Rankine cycle limitation).
2. **High capital cost** and long construction lead time (5–7 years).
3. **Major source of air pollution** — emits CO₂, SO₂, NOₓ, and particulate matter (PM).
4. **Ash handling and disposal** is a serious environmental problem.
5. **Large water requirement** for cooling (≈ **50,000 liters/MWh**).
6. **Slow start-up** (cold start may take 8–10 hours).
7. **High transmission cost** — plants must be located near fuel source, far from load centers.

## 2.2 Merits & Demerits — Diesel Engine Power Plant

### ✅ Merits
1. **Very quick start-up** (a few minutes) and immediate synchronization.
2. **Compact layout** — small footprint per kW installed.
3. **High thermal efficiency** at partial loads compared to steam plants (~ **38–42%**).
4. **Simple operation** — requires less skilled manpower.
5. **Modular capacity addition** possible (add one engine at a time).
6. Ideal for **standby / peaking** duty and **remote locations**.
7. **No need for large cooling water** like steam plants.

### ❌ Demerits
1. **High running cost** due to expensive diesel (HSD/LDO).
2. **Limited unit size** — typically up to **20 MW** per unit.
3. **Higher maintenance** cost (engine wear, lubrication).
4. **Shorter life** (≈ 100,000 operating hours before major overhaul).
5. **Exhaust emissions** — NOₓ, CO, and particulate matter.
6. **Noise pollution** requires acoustic enclosures.
7. Not suitable for continuous **base-load** operation.

## 2.3 Merits & Demerits — Gas Turbine Power Plant

### ✅ Merits
1. **Extremely fast start-up** (2–5 minutes from cold).
2. **Compact, light weight** per kW — about **1/5th** the size of a steam plant of equal capacity.
3. **No boiler required** → lower civil engineering cost.
4. **Simple open-cycle** design possible.
5. **Quick response** to load changes — ideal for **peaking** duty.
6. **Relatively low capital cost** for small installations.
7. Can be combined with a steam plant in **CCGT** configuration to reach **55–60%** efficiency.

### ❌ Demerits
1. **Low efficiency** in simple open cycle (only **15–20%**).
2. **High exhaust gas temperature** (≈ 500 °C) — significant heat loss.
3. **High air consumption** — needs heavy-duty air filters.
4. **Compressor work reduces net output** — only about **1/3** of turbine work is net useful.
5. **Sensitive to ambient conditions** — output drops ~ **1% per 1 °C** rise in ambient temperature.
6. **High fuel cost** (natural gas/diesel) in open cycle.
7. **Blade cooling** limits turbine inlet temperature.

## 2.4 Merits & Demerits — Hydroelectric Power Plant

### ✅ Merits
1. **No fuel cost** — uses renewable water flow.
2. **Highest operational efficiency** of any prime mover (~ **90–95%**).
3. **Long plant life** (≈ **50–100 years**).
4. **No pollution** during operation — green energy.
5. **Quick start-up** (a few minutes for run-of-river; reservoir-based is slower).
6. **Multi-purpose utility** — flood control, irrigation, water supply, navigation.
7. Can be used for **pumped storage** (peak shaving).
8. **Low operating cost** after initial investment.

### ❌ Demerits
1. **Very high capital cost** (dam construction).
2. **Long construction period** (often 10+ years).
3. **Site-specific** — feasible only at suitable river sites.
4. **Displaces human populations** (reservoir submergence).
5. **Affects aquatic ecosystem** — disrupts fish migration.
6. **Sedimentation** reduces reservoir capacity over time.
7. **Output depends on rainfall** — seasonal/climatic variation.
8. Risk of **dam failure** (catastrophic downstream flooding).

## 2.5 Merits & Demerits — Nuclear Power Plant

### ✅ Merits
1. **Enormous energy density** — 1 kg of U-235 ≈ **2,000,000 kg** of coal.
2. **Very low fuel cost** per kWh.
3. **No combustion gases** — minimal air pollution (CO₂, SO₂, NOₓ).
4. **Reliable base-load** operation (capacity factor ~ **85–90%**).
5. **Small fuel storage** — refueling interval typically **12–18 months**.
6. **Land footprint** is small for the energy produced.
7. **Stable fuel prices** — not subject to oil/gas market volatility.

### ❌ Demerits
1. **Very high capital cost** — most expensive per kW installed.
2. **Radioactive waste disposal** — spent fuel remains hazardous for **thousands of years**.
3. **Risk of nuclear accidents** (Chernobyl 1986, Fukushima 2011).
4. **Long construction time** (8–12 years).
5. **Public opposition** and lengthy licensing process.
6. **Decommissioning cost** at end of life is enormous.
7. **Proliferation risk** — nuclear material may be diverted for weapons.
8. **Low thermal efficiency** (~ **33%** — limited by coolant temperature in PWR).

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Comparison Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates the critical formulas and comparative metrics for all five power plants. **Memorize this — it is a high-frequency KTU Part A and Part B topic.**

| **Parameter** | **Symbol / Formula** | **Unit** | **Significance** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Plant Load Factor | $PLF = \dfrac{E_{actual}}{P_{installed} \times h}$ | fraction or % | Measures utilization |
| Heat Rate | $HR = \dfrac{3600}{\eta_{thermal}}$ | kJ/kWh | Inverse of efficiency |
| Thermal Efficiency | $\eta_{th} = \dfrac{3600}{HR}$ | % | Energy conversion quality |
| Specific Fuel Consumption | $SFC = \dfrac{m_{fuel}}{P_{output}}$ | kg/kWh | Fuel economy |
| Carnot Efficiency | $\eta_{carnot} = 1 - \dfrac{T_{low}}{T_{high}}$ | % | Maximum possible |
| Rankine Efficiency | $\eta_{rankine} = \dfrac{W_{turbine} - W_{pump}}{Q_{in}}$ | % | Steam plant basis |
| Overall Plant Efficiency | $\eta_{overall} = \eta_{boiler} \times \eta_{cycle} \times \eta_{mech} \times \eta_{gen}$ | % | Product of stages |
| Capacity Factor (typical) | $CF_{nuclear} \approx 0.90$ | fraction | High base-load reliability |
| Hydro Efficiency (typical) | $\eta_{hydro} \approx 0.90$ | fraction | Best of all prime movers |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU valuation key expects students to **state numerical values** of efficiency, start-up time, and capital cost in answers. Vague qualitative answers lose marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Heat Rate from Thermal Efficiency

The **Heat Rate (HR)** is the most commonly cited performance metric in power plants. It is defined as the heat input required per unit of electrical energy output.

### Given Data
- Electrical energy output = $1 \text{ kWh}$
- Thermal efficiency of the plant = $\eta_{th}$

### Step 1: Express Energy Output in SI units

$$
\begin{aligned}
1 \text{ kWh} &= 1 \text{ kW} \times 1 \text{ hr} \\
&= 1000 \text{ W} \times 3600 \text{ s} \\
&= 3.6 \times 10^6 \text{ J} \\
&= 3600 \text{ kJ}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** We are converting the unit of energy from the practical kWh to the SI-consistent kilojoule so that it can be related to the heat input (also in kJ).

### Step 2: Apply the Definition of Thermal Efficiency

By definition, the thermal efficiency is the ratio of useful work output to the heat input:

$$
\eta_{th} = \frac{\text{Work Output}}{\text{Heat Input}}
$$

Substituting the work output of 3600 kJ per kWh:

$$
\eta_{th} = \frac{3600 \text{ kJ}}{Q_{in} \text{ (kJ)}}
$$

### Step 3: Solve for Heat Input

$$
Q_{in} = \frac{3600}{\eta_{th}} \text{ kJ per kWh}
$$

### Step 4: Recognize the Result as Heat Rate

Since the **Heat Rate** is defined as the heat input per kWh of electrical output:

$$
\boxed{HR = \frac{3600}{\eta_{th}} \text{ kJ/kWh}}
$$

### Step 5: Illustrative Numerical Check (KTU-style)

For a thermal power plant with $\eta_{th} = 0.40$ (40%):

$$
\begin{aligned}
HR &= \frac{3600}{0.40} \\
&= 9000 \text{ kJ/kWh}
\end{aligned}
$$

For a nuclear plant with $\eta_{th} = 0.33$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
HR &= \frac{3600}{0.33} \\
&\approx 10909 \text{ kJ/kWh}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Note:** The lower the efficiency, the higher the heat rate — meaning more fuel is burned for the same electricity. This is the mathematical basis for stating that nuclear plants consume more heat per kWh than modern combined-cycle gas plants.

## 3.2 Derivation — Energy Density of Nuclear Fuel

This is a **favourite KTU viva and Part A question**.

### Step 1: Energy released by 1 kg of coal

Using calorific value of coal: $CV_{coal} \approx 25,000 \text{ kJ/kg}$

$$
E_{coal} = 25{,}000 \text{ kJ per kg}
$$

### Step 2: Energy released by 1 kg of U-235 via fission

Using Einstein's mass-energy equivalence for fission of U-235 (≈ **0.1%** mass conversion):

$$
E_{U-235} = 0.001 \times c^2
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{U-235} &= 0.001 \times (3 \times 10^8)^2 \text{ J} \\
&= 9 \times 10^{13} \text{ J} \\
&= 9 \times 10^{10} \text{ kJ} \\
&\approx 8.2 \times 10^{10} \text{ kJ per kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Compute the Ratio

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Ratio} &= \frac{E_{U-235}}{E_{coal}} \\
&= \frac{8.2 \times 10^{10}}{2.5 \times 10^4} \\
&\approx 3.28 \times 10^6
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Interpret

$$
\boxed{1 \text{ kg of U-235} \approx 3 \times 10^6 \text{ kg of coal} \approx 3{,}280 \text{ tonnes of coal}}
$$

This mathematically proves the **enormous energy density** advantage of nuclear fuel, justifying its use despite the high capital cost.

## 3.3 KTU Master Comparison Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **single most important table** for the topic. KTU examiners frequently award 7-mark sub-questions requiring a structured comparison.

| **Criterion** | **Steam Plant** | **Diesel Plant** | **Gas Turbine** | **Hydro Plant** | **Nuclear Plant** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Prime Mover** | Steam Turbine | Diesel Engine | Gas Turbine | Water Turbine | Steam Turbine |
| **Fuel** | Coal/Fossil | Diesel (HSD) | Natural Gas / Diesel | Water (Renewable) | Uranium-235 |
| **Thermal Efficiency** | 35–42% | 38–42% | 15–20% (OC) / 55–60% (CCGT) | 85–95% | 30–35% |
| **Capital Cost** | High | Low | Moderate | Very High | Very High |
| **Operating Cost** | Moderate | High | High | Very Low | Low |
| **Start-up Time** | 8–10 hrs | 2–5 min | 2–5 min | 5–10 min (Run-of-river) | 2–3 days |
| **Site Dependency** | Near fuel source | Anywhere | Anywhere | Specific river site | Far from populated areas |
| **Plant Life** | 30–40 years | 20–25 years | 20–30 years | 50–100 years | 40–60 years |
| **Pollution** | High (CO₂, SO₂, ash) | Moderate (NOₓ, CO) | Moderate (NOₓ, CO) | None | Low (radiological risk) |
| **Capacity Range** | Up to 5000 MW | Up to 20 MW | Up to 250 MW | Up to 10,000 MW | Up to 1600 MW |
| **Suitability** | Base load | Standby / Peaking | Peaking | Base / Peak (pumped) | Base load |
| **Cooling Water Need** | Very High | Low | Low | None (water IS the fuel) | High |
| **Reliability (CF)** | 0.60–0.75 | 0.20–0.40 | 0.05–0.20 | 0.40–0.60 | 0.85–0.90 |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Decision Logic for Power Plant Selection

```mermaid

    C1 -- No --> C3[Consider Small Gas Turbine]

    E5 --> F
```
