# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**List any four merits of a hydroelectric power plant.**

**Model Answer (4 merits, with brief justification):**
1. **No fuel cost** — uses renewable water; energy is virtually free after installation. *(1 mark)*
2. **High efficiency** — water turbines achieve 85–95% conversion efficiency, the highest of any prime mover. *(1 mark)*
3. **Long plant life** — dams and turbines can operate for 50–100 years with proper maintenance. *(1 mark)*
4. **Multipurpose utility** — a single dam provides flood control, irrigation, water supply, navigation, and recreation in addition to power. *(1 mark)*

> [!NOTE]
> Award full marks only if the student provides **at least one quantitative value** (e.g., efficiency percentage or plant life in years). Pure qualitative one-word answers are penalized.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**Mention any three demerits of a nuclear power plant.**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Radioactive waste disposal** — spent fuel remains hazardous for thousands of years and requires secure, deep geological repositories. *(1 mark)*
2. **Very high capital cost** — typically ₹8–15 crores per MW installed, the highest among all power plants. *(1 mark)*
3. **Risk of nuclear accidents** — potential release of radioactive material can have catastrophic health and environmental consequences (e.g., Chernobyl, Fukushima). *(1 mark)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]**
**(a)** Explain the merits and demerits of a **steam power plant** in detail. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Compare the **diesel engine power plant** and **gas turbine power plant** on the basis of efficiency, start-up time, fuel cost, and typical applications. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a) — Merits and Demerits of Steam Power Plant

**Merits (3.5 marks):**
1. **Mature and reliable technology** — operational history of over 100 years, well-established manufacturing and maintenance ecosystem.
2. **Fuel flexibility** — can use coal, lignite, biomass, bagasse, and industrial waste, providing energy security.
3. **Suitable for very large capacities** — single units up to **1000 MW** and stations up to **5000 MW** are operational worldwide.
4. **Low fuel cost per kWh** — coal is relatively inexpensive per unit of energy.
5. **Long plant life** — 30–40 years of operation with periodic overhauls.
6. **Good base-load capability** — runs continuously at near-rated output.

**Demerits (3.5 marks):**
1. **Low thermal efficiency** — limited to **35–42%** due to the Rankine cycle's working fluid (water/steam) temperature constraints.
2. **High capital cost** and long gestation period — 5–7 years for construction.
3. **Major environmental polluter** — emits CO₂ (greenhouse gas), SO₂ (acid rain), NOₓ (smog), and **fly ash** (respiratory hazard).
4. **Ash handling and disposal** — large coal-fired plants generate tonnes of ash daily, requiring dedicated ash ponds.
5. **Large cooling water requirement** — approximately **50,000 litres per MWh**, causing thermal pollution of water bodies.
6. **Slow start-up and shut-down** — a cold start takes **8–10 hours**, making it unsuitable for peak loads.
7. **Remote location from load centers** — coal handling and water needs force siting far from cities, increasing transmission cost.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b) — Diesel vs Gas Turbine Power Plant

| **Parameter** | **Diesel Engine Plant** | **Gas Turbine Plant** |
|---|---|---|
| **Efficiency** | **38–42%** (higher, especially at partial load) | **15–20%** in open cycle; up to **55–60%** in combined cycle (CCGT) |
| **Start-up Time** | **2–5 minutes** | **2–5 minutes** (faster for aero-derivative units) |
| **Fuel Cost** | **High** (diesel/HSD is expensive) | **Moderate to High** (natural gas cheaper than diesel; gas turbines can use gas, dual fuel) |
| **Capacity Range** | Typically up to **20 MW** per unit | Up to **250 MW** per unit |
| **Capital Cost** | Low to moderate | Moderate |
| **Footprint** | Compact | Very compact (1/5th of steam plant) |
| **Maintenance** | Frequent (engine wear, lube oil) | Less frequent, but compressor/turbine blade maintenance is specialized |
| **Typical Applications** | Standby power, remote installations, captive power, mobile generators | **Peaking duty**, emergency power, CCGT base-load plants, offshore platforms |

**Synthesis (2 marks):**
- Diesel plants are best suited for **small-scale, decentralized, standby applications** where fuel cost is secondary to availability and quick start.
- Gas turbines dominate **medium-capacity peaking and combined-cycle base-load** applications, especially where natural gas is available.
- In terms of pure **efficiency at small scale**, diesel wins; in terms of **scale and speed of installation**, gas turbine wins.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**(a)** Discuss the merits and demerits of a **nuclear power plant**. Why is it preferred for base-load generation despite its high capital cost? *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A **hydroelectric power plant** has an effective head of $H = 200$ m, discharge $Q = 50$ m³/s, and overall efficiency $\eta = 0.90$. Compute the power generated in MW and the annual energy output in GWh (assume 80% PLF). *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a) — Nuclear Power Plant Merits and Demerits

**Merits (3 marks):**
1. **Enormous energy density** — 1 kg of U-235 ≈ **3 million kg** of coal.
2. **Very low fuel cost per kWh** — once loaded, the fuel operates for 12–18 months continuously.
3. **Minimal air pollution** — no CO₂, SO₂, or NOₓ from combustion.
4. **High capacity factor (85–90%)** — best among all power plants, ideal for **base load**.
5. **Small land footprint** for the energy produced.
6. **Energy independence** — reduces dependence on imported fossil fuels.

**Demerits (2 marks):**
1. **Very high capital cost** — ₹8–15 crores/MW; among the highest of any power plant.
2. **Radioactive waste management** — spent fuel remains hazardous for **10,000+ years**; needs deep geological disposal.
3. **Risk of catastrophic accidents** — Chernobyl (1986), Fukushima (2011).
4. **Long construction time** — 8–12 years; plus several years for licensing and public consultation.
5. **Huge decommissioning cost** at end of life.

**Why preferred for base load despite high capital cost (2 marks):**
- The **plant load factor** of nuclear plants is **85–90%**, far higher than coal (**65–75%**), gas (**20–40%**), or even hydro (**40–60%** for run-of-river).
- This means the **per-unit cost of electricity is competitive** because the high capital cost is amortized over a large number of kWh generated.
- The **fuel cost component** is only about **5–10%** of the total generation cost, so it is insulated from fuel price volatility.
- Combined with **low emissions**, nuclear provides reliable, large-scale, baseload clean energy, which is essential for grid stability.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b) — Hydroelectric Power Calculation

**Given:**
- Head, $H = 200$ m
- Discharge, $Q = 50$ m³/s
- Overall efficiency, $\eta = 0.90$
- Density of water, $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³
- Acceleration due to gravity, $g = 9.81$ m/s²
- Plant Load Factor, $PLF = 0.80$
- Time period, $t = 1 \text{ year} = 8760$ hours

**Formula:**

$$
P = \rho \cdot g \cdot Q \cdot H \cdot \eta \quad \text{(watts)}
$$

**Step 1: Calculate the Available Hydraulic Power**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{hydraulic} &= \rho \times g \times Q \times H \\
&= 1000 \times 9.81 \times 50 \times 200 \\
&= 1000 \times 9.81 \times 10{,}000 \\
&= 9.81 \times 10^7 \text{ W} \\
&= 98.1 \text{ MW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Apply Overall Efficiency to Get Electrical Power Output**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{electrical} &= P_{hydraulic} \times \eta \\
&= 98.1 \times 0.90 \\
&= 88.29 \text{ MW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Apply Plant Load Factor to Get Actual Average Power**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{average} &= P_{electrical} \times PLF \\
&= 88.29 \times 0.80 \\
&= 70.632 \text{ MW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Calculate Annual Energy Output**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{annual} &= P_{average} \times 8760 \text{ hours} \\
&= 70.632 \times 8760 \\
&= 618{,}736.32 \text{ MWh} \\
&\approx 618.74 \text{ GWh}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answers:**

$$
\boxed{P_{electrical} \approx 88.29 \text{ MW}, \quad E_{annual} \approx 618.74 \text{ GWh}}
$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls — Merits & Demerits Questions:**
> 1. **Writing only one-word answers** like "high efficiency" or "low cost" — the examiner expects a **quantified statement** (e.g., "thermal efficiency of 35–42%"). Vague answers lose 50% of allotted marks.
> 2. **Confusing steam and nuclear plants** — both use steam turbines, but the heat source differs. Examiners will deduct 1 mark for this mix-up.
> 3. **Missing the "renewable" tag** for hydro — it is the only major conventional plant with no fuel cost.
> 4. **Forgetting capital vs. operating cost distinction** — e.g., hydro has very high capital but negligible operating cost. Examiners test this distinction.
> 5. **Not comparing two plants side-by-side** in "compare" questions — always use a **table format** for clarity; this also signals structured thinking to the examiner.
> 6. **Wrong unit in hydro calculation** — confusing kW and MW, or forgetting to multiply by PLF. Carefully re-check unit conversions.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Merits and Demerits of Power Plants (KTU Module 1):**

- ⭐ **Steam Plant:** Mature, large capacity, but polluting and slow. Efficiency **35–42%**. Best for base load.
- ⭐ **Diesel Plant:** Quick start, compact, but **high running cost** (HSD). Limited to **≤ 20 MW**. Ideal for standby/peak.
- ⭐ **Gas Turbine:** Fastest start, compact, but **low efficiency in open cycle (15–20%)**. Sensitive to ambient. **CCGT reaches 55–60%**.
- ⭐ **Hydro Plant:** **No fuel cost, 90% efficiency, 50–100 yr life**, but site-specific and high capital. **Multipurpose utility.**
- ⭐ **Nuclear Plant:** **Energy density ≈ 3 million × coal**, low emissions, but **radioactive waste, high capital, accident risk**. Best for base load (CF ≈ 90%).
- ⭐ **Key Formula:** $P = \rho g Q H \eta$ (hydro power) and $HR = \dfrac{3600}{\eta_{th}}$ kJ/kWh.
- ⭐ **Quick comparison numbers to memorize:**
    - Steam: 35–42% efficient, 8–10 hr start
    - Diesel: 38–42% efficient, 2–5 min start
    - Gas Turbine OC: 15–20% efficient, 2–5 min start
    - Hydro: 85–95% efficient, no fuel
    - Nuclear: 30–35% efficient, 2–3 day start
- ⭐ **For every merit given, students should be able to state the converse demerit** — examiners often ask for paired comparisons.
- ⭐ **Always use tables for Part B comparisons** — they earn extra presentation marks.
- ⭐ **Numerical values are non-negotiable** — write efficiencies, start-up times, and capital costs in every answer.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Combined Cycle Power Plants
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT411 - ENERGY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Combined Cycle Power Plants

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Combined Cycle Power Plant (CCPP)** is a highly efficient electricity generation facility that integrates two distinct thermodynamic cycles — the **Brayton cycle** (gas turbine) and the **Rankine cycle** (steam turbine) — to extract maximum useful work from a single primary fuel source. The defining engineering principle is the **cascade utilisation of thermal energy**, where the high-temperature exhaust gases from the gas turbine (typically between **450°C and 600°C**) are not released to the atmosphere but are instead routed through a **Heat Recovery Steam Generator (HRSG)** to produce steam that drives a secondary steam turbine cycle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PECHT411 – Energy Engineering, Module 1):**
> A Combined Cycle Power Plant combines a gas turbine (Brayton cycle) and a steam turbine (Rankine cycle) in series such that the waste heat rejected from the gas turbine is recovered and utilised as the heat source for the steam cycle, thereby achieving a higher overall thermal efficiency than either cycle could achieve independently.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Classification Terminology (KTU Board Expected):**
> - **1-1 Configuration:** One Gas Turbine (GT) + One Steam Turbine (ST) + One HRSG
> - **2-1 Configuration:** Two GTs + One ST + Two HRSGs
> - **3-1 Configuration:** Three GTs + One ST + Three HRSGs
> - **NGCC:** Natural Gas Combined Cycle (most common modern variant)
> - **CCGT:** Combined Cycle Gas Turbine (synonymous with NGCC)

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

**The Tea-Kettle Analogy (for the absolute beginner):**

Imagine you are boiling water on a gas stove to make tea. You light the burner (the **flame** does useful cooking work), and once the water boils, a tremendous amount of **hot exhaust** rises from the kettle. Normally, that heat is simply lost.

A Combined Cycle Power Plant works on the exact same logic — but in reverse:
- **Step 1 (The Burner = Gas Turbine):** Natural gas is burnt in a combustion chamber. The expanding hot gases spin a gas turbine, producing electricity. (This is your "tea-making" energy.)
- **Step 2 (The Steam = Steam Turbine):** The hot exhaust gases, instead of being wasted, are passed over pipes full of water. The water turns to steam. That steam spins a separate steam turbine, producing *more* electricity from the *same fuel*. (This is your "free hot water" energy.)

> So, a Combined Cycle is essentially saying: *"Don't throw away the exhaust heat — squeeze out every last joule of energy from the fuel you already paid for."*

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Metric to Impress in Exams:**
> - Modern CCPPs achieve efficiencies in the range of **55% – 62%** (LHV basis).
> - A standalone thermal power plant (Rankine only) rarely exceeds **38% – 42%**.
> - This means CCPPs produce **up to 50% more electricity from the same quantity of fuel**, drastically reducing CO₂ emissions per kWh.

## 1.3 The "Why" Behind Combined Cycles

The fundamental reason combined cycles are so efficient lies in the **thermodynamic mean effective temperature** of heat addition. The Brayton cycle operates at a very **high peak temperature** (turbine inlet temperatures of **1300°C – 1500°C** in modern F-class machines), but rejects a lot of useful heat at moderately high temperatures. The Rankine cycle excels at converting *moderate-temperature* heat into work. By stacking them, the combined system **broadens the temperature range** over which heat is converted to work — moving us closer to the **Carnot ideal** without violating the second law.

| Component | Cycle | Heat Source | Working Fluid | Typical Output Share |
|-----------|-------|-------------|---------------|---------------------|
| Gas Turbine | Brayton | Fuel Combustion | Air + Combustion Gases | **~ 65–70%** of total power |
| HRSG | Heat Exchanger | GT Exhaust | Water / Steam | Transfers heat |
| Steam Turbine | Rankine | HRSG Steam | Water-Steam | **~ 30–35%** of total power |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown

A modern NGCC operates on the following sequential logic:

**Stage 1 — Air Compression (Brayton – Compressor):**
- Ambient atmospheric air at roughly **1.013 bar** and **25°C – 30°C** is drawn into a multi-stage axial flow compressor.
- The compressor pressurises the air to a pressure ratio typically between **10:1 and 25:1** (modern F-class machines use ~ 18:1).
- Compression is essentially isentropic, with isentropic efficiencies in the range of **86% – 92%**.

**Stage 2 — Combustion (Brayton – Combustion Chamber):**
- The compressed air mixes with natural gas (or other fuel) in the combustion chamber.
- Continuous combustion raises the temperature to the **Turbine Inlet Temperature (TIT)**, which in modern machines ranges from **1300°C to 1500°C** (often called the "firing temperature").
- Heat addition occurs at (approximately) constant pressure.

**Stage 3 — Expansion (Brayton – Gas Turbine):**
- The high-energy combustion gases expand through the gas turbine, dropping in both pressure and temperature.
- The turbine is mechanically coupled to the generator (and the compressor via a common shaft).
- A portion of the work produced is consumed internally to drive the compressor; the **net work output** is what goes to the electrical generator.
- Exhaust gases leave the gas turbine at **T₃ ≈ 500°C – 620°C** — this is the **"free energy"** that the combined cycle exploits.

**Stage 4 — Heat Recovery (HRSG):**
- The hot exhaust gases flow over banks of tubes containing water/steam.
- Heat transfer occurs from gas (hot) to water/steam (cold) at approximately constant pressure.
- A typical HRSG has **three sections**:
  - **Economiser:** Preheats feedwater using the coolest exhaust gas.
  - **Evaporator:** Converts water to saturated steam.
  - **Superheater:** Raises steam temperature above saturation (often to **540°C – 565°C**, matching modern Rankine practice).
- Some advanced HRSGs include **reheater** and **multiple pressure levels** (dual-pressure, triple-pressure).

**Stage 5 — Steam Expansion (Rankine – Steam Turbine):**
- The superheated steam expands through the steam turbine, producing work.
- Steam turbine may be **condensing** (most common) or **back-pressure**.
- Condenser operates at very low pressure (~ **0.05 bar**) for high expansion ratio.

**Stage 6 — Condensation & Feedwater Return:**
- Exhaust steam is condensed in a surface condenser cooled by water (cooling tower or once-through).
- Condensate is pumped back to the HRSG via a feedwater pump, completing the Rankine loop.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology Students Must Know:**
> - **TIT / TIT (Turbine Inlet Temperature):** The maximum temperature of gases entering the gas turbine. Higher TIT = higher efficiency but requires advanced blade cooling.
> - **Pressure Ratio (rp):** Compressor outlet pressure ÷ compressor inlet pressure. Higher rp = higher Brayton efficiency up to an optimum.
> - **Pinch Point:** The minimum temperature difference between the exhaust gas and the working fluid in the HRSG evaporator. Typical value: **10°C – 20°C**.
> - **Approach Point:** The minimum temperature difference between the exhaust gas leaving the economiser and the feedwater. Typical value: **5°C – 10°C**.

## 2.2 The Combined Cycle Efficiency — The Master Equation

The overall thermal efficiency of a combined cycle power plant is governed by a remarkably elegant relationship:

$$\eta_{CC} = \eta_{BT} + \eta_{ST} - (\eta_{BT} \times \eta_{ST})$$

Or equivalently:

$$\eta_{CC} = 1 - (1 - \eta_{BT}) \times (1 - \eta_{ST})$$

Where:
- $\eta_{CC}$ = Combined cycle thermal efficiency
- $\eta_{BT}$ = Gas turbine (Brayton cycle) thermal efficiency, considered as a standalone system
- $\eta_{ST}$ = Steam turbine (Rankine cycle) thermal efficiency, considered as a standalone system

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this equation works (Physical Intuition):**
> The Brayton cycle rejects heat that the Rankine cycle uses as its heat input. If you treated the two cycles independently, the efficiency of each would be calculated against its own fuel input. The product term $\eta_{BT} \times \eta_{ST}$ is **subtracted** because the heat rejected by the gas cycle is *not* a loss anymore — it is *recaptured*. Thus, the true efficiency of the *combined* system is **greater than the sum of individual efficiencies**.

### 2.2.1 Alternative Form: Efficiencies on a Common Heat-Input Basis

Some textbooks (and certain KTU questions) define both cycle efficiencies against the *same* fuel input $Q_{in}$:

$$\eta_{BT} = \frac{W_{BT}}{Q_{in}}, \quad \eta_{ST} = \frac{W_{ST}}{Q_{in}}$$

In this case:

$$\eta_{CC} = \eta_{BT} + \eta_{ST} \quad \text{(with heat-cascade correction)}$$

> ⚠️ **Valuation Warning:** Always check whether the question specifies each cycle's efficiency on its **own heat input** (in which case use $1 - (1-\eta_{BT})(1-\eta_{ST})$) or on a **common heat input** (a less common but occasionally used approach).

## 2.3 High-Yield KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Variables / Units | Notes |
|---|----------|---------|-------------------|-------|
| 1 | Combined Cycle Efficiency (independent heat inputs) | $\eta_{CC} = 1 - (1-\eta_{BT})(1-\eta_{ST})$ | Dimensionless | Most commonly used |
| 2 | Combined Cycle Efficiency (explicit form) | $\eta_{CC} = \eta_{BT} + \eta_{ST} - \eta_{BT}\eta_{ST}$ | Dimensionless | Equivalent to (1) |
| 3 | Specific Work Output of CCPP | $W_{net,CC} = W_{BT,net} + W_{ST,net}$ | kJ/kg or MW | Additive property |
| 4 | Brayton (Gas Turbine) Efficiency | $\eta_{BT} = \dfrac{W_{net,BT}}{Q_{in,BT}}$ | Dimensionless | $W_{net,BT} = W_T - W_C$ |
| 5 | Rankine (Steam Turbine) Efficiency | $\eta_{ST} = \dfrac{W_{net,ST}}{Q_{in,ST}}$ | Dimensionless | $W_{net,ST} = W_T - W_P$ |
| 6 | Gas Turbine Power Output | $P_{GT} = \dot{m}_g (h_3 - h_4)$ | kW | Enthalpy drop in turbine |
| 7 | Compressor Power Input | $P_C = \dot{m}_a (h_2 - h_1)$ | kW | Enthalpy rise in compressor |
| 8 | Net Gas Turbine Power | $P_{GT,net} = P_{GT} - P_C$ | kW | After subtracting compressor work |
| 9 | Steam Turbine Power Output | $P_{ST} = \dot{m}_s (h_5 - h_6)$ | kW | Enthalpy drop in ST |
| 10 | HRSG Heat Transfer Rate | $\dot{Q}_{HRSG} = \dot{m}_g \cdot c_{p,g} \cdot (T_4 - T_5)$ | kW | Exhaust gas cooling |
| 11 | HRSG Steam Generation | $\dot{m}_s = \dfrac{\dot{Q}_{HRSG}}{h_{steam} - h_{feedwater}}$ | kg/s | Energy balance |
| 12 | Specific Fuel Consumption | $SFC = \dfrac{3600}{\eta_{th} \cdot CV}$ | kg/kWh | Lower is better |
| 13 | Heat Rate | $HR = \dfrac{3600}{\eta_{th}}$ | kJ/kWh | Reciprocal of efficiency |
| 14 | CO₂ Emission Rate | $E_{CO_2} = \dfrac{44}{12} \cdot \dfrac{SFC}{CV_{fuel}}$ | kg CO₂ / kWh | Stoichiometric factor 44/12 |
| 15 | Optimum Pressure Ratio (Brayton) | $r_{p,opt} = \left(\dfrac{T_3}{T_1}\right)^{0.5 \cdot \gamma/(\gamma-1)}$ | Dimensionless | Maximises net work |
| 16 | Maximum Brayton Efficiency | $\eta_{B,max} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{(r_p)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}}$ | Dimensionless | Cold-air-standard |
| 17 | Pinch Point Condition | $\Delta T_{pinch} = T_{gas,evap} - T_{sat,steam}$ | K or °C | Usually 10–20°C |
| 18 | Fuel Utilisation Factor (Cogeneration) | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{W_{net} + Q_{useful}}{Q_{in}}$ | Dimensionless | > 1 for cogeneration |
| 19 | Cogeneration Efficiency (Combined) | $\eta_{cogen} = \dfrac{W_{net}}{\eta_{th} \cdot Q_{in}} + \dfrac{Q_{useful}}{Q_{in}}$ | Dimensionless | = $\varepsilon$ for cogeneration |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Subscript Convention Used (Important for Exam Answers):**
> - 1 → Compressor inlet (ambient air)
> - 2 → Compressor outlet
> - 3 → Gas turbine inlet (after combustion)
> - 4 → Gas turbine outlet (HRSG inlet)
> - 5 → HRSG outlet (stack)
> - 6 → Steam turbine inlet
> - 7 → Steam turbine outlet
> - 8 → Condenser exit
> - 9 → Pump exit / Economiser inlet

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> **Why KTU tests this topic heavily:**
> 1. It is a *workhorse* technology — over **70%** of new global power capacity in the last decade uses combined cycle.
> 2. It elegantly connects two fundamental cycles (Brayton + Rankine), making it a perfect test of integrated thermodynamic understanding.
> 3. It directly addresses **carbon neutrality** goals, a frequent KTU contemporary context question.

Combined cycle technology is also the basis for:
- **Cogeneration (CHP):** Capturing low-grade exhaust heat for district heating or industrial process steam.
- **Trigeneration (CCHP):** Combined cooling, heating, and power — where the steam turbine exhaust or HRSG heat drives an absorption chiller.
- **Integrated Gasification Combined Cycle (IGCC):** Coal gasification followed by combined cycle.
- **STIG (Steam Injected Gas Turbine):** Steam from HRSG is injected into the gas turbine combustor for additional power augmentation.

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Combined Cycle Thermal Efficiency from First Principles

We will derive the master combined cycle efficiency equation rigorously.

### Step 1: Define the Heat and Work Streams

Let:
- $\dot{Q}_{in}$ = Rate of heat input from fuel combustion (entering the gas turbine system)
- $\dot{Q}_{BT,out}$ = Rate of heat rejected by the gas turbine cycle (entering the HRSG)
- $\dot{Q}_{ST,in}$ = Rate of heat input to the steam cycle (leaving the HRSG, to the steam)
- $\dot{Q}_{ST,out}$ = Rate of heat rejected by the steam cycle (at the condenser)

**Energy balance on the Brayton (gas) cycle:**

$$\dot{Q}_{in} = \dot{W}_{BT,net} + \dot{Q}_{BT,out}$$

**Energy balance on the Rankine (steam) cycle:**

$$\dot{Q}_{ST,in} = \dot{W}_{ST,net} + \dot{Q}_{ST,out}$$

### Step 2: Express Individual Cycle Efficiencies

**Brayton (Gas Turbine) Cycle Efficiency:**

By definition, the gas turbine cycle efficiency is:

$$\eta_{BT} = \frac{\dot{W}_{BT,net}}{\dot{Q}_{in}}$$

The rejected heat is therefore:

$$\dot{Q}_{BT,out} = \dot{Q}_{in} - \dot{W}_{BT,net} = \dot{Q}_{in} (1 - \eta_{BT})$$

### Step 3: Apply the HRSG Energy Balance

The HRSG is a heat exchanger. It transfers the rejected heat from the gas cycle to the steam cycle. In an **ideal HRSG** (no losses to surroundings, no stack losses):

$$\dot{Q}_{ST,in} = \dot{Q}_{BT,out} = \dot{Q}_{in} (1 - \eta_{BT})$$

> **Engineering Reality:** Real HRSGs have stack losses (the exhaust gas is not cooled to ambient — it leaves at ~ 100°C – 150°C), so $\dot{Q}_{ST,in} < \dot{Q}_{BT,out}$ by a small amount. We assume an ideal HRSG for this derivation.

### Step 4: Compute the Work Output of the Steam Cycle

The Rankine cycle's work output is:

$$\dot{W}_{ST,net} = \eta_{ST} \cdot \dot{Q}_{ST,in}$$

Substituting:

$$\dot{W}_{ST,net} = \eta_{ST} \cdot \dot{Q}_{in} (1 - \eta_{BT})$$

### Step 5: Total Net Work Output of the Combined System

The combined cycle's total net work output is the sum:

$$\dot{W}_{CC,net} = \dot{W}_{BT,net} + \dot{W}_{ST,net}$$

Substituting both terms:

$$\dot{W}_{CC,net} = \eta_{BT} \cdot \dot{Q}_{in} + \eta_{ST} \cdot \dot{Q}_{in} (1 - \eta_{BT})$$

Factor out $\dot{Q}_{in}$:

$$\dot{W}_{CC,net} = \dot{Q}_{in} \left[ \eta_{BT} + \eta_{ST} (1 - \eta_{BT}) \right]$$

### Step 6: Define the Combined Cycle Efficiency

By definition:

$$\eta_{CC} = \frac{\dot{W}_{CC,net}}{\dot{Q}_{in}}$$

Therefore:

$$\eta_{CC} = \eta_{BT} + \eta_{ST} (1 - \eta_{BT})$$

### Step 7: Simplify to the Master Equation

Expand the term:

$$\eta_{CC} = \eta_{BT} + \eta_{ST} - \eta_{ST} \cdot \eta_{BT}$$

Rearrange:

$$\boxed{\eta_{CC} = \eta_{BT} + \eta_{ST} - \eta_{BT} \cdot \eta_{ST}}$$

**Equivalent factorised form:**

$$\boxed{\eta_{CC} = 1 - (1 - \eta_{BT})(1 - \eta_{ST})}$$

This is the **KTU High-Yield Master Equation** for combined cycle efficiency.

### Step 8: Sanity Check — Numerical Illustration

Suppose:
- $\eta_{BT} = 0.35$ (35%, typical gas turbine)
- $\eta_{ST} = 0.35$ (35%, typical steam cycle, treated independently)

Using the master equation:

$$\eta_{CC} = 0.35 + 0.35 - (0.35 \times 0.35)$$

$$\eta_{CC} = 0.70 - 0.1225 = 0.5775$$

$$\eta_{CC} = 57.75\%$$

**Interpretation:** Even with mediocre individual cycle efficiencies of 35% each, the combined cycle achieves **57.75%** — almost double the efficiency of either cycle alone. This vividly demonstrates the power of the cascade principle.

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on Practical Efficiencies:** Modern F-class gas turbines have $\eta_{BT} \approx 38\% - 42\%$, and the steam bottoming cycle typically achieves $\eta_{ST} \approx 30\% - 35\%$ (when measured against its own heat input). Plugging these realistic numbers into the master equation gives $\eta_{CC}$ in the **55% – 62%** range, matching commercial CCPP performance.

## 3.2 Derivation: Brayton Cycle Cold-Air-Standard Net Work

The cold-air-standard assumption treats the working fluid as ideal air with constant specific heats.

**Turbine work per unit mass:**

$$w_T = c_p (T_3 - T_4)$$

**Compressor work per unit mass:**

$$w_C = c_p (T_2 - T_1)$$

**Net work per unit mass of air:**

$$w_{net} = c_p \left[ (T_3 - T_4) - (T_2 - T_1) \right]$$

For the isentropic process in the compressor ($1 \to 2s$):

$$T_{2s} = T_1 \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} = T_1 \cdot r_p^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}}$$

For the isentropic process in the turbine ($3 \to 4s$):

$$T_{4s} = T_3 \left(\frac{P_4}{P_3}\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} = T_3 \cdot \left(\frac{1}{r_p}\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}}$$

Therefore:

$$w_{net} = c_p \left[ T_3 - \frac{T_3}{r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}} - T_1 \left( r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1 \right) \right]$$

Simplifying:

$$w_{net} = c_p \cdot T_1 \left[ \frac{T_3}{T_1} - \frac{T_3}{T_1 \cdot r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}} - r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} + 1 \right]$$

Define the temperature ratio $\tau = T_3/T_1$. Then:

$$w_{net} = c_p \cdot T_1 \left[ \tau \left(1 - \frac{1}{r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}}\right) - \left( r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1 \right) \right]$$

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** You will rarely be asked to derive the full Brayton cycle in KTU exams. Focus on plugging values into the basic equations. The derivation above is for deep understanding only.

## 3.3 Derivation: Power Output in Real Units (Numerical Type Problem Template)

**Given** (typical KTU problem):
- Inlet air: $T_1 = 300$ K, $P_1 = 1.0$ bar
- Pressure ratio: $r_p = 12$
- TIT: $T_3 = 1500$ K
- Mass flow rate: $\dot{m}_a = 50$ kg/s
- $\gamma = 1.4$, $c_p = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K
- Gas turbine isentropic efficiency: $\eta_T = 0.90$
- Compressor isentropic efficiency: $\eta_C = 0.88$

**Step 1 — Compressor exit temperature (isentropic):**

$$T_{2s} = T_1 \cdot r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} = 300 \cdot (12)^{0.4/1.4} = 300 \cdot (12)^{0.2857}$$

$$(12)^{0.2857} \approx 2.034$$

$$T_{2s} = 300 \times 2.034 = 610.2 \text{ K}$$

**Step 2 — Compressor exit temperature (actual):**

$$T_2 = T_1 + \frac{T_{2s} - T_1}{\eta_C} = 300 + \frac{610.2 - 300}{0.88}$$

$$T_2 = 300 + \frac{310.2}{0.88} = 300 + 352.5 = 652.5 \text{ K}$$

**Step 3 — Turbine exit temperature (isentropic):**

$$T_{4s} = T_3 \cdot \left(\frac{1}{r_p}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} = 1500 \cdot \frac{1}{2.034}$$

$$T_{4s} = \frac{1500}{2.034} \approx 737.5 \text{ K}$$

**Step 4 — Turbine exit temperature (actual):**

$$T_4 = T_3 - \eta_T (T_3 - T_{4s}) = 1500 - 0.90 \cdot (1500 - 737.5)$$

$$T_4 = 1500 - 0.90 \cdot 762.5 = 1500 - 686.25 = 813.75 \text{ K}$$

**Step 5 — Net work per kg of air:**

$$w_{net} = c_p \left[ (T_3 - T_4) - (T_2 - T_1) \right]$$

$$w_{net} = 1.005 \left[ (1500 - 813.75) - (652.5 - 300) \right]$$

$$w_{net} = 1.005 \left[ 686.25 - 352.5 \right] = 1.005 \times 333.75$$

$$w_{net} = 335.42 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 6 — Net power output of gas turbine:**

$$P_{GT,net} = \dot{m}_a \cdot w_{net} = 50 \times 335.42 = 16,771 \text{ kW} \approx 16.77 \text{ MW}$$

> This template is directly applicable to most KTU numerical questions on this topic. Memorise the flow: **Compressor** (T₁→T₂) → **Combustor** (T₂→T₃) → **Turbine** (T₃→T₄) → **HRSG** (T₄→T₅) → **Steam cycle**.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Variants of Combined Cycle Plants

| Variant | Configuration | Key Feature | Typical η_Cc | Use Case |
|---------|---------------|-------------|-------------|----------|
| **CCGT (NGCC)** | GT + HRSG + ST | Standard natural gas combined cycle | 55–62% | Base/peak load power |
| **STIG** | GT + Steam injection | Steam injected into combustor | 45–50% | Power augmentation |
| **Cheng Cycle** | GT + Steam-cooled blades | Recovers cooling air heat | +2–3% over base | High-TIT operation |
| **Kalina Cycle** | GT + Binary fluid (NH₃-H₂O) | Variable boiling mixture | 50–55% | Low-temp heat recovery |
| **Cogeneration (CHP)** | CCPP + Heat extraction | Useful thermal output | 75–90% (ε) | District heating, industry |
| **Trigeneration (CCHP)** | CCPP + Absorption chiller | Electricity + cooling + heat | 70–85% (ε) | Hospitals, malls, IT parks |
| **IGCC** | Coal gasifier + CCPP | Gasifies coal to syngas | 42–48% | Coal with low emissions |
| **Triple-Pressure HRSG CCPP** | GT + 3-pressure HRSG + ST | Better heat recovery | 58–62% | Latest F-class & G-class |

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Combined Cycle Power Plant

The following Mermaid block diagram represents the complete functional flow of energy, mass, and electricity through a modern NGCC plant.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Ambient Air<br/>T1 298 K<br/>P1 1.013 bar] --> A2[AXIAL COMPRESSOR<br/>Multistage<br/>Pressure Ratio 10 to 25]
    A2 -- Compressed Air T2 600 to 700 K --> A3[COMBUSTION CHAMBER<br/>Natural Gas Fuel<br/>TIT 1300 to 1500 C]
    A3 -- Hot Combustion Gases T3 --> A4[GAS TURBINE<br/>3 to 4 Stages<br/>Isentropic Expansion]
    A4 -- GT Exhaust T4 500 to 620 C --> A5[HRSG<br/>3 Sections Economiser Evaporator Superheater]
    A4 -- Mechanical Shaft --> A6[GT GENERATOR<br/>65 to 70 percent of total output]
    A5 -- High Pressure Steam 540 to 565 C --> A7[STEAM TURBINE<br/>HP IP LP Stages<br/>Condensing Type]
    A7 -- Mechanical Shaft --> A8[ST GENERATOR<br/>30 to 35 percent of total output]
    A7 -- Low Pressure Steam --> A9[CONDENSER<br/>Shell and Tube<br/>Cooling Water]
    A9 -- Condensate --> A10[FEEDWATER PUMP<br/>Booster plus Main Pump]
    A10 -- Pressurised Feedwater --> A5
    A5 -- Cooled Flue Gas 100 to 150 C --> A11[STACK<br/>Atmospheric Discharge]
    F1[Natural Gas Fuel<br/>Pipeline Supply] --> A3
    CW1[Cooling Tower Water<br/>30 C Inlet] --> A9
    A9 -- Warm Water 40 C --> CW2[Cooling Tower]
    CW2 --> CW1
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Cascade Energy Flow with State Points

This diagram emphasises the **state numbers** commonly used in textbooks and KTU board questions. Memorise the numbering convention.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SP1[State 1 Compressor Inlet<br/>Ambient Air T1] --> COMP[COMPRESSOR<br/>Process 1 to 2 Isentropic]
    COMP --> SP2[State 2 Compressor Outlet<br/>High Pressure Air T2]
    SP2 --> CC[COMBUSTION CHAMBER<br/>Process 2 to 3 Constant Pressure]
    CC --> SP3[State 3 Turbine Inlet<br/>TIT Combustion Products T3]
    SP3 --> GT[GAS TURBINE<br/>Process 3 to 4 Isentropic Expansion]
    GT --> SP4[State 4 HRSG Inlet<br/>GT Exhaust T4]
    GT -.-> WBT[Work Output of Brayton Cycle]
    SP4 --> HRSG[HEAT RECOVERY STEAM GENERATOR<br/>Process 4 to 5 Constant Pressure]
    HRSG --> SP5[State 5 HRSG Outlet<br/>Stack Gas T5]
    HRSG --> SP6[State 6 Steam Turbine Inlet<br/>Superheated Steam T6]
    SP6 --> ST[STEAM TURBINE<br/>Process 6 to 7 Isentropic Expansion]
    ST --> SP7[State 7 Steam Turbine Outlet<br/>Wet Steam T7]
    ST -.-> WST[Work Output of Rankine Cycle]
    SP7 --> COND[CONDENSER<br/>Process 7 to 8 Constant Pressure]
    COND --> SP8[State 8 Condensate<br/>Saturated Water T8]
    SP8 --> PUMP[PUMP<br/>Process 8 to 9 Isentropic Compression]
    PUMP --> SP9[State 9 Economiser Inlet<br/>Compressed Liquid T9]
    SP9 --> HRSG
```

## 4.3 Schematic Block Diagram: Comparison of Topping vs Bottoming Cycles

This diagram clarifies the **topping** and **bottoming** cycle concepts, which are commonly tested.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TOPP[BRAYTON TOPPING CYCLE]
        direction LR
        TOP1[Fuel Combustion] --> TOP2[High Temperature Heat Input]
        TOP2 --> TOP3[High Grade Work Output]
        TOP3 --> TOP4[Moderate Temperature Exhaust Heat]
    end
    subgraph BOTTOM[RANKINE BOTTOMING CYCLE]
        direction LR
        BOT1[Exhaust Heat to HRSG] --> BOT2[Steam Generation]
        BOT2 --> BOT3[Lower Grade Work Output]
        BOT3 --> BOT4[Low Temperature Condenser Rejection]
    end
    TOP4 ==> BOT1
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examination Note:** A **topping cycle** is the high-temperature cycle that produces work *first* (Brayton in CCPP). A **bottoming cycle** uses the *waste heat* of the topping cycle to produce additional work (Rankine in CCPP). KTU frequently asks the student to identify which cycle is the topping and which is the bottoming in a given configuration.

## 4.4 Equipment Layout (Text Schematic for Spatial Understanding)

For visualising plant layout, the standard arrangement is:

```
[ GT Hall ]   [ HRSG ]   [ ST Hall ]   [ Condenser ]   [ Cooling Tower ]
   G1          H1          S1             C1               CT1
   |           |           |               |                |
   +---- Generator G1 -----+                |                |
                                               +-- Water Return --+
```

The gas turbine is followed by the HRSG (vertical), which is followed by the steam turbine, condenser, and cooling tower. This linear arrangement is the industry standard for single-shaft CCPPs.
