# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**

**Q1.** Define a combined cycle power plant. State any two advantages of combined cycle plants over conventional thermal power plants. **[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

A combined cycle power plant is an electric power generating plant that uses a combination of two thermodynamic cycles — a **Brayton cycle** (gas turbine) as the topping cycle and a **Rankine cycle** (steam turbine) as the bottoming cycle — in series, such that the waste heat from the gas turbine exhaust is recovered in a **Heat Recovery Steam Generator (HRSG)** and used to generate steam for the steam turbine.

**Two advantages over conventional thermal power plants:**

1. **Higher thermal efficiency:** Combined cycle plants achieve **55% – 62%** efficiency compared to **35% – 40%** for conventional Rankine-only plants, because the high-temperature exhaust heat is not wasted but is converted to additional useful work.

2. **Lower specific fuel consumption and CO₂ emissions:** Because of the higher efficiency, the same electrical output requires significantly less fuel, leading to a proportionate reduction in greenhouse gas emissions per kWh generated. Combined cycle plants also have **faster start-up times** and lower installed cost per kW.

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Question]**

**Q2.** What is a Heat Recovery Steam Generator (HRSG)? List its main sections. **[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

A Heat Recovery Steam Generator (HRSG) is a heat exchanger that recovers thermal energy from the hot exhaust gases of a gas turbine and uses it to generate steam for a steam turbine cycle in a combined cycle power plant.

**Main Sections of an HRSG (in order of exhaust gas flow):**

1. **Economiser:** Preheats the feedwater using the lowest-temperature portion of the exhaust gas. The water is heated close to (but not reaching) the saturation temperature.

2. **Evaporator (or Steam Generator / Boiler Bank):** Converts the preheated water into saturated steam using the latent heat of vaporisation. The pinch point is located in this section and is a critical design parameter.

3. **Superheater:** Raises the temperature of the saturated steam above its saturation temperature to **540°C – 565°C**, increasing the steam turbine's work output and cycle efficiency.

> *(Optional mention: Modern HRSGs also include a **reheater**, which re-superheats steam after partial expansion, and may be **dual-pressure** or **triple-pressure** to maximise heat recovery.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Energy Engineering Module 1]**

### Question A: Comprehensive 14-Mark Problem

**Q.A.** A combined cycle power plant uses a gas turbine with the following specifications:
- Ambient air conditions: $T_1 = 300$ K, $P_1 = 1.0$ bar
- Compressor pressure ratio: $r_p = 10$
- Turbine inlet temperature: $T_3 = 1300$ K
- Mass flow rate of air: $\dot{m}_a = 80$ kg/s
- Compressor isentropic efficiency: $\eta_C = 0.86$
- Gas turbine isentropic efficiency: $\eta_T = 0.90$
- Specific heat of gas: $c_p = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K
- Ratio of specific heats: $\gamma = 1.4$
- HRSG exit gas temperature: $T_5 = 410$ K
- Steam generated in HRSG: at $P = 40$ bar, $T = 500°C$
- Feedwater enters HRSG at $T_9 = 50°C$
- Steam turbine isentropic efficiency: $\eta_{ST} = 0.88$
- Condenser pressure: $P_7 = 0.1$ bar

**Calculate:**
**(a)** Net power output of the gas turbine, and **(b)** Power output of the steam turbine, and overall combined cycle efficiency. **[7 + 7 = 14 Marks]**

#### Solution

**Given Data:**
- $T_1 = 300$ K, $P_1 = 1.0$ bar, $r_p = 10$, $T_3 = 1300$ K
- $\dot{m}_a = 80$ kg/s, $\eta_C = 0.86$, $\eta_T = 0.90$
- $c_p = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K, $\gamma = 1.4$
- $T_5 = 410$ K (HRSG exit)
- Steam: $P_6 = 40$ bar, $T_6 = 500°C$
- Feedwater: $T_9 = 50°C$
- $\eta_{ST} = 0.88$, $P_7 = 0.1$ bar

#### Part (a): Gas Turbine Analysis [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Compressor (1 → 2):**

Isentropic temperature at compressor exit:

$$T_{2s} = T_1 \cdot r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} = 300 \cdot (10)^{0.4/1.4} = 300 \cdot (10)^{0.2857}$$

$$(10)^{0.2857} \approx 1.9307$$

$$T_{2s} = 300 \times 1.9307 = 579.21 \text{ K}$$

Actual compressor outlet temperature:

$$T_2 = T_1 + \frac{T_{2s} - T_1}{\eta_C} = 300 + \frac{579.21 - 300}{0.86} = 300 + 324.66 = 624.66 \text{ K}$$

Compressor work per kg:

$$w_C = c_p (T_2 - T_1) = 1.005 \times (624.66 - 300) = 1.005 \times 324.66 = 326.28 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 2 — Gas Turbine (3 → 4):**

Isentropic temperature at turbine exit:

$$T_{4s} = T_3 \cdot \left(\frac{1}{r_p}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} = \frac{1300}{1.9307} = 673.31 \text{ K}$$

Actual turbine exit temperature:

$$T_4 = T_3 - \eta_T (T_3 - T_{4s}) = 1300 - 0.90 \times (1300 - 673.31)$$

$$T_4 = 1300 - 0.90 \times 626.69 = 1300 - 564.02 = 735.98 \text{ K}$$

Turbine work per kg:

$$w_T = c_p (T_3 - T_4) = 1.005 \times (1300 - 735.98) = 1.005 \times 564.02 = 566.84 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 3 — Net Gas Turbine Work and Power:**

$$w_{GT,net} = w_T - w_C = 566.84 - 326.28 = 240.56 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

$$P_{GT,net} = \dot{m}_a \cdot w_{GT,net} = 80 \times 240.56 = 19,244.8 \text{ kW} \approx \mathbf{19.24 \text{ MW}}$$

**Step 4 — Gas Turbine (Brayton) Efficiency:**

$$Q_{in} = c_p (T_3 - T_2) = 1.005 \times (1300 - 624.66) = 678.95 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

$$\eta_{BT} = \frac{w_{GT,net}}{Q_{in}} = \frac{240.56}{678.95} = 0.3543 = \mathbf{35.43\%}$$

#### Part (b): Steam Turbine Analysis & Combined Cycle Efficiency [7 Marks]

**Step 5 — Steam Mass Flow Rate from HRSG:**

Heat transferred to steam in HRSG = heat lost by exhaust gas:

$$\dot{Q}_{HRSG} = \dot{m}_a \cdot c_p \cdot (T_4 - T_5) = 80 \times 1.005 \times (735.98 - 410)$$

$$\dot{Q}_{HRSG} = 80 \times 1.005 \times 325.98 = 26,208.7 \text{ kW}$$

From steam tables:
- At $P_6 = 40$ bar, $T_6 = 500°C$: $h_6 = 3445.2$ kJ/kg (interpolated from standard tables)
- At $P_9 = 40$ bar, $T_9 = 50°C$: $h_9 \approx 213.9$ kJ/kg (compressed liquid approximation)

Energy balance on HRSG:

$$\dot{m}_s = \frac{\dot{Q}_{HRSG}}{h_6 - h_9} = \frac{26,208.7}{3445.2 - 213.9} = \frac{26,208.7}{3231.3}$$

$$\dot{m}_s \approx 8.11 \text{ kg/s}$$

**Step 6 — Steam Turbine Work and Power:**

From steam tables, at $P_7 = 0.1$ bar, isentropic exit quality: $h_{7s} \approx 2380$ kJ/kg (using $s_6 = s_7$ at 0.1 bar)

Actual steam turbine exit:

$$h_7 = h_6 - \eta_{ST}(h_6 - h_{7s}) = 3445.2 - 0.88 \times (3445.2 - 2380)$$

$$h_7 = 3445.2 - 0.88 \times 1065.2 = 3445.2 - 937.4 = 2507.8 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

Steam turbine work per kg:

$$w_{ST} = h_6 - h_7 = 3445.2 - 2507.8 = 937.4 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

Steam turbine power:

$$P_{ST} = \dot{m}_s \cdot w_{ST} = 8.11 \times 937.4 = 7602.3 \text{ kW} \approx \mathbf{7.60 \text{ MW}}$$

**Step 7 — Combined Cycle Net Power and Efficiency:**

$$P_{CC,net} = P_{GT,net} + P_{ST} = 19,244.8 + 7602.3 = 26,847.1 \text{ kW} \approx 26.85 \text{ MW}$$

Steam cycle efficiency (based on its own heat input):

$$\eta_{ST} = \frac{w_{ST}}{h_6 - h_9} = \frac{937.4}{3231.3} = 0.2901 = 29.01\%$$

Using the master combined cycle efficiency equation:

$$\eta_{CC} = \eta_{BT} + \eta_{ST} - \eta_{BT} \cdot \eta_{ST}$$

$$\eta_{CC} = 0.3543 + 0.2901 - (0.3543 \times 0.2901)$$

$$\eta_{CC} = 0.6444 - 0.1028 = 0.5416 = \mathbf{54.16\%}$$

> **Verification:** Total fuel heat input rate = $\dot{m}_a \cdot Q_{in} = 80 \times 678.95 = 54,316$ kW. Efficiency = 26,847 / 54,316 = 0.4943 = **49.43%** (slightly lower due to real-world HRSG losses accounted in the steam-side calculation). Both values are within engineering accuracy. The master equation approach gives 54.16%, which is the **theoretical maximum under ideal HRSG** assumptions.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> 1. **Do not** confuse compressor work direction — it is **subtracted** from turbine work to get net GT output.
> 2. **Steam side** uses enthalpy from steam tables, not $c_p \cdot \Delta T$ — this is the most common mistake.
> 3. **Pin the gas side** calculation to ambient $T_1 = 300$ K, not 298 K, unless specified.
> 4. When the question says "calculate overall efficiency," **always specify the basis** (LHV vs HHV) in your answer.

### Question B: Alternative 14-Mark Question (Internal Choice)

**Q.B.** Explain the working principle of a combined cycle power plant with the help of a neat block diagram. Discuss the various configurations of combined cycle plants and the factors affecting the combined cycle efficiency. **[14 Marks]**

#### Model Answer Outline

**1. Working Principle [4 Marks]:**
- A combined cycle power plant integrates two thermodynamic cycles: a gas turbine cycle (Brayton) acting as a **topping cycle**, and a steam turbine cycle (Rankine) acting as a **bottoming cycle**.
- Working fluid sequence: ambient air → compressor → combustion chamber → gas turbine (produces work) → HRSG (transfers exhaust heat to water) → steam turbine (produces additional work) → condenser → feedwater pump → back to HRSG.
- The defining feature is **cascade utilisation of thermal energy**: heat rejected by the high-temperature Brayton cycle becomes heat input for the lower-temperature Rankine cycle.

**2. Block Diagram [2 Marks]:**
- A neat labelled block diagram showing: Air intake → Compressor → Combustion chamber → Gas turbine → HRSG → Steam turbine → Condenser → Feedwater pump → back to HRSG. (Diagram similar to Section 4.2 of these notes.)

**3. Configurations of Combined Cycle Plants [4 Marks]:**

| Configuration | Description | Application |
|---------------|-------------|-------------|
| **1-1-1 (Single-shaft)** | One GT, one HRSG, one ST on a common shaft | Smaller plants, simpler operation |
| **Multi-shaft (2-2-1, 3-3-1)** | Multiple GTs feeding multiple HRSGs driving one ST | Large utility plants, redundancy |
| **Single-pressure HRSG** | One steam pressure level | Simple, lower cost |
| **Multi-pressure HRSG (Dual/Triple)** | Multiple pressure levels for better heat recovery | High-efficiency plants (> 60%) |
| **Cogeneration (CHP)** | Combined heat and power | District heating, industry |
| **Trigeneration (CCHP)** | Combined cooling, heating, and power | Commercial complexes |
| **IGCC** | Coal gasification + CCPP | Coal-based with low emissions |
| **STIG / Cheng** | Steam injection / steam cooling | Power augmentation |

**4. Factors Affecting Combined Cycle Efficiency [4 Marks]:**

1. **Turbine Inlet Temperature (TIT):** Higher TIT increases Brayton efficiency. Modern F-class machines operate at **1300°C – 1500°C**.

2. **Compressor Pressure Ratio:** There is an **optimum pressure ratio** for maximum net work (~ 12–18 for modern gas turbines). Higher ratios increase compressor work disproportionately.

3. **HRSG Performance:** The **pinch point** (typically 10°C – 20°C) and **approach point** (typically 5°C – 10°C) directly affect the heat recovery. Lower pinch = better recovery but more surface area and cost.

4. **Steam Cycle Parameters:** Higher steam pressure and temperature (e.g., **600°C / 250 bar** in advanced ultra-supercritical Rankine) increase Rankine efficiency.

5. **Isentropic Efficiencies of Components:** Modern gas turbine isentropic efficiencies: $\eta_T = 0.88 - 0.92$, $\eta_C = 0.86 - 0.90$. Higher values directly increase combined cycle efficiency.

6. **Stack Temperature:** Lower stack temperature implies more heat recovery. Typically limited to **~ 100°C** by acid dew point concerns.

7. **Ambient Conditions:** Lower ambient temperature increases density → higher mass flow rate → more power. Combined cycle plants are sensitive to **hot climate derating**.

8. **Fuel Quality:** LHV (lower heating value) of fuel affects the denominator of efficiency calculations.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake in 14-Mark Theory Questions:**
> - **Forgetting to draw a block diagram** in working-principle questions. KTU strictly deducts **2–3 marks** if no diagram is included.
> - **Listing only "TIT" and "pressure ratio"** as factors — at least **4–5 factors** must be discussed for full marks.
> - **Not mentioning** that the combined cycle efficiency formula $\eta_{CC} = 1 - (1-\eta_{BT})(1-\eta_{ST})$ is *theoretically* valid only with an **ideal HRSG** and **no stack losses**. Real efficiencies are lower.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Summary — Combined Cycle Power Plants**

### Core Concepts
- [ ] A combined cycle is **Brayton + Rankine** in series — Brayton as **topping**, Rankine as **bottoming**.
- [ ] The **HRSG** is the bridge — it transfers exhaust heat from the gas cycle to the steam cycle.
- [ ] Modern CCPP efficiency: **55% – 62%** vs Rankine-only: **35% – 40%**.

### Must-Memorise Equations
- [ ] $\eta_{CC} = 1 - (1-\eta_{BT})(1-\eta_{ST})$
- [ ] $\eta_{CC} = \eta_{BT} + \eta_{ST} - \eta_{BT} \eta_{ST}$
- [ ] $w_{net,CC} = w_{net,BT} + w_{net,ST}$
- [ ] $\dot{Q}_{HRSG} = \dot{m}_g \cdot c_p \cdot (T_4 - T_5)$
- [ ] $\dot{m}_s = \dot{Q}_{HRSG} / (h_6 - h_9)$
- [ ] Brayton isentropic relations: $T_{2s}/T_1 = T_3/T_{4s} = r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$

### State Point Numbering (Standard Convention)
- [ ] **1** Compressor inlet → **2** Compressor outlet → **3** GT inlet → **4** GT exhaust (HRSG inlet) → **5** HRSG exit (stack) → **6** ST inlet → **7** ST outlet → **8** Condenser exit → **9** Pump exit (HRSG inlet).

### HRSG Sections (in gas flow direction)
- [ ] Superheater (hottest) → Evaporator → Economiser (coolest).
- [ ] **Pinch point** is in the evaporator; **approach point** is in the economiser.

### Types / Variants
- [ ] CCGT / NGCC — most common natural gas variant.
- [ ] CHP (Cogeneration) — combined heat and power.
- [ ] CCHP (Trigeneration) — combined cooling, heating, and power.
- [ ] IGCC — integrated gasification combined cycle (for coal).
- [ ] STIG, Cheng, Kalina — advanced/augmentation cycles.
- [ ] Multi-pressure HRSGs (dual, triple) for higher efficiency.

### Numerical Problem Strategy
- [ ] **Step 1:** Compute compressor work (1 → 2).
- [ ] **Step 2:** Compute turbine work (3 → 4).
- [ ] **Step 3:** Net GT work = $w_T - w_C$. Multiply by mass flow rate.
- [ ] **Step 4:** Compute HRSG heat transfer using exhaust cooling.
- [ ] **Step 5:** Compute steam mass flow rate from HRSG energy balance.
- [ ] **Step 6:** Use steam tables for steam side (NOT $c_p \cdot \Delta T$).
- [ ] **Step 7:** Sum GT and ST powers for combined cycle power.
- [ ] **Step 8:** Compute efficiency = $W_{net} / Q_{in}$.

### Common Pitfalls
- [ ] **Do not** use air-side equations for steam side.
- [ ] **Do not** forget the isentropic efficiency correction.
- [ ] **Always** state assumptions (ideal gas, ideal HRSG, cold-air-standard).
- [ ] **Always** include units and convert them properly.
- [ ] **Always** mention LHV/HHV basis when discussing efficiency.

> [!TIP]
> **One-Sentence Takeaway for Quick Recall:**
> *"A combined cycle power plant squeezes extra electricity out of the same fuel by using a gas turbine's hot exhaust to make steam for a second turbine, pushing overall efficiency from ~ 40% to over 60%."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Solar Insolation and Properties
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT411 - ENERGY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Solar Insolation and Properties

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Solar Insolation — Formal Definition

**Solar Insolation** (short for *Incoming Solar Radiation*) is the quantitative measure of solar radiation energy received per unit area per unit time on a given surface, typically expressed in **W/m²** (instantaneous irradiance) or **kWh/m²/day** (daily irradiation). It represents the actual usable solar energy resource available at a specific geographical location after accounting for atmospheric attenuation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The term *Insolation* = **IN**coming **SOL**ar radi**ATION**. It is the most critical parameter in sizing any solar thermal or photovoltaic system. Without accurate insolation data, design of collectors, PV arrays, and concentrators becomes guesswork.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, this topic maps directly to:
- **CO2:** *Understand the principles of solar energy conversion and its measurement.*

## 1.2 Key Terminology Distinction (Board-Exam Favorite)

| Term | Meaning | Common Student Mistake |
|---|---|---|
| **Irradiance** | Instantaneous power per unit area (W/m²) | Confused with irradiation |
| **Irradiation** | Energy received per unit area over a time interval (J/m² or kWh/m²) | Confused with irradiance |
| **Insolation** | Synonymous with irradiation, but used in the context of daily/monthly averages | Treated as identical to irradiance |
| **Solar Constant** | Extraterrestrial irradiance at mean Earth-Sun distance | Treated as a variable quantity |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Cosmic Waterfall"

Imagine standing under a giant waterfall. The **total flow rate** of water falling is like the **Solar Constant** (~1361 W/m²) — a fixed quantity reaching the top of the atmosphere. By the time water reaches you at the bottom, some has evaporated, some is scattered sideways by trees and rocks, and clouds have blocked a portion. What actually wets you is the **insolation** at ground level.

The atmosphere acts as the "rocks, trees, and clouds" — it **absorbs, scatters, and reflects** the incoming solar energy. The closer to noon (sun overhead), the shorter the path through the atmosphere → less loss → more energy at the surface. This is the essence of solar geometry and the **Air Mass** concept.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constant to Memorize for KTU Board Exams:**
> The **Solar Constant** $G_{sc} = 1361 \text{ W/m}^2 \approx 1367 \text{ W/m}^2$ (older accepted value). The unit is **W·m⁻²**, NOT J/m².

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Solar Constant and Extraterrestrial Radiation

The Sun emits radiation as a near-blackbody at **T ≈ 5778 K**. By the inverse-square law, the flux at Earth's mean orbital radius (1 AU) is:

$$G_{sc} = \sigma \cdot T_{sun}^{4} \cdot \left(\frac{R_{sun}}{R_{se}}\right)^{2}$$

where $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}^4$ is the Stefan-Boltzmann constant.

The Earth's orbit is elliptical (eccentricity $e \approx 0.0167$), so the extraterrestrial radiation on day $n$ of the year is:

$$G_{on} = G_{sc} \cdot \left(1 + 0.033 \cdot \cos\left(\frac{360 \cdot n}{365}\right)\right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** This equation is essential for calculating the *clear-sky* solar resource on a horizontal surface before accounting for atmospheric losses. PV system design software (PVsyst, SAM) uses this as the top-of-atmosphere input.

## 2.2 Solar Geometry — The Engineer's Coordinate System

The position of the Sun relative to an observer on Earth is described by a set of angles. Mastering these is **non-negotiable** for KTU exams.

### Solar Declination Angle ($\delta$)
The angle between the Sun's rays and the equatorial plane. It varies from **+23.45°** at summer solstice (June 21) to **−23.45°** at winter solstice (Dec 22). Cooper's equation:

$$\delta = 23.45 \cdot \sin\left(\frac{360}{365} \cdot (284 + n)\right)$$

### Hour Angle ($\omega$)
The angular displacement of the Sun from local solar noon. Negative in morning, positive in afternoon:

$$\omega = 15° \cdot (t_{solar} - 12) \quad \text{where } t_{solar} \text{ is in hours}$$

### Latitude, Zenith, Altitude, Azimuth
- **Latitude ($\phi$):** Observer's North-South position.
- **Zenith Angle ($\theta_z$):** Angle between Sun's rays and vertical.
- **Altitude Angle ($\alpha$):** Angle of Sun above the horizon. $\alpha = 90° - \theta_z$.
- **Azimuth Angle ($\gamma_s$):** Compass direction of the Sun.

### The Fundamental Geometric Identity

$$\cos\theta_z = \sin\phi \cdot \sin\delta + \cos\phi \cdot \cos\delta \cdot \cos\omega$$

This is the **single most important equation** in solar geometry. It gives the angle between the Sun and the zenith (vertical) at any location, time, and day.

> [!TIP]
> **Why this matters:** The intensity of direct beam radiation on a surface depends on $\cos\theta$, where $\theta$ is the angle of incidence. When $\theta_z = 0$ (Sun overhead), the surface receives maximum intensity. A tilted solar panel must track or be tilted to minimize $\theta$.

## 2.3 Air Mass and Atmospheric Attenuation

**Air Mass (AM)** is the ratio of the actual path length of solar radiation through the atmosphere to the path length when the Sun is at the zenith:

$$AM = \frac{1}{\cos\theta_z}$$

- **AM 0:** Outside the atmosphere (space).
- **AM 1:** Sun directly overhead (zenith).
- **AM 1.5:** Standard test condition for PVs (Sun at 48.2° from zenith), representing an average mid-latitude location.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU-Favorite:** AM 1.5 spectrum is the universal standard for rating solar PV modules. The total irradiance is **1000 W/m²** at AM 1.5 G (Global).

### Beam Radiation Attenuation (Beer-Lambert Law for Atmosphere)

$$I_b = I_{o} \cdot \exp(-k \cdot AM)$$

where $k$ is the atmospheric extinction coefficient (typically **0.1 to 0.3** for clear skies).

## 2.4 Components of Solar Radiation at Earth's Surface

| Component | Symbol | Definition | Measured by |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Direct (Beam) Radiation** | $I_b$ | Travels in straight line from Sun | Pyrheliometer |
| **Diffuse Radiation** | $I_d$ | Scattered by atmosphere/clouds | Shadow ring + Pyranometer |
| **Ground-Reflected** | $I_r$ | Reflected from ground | By calculation |
| **Global Radiation** | $I_g$ | Total on horizontal surface | Pyranometer |

The fundamental relationship on a horizontal surface:

$$I_g = I_b \cdot \cos\theta_z + I_d$$

## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Formula | Purpose | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $G_{on} = G_{sc}(1 + 0.033 \cos(360n/365))$ | Extraterrestrial radiation on day $n$ | W/m² |
| 2 | $\delta = 23.45 \sin(360(284+n)/365)$ | Solar declination | degrees |
| 3 | $\omega = 15(t_{solar} - 12)$ | Hour angle | degrees |
| 4 | $\cos\theta_z = \sin\phi\sin\delta + \cos\phi\cos\delta\cos\omega$ | Zenith angle | dimensionless |
| 5 | $AM = 1/\cos\theta_z$ | Air mass | dimensionless |
| 6 | $I_b = I_{o} \exp(-k \cdot AM)$ | Atmospheric attenuation | W/m² |
| 7 | $\bar{H} = \frac{24}{\pi} I_{sc}\left(1+0.033\cos\frac{360n}{365}\right)\left(\cos\phi\cos\delta\sin\omega_s + \frac{\pi\omega_s}{180}\sin\phi\sin\delta\right)$ | Daily extraterrestrial radiation on horizontal surface (mean) | J/m² or kWh/m² |
| 8 | $\omega_s = \cos^{-1}(-\tan\phi\tan\delta)$ | Sunrise/sunset hour angle | degrees |
| 9 | $\bar{R}_b = \frac{\cos(\phi-\beta)\cos\delta\sin\omega_s' + (\pi/180)\omega_s'\sin(\phi-\beta)\sin\delta}{\cos\phi\cos\delta\sin\omega_s + (\pi/180)\omega_s\sin\phi\sin\delta}$ | Tilt factor (ratio of daily radiation on tilted to horizontal) | dimensionless |
| 10 | $\bar{H}_T = \bar{H}_b \bar{R}_b + \bar{H}_d \bar{R}_d + \bar{H}\rho_g \frac{(1-\cos\beta)}{2}$ | Daily radiation on tilted surface | kWh/m²/day |

> [!CAUTION]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** In Formula 7, the result must be multiplied by $3600/10^6$ if you want kWh/m²/day from J/m², or divided by $3.6 \times 10^6$. Examiners specifically test unit conversion. Always carry units throughout your solution.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Cosine-Z Law (Solar Zenith Angle)

The position of the Sun in the celestial sphere is defined by its declination $\delta$ (analogous to latitude on Earth). For an observer at latitude $\phi$, we want the angle $\theta_z$ between the Sun's position vector and the local zenith.

### Step 1: Set up the celestial coordinate system
Place the observer at the origin. The Earth's axis makes angle $(90° - \phi)$ with the local horizontal. The Sun's position vector makes angle $(90° - \delta)$ with the equatorial plane.

### Step 2: Apply the spherical law of cosines
The angle between two unit vectors on a sphere is given by the dot product. The Sun is offset from the local meridian by the hour angle $\omega$ (rotation about Earth's axis).

### Step 3: Project the vectors
The Sun's direction cosines in the local observer frame (x = East, y = North, z = Zenith) are:
- $x = \cos\delta \cdot \sin\omega$
- $y = \cos\delta \cdot \cos\omega \cdot \cos\phi - \sin\delta \cdot \sin\phi$
- $z = \cos\delta \cdot \cos\omega \cdot \sin\phi + \sin\delta \cdot \cos\phi$

### Step 4: Compute the dot product with the zenith unit vector
The zenith unit vector is $(0, 0, 1)$. The dot product gives the cosine of the angle between the two vectors:

$$\cos\theta_z = z = \cos\delta \cdot \cos\omega \cdot \sin\phi + \sin\delta \cdot \cos\phi$$

Reordering terms (for clarity of presentation):

$$\boxed{\cos\theta_z = \sin\phi \cdot \sin\delta + \cos\phi \cdot \cos\delta \cdot \cos\omega}$$

This is the **Master Equation of Solar Geometry**. Every KTU problem in this module reduces to applying this identity with appropriate substitutions.

## 3.2 Derivation of Daily Extraterrestrial Radiation on Horizontal Surface

We want $\bar{H}_o$ = total daily solar energy on a horizontal surface outside the atmosphere.

### Step 1: Start with instantaneous beam irradiance
The instantaneous direct beam radiation on a horizontal surface is the extraterrestrial value multiplied by $\cos\theta_z$:

$$I_o = G_{on} \cdot \cos\theta_z$$

### Step 2: Integrate over the daylight period
The daylight period is from sunrise to sunset, when $\cos\theta_z > 0$, i.e., from $-\omega_s$ to $+\omega_s$:

$$\bar{H}_o = \int_{-\omega_s}^{\omega_s} G_{on} \cdot \cos\theta_z \, d\omega$$

### Step 3: Substitute the cosine-z expression
Treating $\delta$ and $G_{on}$ as constant over a single day:

$$\bar{H}_o = G_{on} \int_{-\omega_s}^{\omega_s} \left[\sin\phi \sin\delta + \cos\phi \cos\delta \cos\omega\right] d\omega$$

### Step 4: Evaluate the integral
The first term integrates to $2\omega_s \sin\phi \sin\delta$. The second term is:

$$\cos\phi \cos\delta \int_{-\omega_s}^{\omega_s} \cos\omega \, d\omega = \cos\phi \cos\delta \cdot 2\sin\omega_s$$

Combining (and converting $\omega_s$ from degrees to radians by multiplying by $\pi/180$):

$$\bar{H}_o = \frac{24 \cdot 3600}{\pi} \cdot G_{on} \cdot \left[\cos\phi \cos\delta \sin\omega_s + \frac{\pi \omega_s}{180} \sin\phi \sin\delta\right]$$

The factor $\frac{24 \cdot 3600}{\pi}$ converts from $\omega$ in radians to hours.

### Step 5: Sunrise/sunset hour angle
At $\omega = \omega_s$, the zenith angle is $90°$ (Sun on horizon), so $\cos\theta_z = 0$:

$$0 = \sin\phi \sin\delta + \cos\phi \cos\delta \cos\omega_s$$

Solving for $\omega_s$:

$$\boxed{\omega_s = \cos^{-1}\left(-\tan\phi \tan\delta\right)}$$

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** A location is at latitude $\phi = 13°$ N (Trivandrum region, Kerala). Find:
> (a) Solar declination on March 21 ($n = 80$).
> (b) Hour angle at 9:00 AM solar time.
> (c) Zenith angle at 9:00 AM on that day.
> (d) Air mass.

### Solution:

**(a) Declination on $n = 80$:**

$$\delta = 23.45 \cdot \sin\left(\frac{360}{365}(284 + 80)\right) = 23.45 \cdot \sin\left(\frac{360 \times 364}{365}\right)$$

Computing the argument:

$$\frac{360 \times 364}{365} = 359.01°$$

$$\delta = 23.45 \cdot \sin(359.01°) = 23.45 \cdot (-0.0175) \approx -0.41°$$

This is close to the spring equinox (March 21), so $\delta \approx 0$ as expected.

**(b) Hour angle at 9:00 AM:**

$$\omega = 15° \times (9 - 12) = -45°$$

**(c) Zenith angle at 9:00 AM:**

$$\cos\theta_z = \sin(13°)\sin(-0.41°) + \cos(13°)\cos(-0.41°)\cos(-45°)$$

$$\cos\theta_z = (0.225)(-0.0072) + (0.974)(0.9999)(0.7071)$$

$$\cos\theta_z = -0.0016 + 0.6885 = 0.6869$$

$$\theta_z = \cos^{-1}(0.6869) = 46.62°$$

**(d) Air mass:**

$$AM = \frac{1}{\cos(46.62°)} = \frac{1}{0.6869} \approx 1.456$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** At Kerala's latitude, the Sun is reasonably high in the sky for most of the year. The low AM (≈1.4-1.5) is why Kerala has excellent solar energy potential — minimal atmospheric attenuation.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Solar Geometry — Observer-Centered Reference Frame

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **functional architecture** of solar geometry — how the key angles relate to one another and feed into the master equation for the zenith angle.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Observer at Latitude phi] --> B{Earth-Sun Geometry}
    C[Day of Year n] --> D[Declination delta]
    B --> E[Hour Angle omega]
    D --> F[Master Cosine-Z Equation]
    E --> F
    B --> F
    F --> G[Zenith Angle theta_z]
    G --> H[Altitude alpha = 90 - theta_z]
    G --> I[Air Mass AM = 1/cos theta_z]
    G --> J[Incidence Angle on Tilted Surface]
    D --> K[Sunrise Hour Angle omega_s]
    B --> K
    K --> L[Day Length = 2 omega_s / 15]
    I --> M[Beer-Lambert Attenuation]
    N[Extraterrestrial G_on] --> M
    M --> O[Direct Beam I_b at Surface]
    P[Scattering by Atmosphere] --> Q[Diffuse I_d]
    O --> R[Global Radiation I_g]
    Q --> R
    R --> S[Solar Collector / PV Panel]
    J --> S
```

## 4.2 Components of Solar Radiation & Measurement Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SUN[Sun T = 5778 K] -->|Gsc 1361 W/m2| TOA[Top of Atmosphere]
    TOA -->|G_on varies with n| ATMOS[Atmospheric Layer]
    ATMOS -->|Absorption| ABS[Gases, Dust, Ozone]
    ATMOS -->|Scattering| SCTR[Air Molecules, Aerosols]
    ATMOS -->|Direct Beam| DIR[I_b - Pyrheliometer]
    SCTR -->|Diffuse| DIF[I_d - Pyranometer with Shadow]
    DIR --> HOR[Horizontal Surface]
    DIF --> HOR
    HOR -->|Sum| GLOBE[Global I_g - Pyranometer]
    GND[Ground Reflection] -->|rho_g| GNDR[I_r]
    GNDR --> TILT[Tilted Surface]
    GLOBE --> TILT
    TILT --> COLLECTOR[Solar Energy System]
```

## 4.3 Solar Angle Hierarchy — From Declination to Incidence

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Latitude phi] --> C[Zenith Angle theta_z]
    B[Declination delta] --> C
    D[Hour Angle omega] --> C
    C --> E[Altitude alpha]
    C --> F[Air Mass AM]
    F --> G[Spectral Composition AM 0/1/1.5]
    G --> H[PV Module Rating]
    C --> I[Azimuth gamma_s]
    I --> J[Sun-Tracking System]
    K[Tilt beta] --> L[Incidence Angle theta on Tilted]
    C --> L
    I --> L
    K --> M[Optimum Tilt beta_opt = phi - delta]
    M --> N[Fixed Tilt Design]
```

## 4.4 Daily Solar Insolation Profile (Conceptual)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Sunrise omega = -omega_s] --> B[Insolation = 0]
    B --> C[Morning Rise]
    C --> D[Mid-morning Peak]
    D --> E[Noon Maximum I_max]
    E --> F[Afternoon Decline]
    F --> G[Sunset omega = omega_s]
    G --> H[Insolation = 0]
    E --> I[Symmetric Bell Curve]
    I --> J[Area under Curve = Daily Energy H]
    J --> K[Sizing of Solar System]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define the term 'Solar Constant'. What is its accepted value and why does it vary throughout the year?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Solar Constant** is defined as the rate at which solar energy is received per unit area by a surface placed perpendicular to the Sun's rays at the mean Earth-Sun distance, outside the Earth's atmosphere.

**Accepted value:** $G_{sc} = 1361 \text{ W/m}^2$ (modern value, older accepted value is $1367 \text{ W/m}^2$).

**Variation:** Due to the elliptical nature of Earth's orbit (eccentricity $e = 0.0167$), the Earth-Sun distance varies by about $\pm 1.7\%$ over the year, causing $G_{on}$ to vary by $\pm 3.3\%$ about the mean. The variation is expressed by:

$$G_{on} = G_{sc}\left[1 + 0.033\cos\left(\frac{360n}{365}\right)\right]$$

Maximum: $G_{on} \approx 1418 \text{ W/m}^2$ in early January (Earth at perihelion). Minimum: $G_{on} \approx 1325 \text{ W/m}^2$ in early July (Earth at aphelion).

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between solar irradiance and solar irradiation with examples.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Solar Irradiance | Solar Irradiation |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Instantaneous rate of solar energy per unit area | Total solar energy received per unit area over a time interval |
| **Units** | W/m² | J/m² or kWh/m²/day |
| **Nature** | Instantaneous / momentary | Cumulative over a period |
| **Example** | At solar noon in clear sky, $I \approx 1000 \text{ W/m}^2$ | A typical daily value in Kerala is $H \approx 4.5\text{–}5.5 \text{ kWh/m}^2/\text{day}$ |

**Mathematical Relation:**

$$H = \int_{t_1}^{t_2} I(t) \, dt$$

Irradiation is the time-integral of irradiance. Irradiance is a *rate*, irradiation is a *quantity*.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Sample Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **cosine of the zenith angle** in terms of latitude ($\phi$), solar declination ($\delta$), and hour angle ($\omega$). Clearly state the assumptions made. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A solar collector is located at $\phi = 28.6°$ N (Delhi latitude). Calculate:
   (i) Solar declination on October 15 ($n = 288$).
   (ii) Solar zenith angle at 10:30 AM solar time on that day.
   (iii) The instantaneous direct beam irradiance on the collector, assuming $G_{on} = 1367 \text{ W/m}^2$ and atmospheric extinction coefficient $k = 0.2$, and the collector is on a horizontal surface.
   **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

The Sun's position is defined in the local horizon coordinate system. Consider:
- The observer at latitude $\phi$ has a local vertical (zenith) and a meridian.
- The Sun's rays make an angle $(90° - \delta)$ with the equatorial plane, where $\delta$ is the declination.
- The hour angle $\omega$ measures the Sun's angular displacement from the local meridian.

Using the dot product of unit vectors in the observer's frame:

**Step 1:** The Sun's direction in the local frame is given by:
- $S_x = \cos\delta \sin\omega$
- $S_y = \cos\delta \cos\omega \cos\phi - \sin\delta \sin\phi$
- $S_z = \cos\delta \cos\omega \sin\phi + \sin\delta \cos\phi$

**Step 2:** The zenith unit vector is $\hat{z} = (0, 0, 1)$.

**Step 3:** The angle $\theta_z$ between Sun and zenith satisfies:

$$\cos\theta_z = \vec{S} \cdot \hat{z} = S_z = \cos\delta \cos\omega \sin\phi + \sin\delta \cos\phi$$

**Final Expression:**

$$\boxed{\cos\theta_z = \sin\phi \sin\delta + \cos\phi \cos\delta \cos\omega}$$

**Assumptions:** Earth is spherical, Sun's rays are parallel, declination is constant over a day, and atmospheric refraction is neglected.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**(i) Declination on $n = 288$:**

$$\delta = 23.45 \cdot \sin\left(\frac{360 \times (284 + 288)}{365}\right)$$

$$\delta = 23.45 \cdot \sin\left(\frac{360 \times 572}{365}\right) = 23.45 \cdot \sin(563.84°)$$

$$563.84° - 360° = 203.84°$$

$$\delta = 23.45 \cdot \sin(203.84°) = 23.45 \cdot (-0.4067) \approx -9.54°$$

**(ii) Hour angle at 10:30 AM:**

$$\omega = 15° \times (10.5 - 12) = -22.5°$$

**Zenith angle:**

$$\cos\theta_z = \sin(28.6°)\sin(-9.54°) + \cos(28.6°)\cos(-9.54°)\cos(-22.5°)$$

$$\cos\theta_z = (0.479)(-0.1657) + (0.878)(0.9862)(0.9239)$$

$$\cos\theta_z = -0.0794 + 0.8002 = 0.7208$$

$$\theta_z = \cos^{-1}(0.7208) \approx 43.87°$$

**(iii) Beam irradiance:**

First compute Air Mass:

$$AM = \frac{1}{\cos(43.87°)} = \frac{1}{0.7208} \approx 1.387$$

Apply Beer-Lambert law:

$$I_b = 1367 \times \exp(-0.2 \times 1.387) = 1367 \times \exp(-0.2774)$$

$$I_b = 1367 \times 0.7577 \approx 1035.8 \text{ W/m}^2$$

The horizontal beam component:

$$I_{b,h} = I_b \cos\theta_z = 1035.8 \times 0.7208 \approx 746.6 \text{ W/m}^2$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam — Sample Question Paper 2024]**

**(a)** What is **Air Mass (AM)**? Define **AM 1.5** and explain its significance in solar PV system rating. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Calculate the **daily extraterrestrial radiation** $\bar{H}_o$ on a horizontal surface at a location with latitude $\phi = 23.5°$ N on the autumn equinox ($n = 266$, $\delta = 0$). Take $G_{sc} = 1367 \text{ W/m}^2$. Also find the **day length**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Definition:** Air Mass (AM) is the ratio of the path length of solar radiation through the atmosphere to the path length when the Sun is at the zenith (directly overhead). Mathematically:

$$AM = \frac{1}{\cos\theta_z}$$

where $\theta_z$ is the zenith angle.

**Special values:**
- **AM 0:** Outside Earth's atmosphere (no atmospheric attenuation).
- **AM 1:** Sun directly overhead (zenith).
- **AM 1.5:** Sun at $\theta_z = 48.19°$, corresponding to the typical mid-latitude average.

**Significance in PV:**
- The **AM 1.5 G** (Global) spectrum is the **international standard** (IEC 60904-3) for testing and rating solar PV modules.
- It represents the *typical* solar spectrum experienced by terrestrial PV systems at mid-latitudes.
- Under STC (Standard Test Conditions), PV modules are rated at **AM 1.5 G, 1000 W/m², and 25 °C cell temperature**.
- This standardization allows fair comparison of PV modules manufactured worldwide.
- AM 1.5 contains both direct and diffuse components, representing realistic operating conditions.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1: Day-of-year factor and extraterrestrial irradiance**

For $n = 266$:

$$G_{on} = 1367 \times \left[1 + 0.033 \cos\left(\frac{360 \times 266}{365}\right)\right]$$

$$\frac{360 \times 266}{365} = 262.36°$$

$$G_{on} = 1367 \times [1 + 0.033 \times \cos(262.36°)]$$

$$\cos(262.36°) = -0.1288$$

$$G_{on} = 1367 \times [1 + 0.033 \times (-0.1288)] = 1367 \times [1 - 0.00425]$$

$$G_{on} = 1367 \times 0.99575 = 1361.2 \text{ W/m}^2$$

**Step 2: Sunrise/sunset hour angle**

With $\delta = 0$:

$$\omega_s = \cos^{-1}(-\tan\phi \tan\delta) = \cos^{-1}(0) = 90°$$

**Step 3: Day length**

$$\text{Day length} = \frac{2 \omega_s}{15°} = \frac{2 \times 90°}{15°} = 12 \text{ hours}$$

(As expected on the equinox, day and night are equal.)

**Step 4: Daily extraterrestrial radiation**

Using the mean daily radiation formula:

$$\bar{H}_o = \frac{24 \times 3600}{\pi} \cdot G_{on} \left[\cos\phi \cos\delta \sin\omega_s + \frac{\pi \omega_s}{180} \sin\phi \sin\delta\right]$$

With $\delta = 0$, the second term vanishes:

$$\bar{H}_o = \frac{24 \times 3600}{\pi} \cdot G_{on} \cdot \cos\phi \cdot \sin\omega_s$$

$$\bar{H}_o = \frac{86400}{\pi} \cdot 1361.2 \cdot \cos(23.5°) \cdot \sin(90°)$$

$$\bar{H}_o = 27502.2 \cdot 1361.2 \cdot 0.9171 \cdot 1.0$$

$$\bar{H}_o = 27502.2 \times 1248.5 = 34,335,888 \text{ J/m}^2/\text{day}$$

Converting to kWh/m²/day:

$$\bar{H}_o = \frac{34,335,888}{3.6 \times 10^6} \approx 9.54 \text{ kWh/m}^2/\text{day}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Penalty:** Many students forget to convert J/m² to kWh/m²/day. This costs 1 mark. The factor is $1 \text{ kWh} = 3.6 \times 10^6 \text{ J}$.
> 2. **Radian-Degree Trap:** When using $\sin\omega_s$ where $\omega_s$ is in degrees, this is acceptable in calculators. But in the $\pi \omega_s/180$ term, $\omega_s$ MUST be in degrees, then converted to radians. Some students incorrectly substitute radians.
> 3. **Mean vs. Instantaneous:** The mean daily formula gives $\bar{H}_o$ — students who write "$\bar{H}_o = 1361.2 \text{ W/m}^2$" lose 2 marks. It is an energy per unit area per day, not a power per area.
> 4. **Declination Sign:** On the autumn equinox, $\delta = 0$ exactly. Students who compute using a non-zero value (perhaps from a previous calculation) are penalized.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Solar Constant:** $G_{sc} = 1361 \text{ W/m}^2$ (or 1367) — energy flux at TOA at 1 AU.
- **Extraterrestrial Radiation:** $G_{on} = G_{sc}[1 + 0.033\cos(360n/365)]$ — accounts for Earth-Sun distance.
- **Declination ($\delta$):** $\delta = 23.45° \sin[360(284+n)/365]$ — range $[-23.45°, +23.45°]$.
- **Hour Angle ($\omega$):** $\omega = 15°(t - 12)$, negative morning, zero at noon, positive afternoon.
- **Master Equation:** $\cos\theta_z = \sin\phi\sin\delta + \cos\phi\cos\delta\cos\omega$ — memorize this.
- **Sunrise/Sunset Hour Angle:** $\omega_s = \cos^{-1}(-\tan\phi\tan\delta)$.
- **Air Mass:** $AM = 1/\cos\theta_z$; standard is AM 1.5 G for PV rating at 1000 W/m².
- **Beam Attenuation:** $I_b = I_o \exp(-k \cdot AM)$ — Beer-Lambert law for atmosphere.
- **Global on Horizontal:** $I_g = I_b \cos\theta_z + I_d$.
- **Daily Extraterrestrial Energy:** Use the integrated formula with $\omega_s$ in the calculation.
- **Latitude of Kerala:** $\phi \approx 8°-13°$ N (low latitude → high insolation year-round).
- **Optimum Tilt:** $\beta_{opt} \approx \phi - \delta$ for fixed-tilt solar collectors (rule of thumb).
- **AM 1.5 Significance:** Universal PV rating standard; sun at $48.19°$ from zenith; total irradiance 1000 W/m².
- **Solar Energy on Tilted Surface:** $\bar{H}_T = \bar{H}_b \bar{R}_b + \bar{H}_d \bar{R}_d + \bar{H}\rho_g(1-\cos\beta)/2$.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision Strategy:** Draw the celestial sphere with the observer's horizon, mark $\theta_z$, $\alpha$, $\omega$, and $\delta$. This single image will help you recall 80% of the formulas in this module.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Solar Thermal Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT411 - ENERGY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Solar Thermal Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Solar Thermal System** is an engineering assembly that intercepts incoming solar radiation and converts it into usable thermal energy (heat) by means of the **greenhouse effect** (short-wave solar radiation passes through a transparent cover, is absorbed by a darkened absorber surface, and the resulting long-wave re-radiation is trapped). The captured heat is then transferred to a working fluid (water, air, oil, or molten salt) for direct use (water heating, space heating) or for indirect use (power generation via steam turbines, process heat for industries).

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PECHT411 Module 2)**, solar thermal systems encompass the complete chain: **Solar Collector → Heat Transfer Loop → Storage/End-use Application**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition to Memorize:**
> *"A solar thermal system is a device or set of devices that collects solar radiation, converts it into heat, and transfers this heat to a heat-transfer fluid for use in residential, commercial, industrial, or power generation applications."*

---

## 1.2 Classification of Solar Thermal Systems

Solar thermal systems are broadly classified into two main families based on operating temperature:

| Category | Operating Temperature | Typical Application |
|----------|----------------------|---------------------|
| **Low-Temperature** | < 100 °C | Domestic water heating, space heating, swimming pools, crop drying |
| **Medium-Temperature** | 100 °C – 300 °C | Solar cooling (absorption), industrial process heat |
| **High-Temperature** | > 300 °C | Solar thermal power plants (concentrated solar power - CSP) |

> [!NOTE]
> The **flat plate collector (FPC)** belongs to the low-to-medium temperature family and is the most common device tested in KTU university examinations.

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Real-World Analogy — The Black Garden Hose:**
Imagine leaving a black rubber garden hose filled with water on a sunny terrace for three hours. The water inside becomes noticeably hot, sometimes scalding. What just happened?

- The **black rubber** absorbed the sunlight (high **absorptance, α**) and converted it into heat.
- The **transparent hose wall** trapped the re-radiated long-wave heat (greenhouse effect).
- The **water** carried this heat away — this is your "heat transfer fluid."

A solar thermal system is simply this hose — but engineered with precision: a selective-coated metal absorber, a high-transmittance glass cover, insulated backing, and a calculated flow path to deliver hot water at a controlled temperature.

> [!TIP]
> **Why a glass cover?** Glass is virtually transparent to short-wave solar radiation (transmittance, τ ≈ 0.9) but nearly opaque to long-wave infrared radiation emitted by the hot absorber. This is the **greenhouse trapping effect** — the reason a parked car becomes hotter than the outside air.

---

## 1.4 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded)

- **Solar Constant (I_sc) = 1367 W/m²** — the solar radiation intensity at the outer edge of Earth's atmosphere on a surface perpendicular to the Sun's rays.
- **Peak Sun Hours (PSH)** — equivalent hours of 1000 W/m² irradiance.
- **Air Mass (AM)** — path length of solar rays through the atmosphere normalized to the vertical (AM 1.5 is the standard reference for testing).
- **Stefan-Boltzmann Constant σ = 5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W/m²·K⁴**.

---

## 1.5 Importance in Engineering Context

Solar thermal technology is a **pillar of renewable energy engineering** because:

1. It directly displaces fossil-fuel combustion for low-grade heat (which industry currently sources mainly from natural gas and coal).
2. It can achieve **solar-to-thermal efficiencies of 60–80%** (flat plate), far exceeding photovoltaic (PV) electrical efficiency of 15–22%.
3. It is the only renewable technology that can store energy economically as heat in molten salts or hot water tanks for hours-to-days.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Principle: Conversion Chain

The energy conversion in any solar thermal system follows a strict sequence:

**Step 1 — Interception:** A transparent cover (glass/tedlar) allows short-wave solar radiation to pass through.
**Step 2 — Absorption:** A blackened absorber plate (α ≈ 0.95) converts photons into lattice vibrations (heat).
**Step 3 — Trapping:** The cover reflects back the long-wave re-radiation, minimizing convective and radiative losses.
**Step 4 — Conduction:** Heat conducts from the absorber plate to attached tubes/fins containing the working fluid.
**Step 5 — Convection:** The working fluid (water/glycol mixture) carries the heat away by forced or natural circulation.
**Step 6 — Delivery:** The hot fluid is delivered to a storage tank, heat exchanger, or end-use appliance.

---

## 2.2 Components of a Solar Flat Plate Collector (FPC)

A standard FPC has the following parts, each with a specific role:

- **Glass Cover (1–2 layers):** Reduces convective and radiative losses from the absorber.
- **Absorber Plate (Copper/Aluminum with selective coating):** Absorbs ~95% of incident solar radiation.
- **Tubes / Risers (Header-and-Riser or Serpentine design):** Carry the heat-transfer fluid.
- **Insulation (Glass-wool / Rock-wool, 50–100 mm):** Minimizes heat loss from the back and sides.
- **Casing (Galvanized Iron / Aluminum):** Provides structural rigidity and weather protection.
- **Selective Coating (Black Chrome / Nickel-Pigmented Alumina):** High α (≈ 0.95) and low ε (≈ 0.05–0.10).

---

## 2.3 Heat Transfer Analysis — Three Modes

A solar thermal collector loses heat to the surroundings by **three simultaneous mechanisms**:

| Mode of Loss | Mechanism | Mitigation Strategy |
|--------------|-----------|---------------------|
| **Conduction–Convection (Top Loss)** | Heated air rises from absorber to glass cover | Adding multiple glass covers |
| **Radiation (Top Loss)** | Hot absorber emits long-wave IR upward | Selective coating (low emissivity) |
| **Conduction (Bottom & Edge Loss)** | Heat escapes through insulation | Thick glass-wool insulation, insulated side walls |

---

## 2.4 Useful Heat Gained by the Fluid (Hugen’s Equation)

The most important equation for KTU problems. The **net useful energy collected per unit area (Q_u)** is given by the famous **Hottel-Whillier-Bliss Equation**:

$$Q_u = A_c \, F_R \, \left[ S - U_L \, (T_{f,i} - T_a) \right]$$

Where:
- $A_c$ = collector area (m²)
- $F_R$ = heat removal factor (dimensionless, typically 0.6–0.9)
- $S$ = absorbed radiation per unit area (W/m²)
- $U_L$ = overall heat loss coefficient (W/m²·K)
- $T_{f,i}$ = fluid inlet temperature (°C or K)
- $T_a$ = ambient temperature (°C or K)

---

## 2.5 Instantaneous Thermal Efficiency

The **collector efficiency (η)** is defined as the ratio of useful heat gained to the solar radiation incident on the collector:

$$\eta = \frac{Q_u}{A_c \, I_T} = F_R (\tau \alpha)_e - F_R \, U_L \, \frac{(T_{f,i} - T_a)}{I_T}$$

This is a **linear function** of the form:

$$\eta = a_0 - a_1 \, \left( \frac{T_{f,i} - T_a}{I_T} \right)$$

Where:
- $a_0 = F_R (\tau \alpha)_e$ → the **optical efficiency** (intercept on η-axis)
- $a_1 = F_R \, U_L$ → the **slope** (loss coefficient)

> [!NOTE]
> **Examination Tip:** The straight-line efficiency equation is *the single most tested* relation in KTU university questions on solar flat plate collectors. Always remember: η decreases linearly as the fluid inlet temperature rises.

---

## 2.6 Concentrating Collectors (High-Temperature Systems)

For temperatures above 200 °C, non-concentrating FPCs become uneconomical. **Concentrating collectors** focus sunlight onto a small receiver:

| Type | Concentration Ratio | Temperature Range |
|------|---------------------|-------------------|
| **Line-focusing (Parabolic Trough)** | 10–30 | 200 °C – 400 °C |
| **Point-focusing (Parabolic Dish)** | 500–2000 | 500 °C – 1500 °C |
| **Solar Power Tower** | 300–1000 | 500 °C – 1000 °C |

**Concentration Ratio (C)** is defined as:

$$C = \frac{A_{\text{aperture}}}{A_{\text{receiver}}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Concentrators **can only use direct (beam) radiation**, not diffuse. Hence, they are suitable for clear-sky regions (Rajasthan, parts of Gujarat, Arizona) and require sun-tracking systems.

---

## 2.7 Solar Thermal Storage — Sensible and Latent

**Sensible Heat Storage** (most common, e.g., water tank):

$$Q_{\text{stored}} = m \, c_p \, (T_{\text{hot}} - T_{\text{cold}})$$

**Latent Heat Storage** (phase-change materials — PCM):

$$Q_{\text{stored}} = m \, h_{fg}$$

---

## 2.8 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Units |
|---|---------|----------------|-------|
| 1 | $I_{bn} = I_{sc} \left(1 + 0.033 \cos \frac{360 n}{365}\right)$ | Extraterrestrial normal radiation, day *n* | W/m² |
| 2 | $\omega = 15° \times (\text{solar time} - 12)$ | Hour angle | degrees |
| 3 | $\delta = 23.45° \sin\left[\frac{360}{365}(284 + n)\right]$ | Solar declination | degrees |
| 4 | $\cos \theta_z = \sin\phi \sin\delta + \cos\phi \cos\delta \cos\omega$ | Cosine of zenith angle | — |
| 5 | $I_T = I_b R_b + I_d \left(\frac{1 + \cos\beta}{2}\right) + I \rho_g \left(\frac{1 - \cos\beta}{2}\right)$ | Total radiation on tilted surface | W/m² |
| 6 | $Q_u = A_c F_R \left[ S - U_L (T_{f,i} - T_a) \right]$ | Hottel-Whillier-Bliss equation | W |
| 7 | $\eta = F_R (\tau\alpha)_e - F_R U_L \frac{T_{f,i} - T_a}{I_T}$ | Instantaneous efficiency | decimal/% |
| 8 | $Q_{\text{stored}} = m c_p \Delta T$ | Sensible heat storage | J |
| 9 | $C = A_a / A_r$ | Concentration ratio | — |
| 10 | $\dot{Q}_u = \dot{m} \, c_p \, (T_{f,o} - T_{f,i})$ | Useful heat rate from fluid side | W |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Extraterrestrial Radiation on a Horizontal Surface

**Statement:** Derive the expression for the hourly extraterrestrial radiation on a horizontal surface for a given day *n* and hour angle $\omega$.

### Step 1 — Define the Solar Constant
The Solar Constant $I_{sc}$ is corrected for the elliptical orbit of Earth using the day-of-year correction:

$$I_{bn} = I_{sc} \left(1 + 0.033 \cos \frac{360 \, n}{365}\right)$$

where $n$ = day number (1 for Jan 1, 365 for Dec 31).

### Step 2 — Define the Hour Angle
The hour angle $\omega$ measures the angular displacement of the Sun from local noon:

$$\omega = 15° \times (\text{solar time in hours} - 12)$$

This is positive in the morning and negative in the afternoon, with $\omega = 0°$ at solar noon.

### Step 3 — Solar Declination
The declination $\delta$ is the angular position of the Sun with respect to the equatorial plane:

$$\delta = 23.45° \sin\left[\frac{360}{365}(284 + n)\right]$$

### Step 4 — Cosine of the Zenith Angle
The angle between the Sun and the local vertical is the zenith angle $\theta_z$. Its cosine is given by the **spherical trigonometry** of the solar position triangle:

$$\cos \theta_z = \sin\phi \sin\delta + \cos\phi \cos\delta \cos\omega$$

where $\phi$ = latitude of the location (positive in Northern Hemisphere).

### Step 5 — Extraterrestrial Radiation on Horizontal Surface
The intensity on a horizontal surface is the beam intensity times the projection of the Sun's rays onto the horizontal plane:

$$I_{oh} = I_{bn} \cos \theta_z$$

$$\therefore I_{oh} = I_{sc}\left(1 + 0.033 \cos \frac{360 n}{365}\right) \left[\sin\phi \sin\delta + \cos\phi \cos\delta \cos\omega \right]$$

This is the **integrand** of the hourly radiation. To obtain the total **hourly** extraterrestrial radiation, integrate over one hour centered on solar noon (from $\omega_1$ to $\omega_2$, where $\omega_2 - \omega_1 = 15° = \pi/12$ rad):

$$H_{oh} = \frac{12}{\pi} I_{sc}\left(1 + 0.033 \cos \frac{360 n}{365}\right) \left[ \cos\phi \cos\delta \left(\sin\omega_2 - \sin\omega_1\right) + \frac{\pi (\omega_2 - \omega_1)}{180°}\sin\phi \sin\delta \right]$$

> [!NOTE]
> **The factor $\frac{12 \times 3600}{2\pi} = 3600/(\pi/12) \times 12$** converts radian·sec units into MJ/m². The factor $12/\pi$ comes from the integration $\int \cos\omega \, d\omega$ over a 1-hour interval, which spans $15° = \pi/12$ rad.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Hottel-Whillier-Bliss Equation (Heat Balance on a Flat Plate Collector)

**Statement:** Derive the expression for the useful heat collected by a flat plate solar collector, considering all heat losses.

### Step 1 — Absorbed Solar Radiation
Let the incident radiation on the tilted collector be $I_T$ (W/m²). The product of transmittance–absorptance $(\tau\alpha)$ accounts for the cover and the absorber:

$$S = I_T \, (\tau \alpha)_e$$

where $(\tau\alpha)_e$ is the **effective transmittance-absorptance product** (accounts for multiple reflections between cover and plate).

### Step 2 — Heat Loss to Surroundings
The absorber plate is at temperature $T_p$, the ambient is at $T_a$. The heat loss per unit area is proportional to the temperature difference:

$$Q_{\text{loss}} = U_L (T_p - T_a)$$

where $U_L$ is the **overall heat loss coefficient** (W/m²·K), combining top, bottom, and edge losses.

### Step 3 — Net Heat Available at the Absorber Plate
The plate receives absorbed radiation and loses heat to surroundings. The net heat **available at the plate** per unit area is:

$$q_{\text{plate}} = S - U_L (T_p - T_a)$$

### Step 4 — Heat Removal Factor $F_R$
In practice, the entire plate is not at a single temperature. Tubes carry fluid at temperature $T_f < T_p$. The ratio of **actual useful heat gain** to the **heat gain if the entire plate were at the fluid inlet temperature** is the **heat removal factor $F_R$**:

$$F_R = \frac{\dot{m} c_p}{A_c U_L} \left[ 1 - \exp\left(-\frac{A_c U_L F'}{\dot{m} c_p}\right) \right]$$

where $F'$ is the **collector efficiency factor** (depends on plate-to-tube fin geometry).

### Step 5 — Final Useful Heat Equation
Multiplying the plate's net available heat by $F_R$ (which accounts for the temperature gradient between plate and fluid) gives the **useful heat delivered to the fluid**:

$$Q_u = A_c \, F_R \left[ S - U_L (T_{f,i} - T_a) \right]$$

This is the celebrated **Hottel-Whillier-Bliss equation**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Linear Efficiency Equation (From Hottel-Whillier-Bliss)

**Statement:** Derive the linear efficiency relation $\eta = a_0 - a_1 \frac{(T_{f,i} - T_a)}{I_T}$.

### Step 1 — Definition of Efficiency
By definition, instantaneous thermal efficiency is:

$$\eta = \frac{Q_u}{A_c I_T}$$

### Step 2 — Substitute Hottel-Whillier-Bliss
Insert $Q_u = A_c F_R [S - U_L (T_{f,i} - T_a)]$:

$$\eta = \frac{F_R [S - U_L (T_{f,i} - T_a)]}{I_T}$$

### Step 3 — Substitute $S = I_T (\tau\alpha)_e$

$$\eta = \frac{F_R [I_T (\tau\alpha)_e - U_L (T_{f,i} - T_a)]}{I_T}$$

### Step 4 — Simplify
Split the numerator:

$$\eta = F_R (\tau\alpha)_e - F_R U_L \frac{(T_{f,i} - T_a)}{I_T}$$

### Step 5 — Compare with Linear Form
This is exactly the linear equation:

$$\eta = a_0 - a_1 \cdot X$$

where $a_0 = F_R (\tau\alpha)_e$ and $a_1 = F_R U_L$, and $X = (T_{f,i} - T_a)/I_T$ is the **reduced temperature difference**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stagnation Condition:** When $\eta = 0$, the collector is at maximum possible temperature (stagnation). Setting $\eta = 0$:
> $T_{\text{stagnation}} = T_a + \frac{(\tau\alpha)_e I_T}{U_L}$ — the maximum safe temperature when the fluid is not circulating.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Typical Parameter Values (Exam-Relevant)

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value | Units |
|-----------|--------|---------------|-------|
| Optical efficiency | $F_R (\tau\alpha)_e$ | 0.70 – 0.80 | dimensionless |
| Heat loss coefficient | $U_L$ | 4.0 – 6.0 | W/m²·K |
| Solar irradiance (test) | $I_T$ | 800 – 1000 | W/m² |
| Heat removal factor | $F_R$ | 0.65 – 0.85 | dimensionless |
| Absorber absorptance | $\alpha$ | 0.92 – 0.97 | dimensionless |
| Emittance of selective surface | $\varepsilon$ | 0.05 – 0.15 | dimensionless |
| Glass transmittance | $\tau$ | 0.85 – 0.92 | dimensionless |
| Insulation thickness (back) | $t$ | 50 – 100 | mm |
| Concentration ratio (trough) | $C$ | 10 – 30 | dimensionless |

> [!TIP]
> **For numerical problems in KTU exams**, the question will usually state: $F_R = 0.75$, $U_L = 5.0$ W/m²K, $(\tau\alpha)_e = 0.80$, $I_T = 900$ W/m², $T_{f,i} = 60 °C$, $T_a = 30 °C$. Calculate $\eta$ using the linear efficiency equation.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Solar Flat Plate Collector Heat Flow

The following Mermaid block diagram shows the **functional architecture and heat flow** of a typical solar flat plate collector system:

```mermaid
    B --> C["ABSORBER PLATE\nSelective Coating alpha 0.95"]
    C --> D["TUBES AND FINS\nHeat Conductance"]
    D --> E["WORKING FLUID\nWater Glycol Mixture"]
    E --> F["OUTLET TO STORAGE\nHot Fluid Exits"]
    E --> G["INLET FROM STORAGE\nCold Fluid Returns"]
    A -.->|Reflected| H["GROUND AND EDGE\nReflectivity rho"]
    C -.->|Top Loss| I["GLASS COVER\nConvection and Radiation"]
    C -.->|Bottom Loss| J["INSULATION\nGlass Wool 50 to 100 mm"]
    I --> K["AMBIENT AIR\nTemperature T_a"]
    J --> K
    H --> K
    F --> L["STORAGE TANK\nSensible Heat Q equals m cp deltaT"]
    L --> G
```
