# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define solar flat plate collector. List its main components.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **solar flat plate collector** is a non-concentrating, stationary device that intercepts solar radiation over a flat absorbing surface and converts it into thermal energy, transferring the heat to a fluid flowing through tubes attached to the absorber.

**Main Components (½ mark each for any 4):**
1. **Glazing / Glass cover** — reduces convective and radiative losses (greenhouse effect).
2. **Absorber plate** — coated with selective material (high α, low ε); captures solar radiation.
3. **Tubes / Risers** — carry the heat-transfer fluid; bonded to the absorber by conduction.
4. **Back insulation** — glass-wool or rock-wool; reduces bottom heat loss.
5. **Casing / Frame** — GI or aluminum; structural rigidity, weather protection.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Explain the concept of "Heat Removal Factor" $F_R$ in a flat plate collector.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **heat removal factor $F_R$** is defined as the ratio of the **actual useful heat gain** of a collector to the **useful heat gain that would occur if the entire absorber plate were at the fluid inlet temperature $T_{f,i}$**.

**Mathematical Expression (2 marks):**

$$F_R = \frac{\dot{m} c_p}{A_c U_L} \left[ 1 - \exp\left( -\frac{A_c U_L F'}{\dot{m} c_p} \right) \right]$$

where $F'$ is the collector efficiency factor depending on plate-fin geometry, $\dot{m}$ is the mass flow rate, $A_c$ is the collector area, and $U_L$ is the overall loss coefficient.

**Significance (1 mark):** $F_R$ accounts for the temperature gradient between the absorber plate and the working fluid. Typical values lie between 0.6 and 0.9 for well-designed collectors.

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question Choice A [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**A) (a)** With a neat sketch, explain the working of a flat plate solar collector. Derive the Hottel-Whillier-Bliss equation for useful heat collected. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Sketch & Components (2 marks):** Refer to the block diagram in Section 4.1. Label: glass cover, absorber plate with selective coating, tubes, insulation, casing. Mark: $I_T$ (incident radiation), $S$ (absorbed), $Q_u$ (useful heat), $T_{f,i}, T_{f,o}$ (fluid temperatures), $T_a$ (ambient).

**Working Principle (2 marks):** Solar radiation passes through the glass cover, is absorbed by the selective coating on the absorber plate, and converted to heat. This heat conducts through the plate to the fluid tubes, where the heat-transfer fluid carries it away. Heat losses occur from the top (convection + radiation) and bottom/edges (conduction through insulation).

**Derivation (3 marks):** See Section 3.2 (Derivation 2) of these notes. Start from absorbed radiation $S = I_T (\tau\alpha)_e$, then write plate net heat: $q_{\text{plate}} = S - U_L (T_p - T_a)$, apply heat removal factor $F_R$, and obtain:

$$Q_u = A_c F_R \left[ S - U_L (T_{f,i} - T_a) \right]$$

**Final Statement:** This is the Hottel-Whillier-Bliss equation used to size solar flat plate collector systems.

---

**A) (b)** A flat plate solar collector has the following specifications: collector area $A_c = 2.0$ m², $F_R = 0.75$, $U_L = 5.5$ W/m²·K, effective transmittance-absorptance $(\tau\alpha)_e = 0.80$, solar radiation on tilted surface $I_T = 900$ W/m², ambient temperature $T_a = 30 °C$, fluid inlet temperature $T_{f,i} = 65 °C$. Calculate:
(i) the useful heat collected per hour, and
(ii) the instantaneous thermal efficiency. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data (1 mark):**
- $A_c = 2.0$ m²
- $F_R = 0.75$
- $U_L = 5.5$ W/m²·K
- $(\tau\alpha)_e = 0.80$
- $I_T = 900$ W/m²
- $T_{f,i} = 65 °C$
- $T_a = 30 °C$

**Step 1: Absorbed Radiation per unit area** (0.5 mark):

$$S = I_T (\tau\alpha)_e = 900 \times 0.80 = 720 \text{ W/m}^2$$

**Step 2: Useful Heat Collected (Watts)** (2 marks):

$$Q_u = A_c F_R \left[ S - U_L (T_{f,i} - T_a) \right]$$

$$Q_u = 2.0 \times 0.75 \times \left[ 720 - 5.5 \times (65 - 30) \right]$$

$$Q_u = 1.5 \times \left[ 720 - 5.5 \times 35 \right]$$

$$Q_u = 1.5 \times \left[ 720 - 192.5 \right]$$

$$Q_u = 1.5 \times 527.5 = 791.25 \text{ W}$$

**Step 3: Useful Heat per Hour** (1 mark):

$$Q_{u,\text{hourly}} = 791.25 \times 3600 = 2{,}848{,}500 \text{ J} = 2.85 \text{ MJ}$$

**Step 4: Instantaneous Efficiency** (1.5 marks):

$$\eta = \frac{Q_u}{A_c I_T} = \frac{791.25}{2.0 \times 900} = \frac{791.25}{1800} = 0.4396 = 43.96\%$$

**Cross-verification using linear equation** (1 mark):

$$\eta = F_R (\tau\alpha)_e - F_R U_L \frac{T_{f,i} - T_a}{I_T}$$

$$\eta = 0.75 \times 0.80 - 0.75 \times 5.5 \times \frac{65 - 30}{900}$$

$$\eta = 0.60 - 0.75 \times 5.5 \times 0.0389$$

$$\eta = 0.60 - 0.1604 = 0.4396 = 43.96\% \quad \checkmark$$

**Final Answer:** $Q_{u,\text{hourly}} = 2.85$ MJ, $\eta = 43.96\%$.

---

### Question Choice B [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**B) (a)** Differentiate between flat plate collector and concentrating solar collector. Explain parabolic trough collector with a neat diagram. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Feature | Flat Plate Collector | Concentrating Collector |
|---------|----------------------|--------------------------|
| Concentration ratio | C = 1 (none) | C = 10 to 2000 |
| Operating temperature | Up to ~100 °C | 200 °C to 1500 °C |
| Tracks the Sun? | No (stationary) | Yes (1-axis or 2-axis) |
| Radiation used | Beam + Diffuse | Only Beam (direct) |
| Efficiency | 40% to 70% | 60% to 90% (optical) |
| Application | Water heating, space heating | Power generation, process heat |
| Cost per m² | Low | High |
| Receiver shape | Flat | Curved / Point |

**Parabolic Trough Collector — Diagram (2 marks):** (Describe in words since exam expects a sketch.)
- A long parabolic-shaped reflective surface (mirror) focuses parallel sun rays onto a linear absorber tube placed at the focus.
- The receiver tube contains the heat-transfer fluid (synthetic oil or molten salt).
- The trough rotates about a single axis (usually N-S, tracking E-W) to follow the sun.

**Working Principle (2 marks):**
1. Sun rays parallel to the trough's plane of symmetry strike the curved mirror.
2. The parabolic shape reflects all rays to a single focal line where the absorber tube sits.
3. The concentrated solar flux (10–30 times) heats the fluid to 200–400 °C.
4. The hot fluid is piped to a heat exchanger, where steam is generated for a Rankine-cycle turbine (typical SEGS plant in California).

---

**B) (b)** A solar thermal water heating system supplies 200 litres of water per day, heated from 25 °C to 60 °C. If the collector efficiency is 50% and the average daily solar radiation on the tilted surface is 5.0 kWh/m²·day, find the required collector area. Take the density and specific heat of water as 1000 kg/m³ and 4.18 kJ/kg·K respectively. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data (1 mark):**
- Volume = 200 L = 0.2 m³ → mass $m = 0.2 \times 1000 = 200$ kg
- $T_i = 25 °C$, $T_f = 60 °C$, $\Delta T = 35 °C$
- $\eta = 0.50$
- Daily radiation $H_T = 5.0$ kWh/m²·day
- $c_p = 4.18$ kJ/kg·K

**Step 1: Useful Heat Required** (2 marks):

$$Q_u = m \, c_p \, \Delta T = 200 \times 4.18 \times 35$$

$$Q_u = 29{,}260 \text{ kJ/day}$$

Convert to kWh: $1 \text{ kWh} = 3600 \text{ kJ}$

$$Q_u = \frac{29{,}260}{3600} = 8.128 \text{ kWh/day}$$

**Step 2: Energy Balance on the Collector** (1 mark):
Energy delivered by the sun per m² of collector per day = $H_T = 5.0$ kWh/m²·day.
Useful energy captured per m² = $\eta \times H_T = 0.50 \times 5.0 = 2.5$ kWh/m²·day.

**Step 3: Required Collector Area** (1.5 marks):

$$A_c = \frac{Q_u}{\eta \, H_T} = \frac{8.128}{2.5} = 3.251 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 4: Practical Sizing with Heat Losses** (1.5 marks):
A 10–15% safety margin is added to account for pipe losses, heat exchanger losses, and cloudy days:

$$A_{\text{design}} = 3.251 \times 1.10 = 3.58 \text{ m}^2$$

**Final Answer:** Required collector area ≈ **3.25 m²** (theoretical), or **3.6 m²** (practical with safety factor).

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in Solar Thermal Questions:**
> 1. **Unit Errors in $\eta$ Calculation:** $\eta = Q_u / (A_c I_T)$ — students often forget to multiply by 100 when writing the answer in percent. Always state the decimal AND the percent.
> 2. **Confusing $T_p$ and $T_{f,i}$:** The Hottel-Whillier-Bliss equation uses the **fluid inlet** temperature, NOT the **plate** temperature. The plate is hotter than the fluid.
> 3. **Sign Convention for Heat Loss:** The term $U_L (T_{f,i} - T_a)$ is **subtracted** from absorbed radiation. If $T_{f,i} < T_a$ (impossible in practice), the term becomes negative — students often invert this sign.
> 4. **Forgetting the Hourly Integration Factor:** $H_{oh} = (12/\pi) \times (\text{integrand})$ — the $12/\pi$ factor is frequently missed in derivations.
> 5. **Mixing Beam and Total Radiation:** Concentrating collectors use **only beam** radiation $I_b$, not total $I_T$. Using $I_T$ in a parabolic trough problem is a sure 1-mark loss.
> 6. **Stagnation Temperature Mistakes:** At $\eta = 0$, $T_{\text{stag}} = T_a + (\tau\alpha)_e I_T / U_L$. Students forget that $(\tau\alpha)_e$ here is without $F_R$ (use the plate-side expression).
> 7. **No Sketch in 7-Mark Questions:** When a question says "with a neat sketch," failure to draw (or describe) the components leads to a 1–2 mark deduction.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Solar Flat Plate Collector (FPC)** is the most common low-to-medium temperature solar device; it works on the **greenhouse principle** (short-wave solar radiation passes through a transparent cover, is absorbed as heat, and the long-wave re-radiation is trapped).
- **Key components:** Glass cover (transmittance τ ≈ 0.9), selective-coated absorber plate (α ≈ 0.95, ε ≈ 0.1), tubes, insulation, casing.
- **Hottel-Whillier-Bliss equation** is the master equation: $Q_u = A_c F_R [S - U_L (T_{f,i} - T_a)]$.
- **Linear efficiency:** $\eta = F_R (\tau\alpha)_e - F_R U_L (T_{f,i} - T_a)/I_T$. This is the **most-tested relation** in KTU 2024 scheme exams.
- **Heat Removal Factor $F_R$** corrects for the temperature gradient between plate and fluid. Typical range: 0.6–0.9.
- **Overall heat loss coefficient $U_L$** combines top (convection + radiation), bottom (conduction), and edge losses. Typical: 4–6 W/m²·K for an FPC.
- **Concentrating collectors** (parabolic trough, dish, tower) require **direct beam radiation** and **sun tracking**; they achieve higher temperatures (200 °C to 1500 °C) but cost more per m².
- **Concentration Ratio** $C = A_{\text{aperture}} / A_{\text{receiver}}$. Trough: 10–30, Dish: 500–2000.
- **Solar geometry essentials:** Hour angle $\omega = 15°(\text{ST} - 12)$; Declination $\delta = 23.45° \sin[360(284+n)/365]$; Zenith angle $\cos \theta_z = \sin\phi \sin\delta + \cos\phi \cos\delta \cos\omega$.
- **Extraterrestrial radiation on horizontal surface:** $H_{oh} = (12/\pi) \, I_{bn} \, [\cos\phi \cos\delta (\sin\omega_2 - \sin\omega_1) + (\pi (\omega_2-\omega_1)/180) \sin\phi \sin\delta]$.
- **Stagnation temperature** (maximum no-flow temperature): $T_{\text{stag}} = T_a + (\tau\alpha)_e I_T / U_L$.
- **Sensible heat storage** in water tanks: $Q = m c_p \Delta T$. For domestic use, water's high $c_p$ (4.18 kJ/kg·K) makes it an excellent storage medium.
- **Solar Constant:** $I_{sc} = 1367$ W/m² (at AM 0).
- **Efficiency Hierarchy:** FPC (40–70%) > Evacuated Tube (50–80%) > Parabolic Trough (60–80% thermal) > PV (15–22% electrical).
- **Selection rule of thumb:** For temperatures below 100 °C, use FPC; between 100–200 °C, use evacuated tubes; above 200 °C, use concentrating systems (only in clear-sky regions).
- **Storage sizing rule:** Daily useful heat / (ρ × $c_p$ × $\Delta T$) gives the minimum water tank volume.
- **Practical efficiency losses** occur from: dust on the glass (10–20% loss), wind-driven convection, pipe heat loss, suboptimal tilt angle, and cloudy weather.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Strategy:** For any 7-mark numerical, always (1) write the given data with units, (2) state the formula, (3) show the substitution step, (4) box the final answer. For any 7-mark derivation, include a labelled sketch and state assumptions explicitly. This single discipline can lift your score by 8–12 marks in the end-semester exam.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Flat Plate Collectors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECHT411 - ENERGY ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Flat Plate Collectors # 🔆 Module 2 — Flat Plate Collectors: Core Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Flat Plate Collector (FPC):** A non-concentrating, non-imaging **solar thermal energy capture device** that absorbs both **beam and diffuse solar radiation** incident on a flat absorbing surface, converting it into useful thermal energy (heat) which is then transferred to a working fluid (water, air, or heat transfer oil) flowing through an integral passage beneath the absorber. The collector is *non-concentrating*, meaning the entire area intercepting solar radiation equals the area of the absorber — no optical concentration of sunlight occurs. Because of this, FPCs operate efficiently even under **diffuse radiation** conditions (cloudy skies), unlike concentrating collectors. ## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy 🌞 > [!NOTE] > **Analogy — The Parked Black Car on a Summer Noon** > Imagine parking a black-painted car in direct sunlight. The dashboard, seats, and metal surfaces all get hot. Now imagine fitting **transparent glass windows** above the car (to trap heat like a greenhouse), insulating the underside (so heat doesn't escape to the ground), and running **water pipes through the hot metal roof**. As water flows through those pipes, it gets heated — usable for bathing, washing, or even driving a turbine. > > **The car body = Absorber plate** > **Glass windows = Transparent cover (greenhouse effect)** > **Ground foam = Bottom insulation** > **Water pipes = Fluid tubes** > **Hot water coming out = Useful thermal energy** This is precisely how a **Flat Plate Collector** functions — a passive solar "heat trap" combined with a fluid-flow heat exchanger. ## 1.3 Classification of Solar Collectors (Context) | Type | Tracking Required? | Temperature Range | Concentration Ratio | |---|---|---|---| | **Flat Plate Collector (FPC)** | No (fixed) | 30 °C – 100 °C | **1 (unity)** | | Evacuated Tube Collector | No (fixed) | 50 °C – 200 °C | 1 | | Compound Parabolic Collector (CPC) | Periodic | 60 °C – 200 °C | 1 – 5 | | Parabolic Trough | Single-axis | 150 °C – 400 °C | 10 – 30 | | Parabolic Dish | Two-axis | 400 °C – 1000 °C | 500 – 2000 | > The FPC occupies the **low-temperature, simplest, cheapest** end of this spectrum — making it the most widely deployed solar thermal device globally (≈ **80 %** of all installed solar thermal capacity uses FPCs**). ## 1.4 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics | Parameter | Standard Value / Range | |---|---| | Solar Constant (Gsc) | **1367 W/m²** | | Air Mass (AM) at sea level, sun overhead | **AM 1** | | AM at 48.2° zenith | **AM 1.5** (STC) | | Standard Test Condition Irradiance | **1000 W/m²** | | Thermal conductivity of fiberglass insulation | **0.04 W/m·K** | | Emissivity of black chrome selective surface | **0.10 – 0.15** | | Absorptivity of black chrome selective surface | **0.90 – 0.95** | --- # ⚙️ Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Constructional Anatomy of a Flat Plate Collector A FPC is a layered sandwich of functional components. From top to bottom: ### A. **Transparent Cover (Glazing)** - Usually **low-iron tempered glass**, 3–4 mm thick. - Transmits short-wave solar radiation (0.3 – 2.5 µm) but blocks long-wave re-radiation from the hot absorber (5 – 50 µm). - One or two covers are used; double-glazing reduces top loss by ≈ 30 %. - Plastic films (Tedlar, Tefzel) used in cheap pool heaters. ### B. **Absorber Plate (The Heart of FPC)** - Made of **copper, aluminium, or steel**, 0.5–1.0 mm thick. - Surface coated with: - **Non-selective coating** (mat black paint, ε ≈ 0.95, α ≈ 0.95) - **Selective coating** (black chrome, black nickel, alumina; α = 0.90–0.95, ε = 0.10–0.20) - Two configurations: 1. **Tube-in-Plate** — fluid tubes bonded/riveted/soldered to flat sheet. 2. **Roll-Bond** — fluid passages formed by hydraulic pressure-bonding two aluminium sheets. ### C. **Fluid Tubes (Header & Riser System)** - Diameter: 10–15 mm copper. - Spacing (tube pitch, W): 50–200 mm. - Header (manifold) tubes at top & bottom: 25–40 mm diameter. - Working fluid: water, water-glycol mix, air, or thermic oil. ### D. **Insulation (Bottom & Sides)** - **Bottom insulation:** 50–100 mm of glass wool / rock wool / polyurethane foam (k ≈ 0.04 W/m·K). - **Side insulation:** 25–50 mm same material. - Reduces bottom and edge heat losses to ≈ 5–10 % of total loss. ### E. **Casing / Frame** - Galvanized iron, aluminium, or FRP. - Weather-proof housing supporting all internal layers. - Tilted at latitude angle (φ) for year-round optimum. ## 2.2 Working Principle — Stepwise Logic > [!NOTE] > **Step 1:** Solar radiation (beam + diffuse) strikes the **transparent cover**. ≈ 90 % transmitted (τ ≈ 0.90). > > **Step 2:** Transmitted radiation hits the **absorber plate**. Most of it is absorbed (α ≈ 0.90–0.95). Absorber temperature rises to **70–120 °C**. > > **Step 3:** Absorbed heat is conducted through the plate to bonded **fluid tubes**. > > **Step 4:** Working fluid flowing through tubes picks up heat by **forced or thermosiphon convection** and carries it to storage / load. > > **Step 5:** Absorber also **loses heat** to surroundings by: > - Convection + radiation from **top** (through glazing) → **Top loss (Ut)** > - Conduction through **bottom insulation** → **Bottom loss (Ub)** > - Conduction through **side edges** → **Side loss (Us)** > > **Step 6:** Net heat gained = (Absorbed solar energy) − (Total losses) — this is the **useful heat gain (Qu)**. ## 2.3 Energy Balance on the FPC The fundamental steady-state energy balance for the collector at any instant: $$ S = Q_u + Q_L $$ Where: | Symbol | Meaning | |---|---| | S | Total solar energy absorbed by plate per unit time | | Q_u | Useful heat energy transferred to fluid per unit time | | Q_L | Total thermal losses (top + bottom + side) per unit time | ## 2.4 The Hottel-Whillier-Bliss Equation (THE Master Equation ⭐) This is the single most important equation for KTU exams. It relates useful heat gain of a flat plate collector to absorbed radiation and operating temperature. $$ Q_u = A_c \, F_R \left[ (\tau\alpha) \, G_T - U_L \, (T_i - T_a) \right] $$ Where: | Symbol | Meaning | Typical Unit | |---|---|---| | A_c | Aperture / gross collector area | m² | | F_R | **Heat Removal Factor** (0.6 – 0.9) | dimensionless | | (τα) | Effective transmittance-absorptance product | 0.7 – 0.85 | | G_T | Total (beam + diffuse) incident solar radiation on tilted surface | W/m² | | U_L | Overall (top + bottom + side) heat loss coefficient | W/m²·K | | T_i | Fluid inlet temperature | °C or K | | T_a | Ambient temperature | °C or K | ## 2.5 Thermal Efficiency Definition $$ \eta = \dfrac{Q_u}{A_c \, G_T} = F_R \left[ (\tau\alpha) - U_L \dfrac{(T_i - T_a)}{G_T} \right] $$ This is a **linear equation in (T_i − T_a)/G_T** — hence plotting η vs. reduced temperature gives a **straight line** with slope = −F_R·U_L and intercept = F_R·(τα). ## 2.6 Heat Loss Coefficients — Sub-Formulas ### A. Top Loss Coefficient (U_t) Empirical correlation by **Klein (1975)** — approved by ASHRAE & used in KTU textbooks: $$ U_t = \left[ \dfrac{N}{\dfrac{C}{T_p}\left[\dfrac{T_p - T_a}{N + f}\right]^{e}} + \dfrac{1}{h_w} \right]^{-1} + \dfrac{\sigma (T_p^2 + T_a^2)(T_p + T_a)}{\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_p + 0.00591 N h_w} + \dfrac{2N + f - 1 + 0.133 \varepsilon_p}{\varepsilon_g} - N} $$ Where: - N = number of glass covers (1, 2, 3…) - T_p = mean plate temperature (K), T_a = ambient (K) - C, e, f = empirical wind-convection constants (table below) - h_w = wind convective coefficient ≈ **5.7 + 3.8 V** (V in m/s) - ε_p = plate emissivity, ε_g = glass emissivity ≈ 0.88 - σ = Stefan-Boltzmann constant = **5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W/m²·K⁴** ### B. Klein's Empirical Constants | N (covers) | C | e | f | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | 520 | 0.25 | 0.5 | | 2 | 270 | 0.27 | 0.32 | | 3 | 180 | 0.32 | 0.25 | ### C. Bottom Loss Coefficient (U_b) $$ U_b = \dfrac{k_{ins}}{L_{ins}} $$ ### D. Side Loss Coefficient (U_s) $$ U_s = \dfrac{(U_b \, A_b) + (U_t \, A_s) + (U_{edge} \, A_{edge})}{A_c} $$ ### E. Overall Heat Loss Coefficient $$ U_L = U_t + U_b + U_s \quad [\text{W/m}^2\cdot\text{K}] $$ ## 2.7 Heat Removal Factor (F_R) — Hottel's Equation $$ F_R = \dfrac{\dot{m} C_p}{A_c U_L} \left[ 1 - \exp\left(-\dfrac{A_c U_L F'}{\dot{m} C_p}\right) \right] $$ Where **F′** is the **Collector Efficiency Factor**: $$ F' = \dfrac{1/U_L}{W \left[ \dfrac{1}{U_L [D_o + (W - D_o)F]} + \dfrac{1}{C_b} + \dfrac{1}{\pi D_o h_{f,i}} \right] } $$ With **Fin efficiency F** for tube spacing W: $$ F = \dfrac{\tanh\left[ m(W - D_o)/2 \right]}{m(W - D_o)/2}, \quad \text{where } m = \sqrt{\dfrac{U_L}{k_{plate} \, \delta_{plate}}} $$ ## 2.8 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Exam-Ready Table) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Save this table for the exam. It covers ~80 % of numerical questions on FPC.** | # | Formula | Quantity Computed | |---|---|---| | 1 | Q_u = A_c F_R [(τα)G_T − U_L(T_i − T_a)] | Useful heat gain (W) | | 2 | η = F_R[(τα) − U_L(T_i − T_a)/G_T] | Instantaneous efficiency | | 3 | U_L = U_t + U_b + U_s | Overall heat loss coefficient | | 4 | U_b = k_ins / L_ins | Bottom loss coefficient | | 5 | F_R = (ṁC_p/A_c U_L)[1 − exp(−A_c U_L F′/ṁC_p)] | Heat removal factor | | 6 | η_fin = tanh(m(W−D_o)/2)/(m(W−D_o)/2) | Fin efficiency F | | 7 | m = √(U_L / kδ) | Fin parameter | | 8 | S_abs = A_c (τα) G_T | Absorbed solar energy | | 9 | τ = 1.012 − 0.2331(n·cosθ) + ... | Transmittance of glass cover | | 10 | α_selective ≈ 0.95, ε ≈ 0.12 | Selective surface optical properties | ## 2.9 Real-World Engineering Utility | Application Domain | Typical Operating Temperature | |---|---| | Domestic water heating | 40 – 60 °C | | Space heating (radiant floor) | 30 – 50 °C | | Swimming pool heating | 25 – 32 °C | | Solar drying (crops, fish, timber) | 40 – 70 °C | | Industrial process heat (low-grade) | 60 – 100 °C | | Absorption refrigeration (LiBr-H₂O) | 80 – 100 °C | | Desalination (small scale) | 70 – 90 °C | FPCs form the **backbone of solar water heaters (SWH)** — the world's most-installed renewable thermal technology. --- # 📐 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 DERIVATION 1 — Energy Balance on a Single-Glazed FPC ### Step 1 — Define the control volume Take the **absorber plate** bounded by the glazing (top), insulation (bottom, sides), and fluid tubes. ### Step 2 — Write the energy entering Solar radiation incident on aperture area A_c = G_T · A_c. After transmission through cover (τ) and absorption by plate (α): $$ \dot{Q}_{in} = (\tau \alpha) \, G_T \, A_c $$ ### Step 3 — Write the energy leaving Heat transferred to fluid: $$ \dot{Q}_{fluid} = \dot{m} \, C_p \, (T_o - T_i) $$ Total heat losses (top + bottom + sides): $$ \dot{Q}_{loss} = U_L \, A_c \, (T_p - T_a) $$ ### Step 4 — Apply steady-state energy balance $$ \dot{Q}_{in} = \dot{Q}_{fluid} + \dot{Q}_{loss} $$ $$ (\tau \alpha) \, G_T \, A_c = \dot{m} \, C_p \, (T_o - T_i) + U_L \, A_c \, (T_p - T_a) $$ ### Step 5 — Introduce Heat Removal Factor F_R F_R accounts for the fact that fluid outlet temperature T_o is **less than** plate temperature T_p, and also for non-uniform temperature along the flow path. It is defined as: $$ F_R = \dfrac{\dot{m} C_p (T_o - T_i)}{A_c \left[ G_T (\tau\alpha) - U_L(T_i - T_a) \right]} $$ ### Step 6 — Rearrange into Hottel-Whillier-Bliss form $$ Q_u = \dot{m} C_p (T_o - T_i) = A_c \, F_R \left[ (\tau\alpha) G_T - U_L (T_i - T_a) \right] $$ **Bingo — this is the canonical HWB equation.** ⭐ ## 3.2 DERIVATION 2 — Fin Efficiency F for Tube-in-Plate Absorber ### Step 1 — Consider one repeating section between two adjacent tubes Width = W (tube pitch). Tube outer diameter = D_o. Plate thickness = δ. Plate thermal conductivity = k. ### Step 2 — Write 1-D fin heat conduction equation Let x be measured from the tube center. For the region between tubes, the plate temperature T(x) satisfies: $$ k \, \delta \, \dfrac{d^2 T}{dx^2} - U_L (T - T_a) = 0 $$ ### Step 3 — Re-arrange as standard ODE Define θ = T − T_a and m² = U_L / (kδ): $$ \dfrac{d^2 \theta}{dx^2} - m^2 \, \theta = 0 $$ ### Step 4 — General solution $$ \theta(x) = C_1 \cosh(mx) + C_2 \sinh(mx) $$ ### Step 5 — Apply symmetry boundary condition By symmetry, dθ/dx = 0 at x = W/2 (midway between tubes): $$ \dfrac{d\theta}{dx}\bigg|_{x=W/2} = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; C_2 = 0 $$ ### Step 6 — Apply base condition at tube edge At x = D_o/2, θ = θ_b (tube-base temperature): $$ \theta(x) = \theta_b \dfrac{\cosh[m(W/2 - x)]}{\cosh[m(W/2 - D_o/2)]} $$ ### Step 7 — Compute heat conducted from fin Heat flow per unit length of tube from one fin: $$ q_{fin} = -k\delta \dfrac{d\theta}{dx}\bigg|_{x=D_o/2} $$ ### Step 8 — Substitute and simplify $$ q_{fin} = k\delta \, m \, \theta_b \tanh\!\left[ m(W - D_o)/2 \right] $$ ### Step 9 — Maximum possible heat flow (if entire fin were at θ_b) $$ q_{max} = U_L \, (W - D_o) \, \theta_b $$ ### Step 10 — Fin efficiency ratio $$ F = \dfrac{q_{fin}}{q_{max}} = \dfrac{\tanh\!\left[ m(W - D_o)/2 \right]}{m(W - D_o)/2} $$ **Hence proved.** ✅ ## 3.3 DERIVATION 3 — Collector Efficiency Factor F′ ### Step 1 — Total useful heat from plate = (tube-base heat) + (2 × fin heat) For one repeating section of width W and tube length L: $$ q_{total} = q_{tube} + 2 \, q_{fin} $$ where: - q_tube = (T_b − T_a) · (π D_o) · h_{f,i} (convection into fluid inside tube) - q_tube = (T_b − T_a) · (π D_o) · h_{f,i} = (T_b − T_a) · U_L · (D_o · F) per unit length equivalent - 2 × q_fin = 2 × U_L · (W − D_o) · (T_b − T_a) · F / 2 — wait, redo. ### Step 2 — More rigorously Heat per unit length from one tube + two adjacent half-fins, expressed through **base temperature T_b**: $$ q_{total} = (T_b - T_a)\left[\pi D_o h_{f,i} + U_L (W - D_o) F \right] $$ ### Step 3 — Now express total heat through absorber efficiency If **entire plate were at T_b**, heat loss would be: $$ q_{ideal} = U_L \, W \, (T_b - T_a) $$ ### Step 4 — Collector efficiency factor F′ $$ F' = \dfrac{q_{total}}{q_{ideal}} = \dfrac{1}{U_L \, W} \left[\pi D_o h_{f,i} + U_L (W - D_o) F \right] $$ ### Step 5 — Rearranged standard textbook form (Duffie & Beckman) $$ F' = \dfrac{1/U_L}{W \left[ \dfrac{1}{U_L[D_o + (W - D_o)F]} + \dfrac{1}{C_b} + \dfrac{1}{\pi D_o h_{f,i}} \right] } $$ where **C_b** = bond conductance between tube and plate (W/K). This is the form used in KTU textbooks. ## 3.4 DERIVATION 4 — Effect of Selective Surface on (τα)/U_L Ratio For a **non-selective** (black paint) plate: - α = 0.95, ε = 0.95 → U_L large, (τα) ≈ 0.80 → (τα)/U_L small. For a **selective** (black chrome) plate: - α = 0.92, ε = 0.12 → U_L small (radiation loss ∝ ε drops 8×), (τα) ≈ 0.78 → (τα)/U_L large. Quantitatively, with single glass cover, no wind: | Surface Type | α | ε | U_L (W/m²K) | (τα)/U_L (K·m²/W) | |---|---|---|---|---| | Flat black paint | 0.95 | 0.90 | 7.5 | 0.107 | | Black chrome (selective) | 0.92 | 0.12 | 4.0 | 0.195 | | Improvement factor | — | — | — | **~ 1.82 ×** | ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Design Parameters on Efficiency > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master the following trend — examiners love asking "what happens if we increase X?"** | Parameter Change | (τα) | U_L | η | |---|---|---|---| | ↓ Number of glass covers | ↑ | ↑ | Trade-off | | ↑ Number of glass covers | ↓ | ↓ | Trade-off | | Selective surface | ↑ slightly | ↓↓↓ | **↑ Strongly** | | ↑ Wind speed | ≈ same | ↑ | ↓ | | ↑ Tilt angle toward winter sun | G_T ↑ | — | ↑ | | ↑ Tube pitch W | — | — | ↓ (worse fin) | | ↑ Plate thickness δ | — | — | ↑ (fin improved) | | ↓ Plate thermal conductivity k | — | — | ↓ | | Selective black coating | ↑ | ↓ | **↑↑** | ## 3.6 Sample Numerical — HWB Equation (Worked) **Problem (KTU 2024 pattern):** A flat plate collector has area A_c = 2.5 m², F_R = 0.78, (τα) = 0.78, U_L = 5.5 W/m²K. If G_T = 850 W/m², T_i = 35 °C, T_a = 28 °C, calculate: (a) Useful heat gain Q_u (b) Instantaneous efficiency η ### Solution: **Part (a):** $$ Q_u = A_c F_R \left[ (\tau\alpha) G_T - U_L (T_i - T_a) \right] $$ Plug values: $$ Q_u = (2.5)(0.78) \left[ (0.78)(850) - (5.5)(35 - 28) \right] $$ $$ Q_u = 1.95 \left[ 663 - 38.5 \right] $$ $$ Q_u = 1.95 \times 624.5 $$ $$ \boxed{Q_u \approx 1217.8 \; \text{W}} $$ **Part (b):** $$ \eta = \dfrac{Q_u}{A_c \, G_T} = \dfrac{1217.8}{2.5 \times 850} = \dfrac{1217.8}{2125} $$ $$ \boxed{\eta \approx 0.573 \; \text{or} \; 57.3 \%} $$ --- # 🏗️ Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Functional Architecture — FPC System Block Flow ```mermaid flowchart TB SUN["SOLAR RADIATION SOURCE"]:::source GLAZE["TRANSPARENT GLASS COVER
