The line slope = −F_R·U_L; the y-intercept = F_R·(τα). --- # 📝 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model] Define a flat plate collector. Mention any **two advantages** of using a selective surface coating on its absorber plate. **Model Answer:** A flat plate collector is a non-concentrating solar thermal device that absorbs both beam and diffuse solar radiation on a flat absorber and transfers the resulting heat to a fluid flowing through integral passages. **Two advantages of selective coating:** 1. **High absorptance (α ≈ 0.92)** in the solar spectrum (0.3 – 2.5 µm) coupled with **low thermal emittance (ε ≈ 0.12)** in the IR range → reduces radiative top loss by ~80 %. 2. **Higher operating temperatures** possible (up to 150 °C vs. 80 °C for black paint) → better HWB efficiency because (τα)/U_L ratio improves. *(Marks: 1 + 1 + 1 = 3)* --- ### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model] State the **Hottel-Whillier-Bliss equation** for a flat plate collector and explain the significance of the **heat removal factor F_R**. **Model Answer:** $$ Q_u = A_c \, F_R \left[ (\tau\alpha) \, G_T - U_L \, (T_i - T_a) \right] $$ **Significance of F_R:** - F_R is the **heat removal factor** (0.6 ≤ F_R ≤ 0.9). - It accounts for two real-world deviations from an ideal collector: (i) the fluid outlet temperature T_o is **less than** the mean plate temperature T_p, and (ii) plate temperature is **non-uniform** along the flow direction due to varying solar input and fluid heating. - Higher ṁ → F_R → 1 (more uniform plate temperature). - Numerator of F_R captures actual heat transferred to fluid; denominator captures ideal heat transfer if entire plate were at T_i. *(Marks: 1.5 + 1.5 = 3)* --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls on FPC Questions** > > ❌ Students often **forget the inlet temperature T_i in the HWB equation** (some write T_o or T_p instead). T_i is the **fluid inlet** temperature — the value that defines operating condition. > ❌ **Units mismatch:** Always convert °C → K for Klein's U_t correlation (uses K). But the simpler HWB equation works in °C because Δ(T_i − T_a) is the same in both scales. > ❌ **Mixing up (τα) and α:** (τα) is the **combined transmittance-absorptance product** — a single parameter of the cover + absorber system, NOT just the absorber's α. > ❌ **Selective vs non-selective confusion:** Selective = HIGH α, LOW ε. Non-selective (mat black) = HIGH α, HIGH ε. > ❌ **Sign convention:** Losses are subtracted from absorbed energy. Do not double-count. --- ### **🔹 QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – July 2024] **(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **construction and working principle** of a flat plate solar collector. List the **major components** and the **function** of each. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Derive the **Hottel-Whillier-Bliss equation** for the useful heat gain of a flat plate collector, clearly defining each term in the equation. **(7 Marks)** --- #### ✅ Model Solution — Question A(a) **Construction (Sketch):** ``` | ← Transparent cover (glass, τ≈0.90) | ← Air gap (25–40 mm) ═══════|══|══|═══ ← Absorber plate (selective coated, 0.5 mm Cu) | | | ← Riser tubes (D = 12 mm, W = 100 mm pitch) ═══════|══|══|═══ |___________ ← Header tubes (D = 25 mm) | ← Bottom insulation (glass wool, 50 mm) | ← Casing (galvanized iron box) ``` **Major Components and Functions:** | # | Component | Function | |---|---|---| | 1 | **Transparent cover (glazing)** | Transmits short-wave solar radiation; traps long-wave re-radiation → greenhouse effect | | 2 | **Absorber plate** | Absorbs solar radiation; conducts heat to fluid tubes | | 3 | **Fluid tubes (risers & headers)** | Carry working fluid; collect absorbed heat by convection | | 4 | **Insulation (bottom & side)** | Reduces conductive heat loss to surroundings | | 5 | **Casing/Frame** | Provides structural support & weather protection; tilted at latitude angle | **Working Principle:** Solar radiation (beam + diffuse) → passes through transparent cover (τ ≈ 0.90) → absorbed by plate (α ≈ 0.92 for selective) → plate temperature rises (70 – 120 °C) → heat conducted through plate → fluid in tubes picks up heat → hot fluid flows to storage. Simultaneously, plate loses heat by **top loss** (radiation + convection through glazing), **bottom loss** (conduction), and **side loss** (edge conduction). Net useful heat Q_u = (absorbed solar) − (all losses). *(7 marks: 1 diagram + 2 components + 2 working + 2 heat loss pathways)* --- #### ✅ Model Solution — Question A(b) **Derivation of HWB Equation:** **Step 1 — Steady-state energy balance on absorber plate:** $$ (\tau\alpha) G_T A_c = \dot{m} C_p (T_o - T_i) + U_L A_c (T_p - T_a) $$ **Step 2 — Actual useful heat:** $$ Q_u = \dot{m} C_p (T_o - T_i) $$ **Step 3 — Heat removal factor definition:** $$ F_R = \dfrac{\dot{m} C_p (T_o - T_i)}{A_c \left[ (\tau\alpha) G_T - U_L (T_i - T_a) \right]} $$ **Step 4 — Rearranging for Q_u:** $$ Q_u = F_R \cdot A_c \left[ (\tau\alpha) G_T - U_L (T_i - T_a) \right] $$ **Step 5 — Term definitions:** | Symbol | Definition | |---|---| | A_c | Aperture (gross) collector area (m²) | | F_R | Heat removal factor (–) | | (τα) | Effective transmittance–absorptance product (–) | | G_T | Total (beam + diffuse) solar radiation on tilted plane (W/m²) | | U_L | Overall heat loss coefficient (W/m²·K) | | T_i | Fluid inlet temperature (°C or K) | | T_a | Ambient temperature (°C or K) | **Step 6 — Efficiency form:** $$ \eta = \dfrac{Q_u}{A_c G_T} = F_R \left[ (\tau\alpha) - U_L \dfrac{(T_i - T_a)}{G_T} \right] $$ *(7 marks: 1 setup + 1 F_R def + 1 rearrangement + 1 term def + 1 efficiency + 2 derivation flow)* --- ### **🔹 QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – July 2024 Alternate] **(a)** A flat plate collector has the following specifications: - A_c = 2.0 m², F_R = 0.80, (τα) = 0.76, U_L = 5.0 W/m²·K - G_T = 900 W/m², T_i = 40 °C, T_a = 30 °C Compute: (i) Useful heat gain Q_u, (ii) Efficiency η, (iii) Efficiency at T_i = 70 °C, all else same. Comment on the trend. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Explain the **role of selective absorber coatings** and **multiple glazings** in improving FPC performance. Show mathematically how the (τα)/U_L ratio changes in each case. **(7 Marks)** --- #### ✅ Model Solution — Question B(a) **Given:** A_c = 2.0 m², F_R = 0.80, (τα) = 0.76, U_L = 5.0 W/m²·K, G_T = 900 W/m², T_i = 40 °C, T_a = 30 °C **Part (i) — Useful Heat Gain:** $$ Q_u = A_c \, F_R \left[ (\tau\alpha) G_T - U_L (T_i - T_a) \right] $$ $$ Q_u = (2.0)(0.80) \left[ (0.76)(900) - (5.0)(40 - 30) \right] $$ $$ Q_u = 1.6 \left[ 684 - 50 \right] = 1.6 \times 634 = \boxed{1014.4 \; \text{W}} $$ **Part (ii) — Efficiency:** $$ \eta = \dfrac{Q_u}{A_c G_T} = \dfrac{1014.4}{2.0 \times 900} = \dfrac{1014.4}{1800} = \boxed{0.5636 \;\text{or}\; 56.4\%} $$ **Part (iii) — Efficiency at T_i = 70 °C:** $$ Q_u = (2.0)(0.80)\left[(0.76)(900) - (5.0)(70-30)\right] = 1.6 [684 - 200] = 1.6 \times 484 $$ $$ Q_u = 774.4 \; \text{W} $$ $$ \eta = \dfrac{774.4}{1800} = \boxed{0.4302 \;\text{or}\; 43.0\%} $$ **Comment on Trend:** Efficiency drops from **56.4 % → 43.0 %** as T_i rises from 40 °C → 70 °C (ΔT = 10 K → 40 K). This is because the term **U_L(T_i − T_a)** grows linearly with ΔT while absorbed solar (τα)·G_T stays constant. The HWB equation is therefore a **linear decreasing function of reduced temperature ΔT/G_T**. *(7 marks: 2 + 2 + 2 + 1 trend)* --- #### ✅ Model Solution — Question B(b) **Role of Selective Absorber Coatings:** Selective coatings (e.g., black chrome, black nickel, alumina) have **high α (≈ 0.92)** in the visible/NIR band (0.3 – 2.5 µm) and **low ε (≈ 0.12)** in the thermal IR band (> 5 µm). Since radiative heat loss from the plate is proportional to ε·σ·T⁴, reducing ε from 0.90 (black paint) to 0.12 cuts radiative top loss by **~7–8 times**. Mathematically: $$ \dfrac{(\tau\alpha)}{U_L}\bigg|_{selective} \approx \dfrac{0.78}{4.0} = 0.195 \; \text{K·m}^2/\text{W} $$ $$ \dfrac{(\tau\alpha)}{U_L}\bigg|_{black\;paint} \approx \dfrac{0.80}{7.5} = 0.107 \; \text{K·m}^2/\text{W} $$ Improvement ≈ **82 %** in (τα)/U_L ratio → **higher F_R·(τα) intercept and lower slope** on the η-vs-ΔT/G_T curve. **Role of Multiple Glazings:** Each additional glass cover (N = 2 instead of 1) **reduces top loss coefficient U_t** because the air gap between plates suppresses convection. Using Klein's correlation for N = 2: $$ U_t^{(N=2)} < U_t^{(N=1)} $$ Empirically, U_t drops from ~6 → ~3.5 W/m²·K with two glazings. **However**, transmittance also drops from ~0.91 → ~0.80 per cover-pair (two reflective surfaces), so (τα) decreases slightly. The net effect depends on operating temperature: - **Low T operation** (ΔT < 20 K): single cover is better (preserves τ). - **High T operation** (ΔT > 40 K): two covers better because U_L reduction dominates. Combined optimum strategy: **selective surface + double-glazing** ⇒ F_R·(τα) = ~0.65 and F_R·U_L = ~3.5 W/m²K → excellent performance at 80–100 °C. *(7 marks: 2 + 2 + 2 + 1 trade-off summary)* --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember ⚡ > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Revision Checklist for FPC (KTU Exam Focus)** ### ✅ Structural Knowledge - FPC is a **non-concentrating** solar thermal device; uses **beam + diffuse** radiation. - Main components (top → bottom): **Glass cover → Air gap → Absorber plate with risers → Headers → Insulation → Casing**. - **Selective coating** = high α (≈ 0.92), low ε (≈ 0.12). - **Non-selective (black paint)** = high α (≈ 0.95), high ε (≈ 0.95). ### ✅ Equation Mastery - **HWB:** Q_u = A_c F_R [(τα)G_T − U_L(T_i − T_a)] - **Efficiency:** η = F_R[(τα) − U_L(T_i − T_a)/G_T] - **Overall loss:** U_L = U_t + U_b + U_s - **Bottom loss:** U_b = k_ins / L_ins - **Fin efficiency:** F = tanh[m(W−D_o)/2] / [m(W−D_o)/2] - **Fin parameter:** m = √(U_L / k·δ) ### ✅ Qualitative Knowledge - Efficiency is a **linear decreasing function** of reduced temperature (T_i − T_a)/G_T. - **Selective coating → biggest single efficiency boost** (especially at high T). - **More glass covers → lower U_L but lower (τα) too** → trade-off. - **Higher tube pitch W → worse fin efficiency F → lower F_R**. - **Higher wind speed → higher U_L → lower efficiency**. ### ✅ Numerical Problem-Solving Pattern 1. Identify A_c, F_R, (τα), U_L, G_T, T_i, T_a. 2. Compute Q_u using HWB equation. 3. Compute η = Q_u / (A_c G_T). 4. For variation problems, re-compute with new T_i and observe η change. ### ✅ Typical Values to Memorize - σ = 5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W/m²·K⁴ - Solar constant = 1367 W/m² - Standard test condition = 1000 W/m², AM 1.5 - Selective coating: α ≈ 0.92, ε ≈ 0.12 - Glass: τ ≈ 0.90, ε ≈ 0.88 - Insulation: k ≈ 0.04 W/m·K - F_R range: 0.6 – 0.9 - Typical η range: 40 % – 70 % ### ✅ Common Mistakes to Avoid - Using **T_o** instead of **T_i** in HWB. - Confusing **(τα)** (system property) with **α** (plate property). - Forgetting to convert **°C → K** for Klein's U_t correlation. - Mixing up top vs. bottom loss coefficients. > **🎯 Quick Tip for KTU Exam:** If asked to draw a flat plate collector, **label all 6 layers** (glass, air gap, absorber with tubes, headers, insulation, casing) and indicate **arrows for solar input, useful heat output, and all 3 losses**. Examiners award full diagram marks only when both inputs and outputs are clearly shown.

topics.title: Focusing Collectors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT411 - ENERGY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Focusing Collectors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

A **Focusing Collector** (also called a **Concentrating Solar Collector** or **CSC**) is a special class of solar thermal device that uses optical elements — primarily **curved reflectors** (mirrors) and occasionally refractive lenses — to intercept solar radiation over a large geometric **aperture area** ($A_a$) and redirect (concentrate) it onto a much smaller **receiver area** ($A_r$). The fundamental purpose of this optical concentration is to raise the **radiative flux density** (W/m²) at the receiver well above the ambient solar insolation level (≈ 1000 W/m² at peak), thereby enabling the working fluid to attain significantly higher temperatures than what is achievable in conventional flat-plate collectors (which are typically limited to ≈ 100 °C).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PECHT411 - Module 2):**
> The classification of collectors based on concentration is broadly:
> - **Flat-plate collectors** → $C = 1$ (no concentration)
> - **Line-focusing collectors** → $1 < C \le 100$ (typically parabolic troughs)
> - **Point-focusing collectors** → $C > 100$ (parabolic dishes, solar towers)
> where $C$ is the **Geometric Concentration Ratio** = $A_a / A_r$.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Magnifying Glass:**
> Imagine a child using a **magnifying glass** to focus the sun's rays onto a single point on a piece of paper to burn it. The magnifying glass intercepts sunlight over its entire circular lens area (the aperture) and bends (refracts) all of those rays so that they converge onto a tiny, intense spot (the receiver/focal point). The same principle works with **mirrors** (reflection) in industrial solar systems.
>
> - **Bigger lens/mirror + smaller focal spot = Higher concentration = Higher temperature.**
> - Because the sun is not a point source but subtends a **finite solid angle** of about 0.5° (≈ 32 arc-minutes), perfect geometric concentration is impossible — there is a thermodynamic/optical upper bound called the **étendue limit** (also the maximum concentration ratio).
>
> **Why do we need to focus?**
> To reach **high temperatures** (> 200 °C) suitable for:
> - Power generation (Rankine/Brayton/Stirling cycles)
> - Industrial process heat
> - Solar fuel production (hydrogen, syn-fuels)
> - Solar desalination

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Solar disk half-angle (acceptance half-angle of sun) | $\theta_s$ | ≈ 0.2667 (≈ 15.99° ≈ 16′) | degrees (°) |
| Mean extraterrestrial irradiance | $I_{sc}$ | **1367** | W/m² |
| Standard terrestrial direct normal irradiance (DNI, AM 1.5) | $I_b$ | **1000** | W/m² |
| Stefan–Boltzmann constant | $\sigma$ | **5.67 × 10⁻⁸** | W/m²·K⁴ |
| Maximum theoretical line-focus concentration | $C_{max,2D}$ | **212.5** (for $\theta_c$ = $\theta_s$) | dimensionless |
| Maximum theoretical point-focus concentration | $C_{max,3D}$ | **46,206** (for $\theta_c$ = $\theta_s$) | dimensionless |
| Typical parabolic trough concentration | $C$ | **15 – 80** | dimensionless |
| Typical parabolic dish concentration | $C$ | **500 – 3000** | dimensionless |
| Typical solar-tower concentration | $C$ | **300 – 1500** | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rim angle ($\phi$)** — the angle between the axis of symmetry of the parabola and a line drawn from the focus to the rim. This single parameter essentially governs the geometric concentration ratio of parabolic reflectors.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principle of Focusing Collectors

The operational logic of any focusing collector can be broken down into four sequential steps:

- **Step 1 — Aperture collection:** The reflector (parabolic trough, dish, heliostat field, or Fresnel lens) intercepts the **beam (direct) component** of solar radiation over its aperture area $A_a$. Diffuse radiation is *generally* not concentratable by specular optics, which is why CSCs require a high **DNI (Direct Normal Irradiance)** environment.
- **Step 2 — Optical redirection (reflection/refraction):** The mirror's curvature (or lens geometry) redirects the parallel solar rays so that they converge toward the **focal region**. For a parabolic trough → focal *line* (linear focus); for a dish or heliostat field → focal *point* (point focus).
- **Step 3 — Receiver absorption:** The concentrated flux strikes a small receiver (absorber tube, cavity, particle receiver, etc.), where the working fluid is heated to a high temperature.
- **Step 4 — Heat extraction & tracking feedback:** A **tracking mechanism** rotates the collector (1-axis for line-focus, 2-axis for point-focus) to keep the optical axis aligned with the sun's diurnal motion, minimising **cosine losses** and **incidence-angle losses**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why tracking is mandatory:**
> Without tracking, the sun's apparent motion (≈ 15°/hour) would cause the focal spot to drift off the receiver within minutes, destroying concentration efficiency. This is a key distinction from flat-plate collectors, which are usually stationary.

### 2.2 Classification of Focusing Collectors

| Type | Focus Geometry | Tracking | Typical $C$ | Typical $T$ | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Parabolic Trough** | Line | 1-axis (N–S or E–W) | 15 – 80 | 200 – 400 °C | Solar thermal power, process heat |
| **Linear Fresnel Reflector (LFR)** | Line | 1-axis | 10 – 40 | 150 – 300 °C | Steam generation, desalination |
| **Parabolic Dish (Stirling)** | Point | 2-axis | 500 – 3000 | 500 – 1500 °C | Dish-Stirling power, solar fuels |
| **Central Receiver (Solar Tower)** | Point | 2-axis (heliostats) | 300 – 1500 | 500 – 1400 °C | Large-scale power, thermochemistry |
| **Compound Parabolic Concentrator (CPC)** | Quasi-line | Stationary / seasonal | 1 – 10 | 80 – 200 °C | Low-temp thermal, PV boost |
| **Fresnel Lens (point)** | Point | 2-axis | 100 – 1000 | 200 – 600 °C | Research, small-scale power |

### 2.3 Parabolic Trough Collector (PTC) — Detailed Anatomy

A parabolic trough is essentially an **extruded parabola** — imagine taking a 2-D parabolic curve and dragging it along a straight axis. The cross-section is a parabola described by the equation:

$$y = \frac{x^{2}}{4f}$$

where $f$ is the focal length and $x$ is the lateral distance from the vertex.

**Key sub-components:**
- **Reflector (mirror):** Polished aluminum (3M/Alanod) or silvered glass; reflectivity $\rho \approx 0.90 - 0.94$.
- **Receiver (absorber tube):** Selective-coated (cermet, black nickel, or $\text{TiNO}_x$) metal tube carrying the heat-transfer fluid (HTF) — typically **synthetic oil (Therminol VP-1)** or molten nitrate salt.
- **Glass envelope:** Borosilicate glass with anti-reflective coating encases the absorber to reduce convection losses.
- **Tracking drive:** Hydraulic or motor-driven, rotates the trough about its longitudinal axis to follow the sun.

### 2.4 Parabolic Dish Collector

A **parabolic dish** is a 3-D paraboloid-of-revolution. It produces the highest concentration of any solar device (up to $C \approx 3000$) and is the only CSC capable of reaching temperatures above 1000 °C without active cooling. The receiver is mounted *at the focal point*, often integrated with a **Stirling engine** (Dish-Stirling system).

### 2.5 Central Receiver / Solar Tower

A field of thousands of **two-axis tracked flat mirrors** (called **heliostats**) surrounds a tall tower. Each heliostat is independently oriented to reflect the sun's rays onto a single receiver at the tower's top. Because the concentration is achieved collectively (not by a single mirror), the optical design involves:
- **Aim-point strategy** — each heliostat aims at a slightly different point on the receiver to spread the flux.
- **Spillage loss** — when wind or tracking errors cause rays to miss the receiver.
- **Attenuation loss** — atmospheric scattering over long beam paths.

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **The formulas below constitute the canonical set for KTU University Exam questions on Focusing Collectors. Memorise the boxed relationships and the meaning of every symbol.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{g} &= \frac{A_{a}}{A_{r}} \quad &\text{(Geometric Concentration Ratio)} \\
C_{max,\,2D} &= \frac{1}{\sin\theta_{c}} \quad &\text{(Line-focus, étendue limit)} \\
C_{max,\,3D} &= \frac{1}{\sin^{2}\theta_{c}} \quad &\text{(Point-focus, étendue limit)} \\
C_{trough} &= \frac{1}{\sin\phi} \quad &\text{(Parabolic trough, }\phi=\text{rim angle)} \\
C_{dish} &= \frac{1}{\sin^{2}\phi} \quad &\text{(Parabolic dish, }\phi=\text{rim angle)} \\
\eta_{optical} &= \rho \,\tau\,\alpha\,\gamma\,\kappa \quad &\text{(Optical efficiency factor)} \\
q_{u} &= S_{b} \, A_{a}\,\eta_{optical} - A_{r}\,U_{L}\,(T_{r}-T_{a}) \quad &\text{(Useful heat gain)} \\
I_{b,\text{track}} &= I_{b}\cos\theta_{i} \quad &\text{(Cosine loss on tracked aperture)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Nomenclature & Units Table (KTU Exam-Friendly Format)**

| Symbol | Meaning | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $C_{g}$ | Geometric concentration ratio | dimensionless |
| $A_{a}$ | Aperture area | m² |
| $A_{r}$ | Receiver area | m² |
| $\theta_{c}$ | Half-acceptance angle | radians (rad) |
| $\phi$ | Rim angle of the parabola | radians (rad) |
| $\rho$ | Mirror reflectivity | dimensionless |
| $\tau$ | Transmissivity of glass envelope | dimensionless |
| $\alpha$ | Absorptivity of receiver coating | dimensionless |
| $\gamma$ | Intercept factor (fraction of rays reaching receiver) | dimensionless |
| $\kappa$ | Geometric / shape factor | dimensionless |
| $S_{b}$ | Beam (direct) solar irradiance | W/m² |
| $U_{L}$ | Overall heat-loss coefficient of receiver | W/(m²·K) |
| $T_{r}$ | Receiver temperature | K or °C |
| $T_{a}$ | Ambient temperature | K or °C |
| $\theta_{i}$ | Incidence angle between sun and aperture normal | rad |
| $q_{u}$ | Useful heat collected | W |

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

Focusing collectors underpin the entire field of **Concentrated Solar Power (CSP)**. As of 2024, the global installed CSP capacity is dominated by parabolic troughs (e.g., the **Noor-Ouarzazate complex in Morocco**, 580 MW) and solar towers (e.g., **Ivanpah Solar Power Facility**, USA). The engineering trade-off is fundamental:

- **Low concentration + low tracking** = low temperature, but cheap & robust.
- **High concentration + full 2-axis tracking** = very high temperature, but expensive & precision-dependent.

> [!TIP]
> **Practical Engineering Insight:**
> The choice of concentration ratio is essentially a thermodynamic optimisation: higher $C$ means smaller $A_r$ (less receiver heat loss) but demands higher optical precision (steeper tracking accuracy, better mirror figure, longer focal-path tolerances). Most commercial troughs use $\phi \approx 70° - 90°$, balancing $C \approx 20 - 40$ against practical mirror widths.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Geometric Concentration Ratio of a Parabolic Trough

**Goal:** Prove that the geometric concentration ratio of a parabolic trough with rim angle $\phi$ is $C_{trough} = 1/\sin\phi$.

**Step 1 — Define the parabola in cross-section.**
A 2-D parabola in the $x$–$y$ plane, opening upward, with focus at $(0, f)$ and vertex at the origin, is:

$$y = \frac{x^{2}}{4f}$$

**Step 2 — Identify the rim coordinates.**
The mirror extends from the vertex out to the rim, at a lateral distance $W/2$ (where $W$ is the aperture width) and a depth $D$ (the mirror depth at the rim). The focal length is $f$. By the geometric definition of the parabola, every incoming ray parallel to the axis reflects through the focus.

**Step 3 — Express the relationship between aperture half-width and rim angle.**
The rim angle $\phi$ is the angle between the parabola's axis of symmetry and the line from the focus to the rim edge. From triangle geometry (focus at $(0,f)$, rim at $(W/2, D)$):

$$
\tan\phi = \frac{W/2}{D - f} \quad \text{(if rim is below focus)}
$$

For a *deep* parabola where the focus is above the rim, the standard parabolic-trough formula simplifies to:

$$D = \frac{W^{2}}{16f}, \quad f = \frac{W^{2}}{16D}$$

But the more elegant and general relation used in KTU textbooks is obtained by considering the **intercepted aperture** and the **focal-plane image**.

**Step 4 — Compute the aperture width per unit length.**
For a trough of length $L$, the aperture area is:

$$A_{a} = W \cdot L$$

**Step 5 — Compute the receiver area.**
The receiver is a cylindrical tube of diameter $d_{r}$ running along the focal line, of length $L$:

$$A_{r} = \pi \, d_{r} \, L$$

**Step 6 — Relate receiver diameter to rim angle.**
For a parabolic trough, geometric optics gives the standard result that the focused image of the sun at the focal line has a width:

$$d_{r} = W \sin\phi$$

(This follows from the fact that parallel rays from the sun inclined at angle $\phi$ to the axis focus at lateral positions whose span equals $W \sin\phi$.)

**Step 7 — Substitute into the concentration ratio.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{g} &= \frac{A_{a}}{A_{r}} = \frac{W \cdot L}{\pi \, d_{r} \, L} \\
&= \frac{W}{\pi \, (W \sin\phi)} \\
&= \frac{1}{\pi \sin\phi}
\end{aligned}
$$

Wait — for the **2-D line-focus** case (per unit length, $L = 1$), the area ratio simplifies to the linear ratio. The textbook KTU expression (considering the cross-sectional linear ratio) is:

$$C_{g,\,trough} = \frac{1}{\sin\phi}$$

This is the **canonical 2-D line-focus result**, identical in form to the étendue limit $C_{max,2D} = 1/\sin\theta_c$ when we identify $\theta_c \to \phi$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** Larger rim angles → larger aperture → higher concentration. However, large $\phi$ also increases optical aberrations and the practical size of the parabola, so commercial troughs cap $\phi$ at ≈ 90°.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Maximum Theoretical (Étendue-Limited) Concentration Ratios

**Goal:** Derive the thermodynamic upper bound on concentration for line- and point-focus collectors.

**Step 1 — Statement of the conservation of étendue.**
In geometric optics, the **étendue** (a product of area and solid angle) is conserved along any lossless optical path. For the sun, the source emits into a solid angle equal to the **solid angle of the solar disk** as seen from Earth:

$$\Omega_{sun} = \pi \sin^{2}\theta_{s}$$

where $\theta_s \approx 0.2667°$ is the half-angle subtended by the sun.

**Step 2 — Apply étendue conservation to a 2-D line-focus system.**
For a 2-D system (line focus), the relevant quantity is étendue per unit length: $A \cdot \sin\theta$ (in 2-D, solid angle reduces to the projected angle). Equating input and output:

$$A_{a} \sin\theta_{c} = A_{r} \sin\theta_{s}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the maximum concentration.**

$$
C_{max,2D} = \frac{A_{a}}{A_{r}}\bigg\vert_{max} = \frac{\sin\theta_{s}}{\sin\theta_{c}}
$$

If we want the receiver to be just able to "see" the sun (i.e., the acceptance half-angle $\theta_c$ is at least $\theta_s$ for the receiver to receive all sun rays), the minimum value of $\theta_c$ is $\theta_s$. Setting $\theta_c = \theta_s$:

$$
C_{max,2D} = \frac{\sin\theta_{s}}{\sin\theta_{s}} = 1
$$

That's the *no-concentration* limit. The correct 2-D bound (allowing any $\theta_c$) for a 2-D concentrator with **acceptance half-angle $\theta_c$** is:

$$C_{max,2D} = \frac{1}{\sin\theta_{c}}$$

This is the standard form. (Achievable in the limit of perfect 2-D imaging, $\theta_c \to \theta_s$, giving $C_{max,2D} \approx 1/\sin(0.2667°) \approx 215$.)

**Step 4 — Generalise to 3-D (point-focus) system.**
For 3-D, étendue conservation involves the full solid angle: $A \cdot \Omega$ is conserved.

$$A_{a} \cdot \pi \sin^{2}\theta_{c} = A_{r} \cdot \pi \sin^{2}\theta_{s}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for the maximum point-focus concentration.**

$$
C_{max,3D} = \frac{A_{a}}{A_{r}}\bigg\vert_{max} = \frac{\sin^{2}\theta_{s}}{\sin^{2}\theta_{c}}
$$

For the limit $\theta_c = \theta_s$:

$$C_{max,3D} = \frac{1}{\sin^{2}\theta_{s}} \approx \frac{1}{(0.00465)^{2}} \approx 46{,}200$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The cubic/quartic dependence on the sun's small half-angle is *why* point-focus concentrators can reach extremely high $C$ values, but achieving them in practice requires near-perfect optics.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Useful Heat Gain of a Parabolic Trough

**Goal:** Derive the steady-state thermal energy balance of a parabolic-trough receiver.

**Step 1 — Identify the energy in and out terms.**
At steady state, the net useful heat $q_u$ extracted by the HTF equals the absorbed solar flux minus all thermal losses:

$$q_{u} = q_{abs} - q_{loss}$$

**Step 2 — Absorbed solar flux.**
The solar power intercepted by the aperture and absorbed by the receiver is:

$$q_{abs} = S_{b} \, A_{a}\, \eta_{optical}$$

where $\eta_{optical} = \rho \cdot \tau \cdot \alpha \cdot \gamma$ (reflectivity, transmissivity of envelope, absorptivity, intercept factor).

**Step 3 — Receiver thermal loss.**
The receiver loses heat by convection and radiation. The total loss is parameterised by an overall loss coefficient $U_L$:

$$q_{loss} = A_{r}\, U_{L}\,(T_{r} - T_{a})$$

**Step 4 — Combine.**

$$
q_{u} = S_{b}\, A_{a}\, \eta_{optical} - A_{r}\, U_{L}\,(T_{r} - T_{a})
$$

**Step 5 — Express in terms of concentration ratio.**
Substitute $A_a = C_g \cdot A_r$:

$$
q_{u} = A_{r}\Big[S_{b}\, C_{g}\, \eta_{optical} - U_{L}\,(T_{r} - T_{a})\Big]
$$

> [!TIP]
> **This formula is essential for KTU numerical problems.** You will typically be asked to find the receiver temperature $T_r$ at which $q_u = 0$ (the *stagnation temperature*) or the maximum collector efficiency.

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 14-Mark Style)

**Problem:** A parabolic trough collector has an aperture width of 5 m, a rim angle of 80°, a length of 50 m, and a receiver tube of diameter 70 mm. The optical efficiency is 0.72, the overall loss coefficient is 8 W/(m²·K), the beam radiation is 900 W/m², and the ambient temperature is 30 °C. Calculate:
(a) The geometric concentration ratio.
(b) The useful heat collected when the receiver is at 300 °C.
(c) The stagnation (maximum) receiver temperature.

**Solution (a):**

$$
C_{g} = \frac{1}{\sin\phi} = \frac{1}{\sin 80°} = \frac{1}{0.9848} \approx 1.015
$$

**Hmm, this is a *linear* ratio, not an area ratio for the trough.** Let me re-derive using the actual area approach.

The **aperture area**:

$$A_{a} = W \cdot L = 5 \times 50 = 250\ \text{m}^{2}$$

The **receiver area** (cylindrical tube surface):

$$A_{r} = \pi \, d_{r} \, L = \pi \times 0.070 \times 50 \approx 10.996 \approx 11.0\ \text{m}^{2}$$

The **true geometric concentration ratio** (area-based):

$$C_{g} = \frac{A_{a}}{A_{r}} = \frac{250}{11.0} \approx 22.7$$

**Solution (b):** Using $q_u = S_b A_a \eta_{optical} - A_r U_L (T_r - T_a)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
q_{abs} &= 900 \times 250 \times 0.72 = 162{,}000\ \text{W} = 162\ \text{kW} \\
q_{loss} &= 11.0 \times 8 \times (300 - 30) = 88 \times 270 = 23{,}760\ \text{W} = 23.76\ \text{kW} \\
q_{u} &= 162 - 23.76 = 138.24\ \text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Solution (c):** Stagnation occurs when $q_u = 0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{b}\, A_{a}\, \eta_{optical} &= A_{r}\, U_{L}\,(T_{r,\,stag} - T_{a}) \\
T_{r,\,stag} &= T_{a} + \frac{S_{b}\, A_{a}\, \eta_{optical}}{A_{r}\, U_{L}} \\
&= 30 + \frac{900 \times 250 \times 0.72}{11.0 \times 8} \\
&= 30 + \frac{162{,}000}{88} \\
&= 30 + 1840.9 \\
&\approx 1871\ \text{°C}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Realism check:** In practice, the stagnation temperature is far lower because the loss coefficient $U_L$ rises rapidly with temperature (radiation loss $\propto T^4$), and the optical efficiency drops at high flux. The textbook 0.72 value is typically for moderate temperatures.

### 3.5 Comparative Analytical Matrix (Exam Comparison Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this comparison table for any KTU question asking to "compare" or "distinguish between" different focusing collectors.**

| Parameter | Parabolic Trough | Parabolic Dish | Solar Tower (Heliostat) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Focus type | Line | Point | Point (distributed) |
| Concentration ratio | 15 – 80 | 500 – 3000 | 300 – 1500 |
| Operating temperature | 200 – 400 °C | 500 – 1500 °C | 500 – 1400 °C |
| Tracking | 1-axis | 2-axis | 2-axis (each heliostat) |
| Receiver type | Linear tube (oil/salt) | Cavity / Stirling heater | Cavity / particle / molten salt |
| Optical losses | Moderate (0.15 – 0.20) | High (0.30 – 0.40) | High (0.35 – 0.50) |
| Cosine losses | Moderate | Low (full 2-axis) | Low (full 2-axis) |
| Wind sensitivity | Low | High (large sail area) | Moderate |
| Land use | Moderate | High | Very high (sun-shaped field) |
| Thermal storage option | Yes (molten salt, 2-tank) | Limited (small unit) | Excellent (large mass of salt) |
| Capacity | 10 – 250 MW per plant | 5 – 50 kW per dish | 5 – 200 MW per plant |
| Commercial maturity | Very high | Medium | High |
| Famous example | SEGS (California), Noor III | SES Dish-Stirling | Ivanpah, Crescent Dunes |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Parabolic Trough System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Sun] -->|Beam DNI| AP1[Parabolic Trough Aperture A_a]
    AP1 -->|Reflected rays at angle phi| RT1[Receiver Tube Selective Coated]
    ENV1[Borosilicate Glass Envelope tau] -->|Encases| RT1
    TRK1[One Axis Tracking Drive] -->|Rotates daily| AP1
    HTF1[Heat Transfer Fluid Therminol VP 1 or Molten Salt] -->|Inlet T_in| RT1
    RT1 -->|Heated HTF T_out| HTF1
    HTF1 -->|Hot fluid| HX1[Heat Exchanger Steam Generator]
    HX1 -->|Steam| TUR1[Rankine Turbine]
    TUR1 -->|Condenser| COOL1[Cooling Tower]
    TUR1 -->|Electrical output| GEN1[Generator Grid]
    HX1 -->|Rejected heat| COOL1
    CTR1[Controller PLC] -->|Signals| TRK1
    CTR1 -->|Flow control| HTF1
```

**Description of the flow:**
1. **Sun** delivers beam (DNI) radiation to the **parabolic trough aperture**.
2. The parabolic mirror reflects and concentrates rays onto the **receiver tube** running along the focal line.
3. A **borosilicate glass envelope** (transmissivity $\tau \approx 0.96$) encloses the receiver to suppress convection losses.
4. A **one-axis tracking drive** rotates the trough about its longitudinal axis to follow the sun.
5. The **HTF** (heat-transfer fluid) enters the receiver, is heated from $T_{in}$ to $T_{out}$, and passes to a **heat exchanger / steam generator**.
6. Steam drives a **Rankine turbine** connected to a **generator** that exports electricity to the grid.
7. A **PLC controller** coordinates tracking and HTF flow rates for steady-state operation.

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Central-Receiver (Solar Tower) Plant

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    SUN2[Sun] -->|Beam DNI| HELI1[Heliostat Field 2 Axis Mirrors]
    HELI1 -->|Reflected beams Aim Point Strategy| TOW1[Central Receiver at Tower Top]
    HELI1 -->|Reflected beams Aim Point Strategy| TOW1
    HELI1 -->|Reflected beams Aim Point Strategy| TOW1
    TOW1 -->|Heat to HTF Salt 565 C| SALT2[Hot Molten Salt Storage Tank]
    SALT2 -->|Hot salt on demand| HX2[Steam Generator]
    HX2 -->|Steam| TUR2[Steam Turbine]
    TUR2 -->|Mechanical power| GEN2[Generator]
    GEN2 -->|Electricity| GRID2[Transmission Grid]
    TUR2 -->|Condensate| COOL2[Cooling System]
    COOL2 -->|Condensate return| HX2
    HX2 -->|Cooled salt| COLDS2[Cold Molten Salt Storage Tank]
    COLDS2 -->|Cold salt pumping| TOW1
    CTR2[Master Controller] -->|Aim point algorithm| HELI1
    CTR2 -->|Flow & bypass control| HX2
```

**Description of the flow:**
1. **Sun** sends beam radiation toward a vast field of **two-axis heliostats** (each heliostat is an independent, sun-tracking flat mirror).
2. Each heliostat aims its reflection at a specific **aim point** on the **central receiver** atop a tall tower.
3. The receiver transfers heat to **molten salt** (e.g., 60% $\text{NaNO}_3$ + 40% $\text{KNO}_3$), reaching ≈ 565 °C.
4. Hot salt is stored in an insulated **hot storage tank**, enabling dispatchable generation (a key CSP advantage over PV).
5. On demand, the hot salt flows to a **steam generator (heat exchanger)**, producing superheated steam.
6. Steam drives a **turbine → generator → grid** path.
7. Spent steam is condensed, cooled, and the cooled salt returns to the **cold storage tank**, completing the loop.
8. A **master controller** runs the **aim-point algorithm** to balance flux across the receiver face, reducing peak-flux hot spots.

### 4.3 Classification Tree of Focusing Collectors

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    CSC[Focusing Solar Collectors] --> LFC[Line Focusing Collectors 1 Axis Tracking]
    CSC --> PFC[Point Focusing Collectors 2 Axis Tracking]

    LFC --> PTC1[Parabolic Trough Collector]
    LFC --> LFR1[Linear Fresnel Reflector LFR]
    LFC --> CPC1[Compound Parabolic Concentrator CPC]

    PFC --> PD1[Parabolic Dish Concentrator]
    PFC --> ST1[Central Receiver Solar Tower]
    PFC --> LENS1[Fresnel Lens Point Focus]

    PTC1 --> USE1[Process Heat 200 to 400 C]
    ST1 --> USE2[Utility Scale Power 50 to 200 MW]
    PD1 --> USE3[Dish Stirling Unit 5 to 50 kW]
    LFR1 --> USE4[Low Cost Steam Generation]
```

### 4.4 Ray-Optics Schematic of a Parabolic Dish

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    DSH[Parabolic Dish Mirror] -->|Incoming parallel sun rays| DSH
    DSH -->|Reflected converging rays| FOC[Focal Point]
    DSH -->|Reflected converging rays| FOC
    DSH -->|Reflected converging rays| FOC
    FOC -->|Concentrated flux| RECD[Receiver Cavity at Focus]
    RECD -->|Heat to engine| STIR[Stirling Engine or Micro Turbine]
    STIR -->|Mechanical shaft| ALT[Alternator]
    ALT -->|Electricity| GRID3[Local Grid or Pump]
    TRKD[2 Axis Tracking Mount] -->|Rotates| DSH
    CTRD[Sun Sensor] -->|Feedback signal| TRKD
```

**Description:**
- The **parabolic dish mirror** (3-D paraboloid-of-revolution) intercepts parallel sun rays over its full aperture.
- All rays, regardless of where they strike the dish, reflect through the **focal point**, where the **receiver cavity** is mounted.
- The cavity absorbs the concentrated flux and heats the working gas (helium, hydrogen, or air) of a **Stirling engine** or micro-turbine.
- The engine drives an **alternator** producing electricity.
- A **2-axis tracking mount** keeps the dish aimed at the sun; a **sun sensor** provides the feedback control signal.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** [KTU University Exam - May 2024] Define the **Geometric Concentration Ratio** of a solar collector. Why do focusing collectors need to be tracked while flat-plate collectors do not?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The geometric concentration ratio is defined as the ratio of the **aperture area** $A_a$ of the collector to its **receiver area** $A_r$:

$$C_{g} = \frac{A_{a}}{A_{r}}$$

For a flat-plate collector, $C_g = 1$ (no concentration). For focusing collectors, $C_g > 1$ (e.g., 20 – 3000).

Tracking is required because the concentrating optics (parabola, dish, heliostat) focus sunlight onto a *small* receiver that occupies only a small fraction of the aperture. The sun's apparent motion (15°/hour) would shift the focal image off the receiver within minutes, destroying the concentration. Hence, focusing collectors employ **1-axis (line-focus)** or **2-axis (point-focus)** tracking to keep the optical axis sun-pointed. Flat-plate collectors can absorb diffuse and direct radiation over the full aperture, so alignment is not critical.

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip:** Award 1 mark for the formula, 1 mark for the explanation of $C_g$, 1 mark for the tracking reason.

---

**Q2.** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] Distinguish between **line-focusing** and **point-focusing** collectors. Give one example of each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Line-Focusing | Point-Focusing |
|---|---|
| Produces a focal *line* (1-D focus) | Produces a focal *point* (2-D focus) |
| 1-axis sun tracking | 2-axis sun tracking |
| Lower concentration ($C = 15$ – $80$) | Higher concentration ($C = 500$ – $3000$) |
| Moderate temperatures (≤ 400 °C) | High temperatures (500 – 1500 °C) |
| Example: **Parabolic trough**, Linear Fresnel | Example: **Parabolic dish**, Central receiver |

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip:** Each correct row of distinction = 0.5 marks; the example = 0.5 marks. Total 3 marks.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **maximum theoretical concentration ratio** of a (i) line-focusing and (ii) point-focusing solar collector. State the principle used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A parabolic trough has an aperture width of 4.5 m, rim angle of 75°, and length of 40 m. The receiver tube diameter is 65 mm. If the beam radiation is 850 W/m², the optical efficiency is 0.70, and the overall heat-loss coefficient is 7 W/(m²·K) with ambient temperature 28 °C, calculate:
  (i) the geometric concentration ratio,
  (ii) the useful heat collected at a receiver temperature of 280 °C, and
  (iii) the stagnation temperature. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to Q.A(a) (7 marks):**

**Principle used:** Conservation of **étendue** (also called the *principle of thermodynamic/optical limit*).

For a 2-D line-focus system, the étendue per unit length is $A \cdot \sin\theta$. Equating at aperture and receiver:

$$A_{a} \sin\theta_{c} = A_{r} \sin\theta_{s}$$

For a 3-D point-focus system, the étendue is $A \cdot \pi \sin^{2}\theta$. Equating:

$$A_{a} \pi \sin^{2}\theta_{c} = A_{r} \pi \sin^{2}\theta_{s}$$

**(i) Line-focus maximum:**

$$C_{max,2D} = \frac{A_{a}}{A_{r}} = \frac{\sin\theta_{s}}{\sin\theta_{c}}$$

In the limit $\theta_c \to \theta_s$:

$$C_{max,2D} = \frac{1}{\sin\theta_{s}}$$

**(ii) Point-focus maximum:**

$$C_{max,3D} = \frac{A_{a}}{A_{r}} = \frac{\sin^{2}\theta_{s}}{\sin^{2}\theta_{c}}$$

In the limit $\theta_c \to \theta_s$:

$$C_{max,3D} = \frac{1}{\sin^{2}\theta_{s}}$$

Plugging $\theta_s = 0.2667° \approx 4.654 \times 10^{-3}$ rad:

$$C_{max,2D} \approx 215, \quad C_{max,3D} \approx 46{,}200$$

---

**Solution to Q.A(b) (7 marks):**

**(i) Concentration ratio:**

Aperture area:

$$A_{a} = W \cdot L = 4.5 \times 40 = 180\ \text{m}^{2}$$

Receiver area:

$$A_{r} = \pi d_{r} L = \pi \times 0.065 \times 40 \approx 8.168\ \text{m}^{2}$$

Concentration ratio:

$$C_{g} = \frac{A_{a}}{A_{r}} = \frac{180}{8.168} \approx 22.04$$

**(ii) Useful heat at 280 °C:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
q_{abs} &= S_b \cdot A_{a} \cdot \eta_{optical} = 850 \times 180 \times 0.70 = 107{,}100\ \text{W} = 107.1\ \text{kW} \\
q_{loss} &= A_{r} \cdot U_L \cdot (T_{r} - T_{a}) = 8.168 \times 7 \times (280 - 28) = 8.168 \times 7 \times 252 \\
&= 14{,}408.4\ \text{W} = 14.41\ \text{kW} \\
q_{u} &= 107.1 - 14.41 = 92.69\ \text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Stagnation temperature:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{r,\,stag} &= T_{a} + \frac{S_{b} \cdot A_{a} \cdot \eta_{optical}}{A_{r} \cdot U_{L}} \\
&= 28 + \frac{850 \times 180 \times 0.70}{8.168 \times 7} \\
&= 28 + \frac{107{,}100}{57.176} \\
&= 28 + 1873.3 \\
&\approx 1901\ \text{°C}
\end{aligned}
$$

(Real stagnation is lower due to $U_L$ rising with $T$ and $\eta_{optical}$ dropping, but the textbook closed-form answer is 1901 °C.)

---

#### Question B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper — Internal Choice Alternative]**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, describe the **construction and working of a parabolic dish collector**. List its advantages and limitations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare **parabolic trough**, **parabolic dish**, and **central receiver** collectors on the basis of focus type, concentration ratio, tracking, operating temperature, optical losses, and storage capability. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to Q.B(a) (7 marks):**

**Construction (3 marks):**
- A **parabolic dish** is a 3-D paraboloid-of-revolution made of multiple small **mirror facets** (or a single stretched-membrane reflector).
- Each facet is shaped and oriented to contribute to a common **focal point** suspended above the dish centre.
- A **receiver cavity** is placed at the focus, typically containing a small heat exchanger coupled to a **Stirling engine** (or a micro-turbine, or a thermochemical reactor).
- The entire dish is mounted on a **2-axis tracking structure** (altitude-azimuth) that keeps the optical axis pointed at the sun.
- A **sun sensor** + **control electronics** drive the tracking motors.

**Working (2 marks):**
- Parallel sun rays striking the dish are reflected and concentrated at the focal point.
- The concentrated flux (typically 500 – 3000× ambient) heats the working fluid/gas in the receiver cavity to 500 – 1500 °C.
- The high-temperature working fluid drives a Stirling engine (closed-cycle helium) or micro-turbine, producing mechanical power, which an alternator converts to electricity.
- Because the dish is small (5 – 50 kW per unit), it is modular — multiple dishes can be networked in a dish-farm.

**Advantages (1 mark):**
- Highest concentration ratio and highest temperature among all CSCs.
- Modular and scalable.
- High conversion efficiency (dish-Stirling can reach ≈ 31% electrical).
- No water cooling needed (Stirling is closed-loop).

**Limitations (1 mark):**
- High cost per unit area (precision optics + 2-axis tracking).
- Wind sensitivity (large sail area, must stow in storms).
- Difficult to integrate with large-scale thermal storage.
- Receiver is at the focal point — high heat-loss path; long cables for power take-off.

**Sketch (describe verbally):**
A paraboloidal dish with rim angle ≈ 45° – 60°, focal point suspended in front, 2-axis mount underneath, receiver at focus coupled to engine, alternator at the base.

---

**Solution to Q.B(b) (7 marks):**

**Comparison Table (each row ≈ 1 mark):**

| Feature | Parabolic Trough | Parabolic Dish | Solar Tower |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Focus type** | Line | Point | Point (distributed) |
| **Concentration ratio** | 15 – 80 | 500 – 3000 | 300 – 1500 |
| **Tracking** | 1-axis | 2-axis | 2-axis (per heliostat) |
| **Operating temperature** | 200 – 400 °C | 500 – 1500 °C | 500 – 1400 °C |
| **Optical losses** | Lowest (0.15 – 0.20) | Moderate (0.30 – 0.40) | Highest (0.35 – 0.50) |
| **Thermal storage** | Easy (2-tank molten salt) | Difficult (small unit) | Excellent (large salt inventory) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Verdict:** Parabolic trough is the most commercially mature, parabolic dish has the highest peak efficiency, and solar tower offers the best dispatchability via thermal storage.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in Focusing-Collector Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units before plugging in.** Rim angles must be in **radians** (or degrees, but be consistent — and never mix them with trig functions). The KTU 2024 valuation key explicitly deducts 0.5 marks for unit-inconsistency errors.
>
> 2. **Confusing $1/\sin\theta_s$ with $1/\sin^2\theta_s$.** A line-focus system gives the *first*, a point-focus system gives the *second*. Mixing them up costs 1 full mark.
>
> 3. **Ignoring the glass-envelope term $\tau$.** The optical-efficiency product is $\rho \cdot \tau \cdot \alpha \cdot \gamma \cdot \kappa$. If the problem gives $\eta_{optical}$ directly, use it; if it gives individual factors, **multiply them all**, do not forget $\tau$ (typical value 0.94 – 0.97 for anti-reflection-coated borosilicate).
>
> 4. **Stagnation-temperature trap:** Students often write $T_{r,stag} = q_{abs}/(A_r U_L) + T_a$ and stop. The *complete* expression is $T_{r,stag} = T_a + (S_b A_a \eta_{opt})/(A_r U_L)$. Missing $T_a$ loses 0.5 marks.
>
> 5. **Aperture vs. receiver area confusion:** Aperture area is the projected area *normal to the sun rays*; for a trough, $A_a = W \cdot L$. Receiver area is the *actual surface area* of the tube, $A_r = \pi d_r L$. Do not use $d_r \cdot L$ for the receiver.
>
> 6. **CPC misclassification:** A *Compound Parabolic Concentrator* is a non-imaging line-focus device that can work stationary (with seasonal tilt). Students sometimes call it a "line-focusing" device — true, but they should mention it is *non-imaging* and *stationary* to score full marks.
>
> 7. **Comparison-table omissions:** For 7-mark comparison questions, KTU expects **at least 5 to 6 features** in a tabulated form. A short paragraph form usually scores ≤ 3 marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Focusing Collectors**

- **Core definition:** Focusing collector uses **optical concentration** ($C = A_a/A_r > 1$) to raise radiative flux at the receiver.
- **Two big families:** **Line-focusing** (parabolic trough, LFR, CPC) with 1-axis tracking; **point-focusing** (parabolic dish, solar tower, point Fresnel) with 2-axis tracking.
- **Étendue limit (theoretical maximum):**
  - Line focus: $C_{max,2D} = 1/\sin\theta_c$
  - Point focus: $C_{max,3D} = 1/\sin^2\theta_c$
- **For parabolic reflectors with rim angle $\phi$:** $C_{trough} = 1/\sin\phi$ (2-D) and $C_{dish} = 1/\sin^2\phi$ (3-D).
- **Rim angle range:** Practical $\phi = 60° - 90°$ for troughs; $45° - 60°$ for dishes.
- **Optical efficiency product:** $\eta_{optical} = \rho \cdot \tau \cdot \alpha \cdot \gamma \cdot \kappa$. Typical $\rho \approx 0.93$, $\tau \approx 0.96$, $\alpha \approx 0.95$.
- **Useful-heat formula:** $q_u = S_b A_a \eta_{optical} - A_r U_L (T_r - T_a)$.
- **Stagnation temperature:** $T_{r,stag} = T_a + (S_b A_a \eta_{optical})/(A_r U_L)$.
- **Cosine loss:** $I_{eff} = I_b \cos\theta_i$ — the penalty for off-axis sun alignment.
- **Why only beam (DNI) radiation?** Specular optics cannot focus diffuse radiation; CSCs require high-DNI sites (deserts, semi-arid regions).
- **Tracking necessity:** Without tracking, the focal image walks off the receiver in minutes — 1-axis for line-focus, 2-axis for point-focus.
- **Parabolic trough applications:** Noor (Morocco), SEGS (USA), Solana (Arizona) — utility-scale power, 10 – 250 MW.
- **Parabolic dish applications:** SES Dish-Stirling projects, eSolar's Sierra Sun Tower (hybrid).
- **Central receiver examples:** Ivanpah (USA, 392 MW), Crescent Dunes (USA, 110 MW with 10-hour storage), Noor III (Morocco, 150 MW with storage).
- **Thermal storage:** 2-tank molten-salt system (cold tank ≈ 290 °C, hot tank ≈ 565 °C) is the standard CSP storage — a *major* advantage of CSCs over PV.
- **Collector efficiency vs. temperature:** Higher $C_g$ → higher maximum temperature, but more sensitive to optical errors. Trade-off is fundamental.
- **Comparison-table memorisation:** Be ready to draw a 5–6-row comparison table for trough vs. dish vs. tower (focus, $C$, tracking, $T$, losses, storage).
- **Numerical-problem skill:** Always re-derive the formula, show every substitution step, and carry units to the final answer.
- **KTU 2024 CO mapping:** This topic primarily addresses **CO2** (Apply principles of solar radiation to design thermal collectors) and **CO3** (Analyse the performance of focusing-type solar collectors).
- **Bloom's level:** Most Part-A questions test *Remember/Understand*; Part-B questions test *Apply/Analyse*; numerical parts reach *Evaluate*.
- **Examiner's favourite traps:** Étendue-limit sign error, missing $T_a$ in stagnation, omitting $\tau$ from $\eta_{optical}$, mixing $A_a$ with $A_r$.

> **End of Module 2 — Focusing Collectors Notes (KTU 2024 Scheme, PECHT411)**

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT411 - ENERGY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Solar Water Heating System (SWHS):** A thermal conversion device that captures incident solar radiation using a **solar thermal collector** (Flat Plate Collector or Evacuated Tube Collector), converts it into usable thermal energy, and transfers this heat to a working fluid (typically water) for domestic, commercial, or industrial hot water applications. The system is governed by the energy balance principle: **Incident Solar Energy = Useful Heat Gain + Heat Losses + Reflected Energy**.

The governing energy balance for a solar collector is:

$$Q_{incident} = Q_{useful} + Q_{losses} + Q_{reflected}$$

Or, in terms of the solar collector's instantaneous thermal efficiency ($\eta_i$):

$$\eta_i = \frac{Q_u}{A_c \cdot I_t} = \frac{Useful~Heat~Gained}{Solar~Energy~Incident~on~Collector}$$

Where:
- $A_c$ = Effective collector aperture area (m²)
- $I_t$ = Total (beam + diffuse) solar radiation on the tilted collector surface (W/m²)
- $Q_u$ = Useful heat gain rate (W)

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]  
> **Plain English Analogy:** Imagine leaving a black garden hose full of water in the sun on a hot afternoon. After a few hours, the water inside becomes noticeably warm — even hot. This is a *passive* solar water heater in its most primitive form. A Solar Water Heating System is simply the *engineered, optimized version* of this same phenomenon. A black surface (absorber plate) absorbs sunlight, a transparent cover (glass) traps heat like a greenhouse, insulation prevents heat from escaping, and a fluid (water) is routed through tubes attached to that hot absorber plate to carry the warmth to a storage tank for use later — much like a kettle on a sunny windowsill.

### 1.3 Why Solar Water Heating?

- It is the **most mature and commercially deployed** solar thermal technology globally.
- Reduces dependence on **fossil fuels** (LPG, electricity) for water heating.
- Provides **zero-emission** hot water, supporting India's National Solar Mission targets.
- Lifespan of **15–20 years** with minimal maintenance.
- Payback period typically **3–5 years** for domestic systems in Kerala.

### 1.4 Standard Performance Metrics (Highlighted)

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> The following standard KTU metrics MUST be memorized:
> - **Hottel-Whillier-Bliss (HWB) Equation** — the fundamental design equation for solar thermal collectors.
> - **Heat Removal Factor ($F_R$)** — dimensionless ratio of actual useful heat gain to maximum possible useful heat gain.
> - **Overall Heat Loss Coefficient ($U_L$)** — measured in **W/m²K**, representing total thermal resistance of the collector enclosure.
> - **Transmittance-Absorptance Product ($\tau\alpha$)** — dimensionless product representing optical efficiency of the collector cover-absorbent system.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

A solar water heater operates on the **greenhouse effect combined with selective absorption**. The sequence of energy transfer is:

1. **Solar radiation ($I_t$)** strikes the transparent cover (usually low-iron tempered glass, $\tau \approx 0.91$).
2. A fraction is **reflected** (reducing effective input); the rest is **transmitted** to the absorber plate.
3. The **absorber plate** (coated with a *selective surface* like black chrome or titanium nitride-oxynitride) absorbs $\sim 95\%$ of incident radiation.
4. The plate temperature rises, transferring heat to **risers/tubes** bonded to or integral with the plate.
5. **Water/glycol** flowing through the tubes picks up this heat (sensible heating).
6. Simultaneously, the hot plate **loses heat** through the glass cover (top loss), the back insulation (bottom loss), and the edges (side loss).
7. The net *useful heat* is the difference: $Q_u = Q_{absorbed} - Q_{losses}$.

### 2.2 Types of Solar Water Heating Systems

**A) Passive Systems (No pump — natural circulation drives flow)**

| Type | Description | Application |
|------|-------------|-------------|
| **Thermosiphon** | Cold water at bottom of tank gets heated; rises naturally; replaced by cold water from mains. Tank MUST be above collector. | Domestic hot water |
| **Integral Collector Storage (ICS) / Batch Heater** | Water storage tank IS the collector. Used in non-freezing climates. | Warm climates like Kerala |

**B) Active Systems (Electric pump drives circulation)**

| Type | Description | Application |
|------|-------------|-------------|
| **Direct (Open-loop)** | Water directly circulates through collector. | Non-freezing areas, hard-water avoided |
| **Indirect (Closed-loop)** | Heat transfer fluid (glycol-water mix) circulates; heat is exchanged to potable water via a heat exchanger. | Freezing climates, hard-water areas |

### 2.3 Types of Solar Collectors Used

> [!NOTE]  
> **Flat Plate Collector (FPC):** Box-type insulated collector with a flat absorber plate, glass cover, and tubes. Operating range: **30°C – 100°C**. Best suited for Kerala.
>
> **Evacuated Tube Collector (ETC):** Series of double-walled borosilicate glass tubes with vacuum between. Vacuum eliminates convection losses. Operating range: **70°C – 200°C**. Better in cloudy/cold conditions.

### 2.4 Hottel-Whillier-Bliss (HWB) Equation — The Heart of the Topic

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> The **Hottel-Whillier-Bliss Equation** is the single most important equation for solar thermal design in KTU examinations. It must be memorized and applied correctly.

The instantaneous thermal efficiency of a flat-plate solar collector is:

$$\boxed{\eta_i = F_R(\tau\alpha) - F_R U_L \frac{T_i - T_a}{I_t}}$$

The useful heat gain rate:

$$\boxed{Q_u = A_c \left[ F_R(\tau\alpha) I_t - F_R U_L (T_i - T_a) \right]}$$

Where:
- $F_R$ = **Heat Removal Factor** (dimensionless, $0 < F_R < 1$)
- $(\tau\alpha)$ = Effective transmittance-absorptance product (dimensionless)
- $U_L$ = Overall heat loss coefficient (W/m²·K)
- $T_i$ = Inlet fluid temperature (°C or K)
- $T_a$ = Ambient temperature (°C or K)
- $I_t$ = Total solar radiation on tilted surface (W/m²)
- $A_c$ = Collector aperture area (m²)

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Description | Typical Values / Units |
|---|---------------------|-------------|------------------------|
| 1 | $\eta_i = \dfrac{Q_u}{A_c I_t}$ | Instantaneous efficiency | Dimensionless, 0–1 |
| 2 | $Q_u = A_c I_t (\tau\alpha) - A_c U_L (T_p - T_a)$ | Useful heat (plate-based form) | W |
| 3 | $\eta_i = F_R(\tau\alpha) - F_R U_L \dfrac{T_i - T_a}{I_t}$ | HWB Equation (Hottel-Whillier-Bliss) | Dimensionless |
| 4 | $Q_u = A_c \left[ F_R(\tau\alpha) I_t - F_R U_L (T_i - T_a) \right]$ | Useful heat in HWB form | W |
| 5 | $U_L = U_t + U_b + U_s$ | Total loss coefficient (sum of top, bottom, side) | W/m²·K |
| 6 | $F_R = \dfrac{\dot{m} C_p}{A_c U_L} \left[ 1 - \exp\left(-\dfrac{A_c U_L F'}{\dot{m} C_p}\right) \right]$ | Heat Removal Factor (HWB derivation) | Dimensionless |
| 7 | $F' = \dfrac{1/U_L}{(W (D + (W - D)F))^{-1} + \dfrac{1}{\pi D h_{f,i}}}$ | Collector Efficiency Factor | Dimensionless |
| 8 | $F = \dfrac{\tanh[m(W - D)/2]}{m(W - D)/2}$ | Fin efficiency of plate between tubes | Dimensionless |
| 9 | $m = \sqrt{\dfrac{U_L}{k \delta}}$ | Fin parameter (plate conduction) | 1/m |
| 10 | $q_s = \dfrac{Q_u}{A_c}$ | Specific heat gain per unit area | W/m² |
| 11 | $C_R = \dfrac{Q_u}{A_c I_t} = \eta_i$ | Conversion efficiency | Dimensionless |
| 12 | $T_{stagnation} = T_a + \dfrac{I_t (\tau\alpha)}{U_L}$ | Stagnation temperature (no flow) | °C |

> [!WARNING]  
> **Examination Pitfall:** Students often confuse the **heat removal factor ($F_R$)** with the **collector efficiency factor ($F'$)** and the **fin efficiency ($F$)**. These are three DIFFERENT quantities. $F$ deals with fin geometry, $F'$ combines fin + tube heat transfer, and $F_R$ accounts for fluid flow.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Domestic sector:** Hot water for bathing, washing in homes, hostels, hospitals.
- **Industrial sector:** Pre-heating boiler feed water, process heat in dairy, textile, food processing.
- **Commercial sector:** Hotels, hostels, swimming pool heating, laundry.
- **MNRE (Ministry of New & Renewable Energy, India)** offers subsidies for installation; MNRE-approved models are the industry benchmark.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Hottel-Whillier-Bliss (HWB) Equation

**Step 1 — Start with the energy balance on the absorber plate.**

The useful heat gained by the fluid per unit collector area is equal to the absorbed solar radiation minus all thermal losses from the plate:

$$q_u = S - U_L (T_p - T_a)$$

Where:
- $S = I_t (\tau\alpha)$ = absorbed solar radiation per unit area (W/m²)
- $U_L$ = overall heat loss coefficient per unit area (W/m²·K)
- $T_p$ = mean absorber plate temperature (°C)
- $T_a$ = ambient air temperature (°C)

**Step 2 — Introduce the Collector Efficiency Factor $F'$.**

In practice, the fluid is at a temperature $T_f$ (between $T_i$ and $T_o$), which is *lower* than the plate temperature $T_p$. The relationship is:

$$q_u = F' \left[ S - U_L (T_f - T_a) \right]$$

This is because heat must conduct *through* the plate fins and *across* the tube bond. The factor $F'$ (between 0 and 1) accounts for this temperature drop.

**Step 3 — Replace fluid temperature with inlet temperature using $F_R$.**

The actual fluid inlet temperature is $T_i$, and during flow the fluid heats up to outlet $T_o$. A *heat removal factor* $F_R$ relates the useful heat at inlet conditions to the maximum possible useful heat:

$$q_u = F_R \left[ S - U_L (T_i - T_a) \right]$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $S = I_t (\tau\alpha)$.**

$$q_u = F_R \left[ (\tau\alpha) I_t - U_L (T_i - T_a) \right]$$

**Step 5 — Recognize that $\eta_i = q_u / I_t$.**

$$\eta_i = F_R(\tau\alpha) - F_R U_L \frac{T_i - T_a}{I_t}$$

This is the **HWB Equation**.

**Step 6 — Total useful heat for the whole collector.**

Multiplying by collector area $A_c$:

$$Q_u = A_c \left[ F_R(\tau\alpha) I_t - F_R U_L (T_i - T_a) \right]$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Heat Removal Factor $F_R$

**Step 1 — Start from the collector efficiency factor relation.**

$$q_u = F' \left[ S - U_L (T_f - T_a) \right]$$

**Step 2 — Energy balance on the fluid stream over differential length $dx$.**

$$\dot{m} C_p \, dT_f = U_L (T_p - T_f) \, W \, dx$$

After integration over the full collector length $L$, and using a logarithmic mean temperature difference approach, the final expression for $F_R$ is:

$$F_R = \frac{\dot{m} C_p}{A_c U_L} \left[ 1 - \exp\left( -\frac{A_c U_L F'}{\dot{m} C_p} \right) \right]$$

**Step 3 — Limiting cases (Sanity check).**

- As $\dot{m} \to \infty$ (high flow): $F_R \to F'$ (factor limited only by conduction).
- As $\dot{m} \to 0$ (stagnation): $F_R \to 0$ (no heat removed because no flow).

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Useful Heat in Terms of Outlet and Inlet Temperatures

Energy balance on the fluid over the entire collector:

$$\dot{m} C_p (T_o - T_i) = Q_u = A_c \left[ F_R(\tau\alpha) I_t - F_R U_L (T_i - T_a) \right]$$

Solving for the outlet temperature:

$$T_o = T_i + \frac{A_c}{\dot{m} C_p} \left[ F_R(\tau\alpha) I_t - F_R U_L (T_i - T_a) \right]$$

> [!NOTE]  
> This expression is **highly useful** in KTU numerical problems when $\dot{m}$, $A_c$, $F_R$, $U_L$, $\tau\alpha$, $I_t$, and $T_i$ are given, and you must find $T_o$ or $Q_u$.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Typical Numerical Problem & Solution

**Sample Problem (KTU-style):**

A flat-plate solar collector with aperture area $A_c = 2.5 \text{ m}^2$ receives solar radiation $I_t = 800 \text{ W/m}^2$. The transmittance-absorptance product is $\tau\alpha = 0.78$, the heat removal factor is $F_R = 0.85$, and the overall heat loss coefficient is $U_L = 5.2 \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}$. The inlet water temperature is $T_i = 30°\text{C}$ and the ambient temperature is $T_a = 25°\text{C}$. The mass flow rate of water is $\dot{m} = 0.02 \text{ kg/s}$ with $C_p = 4180 \text{ J/kg·K}$. Find:
1. The useful heat gain $Q_u$.
2. The instantaneous efficiency $\eta_i$.
3. The outlet water temperature $T_o$.

**Solution:**

**Part (1) — Useful Heat Gain:**

$$Q_u = A_c \left[ F_R (\tau\alpha) I_t - F_R U_L (T_i - T_a) \right]$$

Substituting:

$$Q_u = 2.5 \times \left[ 0.85 \times 0.78 \times 800 - 0.85 \times 5.2 \times (30 - 25) \right]$$

$$Q_u = 2.5 \times \left[ 530.4 - 22.1 \right]$$

$$Q_u = 2.5 \times 508.3$$

$$\boxed{Q_u = 1270.75 \text{ W} \approx 1.27 \text{ kW}}$$

**Part (2) — Instantaneous Efficiency:**

$$\eta_i = \frac{Q_u}{A_c I_t} = \frac{1270.75}{2.5 \times 800} = \frac{1270.75}{2000}$$

$$\boxed{\eta_i = 0.6354 \text{ or } 63.54\%}$$

**Cross-verification via HWB formula:**

$$\eta_i = F_R(\tau\alpha) - F_R U_L \frac{T_i - T_a}{I_t}$$

$$\eta_i = (0.85 \times 0.78) - (0.85 \times 5.2) \times \frac{(30 - 25)}{800}$$

$$\eta_i = 0.663 - 4.42 \times \frac{5}{800}$$

$$\eta_i = 0.663 - 0.027625$$

$$\boxed{\eta_i = 0.6354 \text{ ✓}}$$

**Part (3) — Outlet Temperature:**

Using energy balance on the fluid:

$$\dot{m} C_p (T_o - T_i) = Q_u$$

$$0.02 \times 4180 \times (T_o - 30) = 1270.75$$

$$83.6 \times (T_o - 30) = 1270.75$$

$$T_o - 30 = 15.20$$

$$\boxed{T_o = 45.20°\text{C}}$$

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Heat Loss Breakdown for a Flat Plate Collector

| Loss Component | Symbol | Description | Typical Range (W/m²K) |
|----------------|--------|-------------|------------------------|
| **Top loss** | $U_t$ | Convection + radiation from plate to glass to sky | 3.0 – 6.0 |
| **Bottom loss** | $U_b$ | Conduction through insulation at the back | 0.4 – 1.0 |
| **Side loss** | $U_s$ | Conduction through edge frame | 0.1 – 0.3 |
| **Total loss** | $U_L = U_t + U_b + U_s$ | Sum | **3.5 – 7.0** |

For an **Evacuated Tube Collector**, $U_t$ is drastically reduced (to ~1.0 W/m²K) due to the vacuum, so $U_L \approx 1.0 - 2.0$ W/m²K, which is why ETCs perform better in cold weather.

### 4.4 Passive Thermosiphon System Layout

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Cold Water in Storage Tank] -->|Feeds by Gravity| B[Inlet Header at Bottom of Collector]
    B --> C[Heated Water Rises through Risers]
    C --> D[Hot Water Outlet Header at Top]
    D --> E[Hot Water Returns to Top of Storage Tank]
    E --> F[Hot Water Available at Top of Tank to User]
    G[Tank MUST be Mounted Above Collector Level] --- A
    H[No Pump Required Natural Convection Drives Flow] --- C
```

> [!NOTE]  
> The thermosiphon principle works because **hot water is less dense** than cold water. The buoyancy force drives circulation continuously as long as sunlight is incident. This is why the storage tank must be physically elevated above the collector — a critical design rule students often miss in exams.

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`  
**Define a Solar Water Heating System (SWHS). List any two advantages of solar water heating over conventional electric water heating.**

**Model Answer:**  
A Solar Water Heating System (SWHS) is a device that converts incident solar radiation into thermal energy and transfers it to a working fluid (water) for end-use applications. The system typically comprises a solar collector, storage tank, piping, and (in active systems) a pump.

**Advantages:**
1. **Renewable & zero-emission** — reduces carbon footprint and LPG/electricity bills by 60–80%.
2. **Long operational life** — typical lifespan of 15–20 years with minimal maintenance, and short payback period (3–5 years).

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`  
**Differentiate between Flat Plate Collector (FPC) and Evacuated Tube Collector (ETC).**

**Model Answer:**
