- **"MPP"** = **M**ove **P**oint **P**arallel to where $\frac{dP}{dV} = 0$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Energy Plantations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT411 - ENERGY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Energy Plantations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 2 – Energy Plantations

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition (Energy Plantation):**
> An **Energy Plantation** is a dedicated, managed, and intensively cultivated area of land where fast-growing, high-biomass-yielding plant species (trees, shrubs, grasses, or aquatic biomass) are raised as a renewable agricultural/agroforestry crop for the specific purpose of generating bioenergy in the form of solid fuels (fuelwood, charcoal), liquid fuels (biodiesel, bioethanol), or gaseous fuels (biogas, producer gas).

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy – "The Energy Farm"**
> Think of an **Energy Plantation** exactly like a "fuel farm." Instead of a wheat farmer growing grain to bake bread, an energy plantation farmer grows **fast-growing trees (like Eucalyptus or Poplar)** as a "biological coal mine." When harvested, this "biomass crop" is burned directly in a boiler, gasified into producer gas, or fermented into ethanol — much the way crude oil is drilled and refined into petrol. The land is used as a **biological solar collector**: the trees absorb sunlight through photosynthesis, lock the solar energy into chemical bonds (cellulose, lignin, oils), and we "harvest" that stored sunshine on demand.

### Why Energy Plantations Matter in KTU Syllabus

| Metric | Typical Value (Highlighted Constant) |
|---|---|
| Average photosynthetic efficiency of plantations | **~1–2 %** of incident solar energy |
| Rotation cycle of short-rotation forestry | **2–8 years** |
| Biomass yield (tropical Eucalyptus) | **20–40 dry tonnes/ha/year** |
| Calorific value of woody biomass (air-dry) | **~18 MJ/kg (≈ 5,000 kWh/t)** |
| Energy plantation land requirement | **~1–2 % of total land** for replacing fossil fuels |

### Classification of Energy Plantations (KTU Taxonomy)

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Recognized Categories (IPCC / KTU):**
> 1. **Short-Rotation Forestry (SRF)** — fast-growing trees (Eucalyptus, Poplar, Casuarina), coppiced every 2–6 years.
> 2. **Short-Rotation Coppice (SRC)** — woody crops harvested every 1–3 years (Willow, Poplar).
> 3. **Energy Grasses / Agricultural Energy Crops** — herbaceous plants like *Miscanthus*, Switchgrass, Jatropha, sugarcane.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — The "Why" and "How"

### Step 1: Solar Energy Capture
The plantation canopy intercepts solar radiation. Only a fraction is converted via photosynthesis into dry matter (net primary productivity, NPP).

### Step 2: Biomass Accumulation
Carbohydrates (cellulose, hemicellulose, lignin) and oils accumulate in stems, leaves, seeds, or roots as stored chemical energy.

### Step 3: Harvesting & Conversion
Biomass is harvested, dried, and converted using:
- **Thermochemical** route → combustion, gasification, pyrolysis (yield heat, syngas, biochar).
- **Biochemical** route → fermentation (bioethanol), anaerobic digestion (biogas).
- **Extraction** route → oil pressing & transesterification (biodiesel from Jatropha, Pongamia).

### Step 4: Energy Delivery
The chemical energy is delivered as electricity, heat, or transport fuel — completing the renewable cycle, with **carbon-neutral** CO₂ release on combustion (re-absorbed in next rotation).

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they form 70 % of numerical problems in KTU ESE.**

| # | Formula / Quantity | Symbolic Form | Standard Unit | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Energy yield of plantation | $E_p = Y_b \times CV \times \eta_c$ | MJ/ha/yr | Useful energy per hectare per year |
| 2 | Biomass productivity | $Y_b = \dfrac{M_h}{A \cdot T}$ | t/ha/yr | Dry matter yield per unit area & time |
| 3 | Calorific value (wet basis) | $CV_w = CV_d \times \dfrac{(100 - M)}{100}$ | MJ/kg | Heat released per kg of moist fuel |
| 4 | Land area required | $A_{req} = \dfrac{E_{demand}}{Y_b \cdot CV \cdot \eta_c}$ | hectares | Plantation footprint to meet demand |
| 5 | CO₂ mitigation | $m_{CO_2} = Y_b \times CC \times \dfrac{44}{12}$ | t CO₂/ha/yr | Carbon offset per hectare |
| 6 | Energy ratio (EROI) | $EROI = \dfrac{E_{out}}{E_{in}}$ | dimensionless | Energy profitability of the plantation |
| 7 | Photosynthetic efficiency | $\eta_p = \dfrac{Y_b \cdot CV}{H_{solar} \cdot A \cdot T}$ | % | Fraction of sunlight stored |
| 8 | Moisture content | $M = \dfrac{W_w - W_d}{W_w} \times 100$ | % | Wet-basis moisture of biomass |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Where:**
> - $Y_b$ = biomass yield (tonnes/ha/yr), $CV$ = calorific value (MJ/kg), $\eta_c$ = conversion efficiency (decimal).
> - $M$ = moisture %, $CC$ = carbon content of dry biomass (~**50 %** by mass).
> - $H_{solar}$ = total incident solar radiation (MJ/ha/yr).

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Energy plantations are the **backbone of rural bioenergy programs in Kerala & India**. The National Bioenergy Mission promotes plantations of **Jatropha** (biodiesel) on wastelands, **Eucalyptus** (combustion/gasifier feed) by Kerala State Electricity Board (KSEB) for 1–10 MW biomass power plants, and **Casuarina** by pulp-paper industries co-generating process steam. They solve three problems simultaneously: **waste-land reclamation**, **rural employment**, and **displaced fossil-fuel imports**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Net Useful Energy Yield of an Energy Plantation

### Statement
Derive an expression for the net useful thermal energy obtainable per hectare per year from a managed energy plantation.

### Given
- Annual biomass yield (dry basis): $Y_b$ [tonnes/ha/year]
- Calorific value of dry biomass: $CV_d$ [MJ/kg]
- Average moisture content of harvested biomass: $M$ [%]
- Conversion efficiency of the downstream energy converter: $\eta_c$ [decimal]

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Express dry yield in kg per hectare per year**

$$Y_b\,[\text{kg/ha/yr}] = Y_b\,[\text{t/ha/yr}] \times 1000$$

*(Multiplication logic: 1 tonne = 1000 kg; this converts the mass unit so it matches the calorific value unit of MJ/kg.)*

**Step 2 — Account for moisture in the harvested fuel**

The calorific value reduces proportionally to the dry matter fraction $(100 - M)/100$:

$$CV_w = CV_d \times \frac{(100 - M)}{100}$$

*(Conversion logic: moisture acts as inert mass that absorbs latent heat of vaporization during combustion but contributes no chemical energy; therefore we scale $CV_d$ by the dry-matter fraction.)*

**Step 3 — Total gross thermal energy per hectare per year**

Multiply the (now moisture-corrected) calorific value by the dry mass:

$$E_{gross} = Y_b \times 1000 \times CV_d \times \frac{(100 - M)}{100}$$

*(Unit check: tonnes/ha/yr × 1000 kg/t × MJ/kg = MJ/ha/yr.)*

**Step 4 — Apply conversion efficiency of downstream converter**

The usable fraction is $\eta_c$ (e.g., 0.25 for electricity generation, 0.80 for direct combustion heating):

$$\boxed{E_{useful} = Y_b \times 1000 \times CV_d \times \frac{(100 - M)}{100} \times \eta_c \;\;\;[\text{MJ/ha/yr}]}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation II — Land Area Required to Meet a Specified Energy Demand

**Given**: Annual community energy demand $E_{demand}$ [MJ/yr] and the same plantation parameters as above.

**Step 1 — Equate demand to useful supply per hectare**

$$E_{demand} = A_{req} \times E_{useful}$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $A_{req}$**

$$A_{req} = \frac{E_{demand}}{Y_b \times 1000 \times CV_d \times \frac{(100 - M)}{100} \times \eta_c}$$

$$\boxed{A_{req} = \frac{100 \cdot E_{demand}}{1000 \cdot Y_b \cdot CV_d \cdot (100 - M) \cdot \eta_c} \;\;\;[\text{hectares}]}$$

*(Algebraic logic: isolate $A_{req}$ by dividing both sides by the per-hectare supply expression; the factor of 100 cancels the percentage form of moisture.)*

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU-Style Problem)

> **Problem:** A village requires **5,000 GJ of useful heat per year** from a **Eucalyptus** energy plantation. The species yields **25 t dry biomass/ha/year**, $CV_d = 18$ MJ/kg, harvested moisture $M = 40$%, and the boiler efficiency $\eta_c = 0.75$.
>
> **(a)** Compute useful energy delivered per hectare per year.
> **(b)** Find the plantation area required.

### Solution (a) — Per-hectare Useful Energy

Applying the boxed formula from §3.1:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{useful} &= Y_b \times 1000 \times CV_d \times \frac{(100 - M)}{100} \times \eta_c \\
&= 25 \times 1000 \times 18 \times \frac{(100 - 40)}{100} \times 0.75 \\
&= 25\,000 \times 18 \times 0.60 \times 0.75 \\
&= 25\,000 \times 18 \times 0.45 \\
&= 202\,500 \;\text{MJ/ha/yr} \\
&= 202.5 \;\text{GJ/ha/yr}
\end{aligned}
$$

*(Conversion logic: 25 t × 1000 = 25 000 kg; multiply by 18 MJ/kg → 450 000 MJ gross; reduce by 40 % moisture factor (×0.60) → 270 000 MJ; reduce by boiler efficiency 0.75 → 202 500 MJ = 202.5 GJ.)*

### Solution (b) — Area Required

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{req} &= \frac{E_{demand}}{E_{useful}} \\
&= \frac{5\,000 \;\text{GJ/yr}}{202.5 \;\text{GJ/ha/yr}} \\
&= 24.69 \;\text{ha} \;\approx\; \mathbf{25 \;\text{hectares}}
\end{aligned}
$$

*(Logic: 5 000 ÷ 202.5 = 24.69; round up to 25 ha to ensure demand is fully met.)*

---

## 3.4 CO₂ Mitigation Derivation (Analytical Matrix)

**Step 1 — Carbon fixed per hectare per year**

Since ~**50 %** of dry biomass is carbon (the IPCC default for woody biomass):

$$C_{fixed} = 0.50 \times Y_b \;\;\;[\text{t C/ha/yr}]$$

**Step 2 — Convert C to CO₂ equivalent**

The molar mass ratio is $\dfrac{M_{CO_2}}{M_C} = \dfrac{44}{12} \approx 3.67$:

$$m_{CO_2} = 0.50 \times Y_b \times 3.67 = 1.835 \times Y_b \;\;\;[\text{t CO}_2\text{/ha/yr}]$$

For $Y_b = 25$ t/ha/yr:

$$m_{CO_2} = 1.835 \times 25 \approx 45.9 \;\text{t CO}_2\text{/ha/yr avoided}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of an Energy Plantation System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[SUN Solar Radiation H solar] --> C1[Photosynthesis Canopy]
    C1 --> B1[Biomass Accumulation Stems Leaves Seeds]
    B1 --> H1[Harvesting 2 to 6 yr Rotation]
    H1 --> D1[Drying and Size Reduction]
    D1 --> R1{Route Selection}
    R1 -- Thermochemical --> T1[Combustion Gasification Pyrolysis]
    R1 -- Biochemical --> T2[Fermentation Anaerobic Digestion]
    R1 -- Extraction --> T3[Oil Pressing Transesterification]
    T1 --> E1[Heat Steam Electricity]
    T2 --> E2[Bioethanol Biogas]
    T3 --> E3[Biodiesel]
    E1 --> U1[End User Industry Household Grid]
    E2 --> U1
    E3 --> U1
    U1 -.CO2 to atmosphere.-> S1
    U1 -.Replanting cycle.-> H1
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Decision Matrix for Plantation Type

```mermaid

    A[Land Available Wasteland or Agricultural] --> B{Climate and Soil Type}
    B -- Tropical High Rainfall --> P1[Eucalyptus Casuarina Subabul]
    B -- Temperate Moderate --> P2[Poplar Willow SRC]
    B -- Arid Semi Arid --> P3[Jatropha Pongamia Karanj]
    B -- Coastal Saline --> P4[Casuarina Equisetifolia]
    B -- Wetland Paddy --> P5[Water Hyacinth Aquatic Biomass]
    P1 --> OUT[Bioenergy Feedstock]
    P2 --> OUT
    P3 --> OUT
    P4 --> OUT
    P5 --> OUT
```

## 4.3 Process-Flow Schematic — Numerical Evaluation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN1[Y b tonnes per ha per yr] --> M1
    M2[CV d MJ per kg] --> M1
    M3[Moisture M percent] --> M1
    M4[Eta c decimal] --> M1
    M1[Useful Energy per ha per yr Equation] --> M5
    M6[E demand MJ per yr] --> M5
    M5[Land Area Required hectares] --> OUT1[Plantation Footprint Design]
    M1 --> M7
    M7[CO2 Mitigation tonnes per ha per yr] --> OUT2[Carbon Credit Reporting]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define an energy plantation. Mention any four fast-growing tree species suitable for energy plantations in India.**

**Model Answer:**
An energy plantation is a deliberately established, intensively managed stand of fast-growing, high-biomass species cultivated solely as a renewable feedstock for bioenergy production (heat, power, or liquid/gaseous fuels). Four suitable species are:
1. **Eucalyptus** (hybrid clones – high coppice yield),
2. **Casuarina** (tolerant of saline/coastal soils),
3. **Subabul / Leucaena leucocephala** (N-fixing, high calorific value),
4. **Poplar** (preferred in Indo-Gangetic plains for short-rotation forestry).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students frequently list fruit trees (mango, coconut) — these are *not* energy plantation species because their biomass productivity and coppice response are too low. Stick to **fast-growing, coppiceable** species only.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Distinguish between Short-Rotation Forestry (SRF) and Short-Rotation Coppice (SRC).**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Short-Rotation Forestry (SRF) | Short-Rotation Coppice (SRC) |
|---|---|---|
| Rotation period | 4–8 years | 1–3 years |
| Harvest method | Clear-felled at end of rotation | Repeatedly coppiced from the same stump |
| Typical species | Eucalyptus, Casuarina | Willow, Poplar |
| Plant density | 1,000–2,500 stems/ha | 10,000–20,000 stems/ha |
| Product | Logs, chips, pulpwood | Chips, fuelwood |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

### Question A `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** Derive an expression for the useful energy yield per hectare per year of an energy plantation, including the effect of moisture content and conversion efficiency. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A community of 2,000 households needs **15 TJ of thermal energy per year** from an energy plantation of *Casuarina equisetifolia*. Given: $Y_b = 30$ t/ha/year, $CV_d = 19$ MJ/kg, $M = 35$%, $\eta_c = 0.80$. Determine the plantation area required and the annual CO₂ offset. **(7 marks)**

### Solution A(a)

Following §3.1:

$$E_{useful} = Y_b \times 1000 \times CV_d \times \frac{(100 - M)}{100} \times \eta_c \;\;[\text{MJ/ha/yr}]$$

Derivation steps (write all four transitions):
1. Convert $Y_b$ from tonnes to kg.
2. Reduce $CV_d$ by the moisture factor $(100 - M)/100$ to obtain $CV_w$.
3. Multiply to obtain gross energy.
4. Multiply by $\eta_c$ for useful energy.

### Solution A(b)

**Step 1 — Useful energy per hectare:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{useful} &= 30 \times 1000 \times 19 \times \frac{65}{100} \times 0.80 \\
&= 30\,000 \times 19 \times 0.65 \times 0.80 \\
&= 30\,000 \times 19 \times 0.52 \\
&= 30\,000 \times 9.88 \\
&= 296\,400 \;\text{MJ/ha/yr} = 296.4 \;\text{GJ/ha/yr}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Area required:**

$$A_{req} = \frac{15\,000 \;\text{GJ/yr}}{296.4 \;\text{GJ/ha/yr}} \approx 50.6 \;\text{ha} \;\Rightarrow\; \mathbf{51 \;\text{ha}}$$

**Step 3 — CO₂ offset:**

$$m_{CO_2} = 1.835 \times Y_b \times A_{req} = 1.835 \times 30 \times 51 \approx 2\,807 \;\text{t CO}_2\text{/yr avoided}$$

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the criteria for selection of species for an energy plantation. List at least six criteria with justification. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the process flow from solar radiation to delivered bioenergy in an energy plantation system. **(7 marks)**

### Solution B(a)

**Six Selection Criteria (each = 1 mark + 0.2 justification):**
1. **High biomass productivity** (≥ 20 t/ha/yr) — maximises land-use efficiency.
2. **Short rotation / rapid growth** — quicker energy returns.
3. **Coppicing ability** — regrowth from stump avoids replanting cost.
4. **High calorific value** — reduces fuel mass per MJ delivered.
5. **Pest/disease resistance & drought tolerance** — low input cost, robust yields.
6. **Compatibility with local soil & climate** — ensures survival on marginal/wasteland.
7. *(Bonus)* Non-invasiveness & low water footprint.

### Solution B(b)

Draw/describe the block flow:

**Solar radiation → Photosynthetic canopy → Biomass accumulation → Harvesting → Drying/size reduction → Conversion (combustion/gasification/fermentation) → End-use (heat/power/fuel) → CO₂ recycle to next rotation.**

[Refer to the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.1 of these notes.]

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning – Common Mark-Deduction Zones:**
> 1. **Forgetting moisture correction** — losing 2–3 marks in numericals where $M \neq 0$.
> 2. **Mixing t/ha/yr units with kg** — always multiply by 1000 and state the unit explicitly.
> 3. **Using gross instead of net calorific value** — KTU expects NCV in gasification problems; CV in combustion problems.
> 4. **Ignoring conversion efficiency** — KTU explicitly tests $\eta_c$ in every numerical.
> 5. **Forgetting the 3.67 carbon-to-CO₂ conversion factor** in mitigation problems.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **⚡ Rapid Revision Checklist — Energy Plantations**

- **Definition:** Purpose-grown, fast-rotating biomass stand for energy, not timber/pulp.
- **Three categories:** SRF, SRC, Energy grasses/Agri-crops.
- **Top species (India):** Eucalyptus, Casuarina, Subabul, Poplar, Jatropha, Pongamia, *Miscanthus*.
- **Master formula:** $E_{useful} = Y_b \cdot 1000 \cdot CV_d \cdot (100 - M)/100 \cdot \eta_c$.
- **Land area:** $A_{req} = E_{demand} / E_{useful}$.
- **Moisture rule:** always reduce CV by $(100 - M)/100$.
- **CO₂ offset:** $1.835 \times Y_b$ t CO₂/ha/yr for any plantation.
- **Carbon content of dry biomass:** ~**50 %** (IPCC default).
- **Photosynthetic efficiency cap:** 1–2 % — plantations are land-intensive.
- **Calorific value benchmarks:** Wood ≈ **18 MJ/kg**, Jatropha oil ≈ **38 MJ/kg**, Bagasse ≈ **19 MJ/kg**.
- **Kerala context:** KSEB biomass power plants (1–10 MW) primarily use **Eucalyptus** and **cashew waste**.
- **Environmental co-benefits:** wasteland reclamation, soil-carbon build-up, rural employment.

> **Final Examiner's Tip:** In KTU ESE, always **state assumptions**, **show unit conversions**, and **box the final answer**. Half the marks go to methodology, not just the numeric answer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Solar Energy Applications in India
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECHT411 - ENERGY ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Solar Energy Applications in India # SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW ## 1.1 What is Solar Energy? (KTU 2024 Definition) **Solar Energy** is the radiant light and heat emitted by the **Sun**, which is harnessed using a range of ever-evolving technologies such as **photovoltaics (PV)**, **solar thermal energy**, **solar architecture**, and **artificial photosynthesis**. It is the most abundant, clean, and renewable source of energy available on Earth. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PECHT411 Module 2):** > *"Solar Energy Applications encompass all engineered systems that convert incident solar radiation into useful thermal energy (low/medium/high temperature) or directly into electricity, deployed across industrial, commercial, agricultural, and domestic sectors of India."* ## 1.2 The Sun as a Nuclear Reactor: A Simple Analogy Imagine the **Sun** as a giant, continuous, and perfectly safe **nuclear fusion reactor** floating **150 million kilometres** away. Every second, it converts about **600 million tonnes of hydrogen into helium**, releasing an unfathomable amount of energy. Out of this colossal emission, only a tiny fraction — roughly **1.74 × 10¹⁷ Watts** — reaches the top of Earth's atmosphere. > [!NOTE] > **The Solar Constant (I_sc):** The rate at which solar energy arrives at the outer edge of the Earth's atmosphere, perpendicular to the sun's rays, is called the **Solar Constant**. > - **Value: I_sc = 1367 W/m²** (or 1.361 kW/m² as per WMO standard) > - This is the *un-attenuated* energy flux in space. **Intuitive Picture:** Think of the Sun as a giant pressure cooker. The heat it produces travels through the vacuum of space (no medium needed) and falls on India's landmass. India is uniquely blessed because it lies in the **tropical belt** (8°N to 37°N latitude), receiving some of the highest solar insolation on the planet — averaging **4 to 7 kWh/m²/day** across most regions, with the Thar Desert and Rann of Kutch receiving up to **7.5 kWh/m²/day**. ## 1.3 India's Solar Resource Endowment India's land area is roughly **3.287 million km²**. Even covering a fraction of this with solar panels can fulfil its energy dreams for centuries. | Parameter | Value (India Average) | |---|---| | **Annual Solar Insolation** | **1600 – 2200 kWh/m²/year** (≈ 4.4 – 6.0 kWh/m²/day) | | **Sunny Days per Year** | **250 – 300 clear-sky days** | | **Daily Peak Sun Hours (PSH)** | **4.5 – 6.0 hours** | | **Theoretical Solar Power Potential** | **≈ 5000 TWh per year** (≈ 5 × 10¹⁵ kWh) | | **Installed Solar Capacity (2024)** | **≈ 82 GW (and rising rapidly)** | > [!TIP] > **Why India is a Solar Superpower:** > - Geographic location between **Tropic of Cancer (23.5°N)** and the equator > - Low aerosol content in many regions (except Indo-Gangetic plain in winter) > - Low latitude → Sun is closer to zenith for most of the year > - Low population density in high-insolation zones (Rajasthan, Gujarat, Ladakh) # SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET ## 2.1 Classification of Solar Energy Applications Solar energy applications in India are broadly classified into two families: ### A. Solar Thermal Applications (Heat-Based) These systems absorb sunlight to produce **heat**, which is then used for water heating, cooking, drying, cooling, or industrial processes. 1. **Low-Temperature Applications (T < 100°C)** - Solar Water Heaters (domestic & industrial) - Solar Cookers (box & parabolic) - Solar Dryers (agricultural products) - Solar Distillation / Desalination - Solar Ponds 2. **Medium-Temperature Applications (100°C < T < 300°C)** - Solar Concentrators (Parabolic Dish, Scheffler) - Solar Steam Cooking Systems - Process Heat for Industry 3. **High-Temperature Applications (T > 300°C)** - Solar Furnaces - Solar Thermal Power Plants (Parabolic Trough, Solar Tower, Dish-Stirling) ### B. Solar Photovoltaic (SPV) Applications (Electricity-Based) These systems use **semiconductor cells (silicon-based)** to directly convert sunlight into electricity via the **photovoltaic effect** discovered by Edmond Becquerel in 1839. 1. **Standalone (Off-Grid) SPV Systems** - Solar Lanterns, Home Lighting Systems - Solar Water Pumping - SPV for Telecom Towers - Street Lighting 2. **Grid-Connected (On-Grid) SPV Systems** - Rooftop Solar (RTS) - Solar Parks / Ultra-Mega Solar Power Projects - Floating Solar (e.g., Omkareshwar Dam, MP) 3. **Hybrid Systems** - Solar-Diesel, Solar-Biogas, Solar-Wind hybrids ## 2.2 Solar Geometry — The Foundation of Every Application To design any solar system, an engineer **must** know where the Sun is at any given moment. This is governed by **solar geometry**. ### Key Angles Defined - **Latitude (φ):** Angular distance of a location north (+) or south (−) of the equator. - **Declination (δ):** Angular position of the Sun north/south of the equator. Varies from **+23.45°** (Summer Solstice, June 21) to **−23.45°** (Winter Solstice, Dec 22). - **Hour Angle (ω):** Angular displacement of the Sun from local noon. Each hour = **15°** (positive in afternoon). - **Solar Altitude Angle (α):** Height of the Sun above the horizon. - **Solar Azimuth Angle (γ_s):** Compass direction of the Sun's projection. - **Zenith Angle (θ_z):** Angle between Sun's rays and the vertical (= 90° − α). - **Angle of Incidence (θ_i):** Angle between Sun's rays and the normal to the tilted surface. - **Surface Tilt Angle (β):** Inclination of the solar collector from horizontal. - **Surface Azimuth Angle (γ):** Compass direction the tilted surface faces. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Concept Highlight:** *All equations below assume the Northern Hemisphere. For India (which lies in the Northern Hemisphere), the formulas hold as given.* ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet The following table summarises every essential formula tested in KTU examinations. Use `\vert` instead of `|` for absolute values to protect Markdown table formatting. | # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Day Angle** | $\Gamma = \dfrac{2\pi(n-1)}{365}$ | radians | $n$ = day number of year (Jan 1 = 1) | | 2 | **Solar Declination** | $\delta = 23.45° \cdot \sin\left(\dfrac{360}{365}(284+n)\right)$ | degrees | Cooper's Equation | | 3 | **Hour Angle** | $\omega = 15° \cdot (AST - 12)$ | degrees | $AST$ = Apparent Solar Time | | 4 | **Solar Altitude** | $\sin\alpha = \sin\phi\sin\delta + \cos\phi\cos\delta\cos\omega$ | — | From spherical trigonometry | | 5 | **Zenith Angle** | $\cos\theta_z = \sin\phi\sin\delta + \cos\phi\cos\delta\cos\omega$ | — | $\theta_z = 90° - \alpha$ | | 6 | **Solar Azimuth** | $\sin\gamma_s = \dfrac{\cos\delta\sin\omega}{\cos\alpha}$ | degrees | Measured from South in N. Hemisphere | | 7 | **Angle of Incidence on Tilted Surface** | $\cos\theta_i = \sin\delta\sin(\phi-\beta) + \cos\delta\cos(\phi-\beta)\cos\omega$ | — | For surface facing due South | | 8 | **Air Mass (AM)** | $AM = \dfrac{1}{\cos\theta_z}$ | dimensionless | AM0 = space, AM1 = sea-level Sun overhead | | 9 | **Extra-terrestrial Radiation** | $I_{on} = I_{sc}\left(1 + 0.033\cos\dfrac{360n}{365}\right)$ | W/m² | On a plane normal to Sun's rays | | 10 | **Beam Radiation on Tilted Surface** | $I_{bT} = I_{bn}\cdot\max\left(\cos\theta_i, 0\right)$ | W/m² | Zero when Sun behind surface | | 11 | **Useful Heat from Flat Plate Collector** | $Q_u = A_c \cdot F_R \cdot \left[(\tau\alpha) I_T - U_L (T_{fi} - T_a)\right]$ | W | Hottel-Whillier-Bliss Equation | | 12 | **PV Module Output Power** | $P_{PV} = \eta_{PV} \cdot A \cdot G \cdot \eta_{PCU}$ | W | $G$ = solar irradiance (W/m²) | | 13 | **Daily Energy Yield** | $E_d = A \cdot \eta \cdot \overline{PSH}$ | Wh/day | $\overline{PSH}$ = avg. Peak Sun Hours | | 14 | **Solar Fraction** | $f = \dfrac{Q_{solar}}{Q_{total\,load}}$ | fraction (0–1) | Used in solar system design | > [!TIP] > **Engineering Real-World Utility:** These formulas are the *backbone* of design software like **PVsyst, SAM (System Advisor Model)**, and **PVSOL**. Mastering them gives a 5× productivity boost over rote software usage. # SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX ## 3.1 Derivation: Solar Declination Angle (Cooper's Equation) **Starting Premise:** The Earth is tilted on its axis by **23.45°** relative to the ecliptic plane, and orbits the Sun elliptically. The declination $\delta$ is a sinusoidal function of the day of the year $n$. **Step 1:** Define the *day angle* $\Gamma$, which is a normalized angular position in the Earth's orbit (one full revolution = 365 days). $$ \Gamma = \frac{2\pi(n - 1)}{365} $$ **Step 2:** The declination reaches its maximum of $+23.45°$ near the **summer solstice** (Northern Hemisphere). Empirically, Cooper (1969) found this maximum occurs around day $n = 172$ (June 21). **Step 3:** Express $\delta$ as a sine function that peaks at $n = 172$. Mathematically, $\sin(\Gamma)$ peaks at $\Gamma = \pi/2$, which corresponds to $n \approx 91$. To shift the peak to day 172, we add a phase shift: $$ \delta = 23.45° \cdot \sin\left(\Gamma + \text{phase}\right) $$ **Step 4:** Substituting $\Gamma = \dfrac{2\pi(n-1)}{365}$ and solving for the phase such that the sine equals 1 at $n = 172$: $$ \delta = 23.45° \cdot \sin\left[\frac{360°}{365}(n - 1) + \text{constant}\right] $$ **Step 5:** The phase constant is chosen as $360°/365 \cdot 80$ so that the total argument becomes $360°/365 \cdot (n + 83)$. The most commonly accepted empirical form (Cooper's 6th-order, simplified) is: $$ \boxed{\delta = 23.45° \cdot \sin\left[\frac{360°}{365}(n + 284)\right]} $$ **Step 6 — Quick Check:** At $n = 172$ (June 21), the argument is $(172 + 284) \cdot 360/365 = 456 \cdot 360/365 ≈ 449.75°$ → modulo 360° = **89.75°**, and $\sin(89.75°) ≈ 0.99999$, giving $\delta ≈ 23.44°$. ✓ ## 3.2 Derivation: Angle of Incidence on a Tilted Surface **Setup:** Consider a flat surface tilted at angle $\beta$ from the horizontal, with its normal vector pointing in the **south** direction (azimuth $\gamma = 0$ in N. Hemisphere convention). **Step 1:** Express the **unit vector pointing from Earth to Sun** in the local horizon coordinate system. Using the Sun's altitude $\alpha$ and azimuth $\gamma_s$: $$ \hat{S} = (\cos\alpha\sin\gamma_s,\ \cos\alpha\cos\gamma_s,\ \sin\alpha) $$ **Step 2:** Express the **unit normal to the tilted surface** (tilted $\beta$ from horizontal, facing South): $$ \hat{N} = (0,\ \sin\beta,\ \cos\beta) $$ **Step 3:** The angle of incidence $\theta_i$ is given by the dot product: $$ \cos\theta_i = \hat{S} \cdot \hat{N} = \cos\alpha\cos\gamma_s\sin\beta + \sin\alpha\cos\beta $$ **Step 4:** Substitute the spherical trigonometric identities for $\cos\alpha$ and $\sin\gamma_s$ in terms of $\phi$, $\delta$, and $\omega$: $$ \cos\alpha = \cos\phi\cos\delta\cos\omega + \sin\phi\sin\delta $$ $$ \sin\gamma_s = \frac{\cos\delta\sin\omega}{\cos\alpha} $$ **Step 5:** After algebraic substitution and simplification (a lengthy but mechanical process, see Duffie & Beckman), we obtain the canonical result: $$ \boxed{\cos\theta_i = \sin\delta\sin(\phi - \beta) + \cos\delta\cos(\phi - \beta)\cos\omega} $$ **Engineering Insight:** This is the formula programmed into every solar tracking system in India's solar parks. At solar noon ($\omega = 0$), it reduces to: $$ \cos\theta_i \big|_{\omega=0} = \sin\delta\sin(\phi - \beta) + \cos\delta\cos(\phi - \beta) = \cos(\phi - \beta + \delta - \delta) = \cos(\phi - \beta) $$ Wait, let me redo that: at $\omega = 0$, $\cos\omega = 1$, so: $$ \cos\theta_i = \sin\delta\sin(\phi-\beta) + \cos\delta\cos(\phi-\beta) = \cos(\phi - \beta - \delta + \delta) = \cos(\phi - \beta) $$ The optimal tilt for maximum annual energy is therefore $\beta_{opt} \approx \phi$ (tilt equals latitude). For example, in **Thiruvananthapuram ($\phi \approx 8.5°$)**, a tilt of $8° - 10°$ is sufficient; in **Delhi ($\phi \approx 28.6°$)**, a tilt of $28° - 30°$ is ideal for fixed-tilt annual systems. ## 3.3 Worked-Out Analytical Problem (KTU Style) **Problem:** A flat plate solar collector with tilt $\beta = 20°$ (facing South) is installed in **Thiruvananthapuram** ($\phi = 8.5°$ N) on **March 21** at **2:00 PM local apparent solar time**. Determine: (a) Solar declination $\delta$ (b) Hour angle $\omega$ (c) Zenith angle $\theta_z$ (d) Angle of incidence on the collector $\theta_i$ (e) Air mass at that instant ### Solution **(a) Declination:** Day number $n$ for March 21: $n = 31 + 28 + 21 = 80$ (non-leap year) $$ \delta = 23.45° \cdot \sin\left[\frac{360°}{365}(80 + 284)\right] = 23.45° \cdot \sin\left[\frac{360°}{365} \cdot 364\right] $$ $$ \delta = 23.45° \cdot \sin(358.85°) \approx 23.45° \cdot (-0.01998) \approx -0.47° \approx 0° $$ > **Physical Meaning:** March 21 is the **Spring/Vernal Equinox** — Sun is directly above the equator, so $\delta \approx 0°$. ✓ **(b) Hour Angle:** 2:00 PM means 2 hours after solar noon: $$ \omega = 15° \cdot (14 - 12) = +30° $$ **(c) Zenith Angle:** Using $\delta = 0$: $$ \cos\theta_z = \sin\phi\sin\delta + \cos\phi\cos\delta\cos\omega $$ $$ \cos\theta_z = \sin(8.5°)(0) + \cos(8.5°)\cos(0°)\cos(30°) $$ $$ \cos\theta_z = (0.9881)(1)(0.8660) = 0.8558 $$ $$ \theta_z = \cos^{-1}(0.8558) \approx 31.14° $$ **(d) Angle of Incidence on Collector:** With $\beta = 20°$: $$ \cos\theta_i = \sin\delta\sin(\phi - \beta) + \cos\delta\cos(\phi - \beta)\cos\omega $$ $$ \cos\theta_i = (0)\sin(8.5° - 20°) + (1)\cos(8.5° - 20°)\cos(30°) $$ $$ \cos\theta_i = \cos(-11.5°)\cos(30°) = 0.9802 \times 0.8660 = 0.8489 $$ $$ \theta_i = \cos^{-1}(0.8489) \approx 31.93° $$ **(e) Air Mass:** $$ AM = \frac{1}{\cos\theta_z} = \frac{1}{0.8558} \approx 1.169 $$ > This is typical — at 2 PM in March, the Sun is fairly high (altitude ≈ 58.9°), and the air mass is close to 1 (less atmospheric absorption). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Mark Distribution Tip (ESE):** For a 14-mark question on solar geometry, allocate **3 marks for declination**, **2 marks for hour angle**, **3 marks for zenith**, **3 marks for incidence angle**, and **3 marks for air mass + interpretation**. Always draw a labelled diagram for partial marks. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Sizing a SPV Water Pump (Methodical) A common KTU problem is sizing a solar water pump. The matrix below gives the *order* of calculations, not just formulas. | Step | Question to Ask | Formula / Method | Sample Value | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Daily water requirement?** | $V = \text{Number of users} \times \text{lpcd (litres per capita per day)}$ | 100 persons × 40 lpcd = 4000 L | | 2 | **Total Dynamic Head (TDH)?** | $TDH = \text{Static head} + \text{drawdown} + \text{friction losses}$ | 25 m | | 3 | **Hydraulic Energy Required** | $E_{hyd} = \rho g V \cdot TDH$ | 9810 × 4 × 25 = 981 kJ/day | | 4 | **Convert to Electrical Energy** | $E_{elec} = \dfrac{E_{hyd}}{\eta_{pump} \cdot \eta_{motor}}$ | Use $\eta_{pump} = 0.5$, $\eta_{motor} = 0.85$ | | 5 | **Daily Solar Insolation** | From $\overline{PSH}$ maps (India) | $PSH = 5.5$ kWh/m²/day | | 6 | **Size the PV Array** | $P_{PV} = \dfrac{E_{elec\,daily}}{\overline{PSH} \cdot \eta_{overall}}$ | kWp of array | | 7 | **Battery Sizing (if needed)** | $C_{batt} = \dfrac{E_{elec\,daily} \cdot \text{Autonomy days}}{V_{batt} \cdot DoD \cdot \eta_{batt}}$ | Ah rating | > [!NOTE] > **India-Specific Note:** The **MNRE (Ministry of New and Renewable Energy)** standard for solar pumps in India assumes a duty cycle of 6–8 hours/day and a typical head of 30–70 metres. As of 2024, **over 4.5 lakh solar pumps** have been installed under the **PM-KUSUM Scheme** (Pradhan Mantri Kisan Urja Suraksha evam Utthaan Mahabhiyan). # SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS ## 4.1 Master Classification Flowchart of Solar Energy Applications in India The following Mermaid diagram provides a **block-level functional architecture flow** mapping the interactions between solar energy domains, conversion technologies, and end-use sectors in India. ```mermaid flowchart TD SUN((SUN

and Parallel] -->|DC Power| B[DC Combiner Box

Examiners reward clarity — a clean diagram typically fetches 1–2 easy marks even if the accompanying text is imperfect. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP ## 5.1 PART A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]** **List any six major solar energy applications deployed in India, classifying them into solar thermal and solar photovoltaic categories.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Solar Thermal Applications (1.5 marks):** 1. Solar water heaters (FPC and ETC types) — largest deployed solar thermal tech in India 2. Solar cookers (box type for households, parabolic community type) 3. Solar dryers for agricultural produce (chillies, turmeric, fish, wood) 4. Solar steam cooking systems for temples and mass feeding (e.g., Tirumala) 5. Solar ponds for thermal energy storage 6. Solar thermal power plants (parabolic trough / tower) **Solar Photovoltaic Applications (1.5 marks):** 1. SPV water pumping systems (agricultural sector) 2. Solar lanterns and home lighting (rural electrification) 3. Rooftop solar PV (urban / industrial) 4. Grid-connected solar parks (Bhadla, Pavagada) 5. Solar street lighting (municipal) 6. SPV power for telecom towers and signalling --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2023]** **Define the following terms: (i) Solar Constant, (ii) Air Mass, (iii) Declination Angle.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **(i) Solar Constant (I_sc) (1 mark):** The Solar Constant is the rate at which solar energy (electromagnetic radiation) is received *outside* the Earth's atmosphere, on a surface held perpendicular to the Sun's rays, at the mean Sun-Earth distance. **Standard value: I_sc = 1367 W/m².** **(ii) Air Mass (AM) (1 mark):** Air Mass is the ratio of the actual path length of solar radiation through the atmosphere to the path length when the Sun is at the zenith. It is given by **AM = 1 / cos(θ_z)**, where θ_z is the zenith angle. **AM1.5** is the standard reference for testing PV modules (Sun at 48.2° altitude). **(iii) Declination Angle (δ) (1 mark):** The declination angle is the angular position of the Sun with respect to the plane of the equator. It varies from **+23.45° (Summer Solstice, June 21)** to **−23.45° (Winter Solstice, Dec 22)**, with a sinusoidal variation throughout the year, given by **δ = 23.45° · sin[360/365 · (n + 284)]**. --- ## 5.2 PART B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice > **KTU Rule (ESE 2024):** "Answer any ONE full question from each module. Each question carries 14 marks with sub-parts (a) and (b) of 7 marks each." --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** #### **Q.A [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 2, Set A]** **(a) With the help of a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of an Evacuated Tube Collector (ETC) based solar water heater. Discuss its advantages over the Flat Plate Collector (FPC).** (7 marks) **(b) A solar PV system is to be designed for a rural Indian village. The daily electrical load is: 4 LED bulbs of 9 W each running 6 hours/day and 1 ceiling fan of 50 W running 8 hours/day. The system operates at 12 V DC. Given: PSH = 5.0 kWh/m²/day, system efficiency = 0.70, depth of discharge = 0.6, inverter efficiency (if AC) = 0.90, autonomy days = 2, battery nominal voltage = 12 V. Size the (i) PV array, (ii) battery bank, and (iii) cross-check the daily energy yield.** (7 marks) #### **MODEL SOLUTION (A)** **(a) ETC-based Solar Water Heater (7 marks):** **Construction (3.5 marks):** - Array of **glass vacuum tubes** (typically borosilicate glass), each tube has an outer transparent tube and an inner selective-coated absorber tube. - The annular space between the two tubes is **evacuated to < 10⁻³ Pa**, eliminating convective and conductive losses. - Inner tube is coated with **selective coating** (e.g., Al-N/Al, cermet) having high absorptance (α > 0.92) and low emittance (ε < 0.15). - Each tube contains a **copper heat pipe** (or directly the water-filled tube) that transfers heat to a manifold/header at the top. - Header connects to an **insulated hot water storage tank** (SS or galvanised iron with glass wool insulation). **Working (2 marks):** - Solar radiation passes through the outer glass tube (low reflectance due to AR coating), and is absorbed by the selective coating on the inner tube. - The absorbed energy heats the heat pipe / water inside the tube. Heat is conducted to the top manifold. - In the **heat-pipe type**, fluid inside the copper pipe vaporises, rises, transfers heat to the water in the tank via a condenser bulb, condenses, and returns — a closed two-phase thermosiphon cycle. - The evacuated annulus prevents heat loss, allowing the inner tube to reach **200–300°C in stagnation** (though operating temperature is 60–90°C). - In **direct-flow (tube-in-tube) type**, water flows through the inner tube directly, heated by the absorber, and is collected in the tank. **Advantages over FPC (1.5 marks):** 1. Higher efficiency (40–60% vs. 30–45% for FPC) due to vacuum insulation. 2. Effective in **partially cloudy weather** — each tube operates independently. 3. Can be installed on **non-south-facing roofs** because tubes absorb diffuse radiation well. 4. Less prone to breakage from thermal shock (each tube is free to expand independently). 5. Higher operating temperature range (up to 150°C for high-pressure ETCs). 6. **Lower weight per unit area** — suited to weaker Indian rooftop structures. **Neat Sketch:** *(Refer to standard ETC diagram — outer glass tube, vacuum gap, inner selective-coated absorber, heat pipe bulb, manifold, insulated tank.)* --- **(b) PV Sizing (7 marks):** **Step 1: Daily Energy Demand (1 mark):** $$ E_{load} = (4 \times 9 \times 6) + (1 \times 50 \times 8) = 216 + 400 = 616 \text{ Wh/day} $$ **Step 2: Total Energy Required from PV (accounting for losses) (1.5 marks):** Assuming DC loads at 12 V (no inverter), system efficiency accounts for battery + wiring: $$ E_{PV} = \frac{E_{load}}{\eta_{sys}} = \frac{616}{0.70} = 880 \text{ Wh/day} $$ **Step 3: PV Array Sizing (1.5 marks):** $$ P_{PV} = \frac{E_{PV}}{PSH} = \frac{880}{5.0} = 176 \text{ Wp} $$ **Practical choice:** **One 200 Wp module** (standard Indian size). **Step 4: Battery Bank Sizing (1.5 marks):** $$ C_{batt} = \frac{E_{load} \times \text{Autonomy days}}{V_{batt} \times DoD \times \eta_{batt}} $$ $$ C_{batt} = \frac{616 \times 2}{12 \times 0.6 \times 0.85} = \frac{1232}{6.12} = 201.3 \text{ Ah} $$ **Practical choice:** **Two 12 V, 100 Ah batteries in parallel** (or one 12 V, 200 Ah battery if available). **Step 5: Cross-Check of Daily Energy Yield (1.5 marks):** $$ E_{yield} = P_{PV} \times PSH \times \eta_{sys} = 200 \times 5.0 \times 0.70 = 700 \text{ Wh/day} $$ $$ E_{yield} = 700 \text{ Wh/day} \geq E_{load} = 616 \text{ Wh/day} \quad \checkmark $$ > **Verdict:** 700 Wh/day can support the 616 Wh/day load — system is correctly sized with ~14% reserve margin. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** #### **Q.B [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 2, Set B]** **(a) Explain the construction and working of a Box-Type Solar Cooker. Discuss its limitations and the advantages of a Parabolic Dish Solar Cooker over it.** (7 marks) **(b) A solar thermal power plant uses a parabolic trough collector. The system operates at 70% thermal efficiency and 30% electrical efficiency (overall). The plant area is 1 km × 1 km = 10⁶ m², and the average daily solar insolation is 6.0 kWh/m²/day. Estimate: (i) the daily thermal energy collected, (ii) the daily electrical energy generated, and (iii) the cost of electricity generated per day, given an electricity tariff of ₹8/kWh. Comment on the environmental benefits.** (7 marks) #### **MODEL SOLUTION (B)** **(a) Box-Type Solar Cooker (4 marks construction/working):** **Construction:** Double-walled insulated box with **blackened interior** (aluminium or mild steel painted black). Top cover is a **double glass lid** (transparent, allows shortwave radiation to pass). Side walls are insulated with **glass wool / thermocol / PUF**. A **tray** inside holds utensils; **reflecting mirrors** (often 4 fold-out panels) act as boosters to increase concentration. **Working:** Sun's shortwave radiation enters through the glass cover, is absorbed by the blackened interior and the food/utensils. The interior heats up to **140–200°C**; glass cover traps longwave radiation (greenhouse effect), retaining heat. The reflector panels redirect additional sunlight into the box, raising effective intensity. Cooking time for typical Indian meals (rice, dal) is **2–4 hours**. **Limitations (1.5 marks):** - Slow cooking (2–4 hours vs. 30–45 min on LPG). - Only 1–2 meals/day per box, capacity-limited (~4–5 people). - Cannot reach temperatures > 200°C — cannot fry or make chapatis in many models. - Cooking must be timed with peak solar hours (12 noon – 2 PM typically). - Cannot be used on cloudy/rainy days. **Advantages of Parabolic Dish (1.5 marks):** - Achieves **500–1000°C** at the focal point — much higher than box cooker. - Cooking time reduced to **20–30 minutes** (similar to LPG stove). - Higher power output (1–3 kW thermal). - Can be used for industrial process heat. --- **(b) Parabolic Trough Power Plant (7 marks):** **Given:** - Area A = 10⁶ m² (1 km²) - Insolation I = 6.0 kWh/m²/day - η_thermal = 0.70 - η_electrical = 0.30 - Tariff = ₹8/kWh **(i) Daily thermal energy collected (2 marks):** $$ E_{thermal} = A \times I \times \eta_{thermal} = 10^6 \times 6.0 \times 0.70 = 4.2 \times 10^6 \text{ kWh/day} = 4200 \text{ MWh/day} $$ **(ii) Daily electrical energy generated (2 marks):** $$ E_{elec} = E_{thermal} \times \eta_{electrical} = 4.2 \times 10^6 \times 0.30 = 1.26 \times 10^6 \text{ kWh/day} = 1260 \text{ MWh/day} $$ > *Alternative (combined) approach:* > $$ > E_{elec} = A \times I \times \eta_{thermal} \times \eta_{electrical} = 10^6 \times 6.0 \times 0.70 \times 0.30 = 1.26 \times 10^6 \text{ kWh/day} > $$ **(iii) Daily revenue (2 marks):** $$ \text{Revenue} = E_{elec} \times \text{Tariff} = 1.26 \times 10^6 \times 8 = \text{₹}1.008 \times 10^7 = \text{₹}1.008 \text{ crore/day} $$ > **Annual Revenue ≈ ₹ 368 crore/year** (assuming 365 days). **Environmental benefits (1 mark):** - Avoids ≈ **0.985 million kg of CO₂ emission per day** (using 0.78 kg CO₂/kWh Indian grid average). - Equivalent to planting ≈ 16,000 trees per day or removing ≈ 360,000 cars per year. - Reduces dependence on coal (~1 tonne of coal saved per 2 MWh electricity). --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **TOP MARKS-LOSING PITFALLS — KTU 2024 EVALUATION KEY INSIGHTS:** > 1. **Units are NOT optional:** A 14-mark question answered perfectly with wrong units can fetch only 8–9 marks. Always write **kWh, W/m², °C, Ah, V** explicitly. > 2. **Diagram ≠ Decoration:** A labelled diagram is **MANDATORY** for any 7-mark question on solar system construction. The examiner cannot award full marks for a diagramless answer. > 3. **Numericals without assumption statements:** Always state *Given data, Assumption, Formula, Substitution, Result* — the "5-line" rule. > 4. **Confusing Air Mass with Air Mass Ratio:** AM is unitless. Do not write "AM = 1.5 kg" or "AM = 1.5 g/m³." > 5. **Declination sign errors:** δ is **positive** from Vernal Equinox (March 21) to Autumnal Equinox (Sep 22), and **negative** for the other half-year. Many students invert this. > 6. **Confusing solar noon and 12:00 PM IST:** Indian Standard Time is 5:30 ahead of GMT. Solar noon in India typically occurs between 12:15 and 13:00 IST depending on longitude. > 7. **Forgetting to add a 10–15% derating factor in PV sizing:** Real panels lose 0.5%/°C above STC, so always apply a 0.85–0.90 derating when using PSH maps. > 8. **Mixing ETC and FPC advantages:** These are NOT interchangeable. ETCs win in cloudy weather and for higher temperatures; FPCs are cheaper and more robust. --- ## 5.4 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER > [!TIP] > **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Print this section before the exam!** - ☑ **India's solar potential** = 5000 TWh/year theoretical; 4–7 kWh/m²/day received. - ☑ **Solar Constant (I_sc)** = **1367 W/m²** (top of atmosphere). - ☑ **Air Mass (AM)** = 1 / cos(θ_z). Standard test condition: **AM1.5G**, irradiance **1000 W/m²**. - ☑ **Tilt for fixed-tilt annual** ≈ **latitude (φ)**. Winter tilt = φ + 15°; Summer tilt = φ − 15°. - ☑ **Major solar thermal applications:** SWH, cookers, dryers, ponds, parabolic trough, solar tower, dish-Stirling. - ☑ **Major solar PV applications:** Lanterns, pumps, rooftop, solar parks, floating, telecom. - ☑ **Govt. schemes to remember:** **JNNSM (2010)**, **PM-KUSUM** (solar pumps), **PM-Surya Ghar Yojana (2024)**, **CPSU Scheme**, **Rooftop Solar Phase II**. - ☑ **Top solar parks:** Bhadla (Rajasthan, 2.25 GW), Pavagada (Karnataka, 2.05 GW), Charanka (Gujarat, 690 MW). - ☑ **First floating solar plant (India):** Kayamkulam (Kerala, NTPC, 92 MWp in expanded form). - ☑ **Daily energy formula:** E_d = η · A · PSH. - ☑ **Battery sizing:** C = (E_load × Days) / (V × DoD × η). - ☑ **Pump sizing:** P_PV = (ρ · g · V · TDH) / (η_pump · η_motor · PSH). - ☑ **MNRE = Ministry of New and Renewable Energy** (HQ: New Delhi). - ☑ **SECI = Solar Energy Corporation of India** (central PSU for solar tenders). - ☑ **Declination** = +23.45° on June 21; 0° on March 21 / Sep 22; −23.45° on Dec 22. - ☑ **Hour angle** = 15° per hour from solar noon; negative in morning, positive in afternoon. - ☑ **Remember the 3 Ls:** Location (insolation), Load (kWh), Losses (η) — these are the three inputs to any solar design.

topics.title: Ocean Wave Energy Conversion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT411 - ENERGY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Ocean Wave Energy Conversion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Ocean Wave Energy Conversion (OWEC)** is the process of capturing the kinetic and potential energy present in ocean surface waves (generated primarily by wind friction on the sea surface) and transforming it into useful electrical or mechanical work using specialized Wave Energy Converters (WECs).

The total energy of a water wave per unit horizontal area of sea surface is given by the sum of its **kinetic energy** and **potential energy**. In linear (Airy) wave theory, both are equal, so the total wave energy per unit area is:

$$E_{total} = \rho g a^2 = \frac{1}{8} \rho g H^2$$

where:
- $\rho$ = density of sea water ≈ **1025 kg/m³**
- $g$ = acceleration due to gravity = **9.81 m/s²**
- $a = H/2$ = wave amplitude (m)
- $H$ = wave height (m)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine a floating cork on the ocean. As a wave passes, the cork bobs up-down AND moves forward-backward in a circular orbit. The water particles themselves do not travel with the wave — only the **energy** travels. Wave energy converters are simply engineered "smart corks" designed to either move *with* the water (to extract kinetic energy) or to trap the water in a chamber (to extract pneumatic/potential energy).

Think of an ocean wave as a **slow-moving energy conveyor belt**:
- The wave crest carries **potential energy** (water lifted above mean sea level).
- The wave orbital motion carries **kinetic energy** (water moving horizontally).
- A WEC is any device that "taps" this conveyor belt, like a paddle wheel tapping a moving belt.

## 1.3 Wave Energy vs. Other Renewables — Why It Matters

> [!TIP]
> **Key Advantage:** Ocean waves have an **energy density** of 2–3 kW/m² of crest length, which is **5–10 times higher** than wind (≈ 0.4–0.6 kW/m²) and **20–30 times higher** than solar (≈ 0.1–0.2 kW/m²) at the same installation footprint. This is why wave energy is a high-yield renewable resource for coastal Kerala (Vizhinjam, Munambam) and the Indian coastline.

## 1.4 Key Standard Metrics in Wave Energy

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical Range / Unit |
|--------|----------|----------------------|
| $H$ | Wave height | 1 – 6 m (deep ocean) |
| $T$ | Wave period | 5 – 20 s |
| $\lambda$ | Wavelength | 10 – 200 m |
| $c$ | Wave celerity (speed) | 5 – 30 m/s |
| $P_w$ | Wave power per unit crest length | 10 – 100 kW/m |
| $E_{tot}$ | Total energy per unit area | 10 – 200 kJ/m² |

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Linear (Airy) Wave Theory — Foundation of Wave Energy

The Airy wave theory describes small-amplitude waves as **sinusoidal**. The free surface displacement is:

$$\eta(x,t) = a \cos(kx - \omega t)$$

where:
- $k = 2\pi/\lambda$ = wave number
- $\omega = 2\pi/T$ = angular frequency

### 2.1.1 Wave Celerity (Phase Speed)

The speed at which a wave crest moves forward:

$$c = \frac{\lambda}{T} = \frac{\omega}{k}$$

**Deep water limit** ($d > \lambda/2$):

$$c_0 = \frac{gT}{2\pi} \approx 1.56 \, T \;\; \text{(m/s)}$$

**General (finite depth) dispersion relation:**

$$c = \sqrt{\frac{g}{k}\tanh(kd)} = \sqrt{\frac{g\lambda}{2\pi}\tanh\!\left(\frac{2\pi d}{\lambda}\right)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Shallow water limit** ($d < \lambda/20$): $c \approx \sqrt{gd}$ (independent of wavelength). This is why tsunami waves slow dramatically near shore.

### 2.1.2 Water Particle Motion (Orbital)

In **deep water**, water particles describe closed **circular orbits** of radius $a$ that decay exponentially with depth:

$$r(z) = a \, e^{kz} = a \, e^{-2\pi z/\lambda}$$

**Engineering Implication:** A floating buoy moving with the orbital motion can extract energy. Notice that at depth $z = \lambda/2$, orbital radius shrinks to only **4.3%** of surface amplitude — explaining why deep-water WECs are tethered to buoys *at the surface*.

## 2.2 Wave Power Derivation (KTU Favourite)

Consider one wavelength $\lambda$ of a wave. The total mechanical energy (KE + PE) per unit crest length is:

$$E_{\lambda} = \frac{1}{8}\rho g H^2 \lambda$$

Energy is transported forward at **group velocity** $c_g$, not phase velocity $c$:

| Water Depth | Phase Velocity $c$ | Group Velocity $c_g$ |
|-------------|--------------------|-----------------------|
| Deep water  | $\dfrac{gT}{2\pi}$ | $\dfrac{c}{2} = \dfrac{gT}{4\pi}$ |
| Shallow water | $\sqrt{gd}$ | $c = \sqrt{gd}$ |

**Power transmitted per unit crest length (Deep Water):**

$$P_w = E_{total} \times c_g = \left(\frac{1}{8}\rho g H^2\right) \times \frac{gT}{4\pi}$$

$$\boxed{\,P_w = \frac{\rho g^2 H^2 T}{32\pi}\,}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Numerical Simplification for Exams:** $P_w \approx 0.49 \, H^2 T$ (kW/m) when H is in metres and T in seconds. This is a **must-memorize** form for KTU problems.

For **shallow water** (using $\lambda = cT = T\sqrt{gd}$):

$$P_w = \frac{1}{8}\rho g H^2 \cdot c = \frac{1}{8}\rho g H^2 \sqrt{gd}$$

## 2.3 Resource Assessment — The Wave Energy Flux Formula

The **annual wave energy resource** of a coast (in kWh/m/yr) is computed by integrating the spectral power over the year. The most cited global formula is the **Mørk et al. (2010)** expression for average annual wave power at a site:

$$\bar{P} = \frac{\rho g^2}{64\pi} H_{m0}^2 \, T_e$$

where $H_{m0}$ is the **spectral significant wave height** and $T_e$ is the **energy period**.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Use of Wave Energy |
|-------------|---------------------|
| Island grids (Hawaii, Scottish Isles) | Replacing diesel gensets |
| Desalination plants | Powering RO membranes |
| Offshore aquaculture | Powering feed systems |
| Coastal protection | WECs acting as breakwaters (Moss Landing) |
| Hydrogen production | Electrolysis powered by WECs |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Energy of a Progressive Wave (K.E. = P.E. = E_total/2)

Consider a 2-D wave on deep water. The free surface displacement:

$$\eta(x,t) = a\cos(kx-\omega t)$$

The horizontal and vertical water particle velocities (from Airy theory) are:

$$u(x,z,t) = a\omega\, e^{kz}\cos(kx-\omega t)$$

$$w(x,z,t) = a\omega\, e^{kz}\sin(kx-\omega t)$$

### Step 1: Potential Energy per Unit Area

P.E. is the work done to lift water from the equilibrium surface to the actual surface, integrated over one wavelength.

$$PE = \overline{\int_0^{\eta} \rho g z \, dz} = \frac{1}{2}\rho g \overline{\eta^2}$$

For a sinusoid, $\overline{\eta^2} = a^2/2$:

$$PE = \frac{1}{2}\rho g \cdot \frac{a^2}{2} = \frac{1}{4}\rho g a^2$$

### Step 2: Kinetic Energy per Unit Area

KE per unit area = $\frac{1}{2}\rho \overline{\int_{-\infty}^{\eta}(u^2+w^2)dz}$

For deep water:

$$\overline{\int_{-\infty}^{0}(u^2+w^2)dz} = a^2\omega^2 \int_{-\infty}^{0}e^{2kz}dz = \frac{a^2\omega^2}{2k} = \frac{a^2\omega^2\lambda}{4\pi}$$

Substituting $\omega^2 = gk$ (deep-water dispersion):

$$KE = \frac{1}{2}\rho \cdot \frac{a^2 g k \lambda}{4\pi} = \frac{\rho g a^2 \lambda k}{8\pi}$$

Since $k = 2\pi/\lambda$:

$$KE = \frac{1}{4}\rho g a^2$$

### Step 3: Total Energy Equipartition

$$\boxed{\,E_{total} = KE + PE = \frac{1}{4}\rho g a^2 + \frac{1}{4}\rho g a^2 = \frac{1}{2}\rho g a^2 = \frac{1}{8}\rho g H^2\,}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Equipartition Result:** K.E. = P.E. = $E_{total}/2$. This is a classic KTU viva question and is also what justifies taking the average of KE and PE in the final formula.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Power Transmitted per Unit Crest Length

### Step 1: Power = Energy × Group Velocity

The power passing through a vertical plane of unit width is the total energy in one wavelength (per unit area) multiplied by the speed at which this energy packet travels.

For deep water, the **group velocity** is exactly **half** the phase velocity because the wave profile is the envelope of sinusoidal components:

$$c_g = \frac{c}{2} = \frac{1}{2}\cdot\frac{gT}{2\pi} = \frac{gT}{4\pi}$$

### Step 2: Substitute the Energy Equation

$$P_w = E_{total} \cdot c_g = \frac{1}{8}\rho g H^2 \cdot \frac{gT}{4\pi}$$

$$P_w = \frac{\rho g^2 H^2 T}{32\pi}$$

### Step 3: Numerical Constant

Insert $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s²:

$$P_w = \frac{1025 \times (9.81)^2}{32\pi}\, H^2 T = \frac{1025 \times 96.236}{100.531}\, H^2 T$$

$$P_w \approx 980.9 \, H^2 T \;\;\text{(W/m)}$$

> [!TIP]
> Converting to kW/m: $P_w \approx 0.98 \, H^2 T$ (kW/m). The widely-quoted **0.49** uses $H$ as the **amplitude** $a$, while **0.98** uses the full **height** $H$. KTU answers accept either if consistent. **Always state which convention you are using.**

### Numerical Worked Example (KTU Style)

> **Problem:** A wave-measuring buoy off the Kerala coast records a significant wave height of 2.5 m and an average period of 8 s. Calculate (a) the deep-water wave power per metre of crest length, (b) the total energy per square metre of sea surface.

**Solution:**

Given: $H = 2.5$ m, $T = 8$ s, $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s².

**(a) Power per crest length:**

$$P_w = \frac{\rho g^2 H^2 T}{32\pi} = \frac{1025 \times (9.81)^2 \times (2.5)^2 \times 8}{32\pi}$$

$$P_w = \frac{1025 \times 96.236 \times 6.25 \times 8}{100.531} = \frac{4{,}933{,}853}{100.531}$$

$$\boxed{\,P_w \approx 49{,}080 \text{ W/m} \approx 49.1 \text{ kW/m}\,}$$

**(b) Total energy per m²:**

$$E_{tot} = \frac{1}{8}\rho g H^2 = \frac{1}{8} \times 1025 \times 9.81 \times 6.25$$

$$\boxed{\,E_{tot} \approx 7858 \text{ J/m}^2 \approx 7.86 \text{ kJ/m}^2\,}$$

**Check:** $P_w / c_g = E_{tot} \Rightarrow c_g = 49{,}080 / 7858 \approx 6.25$ m/s. Theory: $c_g = gT/(4\pi) = 9.81\times 8 / 12.566 = 6.245$ m/s. ✓

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Capture Width Ratio (WEC Performance Metric)

For any wave energy device, the **Capture Width Ratio (CWR)** is the most important performance metric:

$$CWR = \frac{\text{Useful Power Absorbed by Device}}{P_w \times D} = \frac{P_{captured}}{P_w \cdot D}$$

where $D$ is the device's characteristic width. **A CWR > 1 means the device is collecting power from a wave front wider than itself** (diffraction effects). Pelamis attenuators and Oyster surge converters have achieved CWR ≈ 0.3–0.5 in field tests.

---
