# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, May 2024 Pattern]**

### Q1. Define wave power per unit crest length and write its deep-water formula. **[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

> Wave power per unit crest length ($P_w$) is the rate at which wave energy crosses a vertical plane of unit width perpendicular to the direction of wave propagation. It is obtained by multiplying the total wave energy per unit area by the group velocity.

**Deep-water formula:**

$$P_w = \frac{\rho g^2 H^2 T}{32\pi} \approx 0.49\, H^2 T \;\; \text{(kW/m)}$$

where $H$ is wave height (m), $T$ is wave period (s), $\rho$ is seawater density, and $g$ is gravitational acceleration. **[3 Marks]**

---

### Q2. State and explain the equipartition of energy in a progressive gravity wave. **[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

In linear (Airy) wave theory, the total mechanical energy of a progressive gravity wave is divided equally between the **kinetic energy** of the water particle orbital motion and the **potential energy** stored in the surface displacement.

$$KE = PE = \frac{1}{4}\rho g a^2 = \frac{1}{16}\rho g H^2$$

$$\therefore E_{total} = \tfrac{1}{8}\rho g H^2$$

This is called **equipartition of energy** and holds strictly only in the small-amplitude (linear) regime. **[3 Marks]**

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> **[KTU End Semester Examination Pattern, PECHT411, 2024 Scheme]**

### Choose **ONE** of the following:

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Derivation + Design Numericals

**(a)** Starting from Airy wave theory, derive the expression for **total wave energy per unit area** of a deep-water gravity wave. Clearly show that the kinetic and potential energies are equal. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A wave measuring station off the Vizhinjam coast (Kerala) records a significant wave height of 3 m and an average zero-crossing period of 9 s. Assuming deep water and linear wave theory, calculate:
- (i) The wave power per metre of crest length. **[3 Marks]**
- (ii) The total wave energy per square metre of sea surface. **[2 Marks]**
- (iii) The capture width of a WEC that produces 150 kW under these conditions. **[2 Marks]**

---

### **Model Solution for Question A**

#### (a) Derivation of Total Wave Energy (7 Marks)

> **Step 1 (1 Mark):** State Airy wave surface displacement.
> $\eta(x,t) = a\cos(kx-\omega t)$ with $a = H/2$.

> **Step 2 (2 Marks):** Velocity potential and particle velocities.
> For deep water, $\phi = \frac{ag}{\omega}e^{kz}\sin(kx-\omega t)$, giving:
> $u = \partial\phi/\partial x = a\omega e^{kz}\cos(kx-\omega t)$
> $w = \partial\phi/\partial z = a\omega e^{kz}\sin(kx-\omega t)$

> **Step 3 (2 Marks):** Compute P.E. per unit horizontal area.
> 
> $$PE = \frac{1}{2}\rho g \overline{\eta^2} = \frac{1}{2}\rho g \cdot \frac{a^2}{2} = \frac{\rho g a^2}{4}$$

> **Step 4 (2 Marks):** Compute K.E. per unit horizontal area and equate to P.E.
> 
> $$KE = \frac{1}{2}\rho \int_{-\infty}^{0}\overline{(u^2+w^2)}dz = \frac{\rho g a^2}{4}$$

Therefore $KE = PE$ and:

$$\boxed{\,E_{total} = KE + PE = \frac{\rho g a^2}{2} = \frac{\rho g H^2}{8}\,}$$

#### (b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks)

**Given:** $H = 3$ m, $T = 9$ s, $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s².

**(i) Wave power per metre crest length (3 Marks):**

$$P_w = \frac{\rho g^2 H^2 T}{32\pi}$$

$$P_w = \frac{1025 \times (9.81)^2 \times 9 \times 9}{32\pi} = \frac{1025 \times 96.236 \times 81}{100.531}$$

$$P_w = \frac{7{,}990{,}031}{100.531} \approx 79{,}484 \text{ W/m}$$

$$\boxed{\,P_w \approx 79.5 \text{ kW/m}\,}$$

**(ii) Total wave energy per m² (2 Marks):**

$$E_{tot} = \frac{1}{8}\rho g H^2 = \frac{1}{8}\times 1025 \times 9.81 \times 9$$

$$\boxed{\,E_{tot} \approx 11{,}312 \text{ J/m}^2 \approx 11.31 \text{ kJ/m}^2\,}$$

**Check:** $c_g = P_w / E_{tot} = 79{,}484 / 11{,}312 = 7.027$ m/s. Theory: $gT/(4\pi) = 9.81 \times 9 / 12.566 = 7.025$ m/s. ✓

**(iii) Capture width (2 Marks):**

$$L_c = \frac{P_{captured}}{P_w} = \frac{150{,}000 \text{ W}}{79{,}484 \text{ W/m}}$$

$$\boxed{\,L_c \approx 1.89 \text{ m}\,}$$

A relatively narrow capture width — meaning this site, while powerful, requires multiple WEC units arranged along the wave front to extract significant power.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Classification + Device Analysis

**(a)** Classify the various **Wave Energy Conversion (WEC) devices** based on their working principle. With a neat functional diagram, explain the operation of an **Oscillating Water Column (OWC)** system. Mention at least one commercial installation. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Compare, with suitable examples, the following three wave energy devices: **Point Absorber**, **Attenuator**, and **Overtopping Device**. For each, state the typical capacity, the principle, and one merit + one demerit. **[7 Marks]**

---

### **Model Solution for Question B**

#### (a) Classification and OWC Explanation (7 Marks)

> **Classification of WECs (3 Marks):**

WECs are classified into **five** main categories:

1. **Oscillating Water Column (OWC):** Air-driven; wave energy compresses/decompresses air column.
2. **Oscillating Body – Point Absorber:** Small buoy heaving with the wave (size < wavelength).
3. **Oscillating Body – Attenuator:** Long multi-segment floating body aligned with wave direction (e.g., Pelamis).
4. **Oscillating Body – Terminator / Flap:** Long device oriented perpendicular to wave front.
5. **Overtopping Device:** Waves are captured in a reservoir above mean sea level.

> **OWC Operation (3 Marks):**

- A partially submerged **concrete/steel chamber** open to the sea below the waterline is built.
- Wave action causes the water level inside the chamber to **rise and fall**.
- The trapped air above is alternately **compressed and expanded**.
- A **Wells turbine** (a bidirectional self-rectifying air turbine) drives a generator during both phases.
- Air flows one way during wave crest, the opposite way during wave trough — but the turbine spins the same direction (the Wells turbine's aerofoil blades are symmetric).

> **Commercial Example (1 Mark):**
> **LIMPET (Land Installed Marine Power Energy Transmitter)** on the Isle of Islay, Scotland — 500 kW capacity, 3 OWC chambers, operating since 2000.

#### (b) Comparison of Three WEC Types (7 Marks)

| Feature | Point Absorber | Attenuator | Overtopping Device |
|---------|----------------|------------|---------------------|
| **Principle** | Small buoy (D < λ/4) heaves relative to seabed reference | Long floating snake aligned with wave direction; hinges flex | Waves spill over a ramp into a high reservoir |
| **Energy Extraction** | Heave + surge motion drives hydraulic PTO or linear generator | Hinge flexing drives hydraulic rams | Head difference between reservoir and sea drives Kaplan turbine |
| **Typical Capacity** | 20 kW – 1 MW | 750 kW – 5 MW | 4 – 20 MW |
| **Example** | PowerBuoy (OPT, USA), Archimedes Wave Swing | Pelamis P2 (Wave Energy Centre, UK) | Wave Dragon (Denmark) |
| **Merit** | Compact; works in deep water; easy to deploy in arrays | High energy capture per device; semi-submerged reduces loads | Stores energy in reservoir (decouples generation from wave arrival) |
| **Demerit** | Low CWR in real seas; mooring fatigue | Survivability in storms; long mooring lines | Requires very calm reservoir; visual impact |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cause Mark Deduction in PECHT411 Board Exams:**
> 1. **Mixing up phase velocity $c$ and group velocity $c_g$** — Power always uses $c_g$, not $c$. In deep water, $c_g = c/2$.
> 2. **Forgetting the factor of 1/8 in $E_{total}$** — Many students write $E = \frac{1}{2}\rho g a^2$ correctly but then forget to convert $a = H/2$, leading to $E = \frac{1}{2}\rho g H^2$ (which is **4× too large**).
> 3. **Unit confusion** — $P_w$ formula gives **Watts per metre** unless you use the $0.49 H^2 T$ form (kW/m). Always state units explicitly.
> 4. **Not stating "deep water" assumption** — Examiners explicitly deduct 1 mark when depth conditions are not justified.
> 5. **Confusing Capture Width with Capture Width Ratio** — $L_c$ has units of length, $CWR$ is dimensionless.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision — Pin this in your brain before walking into the exam hall.**

- [ ] **Total wave energy per unit area** = $\frac{1}{8}\rho g H^2$ (deep water, linear).
- [ ] **Equipartition holds:** $KE = PE = \frac{1}{16}\rho g H^2$.
- [ ] **Power per crest length (deep)** = $\frac{\rho g^2 H^2 T}{32\pi} \approx 0.49 H^2 T$ (kW/m).
- [ ] **Use group velocity** $c_g = gT/(4\pi)$, not phase velocity.
- [ ] **Deep water** = depth > half-wavelength. Shallow = depth < $\lambda/20$.
- [ ] **CWR** (Capture Width Ratio) > 1 is theoretically possible; field devices achieve 0.3 – 0.5.
- [ ] **OWC** uses a Wells turbine (bidirectional, self-rectifying).
- [ ] **Pelamis** = attenuator; **LIMPET** = OWC; **Wave Dragon** = overtopping.
- [ ] **Wave energy density** ≈ 2–3 kW/m² (5–10× wind, 20–30× solar).
- [ ] **India potential:** Kerala coast (Vizhinjam), Gujarat, Tamil Nadu, Andaman & Nicobar.
- [ ] **Linear (Airy) wave theory** assumed throughout — mention this as a **limitation** in long-answer answers.
- [ ] **Survivability** in extreme storms (100-year wave) is the #1 engineering challenge for WECs.
- [ ] Always convert: $a$ (amplitude) = $H$ (height) / 2.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ocean Thermal Energy Conversion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECHT411 - ENERGY ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Ocean Thermal Energy Conversion # Module 2: Ocean Thermal Energy Conversion (OTEC) ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Ocean Thermal Energy Conversion (OTEC)** is a renewable energy conversion technology that exploits the natural thermal gradient existing between the **warm surface waters** of tropical/sub-tropical oceans and the **cold deep ocean waters** to drive a heat engine and produce useful work (primarily electricity). The temperature differential between the warm surface layer (typically **25°C–30°C**) and the deep cold layer at depths of **800–1000 m** (typically **4°C–8°C**) forms the thermodynamic potential. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** OTEC is classified under **Ocean Energy** within Module 2 (Solar & Renewable Energy Systems). Students must master the thermodynamic cycle, cycle variants, site selection criteria, and the **net power output equation** as it is a guaranteed high-weightage topic in Part B questions. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Plain-English Intuition:** Imagine a massive, naturally maintained "thermal battery" — the ocean. The sun acts as a continuous heater warming only the top few meters of seawater, while the deep ocean remains perpetually frigid. OTEC plants work much like a household refrigerator running in **reverse**: instead of using electricity to *create* a temperature difference, OTEC *uses* the naturally available temperature difference to **generate** electricity. Just as a small temperature difference (e.g., 4°C vs 30°C) powers a Carnot heat engine in your textbook, the tropical ocean offers this gradient for free — every day, all year round. ### 1.3 Historical & Geographical Context The concept was first proposed by **Jacques Arsene d'Arsonval** in **1881**, and the first experimental plant was built by his student **Georges Claude** in **Cuba in 1930**. Modern viable sites lie within the **±20° latitude belt** of the equator, where the year-round surface-to-depth temperature difference exceeds **20°C** — the minimum threshold for commercial viability. > [!NOTE] > **Standard Reference Metric:** The **Carnot thermal efficiency ceiling** of any OTEC plant is governed solely by the available temperature difference. For T_warm = 27°C (300 K) and T_cold = 5°C (278 K), η_Carnot ≈ **7.3%** — a small figure that dictates every downstream engineering decision in OTEC design. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Thermodynamic Foundation OTEC systems are essentially **low-temperature differential heat engines**. Because the available temperature lift (ΔT) is small (typically 20–25 K), the conversion efficiency is intrinsically low. Hence, OTEC demands: 1. **High volumetric mass flow rates** of both warm and cold seawater. 2. **Very large heat exchangers** with minimal fouling resistance. 3. **Low-friction hydraulic pathways** to minimize pumping losses. ### 2.2 Working Principle (Logical Step Breakdown) - **Step 1 — Warm Water Intake:** Surface seawater (solar-heated) is drawn through a large-diameter pipe. - **Step 2 — Vaporization:** In a heat exchanger (evaporator), a working fluid with a low boiling point (e.g., **ammonia**, R-134a) is vaporized by the warm water. - **Step 3 — Expansion:** The high-pressure vapor expands through a turbine, producing **shaft work** (rotational mechanical energy). - **Step 4 — Condensation:** The vapor is condensed back to liquid by cold deep-ocean water pumped up from ~1000 m depth in a **condenser**. - **Step 5 — Pumping & Recompression:** The liquid working fluid is repressurized by a feed pump and the cycle repeats. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units / Notes | |---|---------------------|------------|---------------| | 1 | Carnot (Ideal) Efficiency | $\eta_{\text{Carnot}} = 1 - \dfrac{T_C}{T_H}$ | Dimensionless; T in **Kelvin (K)** | | 2 | OTEC Available ΔT | $\Delta T = T_{\text{warm}} - T_{\text{cold}}$ | °C or K (numerically same) | | 3 | Net Power Output | $W_{\text{net}} = \eta_{\text{Carnot}} \cdot Q_H - W_{\text{parastic}}$ | Watts (W) | | 4 | Heat Input Rate | $Q_H = \dot{m}_w \cdot c_{p,w} \cdot (T_{\text{warm,in}} - T_{\text{warm,out}})$ | W; $c_{p,w} \approx 4.18$ kJ/kg·K | | 5 | Heat Rejection Rate | $Q_C = \dot{m}_c \cdot c_{p,c} \cdot (T_{\text{cold,out}} - T_{\text{cold,in}})$ | W | | 6 | Cold Water Mass Flow Ratio | $\dfrac{\dot{m}_c}{\dot{m}_w} = \dfrac{T_{\text{warm,in}} - T_{\text{warm,out}}}{T_{\text{cold,out}} - T_{\text{cold,in}}}$ | Dimensionless | | 7 | Energy Ratio (ER) | $ER = \dfrac{W_{\text{net}}}{Q_{\text{pump}}}$ | Must be $> 1$ for viability | | 8 | Minimum Viable ΔT | $\Delta T \geq 20\text{ °C}$ | Empirical threshold | | 9 | Working Fluid Boiling Point | NH₃ ≈ −33°C, Propane ≈ −42°C | Low BP enables vaporization | | 10 | Tropical Site Latitude Limit | $\vert \phi \vert \leq 20°$ | Equatorial belt only | ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Base-load renewable power** in island nations (Hawaii, Maldives, Caribbean, Pacific Islands). - **Freshwater co-production** (Open Cycle OTEC produces desalinated water as a by-product). - **Aquaculture & mariculture** using nutrient-rich deep ocean water. - **Air conditioning and chilled-water agriculture** using the cold deep-water stream. > [!NOTE] > **Critical "Why" Insight:** The biggest engineering challenge is that even with a perfect Carnot cycle, the *gross* efficiency is ~7%. Therefore, the **parasitic pumping power** (drawing 1000 m cold water + warm water) can easily consume the entire gross output if not carefully designed. The **Energy Ratio (ER)** is the key feasibility gate. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Carnot Efficiency for OTEC The fundamental thermodynamic upper bound for an OTEC plant operating between a warm reservoir (T_H) and a cold reservoir (T_C) is given by the reversible Carnot cycle. Starting from the Second Law definition of efficiency for a heat engine: $$\eta = \frac{W_{\text{net}}}{Q_H}$$ For a reversible (Carnot) engine, the entropy change of the working fluid over a complete cycle is zero: $$\oint dS = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{Q_H}{T_H} = \frac{Q_C}{T_C}$$ Rearranging to express $Q_C$ in terms of $Q_H$: $$Q_C = Q_H \cdot \frac{T_C}{T_H}$$ By the First Law (steady-state energy balance on the working fluid): $$W_{\text{net}} = Q_H - Q_C$$ Substituting the expression for $Q_C$: $$W_{\text{net}} = Q_H - Q_H \cdot \frac{T_C}{T_H} = Q_H \left(1 - \frac{T_C}{T_H}\right)$$ Therefore, dividing through by $Q_H$ yields the Carnot efficiency: $$\boxed{\eta_{\text{Carnot}} = 1 - \frac{T_C}{T_H}}$$ **Sample Numerical Evaluation:** For an OTEC site with $T_H = 27\,°C = 300\,K$ and $T_C = 5\,°C = 278\,K$: $$\eta_{\text{Carnot}} = 1 - \frac{278}{300} = 1 - 0.9267 = 0.0733 = 7.33\,\%$$ This 7.33% represents the *absolute maximum* theoretical efficiency. Real plants achieve only 2–3% due to irreversibilities, friction, and heat exchanger losses. ### 3.2 Derivation: Cold-to-Warm Mass Flow Ratio For steady-state operation, the heat absorbed by the working fluid from the warm water stream must equal the heat rejected by the working fluid to the cold water stream (neglecting turbine work in the energy balance of the heat exchangers): $$\dot{m}_w \cdot c_{p,w} \cdot (T_{w,\text{in}} - T_{w,\text{out}}) = \dot{m}_c \cdot c_{p,c} \cdot (T_{c,\text{out}} - T_{c,\text{in}})$$ Assuming $c_{p,w} \approx c_{p,c} = c_p$ (both are seawater), the specific heats cancel: $$\dot{m}_w \cdot (T_{w,\text{in}} - T_{w,\text{out}}) = \dot{m}_c \cdot (T_{c,\text{out}} - T_{c,\text{in}})$$ Rearranging to obtain the cold-to-warm mass flow ratio: $$\boxed{\frac{\dot{m}_c}{\dot{m}_w} = \frac{T_{w,\text{in}} - T_{w,\text{out}}}{T_{c,\text{out}} - T_{c,\text{in}}}}$$ This ratio is typically between **1.5 and 2.0**, meaning the cold water pump must handle **1.5–2× the mass flow** of the warm water pump. This is a critical design constraint for pipe sizing and pump power selection. ### 3.3 Derivation: Net Useful Power Output The gross turbine power output is: $$W_{\text{turbine}} = \eta_{\text{Carnot}} \cdot Q_H = \eta_{\text{Carnot}} \cdot \dot{m}_w \cdot c_{p,w} \cdot (T_{w,\text{in}} - T_{w,\text{out}})$$ The total parasitic power consumption includes: - Warm water pump: $P_{wp}$ - Cold water pump: $P_{cp}$ - Working fluid feed pump: $P_{fp}$ - Seawater intake/outlet losses: $P_{io}$ $$W_{\text{parasitic}} = P_{wp} + P_{cp} + P_{fp} + P_{io}$$ The **net usable electrical power output** is therefore: $$\boxed{W_{\text{net}} = W_{\text{turbine}} - W_{\text{parasitic}}}$$ For commercial viability, it is conventionally required that: $$W_{\text{net}} > 0 \quad \text{and} \quad \frac{W_{\text{net}}}{W_{\text{parasitic}}} > 3 \text{ to } 4$$ This is the **Energy Ratio (ER)** criterion used in KTU numerical problems. ### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: OTEC Cycle Variants | Parameter | Closed Cycle (Anderson) | Open Cycle (Claude) | Hybrid Cycle | |-----------|-------------------------|---------------------|--------------| | Working Fluid | Ammonia / R-134a (closed loop) | Seawater itself (vapor & liquid) | Combines both | | Pressure | High pressure (~10 bar) | Near-vacuum (~0.03 bar at 25°C) | Mixed | | Turbine Size | Compact | Very large (low ρ steam) | Moderate | | By-products | None | **Desalinated freshwater** | Freshwater + electricity | | Efficiency | 3–4% (gross) | 2–3% (gross) | 3–4% (gross) | | Complexity | Moderate (heat exchangers) | High (vacuum + deaeration) | Highest | | KTU Exam Frequency | **Very High** | High | Moderate | --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram: OTEC Closed-Cycle System ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Warm Surface Water
T about 27 C] --> B[Evaporator
Heat Exchanger] B --> C[Vaporized
Working Fluid NH3] C --> D[Turbine
Generates Power] D --> E[Condenser
Heat Exchanger] F[Cold Deep Water
T about 5 C from 1000 m] --> E E --> G[Condensed Liquid
NH3] G --> H[Feed Pump
Recompression] H --> B D --> I[Electrical Generator
Net Power Output] A --> J[Warm Water Out] F --> K[Cold Water Out] ``` ### 4.2 Block Diagram: OTEC Open-Cycle (Claude) System ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Warm Surface Seawater] --> B[Flash Evaporator
Vacuum Chamber] B --> C[Low Pressure Steam] C --> D[Steam Turbine] D --> E[Condenser
Cold Deep Water] E --> F[Condensate Freshwater] F --> G[Desalinated Water
Storage and Use] D --> H[Electrical Generator] B --> I[Brine Discharge] E --> J[Cold Water Out] ``` ### 4.3 Process Flow: Component-Level Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph INPUT[Seawater Intake Subsystem] W1[Warm Water Pipe
Diameter about 10 m] W2[Cold Water Pipe
Diameter about 12 m, Length 1000 m] end subgraph POWER[Power Generation Subsystem] P1[Evaporator] --> P2[Turbine] P2 --> P3[Generator] P3 --> P4[Output Bus] P5[Condenser] --> P1 P6[Feed Pump] --> P1 end subgraph OUTPUT[Output and Discharge Subsystem] O1[Warm Water Outfall] O2[Cold Water Outfall] O3[Nutrient Rich Deep Water
Optional Aquaculture] end W1 --> P1 W2 --> P5 P2 --> P5 P1 --> O1 P5 --> O2 P5 --> O3 ``` ### 4.4 Schematic: T-S Diagram of OTEC Carnot Cycle ```mermaid flowchart LR A[State 1: Saturated Liquid
at T about 5 C, High P] --> B[State 2: Compressed Liquid
at T about 27 C, Higher P] B --> C[State 3: Saturated Vapor
at T about 27 C, High P] C --> D[State 4: Wet Vapor
at T about 5 C, Low P] D --> A C -. Turb T3 to T4 .-> D B -. Pump T1 to T2 .-> A ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the T-S Diagram:** The rectangular area enclosed by states 1 → 2 → 3 → 4 represents the net work output of the ideal Carnot OTEC cycle. The horizontal top edge (2→3) is the heat addition $Q_H$ in the evaporator, and the bottom edge (4→1) is the heat rejection $Q_C$ in the condenser. --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) #### Question 1: Define Ocean Thermal Energy Conversion (OTEC). [3 Marks] `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Model Answer:** OTEC is a renewable energy technology that converts the thermal energy stored in the ocean's vertical temperature gradient into electrical or mechanical work. The system exploits the temperature difference between warm surface seawater (solar-heated, ~25–30°C) and cold deep seawater (drawn from ~1000 m depth, ~4–8°C). A heat engine operating on the Rankine or Carnot cycle uses the warm water as the heat source and the cold water as the heat sink. The minimum viable temperature difference is **20°C**, and economically viable sites lie within **±20° latitude** of the equator. The three main cycle variants are **Closed Cycle (Anderson)**, **Open Cycle (Claude)**, and **Hybrid Cycle**. --- #### Question 2: State the minimum temperature difference required for the economic viability of an OTEC plant. Why is this value significant? [3 Marks] `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Model Answer:** The minimum temperature difference for the **economic viability** of an OTEC plant is **20°C (ΔT ≥ 20 K)** between the warm surface water and the cold deep water. This threshold is significant because the **Carnot efficiency** scales linearly with ΔT (relative to T_H), and below 20°C, the gross thermal efficiency drops to such a low value (~5–6%) that the **parasitic pumping losses** (drawing vast volumes of cold deep water through 1 km pipes) consume the entire net output. Thus, the 20°C threshold ensures a positive **Energy Ratio (ER > 1)**, making the plant net-power-positive. --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice) #### Question A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(a)** Explain the principle, working, and components of a **Closed Cycle OTEC system** with a neat sketch. Discuss the role of the working fluid. **[7 Marks]** **(b)** A Closed Cycle OTEC plant operates with warm surface water at **27°C** and cold deep water at **5°C**. The warm water enters the evaporator at 27°C and leaves at 24°C. The cold water enters the condenser at 5°C and leaves at 8°C. The warm water mass flow rate is **2000 kg/s**. Assuming ideal Carnot performance, calculate: (i) The Carnot efficiency. (ii) The cold water mass flow rate. (iii) The gross turbine power output (take $c_p = 4.18$ kJ/kg·K). **[7 Marks]** --- **Model Solution:** **(a) Closed Cycle OTEC — Principle & Components:** In a **Closed Cycle (Anderson) OTEC** system, a low-boiling-point working fluid (typically **ammonia, NH₃**, or refrigerant R-134a) is recirculated in a sealed loop. The warm surface seawater passes through the **evaporator** (a heat exchanger), where it vaporizes the working fluid. The high-pressure vapor expands through a **turbine** coupled to an **electrical generator**, producing power. The expanded low-pressure vapor is then condensed in the **condenser** by the cold deep ocean water, and the liquid working fluid is pumped back to the evaporator by a **feed pump**. **Key components:** - Warm water intake pipe & cold water intake pipe (~1000 m depth) - Evaporator (heat exchanger) - Turbine + Generator - Condenser (heat exchanger) - Working fluid feed pump **Role of Working Fluid:** Ammonia is preferred because of its (i) **low boiling point** (≈ −33°C at 1 atm), enabling easy vaporization at warm water temperatures; (ii) **high latent heat of vaporization**, maximizing heat transfer per unit mass; and (iii) **good thermodynamic stability** in the 5–27°C range. --- **(b) Numerical Solution:** **Given Data:** $T_H = 27°C = 300\,K$, $T_C = 5°C = 278\,K$, $T_{w,\text{in}} = 27°C$, $T_{w,\text{out}} = 24°C$, $T_{c,\text{in}} = 5°C$, $T_{c,\text{out}} = 8°C$, $\dot{m}_w = 2000$ kg/s, $c_p = 4.18$ kJ/kg·K. **(i) Carnot Efficiency:** $$\eta_{\text{Carnot}} = 1 - \frac{T_C}{T_H} = 1 - \frac{278}{300} = 1 - 0.9267 = 0.0733$$ $$\boxed{\eta_{\text{Carnot}} = 7.33\%}$$ **(ii) Cold Water Mass Flow Rate:** Using the energy balance relation: $$\frac{\dot{m}_c}{\dot{m}_w} = \frac{T_{w,\text{in}} - T_{w,\text{out}}}{T_{c,\text{out}} - T_{c,\text{in}}} = \frac{27 - 24}{8 - 5} = \frac{3}{3} = 1.0$$ $$\dot{m}_c = 1.0 \times 2000 = \boxed{2000 \text{ kg/s}}$$ **(iii) Gross Turbine Power Output:** First, the heat input rate from warm water: $$Q_H = \dot{m}_w \cdot c_p \cdot (T_{w,\text{in}} - T_{w,\text{out}}) = 2000 \times 4.18 \times (27 - 24)$$ $$Q_H = 2000 \times 4.18 \times 3 = 25{,}080 \text{ kW}$$ Now, gross turbine power output: $$W_{\text{turbine}} = \eta_{\text{Carnot}} \cdot Q_H = 0.0733 \times 25{,}080$$ $$W_{\text{turbine}} = 1838.4 \text{ kW}$$ $$\boxed{W_{\text{turbine}} \approx 1.84 \text{ MW}}$$ --- #### Question B (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a)** Compare **Open Cycle (Claude) OTEC** and **Closed Cycle (Anderson) OTEC** systems. Mention at least six points of comparison. **[7 Marks]** **(b)** Discuss the major **site selection criteria** for OTEC plants. What are the key **environmental impacts** and benefits? **[7 Marks]** --- **Model Solution:** **(a) Comparison: Open vs Closed Cycle OTEC:** | Parameter | Open Cycle (Claude) | Closed Cycle (Anderson) | |-----------|---------------------|-------------------------| | Working fluid | Warm seawater itself (vapor & condensate) | Ammonia or refrigerant (sealed loop) | | Operating pressure | Near vacuum (~0.03 bar) | High pressure (~10 bar) | | Cycle type | Flash evaporation of warm seawater | Conventional Rankine cycle | | By-products | Desalinated freshwater | None | | Turbine design | Very large (low-density steam) | Compact | | Heat exchangers | Not required; direct contact | Required (evaporator + condenser) | | Efficiency | 2–3% (gross) | 3–4% (gross) | | Capital cost | High (vacuum systems) | Moderate | | Complexity | High (deaeration needed) | Moderate | **(b) Site Selection Criteria & Environmental Impacts:** **Site Selection Criteria:** 1. **Temperature difference ΔT ≥ 20°C** between surface and 1000 m depth (year-round stability). 2. **Latitude:** within ±20° of the equator (tropical/sub-tropical belt). 3. **Bathymetry:** steep continental shelf enabling cold water intake within short horizontal distance from shore. 4. **Depth:** access to 800–1500 m within 5–10 km of shore. 5. **Stable, low-storm location** (island nations preferred). 6. **Proximity to load centers** (island grids) to minimize transmission losses. 7. **Ocean current stability** and low surface wave action to protect the intake structure. **Environmental Impacts & Benefits:** > [!IMPORTANT] > - **Negative:** Large seawater intake may entrain/mortal small marine organisms; discharge of cold nutrient-rich water may create localized thermal and ecological disturbance; risk of working fluid leaks (ammonia toxicity). > - **Positive:** Zero fuel combustion → zero CO₂ emissions; co-production of **freshwater**; nutrient-rich deep water supports **aquaculture**; provides **base-load renewable power** (24/7, unlike solar/wind). --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting to convert °C to Kelvin** in the Carnot efficiency formula — this costs 1 full mark. Always use T in K. > 2. **Sign error in cold water flow ratio** — ensure you use the temperature *differences* of the respective streams, not absolute temperatures. > 3. **Not subtracting parasitic losses** in net power — examiners specifically test whether students know that gross turbine power ≠ net useful power. > 4. **Confusing Open and Closed Cycle** working fluids — remember: Open = seawater itself; Closed = ammonia/R-134a. > 5. **Forgetting the latitude (±20°) and ΔT (≥20°C) site selection criteria** in short-answer questions. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **High-Density Revision Checklist:** > - OTEC = Ocean Thermal Energy Conversion; exploits **ΔT between surface (~27°C) and deep (~5°C) ocean water**. > - Minimum viable **ΔT = 20°C**; viable latitude **±20°** of equator. > - **Carnot efficiency** $\eta = 1 - T_C / T_H$ — always use **Kelvin**. > - **Closed Cycle (Anderson):** uses ammonia as working fluid in sealed loop; high pressure (~10 bar); compact. > - **Open Cycle (Claude):** uses seawater itself as working fluid; near-vacuum; produces **freshwater** as by-product. > - **Hybrid Cycle:** combines features of both; produces both electricity and freshwater. > - **Cold-to-warm flow ratio** typically 1.5–2.0; cold water pipe ~1000 m long. > - **Energy Ratio (ER)** must be > 1 (preferably > 3) for commercial viability. > - **Pioneers:** Jacques d'Arsonval (concept, 1881); Georges Claude (first plant, Cuba, 1930). > - **Real efficiency** ~2–3% (gross); Carnot ceiling ~7.3% for ΔT = 22 K. > - **Environmental concerns:** marine organism entrainment, ammonia leak risk, thermal discharge. > - **Co-benefits:** desalination, aquaculture (using cold nutrient-rich deep water), air conditioning. ---

topics.title: Tidal Energy Conversion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT411 - ENERGY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Tidal Energy Conversion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Tidal Energy** is the form of **renewable ocean energy** that is generated by exploiting the natural rise and fall of sea water levels caused by the **gravitational interaction** between the Earth, the Moon, and the Sun. The engineering discipline that harnesses this predictable mechanical motion of sea water and converts it into useful electrical energy is called **Tidal Energy Conversion (TEC)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *"Tidal energy is the energy captured from the kinetic energy of moving water (tidal streams) and the potential energy created by the difference in height between high and low tides (tidal range)."*

The predictable, cyclic nature of tides makes tidal energy one of the most **reliable renewable resources**, unlike solar or wind which are intermittent. Tides occur **twice a day** (semi-diurnal) in most coastal regions, with a cycle period of approximately **12 hours 25 minutes** (a full lunar day).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have a **giant bathtub** connected to the ocean through a small pipe. As the ocean tide rises, water fills the bathtub. When the tide falls, the water trapped in the bathtub is at a higher level than the outside ocean. If you open a valve, water rushes out through a turbine, just like water flowing out of a kitchen sink when you pull the plug — but in slow motion and on a massive scale. This height difference (called **Head**) is what we exploit.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy:** A tidal barrage is conceptually similar to a **hydroelectric dam**, but instead of a river, the "river" is the ocean itself, and the "dam" is built across a coastal bay or estuary. The Moon acts as the invisible engine that pumps water up and down twice a day.

### 1.3 Origin of Tides — The Physics Behind the Phenomenon

The primary cause of ocean tides is the **gravitational pull of the Moon** (≈ **67%** contribution) and the **Sun** (≈ **33%** contribution). Two fundamental forces govern tidal generation:

| Force | Mechanism | Magnitude |
|---|---|---|
| **Gravitational Attraction** | Moon/Sun pulls ocean water toward itself | Directly proportional to mass, inversely to distance² |
| **Centrifugal Force** | Earth-Moon system rotates around common barycenter | Creates opposing bulge on far side |

These two forces create **two tidal bulges** on opposite sides of the Earth — one facing the Moon (due to gravitational pull) and one on the far side (due to centrifugal force). As the Earth rotates, every coastal location passes through these two bulges, producing **two high tides and two low tides per day**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric:** The **mean tidal range** (difference between average high tide and average low tide) across the world is approximately **0.6 m to 1 m**. Sites become **economically viable for tidal power only when the tidal range exceeds 5 m**, with ideal sites having ranges of **7–10 m or higher**.

### 1.4 Classification of Tidal Energy Systems

Tidal energy conversion is broadly classified into **three major technologies**, each exploiting a different physical aspect of the tide:

1. **Tidal Barrage (Tidal Range Technology)** — Exploits the *potential energy* from the height difference (head) between the basin and the sea.
2. **Tidal Stream Technology** — Exploits the *kinetic energy* of fast-flowing tidal currents, similar to underwater wind turbines.
3. **Tidal Lagoon** — An artificial enclosure built offshore where tidal range is exploited using bidirectional turbines.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Module Highlight:** Under Module 2, special emphasis is placed on **Tidal Barrage systems** — their single-basin (single-effect & double-effect) and double-basin configurations, along with numerical estimation of available power.

### 1.5 Key Tidal Terminology (KTU 2024 Vocabulary)

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Tidal Range (R)** | Vertical distance between high tide and low tide levels, measured in meters |
| **Spring Tide** | Maximum tidal range occurring at **new moon** and **full moon** (Sun + Moon aligned) |
| **Neap Tide** | Minimum tidal range occurring at **first quarter** and **third quarter** moon (Sun + Moon at 90°) |
| **High Water Mark (HWM)** | Maximum height reached by seawater during high tide |
| **Low Water Mark (LWM)** | Minimum height reached by seawater during low tide |
| **Basin Area (A)** | Surface area of the impounded water body behind the barrage, in m² |
| **Head (h)** | Difference in water level between basin side and sea side, in meters |
| **Cycle Time (T)** | Time between successive high tides ≈ **12.42 hours** |

### 1.6 Why Tidal Energy is Important for India and the World

- **Predictability:** Tides can be predicted centuries in advance with high accuracy.
- **High Energy Density:** Water has ≈ **834 times** the density of air, so tidal currents carry much more energy than wind at the same speed.
- **Zero Fuel Cost:** No carbon emissions during operation.
- **Long Lifespan:** Tidal barrages have a design life of **75–100 years**, longer than most power plants.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** When writing answers, always emphasize that tidal energy is a **non-conventional, renewable, sustainable, and predictable** energy source. Examiners reward answers that correctly distinguish tidal energy from wave energy and ocean thermal energy.

---

## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Working Principle of a Tidal Barrage Plant

A **tidal barrage** is a dam-like structure constructed across a bay, estuary, or river mouth. It contains **low-head turbines**, **sluice gates**, and **lock gates** to allow ships to pass. The operational logic proceeds in **four distinct phases** per cycle:

#### Phase 1 — Flooding (Generation Phase I)
- At **low tide**, the **sluice gates** are opened.
- Sea water flows into the basin through the sluices (no power generated yet).
- As the sea level rises outside and reaches the basin level, the sluice gates are **closed**.
- A **head difference** develops as the sea continues to rise.

#### Phase 2 — Generating at High Tide
- Once sufficient **head (typically 2–5 m)** develops, the **turbine gates** are opened.
- Water flows from the sea (higher level) into the basin (lower level) through the **turbines**, generating electricity.

#### Phase 3 — Holding Phase (Storage)
- After the sea reaches **high tide**, all gates are closed.
- Water is held in the basin until the sea level on the ocean side drops below the basin level.

#### Phase 4 — Ebbing (Generation Phase II)
- When the sea outside drops sufficiently, turbines are opened again.
- Water flows **from basin to sea** through turbines, generating electricity during the **ebb tide**.

### 2.2 Types of Tidal Power Plants

#### 2.2.1 Single Basin — Single Effect (One-Way Generation)

- Power is generated **only during the flood tide** (or only during the ebb tide), not both.
- **Cycle:** Low tide → fill basin → generate at high tide → empty basin at low tide.
- **Period of generation:** ≈ **6–7 hours per cycle**.
- **Disadvantage:** Continuous power supply is interrupted; requires backup or grid storage.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Think of it as a bucket that fills, then tips over once and stops — you have to wait for the next fill.

#### 2.2.2 Single Basin — Double Effect (Two-Way Generation)

- Power is generated **during both flood and ebb tides**.
- **Cycle:** Sea fills → generate (flood) → hold → empty → generate (ebb).
- **Period of generation:** ≈ **10–14 hours per cycle**, providing better continuity.
- This is the configuration used at the **La Rance Tidal Power Station, France (240 MW)**.

#### 2.2.3 Double Basin (Two-Basin Scheme)

- Two interconnected basins with a continuous **low head** maintained between them.
- **Basin 1** fills during high tide; **Basin 2** empties during low tide.
- Continuous generation of electricity for **up to 24 hours**.
- **Disadvantage:** High capital cost; complex civil engineering.

#### 2.2.4 Tidal Stream Technology

- Uses **underwater rotors / axial-flow turbines** mounted on the seabed.
- Exploits **kinetic energy** of fast-flowing tidal currents (typically 2–5 m/s).
- No barrage, no large civil works — minimal environmental impact.
- Examples: **SeaGen (Northern Ireland, 1.2 MW)**, **MeyGen (Scotland, 398 MW planned)**.

### 2.3 Site Selection Criteria (KTU 2024 Module Outcome)

A site must satisfy all of the following conditions for **economic viability**:

| Sl. No. | Criterion | Minimum Acceptable Value |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Tidal Range** | > **5 m** (ideal: 7–10 m) |
| 2 | **Basin Area (A)** | Large natural bay or estuary |
| 3 | **Narrow Tidal Inlet** | Reduces barrage length & cost |
| 4 | **Suitable Geology** | Bedrock capable of supporting barrage |
| 5 | **Environmental Acceptability** | Minimal disruption to marine ecology |
| 6 | **Distance to Load Centre** | < **50–100 km** for transmission feasibility |
| 7 | **Storm Protection** | Natural shelter from cyclones/tsunamis |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **World-class Tidal Sites:** Bay of Fundy (Canada, R = 16.3 m), La Rance (France, R = 8.5 m), Severn Estuary (UK, R = 14 m), Gulf of Kutch (India, R = 7 m), Gulf of Cambay (India, R = 8.4 m potential).

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following equations form the **core mathematical toolkit** for solving any KTU examination problem on Tidal Energy Conversion.

| Equation | Formula | Description / Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Potential Energy Stored in Basin** | $E = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot g \cdot A \cdot R^2$ | Total energy in one full tidal cycle (Joules) |
| **Average Power (Single-Basin, Double-Effect)** | $P_{avg} = \dfrac{\rho \cdot g \cdot A \cdot R^2}{2T}$ | T = cycle period (s); one cycle = 12.42 h |
| **Average Power (in 24 h, Double-Effect)** | $P_{avg} = \dfrac{\rho \cdot g \cdot A \cdot R^2 \cdot n}{2}$ | n = number of cycles in 24 h (≈ **2**) |
| **Annual Energy Output** | $E_{annual} = P_{avg} \times 8760 \times \eta$ | η = overall efficiency (typically 0.30–0.45) |
| **Power from Tidal Stream** | $P = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot A \cdot V^3 \cdot C_p$ | V = current velocity, $C_p$ = power coefficient (max **0.593**, Betz limit) |
| **Average Head** | $h_{avg} = \dfrac{R}{2}$ | Useful when using average values for estimates |
| **Volume Flow Rate** | $Q = A_b \cdot v$ | $A_b$ = barrage cross-section, v = flow velocity |
| **Power Through Turbine** | $P_{turbine} = \rho \cdot g \cdot Q \cdot h \cdot \eta_t$ | $h$ = instantaneous head, $\eta_t$ = turbine efficiency |
| **Density of Seawater** | $\rho_{sw} = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$ | Use **1025**, not 1000 (freshwater) |
| **Acceleration due to Gravity** | $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ | Standard KTU value |
| **Tidal Cycle Period** | $T = 12.42 \text{ hours} = 44712 \text{ s}$ | One complete tide cycle |
| **Number of Cycles per Day** | $n = 24 / 12.42 \approx 1.93 \approx 2$ | Use **n = 2** for most KTU problems |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Constant Values to Memorize:**
> - $\rho_{seawater} = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$
> - $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
> - $\rho \cdot g \approx 10054 \text{ N/m}^3$ (for seawater)

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Tidal energy systems are deployed in regions where:
- Conventional hydropower is **geographically impossible** (flat coastal areas).
- Grid stability requires **baseload renewable power** (predictable dispatch).
- Strategic island nations (Indonesia, Philippines, Japan, UK) need to **reduce fossil fuel imports**.

The technology has direct applications in:
- **Coastal desalination plants** powered by adjacent tidal barrages.
- **Hydrogen production** via electrolysis using surplus tidal electricity.
- **Remote island microgrids** in the Andaman & Nicobar Islands (India).

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Tidal power plants have a **capacity factor of 22–28%**, compared to 12–25% for solar PV and 25–45% for wind — making them among the most reliable renewables available.

### 2.6 Advantages and Limitations

**Advantages:**
- Renewable, pollution-free, predictable
- High energy density (water vs. air)
- Long plant life (75–100 years)
- No fuel cost, low operational cost

**Limitations:**
- High **capital cost** (₹ crores for barrage construction)
- Intermittent generation (only during tide windows)
- Environmental impact on marine life (fish migration, sediment transport)
- Limited to coastal sites with high tidal range
- Possible siltation and ecological disruption

## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Potential Energy Stored in a Tidal Basin

**Problem Setup:** Consider a tidal basin of surface area **A** (m²) filled during high tide to a height **R** above low tide level. We want to compute the gravitational potential energy that can be extracted as the basin empties.

#### Step 1: Define an Element of Water

Let us consider a thin horizontal slice of water at depth **x** below the high tide mark. The thickness of this slice is **dx**, and its surface area is approximately **A** (assuming a vertical basin wall — a standard simplifying assumption).

The mass of this elemental slice is:

$$dm = \rho \cdot A \cdot dx$$

The elevation of this slice above the low tide level is **x** meters (since it is **x** meters below the high tide mark, and the low tide level is **R** meters below the high tide mark, so its height above low tide = R − x).

#### Step 2: Potential Energy of the Element

The potential energy of this elemental slice relative to the low tide level is:

$$dE = dm \cdot g \cdot (R - x) = \rho \cdot A \cdot g \cdot (R - x) \cdot dx$$

This expresses the energy that this thin layer would release if the basin were emptied down to low tide level.

#### Step 3: Integrate Over the Full Depth

To obtain the total potential energy stored in the entire water column (from **x = 0** at the high tide surface to **x = R** at the low tide level), integrate:

$$E = \int_{0}^{R} \rho \cdot A \cdot g \cdot (R - x) \, dx$$

#### Step 4: Evaluate the Integral

$$E = \rho \cdot A \cdot g \int_{0}^{R} (R - x) \, dx$$

Expanding the integrand:

$$E = \rho \cdot A \cdot g \left[ Rx - \dfrac{x^2}{2} \right]_{0}^{R}$$

#### Step 5: Substitute the Limits

$$E = \rho \cdot A \cdot g \left[ (R \cdot R) - \dfrac{R^2}{2} - (0 - 0) \right]$$

$$E = \rho \cdot A \cdot g \left[ R^2 - \dfrac{R^2}{2} \right]$$

$$E = \rho \cdot A \cdot g \cdot \dfrac{R^2}{2}$$

#### Step 6: Final Result

$$\boxed{E = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot g \cdot A \cdot R^2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Interpretation:** The potential energy scales with the **square of the tidal range** — this is why sites with even moderately higher tidal range are exponentially more productive. A site with R = 8 m yields **4 times more energy** than a site with R = 4 m for the same basin area.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Average Power Output of a Tidal Barrage

**Problem Setup:** We now want to find the average power delivered continuously over the full tidal cycle period **T** (≈ 12.42 hours = 44712 seconds). For a **double-effect scheme**, two cycles occur in 24 hours.

#### Step 1: Energy Generated Per Cycle

Using the result from Derivation 1, the energy generated per filling/emptying cycle is:

$$E_{cycle} = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot g \cdot A \cdot R^2$$

This represents the energy from one complete tidal cycle (one fill + one empty operation).

#### Step 2: Define Average Power

Average power is defined as the total energy divided by the time period:

$$P_{avg} = \dfrac{E_{cycle}}{T}$$

#### Step 3: Substitute and Simplify

$$P_{avg} = \dfrac{\rho \cdot g \cdot A \cdot R^2}{2T}$$

#### Step 4: Account for Number of Cycles per Day

For **n** cycles per day (typically **n = 2**), the total energy per day is:

$$E_{day} = n \cdot E_{cycle} = \dfrac{n \cdot \rho \cdot g \cdot A \cdot R^2}{2}$$

The 24-hour average power is:

$$P_{day,avg} = \dfrac{E_{day}}{86400} = \dfrac{n \cdot \rho \cdot g \cdot A \cdot R^2}{2 \times 86400}$$

#### Step 5: Simplification for 24-Hour Cycle

Alternatively, dividing the cycle energy by 24 hours gives the average daily power:

$$\boxed{P_{avg} = \dfrac{\rho \cdot g \cdot A \cdot R^2 \cdot n}{2 \times 24 \times 3600}}$$

or equivalently, using the cycle period directly:

$$\boxed{P_{avg} = \dfrac{\rho \cdot g \cdot A \cdot R^2}{2T} \quad \text{(where T = 12.42 h = 44712 s)}}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Power from Tidal Streams (Kinetic Energy)

**Problem Setup:** Tidal stream systems extract kinetic energy from moving water using underwater turbines. We treat the water mass as a fluid moving at velocity **V** through a swept area **A**.

#### Step 1: Mass Flow Rate

The mass of water passing through the swept area per unit time is:

$$\dot{m} = \rho \cdot A \cdot V$$

#### Step 2: Kinetic Energy Per Unit Mass

The kinetic energy per unit mass of water moving at velocity V is:

$$KE_{unit} = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot V^2$$

#### Step 3: Kinetic Power in the Fluid

The total kinetic power available in the fluid stream is:

$$P_{available} = \dot{m} \cdot KE_{unit} = \rho \cdot A \cdot V \cdot \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot V^2$$

$$P_{available} = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot A \cdot V^3$$

#### Step 4: Apply the Turbine Power Coefficient

Not all this power can be extracted. The fraction extractable is the **power coefficient $C_p$**, theoretically bounded by the **Betz limit** ($C_p \leq 16/27 \approx 0.593$).

$$\boxed{P_{tidal} = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot A \cdot V^3 \cdot C_p}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** Tidal stream power scales with the **cube of velocity**, meaning a 2× increase in current speed gives **8× more power**. This makes site velocity selection the single most critical design parameter for tidal stream devices.

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Board Pattern

**Problem (Typical 14-mark KTU question):**

> A tidal barrage is proposed across a coastal bay with an effective basin area of **30 km²** and a mean tidal range of **8 m**. Calculate:
> (a) The total potential energy available per tidal cycle.
> (b) The average power output (double-effect scheme).
> (c) The annual energy generation in MWh at an overall efficiency of 40%.

#### Given Data:
- Basin area, $A = 30 \text{ km}^2 = 30 \times 10^6 \text{ m}^2$
- Tidal range, $R = 8 \text{ m}$
- Density of seawater, $\rho = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- Acceleration due to gravity, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
- Cycle period, $T = 12.42 \text{ h} = 44712 \text{ s}$
- Number of cycles per day, $n = 2$
- Overall efficiency, $\eta = 0.40$

#### Part (a): Total Energy per Cycle

$$E = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot g \cdot A \cdot R^2$$

$$E = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 9.81 \times (30 \times 10^6) \times (8)^2$$

$$E = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 9.81 \times 30 \times 10^6 \times 64$$

Computing step-by-step:

$$E = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 9.81 \times 1.92 \times 10^9$$

$$E = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 9.81 \times 1.92 \times 10^9$$

$$E = 0.5 \times 9.65 \times 10^12 \approx 9.65 \times 10^{12} \text{ J}$$

(Detailed: 1025 × 9.81 = 10055.25; × 30 × 10⁶ = 3.017 × 10¹¹; × 64 = 1.931 × 10¹³; × 0.5 = **9.654 × 10¹² J**)

$$\boxed{E_{cycle} \approx 9.65 \times 10^{12} \text{ J} = 2682 \text{ MWh}}$$

#### Part (b): Average Power Output

$$P_{avg} = \dfrac{\rho \cdot g \cdot A \cdot R^2}{2T}$$

$$P_{avg} = \dfrac{1025 \times 9.81 \times 30 \times 10^6 \times 64}{2 \times 44712}$$

Numerator = 9.654 × 10¹² J (from above)
Denominator = 89424 s

$$P_{avg} = \dfrac{9.654 \times 10^{12}}{89424} \approx 1.08 \times 10^8 \text{ W}$$

$$\boxed{P_{avg} \approx 108 \text{ MW}}$$

#### Part (c): Annual Energy at 40% Efficiency

Energy per day = $P_{avg} \times n \times T = 2 \times 9.654 \times 10^{12}$ J = 1.931 × 10¹³ J/day

After efficiency:

$$E_{useful,day} = 0.40 \times 1.931 \times 10^{13} = 7.723 \times 10^{12} \text{ J/day}$$

Converting to MWh (1 MWh = 3.6 × 10⁹ J):

$$E_{useful,day} = \dfrac{7.723 \times 10^{12}}{3.6 \times 10^9} \approx 2145 \text{ MWh/day}$$

Annual:

$$E_{annual} = 2145 \times 365 = 782925 \text{ MWh/year}$$

$$\boxed{E_{annual} \approx 783,000 \text{ MWh/year} = 783 \text{ GWh/year}}$$

---

### 3.5 Comparative Analytical Matrix

| Parameter | Tidal Barrage | Tidal Stream | Tidal Lagoon |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Energy Source** | Potential (head) | Kinetic (velocity) | Potential (head) |
| **Range / Velocity** | R = 5–16 m | V = 2–5 m/s | R = 5–8 m |

| **Environmental Impact** | High | Low | Moderate |
| **Capital Cost** | Very High | Moderate | Moderate–High |
| **Famous Example** | La Rance, Sihwa | SeaGen, MeyGen | Swansea Bay (UK) |
| **Capacity Factor** | 22–28% | 25–35% | 20–25% |

## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Tidal Barrage Energy Conversion Pathway

The following block diagram illustrates the complete energy conversion pathway of a tidal barrage power plant, from the celestial cause to the electrical grid output.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[MOON AND SUN] --> A2[GRAVITATIONAL AND CENTRIFUGAL FORCES]
    A2 --> A3[OCEAN TIDAL BULGE RISING AND FALLING]
    A3 --> B1[TIDAL RANGE IN BAY OR ESTUARY]
    A3 --> C1[TIDAL CURRENT IN NARROW INLET]
    B1 --> B2[HEAD DIFFERENCE BETWEEN SEA AND BASIN]
    B2 --> B3[SLUICE GATES OPEN FOR FLOODING]
    B3 --> B4[BASIN FILLS TO HIGH TIDE LEVEL]
    B4 --> B5[TURBINE GATES OPEN WATER FLOWS THROUGH TURBINE]
    B5 --> B6[BULB TURBINE OR STRAFLO TURBINE]
    C1 --> C2[UNDERWATER ROTOR BLADES]
    C2 --> C3[GENERATOR COUPLING]
    B6 --> D1[ROTATIONAL MECHANICAL ENERGY]
    C3 --> D1
    D1 --> D2[GENERATOR PRODUCES ELECTRICAL ENERGY]
    D2 --> D3[STEP UP TRANSFORMER]
    D3 --> D4[TRANSMISSION LINE TO GRID]
    D4 --> E1[END USERS COASTAL CITIES INDUSTRIES]
    E1 --> F1[UNUSED ENERGY DISCHARGED VIA SLUICES DURING HOLDING PHASE]
```

## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**  
Define tidal energy. With a neat sketch, explain the origin of tides due to the gravitational pull of the Moon.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Tidal energy is the renewable energy obtained by harnessing the kinetic energy of tidal currents or the potential energy of the difference in water levels between high and low tides, generated primarily by the gravitational interaction between the Earth, Moon, and Sun.

**Origin of Tides (2 marks):** The Moon's gravity exerts a non-uniform pull on the Earth's ocean — the side closest to the Moon experiences a stronger pull, creating a bulge of water (high tide). On the opposite side, the centrifugal force of the Earth-Moon system creates a second bulge. As the Earth rotates on its axis, coastal regions pass through these two bulges, producing two high tides and two low tides every ~24 hours. The Sun's gravity contributes additively (spring tides, when aligned) or subtractively (neap tides, when perpendicular), modulating the tidal range.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**  
Differentiate between spring tides and neap tides. Why is the tidal range critical for the economic viability of a tidal power plant?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Spring Tide | Neap Tide |
|---|---|---|
| **Cause** | Sun, Moon, Earth aligned (new/full moon) | Sun and Moon at 90° to Earth (quarter moon) |
| **Gravitational Forces** | Combined (additive) | Partially cancelled |
| **Tidal Range** | Maximum (e.g., 16 m at Bay of Fundy) | Minimum (e.g., 4 m at Bay of Fundy) |

**Criticality for Viability (1 mark):** Since available tidal energy scales as **R²**, even a small increase in tidal range produces a dramatic increase in energy output. A site with R = 8 m yields **4 times more energy** than one with R = 4 m for the same basin area. Most economically viable tidal plants require a minimum range of **5 m**, with optimal sites exceeding **7 m**.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Model Paper 2024]**  
**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of a **single-basin double-effect tidal power plant**. Clearly identify all four operational phases in one complete tidal cycle. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A tidal barrage is proposed at a coastal site with the following data:
- Basin area, A = **20 km²**
- Mean tidal range, R = **7 m**
- Cycle period, T = **12.42 hours**
- Overall plant efficiency, η = **35%**

Calculate:
- (i) The total potential energy available per tidal cycle. **(3 marks)**
- (ii) The average power output in MW. **(2 marks)**
- (iii) The annual energy generation in GWh. **(2 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

A **single-basin double-effect** tidal power plant generates electricity during **both the flood (rising) tide and the ebb (falling) tide** in one complete cycle.

**Phase 1 – Flooding Phase (Sluicing):** At low tide, the **sluice gates** in the barrage are opened. Sea water flows into the basin through the sluices. The water level in the basin rises until it equals the sea level at high tide.

**Phase 2 – Generation (Flood Tide):** The sluice gates are closed. As the sea level outside continues to rise beyond the basin level (since basin water is held static), a **head difference** develops. When the head reaches a usable value (≈ 2–3 m), the **turbine gates** are opened. Water flows from the sea to the basin through **bulb turbines** mounted in the barrage, generating electricity. This is the **flood generation phase**.

**Phase 3 – Holding Phase:** Once the sea reaches high tide and begins to recede, all gates are closed. The basin retains water at high-tide level while the sea level drops outside, re-establishing a head difference.

**Phase 4 – Generation (Ebb Tide):** When the sea level drops sufficiently below the basin level, the turbines are restarted. Water flows from the basin back to the sea, generating electricity again — this is the **ebb generation phase**.

At La Rance (France), this scheme provides **continuous generation for 10–14 hours per cycle**, with interruptions only around slack tide periods.

**Block Diagram:**
```
Sea (High) → Turbine (flood) → Basin
Sea (Low) ← Turbine (ebb) ← Basin
```

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

**Given:**  
$A = 20 \text{ km}^2 = 20 \times 10^6 \text{ m}^2$  
$R = 7 \text{ m}$  
$T = 12.42 \text{ h} = 44712 \text{ s}$  
$\rho = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$  
$\eta = 0.35$  
Number of cycles per day, n = 2

**(i) Total potential energy per cycle:**

$$E = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot g \cdot A \cdot R^2$$

$$E = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 9.81 \times 20 \times 10^6 \times 49$$

$$E = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 9.81 \times 9.8 \times 10^8$$

Computing: $1025 \times 9.81 = 10055.25$  
$10055.25 \times 9.8 \times 10^8 = 9.854 \times 10^{12}$  
$\times 0.5 = 4.927 \times 10^{12}$ J

$$\boxed{E \approx 4.927 \times 10^{12} \text{ J} = 1369 \text{ MWh per cycle}}$$

**(ii) Average power output:**

$$P_{avg} = \dfrac{\rho \cdot g \cdot A \cdot R^2}{2T}$$

$$P_{avg} = \dfrac{4.927 \times 10^{12}}{44712} \approx 1.102 \times 10^8 \text{ W}$$

$$\boxed{P_{avg} \approx 110.2 \text{ MW}}$$

**(iii) Annual energy generation at 35% efficiency:**

Energy per day = $2 \times 4.927 \times 10^{12}$ J = $9.854 \times 10^{12}$ J

Useful energy per day = $0.35 \times 9.854 \times 10^{12} = 3.449 \times 10^{12}$ J/day

Converting to GWh: divide by $3.6 \times 10^{12}$ (since 1 GWh = $3.6 \times 10^{12}$ J):

$$E_{daily} = \dfrac{3.449 \times 10^{12}}{3.6 \times 10^{12}} = 0.958 \text{ GWh/day}$$

$$E_{annual} = 0.958 \times 365 = 349.7 \text{ GWh/year}$$

$$\boxed{E_{annual} \approx 350 \text{ GWh/year}}$$

---

#### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – June 2023]**  
**(a)** What are the major **site selection criteria** for establishing a tidal power plant? Compare the **single-effect** and **double-effect** schemes of a single-basin tidal power plant. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A tidal stream farm uses underwater turbines to extract kinetic energy from a tidal current. The swept area of each turbine is **80 m²** and the average current velocity is **2.5 m/s**. If the power coefficient $C_p$ is **0.4**, calculate:
- (i) Available kinetic power per turbine. **(3 marks)**
- (ii) Useful electrical power per turbine. **(1 mark)**
- (iii) Total power output if **25 such turbines** are deployed. **(2 marks)**
- (iv) Comment on what would happen if the current velocity doubled to **5 m/s**. **(1 mark)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

**Site Selection Criteria (4 marks):**

1. **High Tidal Range** — Should exceed **5 m** for economic viability; optimal sites have R > 7 m.
2. **Large Basin Area** — A wide, shallow bay or estuary maximises impounded volume and energy storage.
3. **Narrow Tidal Inlet** — Reduces barrage length, lowering civil engineering costs.
4. **Favourable Geology** — Bedrock capable of bearing barrage weight; minimal seismic risk.
5. **Environmental Compatibility** — Minimal disruption to marine ecosystems, fish migration, and sediment flow.
6. **Proximity to Load Centres** — Within 50–100 km to minimise transmission losses.
7. **Storm Protection** — Natural shelter from cyclones and tsunamis.

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Feature | Single Effect | Double Effect |
|---|---|---|
| **Generation Periods** | Once per cycle (flood or ebb) | Twice per cycle (flood AND ebb) |
| **Generation Hours** | ≈ 6–7 h per cycle | ≈ 10–14 h per cycle |
| **Energy Output** | Lower | ~1.4–1.6× higher |
| **Mechanical Complexity** | Simpler | More complex turbine reversal |
| **Operating Cost** | Lower | Higher |
| **Example** | Kislaya Guba (Russia, small) | La Rance (France, 240 MW) |

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

**Given:** $A = 80 \text{ m}^2$, $V = 2.5 \text{ m/s}$, $C_p = 0.4$, $\rho = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$, Number of turbines = 25

**(i) Available kinetic power per turbine:**

$$P_{available} = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot A \cdot V^3$$

$$P_{available} = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 80 \times (2.5)^3$$

$$P_{available} = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 80 \times 15.625$$

$$P_{available} = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 1250 = 640625 \text{ W}$$

$$\boxed{P_{available} \approx 640.6 \text{ kW per turbine}}$$

**(ii) Useful electrical power per turbine:**

$$P_{electrical} = C_p \times P_{available} = 0.4 \times 640.6 \text{ kW}$$

$$\boxed{P_{electrical} \approx 256.25 \text{ kW per turbine}}$$

**(iii) Total power output for 25 turbines:**

$$P_{total} = 25 \times 256.25 \text{ kW} = 6406.25 \text{ kW}$$

$$\boxed{P_{total} \approx 6.41 \text{ MW}}$$

**(iv) Effect of doubling velocity to 5 m/s:**

Since $P \propto V^3$, doubling the velocity increases the power by a factor of $2^3 = 8$.

$$P_{new} = 8 \times 640.6 \text{ kW} = 5125 \text{ kW per turbine}$$

$$\boxed{P_{new} \approx 5.125 \text{ MW per turbine, an 8-fold increase}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** This $V^3$ relationship is why **tidal stream developers obsess over a 0.5 m/s increase in current speed** — even modest velocity gains translate into disproportionately large power gains.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students typically lose marks on Tidal Energy Conversion problems:**
>
> 1. **Using ρ = 1000 kg/m³ (freshwater) instead of 1025 kg/m³ (seawater).** This is a **silent error** — your numerical answer will be off by ~2.5%, which examiners often dock for under "incorrect constants."
>
> 2. **Forgetting the "n" factor for cycles per day.** A common mistake is computing energy per cycle and then multiplying by 365 directly. The correct approach is: energy per day = n × energy per cycle (where n = 2 for double-effect), then multiply by 365.
>
> 3. **Confusing single-basin single-effect with double-effect.** In single-effect, generation happens once per cycle; in double-effect, twice. This affects the formula by a factor of 2.
>
> 4. **Mixing up units.** Always convert km² to m², hours to seconds, and final answers to standard units (MW for power, GWh for annual energy). Examiners deduct marks for inconsistent units.
>
> 5. **Omitting the efficiency factor (η).** Theoretical energy = E; useful electrical energy = η × E. Without this, you'll overstate the plant output.
>
> 6. **In derivations, failing to integrate properly.** Marks are awarded for the integration step — write $\int_0^R (R-x) dx$ explicitly. Skipping this step loses 1–2 marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Notes for Tidal Energy Conversion:**
>
> - **Primary Cause:** Gravitational pull of Moon (67%) and Sun (33%); two bulges cause **semi-diurnal tides** every **12.42 hours**.
> - **Spring Tides:** Sun + Moon aligned → maximum tidal range.
> - **Neap Tides:** Sun + Moon perpendicular → minimum tidal range.
> - **Economic Threshold:** Tidal range must exceed **5 m** for viability.
> - **Single-Effect Scheme:** Generates only during one phase (flood OR ebb); ~6–7 hours/cycle.
> - **Double-Effect Scheme:** Generates during both phases; ~10–14 hours/cycle; La Rance uses this.
> - **Double-Basin Scheme:** Two interconnected basins provide continuous 24-hour generation.
> - **Tidal Stream:** Uses kinetic energy; $P = \frac{1}{2} \rho A V^3 C_p$; $V^3$ dependence is critical.
> - **Master Formula (Barrage):** $E = \frac{1}{2} \rho g A R^2$ → Energy per cycle.
> - **Master Formula (Average Power):** $P_{avg} = \frac{\rho g A R^2}{2T}$ → continuous average.
> - **Standard Constants:** $\rho_{sw} = 1025$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s², $T = 12.42$ h, $n = 2$ cycles/day.
> - **Betz Limit:** Maximum $C_p = 0.593$ (16/27) for any kinetic energy extraction device.
> - **Energy scales with $R^2$** — small tidal range increases yield exponentially.
> - **Power scales with $V^3$** — even small velocity boosts are hugely valuable for tidal stream.
> - **Capacity Factor:** Tidal = 22–28% (one of the highest among renewables).
> - **Famous Tidal Plants:** La Rance (France, 240 MW), Sihwa Lake (South Korea, 254 MW), Bay of Fundy (Canada, potential 7000 MW).
> - **Indian Potential:** Gulf of Kutch (R ≈ 7 m) and Gulf of Cambay (R ≈ 8.4 m) are prime sites.
> - **Advantages:** Renewable, predictable, no fuel cost, long life (75–100 years).
> - **Limitations:** High capital cost, intermittent (only during tide windows), marine ecology impact.
> - **Major Component:** Sluice gates (for filling/emptying), bulb turbines (for generation), lock gates (for navigation).
> - **Remember the 4 phases:** Flooding → Generation (flood) → Holding → Generation (ebb).
> - **Most Tested Formula in KTU exams:** $E = \frac{1}{2} \rho g A R^2$ and $P_{avg} = \frac{\rho g A R^2}{2T}$.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Geothermal Energy: Applications and Mechanism for Electricity Production
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT411 - ENERGY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Geothermal Energy: Applications and Mechanism for Electricity Production

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Geothermal Energy** is the thermal energy generated and stored within the Earth's crust, originating from the planet's original formation (~4.5 billion years ago), ongoing radioactive decay of isotopes (principally Uranium-238, Thorium-232, and Potassium-40), and convective processes within the mantle. It is harnessed as a renewable resource for direct heating applications and indirect conversion into electrical energy.

According to KTU 2024 syllabus terminology, geothermal resources are systematically classified based on reservoir enthalpy and temperature into three operational categories that dictate the conversion technology to be employed:

| Resource Grade | Temperature Range | Enthalpy Range | Preferred Conversion Technology |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| High-Grade | $> 200^\circ C$ | $> 1500 \text{ kJ/kg}$ | Dry Steam, Flash Steam |
| Intermediate-Grade | $90^\circ C - 200^\circ C$ | $400 - 1500 \text{ kJ/kg}$ | Binary Cycle |
| Low-Grade | $< 90^\circ C$ | $< 400 \text{ kJ/kg}$ | Direct Use, Heat Pumps |

The **Geothermal Gradient** represents the rate of temperature increase with depth in the Earth's crust. The global average geothermal gradient is **$\approx 25 - 30^\circ C$ per km**, but this value can escalate dramatically near tectonic plate boundaries, volcanic arcs, and hotspots.

> [!NOTE]
> **Reference Datum:** The standard surface temperature baseline is taken as **$15^\circ C$** (global annual mean surface temperature as per KTU thermodynamic reference standards).

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Earth's Pressure Cooker

> [!TIP]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine the Earth as a giant, slightly leaky pressure cooker that has been on a stove for 4.5 billion years.
>
> - The **outer shell** (lithosphere) is the rigid metal lid.
> - The **magma chambers** below are the boiling water and steam.
> - **Radioactive decay** acts as the continuous heating element (the stove burner).
> - When we drill wells, we are essentially **inserting a straw** to tap into this natural steam/heat reservoir.

Just as a pressure cooker builds up internal energy that we release via a valve to do work (turning the whistle), geothermal wells release Earth's internal heat to drive turbines and generate electricity. The deeper the straw, the hotter the resource, and the more efficient the conversion.

---

### 1.3 Why Geothermal Energy Matters for KTU Examinations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner Perspective:** Geothermal energy is a **high-frequency conceptual topic** in PECHT411 because it bridges three fundamental engineering domains: **heat transfer (conduction in crust), thermodynamics (Rankine cycle variants), and energy economics (baseload capacity factor of 90–95%)**. Expect both descriptive (4 marks) and numerical derivations (7 marks).

**Key Physical Constants to Memorise:**

- Earth surface heat flux (global average): **$q_0 \approx 65 \text{ mW/m}^2$**
- Average crustal thermal conductivity: **$k \approx 2.0 - 3.0 \text{ W/(m·K)}$**
- Standard geothermal gradient (non-volcanic): **$\mathbf{25 - 30^\circ C/km}$**
- Typical magma chamber temperature: **$700 - 1200^\circ C$**
- Boiling point of common binary fluids: **$R-134a \approx -26^\circ C$, Isobutane \approx -11.7^\circ C, Isopentane \approx 27.8^\circ C$**

---

### 1.4 Classification of Geothermal Resources

The KTU 2024 syllabus specifically categorizes resources by **fluid phase and reservoir characteristics**:

1. **Vapour-Dominated Systems** — Reservoir produces dry or superheated steam (e.g., The Geysers, California, USA; Larderello, Italy).
2. **Liquid-Dominated Systems** — Reservoir produces hot water (or brine), which may be flashed to steam at the surface.
3. **Hot Dry Rock (HDR) / Enhanced Geothermal Systems (EGS)** — Engineered reservoirs where cold water is injected and fractured rock acts as the heat exchanger.
4. **Geo-pressured Systems** — Contain both thermal energy and dissolved natural gas under high pressure.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — The Geothermal Energy Conversion Chain

The complete geothermal-to-electrical pathway involves **four sequential energy transformation stages**:

**Stage 1 — Subsurface Extraction**
Hot fluid (steam or brine) rises through production wells from depths typically between **1.5 km and 4 km**, driven by natural reservoir pressure (artesian flow) or assisted by downhole pumps.

**Stage 2 — Surface Reception & Phase Management**
A wellhead separator divides the two-phase flow into steam and liquid phases. The liquid (brine) may be either reinjected or flashed at lower pressure to extract additional vapour.

**Stage 3 — Mechanical-to-Electrical Conversion**
Steam drives a turbine coupled to a generator. This is where the **specific plant technology** (dry steam / flash / binary) becomes critical.

**Stage 4 — Condensation & Reinjection**
Spent steam is condensed in a cooling tower or direct-contact condenser, and the cooled fluid is reinjected into the reservoir to maintain pressure and ensure sustainability.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Reinjection Matters:** Modern KTU-aligned geothermal engineering mandates **100% reinjection** of cooled brine to ensure reservoir longevity, prevent subsidence, and meet environmental discharge regulations (zero surface emission standard).

---

### 2.2 Three Principal Electricity Production Mechanisms

#### Mechanism A: Dry Steam Power Plant (Simplest, Highest Efficiency)

**Operational Logic:**
- Steam directly from reservoir enters turbine → no phase change needed
- Ideal for vapour-dominated fields where reservoir fluid is already superheated steam
- Historical prototype: **Larderello, Italy (1904)** — world's first geothermal power plant

#### Mechanism B: Flash Steam Power Plant (Most Common Globally)

**Operational Logic:**
- High-pressure hot water ($>180^\circ C$) reaches surface → enters low-pressure separator (flash tank)
- Sudden pressure drop causes a fraction of the brine to "flash" into steam
- Steam drives turbine → remaining brine discarded or reinjected
- **Single-flash** (one stage) or **Double-flash** (two sequential pressure drops, +15–25% efficiency)

#### Mechanism C: Binary Cycle Power Plant (Low-Temperature Friendly)

**Operational Logic:**
- Hot geothermal brine passes through a **Heat Exchanger (Evaporator)** and transfers heat to a secondary working fluid with a low boiling point (e.g., isobutane, isopentane, R-134a)
- Secondary fluid vaporizes → drives organic Rankine cycle (ORC) turbine → condenses → repeats
- Enables electricity generation from **$80^\circ C - 170^\circ C$** resources
- **Closed-loop** — no geothermal fluid contacts the atmosphere (zero emissions, no scaling)

---

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following equations are mandatory for solving KTU numerical problems. Memorise the form, derivation, and units.**

| # | Equation (LaTeX) | Description | Units | Applicable To |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---:|:---|
| 1 | $q = -k \dfrac{dT}{dz}$ | Fourier's Law of Heat Conduction (Earth crust) | $\text{W/m}^2$ | All conduction analysis |
| 2 | $\dfrac{dT}{dz} = \dfrac{q}{k}$ | Geothermal Gradient from Heat Flux | $^\circ C/\text{km}$ | Crustal heat flow |
| 3 | $T(z) = T_0 + \left(\dfrac{dT}{dz}\right) \cdot z$ | Temperature at Depth z | $^\circ C$ | Reservoir estimation |
| 4 | $P_{th} = \dot{m}_{geo} \cdot c_p \cdot (T_{res} - T_{rej})$ | Thermal Power of Geothermal Fluid | $\text{W}$ | Resource sizing |
| 5 | $P_{elec} = \eta_{cycle} \cdot \dot{m}_{geo} \cdot (h_{in} - h_{out})$ | Electrical Power Output | $\text{W}$ | Power plant design |
| 6 | $\eta_{carnot} = 1 - \dfrac{T_{cold}}{T_{hot}}$ | Ideal Carnot Efficiency | dimensionless | Theoretical max |
| 7 | $\eta_{real} = 0.45 \cdot \eta_{carnot}$ | Actual Binary Cycle Efficiency | dimensionless | ORC plants |
| 8 | $\dot{Q}_{HX} = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{LM}$ | Heat Exchanger Sizing | $\text{W}$ | Binary cycle design |
| 9 | $y = \dfrac{h_f - h_{liq}}{h_{fg}}$ | Steam Quality after Flashing | dimensionless | Flash plants |
| 10 | $CF = \dfrac{E_{actual}}{P_{rated} \times 8760}$ | Capacity Factor | dimensionless | Plant economics |

> [!CAUTION]
> **Absolute Value Pitfall:** When writing equations like $y = \vert h_f - h_{liq} \vert / h_{fg}$, **never** use the vertical bar `|` inside markdown tables — it breaks the table parser. Use $\vert$ or $\mid$ LaTeX delimiters instead.

---

### 2.4 Engineering Real-World Utility

> [!TIP]
> **Why this matters in industry:** Geothermal plants achieve a **capacity factor (CF) of 90–95%**, vastly superior to solar (15–25%) and wind (25–45%). This makes geothermal a premier **baseload renewable** — it operates 24/7 regardless of weather. The International Renewable Energy Agency (IRENA) identifies geothermal as a key technology for **decarbonizing industrial process heat**, currently dominated by natural gas and coal.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Temperature Profile of the Earth's Crust (1-D Steady-State Conduction)

**Problem Setup:** A geothermal reservoir is located at depth $z = 3 \text{ km}$ in a region with average thermal conductivity $k = 2.5 \text{ W/(m·K)}$ and surface heat flux $q_0 = 80 \text{ mW/m}^2$. Surface temperature is $T_0 = 15^\circ C$. Calculate the reservoir temperature and the local geothermal gradient.

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Starting from 1-D Fourier conduction:} \quad & q = -k \, \frac{dT}{dz} \\
\text{For steady-state, q is constant with depth:} \quad & \frac{dT}{dz} = -\frac{q}{k} = \frac{q_0}{k} \\
\text{Substituting numerical values (converting units):} \quad & \frac{dT}{dz} = \frac{80 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{W/m}^2}{2.5 \, \text{W/(m·K)}} \\
\text{Computing the gradient magnitude:} \quad & \frac{dT}{dz} = 0.032 \, \text{K/m} = 32 \, ^\circ C/\text{km} \\
\text{Integrating from surface (z=0, T=T_0) to depth z:} \quad & \int_{T_0}^{T(z)} dT = \frac{q_0}{k} \int_{0}^{z} dz \\
\text{Linear temperature profile emerges:} \quad & T(z) = T_0 + \frac{q_0}{k} \cdot z \\
\text{Substituting z = 3000 m, T_0 = 15°C:} \quad & T(3 \text{ km}) = 15 + 32 \times 3 = 15 + 96 \\
\text{Final reservoir temperature:} \quad & \boxed{T(3 \text{ km}) = 111 \, ^\circ C}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** A reservoir at $111^\circ C$ falls in the **intermediate-grade** category — a **binary cycle plant** with isobutane as the secondary fluid would be the appropriate technology choice, achieving an ideal Carnot efficiency of $\eta_{carnot} = 1 - (273+30)/(273+111) \approx 20.9\%$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Electrical Power Output of a Flash Steam Plant

**Problem Setup:** A single-flash geothermal plant receives brine at $T_{res} = 220^\circ C$ and separator pressure $P_{sep} = 5 \text{ bar}$. Geothermal mass flow rate is $\dot{m}_{geo} = 50 \text{ kg/s}$. After flashing, the liquid fraction is rejected at separator saturation temperature. Steam enters turbine at $T_{turb} = 152^\circ C$ and exhausts at condenser pressure $P_{cond} = 0.1 \text{ bar}$. Turbine isentropic efficiency $\eta_T = 0.80$, generator efficiency $\eta_G = 0.95$. Calculate electrical power output.

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1 — Identify saturation state at separator (5 bar):} \quad & T_{sat,5bar} \approx 151.86^\circ C, \quad h_{f} = 640.1 \text{ kJ/kg}, \quad h_{fg} = 2108 \text{ kJ/kg} \\
\text{Step 2 — Enthalpy of flashed steam (= saturated vapour at 5 bar):} \quad & h_{steam} = h_{g} = h_{f} + h_{fg} = 640.1 + 2108 = 2748.1 \text{ kJ/kg} \\
\text{Step 3 — Enthalpy of residual liquid (separator saturation):} \quad & h_{liq} = h_{f} = 640.1 \text{ kJ/kg} \\
\text{Step 4 — Quality (mass fraction vaporized) from energy balance:} \quad & y = \frac{h_{res} - h_{liq}}{h_{fg}} \\
\text{Step 5 — Approximate h_{res} as saturated liquid at 220°C, P ≈ 23.2 bar:} \quad & h_{res} \approx h_{f@220°C} \approx 943.7 \text{ kJ/kg} \\
\text{Step 6 — Numerically compute quality:} \quad & y = \frac{943.7 - 640.1}{2108} = \frac{303.6}{2108} \approx 0.144 \\
\text{Step 7 — Steam mass flow rate to turbine:} \quad & \dot{m}_{steam} = y \cdot \dot{m}_{geo} = 0.144 \times 50 = 7.2 \text{ kg/s} \\
\text{Step 8 — Turbine inlet enthalpy:} \quad & h_{1} = 2748.1 \text{ kJ/kg} \\
\text{Step 9 — Isentropic exit at P_{cond} = 0.1 bar, s_1 = s_{g@5bar} = 6.821 kJ/kg·K:} \quad & h_{2s} \approx 2366 \text{ kJ/kg (interpolated at 0.1 bar)} \\
\text{Step 10 — Actual turbine exit enthalpy using η_T:} \quad & h_{2} = h_{1} - \eta_T (h_{1} - h_{2s}) = 2748.1 - 0.80 \times (2748.1 - 2366) \\
\text{Computing turbine work per kg:} \quad & h_{1} - h_{2} = 0.80 \times 382.1 = 305.7 \text{ kJ/kg} \\
\text{Step 11 — Total turbine mechanical power:} \quad & \dot{W}_{T} = \dot{m}_{steam} \times (h_{1} - h_{2}) = 7.2 \times 305.7 = 2201 \text{ kW} \\
\text{Step 12 — Electrical power output (after generator):} \quad & \boxed{P_{elec} = \eta_G \cdot \dot{W}_T = 0.95 \times 2201 \approx 2091 \text{ kW} \approx 2.1 \text{ MW}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mark Allocation Insight (KTU):** This 7-mark sub-question typically allocates **2 marks for the quality equation**, **2 marks for enthalpy lookups**, **2 marks for the turbine work calculation**, and **1 mark for the generator efficiency application**. Missing the generator efficiency step is a frequent 1-mark deduction.

---
