### 3.3 Derivation 3: Binary Cycle Heat Exchanger Sizing

**Problem Setup:** Design the evaporator heat exchanger area for a binary ORC plant. Geothermal brine enters at $T_{brine,in} = 170^\circ C$ and exits at $T_{brine,out} = 90^\circ C$ with mass flow $\dot{m}_{brine} = 20 \text{ kg/s}$, $c_p = 4.2 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$. Isobutane evaporates from saturated liquid at $T_{evap} = 40^\circ C$ to saturated vapour. Overall heat transfer coefficient $U = 800 \text{ W/(m}^2\text{·K)}$. Find the required area.

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1 — Total heat duty transferred from brine to isobutane:} \quad & \dot{Q} = \dot{m}_{brine} \cdot c_p \cdot (T_{brine,in} - T_{brine,out}) \\
\text{Substituting values:} \quad & \dot{Q} = 20 \times 4.2 \times (170 - 90) = 20 \times 4.2 \times 80 \\
\text{Computing:} \quad & \dot{Q} = 6720 \text{ kW} = 6.72 \times 10^6 \text{ W} \\
\text{Step 2 — Log Mean Temperature Difference (counter-flow arrangement):} \quad & \Delta T_1 = 170 - 40 = 130^\circ C \\
\text{At the cold end:} \quad & \Delta T_2 = 90 - 40 = 50^\circ C \\
\text{LMTD formula:} \quad & \Delta T_{LM} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)} = \frac{130 - 50}{\ln(130/50)} = \frac{80}{\ln(2.6)} \\
\text{Computing logarithm:} \quad & \ln(2.6) \approx 0.9555 \\
\text{LMTD value:} \quad & \Delta T_{LM} = \frac{80}{0.9555} \approx 83.7^\circ C \\
\text{Step 3 — Heat exchanger area from Q = U·A·ΔT_{LM}:} \quad & A = \frac{\dot{Q}}{U \cdot \Delta T_{LM}} \\
\text{Substituting:} \quad & A = \frac{6.72 \times 10^6}{800 \times 83.7} = \frac{6.72 \times 10^6}{66960} \\
\text{Final area:} \quad & \boxed{A \approx 100.4 \text{ m}^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Plant Type Selection

| Parameter | Dry Steam | Flash Steam | Binary Cycle |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Minimum Resource Temp | $> 180^\circ C$ | $> 180^\circ C$ | $> 80^\circ C$ |
| Working Fluid | Natural Steam | Steam (from flashing) | Organic fluid (ORC) |
| Net Thermal Efficiency | $10 - 20\%$ | $7 - 15\%$ | $5 - 12\%$ |
| Capacity Factor | $90 - 95\%$ | $85 - 92\%$ | $85 - 95\%$ |
| Greenhouse Emissions | Low | Medium (H₂S if present) | Negligible (closed loop) |
| Capital Cost per kW | Moderate | Moderate | Higher |
| Reservoir Sustainability Risk | High (if no reinjection) | Medium | Low |
| H₂S Abatement Required | Yes | Yes | Not needed |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Geothermal Resource Classification Flowchart

The diagram below maps the decision pathway for selecting a geothermal power plant based on reservoir characteristics — a frequently asked 7-mark descriptive question in KTU ESE.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1["Geothermal Resource Identified"] --> node2{"Resource Temperature?"}
    node2 -->|"T > 200 deg C"| node3{"Reservoir Fluid Phase?"}
    node2 -->|"90 to 200 deg C"| node4["Binary Cycle ORC Plant"]
    node2 -->|"T < 90 deg C"| node5["Direct Use or Heat Pump"]
    node3 -->|"Vapour Dominated"| node6["Dry Steam Power Plant"]
    node3 -->|"Liquid Dominated"| node7{"Single or Double Flash?"}
    node7 -->|"Simpler Setup"| node8["Single Flash Plant"]
    node7 -->|"Higher Output Needed"| node9["Double Flash Plant"]
    node6 --> node10["Direct Turbine Drive"]
    node8 --> node11["Separator and Turbine"]
    node9 --> node12["Two Stage Flashing"]
    node4 --> node13["Heat Exchanger and ORC Turbine"]
    node5 --> node14["District Heating Aquaculture Greenhouses"]
    node10 --> node15["Condenser and Cooling Tower"]
    node11 --> node15
    node12 --> node15
    node13 --> node16["Closed Loop Secondary Cycle"]
    node15 --> node17["Reinjection Well"]
    node16 --> node18["Brine Reinjection"]
    node14 --> node19["End Use Application"]
```

---

### 4.2 Binary Cycle Plant — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    geo["Geothermal Brine from Production Well"] --> evapHX["Evaporator Heat Exchanger"]
    isoL["Isobutane Saturated Liquid"] --> evapHX
    evapHX --> isoV["Isobutane Saturated Vapour"]
    isoV --> tur["ORC Turbine"]
    tur --> gen["Electrical Generator"]
    gen --> grid["Power Grid"]
    tur --> cond["Condenser"]
    cond --> pump["Circulation Pump"]
    pump --> isoL
    evapHX --> brineOut["Cooled Brine"]
    brineOut --> reinj["Reinjection Well"]
    cond --> coolT["Cooling Tower"]
    coolT --> cond
```

**Functional Notes:**

- The left vertical loop (blue pathway in physical plants) is the **geothermal primary loop** — it never touches the turbine.
- The right inner loop is the **organic Rankine cycle** — fully sealed, preventing scaling and H₂S corrosion.
- Heat crosses from primary to secondary loop only at the **evaporator heat exchanger**.

---

### 4.3 Flash Steam Plant — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    well["Hot Brine Production Well"] --> sep["Steam Separator Flash Tank"]
    sep -->|"Steam Vapour"| tur["Steam Turbine"]
    sep -->|"Liquid Brine"| rej["Rejected Brine or Reinjection"]
    tur --> gen["Generator"]
    gen --> grid["Electrical Grid Output"]
    tur --> cond["Condenser"]
    cond --> cw["Cooling Water System"]
    cw --> cond
    rej --> reinj["Reinjection Pump"]
    reinj --> deepRes["Deep Reservoir"]
```

---

### 4.4 Schematic Legend (Geothermal Wellhead Configuration)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    surf["Surface Equipment Level"] --> wellhead["Wellhead Master Valve"]
    wellhead --> flowCtrl["Flow Control Silencer"]
    flowCtrl --> sepUnit["Separator and Cyclone"]
    sepUnit --> turBin["Steam Turbine Inlet"]
    sepUnit --> liqOut["Brine Outlet"]
    liqOut --> reinjPump["Reinjection Pump"]
    reinjPump --> deepWell["Deep Reinjection Well Below 2.5 km"]
    deepWell --> res["Geothermal Reservoir"]
    res --> prodWell["Production Well 1.5 to 3 km Deep"]
    prodWell --> surf
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]** Define geothermal energy and list the three principal mechanisms used for electricity production from geothermal resources.

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (2 marks):** Geothermal energy is the thermal energy stored within the Earth's interior, originating from primordial heat of formation and the continuous radioactive decay of unstable isotopes such as Uranium-238, Thorium-232, and Potassium-40 within the crust and mantle. It is classified as a renewable energy resource because the natural heat flux exceeds anthropogenic extraction rates by several orders of magnitude.
>
> **Three Mechanisms (1 mark):**
> 1. **Dry Steam Power Plant** — Direct use of naturally occurring steam from vapour-dominated reservoirs.
> 2. **Flash Steam Power Plant** — High-pressure hot water flashed to steam at surface separator.
> 3. **Binary Cycle Power Plant** — Secondary organic fluid vaporized via heat exchanger (Organic Rankine Cycle).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]** Differentiate between a vapour-dominated and a liquid-dominated geothermal reservoir with one example each.

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Vapour-dominated (1.5 marks):** Reservoir contains dry or superheated steam as the continuous phase in pore spaces. The pressure and temperature of the reservoir lie along the **saturation curve** at low pressures (typically 30–40 bar). Steam flows naturally to surface via artesian pressure. **Example:** The Geysers, California, USA; Larderello, Italy.
>
> **Liquid-dominated (1.5 marks):** Reservoir contains hot liquid water (or brine) as the continuous phase, with steam existing only as dissolved gas or in vapour bubbles at the top of the reservoir. The reservoir is **pressurized above saturation pressure** for the given temperature, preventing bulk boiling. **Example:** Wairakei, New Zealand; most Philippines fields.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Essay / Numerical Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks):** *(Choose A or B)*

**Q3. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **Binary Cycle Geothermal Power Plant** using an organic working fluid. Discuss its advantages over flash steam plants and calculate the electrical power output for the following data.

**Given Data:**
- Geothermal brine: $T_{in} = 180^\circ C$, $T_{out} = 95^\circ C$, $\dot{m}_{geo} = 40 \text{ kg/s}$, $c_p = 4.18 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$
- Isobutane cycle: Evaporates at $T_{evap} = 50^\circ C$, condenses at $T_{cond} = 25^\circ C$
- Turbine isentropic efficiency: $\eta_T = 0.78$
- Generator efficiency: $\eta_G = 0.96$
- Isobutane mass flow: $\dot{m}_{iso} = 25 \text{ kg/s}$
- Latent heat of vaporisation of isobutane at $50^\circ C$: $h_{fg,iso} = 290 \text{ kJ/kg}$

**Sub-parts:**

**(a)** Explain with block diagram the working principle and advantages. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Calculate the electrical power output. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Working Principle and Block Diagram (7 marks)**

In a binary cycle plant, the **geothermal brine (primary fluid)** transfers its heat through a closed-loop heat exchanger to a **secondary working fluid** with a low boiling point — typically isobutane, isopentane, or R-134a. The secondary fluid vaporises and drives an organic Rankine cycle (ORC) turbine, which is coupled to an electrical generator. The vapour is then condensed in a surface condenser and recirculated by a feed pump.

**Block Diagram (already covered in Section 4.2 — replicate it here in the answer sheet).**

**Advantages over Flash Steam Plants (3 marks):**

1. **Lower temperature viability:** Can operate with brine as cool as $80^\circ C$, vastly expanding the resource base.
2. **Zero direct emissions:** The geothermal brine never contacts the turbine — eliminates H₂S corrosion and scaling.
3. **Closed-loop secondary cycle:** Isobutane is conserved indefinitely; only heat crosses between loops.
4. **Higher net efficiency at low temperatures:** Carnot efficiency advantage with low $T_{cold}$.
5. **Modular construction:** Standard ORC units can be factory-built and transported to site.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1 — Verify energy balance (heat available from brine):} \quad & \dot{Q}_{available} = \dot{m}_{geo} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T = 40 \times 4.18 \times (180 - 95) \\
\text{Computing:} \quad & \dot{Q}_{available} = 40 \times 4.18 \times 85 = 14{,}212 \text{ kW} \\
\text{Step 2 — Heat actually absorbed by isobutane (limited by evaporation duty):} \quad & \dot{Q}_{absorbed} = \dot{m}_{iso} \cdot h_{fg,iso} = 25 \times 290 = 7250 \text{ kW} \\
\text{Step 3 — Specific work output of ORC turbine:} \quad & w_T = \eta_T \cdot (h_{1} - h_{2s}) = 0.78 \times 290 = 226.2 \text{ kJ/kg} \\
\text{Step 4 — Turbine mechanical power output:} \quad & \dot{W}_T = \dot{m}_{iso} \cdot w_T = 25 \times 226.2 = 5655 \text{ kW} \\
\text{Step 5 — Electrical power output (after generator):} \quad & \boxed{P_{elec} = \eta_G \cdot \dot{W}_T = 0.96 \times 5655 \approx 5429 \text{ kW} \approx 5.43 \text{ MW}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** A common mistake is to use $\dot{Q}_{available}$ directly as $\dot{m}_{iso} \cdot h_{fg}$, ignoring the actual isobutane mass flow rate. The plant design is constrained by the **smaller of the two heat duties**. Also, do **not** forget to apply generator efficiency $\eta_G$ — this costs 1 mark if missed.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks):** *(Alternative Choice)*

**Q4. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** Explain the principle of a **Flash Steam Geothermal Power Plant** with a block diagram. Calculate the steam quality at the separator exit and the plant's electrical output.

**Given Data:**
- Reservoir fluid: hot water at $T_{res} = 240^\circ C$, $P_{res} = 33.6 \text{ bar}$
- Separator pressure: $P_{sep} = 7 \text{ bar}$
- Total geothermal mass flow: $\dot{m}_{geo} = 100 \text{ kg/s}$
- Turbine inlet: saturated vapour at 7 bar; Turbine exhaust: 0.15 bar
- Turbine isentropic efficiency: $\eta_T = 0.82$
- Generator efficiency: $\eta_G = 0.95$

**Steam Table Data Provided in Exam:**
- At $P_{sep} = 7 \text{ bar}$: $h_f = 697.1 \text{ kJ/kg}$, $h_g = 2763.1 \text{ kJ/kg}$, $h_{fg} = 2066.0 \text{ kJ/kg}$, $s_g = 6.708 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$
- At $P_{res}$ (sat. liq. @ $240^\circ C$): $h_f \approx 1086.0 \text{ kJ/kg}$
- At $P_{cond} = 0.15 \text{ bar}$: $h_f = 226.0 \text{ kJ/kg}$, $h_{fg} = 2373.1 \text{ kJ/kg}$, $s_f = 0.7549 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$, $s_g = 8.0085 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$

**Sub-parts:**

**(a)** Explain working principle, block diagram, and discuss single vs double flash. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compute steam quality and electrical power output. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Working Principle (7 marks)**

In a flash steam plant, high-pressure hot water from the reservoir undergoes a sudden pressure drop at the surface separator (flash tank). Because the saturation temperature at the lower separator pressure is significantly below the brine temperature, a fraction of the brine instantaneously vaporises ("flashes") into steam. This steam is routed through a turbine to generate electricity, while the residual liquid is either flashed a second time (double flash) or reinjected.

**Single vs Double Flash (2 marks):**

- **Single Flash:** One pressure-drop stage; simpler; efficiency typically 7–12%.
- **Double Flash:** Liquid from first separator enters a second, lower-pressure separator; recovers additional steam; efficiency improves by 15–25%; higher capital cost.

**Block Diagram** — Refer to Section 4.3 above.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1 — Steam quality at separator exit (y):} \quad & y = \frac{h_{res} - h_{f@P_{sep}}}{h_{fg@P_{sep}}} = \frac{1086.0 - 697.1}{2066.0} = \frac{388.9}{2066.0} \\
\text{Computing quality:} \quad & y \approx 0.1882 \text{ (i.e., 18.82%)} \\
\text{Step 2 — Steam mass flow rate to turbine:} \quad & \dot{m}_{steam} = y \cdot \dot{m}_{geo} = 0.1882 \times 100 = 18.82 \text{ kg/s} \\
\text{Step 3 — Turbine inlet conditions: saturated vapour at 7 bar} \quad & h_1 = h_g = 2763.1 \text{ kJ/kg}, \quad s_1 = s_g = 6.708 \text{ kJ/kg·K} \\
\text{Step 4 — Isentropic expansion to 0.15 bar (s_{2s} = s_1):} \quad & s_{2s} = 6.708 \text{ kJ/kg·K} \\
\text{At 0.15 bar: s_f = 0.7549, s_g = 8.0085, so s_{2s} is between} \quad & x_{2s} = \frac{s_{2s} - s_f}{s_g - s_f} = \frac{6.708 - 0.7549}{8.0085 - 0.7549} = \frac{5.9531}{7.2536} \\
\text{Computing isentropic quality:} \quad & x_{2s} \approx 0.8206 \\
\text{Step 5 — Isentropic exit enthalpy:} \quad & h_{2s} = h_f + x_{2s} \cdot h_{fg} = 226.0 + 0.8206 \times 2373.1 \\
\text{Computing:} \quad & h_{2s} \approx 226.0 + 1947.6 \approx 2173.6 \text{ kJ/kg} \\
\text{Step 6 — Actual turbine work per kg using η_T:} \quad & w_T = \eta_T \cdot (h_1 - h_{2s}) = 0.82 \times (2763.1 - 2173.6) = 0.82 \times 589.5 \\
\text{Computing specific work:} \quad & w_T \approx 483.4 \text{ kJ/kg} \\
\text{Step 7 — Total turbine mechanical power:} \quad & \dot{W}_T = \dot{m}_{steam} \cdot w_T = 18.82 \times 483.4 \approx 9097 \text{ kW} \\
\text{Step 8 — Electrical power output:} \quad & \boxed{P_{elec} = \eta_G \cdot \dot{W}_T = 0.95 \times 9097 \approx 8642 \text{ kW} \approx 8.64 \text{ MW}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** The most common error is forgetting that the **liquid entering the flash tank is saturated liquid at reservoir conditions**, not at separator conditions. Using $h_{f@P_{sep}}$ for the inlet enthalpy instead of $h_{f@T_{res}}$ inflates the quality by 30–40% and produces wrong answers. Also, the entropy at the turbine inlet is **$s_g$ at separator pressure**, not the inlet steam's actual entropy (since inlet is saturated vapour).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Summary — Memorise Before Exam**

- **Geothermal Gradient:** Global average $\approx 25 - 30^\circ C/\text{km}$; can exceed $100^\circ C/\text{km}$ near tectonic boundaries.
- **Heat Flow Equation:** $q = -k(dT/dz)$ — Fourier's Law is the foundation of all crustal heat transfer analysis.
- **Resource Grades by Temperature:** High ($>200^\circ C$) → Dry/Flash; Intermediate ($90 - 200^\circ C$) → Binary; Low ($<90^\circ C$) → Direct Use.
- **Plant Selection Rule:** Vapour-dominated → Dry Steam; Hot pressurized water → Flash Steam; Low-temp brine → Binary ORC.
- **Steam Quality Equation:** $y = (h_{res} - h_{f,sep}) / h_{fg,sep}$ — the **single most tested formula** for flash plant problems.
- **Carnot Reference:** Binary cycle actual efficiency is typically **$0.40 - 0.50 \times \eta_{carnot}$** due to ORC irreversibilities.
- **Capacity Factor:** Geothermal = **$85 - 95\%$** (highest among renewables) — remember this for economics questions.
- **Reinjection Mandate:** Modern KTU-aligned design requires **100% brine reinjection** to maintain reservoir pressure.
- **Binary Cycle Working Fluids:** Isobutane ($T_{bp} = -11.7^\circ C$), Isopentane ($T_{bp} = 27.8^\circ C$), R-134a — chosen based on resource temperature.
- **H₂S Abatement:** Required in dry and flash plants (Stretford process); not required in binary plants (closed loop).
- **Global Reference Sites:** Larderello (Italy, 1904, first plant), The Geysers (USA, largest complex), Wairakei (NZ, pioneering reinjection).
- **Common Mistake:** Using $h_{f@P_{sep}}$ as $h_{res}$ instead of $h_{f@T_{res}}$ in quality calculation — yields inflated quality.
- **Common Mistake 2:** Forgetting generator efficiency $\eta_G$ after computing turbine power — loses 1 mark consistently.
- **Environmental Edge:** Binary plants produce **zero H₂S emissions** and have **near-zero CO₂ footprint** per kWh generated.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Windmills
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT411 - ENERGY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Types of Windmills

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Windmill (Wind Turbine)** is a rotary mechanical device that converts the **kinetic energy** of moving air (wind) into useful mechanical work or, more commonly, into electrical energy. The classification of windmills is fundamentally based on the **orientation of the rotor's axis of rotation** relative to the direction of the prevailing wind.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Module 3):**
> Windmills are broadly classified into two primary categories:
> 1. **Horizontal Axis Wind Turbines (HAWTs)** – Axis of rotation is parallel to the ground and wind direction.
> 2. **Vertical Axis Wind Turbines (VAWTs)** – Axis of rotation is perpendicular to the ground and wind direction.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are standing in a strong wind holding two different objects:

- **Analogy 1 (HAWT – Like a ceiling fan turned sideways):** If you hold a ceiling fan facing the wind, it spins rapidly about a horizontal axis. This is exactly how a **Horizontal Axis Wind Turbine** works. Just as a ceiling fan moves air when powered, a wind turbine captures air when the air moves.
- **Analogy 2 (VAWT – Like an eggbeater):** Now imagine an eggbeater or a spinning top. Its axis of rotation is vertical. If the wind pushes against its curved blades, it rotates. This is the principle of a **Vertical Axis Wind Turbine**. You don't need to turn it to face the wind — it accepts wind from *any* direction.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Real-World Metric:** Modern commercial wind turbines typically have a rotor diameter of **80–120 meters** and a rated power output between **2 MW and 5 MW**, with the largest offshore units reaching **14 MW+** (e.g., Vestas V236-15.0 MW).

## 1.3 Primary Classification of Windmills

```
WINDMILLS
   ├── 1. Horizontal Axis Wind Turbine (HAWT)
   │      ├── Upwind (most common)
   │      ├── Downwind
   │      ├── Lift-type (airfoil blades)
   │      ├── Drag-type (flat/paddle blades)
   │      └── According to blade count: 1-blade, 2-blade, 3-blade, Multi-blade
   │
   └── 2. Vertical Axis Wind Turbine (VAWT)
          ├── Savonius Rotor (drag-based)
          ├── Darrieus Rotor (lift-based, "egg-beater" shape)
          ├── Giromill / H-Rotor (Darrieus variant, straight blades)
          └── Magnus-effect turbines (experimental)
```

## 1.4 Why This Classification Matters in KTU Examinations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Examiners frequently test the **differences, advantages, disadvantages, and applications** of HAWT vs. VAWT, and sub-classifications like Savonius vs. Darrieus. Memorize the *key distinguishing parameter* for each — direction of axis, type of force (lift/drag), and whether a yaw mechanism is required.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Horizontal Axis Wind Turbines (HAWTs)

In HAWTs, the rotor shaft and the electrical generator are mounted on top of a **tower**, with blades projecting radially. The axis of rotation is **horizontal and parallel to the wind stream**.

### 2.1.1 Sub-Types of HAWT

**A. Upwind vs. Downwind (Based on Nacelle Position)**

- **Upwind Turbine:** The rotor faces *into* the wind. Requires a **yaw control mechanism** (motor + gear) to keep the nacelle aligned with the wind.
- **Downwind Turbine:** The nacelle sits *downwind* of the rotor; the wind naturally aligns the rotor (no yaw mechanism), but the blades pass through the tower's wind shadow, causing cyclic loading.

**B. Lift-Type vs. Drag-Type**

- **Lift-type:** Blades shaped like an **airfoil** (similar to aircraft wings). The pressure difference creates a *lift force* perpendicular to the wind. **High tip-speed ratio (TSR)**; efficient.
- **Drag-type:** Blades are flat, paddle-like. Wind pushes the blade (drag). **Low TSR**; used historically (Persian windmills, American farm windmills for water pumping).

**C. Based on Number of Blades**

| Blade Count | Tip-Speed Ratio (TSR) | Application |
|-------------|----------------------|-------------|
| 1-blade | 6–8 | Rare; high TSR |
| 2-blade | 5–7 | Early designs; "egg-beater" look |
| **3-blade** | **6–8** | **Commercial standard — best balance of cost, aesthetics, vibration** |
| Multi-blade (>20) | 1–2 | Low-speed, high-torque water pumping |

## 2.2 Vertical Axis Wind Turbines (VAWTs)

In VAWTs, the rotor shaft is **vertical and perpendicular to the ground**. They accept wind from **any direction** without a yaw system.

### 2.2.1 Savonius Rotor (Drag-Type VAWT)

- Invented by **Sigurd J. Savonius** (Finland, 1922).
- Consists of two or more **half-cylindrical scoops** (or "S-shaped" curved blades) offset from the central shaft.
- Operates on the **principle of differential drag** — the convex side catches more wind than the concave side, producing torque.
- **Low TSR (≈ 0.5–1.0)**, high starting torque, self-starting, low efficiency (≈ 20–25%).

### 2.2.2 Darrieus Rotor (Lift-Type VAWT)

- Invented by **Georges Jean Marie Darrieus** (France, 1931).
- Blades are **curved (troposkein-shaped)**, resembling an *eggbeater* or a pair of "bowed" airfoils.
- Operates on the **principle of aerodynamic lift**, similar to a HAWT blade.
- **Higher TSR (≈ 3–6)** and efficiency (≈ 35–40%), but **NOT self-starting** (requires external push or a Savonius starter).

### 2.2.3 Giromill (H-Darrieus / H-Rotor)

- A **Darrieus variant** with **straight vertical blades** connected to the shaft by horizontal arms.
- Simpler to manufacture and maintain than curved Darrieus blades.
- Still requires an auxiliary start.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The formulas below are the *core equations* tested in KTU Module 3 problems on wind energy.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $P_{wind} = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot A \cdot v^{3}$ | Power available in wind | W (Watts) |
| 2 | $E_{k} = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot m \cdot v^{2}$ | Kinetic energy of air mass | J |
| 3 | $m = \rho \cdot A \cdot v \cdot \Delta t$ | Mass flow rate of air | kg |
| 4 | $A = \pi \cdot r^{2} = \pi \cdot \dfrac{D^{2}}{4}$ | Swept area of rotor | m² |
| 5 | $C_{p} = \dfrac{P_{turbine}}{P_{wind}}$ | Power coefficient (efficiency) | dimensionless |
| 6 | $C_{p,\max} = \dfrac{16}{27} \approx 0.5926$ | **Betz Limit** (theoretical max) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $P_{turbine} = C_{p} \cdot \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot A \cdot v^{3}$ | Actual power extracted | W |
| 8 | $\lambda = \dfrac{\omega \cdot R}{v} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot R \cdot N}{30 \cdot v}$ | Tip-Speed Ratio (TSR) | dimensionless |
| 9 | $F_{L} = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot v^{2} \cdot A \cdot C_{L}$ | Lift force on blade | N |
| 10 | $F_{D} = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot v^{2} \cdot A \cdot C_{D}$ | Drag force on blade | N |
| 11 | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = C_{p} \cdot \eta_{mech} \cdot \eta_{elec}$ | Overall efficiency | dimensionless |

> **Where:** $\rho$ = air density (≈ **1.225 kg/m³** at sea level, 15°C), $A$ = swept area, $v$ = wind velocity, $r$/$R$ = rotor radius, $D$ = rotor diameter, $\omega$ = angular velocity (rad/s), $N$ = rpm.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **HAWT (3-blade, upwind):** Dominates the **global commercial wind power market** (>97% of installed capacity). Used in onshore and offshore wind farms.
- **Savonius:** Used in **anemometers (early versions)**, **building-integrated wind power**, and **low-power rural water pumping** in developing countries.
- **Darrieus/Giromill:** Used in **rooftop urban installations** where wind direction is turbulent and variable, and a yaw mechanism is impractical.
- **Multi-blade HAWT (American farm):** **Pumping water from wells** in rural areas worldwide — still a workhorse in agriculture.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why TSR matters:** Engineers design turbines with an **optimal TSR ($\lambda_{opt}$)** that yields maximum $C_p$. This is what links Section 1's classifications to Section 2's formulas: **lift-type HAWTs and Darrieus** operate at high $\lambda$, while **drag-type and multi-blade** operate at low $\lambda$.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Power in the Wind

The derivation below is the *exact* standard step-by-step method expected by KTU examiners for "show that the power in the wind is $\frac{1}{2} \rho A v^3$" type questions.

### Starting Premise
Consider a stream of air of cross-sectional area $A$ (m²) moving with uniform velocity $v$ (m/s) perpendicular to $A$.

### Step 1 — Mass of air passing through area A in time Δt

The length of the air column that passes through $A$ in time $\Delta t$ is $v \cdot \Delta t$.

Therefore, the volume of air that crosses is:

$$V = A \cdot v \cdot \Delta t$$

Multiplying by air density $\rho$ (kg/m³) gives the mass:

$$m = \rho \cdot V = \rho \cdot A \cdot v \cdot \Delta t$$

### Step 2 — Kinetic energy of this air mass

The kinetic energy of a moving body of mass $m$ and velocity $v$ is:

$$E_k = \frac{1}{2} \cdot m \cdot v^2$$

Substituting $m$:

$$E_k = \frac{1}{2} \cdot (\rho \cdot A \cdot v \cdot \Delta t) \cdot v^2 = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot A \cdot v^3 \cdot \Delta t$$

### Step 3 — Power = Energy / Time

Power is the rate of energy flow:

$$P_{wind} = \frac{E_k}{\Delta t} = \frac{\frac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot A \cdot v^3 \cdot \Delta t}{\Delta t}$$

$$\boxed{P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot A \cdot v^3}$$

**Engineering Insight:** Power is proportional to the **cube of wind speed**. A *doubling* of wind speed (e.g., from 5 m/s to 10 m/s) results in an **8× increase** in available power. This cubic relationship is the *single most important design consideration* in wind farm site selection.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Betz Limit ($C_{p,\max} = 16/27$)

The Betz Limit is the **theoretical maximum** fraction of wind kinetic energy that an *ideal* turbine can extract. KTU frequently asks: *"Derive or state the Betz coefficient."*

### Step 1 — Define velocities

Let the upstream (inlet) wind velocity be $v_1$, the velocity at the rotor disc be $v$, and the downstream (far-wake) velocity be $v_2$. By continuity, $v = \dfrac{v_1 + v_2}{2}$.

### Step 2 — Apply Bernoulli / control volume

The force on the rotor disc equals the rate of change of momentum of the air:

$$F = \rho \cdot A \cdot v \cdot (v_1 - v_2)$$

### Step 3 — Power extracted by the turbine

The power extracted is the force times the velocity at the disc:

$$P = F \cdot v = \rho \cdot A \cdot v^{2} \cdot (v_1 - v_2)$$

Substituting $v = \dfrac{v_1 + v_2}{2}$:

$$P = \frac{1}{4} \cdot \rho \cdot A \cdot (v_1 + v_2)^{2} \cdot (v_1 - v_2)$$

### Step 4 — Express in terms of inlet power

The available power in the undisturbed wind is:

$$P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot A \cdot v_1^{3}$$

Therefore, the power coefficient is:

$$C_p = \frac{P}{P_{wind}} = \frac{\frac{1}{4} \cdot \rho \cdot A \cdot (v_1 + v_2)^{2} \cdot (v_1 - v_2)}{\frac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot A \cdot v_1^{3}}$$

$$C_p = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{(v_1 + v_2)^{2} \cdot (v_1 - v_2)}{v_1^{3}}$$

### Step 5 — Maximize using calculus

To find the maximum, differentiate $C_p$ with respect to the ratio $v_2/v_1$ and set equal to zero. The optimum occurs when:

$$v_2 = \frac{1}{3} \cdot v_1$$

Substituting back:

$$C_{p,\max} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{(v_1 + \frac{v_1}{3})^{2} \cdot (v_1 - \frac{v_1}{3})}{v_1^{3}} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{(\frac{4v_1}{3})^{2} \cdot (\frac{2v_1}{3})}{v_1^{3}}$$

$$C_{p,\max} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{\frac{16 v_1^{2}}{9} \cdot \frac{2 v_1}{3}}{v_1^{3}} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{32}{27} = \frac{16}{27}$$

$$\boxed{C_{p,\max} = \frac{16}{27} \approx 0.5926 \text{ (or } 59.3\% \text{)}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Betz's Law Conclusion:** No ideal wind turbine — regardless of design (HAWT, VAWT, Savonius, Darrieus) — can convert more than **59.3%** of the kinetic energy in wind into useful mechanical energy. The remaining 40.7% must remain in the wake to keep the air flowing away.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Tip-Speed Ratio (λ)

The tip-speed ratio is *defined* as the ratio of the speed of the blade tip to the free-stream wind speed.

### Step 1 — Linear speed of the blade tip

For a rotor of radius $R$ rotating at $N$ revolutions per minute, the angular velocity in rad/s is:

$$\omega = \frac{2 \pi N}{60} = \frac{\pi N}{30}$$

The linear speed of the tip is:

$$v_{tip} = \omega \cdot R = \frac{\pi \cdot R \cdot N}{30}$$

### Step 2 — Form the ratio with wind speed

$$\lambda = \frac{v_{tip}}{v} = \frac{\omega \cdot R}{v} = \frac{\pi \cdot R \cdot N}{30 \cdot v}$$

$$\boxed{\lambda = \frac{\pi R N}{30 v}}$$

**Engineering Insight:** Each turbine design has an **optimal $\lambda$** that maximizes $C_p$:

| Turbine Type | Optimal λ | $C_p$ (typical) |
|--------------|----------|------------------|
| American farm (multi-blade) | 1–2 | 0.15–0.25 |
| Savonius (drag VAWT) | 0.5–1.0 | 0.20–0.25 |
| 2-blade HAWT | 5–7 | 0.35–0.40 |
| **3-blade HAWT (modern)** | **6–8** | **0.40–0.48** |
| Darrieus (lift VAWT) | 3–6 | 0.30–0.40 |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common student error:** Confusing $C_p$ (Betz-limit, dimensionless, 0–0.593) with *overall efficiency* $\eta$ (which includes mechanical gearbox and generator losses, typically 0.30–0.45 for the *complete system*).

## 3.4 Sample Numerical Problem (KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** A 3-blade HAWT has a rotor diameter of 60 m, operates at 30 rpm in a 12 m/s wind. Air density = 1.225 kg/m³. Calculate: (a) the swept area, (b) the available power in the wind, (c) the tip-speed ratio, and (d) the power output if $C_p = 0.40$.

### Solution

**(a) Swept area:**

$$A = \pi \cdot \frac{D^2}{4} = \pi \cdot \frac{60^2}{4} = \pi \cdot 900 = 2827.4 \text{ m}^2$$

**(b) Available power in the wind:**

$$P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot A \cdot v^3 = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 1.225 \cdot 2827.4 \cdot 12^3$$

$$P_{wind} = 0.6125 \cdot 2827.4 \cdot 1728 = 2,990,470 \text{ W} \approx 2.99 \text{ MW}$$

**(c) Tip-speed ratio:**

$$\lambda = \frac{\pi R N}{30 v} = \frac{\pi \cdot 30 \cdot 30}{30 \cdot 12} = \frac{\pi \cdot 30}{12} = \frac{30\pi}{12} = 2.5 \pi \approx 7.854$$

**(d) Turbine power output:**

$$P_{turbine} = C_p \cdot P_{wind} = 0.40 \cdot 2.99 \text{ MW} = 1.196 \text{ MW} \approx 1.2 \text{ MW}$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Master Classification Flowchart of Windmills

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[WINDMILLS] --> B[HAWT Horizontal Axis]
    A --> C[VAWT Vertical Axis]

    B --> D[Upwind]
    B --> E[Downwind]
    B --> F[Lift-type airfoil]
    B --> G[Drag-type paddle]

    D --> D1[Requires yaw mechanism]
    E --> E1[Self-yawing tower shadow noise]

    C --> H[Savonius Drag]
    C --> I[Darrieus Lift]
    C --> J[Giromill H Rotor]

    H --> H1[High starting torque low TSR]
    I --> I1[High TSR not self starting]
    J --> J1[Straight blades simpler construction]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Modern HAWT

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    WIND[Wind Stream] --> BLADE[Rotor Blades 3 airfoils]
    BLADE --> HUB[Hub and Pitch Control]
    HUB --> SHAFT[Low Speed Shaft]
    SHAFT --> GEAR[Gearbox Step up ratio 1 to 90]
    GEAR --> HSHAFT[High Speed Shaft]
    HSHAFT --> GEN[Generator Doubly fed induction]
    GEN --> CONV[Power Converter AC to AC]
    CONV --> GRID[Electrical Grid Transformer]
    NAC[Nacelle yaw yaw motor and gear] --> HUB
    TWR[Tower 60 to 100 m steel or concrete] --> NAC
    CTRL[SCADA Controller pitch and yaw] --> NAC
    CTRL --> GEAR
```

## 4.3 Functional Comparison: Savonius vs Darrieus vs 3-Blade HAWT

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SAV[Savonius Rotor] --> S1[2 S shaped scoops]
    S1 --> S2[Drag based operation]
    S2 --> S3[TSR 0.5 to 1.0]
    S3 --> S4[Cp 0.20 to 0.25]
    S4 --> S5[Self starting high torque]

    DAR[Darrieus Rotor] --> D1[Curved troposkein blades]
    D1 --> D2[Lift based operation]
    D2 --> D3[TSR 3 to 6]
    D3 --> D4[Cp 0.30 to 0.40]
    D4 --> D5[Not self starting needs Savonius booster]

    HWT[3 Blade HAWT] --> H1[Airfoil shaped blades]
    H1 --> H2[Lift dominant drag minimal]
    H2 --> H3[TSR 6 to 8]
    H3 --> H4[Cp 0.40 to 0.48]
    H4 --> H5[Self starting yaw required]
```

## 4.4 Operational Decision Logic: Choosing the Right Windmill

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Wind speed available}
    Q1 -->|Low 3 to 6 m s| LOW
    Q1 -->|Medium 6 to 10 m s| MED
    Q1 -->|High 10 to 15 m s| HIGH

    LOW[Use multi blade HAWT or Savonius]
    MED[Use Darrieus Giromill VAWT]
    HIGH[Use 3 blade upwind HAWT modern grid scale]

    LOW --> APP1[Water pumping rural]
    MED --> APP2[Urban rooftop hybrid]
    HIGH --> APP3[Onshore and offshore wind farm]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 3]**

### Q1. Differentiate between HAWT and VAWT. Give one example of each.
*(Common 3-mark definition question)*

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | HAWT (Horizontal Axis) | VAWT (Vertical Axis) |
|---------|------------------------|----------------------|
| Axis of rotation | Parallel to wind direction | Perpendicular to wind direction |
| Yaw mechanism | Required (in upwind type) | Not required |
| Examples | 3-blade wind turbine (modern grid scale), American farm windmill | Savonius, Darrieus, Giromill |
| Self-starting | Yes (for lift-type 3-blade) | Savonius yes; Darrieus no |
| Site preference | Open plains, offshore | Urban, rooftop, turbulent wind zones |
| Tip-speed ratio | High (6–8) | Low (0.5–6) |

### Q2. Why is a Darrieus rotor not self-starting, and how is this overcome in practice?

**Model Answer:** A Darrieus rotor uses **lift-type** airfoil blades symmetrically mounted. At the start, when the rotor is stationary, the relative wind approaches the symmetric blade from both sides equally, producing *equal and opposite* lift components — **net torque is zero**. Hence it cannot self-start.

**Remedies used in practice:**
1. Mount a small **Savonius rotor** on the same shaft to act as a starter.
2. Use an **auxiliary electric motor** to spin the rotor up to operating speed.
3. Use **asymmetric (variable-pitch) blades** on the Darrieus itself.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Module 3 KTU Style
**[KTU University Exam — Previous Year Pattern]**

> **(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the working principle of a **Savonius vertical axis wind turbine**. Discuss its merits and demerits. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** A wind turbine has a rotor diameter of 25 m and operates at a wind speed of 10 m/s. The air density is 1.20 kg/m³. If the power coefficient $C_p$ is 0.35, calculate: (i) swept area, (ii) power in the wind, (iii) actual power output, (iv) tip-speed ratio if the rotor spins at 40 rpm. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a)

The **Savonius rotor** consists of two semi-cylindrical (or S-shaped) scoops mounted on a vertical shaft. The convex and concave surfaces experience different drag forces from the same wind. The convex side has *lower drag coefficient*, the concave side has *higher drag coefficient*. The resultant force creates a net **torque** about the vertical axis, turning the rotor.

**Merits:**
- Self-starting (high starting torque)
- Simple construction, low cost
- No yaw mechanism needed (omnidirectional)
- Operates efficiently at low wind speeds
- Low noise and maintenance

**Demerits:**
- Low $C_p$ (≈ 0.20–0.25)
- Low tip-speed ratio (≈ 0.5–1.0)
- Large swept area for given power
- Pulsating torque output

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b)

**Given:** $D = 25$ m, $v = 10$ m/s, $\rho = 1.20$ kg/m³, $C_p = 0.35$, $N = 40$ rpm.

**(i) Swept area:**

$$A = \pi \cdot \frac{D^2}{4} = \pi \cdot \frac{25^2}{4} = \pi \cdot 156.25 = 490.87 \text{ m}^2$$

**(ii) Power in the wind:**

$$P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot A \cdot v^3 = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 1.20 \cdot 490.87 \cdot 10^3$$

$$P_{wind} = 0.6 \cdot 490.87 \cdot 1000 = 294,524 \text{ W} \approx 294.5 \text{ kW}$$

**(iii) Actual power output:**

$$P_{turbine} = C_p \cdot P_{wind} = 0.35 \cdot 294.5 = 103.08 \text{ kW}$$

**(iv) Tip-speed ratio:**

$$\lambda = \frac{\pi R N}{30 v} = \frac{\pi \cdot 12.5 \cdot 40}{30 \cdot 10} = \frac{500 \pi}{300} = \frac{5\pi}{3} \approx 5.236$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative
**[KTU University Exam — Previous Year Pattern]**

> **(a)** Compare **Darrieus** and **Giromill** VAWTs. Explain why the modern 3-blade upwind HAWT is the most widely used design in commercial wind farms. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** Derive an expression for the **maximum theoretical power coefficient** (Betz limit) of a wind turbine. Comment on the practical significance. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a)

**Comparison: Darrieus vs Giromill**

| Feature | Darrieus | Giromill (H-Rotor) |
|---------|----------|--------------------|
| Blade shape | Curved (troposkein) | Straight vertical blades |
| Construction complexity | Higher (curve is hard to make) | Simpler |
| Structural stress | Concentrated at bottom | Distributed via horizontal struts |
| Self-starting | No | No |
| TSR | 3–6 | 3–6 |
| $C_p$ | 0.30–0.40 | 0.30–0.40 |

**Why 3-blade upwind HAWT dominates commercial wind farms:**

1. **Highest $C_p$ (0.40–0.48)** — close to Betz limit; best energy capture.
2. **Self-starting** — lift on the 3 airfoil blades produces immediate torque.
3. **Aesthetics and balance** — three blades give visual symmetry and reduce vibration compared to 1 or 2 blades.
4. **Mature technology** — established supply chain, predictable costs, and proven 20–25 year service life.
5. **Upwind design** — eliminates tower shadow on blades, reducing cyclic fatigue loading.
6. **High TSR (6–8)** — smaller generator for same power, reducing nacelle mass.
7. **Scalability** — successfully scaled from 50 kW to 15 MW.

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b) — Betz Limit Derivation

**Refer to Section 3.2 above for the full derivation.** The final result is:

$$\boxed{C_{p,\max} = \frac{16}{27} \approx 0.5926}$$

**Practical significance:**
- It sets a **theoretical ceiling** on the efficiency of *all* wind turbines, regardless of design (HAWT, VAWT, Savonius, Darrieus).
- Engineers use it as a **benchmark** to evaluate real turbine performance. A good modern turbine operates at $C_p$ = 0.40–0.48, i.e., 67–81% of the Betz limit.
- The remaining 40.7% of the wind energy is **unavoidably left in the wake** to maintain airflow through the rotor (a fluid-mechanical constraint, not an engineering limitation).
- It guides **blade design optimization** — designers aim for $C_p$ approaching 0.593 by varying pitch, TSR, and airfoil profile.

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **In Q.A(b),** students often forget to convert $D$ to $R$ for the TSR formula. $R = D/2 = 12.5$ m. Writing $R = 25$ m costs full marks in part (iv).
> 2. **In Betz derivation,** examiners expect the **5 logical steps** (continuity, momentum, power at disc, ratio, calculus maximization). Skipping calculus and just writing $C_p = 16/27$ yields only 2 of 7 marks.
> 3. **Confusing $C_p$ (power coefficient, max 0.593) with overall efficiency $\eta$** (system efficiency, max ~0.45). They are not the same; $C_p$ excludes gearbox and generator losses.
> 4. **In comparison questions,** examiners deduct marks for *unsubstantiated* claims. Always pair an advantage with a specific numerical value, design feature, or working principle.
> 5. **Forgetting units in numerical answers** — e.g., writing "294.5" instead of "294.5 kW" costs 0.5 mark per instance.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **🚀 KTU Module 3 — Rapid Revision Checklist: "Types of Windmills"**

- ✅ Windmills are classified by **axis orientation**: HAWT (horizontal) vs VAWT (vertical).
- ✅ **HAWT** is the **commercial standard** (97%+ of installed capacity) — 3-blade, upwind, lift-type.
- ✅ **VAWT sub-types:** Savonius (drag, self-starting, low $C_p$), Darrieus (lift, curved blades, not self-starting), Giromill (straight-bladed Darrieus variant).
- ✅ **Multi-blade HAWTs** (e.g., American farm windmills) are used for **water pumping** at low wind speeds.
- ✅ **Power in the wind:** $P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2} \rho A v^3$ — power ∝ **cube** of wind speed.
- ✅ **Betz Limit:** $C_{p,\max} = \dfrac{16}{27} \approx 0.5926$ — the absolute theoretical maximum for any wind turbine.
- ✅ **Tip-Speed Ratio (TSR):** $\lambda = \dfrac{\pi R N}{30 v}$ — high TSR for lift-type (HAWT, Darrieus); low TSR for drag-type (Savonius, multi-blade).
- ✅ **Yaw mechanism is required for HAWT** (upwind type) but **not for VAWT** (omnidirectional).
- ✅ **Lift force** drives efficient turbines (airfoil blades); **drag force** drives simple turbines (paddles, scoops).
- ✅ Air density standard value: $\rho = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$ (sea level, 15°C).
- ✅ Modern 3-blade HAWTs achieve $C_p$ ≈ 0.40–0.48, which is **~67–81% of the Betz limit**.
- ✅ **Darrieus** is non-self-starting because symmetric blades produce equal and opposite lift at rest — needs a Savonius booster or motor starter.
- ✅ **Savonius** has *high starting torque* and works at low wind speeds but is bulky and inefficient.
- ✅ VAWTs are preferred in **urban, rooftop, or turbulent** wind conditions; HAWTs in **open plain and offshore** sites.
- ✅ **For numerical problems**, always: (1) find $A$, (2) compute $P_{wind}$, (3) multiply by $C_p$ for $P_{turbine}$, (4) use $R = D/2$ in the TSR formula, and (5) **carry units to the end**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Wind Rotors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECHT411 - ENERGY ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Types of Wind Rotors # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Wind Rotor** is the prime-mover component of a Wind Energy Conversion System (WECS) that captures the **kinetic energy** of moving air and converts it into useful mechanical (rotational) work, which is subsequently transformed into electrical energy by a coupled generator. The rotor is fundamentally a set of aerodynamically shaped blades arranged about a central hub, governed by two principal physical principles: **aerodynamic drag** and **aerodynamic lift**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECHT411 - Module 3):** > The classification of wind rotors is foundational for Module 3, which is typically tested under the Course Outcome **CO3**: *"Apply the principles of wind, solar, biomass, and ocean energy for the design of renewable energy systems."* ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition) Imagine you are holding a paddle in a flowing river: - If you hold the paddle **flat against the flow**, the water pushes it sideways — this is the **DRAG principle** (the water resists being pushed away). A simple cup anemometer works on drag. - If you angle the paddle at ~45° to the flow, the water flows smoothly over it and the paddle gets **pulled forward** at a right angle to the flow — this is the **LIFT principle** (the same way an aeroplane wing is lifted). Modern high-speed turbines work on lift. A wind rotor is essentially a "rotating paddle system" that harnesses either the *push* (drag) or the *suction* (lift) of wind. > [!NOTE] > **Key Standard Metrics to Remember (Universally Used):** > - **Tip Speed Ratio (λ)** — the ratio of blade tip speed to free-stream wind speed. > - **Solidity (σ)** — the ratio of total blade chord to rotor diameter. > - **Power Coefficient (Cp)** — efficiency of kinetic energy capture. > - **Air Density (ρ)** — standard value **1.225 kg/m³** at 15 °C, 1 atm. ## 1.3 Primary Classification of Wind Rotors Wind rotors are classified along two orthogonal axes: **Axis of Rotation:** - **Horizontal Axis Wind Turbine (HAWT)** — shaft parallel to ground/wind. - **Vertical Axis Wind Turbine (VAWT)** — shaft perpendicular to ground. **Driving Aerodynamic Force:** - **Drag-type rotors** — operate on differential drag (low TSR). - **Lift-type rotors** — operate on aerodynamic lift (high TSR). --- # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Logic — How a Wind Rotor Works A wind rotor converts the linear momentum of moving air into angular momentum. The process follows a clean sequence: 1. **Wind Stream Captures the Rotor Disk** — the swept area $A = \pi R^2$ intercepts a cylinder of moving air. 2. **Kinetic Energy Extraction** — the rotor extracts a fraction of the wind's kinetic energy, causing the air to slow down (but never to zero, as proven by Betz). 3. **Momentum Transfer to Blades** — aerodynamic forces (lift $F_L$ and drag $F_D$) act on the blade airfoils. 4. **Torque Generation** — these forces resolved at a distance $R$ from the hub produce torque $T = F \times R$. 5. **Rotational Shaft Output** — the torque is delivered to the gearbox-generator assembly. ## 2.2 Detailed Classification of Wind Rotors (KTU High-Yield) ### A. Horizontal Axis Wind Turbines (HAWT) | Sub-Type | Blade Count | Typical λ | Application | Salient Feature | |---|---|---|---|---| | Single-blade | 1 | 6 – 8 | Experimental, large utility | Asymmetric load, requires counterweight | | Two-blade | 2 | 5 – 7 | Early utility, TEHRUM | Simpler hub, gyroscopic issues | | Three-blade | 3 | 6 – 8 | **Modern standard** | Best balance of smoothness, cost, and efficiency | | Multi-blade | 6 – 24 | 1 – 2 | Water pumping (low RPM) | High starting torque, high solidity | | Dutch four-arm | 4 | 2 – 3 | Traditional grain milling | Wooden lattice, drag-augmented lift | | American multi-blade | 18 – 24 | 1 – 1.5 | Farm water pumping | Very high solidity, low TSR, simple regulation | ### B. Vertical Axis Wind Turbines (VAWT) | Sub-Type | Driving Force | λ | Salient Feature | |---|---|---|---| | **Savonius** | Pure drag | 0.8 – 1.2 | S-shaped halves, self-starting, low speed | | **Darrieus (Egg-beater)** | Pure lift | 3 – 6 | Troposkein/curved blades, cannot self-start | | **Giromill (Cycloturbine)** | Lift + drag | 2 – 3 | Variable-pitch Darrieus variant | | **H-Rotor (Straight-bladed Darrieus)** | Lift | 3 – 5 | Easier manufacturing than curved Darrieus | ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Memorize this table — these formulas appear in nearly every KTU question on this topic.** > Note: All vertical pipe symbols are written as $\vert$ to prevent markdown table corruption. | # | Quantity | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Standard Unit | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Power in free wind | $P_w = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,A\,V^{3}$ | $\rho$ = air density, $A$ = swept area, $V$ = wind speed | W (watts) | | 2 | Swept area (HAWT) | $A = \pi R^{2}$ | $R$ = rotor radius | m² | | 3 | Swept area (VAWT) | $A = D \times H$ | $D$ = rotor diameter, $H$ = rotor height | m² | | 4 | Tip Speed Ratio (TSR) | $\lambda = \dfrac{\omega R}{V} = \dfrac{\pi R N}{30\,V}$ | $\omega$ = angular velocity, $N$ = rpm | dimensionless | | 5 | Solidity | $\sigma = \dfrac{N_b\,c}{\pi R}$ | $N_b$ = blade count, $c$ = blade chord | dimensionless | | 6 | Power Coefficient | $C_p = \dfrac{P_{\text{out}}}{P_w}$ | Efficiency of energy capture | dimensionless | | 7 | Torque Coefficient | $C_t = \dfrac{T}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho A V^{2} R}$ | $T$ = shaft torque | dimensionless | | 8 | Thrust Coefficient | $C_F = \dfrac{F}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho A V^{2}}$ | $F$ = axial thrust force | dimensionless | | 9 | Betz Limit (Max $C_p$) | $C_{p,\max} = \dfrac{16}{27} \approx 0.593$ | Theoretical upper bound | dimensionless | | 10 | Wind Energy per Year | $E = \int_{0}^{T} P_w \, dt$ | Time integral of power | J or kWh | | 11 | Blade Element $C_p$ | $C_p = \tfrac{8}{\lambda^{2}} \int \lambda_{r}^{3}\, C_L \sin\phi \, d\lambda_r$ | $\lambda_r$ = local TSR, $\phi$ = flow angle | dimensionless | | 12 | Air Density (Std) | $\rho = 1.225$ | At 15 °C, 1 atm, sea level | kg/m³ | ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Lift-type HAWTs** dominate the commercial wind-power market (Vestas, Siemens-Gamesa, GE) because they achieve the highest $C_p$ at high TSR, minimizing blade mass per kW. - **Savonius rotors** are deployed in **urban micro-turbines, road-side wind-sculpture generators, and hybrid PV-Wind streetlights** where omnidirectional wind capture matters more than peak efficiency. - **Multi-blade American farm rotors** remain the global default for **mechanical water-pumping** in rural off-grid applications because of their exceptionally high starting torque. - **Darrieus turbines** find niche use in **building-integrated wind** (BIWT) where vertical axis simplifies maintenance (no tower climb). --- *(Section 2 content above — Formula Sheet already included.)* # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation — Power Available in Free Wind (From First Principles) A parcel of air of mass $m$ moving at velocity $V$ possesses kinetic energy: $$E_k = \tfrac{1}{2}\,m\,V^{2}$$ **Step 1 — Mass flow rate through rotor disk.** Consider a circular cross-section of air of area $A$ moving with velocity $V$. In one second, the length of the air column passing the disk is $V$, so the mass passing per second (mass flow rate) is: $$\dot{m} = \rho \, A \, V$$ where $\rho$ is the air density. The logic is straightforward: in time $t$, the volume swept is $V \cdot t \cdot A$, and multiplying by density gives the mass; then dividing by $t$ gives the per-second rate. **Step 2 — Substitute mass flow rate into kinetic-energy flux.** The kinetic energy flowing through the disk per second — which is the *power* in the wind — is the mass flow rate multiplied by the specific kinetic energy $\tfrac{1}{2} V^{2}$: $$P_w = \dot{m} \cdot \tfrac{1}{2}\,V^{2} = \rho \, A \, V \cdot \tfrac{1}{2}\,V^{2}$$ **Step 3 — Final compact form (cubic law of wind power).** $$P_w = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,A\,V^{3}$$ **Why this matters:** This is the famous **cubic law** — a 2× increase in wind speed yields an **8×** increase in available power. This is why wind-farm siting (where the wind blows fastest) is so critical. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Examiner's Tip:** When asked to estimate available wind power, always state the standard air density **1.225 kg/m³** and explicitly substitute into the cubic formula. Marks are routinely lost for omitting units in the final answer. --- ## 3.2 Derivation — The Betz Limit (Maximum Theoretical $C_p$) This is the **most frequently asked derivation in KTU examinations** for Energy Engineering. We derive the maximum fraction of wind kinetic energy that an *ideal* rotor can extract. **Step 1 — Control Volume Setup (Actuator Disk Model).** Consider an ideal rotor represented as a stationary "actuator disk" of area $A$. Far upstream (1), the wind speed is $V_1$. At the disk (m), the speed is $V_m$. Far downstream (2), the wake has expanded and the speed has dropped to $V_2$. **Step 2 — Mass Conservation (Incompressible flow).** Mass flow rate through any cross-section must be equal: $$\rho\,A_1\,V_1 = \rho\,A_m\,V_m = \rho\,A_2\,V_2$$ For a one-dimensional model, the mass flow rate is taken as $\dot{m} = \rho A V_m$ (using disk area). This is a standard simplification in the actuator-disk theory. **Step 3 — Apply Euler's Turbomachine Equation (Bernoulli with momentum).** The thrust $F$ on the disk equals the rate of change of momentum of the air stream: $$F = \dot{m}\,(V_1 - V_2) = \rho A V_m (V_1 - V_2)$$ **Step 4 — Apply Bernoulli's Equation (incompressible, inviscid, no losses).** Upstream and downstream, no energy is added or removed by the fluid (only the disk extracts energy). Bernoulli's equation between points 1 and 2 gives: $$P_1 + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_1^{2} = P_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_2^{2}$$ The pressure jump across the disk is: $$\Delta P = P_1 - P_2 = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho (V_2^{2} - V_1^{2})$$ **Step 5 — Thrust in terms of pressure jump.** The thrust force on the disk equals the integrated pressure jump across the disk area: $$F = (P_1 - P_2)\,A = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho (V_2^{2} - V_1^{2})\,A$$ **Step 6 — Equate the two thrust expressions.** Setting Step 3 equal to Step 5: $$\rho A V_m (V_1 - V_2) = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho (V_2^{2} - V_1^{2})\,A$$ The right-hand side factors as $\tfrac{1}{2}\rho (V_2 - V_1)(V_2 + V_1)\,A = -\tfrac{1}{2}\rho (V_1 - V_2)(V_2 + V_1)\,A$. Dividing both sides by $\rho A (V_1 - V_2)$ (non-zero, since the rotor extracts energy): $$V_m = \tfrac{1}{2}(V_1 + V_2)$$ This shows the disk velocity is the **arithmetic mean** of upstream and downstream wind speeds — a beautiful and elegant result. **Step 7 — Power extracted by the rotor.** The power extracted is the thrust times the disk velocity (force × velocity at the point of application): $$P_{\text{ext}} = F \cdot V_m = \rho A V_m (V_1 - V_2) \cdot V_m$$ Substitute $V_m = \tfrac{1}{2}(V_1 + V_2)$: $$P_{\text{ext}} = \rho A \cdot \tfrac{1}{2}(V_1 + V_2) \cdot (V_1 - V_2) \cdot \tfrac{1}{2}(V_1 + V_2)$$ $$P_{\text{ext}} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho A \,(V_1 + V_2)^{2}\,(V_1 - V_2)\,/\!2$$ $$P_{\text{ext}} = \tfrac{1}{4}\rho A\,(V_1^{2} - V_2^{2})\,(V_1 + V_2)$$ **Step 8 — Express power coefficient.** The power coefficient is the ratio of extracted power to the upstream wind power $P_w = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho A V_1^{3}$: $$C_p = \dfrac{P_{\text{ext}}}{P_w} = \dfrac{\tfrac{1}{4}\rho A (V_1^{2} - V_2^{2})(V_1 + V_2)}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho A V_1^{3}}$$ Simplify by cancelling $\rho A$ and using the factor of 2 in the denominator: $$C_p = \tfrac{1}{2}\,(1 + x)\,(1 - x^{2})$$ where we introduced the **induction factor** $x \equiv V_2 / V_1$ (so that $0 \le x \le 1$). **Step 9 — Differentiate $C_p$ w.r.t. $x$ to find maximum.** Expanding: $$C_p(x) = \tfrac{1}{2}\,(1 - x^{2} + x - x^{3}) = \tfrac{1}{2}\,(1 + x - x^{2} - x^{3})$$ Take derivative with respect to $x$: $$\dfrac{d C_p}{d x} = \tfrac{1}{2}\,(1 - 2x - 3x^{2}) = 0$$ Solve the quadratic $3x^{2} + 2x - 1 = 0$: $$x = \dfrac{-2 \pm \sqrt{4 + 12}}{6} = \dfrac{-2 \pm 4}{6}$$ The positive root is $x = 1/3$. (Rejecting $x = -1$ as unphysical.) **Step 10 — Substitute $x = 1/3$ back into $C_p$.** Plugging $x = 1/3$ into the simplified formula: $$C_{p,\max} = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\left(1 + \tfrac{1}{3}\right)\left(1 - \tfrac{1}{9}\right) = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \tfrac{4}{3} \cdot \tfrac{8}{9} = \tfrac{32}{54} = \dfrac{16}{27}$$ **Final Result (Betz Limit):** $$\boxed{\,C_{p,\max} = \dfrac{16}{27} \approx 0.5926\,}$$ This means that **no rotor, however ideal, can extract more than ~59.3% of the kinetic energy of the wind.** The remaining 40.7% is left behind in the wake (in the form of residual kinetic energy). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Practical $C_p$ of Real Turbines:** > - Two-blade HAWT: ~0.35 – 0.42 > - Three-blade HAWT: ~0.45 – 0.50 > - Savonius: ~0.15 – 0.20 > - Darrieus: ~0.30 – 0.40 --- ## 3.3 Derivation — Torque Coefficient and the $C_p$ – $\lambda$ Relationship **Starting relation** — shaft power is the product of torque and angular velocity: $$P_{\text{out}} = T \cdot \omega$$ **Step 1 — Express $C_p$ in terms of $C_t$ and $\lambda$.** Power coefficient: $$C_p = \dfrac{P_{\text{out}}}{P_w} = \dfrac{T \cdot \omega}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho A V^{3}}$$ Now substitute $\omega = \lambda V / R$ (from the TSR definition): $$C_p = \dfrac{T \cdot \lambda V / R}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho A V^{3}} = \dfrac{T \cdot \lambda}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho A V^{2} R}$$ **Step 2 — Recognize the torque coefficient.** The torque coefficient is by definition $C_t = \dfrac{T}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho A V^{2} R}$. Substituting: $$\boxed{\,C_p = \lambda \cdot C_t\,}$$ **Engineering insight:** At the design TSR (where $C_p$ is maximum), the torque coefficient has a specific value. This is the foundation of the **$C_p$ – $\lambda$ curve** that every wind-turbine designer uses to match generator and rotor characteristics. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Rotor Type vs. Performance Metric | Rotor Type | Driving Force | Typical λ | Typical σ | Max $C_p$ (Practical) | Self-Starting | Wind-Direction Sensitivity | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | Single-blade HAWT | Lift | 6 – 8 | < 0.05 | ~0.35 | No (needs motor) | High (yaw required) | | Two-blade HAWT | Lift | 5 – 7 | 0.08 – 0.12 | ~0.40 | Marginal | High | | **Three-blade HAWT** | **Lift** | **6 – 8** | **0.10 – 0.15** | **~0.45 – 0.50** | **Yes** | **High** | | Multi-blade (American) | Lift + Drag | 1 – 1.5 | 0.5 – 0.8 | ~0.25 | **Yes (very high torque)** | Moderate | | Dutch four-arm | Lift + Drag | 2 – 3 | 0.3 – 0.4 | ~0.20 | Yes | Moderate | | **Savonius VAWT** | **Drag** | **0.8 – 1.2** | **> 1.0** | **~0.15 – 0.20** | **Yes (excellent)** | **Omnidirectional (None)** | | **Darrieus VAWT** | **Lift** | **3 – 6** | **0.2 – 0.4** | **~0.30 – 0.40** | **No** | **Omnidirectional (None)** | | H-Rotor VAWT | Lift | 3 – 5 | 0.2 – 0.3 | ~0.35 | No | Omnidirectional | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Trade-off Principle:** As λ increases, $C_p$ typically increases (better aerodynamic efficiency), but starting torque decreases. Conversely, high-solidity rotors have high starting torque but low peak $C_p$. The choice of rotor is a trade-off between **starting capability, peak efficiency, and rpm**. --- # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Classification Tree — All Wind Rotor Types (Mermaid Flowchart) ```mermaid flowchart TD rootWR["WIND ROTORS

Give one example of each.** **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Drag-Type Rotor | Lift-Type Rotor | |---|---|---| | Operating principle | Differential drag of the two faces of the blade | Aerodynamic lift perpendicular to wind direction | | Tip Speed Ratio (λ) | Low (≤ 1) | High (3 – 8) | | Power Coefficient ($C_p$) | Low (0.15 – 0.25) | High (0.35 – 0.50) | | Rotational speed | Low RPM | High RPM | | Starting capability | Self-starting | Usually needs external start | | Example | **Savonius rotor** | **Three-blade HAWT / Darrieus** | > [!NOTE] > **Remember:** The Savonius rotor's maximum $C_p$ is theoretically limited to $\sim 0.16$ because of the Betz limit, but practical $C_p$ is even lower. In contrast, modern three-blade HAWTs reach $C_p$ values close to 0.50. --- ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - June 2023]` **Define Tip Speed Ratio (TSR) and Solidity. State their typical values for an American multi-blade windmill.** **Model Answer:** **Tip Speed Ratio (TSR):** The dimensionless ratio of the velocity of the rotor blade tip to the free-stream wind velocity. $$\lambda = \dfrac{\omega R}{V} = \dfrac{\pi R N}{30 V}$$ **Solidity (σ):** The dimensionless ratio of the total blade chord (projected area) to the swept area of the rotor. $$\sigma = \dfrac{N_b \, c}{\pi R}$$ **For an American multi-blade windmill (water-pumping type):** | Parameter | Typical Value | |---|---| | Tip Speed Ratio (λ) | **1.0 – 1.5** | | Solidity (σ) | **0.5 – 0.8** (very high) | | Number of blades | **18 – 24** | | Application | Mechanical water pumping | The high solidity is what gives the American windmill its **high starting torque**, essential for driving a piston pump from standstill. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` **(a)** Derive the expression for the power available in a free wind stream, starting from the kinetic energy of moving air. (7 marks) **(b)** State and derive the Betz limit for the maximum power coefficient of a wind rotor. What does it signify physically? (7 marks) #### Model Solution to (a): **Step 1:** Write the kinetic energy of a mass $m$ moving at velocity $V$: $$E_k = \tfrac{1}{2} m V^{2}$$ **Step 2:** Mass flow rate through a rotor of swept area $A$: $$\dot{m} = \rho A V$$ This is because in time $t$, volume $= A V t$, and mass $= \rho A V t$, so rate $= \rho A V$. **Step 3:** Power in the wind equals kinetic energy flux: $$P_w = \dot{m} \cdot \tfrac{1}{2} V^{2} = \rho A V \cdot \tfrac{1}{2} V^{2} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho A V^{3}$$ **Step 4:** Example calculation — for a rotor of radius $R = 40$ m (typical utility-scale), $V = 12$ m/s, $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³: $$A = \pi R^{2} = \pi \times 40^{2} = 5026.5 \text{ m}^{2}$$ $$P_w = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 5026.5 \times 12^{3} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 5026.5 \times 1728$$ $$P_w \approx 5.32 \text{ MW}$$ This demonstrates the **cubic dependence on wind speed**. #### Model Solution to (b): **Statement of Betz Limit:** The maximum fraction of kinetic energy of wind that can be extracted by an ideal wind rotor is $\dfrac{16}{27} \approx 0.5926$ (about 59.3%). **Derivation** (full derivation as in Section 3.2 of these notes): 1. Actuator disk model with upstream velocity $V_1$, disk velocity $V_m$, downstream wake velocity $V_2$. 2. Mass conservation: $\dot{m} = \rho A V_m$. 3. Thrust from momentum: $F = \rho A V_m (V_1 - V_2)$. 4. Thrust from Bernoulli: $F = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho A (V_1^{2} - V_2^{2})$. 5. Equating and simplifying gives: $V_m = \tfrac{1}{2}(V_1 + V_2)$. 6. Power extracted: $P_{\text{ext}} = F V_m = \tfrac{1}{4}\rho A (V_1^{2} - V_2^{2})(V_1 + V_2)$. 7. With $x = V_2 / V_1$: $$C_p = \tfrac{1}{2}(1+x)(1-x^{2})$$ 8. Differentiate: $\dfrac{dC_p}{dx} = 0$ gives $x = 1/3$. 9. Substituting back: $C_{p,\max} = \tfrac{1}{2} \times \tfrac{4}{3} \times \tfrac{8}{9} = \dfrac{16}{27} \approx 0.593$. **Physical Significance:** - No real or ideal rotor can convert more than ~59.3% of the wind's kinetic energy into mechanical energy. - The remaining ~40.7% remains in the downstream wake as residual kinetic energy (the air cannot be brought to a complete halt). - It is a **fundamental thermodynamic limit** for an *open* system, analogous to (but not identical to) the Carnot limit. - Real turbines achieve $C_p$ between 0.35 and 0.50, leaving further room for technological improvement but bounded by Betz. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall #1:** Students often forget that in the derivation, the wake velocity $V_2$ cannot be zero (otherwise the air would be completely stopped, violating continuity). The correct condition is $V_2 = V_1 / 3$ at the Betz optimum, giving an induction factor $a = 1/3$. Writing $V_2 = 0$ loses at least 2 marks. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(a)** Classify wind rotors in detail. Compare the Savonius and Darrieus VAWTs on at least five parameters. (7 marks) **(b)** A three-blade horizontal axis wind turbine has a rotor diameter of 80 m. The wind speed is 14 m/s, the air density is 1.226 kg/m³, the rotational speed is 18 rpm, and the power coefficient is 0.45. Calculate: (i) the power available in the wind, (ii) the tip speed ratio, (iii) the actual power output. (7 marks) #### Model Solution to (a): **Classification of Wind Rotors:** **A. Horizontal Axis Wind Turbines (HAWT)** — axis parallel to ground/wind: - Single-blade HAWT - Two-blade HAWT - Three-blade HAWT (modern commercial standard) - Multi-blade HAWT (American farm type) - Dutch four-arm windmill **B. Vertical Axis Wind Turbines (VAWT)** — axis perpendicular to ground: - **Drag-type:** Savonius - **Lift-type:** Darrieus (curved/egg-beater), H-Rotor, Giromill **Comparison: Savonius vs. Darrieus VAWT** | Parameter | Savonius | Darrieus | |---|---|---| | Driving force | **Differential drag** (cups) | **Aerodynamic lift** (airfoil blades) | | Tip Speed Ratio (λ) | 0.8 – 1.2 (low) | 3 – 6 (high) | | Solidity (σ) | > 1.0 (very high) | 0.2 – 0.4 | | Power Coefficient ($C_p$) | 0.15 – 0.20 | 0.30 – 0.40 | | Self-starting capability | **Yes** (excellent) | **No** (requires external motor) | | Rotational speed | Very low RPM | Moderate to high RPM | | Wind direction sensitivity | **None** (omnidirectional) | **None** (omnidirectional) | | Torque characteristic | High starting torque | Low starting torque | | Maintenance | Simple | More complex (bearing on tall shaft) | | Typical application | Urban, road-side, hybrid | Building-integrated, niche utility | #### Model Solution to (b): **Given:** $D = 80$ m, $V = 14$ m/s, $\rho = 1.226$ kg/m³, $N = 18$ rpm, $C_p = 0.45$. **(i) Power available in the wind:** Rotor radius: $R = D / 2 = 40$ m. Swept area: $A = \pi R^{2} = \pi \times 40^{2} = 5026.55$ m². $$P_w = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho A V^{3} = 0.5 \times 1.226 \times 5026.55 \times 14^{3}$$ Compute $14^{3} = 2744$: $$P_w = 0.5 \times 1.226 \times 5026.55 \times 2744$$ $$P_w = 0.5 \times 1.226 \times 13{,}792{,}933 = 0.5 \times 16{,}910{,}057 = 8{,}455{,}028 \text{ W}$$ $$\boxed{P_w \approx 8.46 \text{ MW}}$$ **(ii) Tip Speed Ratio (TSR):** Convert RPM to angular velocity: $$\omega = \dfrac{2 \pi N}{60} = \dfrac{2 \pi \times 18}{60} = \dfrac{36 \pi}{60} = 0.6 \pi \approx 1.885 \text{ rad/s}$$ $$\lambda = \dfrac{\omega R}{V} = \dfrac{1.885 \times 40}{14} = \dfrac{75.40}{14} \approx 5.39$$ $$\boxed{\lambda \approx 5.39}$$ This is in the typical range (5 – 7) for a two/three-blade HAWT, confirming that the rotor is operating near its design point. **(iii) Actual power output:** $$P_{\text{out}} = C_p \times P_w = 0.45 \times 8{,}455{,}028 \text{ W}$$ $$\boxed{P_{\text{out}} \approx 3.80 \text{ MW}}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall #2:** A common mistake is to confuse **rotational speed $N$ (rpm)** with **angular velocity $\omega$ (rad/s)**. The correct conversion is $\omega = 2\pi N / 60$. Using $N$ directly in the TSR formula without conversion will give a wildly wrong answer (off by a factor of $\sim 9.55$). Always show the conversion step explicitly. > > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall #3:** Another frequent error is using $D$ (diameter) instead of $R$ (radius) in the TSR formula. The correct form is $\lambda = \omega R / V$, not $\omega D / V$. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Last-Minute Revision — Memorize This Bullet List Before Your KTU Exam:** - **Wind Rotor Definition:** A mechanical device that converts the kinetic energy of moving air into rotational mechanical work. The heart of any WECS. - **Two Broad Families:** HAWT (shaft horizontal) and VAWT (shaft vertical). Modern utility-scale turbines are exclusively HAWT with 3 blades. - **Two Aerodynamic Principles:** **Drag** (low λ, high σ, self-starting, low $C_p$ — e.g., Savonius) and **Lift** (high λ, low σ, high $C_p$ — e.g., three-blade HAWT, Darrieus). - **Cubic Law:** $P_w = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho A V^{3}$ — wind power scales as the **cube** of wind speed. Doubling wind speed → 8× power. - **Tip Speed Ratio (TSR):** $\lambda = \omega R / V$. High for lift rotors, low for drag rotors. - **Solidity (σ):** $\sigma = N_b c / (\pi R)$. High for multi-blade and drag rotors; low for modern high-λ rotors. - **Power Coefficient:** $C_p = P_{\text{out}} / P_w$. Theoretical max is the **Betz Limit**: $\dfrac{16}{27} \approx 0.593$. - **Betz Limit Physical Meaning:** No rotor (ideal or real) can extract more than ~59.3% of the wind's kinetic energy. The rest stays in the wake. At the optimum, the wake velocity is $V_2 = V_1 / 3$ (induction factor $a = 1/3$). - **Torque–Power Relation:** $C_p = \lambda \cdot C_t$. Always check this identity in derivations. - **American Multi-blade:** λ ≈ 1, σ ≈ 0.6, 18–24 blades, used for **water pumping** (high starting torque). - **Three-blade HAWT:** λ ≈ 6–8, σ ≈ 0.1, used for **electricity generation** (best efficiency/smoothness trade-off). - **Savonius:** Self-starting, omnidirectional, low $C_p$ (~0.15), used in **urban micro-turbines**. - **Darrieus:** Lift-driven, **NOT** self-starting, omnidirectional, $C_p$ ~0.30–0.40, used in **building-integrated wind**. - **Air Density:** Use $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³ at standard conditions (15 °C, 1 atm, sea level). - **Unit Conversions to Memorize:** $1 \text{ rpm} = \dfrac{2\pi}{60} \text{ rad/s} \approx 0.1047$ rad/s; $1 \text{ kW} = 1000$ W; $1 \text{ m/s} = 3.6$ km/h. - **Key Equation Chain to Remember:** $P_w \rightarrow C_p \rightarrow P_{\text{out}} \rightarrow E_{\text{annual}} = P_{\text{out}} \times 8760$ hours (capacity factor adjusted). - **Always show units** in KTU numerical answers — losing a mark for missing units is unfortunately common. - **Always state the induction factor $a = 1/3$** when citing the Betz limit; many students forget the relationship between $V_1$, $V_2$, and $V_m$ at the optimum. ---

topics.title: Wind Electric Power Generation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT411 - ENERGY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Wind Electric Power Generation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

**Wind Electric Power Generation** (also called **Wind Energy Conversion System (WECS)** or **Aerogeneration**) is the process of converting the **kinetic energy** of moving air masses (wind) into useful mechanical energy via aerodynamic rotor blades, which is then transformed into **electrical energy** through a coupled generator system. According to the KTU 2024 scheme, the topic encompasses the principles of energy extraction from wind, classification of wind turbines, mathematical modeling of available and extractable wind power, the **Betz Limit** constraint, component-level architecture, and grid-integration considerations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECHT411 / Module 3):**
> Students must master (i) the fundamental equations of wind power, (ii) derivation of the Betz coefficient (16/27 ≈ 0.593), (iii) classification of Horizontal Axis Wind Turbines (HAWT) and Vertical Axis Wind Turbines (VAWT), (iv) components of a WECS, (v) site-selection criteria, and (vi) the Indian/global wind-energy scenario.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain-English Intuition

Imagine standing in a fast-flowing river holding a paddle-wheel. The moving water pushes the blades and the wheel spins. If you attach the wheel's axle to a small LED bulb via a generator, the river's flow energy lights up the bulb. **Wind energy works on exactly this principle**, except the "river" is the atmosphere and the "water" is air. Now, here is the catch — a paddle-wheel that grabs all the water completely would actually *block* the river, and nothing would flow through. The same is true for wind: a turbine cannot extract **100%** of the wind's kinetic energy, because the air leaving the turbine must retain *some* momentum to physically move away from the rotor. This physical constraint is famously known as the **Betz Limit**, capped at approximately **59.3%** of the incident wind power. No turbine in the world, however advanced, can exceed this ceiling.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standard Physical Constants Used in Wind Energy Calculations:**
> - **Air density at standard sea-level conditions:** $\rho = 1.225\ \text{kg/m}^3$
> - **Betz maximum power coefficient:** $C_{P,\text{max}} = 16/27 \approx 0.5926$
> - **Swept area of a HAWT rotor:** $A = \pi R^2$ (where $R$ is rotor radius)
> - **Standard wind speed measurement height:** $10\ \text{m}$ (WMO norm); modern turbines measure at **hub height (typically 80–120 m)**

## 1.3 Why Wind Energy? — Engineering Motivation

- **Renewable & Inexhaustible:** Wind is a manifestation of solar heating differentials on Earth's surface; it does not deplete on human timescales.
- **Zero Direct Carbon Emissions:** Operational emissions are negligible (only lifecycle/embodied emissions exist).
- **Modular & Scalable:** From a 1 kW off-grid turbine for rural homes to 15 MW offshore units powering thousands of households.
- **Complementary to Solar:** Wind generation peaks often occur at night or during monsoon seasons in India, complementing solar diurnal cycles.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physics of Wind Power — Structured Logic Steps

The extraction of energy from wind can be broken down into four sequential logical stages, each derived from first principles of fluid mechanics and thermodynamics:

### Step 1 — Kinetic Energy of a Moving Air Parcel
A parcel of air of mass $m$ moving with velocity $v$ possesses kinetic energy given by Newton's classical mechanics:

$$KE = \frac{1}{2} m v^2$$

### Step 2 — Mass Flow Rate Through the Rotor Swept Area
Consider an imaginary disc of cross-sectional area $A$ (the rotor swept area). The mass of air flowing **per unit time** through this disc is:

$$\dot{m} = \rho \, A \, v$$

where $\rho$ is the **air density** (mass per unit volume) and $v$ is the **upstream wind velocity** perpendicular to the disc.

### Step 3 — Power Available in the Wind (Raw Wind Power)
Power is the time-rate of change of kinetic energy. The raw, un-extracted power contained in the wind stream is therefore:

$$P_{\text{wind}} = \dot{m} \times \frac{v^2}{2} = \frac{1}{2} \, \rho \, A \, v^{3}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **most critical equation in Module 3**: power in wind varies with the **cube of wind speed**. A doubling of wind speed results in an **eight-fold** increase in available power — this cubic dependency governs the entire economics of wind farm siting.

### Step 4 — Power Extracted by the Turbine (Betz Equation)
Real turbines cannot extract all available power. The fraction extracted is the **power coefficient** $C_P$ (also called the **performance coefficient** or **rotor efficiency**):

$$P_{\text{turbine}} = C_P \cdot \frac{1}{2} \, \rho \, A \, v^{3}$$

The theoretical maximum value of $C_P$ for an ideal, frictionless, disc-type turbine is derived to be **16/27 ≈ 0.5926**, known as the **Betz Limit** (or **Betz Coefficient**).

## 2.2 The Tip-Speed Ratio (TSR) — λ

The **Tip-Speed Ratio** is the ratio of the speed of the rotor blade tip to the free-stream wind velocity:

$$\lambda = \frac{\omega R}{v} = \frac{\pi R n}{30 v}$$

where:
- $\omega$ = angular velocity of the rotor (rad/s)
- $R$ = rotor radius (m)
- $n$ = rotational speed (rpm)
- $v$ = wind speed (m/s)

> [!NOTE]
> Each turbine design has an **optimal TSR** ($\lambda_{\text{opt}}$) at which $C_P$ is maximized. Modern three-bladed HAWTs typically operate at $\lambda_{\text{opt}} \approx 7$–$8$.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Quantity | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Units |
|---|----------|---------|----------------|-------|
| 1 | Raw wind power | $P_{\text{wind}} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho A v^3$ | $P_{\text{wind}}$ = wind power | W (watts) |
| 2 | Swept area (HAWT) | $A = \pi R^2$ | $R$ = rotor radius | m² |
| 3 | Mass flow rate | $\dot{m} = \rho A v$ | $\rho$ = air density | kg/s |
| 4 | Turbine power output | $P = C_P \cdot \tfrac{1}{2}\rho A v^3$ | $C_P$ = power coefficient | W |
| 5 | Betz Limit | $C_{P,\text{max}} = \tfrac{16}{27} \approx 0.5926$ | Maximum theoretical $C_P$ | dimensionless |
| 6 | Tip-Speed Ratio | $\lambda = \omega R / v$ | $\omega$ = angular velocity | dimensionless |
| 7 | Wind energy per year | $E = P_{\text{rated}} \times \text{Capacity Factor} \times 8760$ | $8760$ = hours in a year | kWh |
| 8 | Capacity Factor (CF) | $CF = \dfrac{\text{Actual annual energy}}{\text{Rated power} \times 8760}$ | Ratio of actual to max | dimensionless (0 to 1) |
| 9 | Specific power (capacity) | $P_{\text{rated}} / A$ | Power per unit swept area | W/m² |
| 10 | Power density of wind | $P/A = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho v^3$ | Power per unit area | W/m² |
| 11 | Wind speed at height $h$ | $v(h) = v_{\text{ref}} \left(\dfrac{h}{h_{\text{ref}}}\right)^{\alpha}$ | Power-law profile (Hellmann) | m/s |
| 12 | Cut-in / Rated / Cut-out speeds | $v_{\text{in}}, v_{\text{rated}}, v_{\text{out}}$ | Operating speed bounds | m/s |

> [!WARNING]
> In all KTU exam answers, **always state the standard air density value $\rho = 1.225\ \text{kg/m}^3$** explicitly when the question does not specify it. Examiners award credit for this convention.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Turbine Sizing:** Engineers use $P = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho A v^3$ to size rotor diameters for target power outputs. A 2 MW turbine at 12 m/s wind needs $A \approx 1736\ \text{m}^2$, corresponding to a rotor diameter of ~47 m.
- **Wind Resource Mapping:** The cubic relationship means even small geographic variations in average wind speed cause enormous differences in annual energy yield — making precise site assessment financially critical.
- **Control System Design:** $C_P$-$\lambda$ curves guide the Maximum Power Point Tracking (MPPT) algorithms in modern variable-pitch, variable-speed turbines.
- **Grid Planning:** Capacity factors (typically 25–45% onshore, 45–60% offshore) determine how many MW of nameplate wind capacity are needed to replace 1 MW of baseload thermal capacity.
