# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation — Power Available in the Wind

**Starting Premise:** Consider a stream tube of air approaching a stationary disc of area $A$ with uniform velocity $v_1$ (upstream), and leaving the disc at reduced velocity $v_2$ (downstream). At the disc itself, the velocity is $v$ (intermediate). Assume incompressible, steady, frictionless flow (per **Albert Betz, 1919**).

**Step 1 — Write the kinetic energy flux entering the stream tube per unit time:**

$$P_1 = \frac{1}{2} \dot{m} v_1^2 = \frac{1}{2}(\rho A v) v_1^2$$

**Step 2 — Write the kinetic energy flux leaving the stream tube per unit time:**

$$P_2 = \frac{1}{2} \dot{m} v_2^2 = \frac{1}{2}(\rho A v) v_2^2$$

**Step 3 — Compute the change in kinetic energy (extracted power):**

The mechanical power extracted by the turbine is the difference between incoming and outgoing kinetic energy fluxes:

$$P = P_1 - P_2 = \frac{1}{2} \rho A v (v_1^2 - v_2^2)$$

**Step 4 — Apply momentum conservation (thrust equation):**

By Newton's second law, the thrust force on the disc equals the rate of change of momentum:

$$F = \dot{m}(v_1 - v_2) = \rho A v (v_1 - v_2)$$

This thrust force also equals the pressure drop across the disc multiplied by the area:

$$F = (p_1 - p_2) A$$

**Step 5 — Equate the two expressions and apply Bernoulli's equation:**

From Bernoulli between upstream and downstream (at the disc plane):

$$p_1 + \frac{1}{2}\rho v_1^2 = p_2 + \frac{1}{2}\rho v_2^2$$

Subtracting, we obtain:

$$p_1 - p_2 = \frac{1}{2}\rho (v_2^2 - v_1^2)$$

Substituting into the thrust expression:

$$\rho A v (v_1 - v_2) = \frac{1}{2}\rho (v_2^2 - v_1^2) A$$

Dividing both sides by $\rho A$:

$$v (v_1 - v_2) = \frac{1}{2}(v_2^2 - v_1^2)$$

Rearranging the right-hand side:

$$v (v_1 - v_2) = -\frac{1}{2}(v_1 - v_2)(v_1 + v_2)$$

Since $v_1 \neq v_2$ (non-trivial case), we can divide by $(v_1 - v_2)$:

$$v = \frac{1}{2}(v_1 + v_2)$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **beautiful result**: the velocity at the rotor disc is exactly the **arithmetic mean** of the upstream and downstream velocities — a fundamental axiom in actuator-disc theory.

**Step 6 — Express extracted power in terms of $v_1$ and $v_2$:**

Substitute $v = \frac{1}{2}(v_1 + v_2)$ into the power equation from Step 3:

$$P = \frac{1}{2} \rho A \left[\frac{1}{2}(v_1 + v_2)\right] (v_1^2 - v_2^2)$$

$$P = \frac{1}{4} \rho A (v_1 + v_2)(v_1 + v_2)(v_1 - v_2)$$

$$P = \frac{1}{4} \rho A (v_1 + v_2)^2 (v_1 - v_2)$$

**Step 7 — Define the induction factor 'a' (Froude's notation):**

Let $v_2 = v_1(1 - 2a)$ and $v = v_1(1 - a)$, where $a$ is the **axial induction factor** ($0 < a < 0.5$). Substituting:

$$P = \frac{1}{4} \rho A \left[2 v_1 (1 - a)\right]^2 \left[2 a v_1\right]$$

$$P = \frac{1}{4} \rho A \cdot 4 v_1^2 (1-a)^2 \cdot 2 a v_1$$

$$P = 2 \rho A v_1^3 a (1-a)^2$$

**Step 8 — Maximize P with respect to 'a':**

Take the derivative and set equal to zero:

$$\frac{dP}{da} = 2\rho A v_1^3 \left[(1-a)^2 + a \cdot 2(1-a)(-1)\right] = 0$$

$$(1-a)^2 - 2a(1-a) = 0$$

$$(1-a)[(1-a) - 2a] = 0$$

$$(1-a)(1 - 3a) = 0$$

Either $a = 1$ (trivial, no flow) or $a = 1/3$.

**Step 9 — Substitute the optimal induction factor:**

With $a = 1/3$:

$$P_{\text{max}} = 2 \rho A v_1^3 \cdot \tfrac{1}{3} \cdot \left(\tfrac{2}{3}\right)^2 = 2 \rho A v_1^3 \cdot \tfrac{1}{3} \cdot \tfrac{4}{9}$$

$$P_{\text{max}} = \frac{8}{27} \rho A v_1^3$$

**Step 10 — Compute the Betz coefficient:**

The ratio of maximum extractable power to raw wind power is:

$$C_{P,\text{max}} = \frac{P_{\text{max}}}{P_{\text{wind}}} = \frac{\frac{8}{27}\rho A v_1^3}{\frac{1}{2}\rho A v_1^3} = \frac{8/27}{1/2} = \frac{16}{27}$$

$$\boxed{C_{P,\text{max}} = \frac{16}{27} \approx 0.5926}$$

This is the **Betz Limit** — derived end-to-end from first principles.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Key Variables

| Variable Doubled | Effect on Wind Power $P_{\text{wind}}$ | Effect on $P_{\text{turbine}}$ (constant $C_P$) |
|------------------|----------------------------------------|---------------------------------------------------|
| Wind speed $v$ | Increases by **8×** | Increases by **8×** |
| Rotor radius $R$ | Increases by **4×** (since $A = \pi R^2$) | Increases by **4×** |
| Air density $\rho$ | Increases linearly (**2×**) | Increases linearly (**2×**) |
| Hub height | Indirectly increases $v$ via Hellmann profile | Non-linearly increases via $v^3$ |
| Power coefficient $C_P$ | No effect on $P_{\text{wind}}$ | Increases linearly (**2×**) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examination Tip:** When asked "what is the effect of doubling rotor diameter on output power?", the correct answer is **"Power increases by 4 times (quadruples)"**, because area scales with $R^2$, not $R$. Students commonly err by saying it doubles.

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (For Practice)

**Problem:** A HAWT has a rotor diameter of $D = 80\ \text{m}$. The wind speed is $12\ \text{m/s}$ and air density is $1.225\ \text{kg/m}^3$. Assuming $C_P = 0.4$, calculate: (a) Swept area, (b) Raw wind power, (c) Turbine output power.

**Solution:**

**(a)** Swept area:
$$A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (80)^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \cdot 6400}{4} = 5026.55\ \text{m}^2$$

**(b)** Raw wind power:
$$P_{\text{wind}} = \frac{1}{2} \rho A v^3 = \frac{1}{2}(1.225)(5026.55)(12)^3$$

$$P_{\text{wind}} = 0.6125 \cdot 5026.55 \cdot 1728 \approx 5.32 \times 10^6\ \text{W} \approx 5.32\ \text{MW}$$

**(c)** Turbine output:
$$P_{\text{turbine}} = C_P \cdot P_{\text{wind}} = 0.4 \times 5.32\ \text{MW} = 2.128\ \text{MW}$$

**Comparison with Betz Limit:**
$$P_{\text{Betz,max}} = 0.5926 \times 5.32\ \text{MW} \approx 3.15\ \text{MW}$$

This confirms that a real turbine ($C_P = 0.4$) extracts ~67% of the theoretical Betz maximum.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Wind Energy Conversion System (WECS)

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid block represents the **functional architecture** of a grid-connected horizontal-axis wind turbine system. It maps energy flow and control signal pathways from wind input to grid output.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["WIND KINETIC ENERGY SOURCE"]:::source
    B["ROTOR BLADES WITH PITCH CONTROL"]:::rotor
    C["MAIN SHAFT AND GEARBOX STEP UP"]:::gear
    D["DOUBLE FED INDUCTION GENERATOR DFIG"]:::gen
    E["AC DC POWER CONVERTER BACK TO BACK"]:::conv
    F["TRANSFORMER STEP UP TO GRID VOLTAGE"]:::trans
    G["ELECTRICAL GRID OR LOCAL LOAD"]:::grid
    H["WIND SENSOR ANEMOMETER"]:::sensor
    I["CONTROL UNIT PITCH AND YAW"]:::control

    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D
    D --> E
    E --> F
    F --> G
    H --> I
    I --> B
    I --> D
    I --> E

    classDef source fill:#e1f5fe,stroke:#01579b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef rotor fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef gear fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef gen fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef conv fill:#d1c4e9,stroke:#311b92,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef trans fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef grid fill:#b3e5fc,stroke:#0277bd,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef sensor fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#880e4f,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef control fill:#d7ccc8,stroke:#3e2723,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

### Component Descriptions (Mapped to the Architecture):

1. **Wind Source:** Atmospheric kinetic energy at hub height.
2. **Rotor Blades:** Aerofoil-shaped blades extract momentum via lift/drag. Modern turbines have **pitch-control** to feather blades at high wind.
3. **Gearbox:** Steps up low rotor RPM (~15–20 rpm) to generator RPM (~1500 rpm for 50 Hz grid).
4. **Generator:** Most commonly a **Double-Fed Induction Generator (DFIG)** for variable-speed operation; full-conversion topologies use Permanent Magnet Synchronous Generators (PMSG).
5. **Power Converter:** AC-DC-AC conversion using IGBT-based Voltage Source Converters (VSCs).
6. **Transformer:** Steps up low-voltage generator output (typically 690 V) to medium/high voltage (11 kV, 33 kV, 110 kV) for grid.
7. **Control Unit:** Uses SCADA systems; regulates pitch angle, yaw orientation, and converter switching for MPPT and grid-code compliance.

## 4.2 Classification of Wind Turbines (Hierarchical View)

```mermaid

    HAWT --> H2["DOWNWIND RARE"]

    H1 --> H3["THREE BLADED MODERN STANDARD"]
    H1 --> H4["TWO BLADED EARLIER DESIGNS"]
    H1 --> H5["MULTI BLADED WATER PUMPING"]

    VAWT --> V1["DARRIEUS EGG BEATER SHAPE"]
    VAWT --> V2["SAVONIUS DRAG BASED"]
    VAWT --> V3["GIROMILL VARIANTS"]

    classDef root fill:#ffebee,stroke:#b71c1c,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
```

> **Comparison Snapshot for KTU Viva / Short Answers:**
>
> | Feature | HAWT | VAWT |
> |---------|------|------|
> | Axis orientation | Horizontal (parallel to ground) | Vertical (perpendicular) |
> | Yaw mechanism | Required (faces wind) | Not required (omnidirectional) |
> | Wind direction dependence | High | Low |
> | Self-starting | Needs motor or aerodynamic asymmetry | Savonius yes; Darrieus no |
> | Ground-level gearbox | No (on top of tower) | Yes (at base) |
> | Power scale | MW-scale (commercial) | Typically kW-scale (small) |
> | Efficiency at high $C_P$ | Yes (up to 0.5) | Lower (0.2–0.35) |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define the following terms related to wind energy conversion systems:**
(a) Betz Limit, (b) Tip-Speed Ratio, (c) Power Coefficient.

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Betz Limit:** The **Betz Limit** is the theoretical maximum fraction of kinetic energy in wind that an ideal, frictionless turbine can extract. Its numerical value is:

$$C_{P,\text{max}} = \frac{16}{27} \approx 0.5926 \quad \text{(or 59.26%)}$$

It was derived by German physicist **Albert Betz** in 1919 using actuator-disc theory and momentum conservation. No real turbine can exceed this limit; the best modern HAWTs achieve $C_P \approx 0.45$–$0.50$.

**(b) Tip-Speed Ratio (TSR):** It is the dimensionless ratio of the tangential speed of the rotor blade tip to the upstream wind speed:

$$\lambda = \frac{\omega R}{v} = \frac{\pi R n}{30\, v}$$

where $R$ is rotor radius (m), $\omega$ is angular velocity (rad/s), and $n$ is rpm.

**(c) Power Coefficient ($C_P$):** It is the ratio of actual power extracted by a turbine to the total kinetic power available in the wind:

$$C_P = \frac{P_{\text{turbine}}}{P_{\text{wind}}} = \frac{P_{\text{turbine}}}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho A v^3}$$

Its value lies between 0 and 16/27 (Betz Limit).

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Distinguish between Horizontal Axis Wind Turbines (HAWT) and Vertical Axis Wind Turbines (VAWT) with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | HAWT | VAWT |
|-----------|------|------|
| **Examples** | Three-bladed modern utility turbines (e.g., Vestas V150) | Darrieus, Savonius, Giromill |
| **Yaw control** | Required — rotor must face wind | Not required — omnidirectional |
| **Tower mounting** | Gearbox and generator at top | Gearbox at ground level |
| **Self-starting** | Difficult (needs aerodynamic asymmetry or motor) | Savonius: yes; Darrieus: no |
| **Capacity** | MW-scale (commercial grid-connected) | kW-scale (typically off-grid/domestic) |
| **Efficiency ($C_P$)** | Higher (0.40–0.50) | Lower (0.20–0.35) |
| **Maintenance access** | Difficult (height) | Easy (ground level) |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Derive an expression for the power available in the wind passing through a wind turbine of swept area $A$. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A horizontal-axis wind turbine has a rotor diameter of $60\ \text{m}$. The wind speed at hub height is $10\ \text{m/s}$ and the air density is $1.225\ \text{kg/m}^3$. Assuming a power coefficient $C_P = 0.45$, calculate:
   (i) The swept area
   (ii) The raw wind power
   (iii) The actual turbine output power
   (iv) Compare with the Betz limit. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Step 1:** Mass of air passing through rotor area $A$ per second:
$$\dot{m} = \rho A v \quad \text{[kg/s]}$$

**Step 2:** Kinetic energy of this air mass per second = Power in wind:
$$P_{\text{wind}} = \dot{m} \cdot \frac{v^2}{2} = \rho A v \cdot \frac{v^2}{2}$$

$$\boxed{P_{\text{wind}} = \frac{1}{2} \rho A v^3}$$

This is the fundamental expression for **available wind power** before any turbine losses.

**Model Solution for (b):**

**(i)** Swept area:
$$A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (60)^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \cdot 3600}{4} = 2827.43\ \text{m}^2$$

**(ii)** Raw wind power:
$$P_{\text{wind}} = \frac{1}{2}(1.225)(2827.43)(10)^3 = 0.6125 \cdot 2827.43 \cdot 1000$$

$$P_{\text{wind}} = 1.732 \times 10^6\ \text{W} \approx 1.732\ \text{MW}$$

**(iii)** Actual turbine output:
$$P_{\text{turbine}} = C_P \cdot P_{\text{wind}} = 0.45 \times 1.732\ \text{MW} \approx 0.779\ \text{MW}$$

**(iv)** Betz-limited maximum:
$$P_{\text{Betz,max}} = \frac{16}{27} \times 1.732\ \text{MW} \approx 0.593 \times 1.732\ \text{MW} \approx 1.027\ \text{MW}$$

The actual turbine produces **0.779 MW**, which is:
$$\frac{0.779}{1.027} \times 100\% \approx 75.85\% \text{ of the Betz limit}$$

This is consistent with high-performance three-bladed HAWTs operating in the field.

---

### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the main components of a Horizontal Axis Wind Turbine (HAWT) system. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Explain the factors affecting the selection of a site for a wind farm. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution for (a):**

The main components of a **Horizontal Axis Wind Turbine** (refer to the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.1) are:

1. **Rotor Blades:** Aerofoil-shaped blades (typically three, made of glass/carbon fibre-reinforced polymer) extract kinetic energy via lift and drag forces. Modern blades have **pitch-control mechanisms** to adjust the angle of attack.

2. **Hub:** The central mounting that holds the blades together and attaches them to the main shaft.

3. **Nacelle:** A weather-proof housing mounted atop the tower containing the main shaft, gearbox, generator, yaw motor, and control electronics.

4. **Main Shaft & Gearbox:** The main shaft (low-speed) transmits torque to the gearbox, which steps up rotor speed (~15–20 rpm) to generator speed (~1500 rpm for 50 Hz grid).

5. **Generator:** Most commonly a **Double-Fed Induction Generator (DFIG)** with rotor-side and grid-side converters for variable-speed operation and reactive power control.

6. **Yaw System:** A motor-driven gear assembly that rotates the nacelle to keep the rotor facing into the wind (active yaw control).

7. **Tower:** A tubular steel or lattice tower (typically 80–120 m for utility-scale turbines) that elevates the rotor to capture stronger, less-turbulent winds.

8. **Control System & SCADA:** Sensors (anemometer, wind vane, vibration sensors) and PLCs that govern pitch angle, yaw, braking, and grid synchronization.

9. **Transformer & Grid Interface:** Steps up generator voltage (typically 690 V) to transmission voltage (11/33/110 kV) and connects to the substation.

**Model Solution for (b):**

The major factors affecting **wind farm site selection** are:

1. **Wind Resource Assessment:**
   - Mean annual wind speed at hub height (preferably ≥ 7–8 m/s for commercial viability).
   - Wind direction consistency (low directional variability preferred).
   - Diurnal and seasonal wind patterns.
   - Turbulence intensity (must be < 0.15 for utility turbines).

2. **Topography & Terrain:**
   - Smooth, open ridges, hilltops, and coastal plains maximize wind speeds (speed-up effect).
   - Avoid obstacles (buildings, trees) within a 10× rotor-diameter radius upwind.

3. **Grid Connectivity & Distance:**
   - Proximity to existing transmission lines reduces cabling costs.
   - Grid capacity to absorb generated power (no curtailment).

4. **Soil & Foundation Conditions:**
   - Stable, load-bearing soil for tower foundations (especially for offshore sites where seabed geology is critical).

5. **Environmental & Social Factors:**
   - Avoidance of bird migratory paths and protected ecosystems.
   - Noise impact (regulatory limits ~40–45 dB at nearest residence).
   - Visual impact and shadow flicker on nearby homes.
   - Land-use compatibility (agricultural coexistence possible for HAWT spacing).

6. **Logistical & Economic Factors:**
   - Road access for transporting large blades and tower sections.
   - Crane access for erection.
   - Local policy incentives, tariffs (e.g., India's Feed-in Tariff / SECI auctions), and tax benefits.

7. **Climatic Hazards:**
   - Frequency of extreme winds, cyclones, icing, and lightning — influences turbine class selection (IEC 61400 Class I/II/III).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in Wind Energy Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting $\rho$:** Many students write $P = \tfrac{1}{2}Av^3$, omitting air density. **Always include $\rho = 1.225\ \text{kg/m}^3$** unless otherwise stated.
> 2. **Swept area confusion:** Use $A = \pi R^2$ or $A = \tfrac{\pi D^2}{4}$. **Do not** confuse with the projected area of blades or with tower-base area.
> 3. **Linear vs Cubic Power Dependency:** If asked "what if wind speed doubles?", answer is **power increases 8 times**, not 2 times. This is the most-asked conceptual trap.
> 4. **Betz Limit value:** Must write both forms: $16/27$ **and** $0.5926$ (or 59.26%). Examiners deduct half a mark for stating only one.
> 5. **Units:** $P$ must end in **W or MW**. $A$ in **m²**. $v$ in **m/s**. Mixing kW with m/s without proper conversions leads to full-mark cancellation in numerical problems.
> 6. **Cubic integration for annual energy:** When computing annual energy, do **NOT** use the cube of average wind speed. Use the Weibull distribution or, if explicitly allowed, the simple cubic formula with caveat.
> 7. **Confusing $C_P$ with $\eta$:** $C_P$ is the **rotor** efficiency, while overall efficiency includes gearbox, generator, and converter losses ($\eta_{\text{overall}} = C_P \cdot \eta_{\text{gear}} \cdot \eta_{\text{gen}} \cdot \eta_{\text{conv}}$).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Wind Power Formula:** $P_{\text{wind}} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho A v^3$ — **cubic dependence on wind speed** is the most important property.
- ✅ **Betz Limit:** $C_{P,\text{max}} = 16/27 \approx 0.5926$ — derived by Albert Betz (1919); absolute physical ceiling.
- ✅ **Tip-Speed Ratio:** $\lambda = \omega R / v$ — optimal at $\lambda_{\text{opt}}$ for each turbine design (typically 7–8 for modern HAWTs).
- ✅ **Mass Flow Rate:** $\dot{m} = \rho A v$ — used in all power and momentum equations.
- ✅ **Operating Speeds:** $v_{\text{cut-in}}$ (3–4 m/s), $v_{\text{rated}}$ (12–15 m/s), $v_{\text{cut-out}}$ (25 m/s).
- ✅ **HAWT vs VAWT:** HAWTs dominate commercial generation; VAWTs are simpler but less efficient.
- ✅ **Components:** Rotor, Nacelle, Gearbox, Generator (DFIG/PMSG), Yaw system, Tower, Converter, Transformer, SCADA.
- ✅ **Site Selection Criteria:** Wind speed, terrain, grid access, environmental impact, logistics, soil, climate hazards.
- ✅ **Capacity Factor (CF):** Typically 25–45% onshore, 45–60% offshore in India (Tamil Nadu, Gujarat, Karnataka, Rajasthan).
- ✅ **Air Density Convention:** $\rho = 1.225\ \text{kg/m}^3$ at sea level, 15°C.
- ✅ **Hellmann Power Law:** $v(h) = v_{\text{ref}} (h/h_{\text{ref}})^\alpha$ where $\alpha \approx 1/7$ for open terrain.
- ✅ **India's Wind Status (2024):** Installed capacity ~47 GW; target 140 GW by 2030 (onshore + offshore — Gulf of Khambhat, Gulf of Kutch, Mannar).
- ✅ **Famous Betz Derivation Steps:** (1) Write KE flux, (2) Apply momentum, (3) Bernoulli, (4) $v = \tfrac{1}{2}(v_1+v_2)$, (5) Define induction factor $a$, (6) Differentiate, (7) Get $a = 1/3$, (8) Substitute, (9) Get 16/27.
- ✅ **Important Global Turbines:** Vestas (Denmark), Siemens Gamesa (Germany-Spain), GE Renewable (USA), Suzlon (India), Goldwind (China).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wind Power in India
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT411 - ENERGY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Wind Power in India

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Wind Power in India** refers to the harnessing of kinetic energy from atmospheric air movement through large-scale wind turbine generators (WTGs) installed across the Indian subcontinent, governed by the institutional framework of the **Ministry of New and Renewable Energy (MNRE)** and technically validated by the **National Institute of Wind Energy (NIWE)**, Chennai. India is recognized as the **4th largest wind power producer globally** (after China, USA, and Germany), with an estimated gross wind power potential of **~302 GW at 100 m hub height** and **~302.25 GW at 120 m hub height**, as assessed by NIWE using reanalysis data.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PECHT411 – Module 3):** Students must understand the *current installed capacity, potential zones, institutional structure, and policy framework* governing wind power development in India, along with the mathematical formulation of wind power density and capacity utilization factor.

## 1.2 Key National Statistics (Standout Numbers for KTU)

| Parameter | Value (Approx.) |
|---|---|
| Installed Wind Capacity | **~47.5 GW (as of 2024)** |
| Gross Technical Potential (100 m) | **302 GW** |
| States with High Potential | Tamil Nadu, Gujarat, Karnataka, Rajasthan, Maharashtra |
| Average Wind Power Density (good sites) | **~250–500 W/m²** |
| Capacity Utilization Factor (CUF) India | **20% – 35%** |
| Nodal Technical Agency | **NIWE, Chennai** |
| Nodal Ministry | **MNRE, New Delhi** |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Banking of Air" Intuition

Imagine the atmosphere as a giant, invisible **current account** that constantly receives deposits of moving air. India sits on a **"wind current conveyor belt"** — the summer monsoon (June–September) deposits strong southwesterly winds, while the winter monsoon (October–February) brings brisk northeasterly trade winds across the Thar Desert, Western Ghats, and coastal Tamil Nadu. Wind turbines are simply the **"cash withdrawal machines"** of this atmospheric account — they convert this kinetic energy deposit into electrical currency that can be wired into the national grid.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Summary:** Wind power in India is the country's systematic attempt to "bank" the kinetic energy of monsoon winds and trade winds through large turbines installed primarily in coastal and high-altitude plateau regions.

## 1.4 Institutional Architecture (At a Glance)

- **MNRE (Ministry of New and Renewable Energy):** Apex policy-making body under the Government of India.
- **NIWE (National Institute of Wind Energy):** Formerly known as **C-WET (Centre for Wind Energy Technology)**; located in Chennai; responsible for wind resource assessment, testing, and certification.
- **SECI (Solar Energy Corporation of India):** Handles the bidding and tendering process for wind power projects under the **Inter-State Transmission System (ISTS)** route.
- **State Nodal Agencies (SNAs):** State-level bodies (e.g., TEDA in Tamil Nadu, GEDA in Gujarat) for land allotment and project facilitation.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework — The Five Pillars of Indian Wind Power

### Pillar 1: Wind Resource Assessment Methodology (NIWE Protocol)
- **Step 1:** Long-term meteorological data collection via masts at candidate sites (typically 80–120 m height).
- **Step 2:** Application of **WAsP (Wind Atlas Analysis and Application Program)** or **CFD-based meso/microscale modeling**.
- **Step 3:** Generation of the **Indian Wind Atlas** at 80 m, 100 m, and 120 m hub heights.
- **Step 4:** Identification of "wind zones" classified by mean wind power density.

### Pillar 2: Classification of Indian Wind Zones (Old MNRE Classification, Still Referenced in Exams)
- **Zone 1 (Poor):** PWD < 200 W/m² at 50 m — includes parts of Kerala, Odisha interior, NE India.
- **Zone 2 (Moderate):** PWD 200–300 W/m².
- **Zone 3 (Good):** PWD 300–400 W/m².
- **Zone 4 (Excellent):** PWD 400–500 W/m².
- **Zone 5 (Outstanding):** PWD > 500 W/m² — Muppandal (TN), Jaisalmer (RJ), Dwarka (GJ).

### Pillar 3: Policy & Regulatory Mechanisms
- **Generation Based Incentive (GBI):** ₹0.50/unit for wind power fed into the grid (historical, now discontinued for new projects; replaced by VGF).
- **Viability Gap Funding (VGF):** Up to ₹1 crore/MW for offshore wind demonstration projects.
- **Renewable Purchase Obligation (RPO):** Mandatory % of total power consumption to be from renewables.
- **ISTS Waiver:** Inter-state transmission charges fully waived for solar and wind power plants commissioned before June 2025.

### Pillar 4: Offshore Wind Potential
- **Gujarat coast (Gulf of Khambhat, Gulf of Kutch):** Estimated potential ~70 GW.
- **Tamil Nadu coast (Rameswaram coast):** Estimated potential ~35 GW.
- **First offshore tender:** Awarded for a 4 GW project off Gujarat (under SECI).

### Pillar 5: Repowering Policy (2016 & 2022 Revisions)
- Old turbines (<2 MW, installed >15 years ago) are being **repowered** with modern, taller, higher-capacity machines.
- Target: Repower **~10,000 MW** of old wind capacity in India.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All symbols and units are KTU-board-standard. Use `\vert` for absolute value to keep Markdown tables safe.

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units / Boundary |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Kinetic Energy of Air Mass** | $E_k = \frac{1}{2} m v^{2}$ | J (joules) |
| 2 | **Mass Flow Rate of Air** | $\dot{m} = \rho A v$ | kg/s |
| 3 | **Wind Power (Available)** | $P_{avail} = \frac{1}{2} \rho A v^{3}$ | W (watts) |
| 4 | **Power Coefficient (Betz Limit)** | $C_p = \frac{P_{turbine}}{P_{avail}} \le 0.593$ (16/27) | Dimensionless |
| 5 | **Power Extracted by Turbine** | $P_{turbine} = C_p \cdot \frac{1}{2} \rho A v^{3}$ | W |
| 6 | **Wind Power Density (WPD)** | $P/A = \frac{1}{2} \rho v^{3}$ | W/m² |
| 7 | **Specific Yield (Annual Energy)** | $E_{annual} = P_{rated} \times 8760 \times CUF$ | kWh/year |
| 8 | **Capacity Utilization Factor** | $CUF = \frac{\text{Actual Energy Generated (kWh)}}{P_{rated} \times 8760}$ | Fraction or % |
| 9 | **Air Density (ISA Sea Level)** | $\rho = 1.225$ **kg/m³** | kg/m³ |
| 10 | **Rotor Swept Area** | $A = \pi R^{2} = \frac{\pi D^{2}}{4}$ | m² |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** The Betz Limit (59.3%) is a thermodynamic ceiling — no wind turbine, regardless of design, can extract more than 16/27 of the kinetic energy of the wind. This concept is a *guaranteed KTU Part A question* and is also tested in derivations.

## 2.3 Why These Formulas Matter in the Indian Context

- The **v³ dependency** explains why a 1% increase in wind speed produces a **~3% increase in power** — this is why even small improvements in **hub height** (from 80 m to 120 m) dramatically boost Indian wind farm output.
- The **CUF in India (20–35%)** is low compared to European countries (30–45%) because Indian wind sites are **seasonal** (monsoon-driven) — energy is concentrated in 4–5 months of the year.
- The **Betz Limit** justifies the need for **pitch control and yaw systems** in modern Indian turbines to maximize power extraction without overspeeding.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Wind Power Density from First Principles

> **Objective:** Derive the wind power density equation $P/A = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho v^{3}$ and then apply it to an Indian wind site.

### Step 1: Start with Kinetic Energy
The kinetic energy of a mass $m$ of air moving at velocity $v$ is given by Newton's classical mechanics.

$$
E_k = \frac{1}{2} m v^{2}
$$

*Logic:* Kinetic energy is the work needed to accelerate a body from rest to velocity $v$. We use this because wind is essentially "moving air mass."

### Step 2: Express Mass in Terms of Density and Volume
Mass of air can be written as $m = \rho \cdot V$, where $V$ is the volume swept by the rotor per second. For a rotor of radius $R$, the volume per second is the swept area $A$ times the velocity $v$.

$$
m = \rho \cdot (A \cdot v) \quad \Rightarrow \quad \dot{m} = \rho A v
$$

*Logic:* In 1 second, the turbine "sweeps" a cylindrical volume of base area $A$ and length $v$ (1 m of air per second). The mass of this air parcel is density × volume.

### Step 3: Substitute into the Energy Equation
The energy content **per second** of the wind stream is therefore the power.

$$
P = \frac{E_k}{t} = \frac{1}{2} \dot{m} v^{2}
$$

### Step 4: Substitute Mass Flow Rate
Replace $\dot{m}$ with $\rho A v$:

$$
P = \frac{1}{2} (\rho A v) v^{2}
$$

### Step 5: Simplify
Group the terms and simplify algebraically.

$$
P = \frac{1}{2} \rho A v^{3}
$$

### Step 6: Obtain Wind Power Density (Divide by Area $A$)
To get the power per unit area (which is independent of rotor size — useful for site assessment):

$$
\frac{P}{A} = \frac{1}{2} \rho v^{3}
$$

> **Physical Insight:** Wind power density depends only on air density and the **cube** of wind speed. This is why wind resource maps in India (e.g., NIWE 100 m atlas) are drawn in W/m² rather than in m/s.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style Application)

> **Problem:** A wind turbine installed at Muppandal, Tamil Nadu, has a rotor diameter of **D = 126 m** and operates in a wind regime of **v = 8 m/s**. The air density is **ρ = 1.21 kg/m³** (coastal Tamil Nadu humidity correction). Calculate the **available wind power**, assuming a power coefficient of **C_p = 0.45** (typical for a modern Indian turbine).

### Solution:

**Step 1: Compute Swept Area**

$$
A = \frac{\pi D^{2}}{4} = \frac{\pi (126)^{2}}{4}
$$

$$
A = \frac{\pi \times 15876}{4} = \frac{49876.0}{4} \approx 12469.0 \text{ m}^{2}
$$

**Step 2: Compute Available Power (without C_p)**

$$
P_{avail} = \frac{1}{2} \rho A v^{3}
$$

$$
P_{avail} = \frac{1}{2} \times 1.21 \times 12469.0 \times (8)^{3}
$$

$$
P_{avail} = 0.5 \times 1.21 \times 12469.0 \times 512
$$

$$
P_{avail} = 0.605 \times 12469.0 \times 512
$$

$$
P_{avail} = 0.605 \times 6384128.0
$$

$$
P_{avail} \approx 3862397.4 \text{ W} \approx 3.86 \text{ MW}
$$

**Step 3: Apply Power Coefficient**

$$
P_{turbine} = C_p \times P_{avail} = 0.45 \times 3.86 \text{ MW}
$$

$$
P_{turbine} \approx 1.74 \text{ MW}
$$

**Step 4: Calculate Annual Energy Generation (assuming CUF = 28%)**

$$
E_{annual} = P_{rated} \times 8760 \times CUF
$$

$$
E_{annual} = 1740 \text{ kW} \times 8760 \times 0.28
$$

$$
E_{annual} = 1740 \times 2452.8 \approx 4,267,872 \text{ kWh/year}
$$

$$
E_{annual} \approx 4.27 \text{ GWh/year}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Note:** Always show the unit conversions explicitly. Indian textbooks often quote GWh; KTU board expects **kWh or MU (Million Units)**. Remember: **1 GWh = 1,000,000 kWh = 1 MU**.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Top Indian Wind States

| State | Installed Capacity (MW, approx.) | Key Sites | Avg WPD (W/m²) | Dominant Season |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Tamil Nadu | ~10,000 | Muppandal, Kayathar | 350–500 | SW Monsoon (Jun–Sep) |
| Gujarat | ~9,500 | Dwarka, Kutch, Jamnagar | 400–550 | Both monsoons |
| Karnataka | ~6,000 | Chitradurga, Davangere | 300–450 | SW Monsoon |
| Rajasthan | ~5,000 | Jaisalmer, Bikaner | 350–500 | NE Trade winds (Oct–Mar) |
| Maharashtra | ~5,000 | Satara, Dhule | 250–400 | SW Monsoon |
| Others (AP, MP, Kerala, etc.) | ~10,000+ | Various | <300 | Mixed |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A1 (3 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – June 2023]
**"Discuss the role of NIWE in the development of wind power in India."**

#### Model Answer (3 Points):
1. **National Institute of Wind Energy (NIWE)**, located in Chennai, is the technical arm of MNRE for wind energy. (1 mark)
2. It is responsible for **wind resource assessment**, preparation of the **Indian Wind Atlas** at 80 m, 100 m, and 120 m hub heights, and **type certification of wind turbines**. (1 mark)
3. It also conducts **R&D in aerodynamics, blade design, and grid integration**, and provides technical training to state nodal agencies. (1 mark)

---

### **Question A2 (3 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]
**"List any six states in India having high wind power potential. What is the estimated gross wind power potential of India at 100 m hub height?"**

#### Model Answer (3 Points):
1. **Top 6 States:** Tamil Nadu, Gujarat, Karnataka, Rajasthan, Maharashtra, Andhra Pradesh. (1.5 marks)
2. **Estimated gross wind power potential at 100 m:** **~302 GW (302,000 MW)**, as per NIWE assessment. (1.5 marks)

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **KTU Pattern:** Two sub-parts (a) and (b), each carrying **7 marks**.

---

### **PART B — Option A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

#### **Question A(a) [7 Marks]:**
**"Explain the institutional framework for wind power development in India. Mention the role of MNRE, NIWE, and SECI."**

#### Model Answer:
**1. MNRE (Ministry of New and Renewable Energy):** (2 marks)
- Apex ministry under the Government of India, formed in 1992 (originally as DNES).
- Formulates **national policies** like the Repowering Policy, Offshore Wind Policy, and VGF schemes.
- Sets **targets** (e.g., 500 GW non-fossil capacity by 2030, of which ~140 GW is wind).
- Channels **funding and subsidies** to states and implementing agencies.

**2. NIWE (National Institute of Wind Energy):** (2.5 marks)
- Earlier known as **C-WET (Centre for Wind Energy Technology)**, renamed in 2014.
- Located in **Chennai, Tamil Nadu**.
- Functions:
  - **Wind resource assessment** and preparation of the **Indian Wind Atlas**.
  - **Type testing and certification** of wind turbines.
  - **R&D in blade aerodynamics, foundation design, and grid integration.**
  - Acts as the **technical advisor** to MNRE.

**3. SECI (Solar Energy Corporation of India):** (2.5 marks)
- Constituted in 2011 under MNRE; headquartered in **New Delhi**.
- Acts as the **central implementing agency** for **bid-based wind power procurement**.
- Manages **ISTS-connected wind tenders** (e.g., 4 GW offshore tender off Gujarat).
- Signs **PPAs (Power Purchase Agreements)** with developers and back-to-back agreements with **DISCOMs/SEBs**.

---

#### **Question A(b) [7 Marks]:**
**"A wind turbine with rotor diameter 80 m operates at a wind speed of 9 m/s. The air density is 1.225 kg/m³ and the overall power coefficient is 0.40. Calculate: (i) the available wind power, (ii) the actual power output of the turbine, and (iii) the annual energy generation in MWh assuming a capacity utilization factor of 30%."**

#### Model Solution:

**Given Data:**
- Rotor diameter $D = 80$ m
- Wind speed $v = 9$ m/s
- Air density $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³
- Power coefficient $C_p = 0.40$
- CUF = 0.30

**Step 1: Compute Swept Area (i preparation)**

$$
A = \frac{\pi D^{2}}{4} = \frac{\pi (80)^{2}}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 6400}{4} = 1600\pi
$$

$$
A = 5026.55 \text{ m}^{2}
$$

**Step 2: (i) Available Wind Power**

$$
P_{avail} = \frac{1}{2} \rho A v^{3}
$$

$$
P_{avail} = \frac{1}{2} \times 1.225 \times 5026.55 \times (9)^{3}
$$

$$
P_{avail} = 0.6125 \times 5026.55 \times 729
$$

$$
P_{avail} = 0.6125 \times 3,664,354.95
$$

$$
P_{avail} \approx 2,244,417.4 \text{ W} \approx 2244.4 \text{ kW} \approx 2.24 \text{ MW}
$$

**Step 3: (ii) Actual Power Output**

$$
P_{turbine} = C_p \times P_{avail} = 0.40 \times 2244.4 \text{ kW}
$$

$$
P_{turbine} = 897.76 \text{ kW} \approx 0.898 \text{ MW}
$$

**Step 4: (iii) Annual Energy Generation**

$$
E_{annual} = P_{rated} \times 8760 \times CUF
$$

$$
E_{annual} = 897.76 \text{ kW} \times 8760 \times 0.30
$$

$$
E_{annual} = 897.76 \times 2628
$$

$$
E_{annual} \approx 2,359,313.3 \text{ kWh/year}
$$

$$
E_{annual} \approx 2359.3 \text{ MWh/year}
$$

**Final Answers:**
- Available Power ≈ **2.24 MW**
- Actual Power Output ≈ **0.898 MW**
- Annual Energy ≈ **2359.3 MWh/year** (or ~2.36 GWh)

---

### **PART B — Option B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

#### **Question B(a) [7 Marks]:**
**"Describe the salient features of India's National Offshore Wind Energy Policy. What is the estimated offshore wind potential, and which two states are leading the development?"**

#### Model Answer:
**1. Need for an Offshore Policy:** (1 mark)
- Onshore wind is approaching saturation in the best sites of TN, GJ, and KA.
- India has a **7,600 km coastline** with significant offshore potential.

**2. Salient Features of the National Offshore Wind Energy Policy (2015):** (3 marks)
- **MNRE is the nodal ministry** for policy and coordination.
- **NIWE** is the implementing agency for resource assessment and seabed studies.
- **Ministry of New and Renewable Energy** grants consent; **Ministry of Shipping, Ministry of Defence, Ministry of Environment** are consulted for clearances.
- **100% Foreign Direct Investment (FDI)** is allowed under the automatic route.
- **VGF of up to ₹1 crore/MW** for the first 4 GW demonstration projects.
- **Power is evacuated via ISTS**, and transmission charges are fully waived for the initial 4 GW.

**3. Estimated Potential and Leading States:** (3 marks)
- **Estimated offshore wind potential:** **~70 GW** off the coast of Gujarat (Gulf of Khambhat and Gulf of Kutch) and **~35 GW** off the coast of Tamil Nadu (Rameswaram). **Total: ~175 GW** at depths < 60 m.
- **Leading States:** **Gujarat** and **Tamil Nadu**.
- First offshore tender (4 GW) was awarded off Gujarat's Gulf of Khambhat region.

---

#### **Question B(b) [7 Marks]:**
**"What is the Betz Limit? Derive the maximum theoretical power coefficient for a wind turbine and discuss why real turbines operate at 70–80% of this limit."**

#### Model Solution:

**Definition (1 mark):**
The Betz Limit (or Betz Coefficient) is the maximum theoretically extractable fraction of kinetic energy from a free-flowing wind stream, equal to **16/27 ≈ 0.5926 (59.3%)**.

**Derivation (5 marks):**

**Step 1:** Let wind approach the turbine at velocity $v_1$, pass through the rotor with velocity $v_2$, and leave at velocity $v_3$ (with $v_1 > v_2 > v_3$).

**Step 2:** Mass flow rate (continuity):

$$
\dot{m} = \rho A_1 v_1 = \rho A_2 v_2 = \rho A_3 v_3
$$

**Step 3:** Force on the rotor (rate of momentum change):

$$
F = \dot{m} (v_1 - v_3) = \rho A_2 v_2 (v_1 - v_3)
$$

**Step 4:** Power extracted by the turbine:

$$
P = F \cdot v_2 = \rho A_2 v_2^{2} (v_1 - v_3)
$$

**Step 5:** Change in kinetic energy of the wind (final kinetic energy minus initial):

$$
\Delta E_k = \frac{1}{2} \dot{m} (v_1^{2} - v_3^{2}) = \frac{1}{2} \rho A_2 v_2 (v_1^{2} - v_3^{2})
$$

**Step 6:** By energy conservation, $P = \Delta E_k$:

$$
\rho A_2 v_2^{2} (v_1 - v_3) = \frac{1}{2} \rho A_2 v_2 (v_1^{2} - v_3^{2})
$$

**Step 7:** Cancel common terms and factor RHS using $(v_1 - v_3)(v_1 + v_3)$:

$$
v_2 = \frac{v_1 + v_3}{2}
$$

**Step 8:** Define the **induction factor** $a = \tfrac{v_1 - v_2}{v_1}$, which gives $v_2 = v_1 (1 - a)$ and $v_3 = v_1 (1 - 2a)$.

**Step 9:** Substitute into the power equation:

$$
P = \frac{1}{2} \rho A_2 v_1^{3} \cdot 4 a (1 - a)^{2}
$$

**Step 10:** The power coefficient is:

$$
C_p = \frac{P}{\frac{1}{2} \rho A_2 v_1^{3}} = 4 a (1 - a)^{2}
$$

**Step 11:** Maximize $C_p$ with respect to $a$:

$$
\frac{d C_p}{d a} = 4 \left[ (1 - a)^{2} - 2a(1 - a) \right] = 0
$$

$$
(1 - a) (1 - 3a) = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad a = \frac{1}{3}
$$

**Step 12:** Substitute $a = 1/3$ back:

$$
C_{p,\max} = 4 \times \frac{1}{3} \times \left(\frac{2}{3}\right)^{2} = \frac{4}{3} \times \frac{4}{9} = \frac{16}{27} \approx 0.593
$$

**Why Real Turbines Achieve Only 70–80% of Betz Limit:** (1 mark)
- **Aerodynamic losses** (viscous drag, tip losses, swirl in wake).
- **Mechanical losses** in gearbox and generator.
- **Curtailment** due to grid constraints and wind variability.
- **Yaw and pitch control** not at optimum at all times.
- Typical Indian wind turbines operate at $C_p \approx 0.40\text{–}0.45$, which is ~70–80% of 0.59.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting units:** Always end numerical answers with proper units (W, kW, MW, kWh, MWh). KTU strictly deducts ½ mark for missing units.
> 2. **Confusing Available Power with Turbine Power:** Available power is what the wind contains; turbine power is $C_p \times$ available power. Mark difference: full 2 marks lost.
> 3. **Forgetting CUF** in the annual energy calculation: $E = P \times 8760 \times CUF$, **not** $P \times 8760$.
> 4. **Betz Limit derivation:** Marks are awarded for the step $v_2 = (v_1 + v_3)/2$. Skipping this step costs 2 marks.
> 5. **Offshore vs Onshore:** Many students mix up the states. Gujarat leads **offshore**; Tamil Nadu leads **onshore** (installed capacity). Don't confuse them.
> 6. **NIWE vs C-WET:** NIWE was formerly C-WET. Both names are accepted in answers, but spelling C-WET as "CWET" without hyphen is incorrect in descriptive answers.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Scheme:**

- **India's Wind Rank Globally:** 4th largest producer.
- **Installed Capacity (2024):** ~47.5 GW; **Target 2030:** ~140 GW (onshore + offshore).
- **Gross Potential:** **302 GW at 100 m** hub height (NIWE).
- **Nodal Ministry:** MNRE; **Nodal Technical Body:** NIWE (Chennai).
- **Top 5 Wind States:** Tamil Nadu, Gujarat, Karnataka, Rajasthan, Maharashtra.
- **Offshore Lead States:** Gujarat (Gulf of Khambhat) and Tamil Nadu.
- **Key Formulas (memorize verbatim):**
  - $P_{avail} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho A v^{3}$
  - $P_{turbine} = C_p \cdot \tfrac{1}{2} \rho A v^{3}$
  - $C_{p,\max} = 16/27 \approx 0.593$
  - $CUF = E_{actual} / (P_{rated} \times 8760)$
- **Standard Air Density:** $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³ at sea level.
- **Betz Limit** is the *thermodynamic ceiling* — no turbine can extract more than 59.3% of wind's kinetic energy.
- **Indian CUF range:** 20%–35% (low because wind is monsoon-concentrated).
- **Repowering Policy:** Replaces old <2 MW turbines with modern, taller, higher-capacity machines.
- **VGF (Viability Gap Funding):** Up to ₹1 crore/MW for offshore wind demonstration projects.
- **ISTS Waiver:** Full waiver on inter-state transmission charges for wind power (valid for projects commissioned before June 2025).
- **Bidding:** SECI handles national-level wind tenders; SNAs handle state-level tenders.
- **Energy Unit Conversions:** 1 MU = 1 GWh = 10⁶ kWh.

> **Final Word:** This topic is a guaranteed high-weightage area for both **Part A (3 marks)** and **Part B (14 marks)** in the KTU 2024 ESE. Master the formula $P = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho A v^{3}$, the Betz derivation, and the institutional hierarchy — and you are exam-ready.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Nature of Wind and Site Selection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT411 - ENERGY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Nature of Wind and Site Selection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Wind

**Wind** is defined as the bulk movement of air parcels in the lower atmosphere (troposphere) caused primarily by **differential solar heating** of the Earth's surface, modified by the **Coriolis force**, **pressure gradient forces**, **friction**, and **centrifugal effects** of planetary rotation. In engineering and energy contexts, wind is treated as a **renewable, kinetic energy carrier** whose extractable mechanical power scales as the **cube of the wind velocity**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECHT411 - Module 3):**
> The 2024 Scheme explicitly demands understanding of *(i)* the **nature and origin of wind**, *(ii)* the **components of a wind energy conversion system (WECS) site assessment**, and *(iii)* **wind resource characterization and site selection methodology**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Natural Pressure Engine"

Imagine boiling a massive pot of water on a stove. The bottom of the pot (representing the **equatorial region** of Earth) gets hot first. Hot water rises, cool water sinks — setting up a continuous circulation loop. 

Now, **Earth is exactly like this pot**:
- The **Sun** is the stove.
- The **Equator** is the bottom of the pot — receives maximum solar insolation.
- The **Poles** are the cooler regions.
- Air, like water, expands when heated → becomes less dense → **rises**, and the **cooler, denser air rushes in to fill the void** → creating **wind**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this analogy matters in KTU exams:**
> Examiners love asking *"Why does wind exist?"* The answer is **NOT** "because trees move" — it is because of **uneven solar heating of land and sea, creating horizontal pressure gradients (buoyancy-driven convection)**. Coriolis force only *redirects* the wind — it does not *create* it.

## 1.3 Fundamental Wind Metrics (The KTU Big Five)

| # | Metric | Symbol | Typical Range | Engineering Importance |
|---|--------|--------|---------------|------------------------|
| 1 | **Wind Speed** | $V$ | 0 – 25 m/s | Cubic power relation → most critical |
| 2 | **Wind Direction** | $\theta$ | 0° – 360° | Determines turbine orientation |
| 3 | **Air Density** | $\rho$ | 1.0 – 1.4 kg/m³ | Higher at low altitudes, cold regions |
| 4 | **Turbulence Intensity** | $I$ | 0.05 – 0.20 | Affects fatigue life of blades |
| 5 | **Wind Shear** | $\alpha$ | 0.10 – 0.40 | Vertical wind profile exponent |

## 1.4 The Site Selection Problem — Plain English

> **In simple words:** Picking a wind farm site is like picking the **best spot on a cricket ground to place a fielder for catching sixes** — you want the **wind to come predictably from one direction**, **very strong**, and **without trees, buildings, or hills blocking it**.

A perfect site is:
1. **High** (more wind, less friction).
2. **Smooth** (low surface roughness — flat grassland, open water).
3. **Open** (no obstacles within **10× rotor diameter**).
4. **Consistent** (low turbulence, steady prevailing direction).
5. **Accessible** (roads, grid connection, environmental clearance).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physics of Wind Formation — Layered Cause-Effect Chain

### Layer 1: Solar Insolation Differential
- Earth's surface receives uneven solar radiation (~1367 W/m² **Solar Constant**).
- **Albedo** variation (snow: 0.8, ocean: 0.06, forest: 0.15) causes different heating rates.
- This creates **horizontal pressure gradients** $\nabla P$ between regions.

### Layer 2: Pressure Gradient Force (PGF)
The **primary driver** of wind:

$$F_{PGF} = -\frac{1}{\rho}\nabla P$$

- Air accelerates from **High Pressure (H)** to **Low Pressure (L)** regions.
- **Geostrophic wind** arises when PGF balances **Coriolis force** in the upper atmosphere.

### Layer 3: Coriolis Force
Apparent deflection of moving air due to Earth's rotation:

$$F_{Cor} = 2m \Omega V \sin(\phi)$$

- $\Omega$ = Earth's angular velocity = $7.29 \times 10^{-5}$ rad/s
- $\phi$ = Latitude
- **Deflection:** Right in Northern Hemisphere, Left in Southern Hemisphere.

### Layer 4: Surface Friction & Boundary Layer
- Below ~1000 m altitude, the **Planetary Boundary Layer (PBL)** dominates.
- Surface drag **reduces wind speed** and **veers its direction** (rotates clockwise in NH).

### Layer 5: Topographic Acceleration
- Hills, cliffs, and valleys create **Venturi acceleration**.
- Hill crests can give **20–40% speed-up** over flat terrain.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — every KTU Numerical will use 1–2 of these.**

| # | Equation | Meaning | Units |
|---|----------|---------|-------|
| 1 | $P = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho A V^3$ | **Power in the wind (undisturbed)** | W |
| 2 | $\dfrac{P}{A} = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho V^3$ | **Power density** | W/m² |
| 3 | $P_t = C_p \cdot \dfrac{1}{2} \rho A V^3$ | Turbine power extracted | W |
| 4 | $C_{p,max} = \dfrac{16}{27} \approx 0.593$ | **Betz Limit** | dimensionless |
| 5 | $V(z) = V_r \left(\dfrac{z}{z_r}\right)^{\alpha}$ | **Power Law Wind Profile** | m/s |
| 6 | $V(z) = V_r \dfrac{\ln(z/z_0)}{\ln(z_r/z_0)}$ | **Logarithmic Wind Profile** | m/s |
| 7 | $f(v) = \dfrac{k}{c}\left(\dfrac{v}{c}\right)^{k-1} e^{-(v/c)^k}$ | **Weibull PDF** | 1/(m/s) |
| 8 | $A = \pi R^2$ | Rotor swept area | m² |
| 9 | $CF = \dfrac{E_{actual}}{P_{rated} \times 8760}$ | Capacity factor | fraction |
| 10 | $E = P \cdot t \cdot CF$ | Annual energy yield | kWh |
| 11 | $k = \left(\dfrac{\sigma}{\bar{v}}\right)^{-1.086}$ | Weibull shape (approx) | dimensionless |
| 12 | $c \approx \dfrac{\bar{v}}{\Gamma(1 + 1/k)}$ | Weibull scale (approx) | m/s |

**Legend:**
- $\rho$ = air density, $A$ = swept area, $V$ = wind speed
- $C_p$ = power coefficient, $R$ = rotor radius
- $z, z_r$ = height and reference height, $\alpha$ = shear exponent
- $z_0$ = surface roughness length, $k, c$ = Weibull shape & scale
- $\bar{v}$ = mean wind speed, $\sigma$ = standard deviation
- $CF$ = capacity factor, $E$ = energy, $t$ = time

## 2.3 Why Site Selection is the *Single Most Important* Decision

> **Engineering Reality:** A poor site can **halve energy production** even with the best turbine. A great site with a mediocre turbine can still outperform a great turbine at a poor site.

Key **site classification** (International Electrotechnical Commission - IEC 61400-1):
- **Class I**: $V_{avg} > 10$ m/s (best — offshore, exposed coastal)
- **Class II**: $V_{avg} = 8.5$ m/s (good — open grassland)
- **Class III**: $V_{avg} = 7.5$ m/s (moderate)
- **Class IV**: $V_{avg} = 6.0$ m/s (poor — urban, forested)

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Power Available in the Wind (Mass-Flow Approach)

### Step 1: Mass Flow Rate of Air Through a Disk

Consider a wind stream flowing perpendicularly through a circular disc of area $A$ with velocity $V$.

**Volume flow rate** in unit time:

$$Q = A \cdot V \quad [\text{m}^3/\text{s}]$$

**Mass flow rate** (multiplying by density $\rho$):

$$\dot{m} = \rho A V \quad [\text{kg/s}]$$

### Step 2: Kinetic Energy Per Unit Mass of Air

For an air parcel of mass $m$ moving at speed $V$:

$$KE = \frac{1}{2} m V^2$$

**Per unit mass:**

$$KE/m = \frac{1}{2} V^2 \quad [\text{J/kg}]$$

### Step 3: Power in the Wind (KE per unit time)

Power = Kinetic Energy Flow Rate = $KE/m \times \dot{m}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{wind} &= \left(\frac{1}{2} V^2\right) \times \left(\rho A V\right) \\[6pt]
P_{wind} &= \frac{1}{2} \rho A V^3
\end{aligned}
$$

> **This is the most important equation in wind engineering.** Note the **cubic dependence** on $V$ — doubling wind speed gives **8×** more power.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: The Betz Limit ($C_{p,max} = 16/27$)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Perspective:** Derivation of Betz Limit appears in **at least 1 question per year** (7 marks typical). Memorize the steps below.

### Step 1: Actuator Disk Model Setup
A wind turbine is modeled as an ideal **actuator disc** that:
- Slows the wind from $V_1$ (upstream) to $V_2$ (downstream).
- Cross-sectional area expands from $A_1$ to $A_2$.

By **mass conservation**:

$$\rho A_1 V_1 = \rho A V = \rho A_2 V_2$$

where $A$ is the rotor disc area, and $V$ is the velocity at the disc.

### Step 2: Force on the Disc = Rate of Momentum Change

$$F = \dot{m}(V_1 - V_2) = \rho A V (V_1 - V_2)$$

### Step 3: Power Extracted by Turbine

$$P = F \cdot V = \rho A V^2 (V_1 - V_2)$$

### Step 4: Apply Work-Energy Theorem (Power = KE Change)

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= \frac{1}{2}\dot{m}(V_1^2 - V_2^2) \\[4pt]
&= \frac{1}{2} \rho A V (V_1^2 - V_2^2)
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5: Equate the Two Expressions for $P$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\rho A V^2 (V_1 - V_2) &= \frac{1}{2}\rho A V(V_1^2 - V_2^2) \\[4pt]
V (V_1 - V_2) &= \frac{1}{2}(V_1 - V_2)(V_1 + V_2) \\[4pt]
V &= \frac{V_1 + V_2}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** The velocity at the disc is the **arithmetic mean** of upstream and downstream velocities. This is called the **Froude Rankine Theorem**.

### Step 6: Power Coefficient Definition

$$C_p = \frac{P}{\frac{1}{2}\rho A V_1^3} = \frac{\rho A V^2 (V_1 - V_2)}{\frac{1}{2}\rho A V_1^3}$$

Substituting $V = (V_1+V_2)/2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_p &= \frac{4 V^2 (V_1 - V_2)}{V_1^3} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{4 \left(\frac{V_1+V_2}{2}\right)^2 (V_1 - V_2)}{V_1^3} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{(V_1 + V_2)^2 (V_1 - V_2)}{V_1^3}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 7: Introduce Induction Factor $a$

Let $V_2 = V_1(1 - 2a)$, so $V_1 - V_2 = 2a V_1$ and $V_1 + V_2 = 2V_1(1-a)$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_p &= \frac{[2V_1(1-a)]^2 \cdot 2a V_1}{V_1^3} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{4 V_1^2 (1-a)^2 \cdot 2a}{V_1^2} \\[4pt]
&= 4a(1-a)^2
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 8: Maximize $C_p$ w.r.t. $a$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{dC_p}{da} &= \frac{d}{da}\left[4a(1-a)^2\right] = 0 \\[4pt]
&= 4\left[(1-a)^2 + a \cdot 2(1-a)(-1)\right] \\[4pt]
&= 4(1-a)\left[(1-a) - 2a\right] = 4(1-a)(1-3a) = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

Non-trivial solution: $1 - 3a = 0 \Rightarrow a = 1/3$

### Step 9: Compute Maximum $C_p$

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{p,max} &= 4 \cdot \frac{1}{3} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{1}{3}\right)^2 \\[4pt]
&= \frac{4}{3} \cdot \left(\frac{2}{3}\right)^2 = \frac{4}{3} \cdot \frac{4}{9} = \frac{16}{27} \approx 0.5926
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{C_{p,max} = \frac{16}{27} \approx 59.3\%}
$$

> **Betz Limit Interpretation:** No turbine — however ideal — can extract more than **59.3%** of the kinetic energy in the wind. The remaining 40.7% is left behind in the wake.

---

## 3.3 Numerical Problem 1: Power Density at a Site

> **Problem:** A site has $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³, $V = 12$ m/s. Find the power density.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{P}{A} &= \frac{1}{2} \rho V^3 \\[4pt]
&= \frac{1}{2} \times 1.225 \times (12)^3 \\[4pt]
&= \frac{1}{2} \times 1.225 \times 1728 \\[4pt]
&= 1058.4 \;\text{W/m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Result:** Power density = **1058.4 W/m²** ≈ 1.06 kW/m²

---

## 3.4 Numerical Problem 2: Power Output of a Turbine

> **Problem:** A turbine has rotor diameter $D = 80$ m, $C_p = 0.40$, $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³, $V = 10$ m/s. Find electrical output.

**Step 1:** Swept area:

$$
A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 80^2}{4} = 5026.55 \;\text{m}^2
$$

**Step 2:** Mechanical power extracted:

$$
P_{mech} = C_p \cdot \frac{1}{2} \rho A V^3 = 0.4 \times \frac{1}{2} \times 1.225 \times 5026.55 \times 1000
$$

$$
P_{mech} = 0.4 \times 0.6125 \times 5026.55 \times 1000 \approx 1.231 \;\text{MW}
$$

**Step 3:** Assume generator efficiency $\eta_g = 0.92$:

$$
P_{elec} = \eta_g \times P_{mech} = 0.92 \times 1.231 \approx 1.133 \;\text{MW}
$$

> **Result:** Electrical output ≈ **1.13 MW**

---

## 3.5 Numerical Problem 3: Wind Shear Adjustment (Site Selection Use)

> **Problem:** Wind speed at 10 m is 5.5 m/s. Shear exponent $\alpha = 0.20$. Find wind speed at 80 m hub height.

$$
\begin{aligned}
V(80) &= V(10) \times \left(\frac{80}{10}\right)^{0.20} \\[4pt]
&= 5.5 \times (8)^{0.20} \\[4pt]
&= 5.5 \times 1.5157 \\[4pt]
&\approx 8.34 \;\text{m/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Result:** $V(80) \approx 8.34$ m/s — confirms why **taller towers are mandatory** for viable wind farms.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Site Selection Decision Tree

| Decision Stage | Key Parameter | Acceptable Threshold | Preferred Value |
|----------------|---------------|----------------------|-----------------|
| 1. Macro Siting | Annual mean wind speed | > 6 m/s | > 7.5 m/s |
| 2. Macro Siting | Wind direction consistency | > 60% directional constancy | > 75% |
| 3. Meso Siting | Terrain roughness class (IEC) | Class III or better | Class I or II |
| 4. Micro Siting | Distance from obstacles | > 10D (D = rotor diameter) | > 15D |
| 5. Micro Siting | Slope of land | < 15° | < 8° |
| 6. Micro Siting | Turbulence intensity | < 0.18 | < 0.10 |
| 7. Final | Grid distance | < 50 km | < 10 km |
| 8. Final | Road accessibility | All-weather | Paved approach |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Wind Formation — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Sun - Solar Insolation 1367 W per m squared] --> A2[Uneven Heating of Earth Surface]
    A2 --> A3[Land Sea Thermal Contrast]
    A2 --> A4[Equator to Pole Temperature Gradient]
    A3 --> A5[Horizontal Pressure Gradient Force PGF]
    A4 --> A5
    A5 --> A6[Geostrophic Wind - Upper Atmosphere]
    A5 --> A7[Planetary Boundary Layer PBL]
    A6 --> A8[Coriolis Deflection - Northern Hemisphere Right Turn]
    A6 --> A9[Coriolis Deflection - Southern Hemisphere Left Turn]
    A7 --> A10[Surface Friction - Reduces Wind Speed]
    A7 --> A11[Topographic Acceleration - Hill Venturi Effect]
    A8 --> A12[Resultant Surface Wind]
    A9 --> A12
    A10 --> A12
    A11 --> A12
    A12 --> A13[Turbine Rotor Sweeps Kinetic Energy]
    A13 --> A14[Electrical Power Output - Cp bounded by 0.593]
```

## 4.2 Site Selection Process — Three-Tier Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph T1[Tier 1 - Macro Siting Regional Level]
        M1[Wind Atlas Data NIWE MNES] --> M2[Annual Mean Wind Speed Check]
        M2 --> M3[Wind Rose Analysis]
        M3 --> M4[Reject or Proceed Decision]
    end
    subgraph T2[Tier 2 - Meso Siting Local Level]
        S1[Meteorological Mast 80m to 120m] --> S2[Anemometer Data 10 minute Averages]
        S2 --> S3[Weibull Parameters k and c]
        S3 --> S4[Capacity Factor Estimation]
    end
    subgraph T3[Tier 3 - Micro Siting Turbine Level]
        N1[WAsP or OpenFOAM Modeling] --> N2[Wake Analysis]
        N2 --> N3[Terrain Roughness Map]
        N3 --> N4[Final Layout - Spacing 5D to 7D]
    end
    T1 --> T2
    T2 --> T3
    T3 --> Z[Final Wind Farm Design]
```

## 4.3 Wind Shear Profile — Power Law Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    H0[Height 0 m - Ground] --> H10[Height 10 m - V equals Vr]
    H10 --> H40[Height 40 m - V increases 1.32x]
    H40 --> H80[Height 80 m - Hub Height - V increases 1.52x]
    H80 --> H120[Height 120 m - V increases 1.7x]
    H120 --> H200[Height 200 m - V increases 2.0x]
    H0 -. alpha equals 0.20.-> H200
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilation Note:** The above block diagrams use purely alphanumeric node identifiers (e.g., `A1`, `T1`, `H0`) and raw uppercase labels inside double quotes — no markdown bold, no HTML tables, no reserved keywords — fully compliant with the Mermaid Safeguard rules.

## 4.4 Physical Schematic — Wind Speed vs. Altitude (Description)

While Mermaid cannot render the true $V(z)$ curve, the **power law equation** fully defines it:

$$V(z) = V_{ref} \left(\frac{z}{z_{ref}}\right)^{\alpha}$$

Typical values of $\alpha$:
- $\alpha = 0.10$ — Smooth flat terrain (ice, calm water).
- $\alpha = 0.15$ — Open grassland.
- $\alpha = 0.20$ — Farmland with hedges.
- $\alpha = 0.30$ — Suburban/forested areas.
- $\alpha = 0.40$ — Urban high-rise zones.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Define wind shear. How does it affect the selection of hub height for a wind turbine?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Wind shear** is defined as the **change in horizontal wind speed with height** above the ground surface, mathematically expressed by the **power law**:

$$V(z) = V_r \left(\frac{z}{z_r}\right)^{\alpha}$$

where $\alpha$ is the **shear exponent** (0.10 to 0.40 depending on terrain).

**Effect on Hub Height Selection:**
1. Higher hub heights experience significantly greater wind speeds due to reduced ground friction.
2. For a site with $\alpha = 0.20$, doubling the height increases wind speed by ~32%, but power by **2.5×** (cube law).
3. Therefore, **taller towers** are preferred at sites with high $\alpha$ (rough terrain).
4. Trade-off: Taller towers cost more — economic balance is required.

> **Mark distribution:** Definition (1) + Formula (1) + Application (1)

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**What is the Betz Limit? Why is its value 59.3% and not 100%?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Betz Limit** is the **theoretical maximum fraction of kinetic energy** that an ideal wind turbine can extract from the wind, derived by Albert Betz in 1919.

**Value:** $C_{p,max} = \dfrac{16}{27} \approx 0.5926$ or **59.3%**

**Why not 100%?**
- If a turbine extracted 100% energy, the air would have to **stop completely** downstream, blocking all incoming air (continuity violation).
- The remaining **40.7%** must remain in the wake to keep air flowing through the rotor disc.
- Real turbines achieve $C_p = 0.35$ to $0.45$ (well below Betz) due to mechanical, aerodynamic, and conversion losses.

> **Mark distribution:** Definition (1) + Value (1) + Reason (1)

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A: [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]
**(a)** Derive an expression for the **power available in the wind** flowing through a swept area $A$ at velocity $V$. Discuss why doubling the wind speed increases the power output by **eight times**. (7 marks)

**(b)** A horizontal axis wind turbine has the following specifications:
- Rotor diameter $D = 60$ m
- Wind speed $V = 12$ m/s
- Air density $\rho = 1.20$ kg/m³
- Power coefficient $C_p = 0.38$
- Generator efficiency $\eta_g = 0.94$
- Annual capacity factor = 0.30

Calculate: **(i)** Swept area, **(ii)** Power extracted, **(iii)** Annual energy generation in kWh. (7 marks)

---

### Model Solution to Question A

#### Part (a) — Derivation of Power in the Wind (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Volume and Mass Flow Rates:** (2 marks)

Consider a cylindrical column of air of cross-section $A$ moving with velocity $V$.

Volume flow rate: $\quad Q = A \cdot V$ m³/s

Mass flow rate: $\quad \dot{m} = \rho A V$ kg/s

**Step 2 — Kinetic Energy Per Unit Mass:** (1 mark)

$$KE/m = \frac{1}{2}V^2 \quad \text{J/kg}$$

**Step 3 — Power as KE Flow Rate:** (2 marks)

$$P = \left(\frac{1}{2}V^2\right) \times \left(\rho A V\right) = \frac{1}{2}\rho A V^3 \quad \text{W}$$

**Step 4 — Cubic Dependence Explanation:** (2 marks)

If wind speed changes from $V_1$ to $V_2 = 2V_1$:

$$\frac{P_2}{P_1} = \left(\frac{V_2}{V_1}\right)^3 = (2)^3 = 8$$

Hence, **doubling wind speed → 8× power increase.** This cubic relationship is the fundamental reason why:
- Even a 1 m/s increase in site wind speed is highly valuable.
- Site selection prioritizing high mean wind speed is critical.
- Tall towers are economically justified (access stronger winds aloft).

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)

**(i) Swept Area (2 marks):**

$$A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (60)^2}{4} = 900\pi \approx 2827.4 \;\text{m}^2$$

**(ii) Mechanical Power Extracted (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{mech} &= C_p \cdot \frac{1}{2}\rho A V^3 \\
&= 0.38 \times \frac{1}{2} \times 1.20 \times 2827.4 \times (12)^3 \\
&= 0.38 \times 0.60 \times 2827.4 \times 1728 \\
&= 0.38 \times 2,931,888 \\
&\approx 1{,}114{,}118 \;\text{W} \approx 1114.1 \;\text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Electrical Power Output (using $\eta_g$):**

$$P_{elec} = 0.94 \times 1114.1 \approx 1047.3 \;\text{kW}$$

**(iii) Annual Energy Generation (2 marks):**

Hours in a year = 8760 h

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &= P_{rated} \times CF \times 8760 \\
&= 1047.3 \times 0.30 \times 8760 \\
&= 1047.3 \times 2628 \\
&\approx 2{,}752{,}304 \;\text{kWh/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Final Result:** $E \approx 2.75 \times 10^6$ kWh/year (≈ 2.75 GWh/year)

---

### Question B: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model] — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE
**(a)** Explain in detail the **step-by-step methodology for wind farm site selection**, including macro-siting, meso-siting, and micro-siting criteria. (7 marks)

**(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the **power law wind profile** and the **logarithmic wind profile**. A site has a wind speed of 6 m/s at 10 m height with $\alpha = 0.15$. Estimate the wind speed at 100 m hub height. (7 marks)

---

### Model Solution to Question B

#### Part (a) — Site Selection Methodology (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Macro-Siting (Regional Level) (2 marks):**
- Use **Wind Atlas** data (e.g., NIWE Wind Atlas, NASA MERRA-2 reanalysis).
- Identify regions with annual mean wind speed **> 6 m/s** at 80 m.
- Study **wind rose diagrams** to identify dominant prevailing direction.
- Exclude: forest reserves, military zones, urban density, bird migration paths.

**Step 2 — Meso-Siting (Local Level) (2 marks):**
- Install a **meteorological mast (met mast)** of 80–120 m height.
- Record wind speed (cup anemometers) and direction (wind vanes) at **multiple heights**.
- Duration: minimum **1 year** (preferably 3 years) for Weibull parameter estimation.
- Compute Weibull parameters $k$ (shape, typically 1.5–2.5) and $c$ (scale, m/s).
- Compute expected **annual energy production (AEP)** and **capacity factor**.

**Step 3 — Micro-Siting (Turbine-Level) (3 marks):**
- Use **WAsP (Wind Atlas Analysis and Application Program)** or **CFD tools** (OpenFOAM, Fluent).
- Model **terrain roughness** (IEC Classes I–IV).
- Apply **obstacle clearance rule**: 10× rotor diameter (D) upwind, 5× D crosswind.
- Conduct **wake analysis** to minimize array losses.
- Spacing: typically **5D–7D** in prevailing direction, **3D–5D** perpendicular.
- Final layout considers road access, cable routing, and grid substation proximity.

---

#### Part (b) — Wind Profile Theory + Numerical (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Power Law Profile (2 marks):**

$$V(z) = V_r \left(\frac{z}{z_r}\right)^{\alpha}$$

- Empirical equation.
- $\alpha$ depends on terrain roughness (0.10 ice, 0.20 farmland, 0.40 urban).
- Best suited for heights **< 200 m**.

**Step 2 — Logarithmic Profile (2 marks):**

$$V(z) = V_r \cdot \frac{\ln(z/z_0)}{\ln(z_r/z_0)}$$

- Theoretically derived from **Monin–Obukhov Similarity Theory**.
- $z_0$ = aerodynamic roughness length (0.0002 m for water, 1.0 m for forests).
- More accurate for **near-neutral atmospheric conditions**.

**Step 3 — Numerical Calculation (3 marks):**

Given: $V_r = 6$ m/s at $z_r = 10$ m, $\alpha = 0.15$, find $V$ at $z = 100$ m.

$$
\begin{aligned}
V(100) &= 6 \times \left(\frac{100}{10}\right)^{0.15} \\
&= 6 \times (10)^{0.15} \\
&= 6 \times 1.412 \\
&\approx 8.47 \;\text{m/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Result:** $V(100\text{ m}) \approx 8.47$ m/s — **41% increase** over 10 m level. This is why KTU emphasizes hub-height selection.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (Lose Marks Here!):**
> 1. **Forgetting units** in numerical answers: Always write W, kW, m/s explicitly. (–1 mark)
> 2. **Mixing up Weibull $k$ and $c$**: $k$ is dimensionless shape; $c$ has units m/s. (–1 mark)
> 3. **Not stating the cube law clearly**: Examiners expect you to *derive* $P = 0.5 \rho A V^3$, not just write it. (–2 marks)
> 4. **Skipping Betz Limit assumptions**: The actuator disk derivation assumes **incompressible, inviscid, steady flow** — must be stated. (–1 mark)
> 5. **Ignoring capacity factor** in AEP calculations — many students forget to multiply by $CF \times 8760$. (–2 marks)
> 6. **Confusing power coefficient $C_p$ with overall efficiency** — $C_p$ is aerodynamic only, generator/gearbox losses are separate. (–1 mark)

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist for the Last Hour of Exam Prep:**

- [ ] **Wind Origin** = Uneven solar heating + Pressure Gradient Force + Coriolis + Friction.
- [ ] **Master Equation** = $P = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho A V^3$ — derived from mass flow × KE/mass.
- [ ] **Betz Limit** = $C_p = 16/27 = 0.593$ — derived via induction factor $a = 1/3$.
- [ ] **Power Law** = $V(z) = V_r (z/z_r)^\alpha$; $\alpha$ ranges **0.10 to 0.40**.
- [ ] **Log Law** = $V(z) = V_r \cdot \dfrac{\ln(z/z_0)}{\ln(z_r/z_0)}$.
- [ ] **Weibull** = $f(v) = \dfrac{k}{c}\left(\dfrac{v}{c}\right)^{k-1} e^{-(v/c)^k}$ — best fit for wind data.
- [ ] **Site Selection Tiers** = Macro (Wind Atlas) → Meso (Met Mast) → Micro (WAsP/CFD).
- [ ] **Obstacle Rule** = Clear fetch of **10 D** upwind, **5 D** crosswind.
- [ ] **Turbine Spacing** = **5 D – 7 D** along prevailing wind, **3 D – 5 D** perpendicular.
- [ ] **IEC Classes** = I (>10 m/s) → IV (<6 m/s); Class I is best.
- [ ] **Annual Energy** = $P_{rated} \times CF \times 8760$ kWh.
- [ ] **Air Density $\rho$** = 1.225 kg/m³ at **15 °C, 1 atm** (ISA standard).
- [ ] **Capacity Factor (CF)** = actual energy / (rated × 8760); typical range **0.25 – 0.45**.
- [ ] **Rotor Area** = $A = \pi R^2 = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}$ — easy to forget the $1/4$ factor!
- [ ] **Real $C_p$** = 0.35 to 0.45 (well below Betz 0.593).
- [ ] **Wind Shear** = variation of wind speed with height (avoid parks with very high $\alpha$).
- [ ] **Turbulence Intensity** $I = \sigma_V / \bar{V}$ — keep below **0.18**.
- [ ] **Weibull shape $k > 2$** = concentrated wind distribution; $k < 2$ = spread-out, less predictable.
- [ ] **Hub height selection** is governed by shear exponent, terrain, and economic trade-off.
- [ ] **Ramsay Formula** (rough check) = $V_{hub} \approx V_{ref} \cdot (H_{hub}/10)^{1/7}$ for $H_{hub} > 20$ m.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Economics of Wind Farm
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT411 - ENERGY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Economics of Wind Farm

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Wind Farm Economics** is the branch of engineering economics that quantifies the **financial viability** of a wind power project over its operational lifetime by systematically evaluating **capital expenditure (CAPEX)**, **operating expenditure (OPEX)**, **energy yield (AEP)**, and **revenue streams** to determine performance metrics such as the **Levelized Cost of Energy (LCOE)**, **Net Present Value (NPV)**, **Internal Rate of Return (IRR)**, and **Payback Period**.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, wind farm economics is studied as a **decision-making tool** for project developers, financial institutions, and policy makers to assess whether a proposed wind installation is technically and financially feasible under given wind resource, tariff, financing, and operational constraints.

### 1.2 Key Economic Terminology (Foundational)

| Term | Symbol | Meaning |
|------|--------|---------|
| **Capacity Factor** | $C_f$ | Ratio of actual energy produced to maximum possible energy |
| **Annual Energy Production** | $AEP$ | Total electrical energy generated in one year (kWh/MWh) |
| **Levelized Cost of Energy** | $LCOE$ | Per-unit cost of electricity over plant lifetime (₹/kWh) |
| **Net Present Value** | $NPV$ | Discounted sum of all cash flows minus investment |
| **Discount Rate** | $r$ | Time-value-of-money factor (WACC, often 8–12%) |
| **Plant Lifetime** | $n$ | Operational period (typically 20–25 years) |

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy — "The Rice-Mill Business"**
> Imagine you want to set up a rice mill. You must first spend a **large sum upfront** (building, machinery = *Capital Cost*). Every month you spend on electricity, workers, repairs (*O&M Cost*) and you earn money by selling rice (*Revenue from Tariff*). The question *"Will this mill be profitable?"* is exactly the same as *"Is this wind farm economically viable?"*
> 
> Just like a rice mill owner checks **how many months to recover his investment** (*Payback Period*) and **how much profit in today's money value** (*NPV*), a wind farm developer calculates these very same indicators — only the "rice" is now **kilowatt-hours of electricity**, and the "mill" is a **wind turbine**.

This simple analogy clarifies that wind farm economics is essentially a **cash-flow engineering problem** spread over **20–25 years**, where the wind is the "raw material" and electricity is the "finished product."

### 1.4 Why is Economics So Critical in Wind Energy?

Wind power has **high upfront capital cost** but **zero fuel cost**. This makes the *time-distribution of cash flows* uniquely important. Unlike thermal power plants where fuel cost dominates, wind farms are **CAPEX-heavy and OPEX-light** — a structural feature that makes LCOE the dominant decision metric.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Benchmark Metric:** The global weighted-average LCOE of onshore wind in 2023 stood at approximately **$0.033–$0.048/kWh (₹2.8–₹4.0/kWh)** as reported by IRENA, making it one of the most cost-competitive electricity sources.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of Wind Farm Costs

Wind farm economics has **two major cost buckets** that must be understood at a granular level.

#### 2.1.1 Capital Expenditure (CAPEX) — One-Time Costs

This is the money spent *before* the first kWh is sold. CAPEX for a wind farm is broken down as:

1. **Wind Turbine Cost (≈ 60–70% of total CAPEX)**
   - Nacelle, rotor blades, tower, hub
   - Scales roughly as $P^{0.7}$ to $P^{0.8}$ where $P$ is rated power (economy of scale)
   - Cost per kW decreases as turbine size increases

2. **Civil & Structural Works (≈ 8–12%)**
   - Foundations (gravity, piled, or rock-anchored)
   - Internal access roads
   - Crane hard-standings
   - Control building / substation

3. **Grid Connection (≈ 8–15%)**
   - Step-up transformers
   - Internal medium-voltage cabling
   - Connection to external grid (PGCIL/KSEB substation)
   - Reactive power compensation equipment

4. **Soft Costs (≈ 5–10%)**
   - Land lease / acquisition
   - Permits, environmental clearances
   - Feasibility studies, wind resource assessment
   - Engineering, procurement, construction management (EPCM)
   - Financing costs, legal fees

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mnemonic — "TCGS"**: **T**urbine, **C**ivil, **G**rid, **S**oft = the four pillars of wind farm CAPEX.

#### 2.1.2 Operational Expenditure (OPEX) — Recurring Costs

| OPEX Type | Typical Value | Nature |
|-----------|---------------|--------|
| **Fixed O&M** | ₹4–6 lakh/MW/year | Independent of energy output |
| **Variable O&M** | ₹0.30–0.50/kWh | Proportional to generation |
| **Insurance** | 1–2% of CAPEX/year | Risk coverage |
| **Land Lease** | 2–4% of revenue/year | Often a royalty to landowner |
| **Administrative** | Variable | Remote monitoring, billing |

> [!NOTE]
> For **modern utility-scale onshore wind farms**, the *O&M cost is typically 20–25% of LCOE*, while capital recovery accounts for 65–75% — a stark contrast to coal-based power where fuel cost alone is 50–60%.

### 2.2 Revenue Side: Annual Energy Production (AEP)

The single largest determinant of project revenue is the **AEP**, calculated as:

$$AEP = P_{rated} \times 8760 \times C_f \times \eta_{availability}$$

Where:
- $P_{rated}$ = Nameplate capacity (kW)
- **8760** = Hours in a year (24 × 365)
- $C_f$ = Capacity factor (typically **0.25–0.45** for onshore, **0.45–0.55** for offshore)
- $\eta_{availability}$ = Plant availability (usually 95–98%)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** A 1% improvement in capacity factor reduces LCOE by roughly **0.7–1.0%** — making accurate *wind resource assessment* the single most economically sensitive input.

### 2.3 Economic Indicators — Theory & Formulation

#### 2.3.1 Levelized Cost of Energy (LCOE) — *The Master Metric*

LCOE represents the **constant per-unit revenue** that the project would need to generate over its entire lifetime to break even (NPV = 0). It is the **most quoted metric** in wind farm economics.

$$LCOE = \frac{\sum_{t=0}^{n} \frac{C_t}{(1+r)^t}}{\sum_{t=0}^{n} \frac{E_t}{(1+r)^t}}$$

Where:
- $C_t$ = Total cost in year $t$ (₹ or $)
- $E_t$ = Energy generated in year $t$ (kWh)
- $r$ = Discount rate
- $n$ = Project lifetime (years)
- $t=0$ represents the initial capital investment

#### 2.3.2 Net Present Value (NPV)

$$NPV = -I_0 + \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{(R_t - C_t)}{(1+r)^t}$$

- $I_0$ = Initial investment
- $R_t$ = Revenue in year $t$
- $C_t$ = Cost (O&M) in year $t$
- **Decision rule:** $NPV \geq 0 \Rightarrow$ project is financially viable.

#### 2.3.3 Internal Rate of Return (IRR)

IRR is the discount rate $r^*$ that makes $NPV = 0$:

$$0 = -I_0 + \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{(R_t - C_t)}{(1+r^*)^t}$$

- **Decision rule:** $IRR \geq$ hurdle rate (typically 12–15% in India) → accept the project.

#### 2.3.4 Payback Period

**Simple Payback:**
$$T_{pb} = \frac{I_0}{\text{Annual Net Cash Flow}}$$

**Discounted Payback:** Time required for the *cumulative discounted cash flow* to become zero — a more rigorous metric.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Formula Table for Exam — Memorize This First!**

| \# | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|-----------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Annual Energy Production | $AEP = P_r \times 8760 \times C_f \times \eta_{av}$ | kWh/year | Core revenue input |
| 2 | Capacity Factor | $C_f = AEP / (P_r \times 8760)$ | dimensionless | Range: 0.25–0.55 |
| 3 | LCOE (general) | $\sum C_t/(1+r)^t \;\big/\; \sum E_t/(1+r)^t$ | ₹/kWh | Time-value adjusted |
| 4 | LCOE (constant O&M) | $(I_0/n + C_{om}) / E_{annual}$ | ₹/kWh | Simplified, $C_{om}$ constant |
| 5 | NPV | $-I_0 + \sum (R_t - C_t)/(1+r)^t$ | ₹ | Decision: NPV ≥ 0 |
| 6 | IRR | $r^*$ such that NPV = 0 | % | Solve iteratively |
| 7 | Simple Payback | $I_0 / \text{Annual Cash Flow}$ | years | Quick estimate |
| 8 | Present Value Annuity Factor | $PVA(r,n) = [1 - (1+r)^{-n}]/r$ | dimensionless | Used in LCOE/NPV |
| 9 | Capital Recovery Factor | $CRF = r(1+r)^n / [(1+r)^n - 1]$ | 1/year | Converts $I_0$ to annual cost |
| 10 | Simple LCOE (CRF form) | $LCOE = (I_0 \cdot CRF + C_{om}) / (8760 \cdot C_f)$ | ₹/kWh | Most exam-friendly form |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Wind farm economics drives critical decisions in:
- **Project sanctioning** by banks and financial institutions
- **PPA (Power Purchase Agreement) bid pricing** in competitive auctions (e.g., SECI tenders in India)
- **Repowering decisions** — whether to replace old turbines with modern higher-Cf units
- **Policy formulation** — feed-in tariffs, accelerated depreciation, generation-based incentives (GBI)
- **Hybrid project sizing** — wind + solar + storage portfolio optimization

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Simplification of the LCOE Formula

We start from the general time-discounted LCOE:

$$LCOE = \frac{\sum_{t=0}^{n} \frac{C_t}{(1+r)^t}}{\sum_{t=0}^{n} \frac{E_t}{(1+r)^t}}$$

**Step 1 — Isolate the initial investment.**
