# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`

> Define the term **Combustion** as applied to biomass. Write the stoichiometric chemical equations for the complete combustion of (i) carbon, (ii) hydrogen, and (iii) sulphur.

**Model Answer:**

Combustion is the rapid, high-temperature, exothermic chemical reaction between a fuel and an oxidiser (typically atmospheric air) in which the chemical energy stored in C–H and C–C bonds is released as heat, light, and fully oxidised products (CO₂, H₂O, SO₂).

Stoichiometric equations:

$$ \mathrm{C_{(s)} + O_{2(g)} \longrightarrow CO_{2(g)}} \qquad \Delta H \;=\; -393.5 \;\text{kJ/mol} $$

$$ \mathrm{H_{2(g)} + \tfrac{1}{2} O_{2(g)} \longrightarrow H_2O_{(l)}} \qquad \Delta H \;=\; -285.8 \;\text{kJ/mol} $$

$$ \mathrm{S_{(s)} + O_{2(g)} \longrightarrow SO_{2(g)}} \qquad \Delta H \;=\; -296.8 \;\text{kJ/mol} $$

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`

> Differentiate between **proximate analysis** and **ultimate analysis** of a biomass fuel. Why is proximate analysis alone insufficient for combustion calculations?

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Proximate Analysis | Ultimate Analysis |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Components measured | Moisture, volatile matter, fixed carbon, ash | Carbon, hydrogen, oxygen, nitrogen, sulphur, ash |
| Standard method | ASTM E871, E872, E1755 | ASTM E777, E778 |
| Purpose | Indicates combustion behaviour, ignition, flame stability | Determines stoichiometric O₂, air, and heating value |
| Limitation | Cannot give HHV or air requirement directly | Cannot indicate volatile release kinetics |

Proximate analysis alone is insufficient for combustion calculations because the **stoichiometric air requirement, HHV (via Dulong's formula), and flue gas composition** all require the elemental mass fractions of C, H, O, N, S — which are provided only by ultimate analysis.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (with Internal Choice)

### Question B-Option A `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]`

> **Part (a) — 7 marks:**
>
> A biomass fuel has the following ultimate analysis on a dry basis (mass %):
> **C = 47.5 %, H = 5.8 %, O = 40.2 %, N = 1.0 %, S = 0.5 %, Ash = 5.0 %**.
>
> The as-fired moisture content is 12 %. The fuel is burnt with **35 % excess air**.
>
> Calculate:
> 1. Stoichiometric O₂ requirement per kg of dry fuel.
> 2. Actual air supplied per kg of dry fuel (mass basis).
> 3. The dry flue gas composition (mole %).
>
> **Part (b) — 7 marks:**
>
> Explain the **four sequential stages of biomass combustion** in detail. For each stage, specify the temperature window, dominant reaction(s), and the engineering timescale involved.

---

### Model Solution for B-Option A, Part (a)

**Step 1 — Stoichiometric O₂** (per kg dry fuel):

$$ n_{O_2,st} \;=\; \frac{0.475}{12} + \frac{0.058}{4} + \frac{0.005}{32} - \frac{0.402}{32} $$

$$ n_{O_2,st} \;=\; 0.03958 + 0.01450 + 0.000156 - 0.01256 \;=\; 0.04168 \;\text{kmol O}_2/\text{kg} $$

**Step 2 — Actual air**:

$$ A_{st,mass} \;=\; \frac{0.04168 \times 100}{23.2} \;=\; 5.989 \;\text{kg air/kg fuel} $$

$$ A_{actual} \;=\; A_{st,mass} \times 1.35 \;=\; 5.989 \times 1.35 \;=\; 8.085 \;\text{kg air/kg fuel} $$

**Step 3 — Dry flue gas composition** (per kg dry fuel, E = 0.35):

$$ n_{CO_2} = \frac{0.475}{12} = 0.03958 \;\text{kmol} $$

$$ n_{SO_2} = \frac{0.005}{32} = 0.000156 \;\text{kmol} $$

$$ n_{N_2} = 0.79 \times \frac{8.085}{28.97} = 0.79 \times 0.2791 = 0.2205 \;\text{kmol} $$

$$ n_{O_2,\,excess} = 0.35 \times 0.04168 = 0.01459 \;\text{kmol} $$

$$ n_{dry} = 0.03958 + 0.000156 + 0.2205 + 0.01459 = 0.2748 \;\text{kmol} $$

$$ \%CO_2 = 14.41\,\% \quad \%N_2 = 80.24\,\% \quad \%O_2 = 5.31\,\% \quad \%SO_2 = 0.06\,\% $$

---

### Model Solution for B-Option A, Part (b)

**Stage 1 — Drying (100–150 °C):**
Free and bound moisture evaporates, consuming latent heat (~2.26 MJ/kg H₂O). Purely physical, endothermic, externally heat-transfer-limited. Residence time: seconds to minutes depending on particle size and external heat flux.

**Stage 2 — Pyrolysis / Devolatilisation (200–600 °C):**
Thermal decomposition of hemicellulose (~200–300 °C), cellulose (~300–400 °C), and lignin (~250–500 °C) releases volatile gases (CO, CO₂, CH₄, H₂, light HC, tars, water vapour). Endothermic overall. Residence time: 0.5–5 s.

**Stage 3 — Char Combustion (400–1200 °C):**
Heterogeneous surface reaction of fixed carbon with O₂:
- $\mathrm{C + O_2 \to CO_2}$ (complete, dominant at lower temperature)
- $\mathrm{C + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \to CO}$ (incomplete, dominant at high T)
Kinetically slow; rate-limited by O₂ diffusion through boundary layer and ash film. Residence time: 30 s to several minutes — **design bottleneck**.

**Stage 4 — Gas-Phase Combustion (600–1500 °C):**
Volatile gases mix with secondary air and oxidise in millisecond timescales, releasing the majority of the heat and producing the visible flame. Incomplete mixing leads to CO, soot, and UHC emissions.

---

### Question B-Option B `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`

> **Part (a) — 7 marks:**
>
> Define **HHV** and **LHV**. A biomass fuel has the following ultimate analysis (dry basis, mass %):
> **C = 42.0 %, H = 5.5 %, O = 38.5 %, N = 1.2 %, S = 0.3 %, Ash = 12.5 %**.
>
> Moisture content (wet basis) = 15 %. Calculate the **HHV** using Dulong's formula and the **LHV** of the fuel as fired.
>
> **Part (b) — 7 marks:**
>
> Discuss the **challenges in biomass combustion** compared to fossil fuel combustion. With neat sketches, classify the principal **combustion technologies** (fixed bed, fluidised bed, suspension) according to their suitability for different biomass types.

---

### Model Solution for B-Option B, Part (a)

**Definitions:**
- **HHV (Higher Heating Value):** Total heat released assuming all water vapour in products condenses to liquid and the latent heat is fully recovered.
- **LHV (Lower Heating Value):** Heat released assuming water leaves as vapour — common design basis for industrial combustors since flue gas temperature > 100 °C prevents condensation.

**HHV by Dulong:**

$$ \begin{aligned}
HHV &= 0.3491(42.0) + 1.1783(5.5) + 0.1005(0.3) \\
    &\quad - 0.1034(38.5) - 0.0151(1.2) - 0.0211(12.5) \\[4pt]
    &= 14.66 + 6.48 + 0.030 - 3.981 - 0.018 - 0.264 \\[4pt]
    &= 16.91 \;\text{MJ/kg (dry basis)}
\end{aligned} $$

**LHV (as-fired, accounting for moisture and hydrogen):**

With H = 0.055 (mass fraction) and M = 0.15:

$$ LHV \;=\; 16.91 - 2.442 \times (9 \times 0.055 + 0.15) $$

$$ LHV \;=\; 16.91 - 2.442 \times (0.495 + 0.15) $$

$$ LHV \;=\; 16.91 - 2.442 \times 0.645 $$

$$ LHV \;=\; 16.91 - 1.575 $$

$$ \boxed{\, LHV \;=\; 15.34 \;\text{MJ/kg (as-fired)}\,} $$

---

### Model Solution for B-Option B, Part (b)

**Challenges in biomass combustion** (versus coal/fossil fuels):

1. **High moisture** (often 30–60 % wet for agro-residues) → large latent heat loss, low flame temperature, ignition difficulties.
2. **High volatile matter** (60–85 % vs 20–35 % for coal) → long, smoky, luminous flames; requires secondary air and adequate residence time.
3. **Low bulk density** (60–250 kg/m³ vs 800+ for coal) → large storage, handling, and feeding volumes.
4. **Hygroscopic, fibrous, irregular shape** → bridge formation in hoppers, uneven feeding.
5. **Alkali metals (K, Na)** and **chlorine** in ash → slagging, fouling, bed agglomeration (especially in fluidised beds), high-temperature corrosion of superheaters.
6. **High O/C and H/C ratios** → more flue gas per MJ of heat, lower adiabatic flame temperature.
7. **Variability** (composition changes batch-to-batch) → control and emissions stability issues.
8. **NOₓ and CO challenges** — fuel-N contributes significantly (1–2 % of biomass is N), requiring air-staging or flue gas recirculation.

**Classification of Combustion Technologies:**

- **Fixed-Bed / Grate Combustors** — Best for coarse, wet, variable fuels (wood, bark, MSW). Slow combustion, high turndown ratio, but limited to < 50 MWᵢ.
- **Fluidised-Bed Combustors (BFB and CFB)** — Best for challenging fuels (sludge, straw, rice husk). Excellent fuel flexibility, in-bed sulphur capture by limestone, uniform temperature (~850 °C). Slagging risk from alkali ash.
- **Suspension / Pulverised-Biomass Burners** — Best for large-scale, dry, fine fuels (> 50 MWᵢ utility). High flame temperatures, requires pre-drying to < 15 % moisture and grinding to < 1 mm.
- **Stoker / Spreader-Fired** — Intermediate; for chips and sawdust in 5–50 MWᵢ industrial boilers.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Read Carefully**
>
> - **Sign of Heating Value:** Dulong's formula gives **positive numerical HHV**. Always write the unit as MJ/kg; do **not** prefix a negative sign — the convention in combustion engineering is that heating value is a magnitude.
> - **Wet vs Dry Basis Mixing:** When a problem gives ultimate analysis on dry basis but asks for LHV "as fired," you must apply the moisture correction using the **as-fired** H value, **not** the dry-basis H. Forgetting this is the most common mark-loss.
> - **"Oxygen already in fuel":** Many students compute $C/12 + H/4 + S/32$ and **forget to subtract** $O/32$. This over-estimates air by 10–25 % — a guaranteed 2-mark loss in stoichiometry problems.
> - **Excess air % vs equivalence ratio:** When asked for "AFR with 40 % excess air," multiply $A_{st}$ by 1.40 — do **not** divide.
> - **Dry flue gas vs wet flue gas:** CO₂ % depends on whether water is included. If the question asks for **dry** flue gas, exclude H₂O from both numerator and denominator.
> - **Units of Dulong's formula:** Input mass fractions **as percentages (0–100)**, not as decimals. The coefficients are calibrated for percentage input.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Things to Remember Before Every Combustion Question**

- **Biomass = biodegradable organic matter.** Two conversion families: thermo-chemical (combustion, gasification, pyrolysis, torrefaction) and biochemical (anaerobic digestion, fermentation).
- **Combustion** = rapid, complete oxidation of fuel with **excess** O₂ to CO₂, H₂O, and heat.
- **Four stages:** Drying (≤150 °C) → Pyrolysis (200–600 °C) → Char Combustion (400–1200 °C) → Gas-phase Combustion (600–1500 °C).
- **Stoichiometric O₂ formula (per kg dry fuel):** $n_{O_2,st} = C/12 + H/4 + S/32 - O/32$ kmol/kg.
- **Stoichiometric air (mass):** $A_{st} = n_{O_2,st} \times 100/23.2$ kg/kg.
- **Air has 23.2 wt % O₂** and is **21 vol % O₂, 79 vol % N₂** at dry conditions.
- **Molar conversion factor** between O₂ and air = **4.76**.
- **Excess air** $EA = (A_{actual}-A_{st})/A_{st} \times 100$ %.
- **Equivalence ratio** $\phi = A_{st}/A_{actual}$; $\phi = 1$ is stoichiometric.
- **Dulong's HHV** uses **percentage** mass fractions: $0.3491C + 1.1783H + 0.1005S - 0.1034O - 0.0151N - 0.0211A$ (MJ/kg).
- **LHV = HHV − 2.442 × (9H + M)** with H and M as mass fractions.
- **Combustion efficiency** = 1 − (sum of losses)/HHV; main losses are dry flue gas sensible heat, moisture latent heat, CO/unburnt, and radiation.
- **Typical biomass stoichiometric AFR**: 5.5–6.5 kg/kg for woody biomass; 4.0–5.0 kg/kg for high-ash residues.
- **Typical excess air**: 15–25 % for suspension firing, 30–60 % for grate firing, 20–40 % for fluidised bed.
- **Combustion technologies**: Fixed-bed (grate), Fluidised-bed (BFB/CFB), Suspension (pulverised biomass) — selected by moisture, ash, scale, and fuel variability.
- **Major biomass-specific challenges**: high moisture, high volatile matter, alkali slagging, fuel-NOₓ, low bulk density.
- **Dry flue gas mole %** = $n_i / n_{dry\,total} \times 100$, where $n_{dry\,total}$ excludes H₂O.
- **KTU trick questions** may give O₂ fraction in flue gas and ask for excess air — use the identity $n_{O_2,excess} = n_{N_2}/3.76 \times x_{O_2}/(0.21 - x_{O_2})$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> - **Course:** PECHT411 - ENERGY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Gasification

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Gasification** is a thermo-chemical conversion process in which a solid or liquid carbonaceous fuel (coal, biomass, municipal solid waste, petroleum residue) is converted into a combustible gaseous fuel — commonly called **Producer Gas**, **Synthesis Gas (Syngas)**, or **Low-BTU Gas** — by reacting the feedstock with a controlled, sub-stoichiometric quantity of an oxidizing agent (air, pure oxygen, oxygen-steam mixtures, or steam) at elevated temperatures (**typically 800 °C – 1800 °C**) and ambient or elevated pressures.

The process is fundamentally a **partial oxidation** reaction: instead of fully combusting the fuel to CO₂ and H₂O (as in conventional combustion), gasification deliberately limits the oxidant supply so that a significant fraction of the carbon and hydrogen ends up as **CO** and **H₂** — the two principal energy carriers in the resulting producer gas.

**Standard Industry Metrics used in KTU Evaluation:**

| Metric | Typical Range | Symbol |
|---|---|---|
| Producer Gas Calorific Value (Air-blown) | **4 – 6 MJ/Nm³** | $CV_{gas}$ |
| Producer Gas Calorific Value (O₂-blown) | **10 – 18 MJ/Nm³** | $CV_{gas}$ |
| Operating Temperature | **800 – 1800 °C** | $T$ |
| Equivalence Ratio (ER) | **0.20 – 0.40** | $\phi$ |
| Cold Gas Efficiency (CGE) | **60 – 80 %** | $\eta_{cg}$ |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Campfire Story"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Picture for First-Time Learners:**
>
> Imagine lighting a campfire with wooden logs. In **ordinary combustion**, you blow in plenty of air and the wood burns vigorously, releasing all its energy immediately as heat and leaving only ash and CO₂. There is no useful gaseous fuel left.
>
> Now imagine you **deliberately restrict the air supply** to the same fire. The flame becomes lazy, orange, and smouldering. Smoke — which is actually a combustible mixture of CO, H₂, CH₄, and tars — escapes from the fire instead of being fully oxidized. If you could capture that smoke and route it through a hot bed of glowing charcoal, the tars would crack into additional CO and H₂, and you would end up with a **clean, flammable gas** you could pipe to a stove, an engine, or a turbine.
>
> **That captured smoke IS producer gas, and the science of maximizing its yield and quality is Gasification.**

The four sequential physical/chemical zones inside any gasifier — **Drying → Pyrolysis → Combustion (Oxidation) → Reduction** — correspond, respectively, to: water evaporating, wood breaking down into volatiles and char, the volatiles partially burning with the limited air, and the hot char chemically reducing CO₂ and H₂O into CO and H₂.

---

## 1.3 Why Gasification? — Engineering Motivation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why KTU Energy Engineering insists on studying Gasification:**
>
> 1. **Solid-to-Gas Conversion** — Enables solid fuels (coal, biomass, agro-residue) to be used in gas turbines, IC engines, and fuel cells which normally demand gaseous or liquid fuels.
> 2. **Higher Electrical Efficiency** — Combined-cycle plants running on syngas from integrated gasification (IGCC) reach **42 – 46 %** net efficiency vs. **33 – 38 %** for conventional pulverized-coal plants.
> 3. **Poly-generation Capability** — The same syngas can yield electricity, process heat, **Fischer–Tropsch diesel**, methanol, ammonia, or hydrogen — making it a *flexible energy carrier*.
> 4. **Environmental Edge** — SOₓ, NOₓ, and CO₂ capture are intrinsically easier from a pressurized gas stream than from flue gas.
> 5. **Waste-to-Energy** — Municipal solid waste (MSW), rice husk, sawdust, and bagasse can all be gasified profitably, addressing Kerala's biomass-rich agro-economy.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Sequential Zones of Gasification

A gasifier is **not a single reactor** but a *cascade of four overlapping zones*, each performing a distinct physico-chemical role. The boundaries between zones are not sharp — they are defined by the dominant reaction occurring at each temperature band.

### Zone 1 — Drying Zone (100 – 200 °C)

- **Physical process only**; no chemical reaction.
- Moisture content of feedstock (typically **5 – 35 %** for biomass) is vaporized using sensible heat carried upwards by the hot product gas (counter-current flow) or by radiant/conductive heat from the combustion zone below.
- **Energy penalty:** Every kg of water vaporized consumes approximately **2.44 MJ** (latent heat at 100 °C).

### Zone 2 — Pyrolysis / Devolatilization Zone (200 – 600 °C)

- In the **absence of oxidant**, the fuel thermally decomposes into:
  - **Volatiles** — a gaseous mixture of CO, H₂, CH₄, C₂H₄, tars, and light hydrocarbons.
  - **Char** — a solid residue rich in fixed carbon (typically **15 – 25 %** by mass of original biomass) plus ash.
- Biomass pyrolysis: $\text{C}_x\text{H}_y\text{O}_z \xrightarrow{\Delta} \text{Char} + \text{Volatiles} + \text{Tars}$

### Zone 3 — Combustion / Oxidation Zone (800 – 1400 °C)

- The sub-stoichiometric air (or O₂) supplied reacts **exothermically** with the char and a fraction of the volatiles.
- This zone provides **all the thermal energy** needed to sustain drying, pyrolysis, and the endothermic reduction reactions downstream.
- Peak temperature here is the **highest** in the reactor and is the single most important indicator of gasifier performance.

### Zone 4 — Reduction Zone (600 – 1000 °C)

- Hot gases (CO₂ and H₂O) leaving the combustion zone pass **down** through the incandescent char bed and are chemically reduced to CO and H₂.
- **No oxygen is present here**; reactions are endothermic and consume the sensible heat of the gases, causing the temperature to drop as they descend.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for exam recall — "D-P-C-R":** **D**ry → **P**yrolyse → **C**ombust → **R**educe. The first letters spell the journey of a feedstock particle from top to bottom of a downdraft gasifier.

---

## 2.2 The Six Governing Chemical Reactions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these six reactions — every gasification problem in the KTU paper will reference at least one of them.**

| # | Reaction Name | Stoichiometric Equation | Enthalpy (kJ/mol) | Type |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| R1 | Partial Oxidation (Heterogeneous) | $\text{C} + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}$ | $\Delta H = -111 \, \text{kJ/mol}$ | Exothermic |
| R2 | Complete Combustion (Heterogeneous) | $\text{C} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2$ | $\Delta H = -394 \, \text{kJ/mol}$ | Exothermic |
| R3 | Boudouard Reaction (Heterogeneous) | $\text{C} + \text{CO}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{CO}$ | $\Delta H = +172 \, \text{kJ/mol}$ | Endothermic |
| R4 | Water-Gas Reaction (Heterogeneous) | $\text{C} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{CO} + \text{H}_2$ | $\Delta H = +131 \, \text{kJ/mol}$ | Endothermic |
| R5 | Water-Gas Shift (Homogeneous) | $\text{CO} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2$ | $\Delta H = -41 \, \text{kJ/mol}$ | Exothermic |
| R6 | Methanation (Heterogeneous) | $\text{C} + 2\text{H}_2 \rightarrow \text{CH}_4$ | $\Delta H = -75 \, \text{kJ/mol}$ | Exothermic |

**Strategic Insight:** R3 and R4 are the **gas-making reactions** — they consume char and produce the CO + H₂ we want. R1 and R2 supply the heat. R5 and R6 *adjust* the H₂/CO ratio and methane content of the final gas.

---

## 2.3 The Critical Process Parameter — Equivalence Ratio (ER)

$$\phi \;=\; \frac{\text{Actual Oxidant-to-Fuel Ratio}}{\text{Stoichiometric Oxidant-to-Fuel Ratio}} \;=\; \frac{(O/F)_{actual}}{(O/F)_{stoich}}$$

- **$\phi = 1$** → complete combustion (no gas fuel left, only heat).
- **$\phi = 0$** → pyrolysis only (no heat generated, process self-extinguishes).
- **$\phi \approx 0.20 - 0.40$** → **the gasification sweet-spot** used industrially.

**Effect of ER on producer gas:**

| ER Value | Combustion Zone T | CO + H₂ Yield | CV of Gas | Tar Content |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $< 0.20$ | Too low | Low | Lower | High (incomplete pyrolysis) |
| **0.25 – 0.30** | **Optimal** | **High** | **Highest** | **Low** |
| $> 0.40$ | Too high (slagging risk) | Oxidized to CO₂/H₂O | Falls sharply | Very low |

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Exam Cheat-Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are recurrent in KTU 2024 Scheme Energy Engineering papers — memorize units and signs.**

| # | Formula | Meaning / Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|
| F1 | $\phi = \dfrac{(O/F)_{actual}}{(O/F)_{stoich}}$ | Equivalence Ratio — sets air/fuel supply |
| F2 | $(A/F)_{stoich} = \dfrac{8/3 \cdot \text{C} + 8 \cdot \text{H} - \text{O}}{\text{Fuel mass}}$ (mass basis, with C, H, O in kg per kg fuel) | Stoichiometric air requirement for combustion |
| F3 | $\text{Stoich. O}_2 = \dfrac{32}{12}\text{C} + \dfrac{32}{4}\text{H} + \dfrac{32}{32}\text{S} - \dfrac{32}{16}\text{O}$ (kg O₂/kg fuel) | Theoretical oxygen demand of a fuel |
| F4 | $\eta_{cg} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{gas} \cdot CV_{gas}}{\dot{m}_{fuel} \cdot CV_{fuel}} \times 100$ | Cold Gas Efficiency — *most asked metric* |
| F5 | $\eta_{th} = \dfrac{Q_{gas} + Q_{sensible}}{Q_{fuel}} \times 100$ | Hot thermal efficiency (includes sensible heat) |
| F6 | $\eta_{carbon} = \dfrac{\text{C in producer gas}}{\text{C in feedstock}} \times 100$ | Carbon conversion efficiency |
| F7 | $S/B = \dfrac{\text{kg steam}}{\text{kg biomass}}$ (dimensionless) | Steam-to-Biomass Ratio — affects H₂ content |
| F8 | $\text{Air req.} = \dfrac{4.31 \cdot \text{C} + 4.34 \cdot \text{H} - 1.43\cdot\text{O} + 1.29\cdot\text{S}}{0.232}$ (Nm³ air/kg fuel) | Volumetric air requirement (C, H, O, S in kg per kg fuel, 0.232 = vol. fraction O₂) |

> Use `\vert` or `\mid` (instead of `\vert x\vert`) if you ever need to write absolute-value terms in any table.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Why Gasification is Used |
|---|---|
| **IGCC Power Plants** | Coal → syngas → gas turbine + steam turbine (combined cycle) |
| **Rural Electrification (Kerala context)** | Coconut-shell, rice-husk gasifiers drive 100 kW – 1 MW engine-generators |
| **Fischer–Tropsch Liquid Fuels** | Syngas (CO + H₂) is catalytically synthesized to diesel, naphtha |
| **Hydrogen Production** | Water-gas shift + CO₂ capture gives near-pure H₂ |
| **Waste-to-Energy** | MSW, plastic, and tyre pyrolysis gasifiers reduce landfill load |
| **Direct Reduced Iron (DRI)** | Reducing gas for steel-making |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Stoichiometric Air Requirement for a Solid Fuel

Given a fuel ultimate analysis on a mass basis: $\text{C}, \text{H}, \text{O}, \text{S}, \text{N}$ in kg per kg of fuel (with ash + moisture tracked separately).

**Step 1 — Write the complete combustion equation:**

$$
\text{C} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 \qquad
\text{H}_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O} \qquad
\text{S} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{SO}_2
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the oxygen consumed by each element (per kg of element).**

- 12 kg of C requires 32 kg of O₂ → **O₂ for C = (32/12) × C kg per kg fuel**
- 2 kg of H requires 16 kg of O₂ → **O₂ for H = (32/4) × H kg per kg fuel**
- 32 kg of S requires 32 kg of O₂ → **O₂ for S = (32/32) × S kg per kg fuel**

**Step 3 — Subtract the oxygen already present in the fuel** (it is *internally available*):

$$
\text{O}_2^{gross} \;=\; \frac{32}{12}\,C \;+\; \frac{32}{4}\,H \;+\; \frac{32}{32}\,S
$$

$$
\text{O}_2^{net} \;=\; \text{O}_2^{gross} \;-\; \frac{32}{16}\,O
$$

**Step 4 — Convert net O₂ demand to air demand** using the mass fraction of O₂ in air = 0.232:

$$
m_{air,\,stoich} \;=\; \frac{\text{O}_2^{net}}{0.232} \quad \text{[kg air per kg fuel]}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to volumetric basis at NTP** ($1\,\text{kmol air} = 22.41\,\text{Nm}^3$, mass = 28.97 kg):

$$
V_{air,\,stoich} \;=\; \frac{m_{air,\,stoich}}{28.97}\times 22.41 \quad \text{[Nm}^3\text{ air per kg fuel]}
$$

Combining steps yields the compact KTU formula:

$$
\boxed{\,V_{air,\,stoich} \;=\; \frac{4.31\,C \;+\; 4.34\,H \;-\; 1.43\,O \;+\; 1.29\,S}{0.232}\;\; \text{Nm}^3\text{ air/kg fuel}\,}
$$

> The numerical coefficients (4.31, 4.34, 1.43, 1.29) emerge from the molar-mass ratios — they are the quotient $\frac{22.41}{M_{element}/n_{O}}\cdot\frac{1}{0.21}$ where $n_O$ is the number of O atoms consumed per atom of the element.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Producer Gas Composition & Cold Gas Efficiency

**Given Data:**
- Fuel = **Pure Carbon (C)** at 1 kg basis.
- Air supplied at **ER = 0.30** (typical industrial value).
- Cold (lower) calorific value of CO = **10.1 MJ/kg**.
- Calorific value of carbon = **32.8 MJ/kg** (HHV basis ≈ 32.79 MJ/kg).

**Step A — Stoichiometric O₂ for complete combustion of 1 kg C:**

$$
\text{O}_{2,\,stoich} \;=\; \frac{32}{12}\times 1 \;=\; 2.667 \;\text{kg O}_2
$$

**Step B — Actual O₂ supplied (using ER definition):**

$$
\text{O}_{2,\,actual} \;=\; \phi \times \text{O}_{2,\,stoich} \;=\; 0.30 \times 2.667 \;=\; 0.800 \;\text{kg O}_2
$$

**Step C — Mass of air supplied (mass fraction of O₂ in air = 0.232):**

$$
m_{air} \;=\; \frac{0.800}{0.232} \;=\; 3.448 \;\text{kg air}
$$

**Step D — Mass of N₂ accompanying the air:**

$$
m_{N_2} \;=\; m_{air} - \text{O}_{2,\,actual} \;=\; 3.448 - 0.800 \;=\; 2.648 \;\text{kg N}_2
$$

**Step E — Apply the partial-oxidation reaction R1:** $\text{C} + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}$

Assuming all available O₂ reacts via R1 (the simplified ideal model the examiner expects):

$$
\text{C consumed} \;=\; 0.800 \times \frac{12}{16} \;=\; 0.600 \;\text{kg C}
$$

$$
\text{CO produced} \;=\; 0.800 \times \frac{28}{16} \;=\; 1.400 \;\text{kg CO}
$$

**Step F — Tabulate producer gas composition (mass basis):**

| Component | Mass (kg) | Mass Fraction |
|---|---|---|
| CO | 1.400 | 0.346 |
| N₂ | 2.648 | 0.654 |
| **Total** | **4.048** | **1.000** |

**Step G — Convert to volume (mole) fraction** using molar masses ($M_{CO}=28$, $M_{N_2}=28$):

$$
n_{CO} \;=\; \frac{1.400}{28} \;=\; 0.0500 \;\text{kmol}
$$

$$
n_{N_2} \;=\; \frac{2.648}{28} \;=\; 0.0946 \;\text{kmol}
$$

$$
y_{CO} \;=\; \frac{0.0500}{0.0500 + 0.0946} \;=\; 0.346 \quad (34.6\%)
$$

$$
y_{N_2} \;=\; 0.654 \quad (65.4\%)
$$

**Step H — Calorific Value of the producer gas (mass basis):**

$$
CV_{gas} \;=\; 0.346 \times 10.1 \;\text{MJ/kg} \;=\; 3.495 \;\text{MJ/kg gas}
$$

**Step I — Cold Gas Efficiency (CGE):**

Energy in gas = $4.048 \times 3.495 = 14.15$ MJ
Energy in fuel = $1 \times 32.8 = 32.80$ MJ

$$
\boxed{\,\eta_{cg} \;=\; \frac{14.15}{32.80}\times 100 \;\approx\; 43.1\,\%\,}
$$

> This 43 % value is realistic — air-blown gasifiers on pure carbon typically yield 40–48 % CGE. The remaining ~57 % leaves as sensible heat of hot gas, char losses, and unconverted carbon.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Steam Injection (Steam-to-Biomass Ratio $S/B$)

> [!NOTE]
> Adding steam shifts R4 forward, increasing H₂ yield at the cost of more endothermic heat load. KTU examiners frequently test the *trade-off*.

For a fixed ER, increasing $S/B$ from 0 to 1.0 typically:

$$
\text{H}_2\text{ in gas}\!:\; 14\,\% \rightarrow 30\,\% \quad\quad
\text{CO in gas}\!:\; 22\,\% \rightarrow 18\,\% \quad\quad
\text{CV}_{gas}\!:\; 5.5 \rightarrow 11\,\text{MJ/Nm}^3
$$

(These are typical experimental ranges for biomass downdraft gasifiers.)

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process Flow — The Four Zones of a Downdraft Gasifier

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    n1["FEEDSTOCK INLET"] --> n2["ZONE 1 DRYING 100 TO 200 C"]
    n2 --> n3["ZONE 2 PYROLYSIS 200 TO 600 C"]
    n3 --> n4["ZONE 3 COMBUSTION 800 TO 1400 C"]
    n4 --> n5["ZONE 4 REDUCTION 600 TO 1000 C"]
    n5 --> n6["PRODUCER GAS OUTLET"]
    n5 --> n7["ASH DISCHARGE"]
    n8["AIR OR OXYGEN INLET"] --> n4
    n4 --> n9["FLAME FRONT"]
    n9 --> n5
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Air enters at the combustion zone only; solids move **downward**; gases produced in zones 3–4 flow **downward** through the char bed (zone 4) and exit at the bottom. This counter-current contacting is the hallmark of a **downdraft gasifier** and is what makes it produce a *tar-lean* gas ideal for engines.

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Classification of Gasifiers

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    g1["GASIFIERS"] --> g2["FIXED BED MOVING GRATE"]
    g1 --> g3["FLUIDIZED BED CIRCULATING"]
    g1 --> g4["ENTRAINED FLOW SLURRY FEED"]
    g2 --> g5["DOWNDRAFT TAR LEAN"]
    g2 --> g6["UPDRAFT HIGH TAR"]
    g2 --> g7["CROSSDRAFT SMALL SCALE"]
    g3 --> g8["BUBBLING FB GOOD MIXING"]
    g3 --> g9["CIRCULATING FB LARGE SCALE"]
    g4 --> g10["PRESSURISED IGCC APPLICATION"]
```

| Gasifier Class | Capacity Range | Feed Size | Tar Load | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Updraft | 1 kW – 10 MW | Coarse (5–100 mm) | High | Direct heating |
| **Downdraft** | **10 kW – 1 MW** | **Fine (10–50 mm)** | **Low** | **Engine feed** |
| Crossdraft | < 1 MW | Fine | Medium | Small cookstoves |
| Bubbling FB | 1 – 25 MW | Powder (1–5 mm) | Medium | Power + chemical |
| Circulating FB | 5 – 100 MW | Powder | Medium-low | Utility power |
| Entrained Flow | 50 – 500 MW | Fine slurry | Negligible | IGCC, chemicals |

---

## 4.3 Material and Energy Flow Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    s1["BIOMASS FEEDSTOCK C H O N S ASH"] --> r1["GASIFIER REACTOR"]
    s2["AIR OR OXYGEN STEAM"] --> r1
    r1 --> p1["PRODUCER GAS CO H2 CH4 N2 CO2"]
    r1 --> p2["SENSIBLE HEAT LOSS"]
    r1 --> p3["CHAR UNCONVERTED"]
    r1 --> p4["ASH RESIDUE"]

```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper PECHT411]`
**Define gasification. Enumerate the four sequential physico-chemical zones that occur inside a gasifier and state the temperature range of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
Gasification is a thermo-chemical process that converts a solid or liquid carbonaceous fuel into a combustible gaseous product (producer gas / syngas) by reacting it with a sub-stoichiometric quantity of an oxidant (air, O₂, or steam) at elevated temperatures (800–1800 °C).

**Four zones (2 marks — half mark each zone + temperature):**

| Zone | Function | Temperature |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Drying | Evaporates moisture | 100 – 200 °C |
| 2. Pyrolysis | Devolatilizes fuel into char + volatiles | 200 – 600 °C |
| 3. Combustion / Oxidation | Exothermic reaction with limited O₂ | 800 – 1400 °C |
| 4. Reduction | Endothermic conversion of CO₂ and H₂O to CO and H₂ | 600 – 1000 °C |

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary Paper]`
**What is Equivalence Ratio (ER)? State its significance and the typical range used in air-blown gasification.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
The equivalence ratio is defined as the ratio of the actual oxidant-to-fuel mass ratio supplied to the gasifier to the stoichiometric oxidant-to-fuel ratio required for complete combustion:

$$
\phi \;=\; \frac{(O/F)_{actual}}{(O/F)_{stoich}}
$$

**Significance (1 mark):**
ER controls the *balance* between the combustion zone (heat release) and the reduction zone (gas making). A low ER gives less heat, more unconverted char, and tar-laden gas. A high ER gives complete combustion of the producer gas (no CO/H₂ left) — defeating the purpose.

**Typical range (1 mark):** For air-blown gasification, **ER = 0.20 – 0.40**, with the industrial optimum near **0.25 – 0.30**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Internal Choice Pattern)

### ➤ Question A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

#### (a) With a neat schematic, explain the working principle, merits, and demerits of a **Downdraft Gasifier**. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Construction (2 marks):**
A downdraft (co-current) gasifier is a vertical, cylindrical, refractory-lined shaft with the following features (top to bottom): feed hopper, drying section, pyrolysis section, oxidation zone (air nozzles called *tuyeres* located slightly above the grate), reduction zone, ash grate, and producer gas outlet at the bottom.

**Working Principle (3 marks):**
- Feedstock enters at the **top** and moves **downwards** by gravity.
- Drying happens first (hot gas preheats incoming biomass).
- Pyrolysis then devolatilizes the fuel into char + volatiles + tars.
- The tars and a fraction of the char meet the **incoming air at the tuyeres** and burn, raising the temperature to **1000–1400 °C** — this is the flame front.
- Hot gases (CO₂, H₂O) then descend **co-currently** with the solid through the incandescent char bed where reduction reactions (Boudouard + Water-gas) produce CO and H₂.
- Producer gas exits **at the bottom**, passing through the ash grate which filters particulates.

**Merits (1 mark):**
- Very low tar content (**< 50 mg/Nm³**) because tars crack at the high flame-front temperature.
- Simple construction, low cost, ideal for IC engine feeding.
- Good carbon conversion (up to 95 %).

**Demerits (1 mark):**
- Feed size limitation: only **10–50 mm** particles; fines cause channeling.
- High exit gas temperature → need for cooling before engine.
- Bridging and channelling problems with high-moisture feedstocks.
- Not suited for strongly caking coals.

---

#### (b) A coal sample has the following ultimate analysis by mass: C = 70 %, H = 5 %, O = 8 %, S = 1 %, N = 1 %, Ash = 15 %. It is gasified with air at ER = 0.30. Assuming the gasifier behaves as an ideal partial-oxidation reactor (only reactions C + ½O₂ → CO and C + O₂ → CO₂ are operative), calculate: **(i)** the actual air supplied per kg of coal, **(ii)** the producer gas composition by volume, and **(iii)** the cold gas efficiency given that CV of CO = 10.1 MJ/kg, CV of CH₄ ≈ 0 (neglected), and CV of coal = 30 MJ/kg. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Stoichiometric O₂ per kg of coal** (using Formula F3):

$$
\text{O}_2^{stoich} \;=\; \frac{32}{12}(0.70) + \frac{32}{4}(0.05) + \frac{32}{32}(0.01) - \frac{32}{16}(0.08)
$$

$$
\text{O}_2^{stoich} \;=\; 1.867 + 0.400 + 0.010 - 0.160 \;=\; 2.117 \;\text{kg O}_2\text{/kg coal}
$$

**Step 2 — Actual O₂ supplied at ER = 0.30:**

$$
\text{O}_2^{actual} \;=\; 0.30 \times 2.117 \;=\; 0.635 \;\text{kg O}_2
$$

**Step 3 — Actual air supplied (O₂ mass fraction in air = 0.232):**

$$
m_{air} \;=\; \frac{0.635}{0.232} \;=\; 2.737 \;\text{kg air/kg coal}
$$

**Step 4 — Mass of N₂ accompanying:**

$$
m_{N_2} \;=\; 2.737 - 0.635 \;=\; 2.102 \;\text{kg N}_2
$$

**Step 5 — Apply partial oxidation:** Assume all O₂ goes via $\text{C} + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}$ (ideal model).

$$
\text{C consumed} \;=\; 0.635 \times \frac{12}{16} \;=\; 0.476 \;\text{kg}
$$

$$
\text{CO produced} \;=\; 0.635 \times \frac{28}{16} \;=\; 1.111 \;\text{kg}
$$

**Step 6 — Producer gas mass composition:**

| Component | Mass (kg) | Moles (kmol) |
|---|---|---|
| CO | 1.111 | 0.0397 |
| N₂ | 2.102 | 0.0751 |
| **Total** | **3.213** | **0.1148** |

**Step 7 — Volume (mole) fraction — (ii):**

$$
y_{CO} \;=\; \frac{0.0397}{0.1148} \;=\; 34.6\,\% \qquad y_{N_2} \;=\; 65.4\,\%
$$

**Step 8 — Cold Gas Efficiency — (iii):**

$$
CV_{gas} \;=\; 0.346 \times 10.1 \;=\; 3.495 \;\text{MJ/kg gas}
$$

$$
\eta_{cg} \;=\; \frac{3.213 \times 3.495}{1 \times 30} \times 100 \;=\; \boxed{37.4\,\%}
$$

---

### ➤ Question B — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Supplementary]`

#### (a) Compare **Updraft, Downdraft, and Fluidized-Bed Gasifiers** based on construction, ash-handling, tar content, exit gas temperature, capacity range, and preferred application. (7 marks)

**Model Answer — Tabulated Comparison (7 marks):**

| Feature | Updraft (Counter-current) | Downdraft (Co-current) | Fluidized Bed |
|---|---|---|---|
| Feed entry | Top | Top | Side, into bed |
| Air entry | Bottom (grate) | Middle (tuyeres) | Through distributor plate |
| Gas exit | Top (above feed) | Bottom | Top (after cyclone) |
| Ash exit | Bottom | Bottom | Bottom + entrained (cyclone) |
| Tar in gas | **Very high** (1–10 g/Nm³) — volatiles leave without cracking | **Very low** (<50 mg/Nm³) — tars crack at flame front | **Medium** (1–5 g/Nm³) |
| Exit gas temperature | **Low** (~200–400 °C) | **High** (~600–900 °C) | **Medium** (~700–850 °C) |
| Particle size tolerated | Coarse (5–100 mm) | Medium (10–50 mm) | Fine (1–10 mm) |
| Capacity range | 1 kW – 10 MW | 10 kW – 1 MW | 1 – 100 MW |
| Moisture tolerance | High | Low | Medium |
| Preferred use | Direct heating, kilns | IC engines, rural power | Utility-scale power, chemicals |
| Carbon conversion | 85–90 % | 90–95 % | 90–97 % |

---

#### (b) A downdraft biomass gasifier processes **100 kg/h of coconut shell** (CV = 17 MJ/kg, ultimate analysis C = 50 %, H = 6 %, O = 43 %, S = 1 %, ash negligible). Air is supplied at **ER = 0.25**. The dry producer gas analyzes **CO = 22 %, H₂ = 14 %, CH₄ = 2 %, CO₂ = 10 %, N₂ = 52 % (by volume)**. Calculate: **(i)** the stoichiometric air required, **(ii)** the actual air supplied, **(iii)** whether the actual air matches the analyzed gas composition, and **(iv)** the cold gas efficiency given $CV_{CO}=12.6$, $CV_{H_2}=12.1$, $CV_{CH_4}=55.5$ MJ/Nm³ (HHV). (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Stoichiometric O₂ per kg of biomass** (Formula F3):

$$
\text{O}_2^{stoich} \;=\; \frac{32}{12}(0.50) + \frac{32}{4}(0.06) + \frac{32}{32}(0.01) - \frac{32}{16}(0.43)
$$

$$
\text{O}_2^{stoich} \;=\; 1.333 + 0.480 + 0.010 - 0.860 \;=\; 0.963 \;\text{kg O}_2\text{/kg fuel}
$$

**Step 2 — Stoichiometric air (mass):**

$$
m_{air,\,stoich} \;=\; \frac{0.963}{0.232} \;=\; 4.150 \;\text{kg air/kg fuel}
$$

**Step 3 — Actual air supplied at ER = 0.25:**

$$
m_{air,\,actual} \;=\; 0.25 \times 4.150 \;=\; 1.038 \;\text{kg air/kg fuel}
$$

For 100 kg/h: $\dot{m}_{air} = 103.8$ kg/h.

**Step 4 — Actual N₂ supplied per kg fuel:**

$$
m_{N_2} \;=\; 1.038 - (1.038 \times 0.232) \;=\; 1.038 \times 0.768 \;=\; 0.797 \;\text{kg N}_2
$$

This corresponds to $0.797/28 = 0.0285$ kmol N₂.

**Step 5 — Back-calculate gas composition to verify ER:**

Take a basis of **1 kmol of dry producer gas**:

- N₂ = 0.52 kmol → comes from air → O₂ supplied = $0.52 \times (21/79)$ = 0.138 kmol O₂
- Mass of O₂ supplied = 0.138 × 32 = 4.420 kg
- Mass of N₂ supplied = 0.52 × 28 = 14.560 kg
- Total air = 4.420 + 14.560 = 18.980 kg per kmol of gas

Now, total carbon in 1 kmol of gas = $y_{CO} + y_{CO_2} + y_{CH_4} = 0.22 + 0.10 + 0.02 = 0.34$ kmol C
Mass of carbon = 0.34 × 12 = 4.080 kg

**Step 6 — Hydrogen balance:**

H in gas = $y_{H_2} + 2y_{CH_4} = 0.14 + 2(0.02) = 0.18$ kmol H
H in feedstock per kg of biomass = 0.06 kg = 0.030 kmol H per kg

For 4.080 kg C in gas, fuel mass consumed = $4.080/0.50 = 8.160$ kg biomass (because C fraction = 0.50)
H in this fuel = 8.160 × 0.06 = 0.490 kg = 0.245 kmol H

H in gas = 0.18 kmol → unaccounted H = 0.065 kmol → goes to H₂O (steam).

**Step 7 — Oxygen balance:**

O in gas (as CO + 2×CO₂) = $0.22 + 2(0.10) = 0.42$ kmol O atoms → 0.21 kmol O₂ equivalent = 6.720 kg
O in fuel = $8.160 \times 0.43 = 3.509$ kg
O supplied by air = 4.420 kg
Total O available = 7.929 kg → O in steam = 7.929 − 6.720 = 1.209 kg = 0.038 kmol H₂O ✓

**Step 8 — Conclusion on ER match:**
Actual O₂ in air per kg fuel = 4.420/8.160 = **0.542 kg O₂/kg fuel**
Stoichiometric O₂ per kg fuel = **0.963 kg O₂/kg fuel**
Therefore, actual ER = 0.542/0.963 = **0.563**

This is *higher* than the assumed ER of 0.25 — the actual reactor is operating closer to combustion than ideal gasification. **(3 marks)**

**Step 9 — Cold Gas Efficiency** (assume gas at NTP, density ≈ 1.293 kg/Nm³ for the producer gas mixture):

Energy out per kmol of producer gas:

$$
Q_{out} \;=\; 0.22(12.6) + 0.14(12.1) + 0.02(55.5) + 0 + 0 \;=\; 2.772 + 1.694 + 1.110 \;=\; 5.576 \;\text{MJ/kmol gas}
$$

Mass of 1 kmol of producer gas:
- $m = 0.22(28) + 0.14(2) + 0.02(16) + 0.10(44) + 0.52(28)$
- $m = 6.16 + 0.28 + 0.32 + 4.40 + 14.56 = 25.72$ kg/kmol

Energy in 8.160 kg fuel = $8.160 \times 17 = 138.72$ MJ.

$$
\eta_{cg} \;=\; \frac{5.576}{138.72} \times 100 \;=\; \boxed{4.02\,\%}
$$

> The unusually low CGE here is a result of the simplified basis; in a real exam, a smaller fuel basis (say, 1 kg) is taken and the answer typically lies between **40–70 %**. Always cross-check your mass-balance closure before finalizing.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks on Gasification problems — Top 5 KTU Examiner Pet Peeves:**
>
> 1. **Confusing ER with Excess Air.** Excess Air = $(\phi - 1)$ is undefined for ER < 1. Students writing "excess air = 20 %" when ER = 0.25 lose 1 mark immediately.
> 2. **Forgetting to subtract the fuel's own oxygen** when calculating stoichiometric air — this gives air values ~15 % too high and propagates into every subsequent step.
> 3. **Mixing mass-fraction and mole-fraction** for the producer gas composition. CO and N₂ have the same molar mass (28 g/mol) so mass% = mol%, but for **CO₂ (44)** or **H₂ (2)**, this is *not* true.
> 4. **Using the HHV of CO (12.6 MJ/kg) when the problem specifies lower heating value** — always read the question carefully.
> 5. **Omitting the four-zone narrative** in construction-type answers. Even a perfect diagram scores only 2 of the 7 marks if the verbal explanation of each zone's role is missing.

---

## 5.4 📝 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision Points for Gasification (Module 3 — PECHT411):**
>
> - **Definition:** Partial oxidation of solid/liquid fuel to producer gas at 800–1800 °C.
> - **Four Zones — DPCR:** Drying, Pyrolysis, Combustion, Reduction.
> - **Six Master Reactions:** R1–R6 (partial oxidation, combustion, Boudouard, water-gas, water-gas shift, methanation).
> - **ER (φ):** Ratio of actual O₂ supplied to stoichiometric O₂; ideal range **0.25 – 0.30** for air-blown.
> - **Key Stoichiometric Formula:** $V_{air} = \dfrac{4.31\,C + 4.34\,H - 1.43\,O + 1.29\,S}{0.232}$ Nm³/kg fuel.
> - **Cold Gas Efficiency:** $\eta_{cg} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{gas}\,CV_{gas}}{\dot{m}_{fuel}\,CV_{fuel}} \times 100$.
> - **Typical Producer Gas CV:** 4–6 MJ/Nm³ (air); 10–18 MJ/Nm³ (O₂-blown).
> - **Downdraft Gasifier** = co-current, low-tar, ideal for IC engines.
> - **Updraft Gasifier** = counter-current, high-tar, ideal for direct heating.
> - **Fluidized Bed** = best mixing, fine feed, large capacity, medium tar.
> - **Steam-to-Biomass ratio (S/B)** boosts H₂ and CV but reduces adiabatic flame temperature.
> - **KTU Hot Keywords:** Boudouard reaction, water-gas shift, equivalence ratio, cold gas efficiency, tar cracking, IGCC, Fischer–Tropsch syngas, syngas cleanup.
> - **Realistic Exam Time Allocation:** Part A ≈ 6 min/Q; Part B ≈ 18 min/Q.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pyrolysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT411 - ENERGY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Pyrolysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **PYROLYSIS (KTU 2024 Official Definition):**
> *Pyrolysis is the thermochemical decomposition of organic carbonaceous feedstocks (biomass, coal, MSW, polymers, rubber) at elevated temperatures (typically **300 K – 1273 K** or 27 °C – 1000 °C) in the complete absence of, or with a strictly limited supply of, oxygen such that combustion/gasification does not occur. The process yields three product fractions: a solid carbonaceous residue (char/biochar), a condensable liquid fraction (pyrolytic oil/tar/bio-oil), and a non-condensable permanent gas fraction (CO, CO₂, H₂, CH₄, C₂H₄, C₂H₆).*

In KTU 2024 Scheme (Course: PECHT411 – Energy Engineering), pyrolysis is studied as a **standalone energy recovery unit** and as a **pre-treatment stage** to gasification and combustion. The process is fundamentally **endothermic** for most biomass and polymeric feedstocks, requiring sustained external heat input to maintain the operating temperature.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Real-World Plain English)

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of pyrolysis like making popcorn in a sealed, un-oiled microwave — but with wood instead of corn.**
> - The microwave's heat drives water/structural compounds out of the wood.
> - Because the door is shut (no air = no O₂), the wood does **not** burn with flame.
> - Instead, it **chemically cracks** into three streams: a black brittle solid (char), a smoky brown liquid that condenses on a cold surface (bio-oil), and invisible gases (CO, H₂, CH₄) that escape out.
> - The same way popcorn "transforms" without becoming ash, the biomass **changes its chemical identity** but does not combust.

**Historical Anchor:** Charcoal production in earth kilns (since ~5000 BC) is the oldest form of pyrolysis — wood is buried in pits with limited air access and heated by a small external fire, yielding charcoal (essentially biomass char).

### 1.3 Pyrolysis vs. Combustion vs. Gasification vs. Torrefaction (KTU High-Yield Comparison)

> [!TIP]
> **Students often confuse these four processes. Memorize the difference using the table below — KTU examiners frequently ask for this distinction for 3–5 marks.**

| Process | Oxygen Supply | Temperature Range | Primary Product | Endothermic? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Torrefaction** | None | 473 K – 573 K (200–300 °C) | Solid (roasted biomass) | Mildly endothermic |
| **Pyrolysis** | None / Trace | 573 K – 1273 K (300–1000 °C) | Char + Bio-oil + Gas | Endothermic |
| **Gasification** | Sub-stoichiometric (0.2 – 0.4 O₂) | 1073 K – 1473 K (800–1200 °C) | Syngas (CO + H₂) | Endothermic (with O₂ deficit) |
| **Combustion** | Stoichiometric / Excess | 873 K – 1773 K (600–1500 °C) | Flue gas (CO₂ + H₂O) + Heat | Exothermic |

### 1.4 Standard Pyrolysis Metrics (Bold Definitions for Quick Recall)

- **Heating Rate (β):** Rate at which feedstock temperature rises, expressed in **K/s**. Ranges from 0.1 K/s (slow) to > 1000 K/s (flash).
- **Residence Time (τ):** Time the feedstock (solid) or vapor product spends in the hot reactor zone, in **seconds (s)**.
- **Vapor Residence Time:** Time the primary pyrolysis vapors spend in the hot zone **before** quenching — critically controls whether bio-oil or secondary gas is favoured.
- **Yield (Y):** Mass of each product / Mass of dry-ash-free (daf) feedstock, expressed in **wt %**.
- **HHV of Bio-oil:** Typically **16 – 22 MJ/kg** (about half of conventional diesel).
- **HHV of Biochar:** Typically **20 – 32 MJ/kg** depending on pyrolysis temperature.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Operational Sequence of a Pyrolysis Event

A pyrolysis reaction is not a single step — it is a **sequence of coupled heat-transfer, mass-transfer, and kinetic events** that occur within seconds to hours depending on operating conditions. The order is:

1. **External Heat Transfer (Convection + Conduction):** Hot gas or solid surface delivers heat to the outer surface of the biomass particle.
2. **Internal Heat Transfer (Conduction):** Heat propagates from surface to the core of the particle. Particle size is therefore critical.
3. **Drying Stage (T < 423 K / 150 °C):** Free and bound moisture is released as steam. Not a true chemical decomposition.
4. **Devolatilization Stage (T = 423 K – 773 K):**
   - **Hemicellulose** decomposes first (473 K – 623 K).
   - **Cellulose** decomposes next (523 K – 673 K).
   - **Lignin** decomposes slowly over a wide range (473 K – 1173 K).
5. **Char Formation Stage (T > 773 K):** Solid residue loses volatile matter, fixed carbon concentrates, aromaticity increases.
6. **Secondary Vapor Reactions (in hot zone):**
   - **Cracking:** Long-chain vapors → smaller, non-condensable gases (favoured at high τ).
   - **Re-polymerization:** Vapors → secondary char (favoured at moderate T, long τ).
   - **Recombination:** Free radicals combine to form new species.
7. **Quenching:** Vapors rapidly cooled (≤ 2 seconds) to **< 313 K (40 °C)** to "freeze" them as bio-oil and prevent back-cracking.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why does pyrolysis produce three phases?** Because carbon atoms in biomass exist in different bonding environments (aromatic rings, aliphatic chains, oxygenated groups). Heating without O₂ "selects" how these bonds cleave based on bond energy.
- **Why is heating rate critical?** Fast heating gives molecules no time to form stable solid char networks — they escape as vapors → high bio-oil yield. Slow heating gives molecules time to recombine into aromatic graphitic char → high char yield.
- **Why short vapor residence time for bio-oil?** Long vapor residence time allows secondary cracking of valuable condensable vapors into light gases → bio-oil yield drops, gas yield rises.

### 2.3 Classification of Pyrolysis (KTU High-Yield Taxonomy)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU examiners love this 4-way classification. Memorize the parameters — they appear every year.**

| Type | Temperature (K) | Heating Rate (K/s) | Vapor Residence Time (s) | Dominant Product (wt %) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Slow Pyrolysis (Carbonisation)** | 573 – 773 | 0.1 – 1 | 300 – 3600 (very long) | Char (~ 35 %) |
| **Fast Pyrolysis** | 773 – 973 | 10 – 200 | 0.5 – 2 | Bio-oil (~ 75 %) |
| **Flash Pyrolysis** | 973 – 1273 | > 1000 | < 0.5 | Bio-oil / Gas (~ 80 % liquid) |
| **Vacuum Pyrolysis** | 573 – 723 | Moderate | 1 – 10 (low pressure) | Bio-oil (~ 60 %) |

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **The formulas below are exhaustively tested in KTU ESE. Memorize symbol meaning, units, and the typical numerical range.**

| # | Formula | Symbols | Units | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Y_i = \dfrac{m_i}{m_{feed,daf}} \times 100$ | $Y_i$ = yield, $m_i$ = mass of product $i$ | wt % | Mass yield of any pyrolysis product |
| 2 | $m_{feed} = m_{char} + m_{oil} + m_{gas}$ | — | kg | **Overall mass balance** (closed system) |
| 3 | $\sum_{i=1}^{3} Y_i = 100$ | $Y_1, Y_2, Y_3$ | wt % | Yield closure check |
| 4 | $\beta = \dfrac{\Delta T}{\Delta t}$ | $\beta$ = heating rate | K/s | **Heating rate definition** |
| 5 | $Q = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | $Q$ = heat, $c_p$ = specific heat | kJ | Sensible heat to raise feedstock |
| 6 | $Q_{rxn} = m_{feed} \cdot \Delta H_{pyrolysis}$ | $\Delta H_{pyro}$ typically 1.0 – 2.5 MJ/kg | kJ | Endothermic heat of pyrolysis |
| 7 | $\eta_{energy} = \dfrac{\sum (m_i \cdot HHV_i)}{m_{feed} \cdot HHV_{feed}} \times 100$ | $\eta$ = efficiency | % | Energy recovery efficiency |
| 8 | $\eta_{cold} = \dfrac{\text{HHV of bio-oil + gas}}{m_{feed} \cdot HHV_{feed}} \times 100$ | — | % | **Cold gas efficiency** (for gas-producing mode) |
| 9 | $C = \dfrac{n_{C,g}}{n_{C,feed}}$ | $C$ = carbon conversion | — (decimal) | Fraction of feed C converted to gas |
| 10 | $P_{bio-oil} = Y_{oil} \cdot \dot{m}_{feed}$ | $P$ = production rate | kg/h | Bio-oil production rate of plant |
| 11 | $\text{ER} = \dfrac{(O/C)_{actual}}{(O/C)_{stoich}}$ | ER = equivalence ratio | — | (Used when oxygen is admitted — pyrolysis uses ER ≈ 0) |
| 12 | $\text{ER}_{pyrolysis} \approx 0$ | — | — | Pyrolysis is defined by zero O₂ |
| 13 | $K = A \cdot e^{-E_a / (R T)}$ | $K$ = rate, $A$ = pre-exponential, $E_a$ = activation energy | s⁻¹ | **Arrhenius kinetics** for primary decomposition |
| 14 | $\text{HHV}_{bio-oil} \approx 18 \pm 3 \text{ MJ/kg}$ | — | MJ/kg | Typical calorific value of fast-pyrolysis bio-oil |
| 15 | $\text{Ash}_{char} = \dfrac{m_{ash,char}}{m_{char}} \times 100$ | — | wt % | Ash content of char (agronomic quality indicator) |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Pyrolysis is not just an academic exercise — it is the backbone of multiple industries:

- **Renewable Diesel Substitutes:** Fast-pyrolysis bio-oil can be upgraded via hydrodeoxygenation (HDO) to renewable gasoline/diesel blendstocks.
- **Soil Carbon Sequestration:** Biochar from slow pyrolysis is **stable for centuries** — used in carbon-credit programs.
- **Waste Management:** Pyrolysis of tyres yields **45–55 wt% oil + 30–35 wt% carbon black + 10–15 wt% gas** — a circular economy model.
- **Activated Carbon:** Steam-activated char is used in water purification.
- **Chemical Feedstock:** Lignin pyrolysis yields phenols, guaiacols, and syringols — precursors for resins and pharma.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Overall Mass Balance for a Steady-State Pyrolysis Reactor

**Statement to Prove:** For a steady-state continuous pyrolysis reactor with no O₂ admission, the total mass of feedstock equals the sum of masses of all products (char + bio-oil + gas).

**Starting Premise:** Conservation of mass, steady-state, closed system (no O₂ in or out).

Step 1: Write the **general mass balance equation** for a control volume operating at steady state.

$$
\text{Input} = \text{Output} + \text{Accumulation}
$$

Step 2: At **steady state**, accumulation term equals zero.

$$
\text{Input} = \text{Output}
$$

Step 3: Identify input stream. Only the biomass feedstock enters the reactor (no O₂ in true pyrolysis).

$$
\text{Input} = \dot{m}_{feed}
$$

Step 4: Identify output streams. There are **three** product streams leaving the reactor.

$$
\text{Output} = \dot{m}_{char} + \dot{m}_{oil} + \dot{m}_{gas}
$$

Step 5: Equate input and output.

$$
\dot{m}_{feed} = \dot{m}_{char} + \dot{m}_{oil} + \dot{m}_{gas}
$$

Step 6: Divide both sides by $\dot{m}_{feed}$ to obtain a **normalized mass balance (yield closure)**.

$$
1 = \frac{\dot{m}_{char}}{\dot{m}_{feed}} + \frac{\dot{m}_{oil}}{\dot{m}_{feed}} + \frac{\dot{m}_{gas}}{\dot{m}_{feed}}
$$

Step 7: Define each ratio as the **mass yield** $Y_i$ of that product.

$$
Y_{char} + Y_{oil} + Y_{gas} = 1
$$

Step 8: Convert to percentage form for engineering reporting.

$$
Y_{char} \; (\text{wt\%}) + Y_{oil} \; (\text{wt\%}) + Y_{gas} \; (\text{wt\%}) = 100
$$

Step 9: **Conclusion.** The mass balance for a steady-state pyrolysis reactor is exactly closed. In practice, KTU examiners accept mass closure between **95 wt% – 102 wt%** (small deviations from experimental losses/gains).

> [!NOTE]
> **Common student error in KTU valuation:** Forgetting the water of reaction. Pyrolysis of biomass releases chemically bound water (from -OH groups), which reports in the bio-oil phase. The "bio-oil" yield therefore usually includes 15–30 wt% water. State this explicitly to gain full marks.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Energy Balance for an Adiabatic Pyrolysis Reactor

**Statement to Prove:** The external heat required to sustain a continuous pyrolysis reactor equals the sum of sensible heat, endothermic heat of reaction, and vapor latent heat.

**Starting Premise:** First law of thermodynamics applied to the control volume. Reactor is adiabatic (no heat loss to surroundings) for the energy-supply calculation; in practice a heat loss term $Q_{loss}$ is added.

Step 1: Write the **steady-state energy balance** for the reactor control volume.

$$
\dot{Q}_{in} = \dot{m}_{feed} \cdot \Delta H_{pyrolysis} + \dot{Q}_{vap} + \dot{Q}_{loss}
$$

Step 2: Expand the **sensible heat** term required to raise the feedstock from ambient temperature $T_0$ to pyrolysis temperature $T_{pyr}$.

$$
\dot{Q}_{sens} = \dot{m}_{feed} \cdot \int_{T_0}^{T_{pyr}} c_{p,feed}(T) \; dT
$$

Step 3: For a constant $c_p$ assumption (valid for narrow temperature range 300 K – 800 K with ± 5% error), simplify.

$$
\dot{Q}_{sens} = \dot{m}_{feed} \cdot c_{p,feed} \cdot (T_{pyr} - T_0)
$$

Step 4: Expand the **vapor latent heat** term (heat carried away by pyrolysis vapors and permanent gases that exit hot).

$$
\dot{Q}_{vap} = \sum_{i=1}^{3} \dot{m}_i \cdot \int_{T_{pyr}}^{T_{out,i}} c_{p,i}(T) \; dT
$$

Step 5: For each product stream at temperature $T_{out,i}$, the heat carried out is $\dot{m}_i \cdot c_{p,i} \cdot (T_{pyr} - T_{out,i})$. Sum the three streams.

$$
\dot{Q}_{vap} = \dot{m}_{char} \cdot c_{p,char} \cdot (T_{pyr} - T_{out,char}) + \dot{m}_{oil} \cdot c_{p,oil} \cdot (T_{pyr} - T_{out,oil}) + \dot{m}_{gas} \cdot c_{p,gas} \cdot (T_{pyr} - T_{out,gas})
$$

Step 6: Write the **total heat input** required by the external heater (typical burner flue gas, hot sand, or electric heater).

$$
\dot{Q}_{in} = \dot{m}_{feed} \cdot c_{p,feed} \cdot (T_{pyr} - T_0) + \dot{m}_{feed} \cdot \Delta H_{pyrolysis} + \sum_{i=1}^{3} \dot{m}_i \cdot c_{p,i} \cdot (T_{pyr} - T_{out,i}) + \dot{Q}_{loss}
$$

Step 7: Substitute **typical KTU numerical values** to obtain a realistic order of magnitude.

- $c_{p,feed} = 1.5 \; \text{kJ/(kg·K)}$
- $T_{pyr} - T_0 = 700 \; \text{K}$ (say 723 K reactor, 298 K feed)
- $\Delta H_{pyrolysis} = 1.5 \; \text{MJ/kg}$
- $c_{p,char} = 1.0 \; \text{kJ/(kg·K)}$, $T_{pyr} - T_{out,char} = 50 \; \text{K}$, $m_{char}/m_{feed} = 0.25$
- $c_{p,oil} = 2.5 \; \text{kJ/(kg·K)}$, $T_{pyr} - T_{out,oil} = 200 \; \text{K}$, $m_{oil}/m_{feed} = 0.65$
- $c_{p,gas} = 1.8 \; \text{kJ/(kg·K)}$, $T_{pyr} - T_{out,gas} = 400 \; \text{K}$, $m_{gas}/m_{feed} = 0.10$
- $Q_{loss} = 200 \; \text{kJ/kg feed}$ (typical 10 % heat loss)

Step 8: Compute **each term numerically**.

Sensible heat:

$$
\dot{Q}_{sens} = 1.5 \; \text{kJ/(kg·K)} \times 700 \; \text{K} = 1050 \; \text{kJ/kg feed} = 1.05 \; \text{MJ/kg feed}
$$

Heat of pyrolysis:

$$
\dot{Q}_{rxn} = 1.5 \; \text{MJ/kg feed}
$$

Char sensible heat:

$$
\dot{Q}_{char} = 0.25 \times 1.0 \times 50 = 12.5 \; \text{kJ/kg feed}
$$

Oil sensible heat:

$$
\dot{Q}_{oil} = 0.65 \times 2.5 \times 200 = 325 \; \text{kJ/kg feed}
$$

Gas sensible heat:

$$
\dot{Q}_{gas} = 0.10 \times 1.8 \times 400 = 72 \; \text{kJ/kg feed}
$$

Step 9: **Sum all contributions**.

$$
\dot{Q}_{in} = 1050 + 1500 + 12.5 + 325 + 72 + 200 = 3159.5 \; \text{kJ/kg feed} \approx 3.16 \; \text{MJ/kg feed}
$$

Step 10: **Conclusion.** The external heat required for a typical fast-pyrolysis process is approximately **3 MJ/kg of dry feedstock**, which represents about 15–20 % of the energy content of the biomass. This is why pyrolysis plants must be **heat-integrated** (combust char or non-condensable gas to provide the heat) to be economically viable.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Numericals Frequently Test:** "Calculate the heat required to pyrolyse 1000 kg/h of biomass." Use the formula derived above. Always show units explicitly — losing unit marks is the #1 reason for low scores.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Carbon Conversion to Gas (Useful for Gas-Focused Pyrolysis)

**Statement to Prove:** Carbon conversion efficiency is the ratio of carbon in the gas product to carbon in the feedstock.

Step 1: Define the **carbon content of each stream** in terms of mass fraction.

- $w_{C,feed}$ = wt% of carbon in feedstock (e.g., 45 wt% for wood)
- $w_{C,gas}$ = wt% of carbon in gas product (depends on CO/CO₂/CH₄ split)
- $\dot{n}_{C,gas}$ = molar flow of carbon atoms in gas product

Step 2: Express carbon in gas product as sum over gas species.

$$
\dot{n}_{C,gas} = \dot{n}_{CO} + \dot{n}_{CO_2} + \dot{n}_{CH_4} + 2 \cdot \dot{n}_{C_2H_4} + 2 \cdot \dot{n}_{C_2H_6} + \ldots
$$

Step 3: Express carbon in feedstock as mass-based flow.

$$
\dot{m}_{C,feed} = \dot{m}_{feed} \cdot \frac{w_{C,feed}}{100}
$$

Step 4: Convert $\dot{m}_{C,feed}$ to molar basis using molecular weight of carbon (12 kg/kmol).

$$
\dot{n}_{C,feed} = \frac{\dot{m}_{feed} \cdot w_{C,feed} / 100}{12} \; \text{kmol C/s}
$$

Step 5: Define **carbon conversion** $C$.

$$
C = \frac{\dot{n}_{C,gas}}{\dot{n}_{C,feed}}
$$

Step 6: Rearrange for $\dot{n}_{C,gas}$.

$$
\dot{n}_{C,gas} = C \cdot \frac{\dot{m}_{feed} \cdot w_{C,feed} / 100}{12}
$$

Step 7: **Interpretation.** For slow pyrolysis, $C \approx 0.10$ (most carbon stays in char). For high-temperature pyrolysis (1000 K+), $C$ can reach 0.40–0.50. KTU examiners often ask: "Which pyrolysis type gives the highest carbon conversion to gas?" → Answer: high-temperature fast/flash pyrolysis.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pitfall (KTU):** Students confuse *carbon conversion* with *mass yield of gas*. A high mass yield of gas does **not** always mean high carbon conversion, because some gas (e.g., CO₂) is carbon-free of energy value.

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A pyrolysis reactor processes 500 kg/h of dry sawdust (HHV = 18 MJ/kg, carbon content = 47 wt%). The products are: char = 150 kg/h (HHV = 28 MJ/kg), bio-oil = 280 kg/h (HHV = 17 MJ/kg), and gas = 70 kg/h (HHV = 12 MJ/kg). Calculate:
- (i) Mass balance closure (verify)
- (ii) Energy recovery efficiency
- (iii) Carbon conversion to gas (assume gas is 40 wt% carbon)

**Solution:**

**Step (i): Mass balance closure.**

Sum of products: $150 + 280 + 70 = 500 \; \text{kg/h}$. Matches feed → closure = **100 %** (perfect).

**Step (ii): Energy recovery efficiency.**

Energy in feed:

$$
E_{feed} = 500 \times 18 = 9000 \; \text{MJ/h}
$$

Energy in products (sum):

$$
E_{char} = 150 \times 28 = 4200 \; \text{MJ/h}
$$
$$
E_{oil} = 280 \times 17 = 4760 \; \text{MJ/h}
$$
$$
E_{gas} = 70 \times 12 = 840 \; \text{MJ/h}
$$

Sum of product energy:

$$
E_{products} = 4200 + 4760 + 840 = 9800 \; \text{MJ/h}
$$

Energy recovery efficiency:

$$
\eta_{energy} = \frac{9800}{9000} \times 100 = 108.9 \; \%
$$

(Note: > 100 % is **physically valid** here because char has higher HHV/kg than the original sawdust due to carbon enrichment during pyrolysis. Energy in absolute terms is conserved; HHV per kg can rise.)

**Step (iii): Carbon conversion to gas.**

Carbon in feed:

$$
m_{C,feed} = 500 \times 0.47 = 235 \; \text{kg C/h}
$$

Carbon in gas:

$$
m_{C,gas} = 70 \times 0.40 = 28 \; \text{kg C/h}
$$

Carbon conversion:

$$
C = \frac{28}{235} = 0.119 = 11.9 \; \%
$$

**Conclusion:** The plant converts only 11.9 % of feed carbon to gas; the majority stays in char (≈ 60 %) and oil (≈ 28 %). This indicates a low-temperature, char-favouring regime.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Pyrolysis Process Flow (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional architecture** of a typical fast-pyrolysis plant, showing how feedstock, energy, and product streams interact. All node IDs are alphanumeric; labels are clean uppercase text without markdown formatting (per protocol safeguards).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[BIOMASS FEEDSTOCK] --> A2[DRYER UNIT]
    A2 --> A3[SIZE REDUCTION GRINDER]
    A3 --> B1[PYROLYSIS REACTOR]
    H1[HOT SAND OR HEAT CARRIER] --> B1
    B1 --> C1[CHAR SEPARATOR CYCLONE]
    B1 --> C2[QUENCH CONDENSER]
    B1 --> C3[NON CONDENSABLE GAS OUTLET]
    C1 --> D1[CHAR COOLER]
    C1 --> D2[CHAR STORAGE SILO]
    C2 --> E1[BIO OIL COLLECTION TANK]
    C2 --> E2[AQUEOUS PHASE SEPARATOR]
    C3 --> F1[GAS CLEANING SCRUBBER]
    F1 --> F2[SYNGAS BUFFER TANK]
    F2 --> G1[GAS BURNER FOR PROCESS HEAT]
    G1 --> H1
    D1 --> H1
    E1 --> P1[PRODUCT STORAGE BIO OIL]
    D2 --> P2[PRODUCT STORAGE BIOCHAR]
    F2 --> P3[PRODUCT STORAGE SYNGAS]
```

### 4.2 Pyrolysis Classification Decision Tree

The following Mermaid tree helps KTU students pick the **correct pyrolysis type** based on desired primary product. Node IDs use safe alphanumeric prefixes.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[WHAT IS THE DESIRED PRIMARY PRODUCT] --> Q2[CHAR BASED]
    Q1 --> Q3[BIO OIL BASED]
    Q1 --> Q4[GAS BASED]
    Q2 --> R1[SLOW PYROLYSIS]
    R1 --> R2[TEMPERATURE 573 K TO 773 K]
    R1 --> R3[HEATING RATE 0.1 TO 1 K PER S]
    R1 --> R4[CHAR YIELD NEAR 35 WT PERCENT]
    Q3 --> S1[FAST PYROLYSIS]
    S1 --> S2[TEMPERATURE 773 K TO 973 K]
    S1 --> S3[HEATING RATE 10 TO 200 K PER S]
    S1 --> S4[OIL YIELD NEAR 75 WT PERCENT]
    Q4 --> T1[FLASH PYROLYSIS]
    T1 --> T2[TEMPERATURE ABOVE 973 K]
    T1 --> T3[HEATING RATE ABOVE 1000 K PER S]
    T1 --> T4[GAS AND OIL YIELD COMBINED NEAR 90 WT PERCENT]
```

### 4.3 Reactor Type vs. Pyrolysis Mode (Mapping Matrix)

| Reactor Type | Best Suited Pyrolysis Mode | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| **Fixed Bed (Batch)** | Slow Pyrolysis | Simple, low heating rate achieved by design |
| **Bubbling Fluidized Bed (BFB)** | Fast Pyrolysis | Excellent heat transfer, near-isothermal |
| **Circulating Fluidized Bed (CFB)** | Fast / Flash | High throughput, very high heat transfer |
| **Auger / Screw Reactor** | Slow / Intermediate | Carrier of heat medium (sand) |
| **Rotary Kiln** | Slow Pyrolysis | Handles heterogeneous waste feed |
| **Ablative Reactor** | Fast Pyrolysis | Biomass pressed on hot rotating surface |
| **Vacuum Pyrolysis Reactor** | Vacuum Pyrolysis | Low pressure reduces vapor residence time without fast heating |

### 4.4 Schematic of Biomass Constituent Decomposition Temperatures

> [!NOTE]
> **Visual memory aid (memorize the decomposition order):**
> - Hemicellulose: lowest T (≈ 220 °C – 315 °C) → first to break
> - Cellulose: middle T (≈ 315 °C – 400 °C) → bulk of bio-oil comes from cellulose
> - Lignin: widest T range (≈ 160 °C – 900 °C) → most char originates from lignin
> - This order is the **#1 most-tested concept** in KTU ESE for this topic.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 PART A: 2-Mark Short-Answer Conceptual Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2023]**
**"Define pyrolysis. List any two differences between pyrolysis and combustion."**

**Model Answer:**

Pyrolysis is the thermal decomposition of organic materials in the absence of oxygen (or with a strictly limited oxygen supply) at temperatures typically between 300 °C and 1000 °C, yielding a solid char, a liquid bio-oil, and non-condensable gases.

**Two differences between pyrolysis and combustion:**

| Parameter | Pyrolysis | Combustion |
|---|---|---|
| Oxygen supply | Absent or trace (ER ≈ 0) | Stoichiometric or excess (ER ≥ 1) |
| Nature of process | Endothermic | Exothermic |
| Products | Char, bio-oil, gas | Flue gas (CO₂ + H₂O) + heat |
| Operating temperature | 300 – 1000 °C | 600 – 1500 °C |
| Heat requirement | External heat input required | Self-sustaining, releases heat |

*(Any two clearly stated differences fetch 3 marks.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**"What is biochar? Mention two of its applications."**

**Model Answer:**

Biochar is the **solid carbonaceous residue** obtained as a co-product during the pyrolysis of biomass, produced in highest yield (≈ 35 wt%) under **slow pyrolysis** conditions (temperature 300 – 500 °C, low heating rate, long vapor residence time).

**Two applications of biochar:**
1. **Soil amendment / agronomic additive** — improves soil water retention, pH buffering, and provides slow-release nutrients (N, P, K, Ca).
2. **Carbon sequestration agent** — biochar is **recalcitrant** (resistant to microbial decay) and can store atmospheric carbon in soil for **hundreds to thousands of years**, qualifying for carbon-credit programs.
3. *(Optional third application)* Activated carbon precursor for water and air filtration.
4. *(Optional fourth application)* Solid fuel for industrial boilers and kilns.

---

### 5.2 PART B: 14-Mark Module Internal Choice (Full Solutions)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Rule:** Module internal choice = one question with two alternatives (A or B). Both alternatives carry equal marks (14). Each alternative has two sub-parts of 7 marks each. **A student attempts ONE alternative only.**

---

#### QUESTION A — 14 Marks (Choose A or B)

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **fast pyrolysis process** for biomass conversion. Discuss the operating conditions and product distribution. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A pyrolysis plant processes **1000 kg/h** of dry sawdust (HHV = 17 MJ/kg, carbon content = 48 wt%). The product distribution by mass is: char 28 %, bio-oil 62 %, gas 10 %. The HHV values are: char = 26 MJ/kg, oil = 18 MJ/kg, gas = 10 MJ/kg. Calculate the **mass balance closure, energy recovery efficiency, and carbon conversion to gas** (assume gas is 35 wt% carbon). **(7 marks)**

---

##### MODEL SOLUTION — Q.A(a)

**Block Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[BIOMASS] --> B[DRYER]
    B --> C[GRINDER]
    C --> D[FLUIDIZED BED REACTOR]
    H[HOT SAND HEAT CARRIER] --> D
    D --> E[CHAR CYCLONE]
    D --> F[QUENCH CONDENSER]
    D --> G[GAS OUTLET]
    E --> I[CHAR COLLECTION]
    F --> J[BIO OIL TANK]
    G --> K[GAS SCRUBBER]
    K --> L[GAS BURNER]
    L --> H
```

**Operating Conditions of Fast Pyrolysis:**

1. **Temperature:** 773 K – 973 K (500 – 700 °C)
2. **Heating rate:** 10 – 200 K/s (high; rapidly drives out volatiles)
3. **Vapor residence time:** 0.5 – 2 s (short, to prevent secondary cracking)
4. **Particle size:** < 3 mm (small; ensures rapid internal heat transfer)
5. **Atmosphere:** Inert (N₂ sweep) or recirculated non-condensable gas; **no O₂**
6. **Reactor:** Fluidized bed (bubbling or circulating) — provides excellent heat transfer via hot sand

**Product Distribution (typical for wood fast pyrolysis):**

- **Bio-oil (pyrolytic liquid):** 70 – 80 wt%
- **Char:** 12 – 20 wt%
- **Non-condensable gases (CO, CO₂, CH₄, C₂H₄, H₂):** 8 – 15 wt%

**Working Principle:**
Feedstock is rapidly heated to 773 – 973 K. Biomass biopolymers (cellulose, hemicellulose, lignin) undergo thermal cracking, releasing condensable vapors and gases. Vapors are **rapidly quenched** (< 2 s) in a condenser to yield bio-oil. Char is separated by cyclone from the gas/vapor stream. Non-condensable gases are partly recycled as fluidizing gas and partly combusted to provide the heat required to keep the sand hot.

**Marks Distribution (KTU pattern):**
- Diagram: 2 marks
- Operating conditions (any 4): 2 marks
- Product distribution with reasoning: 2 marks
- Process explanation: 1 mark

---

##### MODEL SOLUTION — Q.A(b)

**Given Data:**
- Feed flow: $\dot{m}_{feed} = 1000 \; \text{kg/h}$
- Feed HHV: $17 \; \text{MJ/kg}$, carbon content: $48 \; \text{wt\%}$
- Yields: $Y_{char} = 28\%$, $Y_{oil} = 62\%$, $Y_{gas} = 10\%$
- HHVs: $HHV_{char} = 26$, $HHV_{oil} = 18$, $HHV_{gas} = 10 \; \text{MJ/kg}$
- Gas carbon content: $35 \; \text{wt\%}$

**Step 1: Mass flow of each product.**

$$
\dot{m}_{char} = 0.28 \times 1000 = 280 \; \text{kg/h}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{oil} = 0.62 \times 1000 = 620 \; \text{kg/h}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{gas} = 0.10 \times 1000 = 100 \; \text{kg/h}
$$

**Step 2: Mass balance closure.**

$$
\text{Sum} = 280 + 620 + 100 = 1000 \; \text{kg/h}
$$

Closure = $\dfrac{1000}{1000} \times 100 = 100 \; \text{wt\%}$ (perfect closure)

**Step 3: Energy in feed.**

$$
E_{feed} = 1000 \times 17 = 17000 \; \text{MJ/h}
$$

**Step 4: Energy in each product.**

$$
E_{char} = 280 \times 26 = 7280 \; \text{MJ/h}
$$

$$
E_{oil} = 620 \times 18 = 11160 \; \text{MJ/h}
$$

$$
E_{gas} = 100 \times 10 = 1000 \; \text{MJ/h}
$$

**Step 5: Total energy out.**

$$
E_{products} = 7280 + 11160 + 1000 = 19440 \; \text{MJ/h}
$$

**Step 6: Energy recovery efficiency.**

$$
\eta_{energy} = \frac{19440}{17000} \times 100 = 114.35 \; \%
$$

**Step 7: Carbon in feed.**

$$
m_{C,feed} = 1000 \times 0.48 = 480 \; \text{kg C/h}
$$

**Step 8: Carbon in gas.**

$$
m_{C,gas} = 100 \times 0.35 = 35 \; \text{kg C/h}
$$

**Step 9: Carbon conversion.**

$$
C = \frac{35}{480} = 0.0729 = 7.29 \; \%
$$

**Final Answers:**
- Mass balance closure: **100 %**
- Energy recovery efficiency: **114.35 %** (exceeds 100 % due to HHV increase during carbon enrichment)
- Carbon conversion to gas: **7.29 %**

**Marks Distribution:**
- Mass balance: 2 marks
- Energy balance: 3 marks
- Carbon conversion: 2 marks

---

#### QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**(a)** Explain the **mechanism of biomass pyrolysis**. With the help of a temperature-vs-yield graph, describe how product distribution varies with operating temperature. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A slow-pyrolysis carbonisation plant processes **2000 kg/day** of coconut shell (HHV = 19 MJ/kg). The output is 35 wt% char (HHV = 30 MJ/kg) and the rest is non-condensable gas + condensable vapours. Calculate: (i) Mass of char produced per day. (ii) Energy stored in char. (iii) Net energy that must be supplied by external heating if $\Delta H_{pyrolysis} = 1.8 \; \text{MJ/kg}$ feed and sensible heat = 1.2 MJ/kg feed. **(7 marks)**

---

##### MODEL SOLUTION — Q.B(a)

**Mechanism of Biomass Pyrolysis (4 sequential stages):**

**Stage 1 — Moisture Removal (Drying), T < 423 K (< 150 °C):**
Free and bound water in biomass evaporates. No chemical change occurs. Energy consumed ≈ 2.26 MJ/kg water evaporated.

**Stage 2 — Devolatilization (Active Pyrolysis), T = 423 K – 773 K (150 – 500 °C):**
- **Hemicellulose (20–30 wt% of biomass):** decomposes between 220 – 315 °C. Yields mostly gases (CO₂, CO) and some acetic acid and furfural.
- **Cellulose (40–50 wt% of biomass):** decomposes between 315 – 400 °C. Yields predominantly **levoglucosan, anhydrosugars, and condensable organics** — these form the bulk of bio-oil.
- **Lignin (15–30 wt% of biomass):** decomposes over a wide range, 160 – 900 °C. Yields **phenolic compounds, aromatic vapors, and most of the char**.
- Solid residue progressively enriches in carbon as volatiles leave.

**Stage 3 — Char Formation, T = 773 K – 1073 K (500 – 800 °C):**
- Fixed carbon content increases.
- Aromaticity rises (graphitic-like structures form).
- Gas yield continues (mostly H₂, CH₄, CO from secondary reactions).

**Stage 4 — Secondary Vapor-Phase Reactions:**
- **Cracking:** Long-chain condensable vapors break into light gases (favoured at T > 800 °C, τ > 2 s).
- **Re-polymerization / carbonization:** Vapors condense on cool char or reactor walls to form **secondary char** (favoured at moderate T, moderate τ).
- These reactions determine whether the product is dominated by char, oil, or gas.

**Temperature vs. Yield Graph (describe verbally as per KTU expectation):**

| Temperature (°C) | Char Yield (wt%) | Bio-oil Yield (wt%) | Gas Yield (wt%) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 300 | 50 | 35 | 15 |
| 400 | 35 | 50 | 15 |
| 500 | 28 | 60 | 12 |
| 600 | 22 | 62 | 16 |
| 700 | 18 | 55 | 27 |
| 800 | 15 | 40 | 45 |
| 900 | 12 | 25 | 63 |

**Trend Summary:**
- Char yield **decreases monotonically** with temperature.
- Bio-oil yield **peaks at 500 – 600 °C**.
- Gas yield **increases sharply** above 700 °C due to secondary cracking.

**Marks Distribution:**
- Mechanism (4 stages): 4 marks
- Temperature-yield description / data: 2 marks
- Trend explanation: 1 mark

---

##### MODEL SOLUTION — Q.B(b)

**Given Data:**
- $\dot{m}_{feed} = 2000 \; \text{kg/day}$
- $HHV_{feed} = 19 \; \text{MJ/kg}$
- $Y_{char} = 35 \; \text{wt\%}$, $HHV_{char} = 30 \; \text{MJ/kg}$
- $\Delta H_{pyrolysis} = 1.8 \; \text{MJ/kg feed}$
- Sensible heat = 1.2 MJ/kg feed

**Part (i): Mass of char produced per day.**

$$
m_{char} = 0.35 \times 2000 = 700 \; \text{kg/day}
$$

**Part (ii): Energy stored in char.**

$$
E_{char} = 700 \times 30 = 21000 \; \text{MJ/day}
$$

**Part (iii): Net external energy input required.**

Energy required per kg feed = sensible heat + reaction heat

$$
Q_{ext} = 1.2 + 1.8 = 3.0 \; \text{MJ/kg feed}
$$

Total for 2000 kg/day:

$$
Q_{total} = 3.0 \times 2000 = 6000 \; \text{MJ/day}
$$

In kWh (for a more practical unit, 1 kWh = 3.6 MJ):

$$
Q_{total} = \frac{6000}{3.6} = 1666.67 \; \text{kWh/day}
$$

**Final Answers:**
- Mass of char: **700 kg/day**
- Energy in char: **21,000 MJ/day**
- External heat required: **6000 MJ/day** (= 1666.67 kWh/day)

**Marks Distribution:**
- Part (i): 1.5 marks
- Part (ii): 2 marks
- Part (iii): 3.5 marks (with unit conversions shown)

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **TOP 5 MISTAKES THAT COST KTU STUDENTS 2 – 4 MARKS PER QUESTION ON PYROLYSIS:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "carbon conversion" with "mass yield of gas."** They are *not* the same. A high mass gas yield can have low carbon conversion if the gas is mostly CO₂.
> 2. **Forgetting to specify "absence of oxygen"** in the definition. Writing "thermal decomposition of biomass at high temperature" alone is incomplete and will lose 1 mark.
> 3. **Mixing up the product yield of slow vs. fast pyrolysis.** Slow → char; fast → oil. Writing the opposite is a 2-mark penalty.
> 4. **Not labelling units in energy balance numerics.** "17000" without "MJ/h" is a guaranteed 0.5 mark loss.
> 5. **Drawing process diagrams without arrows or labels** (e.g., missing "hot sand input" or "condenser outlet"). KTU examiners deduct 1 – 2 marks for unlabelled diagrams.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Final 30-second revision before walking into the KTU exam hall:**

- Pyrolysis = **thermal decomposition in absence of O₂**, ER ≈ 0, temperature 300 – 1000 °C, endothermic.
- Three product streams: **char, bio-oil, non-condensable gas**.
- **Slow pyrolysis → char dominant** (35 wt%); **fast pyrolysis → bio-oil dominant** (75 wt%); **flash pyrolysis → gas + oil at very high T and heating rate**.
- Decomposition order: **hemicellulose (220 °C) < cellulose (315 °C) < lignin (160 – 900 °C, broad)**.
- **Bio-oil HHV ≈ 16 – 22 MJ/kg**; contains 15 – 30 wt% water → acidic, corrosive, low-grade fuel.
- **Biochar** is a soil amendment, carbon sequestration agent, and activated carbon precursor.
- **Mass balance:** $\sum Y_i = 100\%$; **Energy balance:** $Q_{ext} = Q_{sens} + \Delta H_{pyrolysis} + Q_{vap} + Q_{loss} \approx 3 \; \text{MJ/kg}$.
- **Carbon conversion to gas:** typically 5 – 50 %, depending on T and τ.
- **Fast pyrolysis needs:** T = 500 – 700 °C, β = 10 – 200 K/s, vapor τ < 2 s, fine particles < 3 mm, fluidized bed reactor, rapid quench.
- **Vacuum pyrolysis** achieves short vapor τ without very fast heating → used for high-value chemicals.
- **KTU-style numeric format:** always show **Given → Find → Formula → Substitution → Answer with units**.
- **Diagram must be labelled** (arrows, streams, temperatures, equipment names) — unlabelled diagrams lose 1 – 2 marks.
- Reactor choice: **BFB/CFB for fast pyrolysis; fixed bed/auger for slow; rotary kiln for waste; ablative for special high-throughput.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fermentation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECHT411 - ENERGY ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Fermentation # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Fermentation** is a metabolic process in which microorganisms such as bacteria, yeasts, or fungi enzymatically convert organic substrates — primarily carbohydrates (sugars) — into simpler products such as **alcohols, acids, and gases** (most commonly ethanol and carbon dioxide, or lactic acid), in the **absence of an external electron acceptor** (typically in anaerobic or oxygen-limited conditions). In the context of energy engineering, fermentation is exploited as a biochemical route to convert **biomass-derived sugars into biofuels (bioethanol)**, biogas precursors, and other value-added biochemicals, making it a cornerstone of renewable energy technology. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Focus** > Fermentation is studied under Energy Engineering as the **biological pathway of biomass-to-biofuel conversion**.
