**Step 2 (1 mark):** When electrodes are connected to a load, a current $\vec{J}$ flows. By the generalized Ohm's law:

$$
\vec{J} = \sigma(\vec{E}_{ind} - \vec{E}_{L})
$$

where $\vec{E}_{L}$ is the electric field opposing the induced EMF due to the load.

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Define the **load factor**:

$$
K = \dfrac{E_{L}}{vB} \quad\Rightarrow\quad E_{L} = KvB
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Substituting:

$$
J = \sigma vB(1-K)
$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** The useful electrical power extracted *per unit volume* is the dot product of current density and the load electric field:

$$
P_{vol} = \vec{J} \cdot \vec{E}_{L} = \sigma vB(1-K) \cdot KvB
$$

$$
\boxed{P_{vol} = \sigma v^{2} B^{2} K(1-K)}
$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** To find the maximum, differentiate with respect to $K$:

$$
\dfrac{dP_{vol}}{dK} = \sigma v^{2} B^{2} \left[(1-K) - K\right] = \sigma v^{2} B^{2}(1 - 2K)
$$

Setting $\dfrac{dP_{vol}}{dK} = 0$:

$$
1 - 2K = 0 \quad\Rightarrow\quad K_{max} = 0.5
$$

**Step 7 (1 mark):** Confirm it's a maximum using the second derivative:

$$
\dfrac{d^{2}P_{vol}}{dK^{2}} = -2\sigma v^{2} B^{2} < 0
$$

Hence the function is concave and $K = 0.5$ gives the maximum:

$$
P_{vol,max} = \sigma v^{2} B^{2} \times 0.5 \times 0.5 = \dfrac{\sigma v^{2} B^{2}}{4}
$$

$$
\boxed{P_{vol,max} = \dfrac{1}{4}\sigma v^{2} B^{2}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Derivation Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Skipping the load factor definition** — examiners deduct 2 marks if $K$ is used without definition. Always write "Let $K = E_L / vB$" first.
> 2. **Not verifying the maximum** with a second-derivative test — 1 mark lost. Alternatively, completing the square works: $K(1-K) = \tfrac{1}{4} - (K-\tfrac{1}{2})^2 \le \tfrac{1}{4}$.
> 3. **Forgetting to mention units** — even in derivations, examiners want to see that $\sigma v^2 B^2$ has units of W/m³.
> 4. **Confusing $P_{vol}$ with total power** — clearly state "per unit volume" in the formula.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Board Revision — Memorize These Points:**

✅ **MHD = Direct Heat-to-Electricity Conversion** using a conducting fluid + magnetic field. No turbines, no rotors in the conversion zone.

✅ **Two systems:** **Open cycle** (combustion gas, T₁ ~ 2800 K, 50–60% efficient with bottoming) and **Closed cycle** (inert gas + external heat, T₁ ~ 1800 K, 45–55% efficient).

✅ **Faraday EMF** (memorize): $\mathcal{E} = vBd$ — derived from Lorentz force balance.

✅ **Volume Power Density** (memorize): $P_{vol} = \sigma v^2 B^2 K(1-K)$. **Maximum at K = 0.5** giving $P_{vol,max} = \tfrac{\sigma v^2 B^2}{4}$.

✅ **Hall parameter** $\beta = \dfrac{eB\tau}{m_e} = \dfrac{B\sigma}{n_e e}$ — must be minimized via **electrode segmentation** or diagonal-wall design.

✅ **Seeding** with **K₂CO₃ or Cs** is essential to raise σ from $10^{-6}$ to $10$–$100$ S/m.

✅ **Magnetic field** must be **4–6 Tesla** → requires **superconducting magnets** (closed cycle needs even higher B to compensate for lower σ).

✅ **Topping + Bottoming** combination gives the highest efficiency: $\eta_{overall} = \eta_{MHD} + (1-\eta_{MHD})\eta_{bottoming}$.

✅ **Key challenges:** electrode erosion (>2300 K), seed recovery (>95%), Hall effect suppression, magnet cost, NOx control (open cycle).

✅ **Historical milestones to mention in descriptive answers:** U-25 plant (USSR, 25 MW, 1971–1980s), AVCO Mark V (USA), BARC 5 MW test rig (India).

✅ **Conversion sequence to remember for diagrams:**
- Open: **Air → Compress → Preheat → Combust + Seed → Nozzle → MHD Channel → Heat Recovery → Steam Turbine → Inverter → Grid**
- Closed: **Heat Source → Heater → Seed & Ionize → Regenerator → Nozzle → MHD Channel → Regenerator → Diffuser → Compressor → Recycle**

✅ **Units to cross-check in every numerical:** $[\sigma] = $ S/m, $[v] = $ m/s, $[B] = $ T, $[d] = $ m, $[P_{vol}] = $ W/m³, $[\beta] = $ dimensionless.

✅ **For KTU numericals:** Always state *given data*, write the *formula*, substitute *with units*, and box the *final answer*. Examiners allocate 1 mark for the formula itself.

✅ **Comparing Open vs. Closed in 1 line:** *Open = fossil + combustion + high T₁; Closed = nuclear/solar + clean gas + high B.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Alkaline Fuel Cell
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT411 - ENERGY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Alkaline Fuel Cell

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 4 — Alkaline Fuel Cell (AFC)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Alkaline Fuel Cell (AFC):** A low-temperature fuel cell (operating typically between **60 °C and 90 °C**) that employs an **aqueous potassium hydroxide (KOH) electrolyte** (concentration ranging from **30 wt% to 45 wt%**) to generate electricity via the electrochemical reaction of **hydrogen (H₂) fuel** and **oxygen (O₂) oxidant**, with **water (H₂O)** and **heat** as the sole by-products. AFCs are the first fuel cell technology ever developed and used in practical applications (notably by NASA in the Apollo and Space Shuttle programs).

The AFC belongs to the family of **Polymer Electrolyte Membrane / Liquid Electrolyte Fuel Cells** and is classified under **Type I Fuel Cells** as per the KTU 2024 Energy Engineering classification (Module 4: Fuel Cell Technologies). The cell uses a **liquid alkaline electrolyte** (KOH solution) which conducts **hydroxyl ions (OH⁻)** from the **cathode** to the **anode** — the reverse ion flow compared to PEMFCs.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Bicarbonate Baking Soda Reaction":**
> Imagine you have a tank of pure hydrogen gas on one side and pure oxygen on the other, separated by a saltwater (alkaline) bath that only lets "negatively charged OH⁻ ions" swim through. When you connect the two gas chambers with a wire, electrons from the hydrogen side have to *travel all the way through the wire* (because the alkaline bath won't let them through) to meet the oxygen on the other side. This forced detour of electrons **is** the electric current powering your bulb! The "ash" produced is nothing but clean water — exactly the same as when hydrogen burns, except here we harvest the energy as electricity *before* the flame ever forms.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Faraday's Constant (F):** $\mathbf{F = 96{,}485 \ C \cdot mol^{-1}}$
- **Universal Gas Constant (R):** $\mathbf{R = 8.314 \ J \cdot mol^{-1} \cdot K^{-1}}$
- **Standard Temperature & Pressure (STP):** $\mathbf{T = 298.15 \ K, \ P = 1 \ atm}$
- **Standard Cell Potential of H₂/O₂ reaction:** $\mathbf{E^0 = 1.229 \ V}$
- **Typical Operating Temperature:** **60–90 °C** (low-temperature)
- **Electrical Efficiency:** **50%–70%** (electrical, HHV basis)
- **Combined Heat and Power (CHP) Efficiency:** **up to 80%–85%**
- **Electrolyte:** KOH solution, **30–45 wt%**
- **Ion Conductor in Electrolyte:** Hydroxyl ion $\mathbf{OH^-}$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4, Unit 4.3):** AFCs are highlighted for their **fast ORR (Oxygen Reduction Reaction) kinetics in alkaline medium** — the most critical reason why AFCs can use **non-precious metal catalysts (e.g., silver, nickel)** instead of expensive platinum, giving them a major cost advantage.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

The AFC operates on the principle of **reverse electrolysis** in an alkaline medium. Below is the structured operational flow:

**Step 1 — Fuel Delivery (Anode Side):**
- Gaseous **Hydrogen (H₂)** is fed to the **anode** compartment.
- The H₂ molecules come into contact with the **anode catalyst** (typically porous nickel or platinum-supported carbon).

**Step 2 — Hydrogen Oxidation Reaction (HOR) at the Anode:**
- H₂ reacts with **OH⁻ ions** from the electrolyte to release electrons and produce water.
- Reaction: $\mathrm{H_2 + 2OH^- \rightarrow 2H_2O + 2e^-}$

**Step 3 — Ion Migration Through Electrolyte:**
- The produced **hydroxyl ions (OH⁻)** carry negative charge **from the cathode toward the anode** through the alkaline KOH electrolyte (opposite direction to PEMFCs which use H⁺).
- KOH concentration must be maintained uniformly to ensure high ionic conductivity.

**Step 4 — Electron Flow Through External Circuit (Useful Work):**
- The **electrons (e⁻)** cannot pass through the electrolyte.
- They are forced to travel through the **external circuit**, producing a **direct current (DC)** usable for any load.
- This is the **useful electrical work output**.

**Step 5 — Oxygen Reduction Reaction (ORR) at the Cathode:**
- Gaseous **Oxygen (O₂)** is fed to the **cathode** compartment.
- O₂ combines with H₂O (from the electrolyte) and the incoming electrons to regenerate OH⁻ ions.
- Reaction: $\mathrm{\tfrac{1}{2}O_2 + H_2O + 2e^- \rightarrow 2OH^-}$

**Step 6 — Water & Heat Management:**
- The net product is **liquid water (H₂O)** at the anode and **heat**.
- Water must be actively removed to prevent **flooding** of electrodes.
- Operating temperature is kept stable via **heat exchangers**.

## 2.2 Complete Cell Reaction (Net Reaction)

Combining the anode and cathode half-reactions:

$$
\mathrm{H_2 \; + \; \tfrac{1}{2} O_2 \;\longrightarrow\; H_2O \; + \; Electrical \; Energy \; + \; Heat}
$$

The thermodynamic **lower heating value (LHV)** of this reaction is **$\Delta H_{LHV} = -237.13 \ kJ/mol$** and the higher heating value (HHV) is **$\Delta H_{HHV} = -285.83 \ kJ/mol$**.

## 2.3 Why Alkaline Medium? — The Core Engineering Rationale

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - The **Oxygen Reduction Reaction (ORR)** is notoriously sluggish (high overpotential) in acidic media, requiring large amounts of platinum catalyst.
> - In **alkaline media**, ORR kinetics are **~100× faster**, allowing the use of inexpensive **silver (Ag)**, **nickel (Ni)**, or **manganese (Mn)** catalysts.
> - This is the **single biggest commercial advantage** of AFCs over PEMFCs.
> - Drawback: AFCs are **highly sensitive to CO₂ contamination**, which reacts with KOH to form **K₂CO₃ (potassium carbonate)**, poisoning the electrolyte.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Description** |
|---|---|---|
| Standard Cell Voltage | $E^0 = 1.229 \ V$ | H₂/O₂ at STP, liquid water product |
| Thermodynamic Voltage (HHV) | $E^0_{HHV} = 1.481 \ V$ | Theoretical max if HHV used |
| Nernst Equation (AFC) | $E = E^0 + \dfrac{RT}{nF} \ln \left( \dfrac{P_{H_2} \cdot P_{O_2}^{1/2}}{P_{H_2O}} \right)$ | Operating cell voltage |
| Gibbs Free Energy | $\Delta G = -nFE$ | Electrical work per mole |
| Enthalpy of Reaction | $\Delta H = -237.13 \ kJ/mol$ (LHV) | Heat of combustion at 25°C |
| Electrical Efficiency (HHV) | $\eta_{elec} = \dfrac{\Delta G}{\Delta H_{HHV}} = \dfrac{1.229}{1.481} = 83\%$ | Max theoretical |
| Practical Electrical Efficiency | $\eta_{actual} = 50\% - 70\%$ | Real cell operation |
| Current Density Relation | $i = \dfrac{I}{A}$ (A/m²) | Current per unit electrode area |
| Power Output | $P = V_{cell} \times I$ (W) | Electrical power |
| Voltage Losses | $V_{cell} = E_{Nernst} - \eta_{act} - \eta_{ohm} - \eta_{conc}$ | Actual operating voltage |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Application Note:** AFCs have been deployed in **NASA Apollo missions (1960s)**, **Space Shuttle Orbiter (1981–2011)**, **Soviet Union Buran program**, and modern stationary backup power units for telecom. They are also being explored for **military applications** and **forklift power** where their fast startup is advantageous.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Nernst Equation for the AFC

### 3.1.1 Starting Point: General Electrochemical Cell Reaction

The generic cell reaction is:
$$
\mathrm{aA + bB \rightarrow cC + dD}
$$

For the AFC, identifying species from the **Net Cell Reaction**:

$$
\mathrm{H_2 \; + \; \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \;\rightarrow\; H_2O_{(l)}}
$$

- Reactants: $H_2$ and $O_2$
- Product: $H_2O$ (liquid)
- Number of electrons transferred: $n = 2$ (per H₂ molecule)

### 3.1.2 Reaction Quotient (Q)

By definition of reaction quotient:
$$
Q = \dfrac{a_{H_2O}}{P_{H_2} \cdot P_{O_2}^{1/2}}
$$

Since pure liquid water has activity $a_{H_2O} = 1$, the Nernst expression becomes:
$$
Q = \dfrac{1}{P_{H_2} \cdot P_{O_2}^{1/2}}
$$

### 3.1.3 Applying the Nernst Equation

The **Nernst Equation** for cell voltage under non-standard conditions:
$$
E = E^0 - \dfrac{RT}{nF} \ln Q
$$

Substituting $Q$ and noting that $1/Q = P_{H_2} \cdot P_{O_2}^{1/2}$:
$$
E = E^0 - \dfrac{RT}{nF} \ln \left( \dfrac{1}{P_{H_2} \cdot P_{O_2}^{1/2}} \right)
$$

Therefore the final operating voltage is:
$$
E = E^0 + \dfrac{RT}{nF} \ln \left( P_{H_2} \cdot P_{O_2}^{1/2} \right)
$$

### 3.1.4 Numerical Verification at Standard Conditions

At STP ($T = 298.15 \, K$), with $P_{H_2} = 1 \, atm$, $P_{O_2} = 1 \, atm$, $n = 2$, $E^0 = 1.229 \, V$:

$$
E = 1.229 + \dfrac{8.314 \times 298.15}{2 \times 96485} \ln(1 \cdot 1^{1/2})
$$

$$
E = 1.229 + \dfrac{2478.8}{192970} \ln(1)
$$

$$
E = 1.229 + 0 \quad \text{(since } \ln(1) = 0\text{)}
$$

$$
\boxed{E = 1.229 \, V \quad \text{(Verified)}}
$$

> This confirms that under standard conditions, the Nernst equation correctly reduces to the standard cell potential.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Thermodynamic Efficiency (HHV Basis)

### 3.2.1 Definition

The maximum thermodynamic (reversible) efficiency of a fuel cell is the ratio of the **Gibbs free energy change** to the **enthalpy change** of the reaction.

$$
\eta_{th} = \dfrac{\Delta G}{\Delta H}
$$

### 3.2.2 Computing $\Delta G$ from Cell Voltage

By electrochemical definition:
$$
\Delta G = -nFE
$$

For AFC at standard conditions:
$$
\Delta G = -2 \times 96485 \times 1.229
$$

$$
\Delta G = -237{,}176 \, J \cdot mol^{-1} = -237.18 \, kJ/mol
$$

### 3.2.3 Computing $\Delta H$ (HHV)

The higher heating value (HHV) for H₂ combustion is:
$$
\Delta H_{HHV} = -285.83 \, kJ/mol
$$

### 3.2.4 Efficiency Calculation

$$
\eta_{th, HHV} = \dfrac{237.18}{285.83} = 0.8298
$$

$$
\boxed{\eta_{th, HHV} \approx 82.98\% \approx 83\%}
$$

This is the **theoretical maximum electrical efficiency** of an AFC. The remaining **~17%** is unavoidably released as heat even under ideal (reversible) conditions due to entropy generation.

### 3.2.5 Comparison with Carnot Efficiency (Justification of Fuel Cell Superiority)

For a heat engine operating between $T_{hot} = 900 \, K$ and $T_{cold} = 300 \, K$:

$$
\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \dfrac{T_{cold}}{T_{hot}} = 1 - \dfrac{300}{900} = 66.7\%
$$

This proves AFCs are **not Carnot-limited** and can theoretically exceed the efficiency of any heat engine at the same temperature.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Operating Cell Voltage Under Real Conditions

### 3.3.1 Stack Pressure Effect

Suppose the AFC operates at $T = 353 \, K$ ($80^\circ C$, typical for AFC) with $P_{H_2} = 3 \, atm$, $P_{O_2} = 3 \, atm$, $n = 2$:

$$
E = 1.229 + \dfrac{8.314 \times 353}{2 \times 96485} \ln(3 \times 3^{1/2})
$$

Compute the temperature-dependent term:
$$
\dfrac{8.314 \times 353}{2 \times 96485} = \dfrac{2934.8}{192970} = 0.01521 \, V
$$

Compute the logarithm:
$$
\ln(3 \times 3^{0.5}) = \ln(3 \times 1.732) = \ln(5.196) = 1.6485
$$

Therefore:
$$
E = 1.229 + 0.01521 \times 1.6485
$$

$$
E = 1.229 + 0.02508
$$

$$
\boxed{E \approx 1.254 \, V}
$$

> This shows pressurization gives a small voltage gain (~25 mV at 3 atm), demonstrating the mild pressure dependence of cell voltage.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Half-Cell Reaction Summary

| **Parameter** | **Anode (HOR)** | **Cathode (ORR)** |
|---|---|---|
| Fuel / Oxidant | $H_2$ (g) | $O_2$ (g) |
| Reactant from Electrolyte | $OH^-$ (from cathode) | $H_2O$ (from anode product) |
| Products to Electrolyte | $H_2O$ (retained) | $OH^-$ (regenerated) |
| Standard Potential $E^0$ | $0 \, V$ (reference) | $+0.401 \, V$ (vs. SHE) |
| Net Cell Potential | $E^0_{cathode} - E^0_{anode} = 1.229 \, V$ |  |
| Typical Catalyst | Ni, Pt/C | Ag, MnO₂, Pt/C, Ni |
| Direction of Ion Flow | $OH^-$ flows **toward** anode | $OH^-$ generated here |
| Overpotential | Small (~0.05 V) | Large (~0.2–0.3 V) — main loss |
| CO Sensitivity | Moderate | None directly |

> [!NOTE]
> The **ORR overpotential at the cathode** is responsible for the largest single voltage loss in any H₂/O₂ fuel cell. In AFCs, this loss is **lower than in PEMFCs** because the alkaline ORR kinetics are intrinsically faster — a key engineering advantage.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — AFC Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph supply["Fuel and Oxidant Supply"]
        H2tank["H2 Storage Tank"]
        O2tank["O2 Storage Tank or Air Blower"]
    end

    subgraph cell["Alkaline Fuel Cell Stack Core"]
        anode["Anode Chamber Porous Nickel Electrode"]
        cathode["Cathode Chamber Silver or Nickel Electrode"]
        elyte["KOH Electrolyte 30 to 45 wt percent"]
        membrane["Asbestos or PBI Separator"]
    end

    subgraph external["External Circuit and Load"]
        wire["External Conductor and Load"]
        heater["Heat Exchanger for Water Removal"]
        pump["Circulation Pump for KOH"]
    end

    H2tank --> anode
    O2tank --> cathode
    anode -- HOR Reaction Produces Water and Electrons --> elyte
    cathode -- ORR Reaction Consumes Water and Electrons --> elyte
    elyte -- OH Minus Ion Migration --> membrane
    membrane --> elyte
    anode -- e Minus Forced Through External Circuit --> wire
    wire --> cathode
    anode -- Liquid Water Product --> heater
    elyte --> pump
    pump --> elyte

    classDef supplyStyle fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0050b3,color:#000
    classDef cellStyle fill:#d4edda,stroke:#1e7e34,color:#000
    classDef externalStyle fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,color:#000

    class H2tank,O2tank supplyStyle
    class anode,cathode,elyte,membrane cellStyle
    class wire,heater,pump externalStyle
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — AFC Reaction and Energy Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    inputA["Hydrogen Fuel H2 In"]
    inputB["Oxygen Oxidant O2 In"]
    inputC["KOH Electrolyte Recirculation"]

    process1["Anode HOR Reaction H2 plus 2 OH Minus gives 2 H2O plus 2 e Minus"]
    process2["Electron Flow e Minus Through External Load Producing DC Electricity"]
    process3["Cathode ORR Reaction Half O2 plus H2O plus 2 e Minus gives 2 OH Minus"]
    process4["OH Minus Ion Migration from Cathode to Anode through KOH"]
    process5["Heat and Water Management Subsystem"]

    outputA["DC Electrical Power Out"]
    outputB["Heat Out for CHP"]
    outputC["Liquid Water Out"]

    inputA --> process1
    inputC --> process1
    process1 --> process2
    process2 --> outputA
    process2 --> process3
    inputB --> process3
    process3 --> process4
    process4 --> inputC
    process1 --> process5
    process3 --> process5
    process5 --> outputB
    process5 --> outputC

    classDef inStyle fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd,color:#000
    classDef procStyle fill:#fff8e1,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000
    classDef outStyle fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32,color:#000

    class inputA,inputB,inputC inStyle
    class process1,process2,process3,process4,process5 procStyle
    class outputA,outputB,outputC outStyle
```

## 4.3 Schematic Description (ASCII Text Fallback)

> [!NOTE]
> **AFC Cross-Sectional Description (Top-Down View):**
>
> ```
>   HYDROGEN IN  →  [ POROUS Ni ANODE ]  ←  →  OH⁻ TRAVELS RIGHT
>                    |     |     |   (KOH 30-45 wt% matrix)
>                    |  e⁻ flows DOWN through wire
>                    v
>                 [ EXTERNAL LOAD = DC POWER OUT ]
>                    ^
>                    |  e⁻ returns upward
>                    |     |     |
>   OXYGEN/AIR IN →  [ POROUS Ag CATHODE ]  ←  →  H₂O consumed here
>                    ←  ←  ←  OH⁻ produced here
>                    ↓
>                WATER + HEAT removed
> ```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Question, Module 4]**

### Q1. Define Alkaline Fuel Cell. Mention any two advantages and one disadvantage of AFC.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Definition (1 Mark):** An Alkaline Fuel Cell (AFC) is a low-temperature fuel cell that uses an aqueous solution of potassium hydroxide (KOH, 30–45 wt%) as the electrolyte and operates between 60 °C and 90 °C, generating electricity from the electrochemical reaction of H₂ and O₂.
- **Advantage 1 (1 Mark):** The ORR kinetics in alkaline media are much faster than in acidic media, enabling the use of **non-precious metal catalysts** such as Ni and Ag, drastically reducing catalyst cost.
- **Advantage 2 (0.5 Mark):** Higher electrical efficiency (50%–70%) compared to acid-based fuel cells.
- **Disadvantage (0.5 Mark):** AFCs are highly sensitive to **CO₂ contamination**; CO₂ reacts with KOH to form K₂CO₃, which precipitates and blocks the porous electrodes.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question, Module 4]**

### Q2. Write the half-cell and overall reactions of an Alkaline Fuel Cell. What ion is the charge carrier?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Anode (HOR) Reaction (1 Mark):**
$$
\mathrm{H_2 + 2OH^- \rightarrow 2H_2O + 2e^-}
$$
- **Cathode (ORR) Reaction (1 Mark):**
$$
\mathrm{\tfrac{1}{2}O_2 + H_2O + 2e^- \rightarrow 2OH^-}
$$
- **Overall Reaction (0.5 Mark):**
$$
\mathrm{H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow H_2O + Electrical \; Energy + Heat}
$$
- **Charge Carrier (0.5 Mark):** The **hydroxyl ion (OH⁻)** is the charge carrier in the KOH electrolyte, migrating **from the cathode to the anode**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Module 4, ESE Pattern, 14 Marks]**

### Question A (14 Marks)

**a) Explain the construction and working principle of an Alkaline Fuel Cell with a neat diagram. List the components used. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Construction (3.5 Marks):**
- An AFC consists of two porous electrodes (**anode** and **cathode**) separated by a **liquid KOH electrolyte** held within an **asbestos matrix or PBI (polybenzimidazole) separator**.
- **Anode:** Porous nickel or nickel-plated steel, coated with Pt/C or Raney Ni catalyst.
- **Cathode:** Porous silver or lithiated NiO catalyst (Ag is preferred due to low cost and good ORR activity in alkaline media).
- **Electrolyte:** 30–45 wt% aqueous KOH (concentrated for high ionic conductivity).
- **Bipolar plates:** Nickel-plated stainless steel for current collection and gas distribution.
- **External accessories:** H₂ and O₂ (or air) supply manifolds, water/heat removal system, electrolyte circulation pump.

**Working Principle (3.5 Marks):**
- Pure H₂ is fed to the **anode**, where it undergoes the **Hydrogen Oxidation Reaction (HOR)** in the presence of OH⁻ ions:
$$
\mathrm{H_2 + 2OH^- \rightarrow 2H_2O + 2e^-}
$$
- The released **electrons flow through the external circuit** to power the load, producing useful DC electrical work.
- At the **cathode**, O₂ (or air) accepts the electrons and reacts with water to regenerate OH⁻:
$$
\mathrm{\tfrac{1}{2}O_2 + H_2O + 2e^- \rightarrow 2OH^-}
$$
- The regenerated OH⁻ migrates back to the anode through the electrolyte, completing the loop.
- The water produced at the anode and the heat generated are continuously removed by circulation and heat exchangers.
- Net reaction: $\mathrm{H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow H_2O + Electricity + Heat}$

---

**b) Derive the Nernst equation for the Alkaline Fuel Cell and calculate the open-circuit voltage at 80 °C if $P_{H_2} = 2.5 \ atm$ and $P_{O_2} = 2.5 \ atm$. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Derivation (4 Marks):**
The general cell reaction is: $\mathrm{H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow H_2O_{(l)}}$

The number of electrons transferred: $n = 2$

The reaction quotient (since $a_{H_2O} = 1$):
$$
Q = \dfrac{1}{P_{H_2} \cdot P_{O_2}^{1/2}}
$$

The Nernst equation:
$$
E = E^0 - \dfrac{RT}{nF} \ln Q
$$

Substituting $Q$:
$$
E = E^0 + \dfrac{RT}{nF} \ln \left( P_{H_2} \cdot P_{O_2}^{1/2} \right)
$$

This is the operating cell voltage expression for an AFC.

**Numerical Calculation (3 Marks):**

Given: $T = 80^\circ C = 353 \, K$, $P_{H_2} = P_{O_2} = 2.5 \, atm$, $E^0 = 1.229 \, V$, $n = 2$.

Compute the temperature-dependent coefficient:
$$
\dfrac{RT}{nF} = \dfrac{8.314 \times 353}{2 \times 96485} = \dfrac{2934.8}{192970} = 0.01521 \, V
$$

Compute the log term:
$$
\ln(2.5 \times 2.5^{1/2}) = \ln(2.5 \times 1.581) = \ln(3.953) = 1.374
$$

Substitute:
$$
E = 1.229 + 0.01521 \times 1.374
$$

$$
E = 1.229 + 0.0209
$$

$$
\boxed{E \approx 1.250 \, V}
$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Internal Choice Alternative*

**a) Discuss the advantages, limitations, and applications of Alkaline Fuel Cells. Compare AFCs with PEMFCs. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Advantages of AFC (2 Marks):**
- **Faster ORR kinetics** in alkaline medium → use of low-cost non-precious catalysts (Ag, Ni).
- **Higher electrical efficiency** (50%–70%) compared to PEMFC.
- **Mature technology** — first developed by Francis Bacon in 1932; successfully used in space missions.
- **Cheaper stack components** — bipolar plates can be made from nickel-plated steel instead of expensive graphite.

**Limitations (2 Marks):**
- **CO₂ sensitivity:** Atmospheric CO₂ forms K₂CO₃, poisoning the electrolyte; requires pure O₂ instead of air.
- **Electrolyte management:** Liquid KOH requires pumps, leak-proof sealing, and freeze protection.
- **Water management:** Water produced at the anode must be continuously removed to prevent electrode flooding.
- **Lower power density** compared to PEMFC (which operates at higher current density).

**Applications (1 Mark):**
- NASA Apollo and Space Shuttle (primary electric power + drinking water).
- Military portable power units.
- Stationary backup power for telecom.
- Forklifts and material handling vehicles.
- Small-scale CHP units.

**Comparison Table: AFC vs. PEMFC (2 Marks):**

| **Parameter** | **AFC** | **PEMFC** |
|---|---|---|
| Electrolyte | Liquid KOH (30–45 wt%) | Solid Nafion membrane |
| Operating Temp. | 60–90 °C | 60–80 °C |
| Charge Carrier | OH⁻ | H⁺ |
| Catalyst | Ag, Ni (cheap) | Pt (expensive) |
| CO₂ Sensitivity | High (must use pure O₂) | Low (tolerant) |
| CO Tolerance | < 50 ppm | < 10 ppm |
| Efficiency | 50–70% | 40–60% |
| Power Density | Lower | Higher |
| Cost | Lower | Higher |

---

**b) Calculate the maximum theoretical electrical efficiency of an AFC operating on pure H₂ and O₂. Given: $\Delta G = -237.18 \, kJ/mol$, $\Delta H_{HHV} = -285.83 \, kJ/mol$. Also calculate the practical efficiency if the cell operates at 0.85 V average voltage. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Theoretical Efficiency (3.5 Marks):**

Maximum theoretical efficiency is:
$$
\eta_{th} = \dfrac{\Delta G}{\Delta H_{HHV}} = \dfrac{237.18}{285.83}
$$

$$
\boxed{\eta_{th} = 0.8298 \approx 83\%}
$$

This is the **upper limit** of electrical conversion efficiency for an AFC operating on H₂/O₂, even under ideal (reversible) conditions. The remaining ~17% is inevitably released as heat due to entropy generation.

**Practical Efficiency (3.5 Marks):**

The actual Gibbs free energy released per mole of H₂ is:
$$
\Delta G_{actual} = -nFE_{actual} = -2 \times 96485 \times 0.85
$$

$$
\Delta G_{actual} = -164{,}024.5 \, J/mol = -164.02 \, kJ/mol
$$

Practical efficiency is:
$$
\eta_{practical} = \dfrac{\vert \Delta G_{actual} \vert}{\vert \Delta H_{HHV} \vert} = \dfrac{164.02}{285.83}
$$

$$
\boxed{\eta_{practical} \approx 0.5738 \approx 57.4\%}
$$

**Voltage Efficiency Component (Bonus, for understanding):**
$$
\eta_{V} = \dfrac{E_{actual}}{E^0} = \dfrac{0.85}{1.229} = 0.6916 \approx 69.2\%
$$

This shows how the **activation, ohmic, and concentration overpotentials** reduce the cell voltage from 1.229 V to 0.85 V, thereby reducing practical efficiency.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks (Module 4 — AFC):**
> 1. **Confusing the direction of OH⁻ migration:** OH⁻ moves from **cathode to anode**, NOT anode to cathode. Many students incorrectly state the reverse. (-1 to -2 marks)
> 2. **Wrong product at anode:** Students often write H₂O as the *cathode* product. In AFC, **water is produced at the anode**, not the cathode. (-1 mark)
> 3. **Forgetting CO₂ sensitivity in advantages:** When listing "uses cheap catalysts" as an advantage, students must ALSO mention that this benefit is partially offset by the need for CO₂-free oxidant. (-1 mark)
> 4. **Using LHV instead of HHV:** KTU examiners prefer the **HHV basis** for theoretical efficiency calculations (1.481 V reference, 83% efficiency). Using LHV (1.229 V) without clarification is acceptable but less preferred.
> 5. **Calculation errors in Nernst equation:** Forgetting that $O_2$ enters as $\sqrt{P_{O_2}}$, i.e., the **half-power of $P_{O_2}$**. Use $P_{O_2}^{1/2}$, not $P_{O_2}$. (-2 marks)
> 6. **Forgetting units in thermodynamic calculations:** Always state $\Delta G$ in kJ/mol, $E$ in Volts, and $T$ in Kelvin in the final answer. (-0.5 mark per missing unit)
> 7. **Wrong catalyst for cathode:** AFC cathodes typically use **Ag or NiO**, NOT Pt. Mentioning Pt-only shows incomplete understanding. (-1 mark)

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **AFC Quick-Recall Summary (Module 4):**
>
> ☐ **Full Form:** Alkaline Fuel Cell — uses **KOH electrolyte** (30–45 wt% aqueous).
>
> ☐ **Operating Temperature:** **60–90 °C** (low-temperature fuel cell).
>
> ☐ **Charge Carrier:** **OH⁻ (hydroxyl ion)** — moves from **cathode to anode**.
>
> ☐ **Anode Reaction (HOR):** $\mathrm{H_2 + 2OH^- \rightarrow 2H_2O + 2e^-}$
>
> ☐ **Cathode Reaction (ORR):** $\mathrm{\tfrac{1}{2}O_2 + H_2O + 2e^- \rightarrow 2OH^-}$
>
> ☐ **Net Reaction:** $\mathrm{H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow H_2O + Electricity + Heat}$
>
> ☐ **Standard Cell Voltage:** $E^0 = 1.229 \, V$ (liquid water product)
>
> ☐ **Theoretical Efficiency (HHV):** $\eta_{th} = \Delta G / \Delta H_{HHV} = 237.18 / 285.83 \approx 83\%$
>
> ☐ **Practical Efficiency:** 50%–70% (electrical), up to 80%–85% (CHP).
>
> ☐ **Catalysts:** Anode — Ni or Pt/C; Cathode — **Ag, MnO₂, NiO** (NOT necessarily Pt — this is the key cost advantage).
>
> ☐ **Major Advantage:** Fast ORR kinetics in alkaline media → non-precious metal catalysts possible → **lower cost**.
>
> ☐ **Major Disadvantage:** **CO₂ poisoning** — reacts with KOH to form K₂CO₃. Requires pure O₂ instead of air.
>
> ☐ **Water Production Site:** **Anode** (not cathode). Water must be actively removed.
>
> ☐ **Nernst Equation (AFC form):** $E = E^0 + \dfrac{RT}{nF} \ln \left( P_{H_2} \cdot P_{O_2}^{1/2} \right)$
>
> ☐ **Key Applications:** NASA Apollo & Space Shuttle (historical), military portable power, telecom backup, forklifts, small CHP.
>
> ☐ **Electrolyte Management:** KOH must be **continuously circulated** and **CO₂-free** to maintain conductivity.
>
> ☐ **Comparison Anchor:** AFC ≠ PEMFC. AFC uses **liquid KOH + OH⁻**; PEMFC uses **Nafion membrane + H⁺**. Ion flow direction is **opposite** between the two.
>
> ☐ **Why not limited by Carnot?** Fuel cells are **not heat engines**; they directly convert chemical → electrical energy, bypassing Carnot's theorem entirely.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Phosphoric Acid Fuel Cell
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECHT411 - ENERGY ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Phosphoric Acid Fuel Cell** ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Phosphoric Acid Fuel Cell (PAFC)** is a **medium-temperature**, **liquid-electrolyte** type of fuel cell that utilizes **concentrated phosphoric acid (typically 100% H₃PO₄)** immobilized in a **silicon carbide (SiC) matrix** as the electrolyte. It operates in the temperature range of **150 °C to 220 °C** and uses a **hydrogen-rich fuel** (reformed natural gas, methanol, or pure H₂) with **air** as the oxidant. The PAFC is commercially the **most mature** and **first-generation** fuel cell technology deployed in stationary Combined Heat and Power (CHP) applications, achieving **electrical efficiencies of 37–42 %** and **overall (CHP) efficiencies up to 85 %**. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition) > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Analogy — "The Hot Acid Battery That Drinks Hydrogen":** > Imagine a sealed plastic bottle containing a thick, syrupy acid (phosphoric acid) sandwiched between two carbon-platinum plates. If you feed hydrogen gas into the bottom plate (anode) and air into the top plate (cathode), the hydrogen atoms get split into protons and electrons. The protons swim through the warm acid toward the other side, while the electrons are forced to take the "long way around" through an external circuit (your lightbulb, motor, or TV). On the other side, the electrons reunite with the protons and oxygen from the air to form pure water and a little bit of heat. Unlike an alkaline battery, this "acid bottle" never wears out — you just keep feeding it hydrogen and air, and it keeps producing electricity and useful heat. The heat it produces is so good that we use it for hot water or building heating too! ### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics - **Operating temperature:** **150 °C to 220 °C** (typically 200 °C) - **Electrolyte concentration:** **~ 100 % H₃PO₄** (concentrated, low water content) - **Electrolyte support:** Silicon Carbide (SiC) matrix with PTFE binder - **Anode catalyst:** Platinum (Pt) on carbon black, loading ~ 0.1 mg/cm² - **Cathode catalyst:** Platinum (Pt) on carbon black, loading ~ 0.5 mg/cm² - **Charge carrier:** **H⁺ (proton)** - **Faraday's constant (F):** **96,485 C/mol** - **Universal gas constant (R):** **8.314 J/(mol·K)** - **Standard cell EMF (E°):** **1.229 V** (at 25 °C, 1 atm) - **Typical practical cell voltage:** **0.6 V to 0.8 V** under load - **Electrical efficiency:** **37 % – 42 % (LHV basis)** - **CHP efficiency:** **up to 85 %** ### 1.4 Position in the Fuel Cell Family | Fuel Cell Type | Electrolyte | Operating Temp. | Charge Carrier | App. Stage | |---|---|---|---|---| | **PAFC** | **H₃PO₄ (liquid)** | **150–220 °C** | **H⁺** | **Commercial (1st Gen CHP)** | | PEMFC | Polymer membrane | 60–100 °C | H⁺ | Commercial (transport) | | AFC | KOH (liquid) | 60–250 °C | OH⁻ | Space / niche | | MCFC | Molten Li₂CO₃/K₂CO₃ | 600–700 °C | CO₃²⁻ | Stationary (utility) | | SOFC | YSZ (solid ceramic) | 800–1000 °C | O²⁻ | Stationary (utility) | > [!TIP] > PAFC is the **technological stepping-stone** between low-temperature PEMFC and high-temperature MCFC/SOFC systems. Its medium temperature (≈ 200 °C) gives it a unique balance of CO tolerance and reasonable start-up time. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Working Principle — Structured Logic Flow The operation of a PAFC can be broken down into **5 logical steps**: - **Step 1 — Fuel Delivery:** Reformed hydrocarbon fuel (or pure H₂) is humidified and fed to the **anode** flow field. The reformate typically contains ~ 70–80 % H₂, 15–25 % CO₂, and small amounts of CO (~ 10–50 ppm after shift reactor). - **Step 2 — Anode Half-Reaction (Hydrogen Oxidation Reaction — HOR):** At the Pt catalyst surface, H₂ molecules adsorb and dissociate. Each H atom loses an electron to the anode current collector, producing **2 H⁺ ions** and **2 e⁻** per H₂ molecule. - **Step 3 — Ionic Conduction:** The H⁺ ions migrate through the **phosphoric acid electrolyte (H₃PO₄)** from anode to cathode. Phosphoric acid is a proton conductor via the **Grotthuss mechanism** (proton hopping along hydrogen-bonded networks of H₃PO₄/H₂O clusters). - **Step 4 — Cathode Half-Reaction (Oxygen Reduction Reaction — ORR):** At the Pt cathode catalyst, the migrating H⁺ ions, the electrons returning through the external circuit, and the supplied O₂ combine to form **H₂O** and **heat**. - **Step 5 — External Circuit Work:** The electrons travel through the external load, doing useful electrical work. Product water is rejected as **liquid + vapor** at the cathode exhaust. Excess heat is recovered for **CHP**. ### 2.2 Electrode and Overall Reactions The three governing reactions of a PAFC are: **Anode (HOR):** $$ \text{H}_2 \;\longrightarrow\; 2\,\text{H}^+ \;+\; 2\,e^- \qquad E^{\circ}_{a} = 0.000\;\text{V (SHE)} $$ **Cathode (ORR):** $$ \tfrac{1}{2}\,\text{O}_2 \;+\; 2\,\text{H}^+ \;+\; 2\,e^- \;\longrightarrow\; \text{H}_2\text{O}_{(l)} \qquad E^{\circ}_{c} = 1.229\;\text{V (SHE)} $$ **Overall Cell Reaction:** $$ \text{H}_2 \;+\; \tfrac{1}{2}\,\text{O}_2 \;\longrightarrow\; \text{H}_2\text{O}_{(l)} \qquad E^{\circ}_{cell} = 1.229\;\text{V} $$ ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!NOTE] > **Master these six equations — they cover ≥ 70 % of numerical questions in the Energy Engineering module.** | # | Formula / Equation | Meaning | Variables & Units | Boundary / Validity Condition | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\Delta G = -nFE$ | Gibbs free energy ↔ cell EMF | $n$=electrons transferred; $F$=96485 C/mol; $E$=V | Reversible equilibrium | | 2 | $E = E^{\circ} - \dfrac{RT}{nF}\ln Q$ | Nernst Equation (general) | $Q$=reaction quotient; $T$=K | For H₂/O₂ cell: $Q = \dfrac{1}{p_{H_2}\,p_{O_2}^{1/2}}$ | | 3 | $E = 1.229 - 0.00085\,(T-298) + \dfrac{RT}{2F}\ln\!\big(p_{H_2}\,p_{O_2}^{1/2}\big)$ | PAFC Nernst voltage | $T$ in K; $p$ in atm | Standard PAFC operating range | | 4 | $V_{cell} = E - \eta_{act} - \eta_{conc} - \eta_{ohm}$ | Actual cell voltage | $E$=reversible V; $\eta$ = polarisation losses (V) | Each loss is **positive** and subtracts | | 5 | $\eta_{electrical} = \dfrac{\mu_f \cdot \Delta H_{HV}}{\Delta G} = \dfrac{V_{cell}}{1.482}$ | Electrochemical efficiency (H₂, HHV) | $V_{cell}$ = working voltage | HHV of H₂ = 285.8 kJ/mol → 1.482 V | | 6 | $\eta_{stack} = \dfrac{n \cdot V_{cell} \cdot I}{m_{H_2}\cdot\text{LHV}}$ | Stack / system efficiency | $n$ = cells; $I$ = A; $m_{H_2}$ = kg/s | Typical PAFC: 37–42 % LHV | > **PITFALL SAFETY:** In markdown tables, the absolute-value bars and fraction lines above are written using `\vert` and `\dfrac` to keep the table syntax intact. ### 2.4 The Four Sources of Voltage Loss (Polarisation Curve) A real PAFC delivers less than 1.229 V due to four **irreversible losses** that students must memorise for KTU: - **Activation Polarisation (η_act):** The kinetic "energy barrier" required to start the electrochemical reaction, especially the sluggish **ORR** at the cathode. Dominant at **low current densities**. - **Ohmic Polarisation (η_ohm):** Resistance to ion flow through the H₃PO₄ electrolyte and to electron flow through electrodes, bipolar plates, and interconnects. Linear in current density, dominant in the **mid-range**. - **Concentration Polarisation (η_conc):** Mass-transport limitation when reactant gases cannot reach the catalyst fast enough at **high current densities**. Causes the **sharp voltage drop** near the limiting current. - **Internal Currents / Crossover (η_cross):** Small fuel crossover and parasitic currents across the electrolyte, treated as a near-constant loss. The combined polarisation equation: $$ V_{cell}(j) = E_{Nernst} - \eta_{act,a} - \eta_{act,c} - j\cdot R_{ohmic} - \eta_{conc}(j) $$ where $j$ is the current density (A/cm²) and $R_{ohmic}$ is the area-specific resistance (Ω·cm²). ### 2.5 Why PAFC Uses 100 % Phosphoric Acid > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering "Why" — Choice of Electrolyte:** > - At **low temperatures (< 100 °C)**, phosphoric acid is a **poor** proton conductor (H₃PO₄ has very low self-ionisation). > - Above **~ 150 °C**, the conductivity of H₃PO₄ rises sharply (reaching ~ **0.6 S/cm at 200 °C**). > - The **SiC ceramic matrix** is chemically inert to hot H₃PO₄ and provides mechanical strength to retain the liquid acid via capillary action. > - At 200 °C, the **CO tolerance of Pt** increases to **~ 1–2 %** (vs. < 50 ppm for PEMFC), which is a critical advantage when using reformed hydrocarbon fuels. ### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Stationary CHP units (200 kW class):** Deployed in hospitals, hotels, office buildings (e.g., UTC Power PC25™). - **On-site power generation:** Distributed generation for telecom towers, data centres, and waste-water treatment plants. - **Bridge technology:** Connects low-temp PEM research with high-temp MCFC/SOFC commercialisation. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: From Gibbs Free Energy to the Nernst Equation **Starting relation** between maximum reversible electrical work and Gibbs free energy: $$ W_{elec,max} = -\Delta G \quad \text{(J/mol)} $$ **Electrical work done** when $n$ moles of electrons pass through a potential difference $E$: $$ W_{elec} = n \cdot F \cdot E $$ **Equating the two:** $$ -\Delta G = n F E \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \Delta G = -n F E $$ **Generalised (non-standard) expression** using the reaction quotient $Q$: $$ \Delta G = \Delta G^{\circ} + RT\ln Q $$ **Substitute the reversible EMF form** $E = -\dfrac{\Delta G}{nF}$: $$ -nFE = -nFE^{\circ} + RT\ln Q $$ **Dividing both sides by $-nF$** gives the **Nernst Equation**: $$ E = E^{\circ} - \dfrac{RT}{nF}\ln Q $$ For the H₂/O₂ cell, $Q = \dfrac{a_{H_2O}}{p_{H_2}\,p_{O_2}^{1/2}}$ and, assuming liquid water product ($a_{H_2O}=1$): $$ E_{H_2/O_2} = 1.229 - \dfrac{RT}{2F}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{1}{p_{H_2}\,p_{O_2}^{1/2}}\right) = 1.229 + \dfrac{RT}{2F}\ln\!\left(p_{H_2}\,p_{O_2}^{1/2}\right) $$ ### 3.2 Numerical Worked Example 1 — Nernst Voltage at PAFC Operating Conditions **Given:** - Operating temperature: $T = 200\,^{\circ}\text{C} = 473\;\text{K}$ - $p_{H_2} = 1.0\;\text{atm}$ (anode inlet) - $p_{O_2} = 0.21\;\text{atm}$ (air at cathode) **Find:** Reversible open-circuit voltage $E$. **Step 1 — Compute the temperature-correction term** (empirical for PAFC): $$ E^{\circ}(T) = 1.229 - 0.00085\,(T - 298) = 1.229 - 0.00085\,(473 - 298) $$ $$ = 1.229 - 0.00085 \times 175 = 1.229 - 0.14875 = 1.08025\;\text{V} $$ **Step 2 — Apply Nernst correction at $T = 473$ K:** $$ E = E^{\circ}(T) + \dfrac{RT}{2F}\ln\!\left(p_{H_2}\,p_{O_2}^{1/2}\right) $$ **Step 3 — Substitute numerical values:** $$ \dfrac{RT}{2F} = \dfrac{8.314 \times 473}{2 \times 96485} = \dfrac{3932.5}{192970} = 0.02038\;\text{V} $$ $$ \ln(1.0 \times \sqrt{0.21}) = \ln(0.4583) = -0.7803 $$ **Step 4 — Final calculation:** $$ E = 1.08025 + (0.02038)(-0.7803) = 1.08025 - 0.01590 = 1.0644\;\text{V} $$ > [!TIP] > **Result:** $E_{OCV} \approx \mathbf{1.064\;\text{V}}$ at 200 °C and air-feed. This is the **theoretical maximum**; the **measured open-circuit voltage** of a PAFC is typically 1.0–1.05 V due to minor crossover and mixed-potential losses. ### 3.3 Numerical Worked Example 2 — Stack Efficiency at Rated Power **Given:** - 200-cell PAFC stack, rated power $P = 200\;\text{kW}$ - Average cell voltage $V_{cell} = 0.70\;\text{V}$ - Hydrogen consumption $\dot{m}_{H_2} = 11.5\;\text{kg/day}$ **Find:** Electrical efficiency $\eta_{elec}$ on LHV basis. **Step 1 — LHV of hydrogen** = $119.96\;\text{MJ/kg}$ (standard textbook value). **Step 2 — Hydrogen mass flow rate in kg/s:** $$ \dot{m} = \dfrac{11.5}{24 \times 3600} = 1.331 \times 10^{-4}\;\text{kg/s} $$ **Step 3 — Chemical (input) power:** $$ \dot{Q}_{in} = \dot{m} \times \text{LHV} = 1.331 \times 10^{-4} \times 119.96 \times 10^{6} = 15.97\;\text{kW} $$ **Step 4 — Verify electrical output matches:** $$ P_{elec} = 200\;\text{kW} $$ **Step 5 — Compute efficiency:** $$ \eta_{elec} = \dfrac{P_{elec}}{\dot{Q}_{in}} = \dfrac{200}{15.97} \approx 12.5\;\text{kW/kW} \;\text{(input basis)} $$ > [!WARNING] > **Examiner Pitfall:** The 200 kW is far larger than the chemical input of 15.97 kW, indicating a **data inconsistency in this hypothetical problem**. In a real exam question, you would simply compute the ratio as labelled. If the numbers in the actual KTU paper give $\eta > 100\,\%$, the assumed fuel flow was a **daily average including turn-down**; the correct interpretation is to use the **hourly rated fuel consumption**. Always quote the answer with units and a clear basis (LHV or HHV). **Corrected sanity check:** A real 200 kW PAFC consumes ~ 13.5 kg H₂/hr → $\dot{Q}_{in} = 450\;\text{kW}$ → $\eta_{elec} = 200/450 = 44.4\,\%$ ✓ (close to PAFC's typical 37–42 %). ### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (PAFC vs PEMFC vs SOFC) | Parameter | **PAFC** | **PEMFC** | **SOFC** | |---|---|---|---| | Electrolyte | 100 % H₃PO₄ (liquid) | Nafion™ polymer | YSZ (solid ceramic) | | Operating Temp. | **150–220 °C** | 60–100 °C | 800–1000 °C | | Charge carrier | H⁺ | H⁺ | O²⁻ | | Water management | Cathode side | Cathode side | Produced at anode | | CO tolerance | **~ 1–2 %** | < 50 ppm | 100 % (fuel-side) | | Reforming | External | External | Internal (direct) | | Electrical η | 37–42 % | 50–60 % | 50–60 % | | Start-up time | 1–4 h | Seconds–minutes | Hours–days | | Maturity / TRL | **Commercial (1st Gen CHP)** | Commercial (auto) | Demonstration | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 PAFC Single-Cell Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[H2 Fuel Inlet
Reformed Natural Gas] --> B(Anode Flow Field
Graphite Bipolar Plate) B --> C[Anode Catalyst Layer
Pt on Carbon] C --> D[SiC Matrix
H3PO4 Electrolyte 100%] D --> E[Cathode Catalyst Layer
Pt on Carbon] E --> F(Cathode Flow Field
Graphite Bipolar Plate) F --> G[Air / O2 Inlet] F --> H[Product Water + Heat Out] C -->|2 e- flow| I[External Load
Electricity + Heat] I -->|2 e- return| F D -.->|H+ migration| F ``` ### 4.2 PAFC Polarisation Curve (V–j Characteristic) ```mermaid flowchart TB A[Open Circuit Voltage
E ocv 1.06 V] --> B(Activation Loss Region
Low current density) B --> C(Ohmic Loss Region
Linear drop) C --> D(Concentration Loss Region
Sharp drop) D --> E[Limiting Current Density
j L] style A fill:#e0f7fa,stroke:#006064 style E fill:#ffe0e0,stroke:#b71c1c ``` ### 4.3 PAFC Plant Block-Level Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph FEED[Fuel Processing Subsystem] NG[Natural Gas] --> PR[Prereformer] PR --> SR[Steam Reformer
700 to 850 C] SR --> HTS[High Temp Shift] HTS --> LTS[Low Temp Shift] LTS --> PROX[PROX Reactor
CO lt 50 ppm] end subgraph STACK[Power Subsystem PAFC] PROX --> ANO[Anode H2 rich gas] AIR[Air Blower] --> CATH[Cathode Air] ANO --> CELL[200 Cell PAFC Stack
200 C H3PO4] CATH --> CELL CELL --> DC[DC Bus] end subgraph BOP[Balance of Plant] DC --> INV[Inverter DC to AC] INV --> GRID[AC Grid / Load] CELL --> HX[Heat Exchanger] HX --> CHP[Hot Water / Steam
for Building Heat] end ``` ### 4.4 Reaction and Charge-Flow Schematic ```mermaid flowchart LR H2[H2 Molecule] -->|Adsorption on Pt| HA[2 H atoms] HA -->|H oxidation| HP[2 H+ ions] HA -->|2 e- via external circuit| EL[External Load] HP -->|Migration through H3PO4| CA[Cathode] O2[1/2 O2] --> CA EL --> CA CA -->|ORR| W[H2O + Heat] style EL fill:#fff59d,stroke:#f57f17 style HP fill:#b2ebf2,stroke:#006064 style W fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Interpretation Tip for KTU:** Examiners often ask for "sketch and label" questions. In the answer script, convert the Mermaid logic into a **hand-drawn labelled sketch** showing **fuel inlet, oxidant inlet, electrolyte, anode, cathode, electron path, proton path, and water + heat outlet** — and label the half-reactions at each electrode. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model Paper] (3 Marks)** *List the electrolyte, operating temperature, and charge carrier of a Phosphoric Acid Fuel Cell. Why is concentrated (100 %) H₃PO₄ used instead of dilute acid?* **Model Answer (3 marks, key points):** - **Electrolyte:** Concentrated (100 %) phosphoric acid, H₃PO₄, immobilised in a **silicon carbide (SiC) ceramic matrix** with PTFE binder. *(1 mark)* - **Operating temperature range:** **150–220 °C** (typically ~ 200 °C). *(1 mark)* - **Charge carrier:** **H⁺ (proton)** migrating from anode to cathode via the Grotthuss proton-hopping mechanism. *(0.5 mark)* - **Reason for 100 % concentration:** At the operating temperature, only **concentrated (water-free) H₃PO₄** provides high proton conductivity (~ 0.6 S/cm at 200 °C). Dilute acid would have its water evaporate above 100 °C, causing electrolyte drying, gas crossover, and rapid performance loss. *(0.5 mark — for the justification)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Supplementary] (3 Marks)** *Compare the CO tolerance of PAFC and PEMFC. Explain why this difference exists.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **CO tolerance:** PAFC can tolerate **~ 1–2 % (10,000–20,000 ppm) CO** in the fuel stream, while PEMFC tolerates **< 50 ppm** CO. *(1 mark)* - **Reason:** At the higher PAFC operating temperature (~ 200 °C), **CO desorbs from Pt catalyst sites** more readily (CO adsorption is exothermic and weakens with temperature). *(1 mark)* - **Practical impact:** PAFC can use the reformate directly after a high-temperature + low-temperature shift reactor (residual CO ≈ 0.5–1 %) without a complex PROX unit, simplifying the fuel processor. PEMFC requires an additional **PROX (Preferential Oxidation) reactor** to drop CO below 50 ppm. *(1 mark)* --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, typically split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. The student answers **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – July 2024, Suggested Module-4 Pattern]** **(a)** With a neat sketch, describe the construction and working principle of a Phosphoric Acid Fuel Cell. Write the electrode half-reactions and the overall cell reaction. *(7 marks)* **(b)** A PAFC operates at 200 °C with $p_{H_2} = 1.0\;\text{atm}$ and air feed at $p_{O_2} = 0.21\;\text{atm}$. Compute the **reversible open-circuit voltage** using the Nernst equation. If the cell delivers a working voltage of 0.70 V, calculate the **electrochemical efficiency** on HHV basis. *(7 marks)* --- **Model Solution for Q-A(a) — 7 marks:** **Construction (3 marks):** A PAFC single cell consists of (i) a porous **anode** made of Pt catalyst supported on carbon black with a PTFE binder, (ii) a porous **cathode** of similar Pt/C composition, (iii) an **electrolyte layer** of 100 % H₃PO₄ held by capillary action in a **silicon carbide (SiC) matrix** with PTFE, and (iv) **graphite bipolar plates** with machined flow fields for distributing fuel and air and for water/heat removal. The cell operates at **150–220 °C** with humidified H₂ at the anode and air at the cathode. **Working Principle (2 marks):** H₂ is fed to the anode, dissociates on Pt, and is oxidised to **2 H⁺ + 2 e⁻**. The protons migrate through the H₃PO₄ to the cathode; electrons flow through the external load. At the cathode, O₂ combines with H⁺ and e⁻ to form **H₂O and heat**. **Reactions (2 marks):** - Anode: $\text{H}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{H}^+ + 2e^-$ - Cathode: $\tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}$ - Overall: $\text{H}_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{Electrical Energy} + \text{Heat}$ *(Award 1 mark for correct construction list, 1 for sketch with labels, 1 for working principle, 2 for three correct reactions.)* --- **Model Solution for Q-A(b) — 7 marks:** **Given:** $T = 200\,^{\circ}\text{C} = 473\;\text{K}$, $p_{H_2} = 1.0\;\text{atm}$, $p_{O_2} = 0.21\;\text{atm}$, $V_{cell} = 0.70\;\text{V}$. **Step 1 — Temperature-corrected standard EMF** *(1 mark)*: $$ E^{\circ}(T) = 1.229 - 0.00085\,(T - 298) = 1.229 - 0.00085 \times 175 = 1.0803\;\text{V} $$ **Step 2 — Nernst correction** *(2 marks)*: $$ \dfrac{RT}{2F} = \dfrac{8.314 \times 473}{2 \times 96485} = 0.02038\;\text{V} $$ $$ \ln\!\left(p_{H_2}\cdot p_{O_2}^{1/2}\right) = \ln\!\left(1.0 \times \sqrt{0.21}\right) = \ln(0.4583) = -0.7803 $$ **Step 3 — Reversible OCV** *(1 mark)*: $$ E_{OCV} = 1.0803 + 0.02038 \times (-0.7803) = 1.0803 - 0.0159 = 1.0644\;\text{V} $$ **Step 4 — Electrochemical efficiency (HHV basis)** *(2 marks)*: For H₂, HHV = 285.8 kJ/mol → equivalent thermoneutral voltage = $1.482\;\text{V}$: $$ \eta_{electrochem} = \dfrac{V_{cell}}{1.482} = \dfrac{0.70}{1.482} = 0.4723 = 47.23\,\% $$ **Step 5 — Conclusion** *(1 mark)*: $$ \boxed{E_{OCV} \approx 1.064\;\text{V}, \quad \eta_{electrochem} \approx 47.2\,\%} $$ --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]** **(a)** Draw a typical PAFC polarisation curve (cell voltage vs. current density) and explain the **three regions of voltage loss**. State the typical PAFC operating current density and cell voltage at rated load. *(7 marks)* **(b)** A 250 kW PAFC CHP unit operates at 40 % electrical efficiency (LHV basis). If the waste heat recovered is 320 kW, compute (i) the total chemical energy input, (ii) the **overall CHP efficiency**, and (iii) the **waste-heat-to-power ratio**. *(7 marks)* --- **Model Solution for Q-B(a) — 7 marks:** **Sketch (3 marks):** A graph with **V_cell (V)** on Y-axis (0 to 1.2 V) and **j (mA/cm²)** on X-axis (0 to 800). Mark **E_ocv ≈ 1.06 V** at j = 0, a sharp initial drop (activation), a linear middle region (ohmic), and a sharp fall near the limiting current (concentration). **Three regions of loss (3 marks):** - **Activation polarisation** — kinetic barrier for ORR at cathode; dominant at low j; modelled by the Butler–Volmer equation. - **Ohmic polarisation** — linear with j; resistance from H₃PO₄ electrolyte, electrodes, and bipolar plates; dominant in the mid-range. - **Concentration polarisation** — mass-transport limit at high j; gas cannot reach catalyst; steep drop near $j_L$. **Typical values (1 mark):** PAFC operates at **200–400 mA/cm²** with a working cell voltage of **0.6–0.7 V** at rated load. --- **Model Solution for Q-B(b) — 7 marks:** **Given:** $P_{elec} = 250\;\text{kW}$, $\eta_{elec} = 40\,\%$, $Q_{waste} = 320\;\text{kW}$. **Step 1 — Total chemical input** *(2 marks)*: $$ \dot{Q}_{in} = \dfrac{P_{elec}}{\eta_{elec}} = \dfrac{250}{0.40} = 625\;\text{kW} $$ **Step 2 — Overall CHP efficiency** *(3 marks)*: $$ \eta_{CHP} = \dfrac{P_{elec} + Q_{waste}}{\dot{Q}_{in}} = \dfrac{250 + 320}{625} = \dfrac{570}{625} = 0.912 = 91.2\,\% $$ **Step 3 — Waste-heat-to-power ratio** *(2 marks)*: $$ \beta = \dfrac{Q_{waste}}{P_{elec}} = \dfrac{320}{250} = 1.28 $$ **Conclusion**: $$ \boxed{\dot{Q}_{in} = 625\;\text{kW}, \quad \eta_{CHP} = 91.2\,\%, \quad \beta = 1.28} $$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls:** > 1. **Wrong concentration in Nernst equation:** Using $p_{O_2} = 1.0$ instead of $0.21$ (air). Always use **partial pressure**, not mole fraction, when $P_{total} = 1\;\text{atm}$. > 2. **Forgetting the temperature correction term** $0.00085(T-298)$. At 200 °C, skipping this gives a $0.15$ V error — a full **2-mark** loss in numerical questions. > 3. **Confusing HHV vs LHV:** $\eta = V/1.482$ uses **HHV (285.8 kJ/mol)**; $\eta = V/1.253$ uses **LHV (241.8 kJ/mol)**. KTU questions explicitly state which basis. > 4. **Mixing up "charge carrier" terminology:** PAFC carries **H⁺** (not OH⁻ as in AFC, and not O²⁻ as in SOFC). > 5. **Omitting units and rounding:** Always report V in **volts (3 decimals)**, efficiency in **%**, and round only at the final step. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Rapid Revision Checklist (High-Density Summary — Read Twice Before Exam)** - **PAFC = Phosphoric Acid Fuel Cell**, **1st-generation commercial CHP technology**. - **Electrolyte:** **100 % H₃PO₄** immobilised in **SiC matrix**. - **Operating temperature:** **150–220 °C** (design point ≈ 200 °C). - **Charge carrier:** **H⁺** (proton); conduction via **Grotthuss mechanism**. - **Electrodes:** Pt on porous carbon, with PTFE binder; anode Pt loading ~ 0.1, cathode ~ 0.5 mg/cm². - **Bipolar plates:** Graphite (machined flow channels for H₂ and air). - **Half-reactions:** Anode: $\text{H}_2 \to 2\text{H}^+ + 2e^-$; Cathode: $\tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}^+ + 2e^- \to \text{H}_2\text{O}$. - **Overall:** $\text{H}_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \to \text{H}_2\text{O}_{(l)}$, $\Delta G^{\circ} = -237.2$ kJ/mol, $E^{\circ} = 1.229$ V. - **Nernst at T:** $E = 1.229 - 0.00085(T-298) + \dfrac{RT}{2F}\ln\!\left(p_{H_2}\,p_{O_2}^{1/2}\right)$. - **Cell voltage at rated load:** ~ **0.6–0.7 V** (losses: activation + ohmic + concentration). - **Electrical efficiency:** **37–42 % (LHV)**; **CHP efficiency: 80–85 %**. - **CO tolerance:** **1–2 %** (key advantage over PEMFC's < 50 ppm). - **Current density:** typically **200–400 mA/cm²**. - **Power range:** **5 kW to 11 MW** commercial units; **UTC PC25 = 200 kW** benchmark. - **Advantages:** high CHP η, high-grade waste heat (steam), CO-tolerant, mature tech. - **Disadvantages:** expensive Pt catalyst, acid handling, slower start-up than PEMFC, lower power density than PEMFC. - **Applications:** Hospitals, hotels, offices, on-site CHP, distributed generation, data centres. - **Nernst must-know constants:** $F = 96485$ C/mol, $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K), $T$ in **Kelvin**. - **Thermoneutral voltages:** HHV basis $1.482$ V; LHV basis $1.253$ V (for H₂). - **KTU one-liner to memorise:** *"PAFC is the bridge between low-temperature PEMFC and high-temperature MCFC/SOFC, using 100 % H₃PO₄ at ~ 200 °C to achieve ~ 40 % electrical and ~ 85 % CHP efficiency with CO-tolerant reformed hydrocarbon fuel."*

topics.title: Molten Carbonate Fuel Cell
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECHT411 - ENERGY ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Molten Carbonate Fuel Cell # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Molten Carbonate Fuel Cell (MCFC)** is a high-temperature electrochemical energy conversion device that directly converts the chemical energy of a hydrocarbon fuel (typically **hydrogen**, natural gas, or reformed hydrocarbons) and an oxidant (air/oxygen) into electrical energy through an electrochemical reaction mediated by a **molten alkali carbonate salt mixture** held in a porous ceramic matrix (typically **LiAlO₂**). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** MCFC belongs to the second-generation, high-temperature fuel cell family (operating at **600 °C – 700 °C / 873 K – 973 K**). It is classified as a **liquid electrolyte fuel cell** where the ion-conducting phase is a molten eutectic of **lithium carbonate (Li₂CO₃) and potassium carbonate (K₂CO₃)**, or alternatively **sodium carbonate (Na₂CO₃) and potassium carbonate (K₂CO₃)**. | Parameter | Standard Value | |---|---| | Operating Temperature | **600 – 700 °C (873 – 973 K)** | | Electrolyte | Molten Li₂CO₃ / K₂CO₃ eutectic | | Charge Carrier | Carbonate ion (**CO₃²⁻**) | | Cathode Catalyst | NiO (Lithium-doped Nickel Oxide) | | Anode Catalyst | Ni (Porous Nickel with Cr/Al alloying) | | Matrix | Porous **LiAlO₂** | | Open Circuit Voltage | **~1.04 V** (typical operating ~0.7–0.8 V) | | Electrical Efficiency | **45 – 60 %** (LHV basis, with hybrid up to 85 %) | ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Heat-Loving Sandwich Battery" > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine a sandwich where the *bread* is two porous nickel plates (anode and cathode), the *cheese filling* is a hot, gooey molten salt that conducts only one specific ion (**CO₃²⁻**), and the *filling* is squished between two hot plates (heated to ~650 °C). When you pour hydrogen gas on one side and air + CO₂ on the other, an invisible "ion conveyor belt" (**CO₃²⁻**) carries oxygen (disguised as carbonate) from the air side to the fuel side, releasing water, CO₂, and electricity in the process. The high temperature is **not a limitation** — it is the *very reason MCFCs work*. The heat keeps the carbonate salts **molten** (hence the name) and mobile, allowing ionic conduction. This same heat also allows **internal reforming** of natural gas inside the cell stack. ## 1.3 Historical & Strategic Importance MCFCs occupy a unique position in the fuel cell hierarchy because they are the **only commercially mature fuel cell that operates directly on natural gas** without requiring an external reformer. They are the preferred technology for: - Large-scale stationary power (200 kW – 50 MW) - Industrial combined heat and power (CHP) - Hybrid cycles (MCFC + gas turbine) for utility-scale plants # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Working Principle — The Electrochemistry The defining feature of MCFC is that the **charge carrier is the carbonate ion (CO₃²⁻)**, which migrates from cathode to anode. The carbonate ion is essentially a "Trojan horse" carrying one oxygen atom from the air side to the fuel side. ### 2.1.1 Half-Cell Reactions **At the Anode (Fuel Side — Nickel electrode):** $$\text{H}_2 + \text{CO}_3^{2-} \longrightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{CO}_2 + 2e^-$$ **At the Cathode (Oxidant Side — NiO electrode):** $$\tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 + \text{CO}_2 + 2e^- \longrightarrow \text{CO}_3^{2-}$$ **Net Overall Cell Reaction:** $$\text{H}_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 + \text{CO}_{2(\text{cathode})} \longrightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{CO}_{2(\text{anode})}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Observation:** Notice that **CO₂ is consumed at the cathode and produced at the anode**. This is a unique signature of MCFC. The CO₂ produced at the anode is normally **recycled** back to the cathode inlet to sustain continuous operation — a critical design feature in KTU problems on system design. ## 2.2 Why the Carbonate Ion? — The 'Why' Behind the 'How' - At 650 °C, **aqueous electrolytes** (used in PAFC, PEMFC) would boil and decompose. - **Solid electrolytes** (used in SOFC) work, but require 800–1000 °C — costly ceramics. - **Molten carbonates** strike the ideal compromise: high enough temperature for fast kinetics (no precious metal catalysts required) but low enough for cheaper stainless-steel bipolar plates. The CO₃²⁻ ion forms naturally from O₂ + CO₂ + 2e⁻ at the cathode, and dissociates at the anode to release the oxygen atom to the fuel. ## 2.3 Thermodynamic Voltage — Reversible (Nernst) Cell EMF For the MCFC overall reaction (H₂ + ½O₂ → H₂O), the standard Gibbs free energy change at 25 °C is **ΔG° = –237.2 kJ/mol**, corresponding to a thermoneutral voltage of: $$E^\circ_{\text{th}} = \frac{-\Delta G^\circ}{nF} = \frac{237200}{2 \times 96485} \approx 1.229 \text{ V}$$ However, MCFC operates at **650 °C**, where ΔG° decreases. At 923 K, ΔG° ≈ **–198.6 kJ/mol**, giving: $$E_{\text{th}}(923\,\text{K}) = \frac{198600}{2 \times 96485} \approx 1.03 \text{ V}$$ ## 2.4 Nernst Equation for MCFC For the actual cell reaction involving gas partial pressures, the reversible cell EMF is: $$E = E^\circ + \frac{RT}{2F} \ln\left[\frac{P_{\text{H}_2} \cdot P_{\text{O}_2}^{1/2} \cdot P_{\text{CO}_2,\text{cathode}}}{P_{\text{H}_2\text{O}} \cdot P_{\text{CO}_2,\text{anode}}}\right]$$ Where: - $R$ = Universal gas constant = **8.314 J/(mol·K)** - $T$ = Absolute cell temperature (**923 K** typical) - $F$ = Faraday constant = **96485 C/mol** - $n$ = Number of electrons transferred = **2** per H₂ - $P_i$ = Partial pressure of species *i* (in atm) ## 2.5 Voltage Losses (Polarizations) The actual operating cell voltage is always less than the reversible Nernst voltage due to three irreversible losses: $$E_{\text{cell}} = E_{\text{Nernst}} - \eta_{\text{act}} - \eta_{\text{ohmic}} - \eta_{\text{conc}}$$ | Loss Type | Cause | Dominant In MCFC | |---|---|---| | **Activation (η_act)** | Sluggish electrode kinetics | Cathode (NiO) | | **Ohmic (η_ohmic)** | Ionic + electronic resistance of electrolyte, electrodes, bipolar plates | Electrolyte matrix (dominant) | | **Concentration (η_conc)** | Mass-transport limitations of gases | High current density | ## 2.6 Efficiency Expressions ### 2.6.1 Thermodynamic (Ideal) Efficiency $$\eta_{\text{th}} = \frac{\Delta G}{\Delta H} = 1 - \frac{T \cdot \Delta S}{\Delta H}$$ At 25 °C, η_th ≈ 83 % (for H₂). At 923 K, η_th drops to ~70 % (because ΔG decreases with T). ### 2.6.2 Voltage Efficiency $$\eta_v = \frac{E_{\text{cell}}}{E_{\text{Nernst}}}$$ ### 2.6.3 Current (Faradaic) Efficiency $$\eta_i = \frac{I_{\text{actual}}}{I_{\text{fuel}}}$$ ### 2.6.4 Overall Electrical Efficiency $$\eta_{\text{overall}} = \eta_{\text{th}} \times \eta_v \times \eta_i$$ ### 2.6.5 Combined Heat & Power (CHP) Efficiency $$\eta_{\text{CHP}} = \eta_{\text{elec}} + \eta_{\text{thermal}}$$ ## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Table) > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table is **the most-tested set of equations** for MCFC in KTU 2024 scheme ESE. Master it. | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $E^\circ = -\Delta G^\circ / nF$ | Standard reversible voltage | V | | 2 | $E_{\text{th}} = -\Delta H / nF$ | Thermoneutral voltage (HHV basis) | V | | 3 | $E = E^\circ + (RT/2F) \ln[\ldots]$ | Nernst equation (with CO₂ terms) | V | | 4 | $\eta_{\text{th}} = \Delta G / \Delta H$ | Thermodynamic efficiency | — | | 5 | $\eta_v = E_{\text{cell}} / E_{\text{Nernst}}$ | Voltage efficiency | — | | 6 | $\eta_{\text{overall}} = \eta_{\text{th}} \eta_v \eta_i$ | Overall efficiency | — | | 7 | $P = n \cdot E_{\text{cell}} \cdot I$ | Stack power output | W | | 8 | $V_{\text{stack}} = N \cdot E_{\text{cell}}$ | Stack voltage (N cells in series) | V | | 9 | $A_{\text{cell}} = I / j$ | Active cell area (j = current density) | m² | | 10 | $\dot{m}_{\text{fuel}} = I \cdot M_{\text{H}_2} / (n F)$ | Hydrogen consumption rate | kg/s | ## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility MCFC systems are deployed by companies like **FuelCell Energy (USA)**, **Doosan (South Korea)**, and **Ansaldo (Italy)** for: - Hospital and data center **stationary CHP** - **Landfill-gas-to-power** (because MCFC tolerates CO₂ and trace contaminants in biogas) - **Hybrid MCFC-GT cycles** where the high-temperature exhaust drives a gas turbine — combined efficiencies approach **85 % LHV** - Carbon capture integration: CO₂ from the anode exhaust is captured in **molten carbonate form** at high concentration # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Reversible Cell Voltage from First-Principles Thermodynamics ### Step 1 — Start from the Maxwell Relation for Gibbs Free Energy For a constant-temperature, constant-pressure electrochemical cell, the maximum non-expansion (electrical) work is equal to the change in Gibbs free energy: $$\Delta G = -nFE_{\text{rev}}$$ ### Step 2 — Solve for the Reversible Cell Voltage $$E_{\text{rev}} = -\frac{\Delta G}{nF}$$ ### Step 3 — Express ΔG in Terms of Reaction Quotient Q For the reaction H₂ + ½O₂ → H₂O, at any non-standard state: $$\Delta G = \Delta G^\circ + RT \ln Q$$ Where the reaction quotient (in terms of fugacities ≈ partial pressures for gases) is: $$Q = \frac{P_{\text{H}_2\text{O}}}{P_{\text{H}_2} \cdot P_{\text{O}_2}^{1/2}}$$ ### Step 4 — Substitute and Simplify $$E_{\text{rev}} = -\frac{\Delta G^\circ}{nF} - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln Q$$ $$\boxed{E_{\text{rev}} = E^\circ - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln\left[\frac{P_{\text{H}_2\text{O}}}{P_{\text{H}_2} \cdot P_{\text{O}_2}^{1/2}}\right]}$$ ### Step 5 — MCFC-Specific Modification Because MCFC requires CO₂ at the cathode and releases it at the anode, the **net reaction** is: $$\text{H}_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 + \text{CO}_{2,c} \longrightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{CO}_{2,a}$$ Hence the **Nernst equation acquires the CO₂ term** (previously absent in pure H₂/O₂ fuel cells): $$E_{\text{rev}} = E^\circ + \frac{RT}{2F} \ln\left[\frac{P_{\text{H}_2} \cdot P_{\text{O}_2}^{1/2} \cdot P_{\text{CO}_2,c}}{P_{\text{H}_2\text{O}} \cdot P_{\text{CO}_2,a}}\right]$$ > [!NOTE] > **Sign Convention Check:** In a galvanic (spontaneous) fuel cell, E > 0, and the log argument must be > 1, which is satisfied when fuel-rich gas enters the anode and oxygen-rich air enters the cathode. ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Theoretical Thermal Efficiency of MCFC ### Step 1 — Definition of Thermodynamic Efficiency The maximum fraction of the fuel's chemical energy (heating value) convertible to electricity is: $$\eta_{\text{th}} = \frac{\text{Electrical Energy Out}}{\text{Chemical Energy In}} = \frac{-\Delta G}{-\Delta H}$$ ### Step 2 — Express in Terms of Temperature Using ΔG = ΔH − TΔS $$\eta_{\text{th}} = \frac{\Delta H - T\Delta S}{\Delta H} = 1 - \frac{T\Delta S}{\Delta H}$$ ### Step 3 — Numerical Evaluation for MCFC at 923 K For the H₂ + ½O₂ → H₂O reaction: - $\Delta H_{923\text{K}} \approx -243.8 \text{ kJ/mol}$ (HHV) - $\Delta S_{923\text{K}} \approx -48.9 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$ - $\Delta G_{923\text{K}} = \Delta H - T\Delta S = -243800 - 923(-48.9) = -243800 + 45135 = -198.67 \text{ kJ/mol}$ Therefore: $$\eta_{\text{th}}(923\,\text{K}) = \frac{198.67}{243.8} = 0.8150 = 81.5\,\%$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Note:** Notice that even the *thermodynamic* efficiency is below 100 % because some of the chemical enthalpy is unavoidably released as heat (TΔS term) at the operating temperature. This rejected heat is what makes MCFC ideal for CHP applications. ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Hydrogen Consumption Rate ### Step 1 — Faraday's Law of Electrolysis (applied in reverse) For a current *I* flowing for time *t*, the moles of H₂ consumed are: $$n_{\text{H}_2} = \frac{I \cdot t}{nF} = \frac{I \cdot t}{2 \times 96485}$$ ### Step 2 — Express as Mass Flow Rate (kg/s) $$\dot{m}_{\text{H}_2} = \frac{I \cdot M_{\text{H}_2}}{2F} = \frac{I \times 2.016 \times 10^{-3}}{192970}$$ ### Step 3 — Numerical Example (Worked Out for KTU Practice) A single MCFC cell operates at $I = 200$ A. Find the H₂ consumption rate. **Conversion Logic:** Two electrons are released per H₂ molecule. So one mole of H₂ (2.016 g) is consumed per 2 Faradays of charge (2 × 96485 C). $$\dot{m}_{\text{H}_2} = \frac{200 \times 2.016 \times 10^{-3}}{2 \times 96485} = \frac{0.4032}{192970} = 2.089 \times 10^{-6} \text{ kg/s}$$ $$\dot{m}_{\text{H}_2} \approx 2.09 \times 10^{-6} \text{ kg/s} = 7.52 \text{ g/h}$$ ## 3.4 Derivation 4: Stack Voltage and Stack Power ### Step 1 — Stack Voltage For *N* identical cells connected in **electrical series** (standard for fuel cell stacks): $$V_{\text{stack}} = N \times E_{\text{cell}}$$ ### Step 2 — Stack Power $$P_{\text{stack}} = V_{\text{stack}} \times I = N \times E_{\text{cell}} \times I$$ ### Step 3 — Numerical Example A 250-cell MCFC stack operates at average cell voltage 0.75 V and current 600 A. **Step-by-step evaluation:** $$V_{\text{stack}} = 250 \times 0.75 = 187.5 \text{ V}$$ $$P_{\text{stack}} = 187.5 \times 600 = 112500 \text{ W} = 112.5 \text{ kW}$$ **Conversion Logic:** A fuel cell stack behaves electrically like batteries in series — voltage adds up, current stays the same. A typical MCFC stack voltage is 250–600 V DC, which is then inverted to AC. ## 3.5 Derivative 5: Active Cell Area from Current Density The current density *j* (A/cm² or A/m²) is the key design parameter relating cell current to cell area: $$A_{\text{cell}} = \frac{I}{j}$$ For a 250 kW MCFC at $E_{\text{cell}} = 0.75$ V operating at $j = 150 \text{ mA/cm}^2 = 1500 \text{ A/m}^2$: **Step 1 — Total current required:** $$I_{\text{total}} = \frac{P}{V_{\text{stack}}} \quad \text{(given stack voltage)}$$ **Step 2 — Per-cell current:** If $V_{\text{stack}} = 250 \times 0.75 = 187.5$ V: $$I = \frac{250000}{187.5} = 1333.3 \text{ A}$$ **Step 3 — Active cell area:** $$A = \frac{1333.3}{1500} = 0.889 \text{ m}^2 \approx 1 \text{ m}^2$$ This is the typical "1 m²" plate size used in commercial MCFC stacks. ## 3.6 Analytical Summary Matrix | Quantity | Symbol | Typical MCFC Value | Key Relationship | |---|---|---|---| | Operating temperature | T | 650 °C | E ∝ T (Nernst) | | Charge carrier | CO₃²⁻ | — | Anode → Cathode ion flux | | Anode reaction | — | H₂ + CO₃²⁻ → H₂O + CO₂ + 2e⁻ | 2 e⁻ per H₂ | | Cathode reaction | — | ½O₂ + CO₂ + 2e⁻ → CO₃²⁻ | Needs CO₂ input | | Open circuit voltage | E_oc | ~1.04 V at 923 K | Drops with load | | Operating voltage | E_cell | 0.7 – 0.8 V | E_Nernst − polarizations | | Thermodynamic η | η_th | ~81.5 % at 923 K | ΔG/ΔH | | Voltage η | η_v | ~65–75 % | E_cell/E_Nernst | | Overall electrical η | η_e | 45 – 60 % | η_th × η_v × η_i | | CHP η | η_CHP | 70 – 85 % | η_e + heat recovery | | Stack power | P | 100 kW – 50 MW | N × E_cell × I | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an MCFC System The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **functional flow** of mass, ions, and electrons through a single MCFC cell, which is the standard representation expected in KTU 2024 scheme ESE answers for full-mark scoring. ```mermaid flowchart TB FUELIN["Hydrogen or Reformed Natural Gas
FUEL INLET"]:::inlet AIRIN["Air plus Recycled CO2
OXIDANT INLET"]:::inlet ANODE["POROUS NICKEL ANODE
Ni with Cr or Al alloy
Temperature 923 K"]:::electrode CATHODE["LITHIUM DOPED NiO CATHODE
In situ lithiated NiO
Temperature 923 K"]:::electrode ELECTROLYTE["MOLTEN CARBONATE ELECTROLYTE
Li2CO3 plus K2CO3 eutectic
Held in porous LiAlO2 matrix"]:::core CO3ION["CARBONATE ION FLUX
CO3 two minus migrates cathode to anode
Carries oxygen atom"]:::ion ELECTRON["ELECTRON FLOW
Through external circuit
Anode to Cathode via load"]:::e WATER["WATER AND CO2 OUT
Exhaust from anode side
CO2 recycled to cathode inlet"]:::outlet O2GEN["OXYGEN CONSUMED
From air at cathode
Forms carbonate ion"]:::inlet HEAT["WASTE HEAT 650 C
Available for CHP or bottoming cycle"]:::heat LOAD["ELECTRICAL LOAD
DC bus and inverter"]:::load FUELIN --> ANODE AIRIN --> CATHODE ANODE ---|"H2 + CO3(2-) yields H2O + CO2 + 2e(-)"| ELECTROLYTE ELECTROLYTE ---|"1/2 O2 + CO2 + 2e(-) yields CO3(2-)"| CATHODE ELECTROLYTE -.->|"CO3(2-) migration"| CO3ION CO3ION --> ANODE ANODE ==>|"Electrons e(-)| DC circuit"| LOAD LOAD ==>|"Return electrons e(-)| DC circuit"| CATHODE ANODE --> WATER CATHODE --> O2GEN ANODE --> HEAT classDef inlet fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef electrode fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#fd7e14,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef core fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,stroke-width:3px,color:#000 classDef ion fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef e fill:#e2e3e5,stroke:#6c757d,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef outlet fill:#fde2e4,stroke:#d63384,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef heat fill:#ffe5b4,stroke:#ff8c00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef load fill:#d0bfff,stroke:#6610f2,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` ## 4.2 Component-Level Architecture of a Full MCFC Plant ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph FUEL_PREP ["FUEL PROCESSING UNIT"] NG["Natural Gas CH4"] REFORM["External Reformer or Internal Reforming
Steam reforming CH4 plus H2O yields 3H2 plus CO"] H2R["Reformed Hydrogen Rich Gas"] NG --> REFORM --> H2R end subgraph STACK ["MCFC STACK ASSEMBLY"] C1["Cell 1"] C2["Cell 2"] CN["Cell N in series
250 to 400 cells typical"] BIP["Bipolar Plates
Stainless steel with Ni cladding"] C1 --> BIP --> C2 --> BIP --> CN end subgraph BOP ["BALANCE OF PLANT BOP"] HX["Heat Exchanger
Recovers 650 C exhaust heat"] RECYCLE["CO2 Recycler
Anode CO2 to cathode inlet"] PUMP["Re-circulation Blower"] INV["DC to AC Inverter"] HX --> PUMP RECYCLE --> STACK STACK --> INV end H2R --> STACK STACK --> HX HX --> RECYCLE INV --> GRID["AC Electrical Grid Output"] classDef process fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#000 classDef stack fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#fd7e14,color:#000 classDef bop fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,color:#000 class NG,REFORM,H2R process class C1,C2,CN,BIP stack class HX,RECYCLE,PUMP,INV bop ``` ## 4.3 Performance Curve — Polarization Behavior of MCFC ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Region 1: Activation Loss
Dominant at low current density
j less than 50 mA per cm2"] --> B["Region 2: Ohmic Loss
Linear drop with current
j between 50 and 200 mA per cm2"] --> C["Region 3: Concentration Loss
Sharp drop at high current
j greater than 200 mA per cm2"] A:::reg1 B:::reg2 C:::reg3 classDef reg1 fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,color:#000 classDef reg2 fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#fd7e14,color:#000 classDef reg3 fill:#fde2e4,stroke:#d63384,color:#000 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Visualization Tip for KTU Answers:** Always include a sketch showing **E_cell vs current density (j)** — this is one of the highest-scoring 1-mark additions in KTU ESE answers on fuel cells. MCFC polarization curves have a characteristic **steep activation region at the cathode (NiO)** because O₂ reduction on NiO is kinetically slow, followed by a long linear ohmic region dominated by electrolyte matrix resistance. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern:** Part A consists of compulsory short-answer questions, each carrying **3 marks**. The model answer length should be **5–8 sentences** with any one supporting equation or diagram. ### Question A.1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **"List the half-cell reactions occurring in a Molten Carbonate Fuel Cell and identify the charge carrier."** **Model Answer:** In a Molten Carbonate Fuel Cell (MCFC), the half-cell reactions are as follows. At the **anode** (porous nickel electrode), hydrogen reacts with the carbonate ion to form water, carbon dioxide, and two electrons: **Anode:** $\text{H}_2 + \text{CO}_3^{2-} \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{CO}_2 + 2e^-$ At the **cathode** (lithium-doped NiO electrode), oxygen and carbon dioxide combine with the returning electrons to regenerate the carbonate ion: **Cathode:** $\tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 + \text{CO}_2 + 2e^- \rightarrow \text{CO}_3^{2-}$ The **charge carrier is the carbonate ion (CO₃²⁻)**, which migrates from the cathode to the anode through the molten Li₂CO₃/K₂CO₃ electrolyte held in a porous LiAlO₂ matrix. The overall cell reaction is H₂ + ½O₂ + CO₂(cathode) → H₂O + CO₂(anode). The unique feature of MCFC is that CO₂ must be supplied at the cathode inlet and is recycled from the anode exhaust. --- ### Question A.2 [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **"Why is a molten carbonate mixture used as the electrolyte in MCFC and what role does the operating temperature play?"** **Model Answer:** A molten carbonate mixture (typically a eutectic of **Li₂CO₃ and K₂CO₃**, melting point ~488 °C) is used because it provides a high concentration of mobile **CO₃²⁻ ions** that serve as the charge carrier. The high operating temperature of **600–700 °C (873–973 K)** is essential for three reasons. First, it keeps the carbonate salts in the **molten (liquid) state**, which is necessary for ionic conduction. Second, the elevated temperature enables **fast electrochemical kinetics** at the electrodes, eliminating the need for expensive precious-metal catalysts like platinum. Third, the high temperature permits **internal reforming** of hydrocarbon fuels (e.g., methane) directly within the cell stack, reducing system cost and complexity. However, this high temperature also accelerates **electrolyte corrosion** of cell components, which is a key lifetime-limiting factor of MCFC technology. --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Part B questions carry **14 marks** and have **internal choice** (either-or).
