Each long question is divided into two 7-mark sub-parts. ### Question B — CHOICE A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024] **"With the help of a neat schematic, explain the construction, working principle and electrode reactions of a Molten Carbonate Fuel Cell. Also derive the Nernst equation for the same."** #### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Construction and Working Principle **Model Answer:** **1. Construction of MCFC:** A single MCFC unit cell is a sandwich structure with the following components: (i) **Anode:** A porous **nickel (Ni)** electrode, often alloyed with **chromium (Cr) or aluminum (Al)** (typically 2–10 wt %) to prevent creep and sintering at 650 °C. Porosity ~50–70 % with pore size 1–10 μm. Thickness 0.5–1.5 mm. (ii) **Cathode:** **Lithium-doped NiO** (NiO + Li⁺) formed in situ by oxidation of nickel with Li₂CO₃. The Li⁺ doping improves electronic conductivity and reduces dissolution. Porosity ~50–70 %. (iii) **Electrolyte:** Molten eutectic of **62 mol% Li₂CO₃ + 38 mol% K₂CO₃** (melting point ~488 °C) or alternatively (Na₂CO₃ + K₂CO₃). The electrolyte is immobilized in a porous **LiAlO₂ matrix** (50–60 % porosity, thickness 0.5–1.0 mm) by capillary action. (iv) **Bipolar Plates:** Either austenitic **stainless steel (310S, 316L)** or nickel-clad steel, with flow channels for fuel and air distribution. (v) **Manifolds and Seals:** Wet seals or compressible gaskets prevent gas crossover. **2. Working Principle:** Hydrogen-rich fuel (reformed natural gas or pure H₂) is fed to the anode side, while air mixed with recycled CO₂ is fed to the cathode side. At the cathode, O₂ and CO₂ combine with electrons (returning from the external circuit) to form CO₃²⁻ ions. These ions migrate through the molten electrolyte to the anode, where they oxidize H₂, releasing H₂O, CO₂, and 2 electrons. The electrons flow through the external circuit to provide electrical power. The CO₂ produced at the anode is separated and recycled back to the cathode inlet to maintain continuous operation. **(4 marks for schematic + reactions, 3 marks for explanation)** #### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Derivation of Nernst Equation **Model Answer:** For the overall MCFC reaction: $$\text{H}_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 + \text{CO}_{2,c} \longrightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{CO}_{2,a}$$ The Gibbs free energy change is: $$\Delta G = \Delta G^\circ + RT \ln Q$$ Where the reaction quotient (using partial pressures in atm) is: $$Q = \frac{P_{\text{H}_2\text{O}} \cdot P_{\text{CO}_2,a}}{P_{\text{H}_2} \cdot P_{\text{O}_2}^{1/2} \cdot P_{\text{CO}_2,c}}$$ The maximum reversible cell EMF is related to ΔG by: $$E_{\text{rev}} = -\frac{\Delta G}{nF}$$ Substituting: $$E_{\text{rev}} = -\frac{\Delta G^\circ}{nF} - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln Q$$ Since $E^\circ = -\Delta G^\circ / nF$ and noting the sign flip when moving the log term to the numerator: $$E_{\text{rev}} = E^\circ + \frac{RT}{2F} \ln\left[\frac{P_{\text{H}_2} \cdot P_{\text{O}_2}^{1/2} \cdot P_{\text{CO}_2,c}}{P_{\text{H}_2\text{O}} \cdot P_{\text{CO}_2,a}}\right]$$ **Substitution of numerical values at T = 923 K:** For standard conditions at 923 K: $E^\circ \approx 1.03$ V, $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K), $F = 96485$ C/mol, $n = 2$. Thermal voltage term: $$\frac{RT}{2F} = \frac{8.314 \times 923}{2 \times 96485} = \frac{7673.8}{192970} = 0.03976 \text{ V} \approx 39.76 \text{ mV}$$ **Example numerical evaluation** (assume $P_{\text{H}_2} = 0.8$, $P_{\text{O}_2} = 0.15$, $P_{\text{CO}_2,c} = 0.2$, $P_{\text{H}_2\text{O}} = 0.2$, $P_{\text{CO}_2,a} = 0.2$ atm): $$\ln\left[\frac{0.8 \times (0.15)^{1/2} \times 0.2}{0.2 \times 0.2}\right] = \ln\left[\frac{0.8 \times 0.387 \times 0.2}{0.04}\right] = \ln(1.548) = 0.4366$$ $$E_{\text{rev}} = 1.03 + 0.03976 \times 0.4366 = 1.03 + 0.01736 = 1.0474 \text{ V}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Many students make the mistake of writing the Nernst equation for MCFC **without the CO₂ partial pressure terms**, using the form valid for PEMFC or PAFC. In MCFC, CO₂ appears in **BOTH** the numerator (cathode) and denominator (anode) of the log term. Forgetting this loses **2 full marks** in derivation questions. Also, do not confuse E° (1.03 V at 923 K) with E°_H₂O (1.229 V at 25 °C) — both are valid but for different temperatures. --- ### Question B — CHOICE B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2023] **"Discuss the various losses in a Molten Carbonate Fuel Cell with suitable equations. A 300-cell MCFC stack operates at an average cell voltage of 0.75 V and a current of 500 A. Calculate (i) the stack voltage, (ii) the stack power output, and (iii) the mass of hydrogen consumed per hour, assuming 100 % current efficiency."** #### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Voltage Losses (Polarizations) **Model Answer:** The actual operating voltage of an MCFC is always less than the thermodynamic Nernst voltage due to three irreversible losses: $$E_{\text{cell}} = E_{\text{Nernst}} - \eta_{\text{act}} - \eta_{\text{ohmic}} - \eta_{\text{conc}}$$ **(1) Activation Polarization (η_act):** This arises from the **sluggish kinetics of charge transfer** at the electrode-electrolyte interface. The Tafel equation describes it: $$\eta_{\text{act}} = \frac{RT}{\alpha n F} \ln\left(\frac{j}{j_0}\right)$$ where $\alpha$ is the transfer coefficient, $j$ is the current density, and $j_0$ is the exchange current density. In MCFC, activation loss is dominated by the **cathode (NiO)** side because O₂ reduction has slow kinetics. **(2) Ohmic Polarization (η_ohmic):** This is due to **ionic resistance** of the electrolyte matrix and **electronic resistance** of electrodes and bipolar plates. It follows Ohm's law: $$\eta_{\text{ohmic}} = j \cdot R_{\text{ASR}}$$ where $R_{\text{ASR}}$ is the **area-specific resistance** of the cell (typically 0.3–0.5 Ω·cm² for MCFC). Ohmic loss is the **dominant loss** in MCFC and is minimized by reducing electrolyte matrix thickness. **(3) Concentration Polarization (η_conc):** This occurs at **high current densities** when reactant gases cannot be supplied fast enough to the electrode. It is expressed as: $$\eta_{\text{conc}} = -\frac{RT}{nF} \ln\left(1 - \frac{j}{j_L}\right)$$ where $j_L$ is the **limiting current density**. In MCFC, $j_L$ for the cathode is typically 600–1000 mA/cm². #### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Numerical Problem **Given:** N = 300 cells, E_cell = 0.75 V, I = 500 A, η_i = 100 % **(i) Stack Voltage:** Using the standard series-connection formula: $$V_{\text{stack}} = N \times E_{\text{cell}}$$ $$V_{\text{stack}} = 300 \times 0.75 = 225 \text{ V}$$ **Conversion Logic:** Each cell contributes its terminal voltage; connecting 300 cells in series adds the voltages like batteries in series while the current remains 500 A through all cells. **(ii) Stack Power Output:** $$P_{\text{stack}} = V_{\text{stack}} \times I$$ $$P_{\text{stack}} = 225 \times 500 = 112500 \text{ W} = 112.5 \text{ kW}$$ **Conversion Logic:** Electrical power is the product of voltage and current (DC values for the stack before the inverter). **(iii) Mass of Hydrogen Consumed Per Hour:** Faraday's law gives the moles of H₂ consumed per second: $$\dot{n}_{\text{H}_2} = \frac{I}{nF} = \frac{500}{2 \times 96485} = 2.591 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mol/s}$$ Converting to mass flow rate (M_H₂ = 2.016 g/mol = 2.016 × 10⁻³ kg/mol): $$\dot{m}_{\text{H}_2} = 2.591 \times 10^{-3} \times 2.016 \times 10^{-3} = 5.223 \times 10^{-6} \text{ kg/s}$$ Mass per hour: $$m_{\text{H}_2,\text{hour}} = 5.223 \times 10^{-6} \times 3600 = 0.0188 \text{ kg/h} = 18.8 \text{ g/h}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Common mistakes in this problem include: (1) using $n = 4$ instead of $n = 2$ (which would be correct for direct methanol or for O₂ reduction; for H₂ oxidation, $n = 2$ is correct), (2) forgetting to convert seconds to hours, (3) using the molecular mass 1 g/mol instead of 2.016 g/mol. Any of these will cost **2 marks** in a 7-mark sub-question. Always state the value of *n* explicitly before applying Faraday's law. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Last-Minute Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE — Molten Carbonate Fuel Cell** - **MCFC** = Molten Carbonate Fuel Cell, a high-temperature (600–700 °C) fuel cell. - **Electrolyte:** Molten Li₂CO₃/K₂CO₃ eutectic immobilized in porous LiAlO₂ matrix. - **Charge carrier:** Carbonate ion **CO₃²⁻** (migrates cathode → anode). - **Anode:** Porous Ni (+ Cr/Al alloy); reaction: **H₂ + CO₃²⁻ → H₂O + CO₂ + 2e⁻**. - **Cathode:** In-situ lithiated NiO; reaction: **½O₂ + CO₂ + 2e⁻ → CO₃²⁻**. - **Overall:** **H₂ + ½O₂ + CO₂(c) → H₂O + CO₂(a)**. - **CO₂ recycling** from anode exhaust back to cathode inlet is a unique design requirement of MCFC. - **Nernst equation for MCFC** uniquely contains BOTH CO₂ partial pressures: $E = E^\circ + (RT/2F) \ln\left[\frac{P_{\text{H}_2} \cdot P_{\text{O}_2}^{1/2} \cdot P_{\text{CO}_2,c}}{P_{\text{H}_2\text{O}} \cdot P_{\text{CO}_2,a}}\right]$ - **No precious metal catalyst** required — high temperature enables Ni/NiO catalysis. - **Internal reforming** of CH₄ is possible: CH₄ + H₂O → 3H₂ + CO (occurs in the anode compartment). - **Three voltage losses:** Activation (dominant at cathode), Ohmic (dominant in electrolyte matrix — biggest in MCFC), Concentration (at high j). - **Electrical efficiency:** 45–60 % LHV standalone; up to **85 %** in MCFC + gas turbine hybrid. - **Thermodynamic efficiency** at 923 K: η_th = ΔG/ΔH ≈ **81.5 %**. - **Standard reversible voltage** at 25 °C: 1.229 V; at 923 K: ~**1.03 V**. - **Cell voltage under load:** typically 0.7–0.8 V (efficiency ratio η_v ≈ 65–75 %). - **Faraday's law** for H₂ consumption: $\dot{m}_{\text{H}_2} = I \cdot M_{\text{H}_2} / (2F)$. - **Stack voltage** = N × E_cell (series connection). - **Advantages:** high efficiency, fuel flexibility (H₂, NG, biogas, coal gas), internal reforming, CO₂ separation bonus. - **Disadvantages:** slow startup (must reach 650 °C), electrolyte corrosion, CO₂ recycling complexity, short lifetime (~40,000 h). - **Applications:** Large-scale stationary power (200 kW–50 MW), CHP, biogas plants, hybrid MCFC-GT utility plants. - **Key manufacturers:** FuelCell Energy (USA), Doosan Fuel Cell (S. Korea), Ansaldo Fuel Cells (Italy). - **Comparison memory trick:** MCFC uses **liquid** electrolyte, PAFC uses **liquid phosphoric acid**, SOFC uses **solid** ceramic, PEMFC uses **solid polymer membrane**. - **Numerical constants to memorize:** R = 8.314 J/(mol·K), F = 96485 C/mol, M_H₂ = 2.016 g/mol, M_O₂ = 32 g/mol, M_CO₂ = 44 g/mol.

topics.title: Solid Oxide Fuel Cell
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT411 - ENERGY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Solid Oxide Fuel Cell

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Solid Oxide Fuel Cell (SOFC)** is a high-temperature, solid-state electrochemical energy conversion device that directly converts the chemical energy of a fuel (typically hydrogen, natural gas, or hydrocarbons) and an oxidant (oxygen from air) into electrical energy through an electrochemical reaction, using a **solid ceramic electrolyte** that conducts oxygen ions (O²⁻) from the cathode to the anode.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** SOFCs belong to the **high-temperature fuel cell** category and operate in the temperature range of **600°C – 1000°C**. They are characterized by their solid, non-porous, oxygen-ion-conducting ceramic electrolyte (most commonly **Yttria-Stabilized Zirconia, YSZ**).

### Key Operating Parameters

| Parameter | Value / Description |
|---|---|
| Operating Temperature | **600°C – 1000°C** |
| Electrolyte Material | **Yttria-Stabilized Zirconia (YSZ) — ZrO₂(Y₂O₃)** |
| Charge Carrier | **Oxygen ion (O²⁻)** |
| Fuel (at Anode) | H₂, CO, CH₄, hydrocarbons |
| Oxidant (at Cathode) | O₂ from atmospheric air |
| Electrical Efficiency | **50% – 60%** (standalone) |
| Combined Heat & Power (CHP) Efficiency | **85% – 90%** |
| Standard Cell Potential (E°) | **1.229 V** at 25°C for H₂/O₂ |
| Power Output Range | **1 kW – multi-MW** |

> [!NOTE]
> The high operating temperature eliminates the need for precious metal catalysts (like platinum in PEMFCs) and enables the use of various hydrocarbon fuels via **internal reforming**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy: The "Hot Sandwich Battery"

Imagine a **hot sandwich** that breathes and produces electricity:

- **Top slice (Cathode):** A porous, sponge-like ceramic that "inhales" oxygen from the air.
- **Filling (Electrolyte):** A thin, dense ceramic layer that is a perfect filter — it only allows oxygen atoms (with their negative charge) to pass through, blocking everything else.
- **Bottom slice (Anode):** A porous layer that receives a fuel gas (like hydrogen) and "combines" it with the arriving oxygen ions to release electricity and water/CO₂ as exhaust.

The key insight: **combustion is replaced by controlled, electrochemical "burning"** — fuel is not ignited, but is chemically combined at the atomic level, capturing electrons directly as electricity. The high temperature is essential to make the ceramic "filling" conductive to oxygen ions (at room temperature, it would behave like a brick and block all ion flow).

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Deployment:** SOFCs are used in **stationary power generation**, **auxiliary power units (APUs)** for trucks, **residential CHP systems** (e.g., Bloom Energy Servers), and **large-scale industrial power plants**. They are the preferred choice for applications where waste heat can be productively used.

---

## 1.3 Why SOFC? Engineering Motivation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Engineering Advantage:** SOFCs offer **fuel flexibility** (can directly use natural gas, biogas, syngas), **high efficiency**, **no liquid electrolyte management** (unlike MCFC or PAFC), and **long-term stability** when operated within thermal cycling limits.

- **No precious metal catalysts** required.
- **Solid electrolyte** → no leakage, no corrosion, no liquid handling.
- **High-grade waste heat** suitable for cogeneration or bottoming cycles.
- **Modular scalability** from residential (1 kW) to grid-scale (multi-MW).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — Structured Logic Breakdown

The operation of an SOFC follows a precise sequence of physicochemical steps, each occurring at one of the three functional layers:

### Step 1: Oxidant Supply at the Cathode (Air Side)
- Air (O₂ + N₂) is supplied to the porous cathode.
- Oxygen molecules diffuse to the **Triple Phase Boundary (TPB)** — the critical interface where gas, electrolyte, and electrode meet.
- At the cathode, oxygen molecules gain electrons (from the external circuit) and **reduce** to oxygen ions:

$$\frac{1}{2}O_2 + 2e^- \rightarrow O^{2-}$$

### Step 2: Oxygen Ion Migration Through the Electrolyte
- The dense YSZ electrolyte conducts **O²⁻ ions** from cathode to anode.
- Conduction occurs via **oxygen vacancies** in the zirconia crystal lattice (created by Y₂O₃ doping).
- The high temperature (**800–1000°C**) is essential to overcome activation energy for ion hopping.
- Electrons **cannot** pass through the electrolyte (it is an electronic insulator), forcing them to flow through the external circuit → **this is the source of useful electrical work**.

### Step 3: Fuel Oxidation at the Anode (Fuel Side)
- Fuel (H₂ or CO) reaches the porous anode.
- It reacts with the arriving O²⁻ ions, releasing electrons to the external circuit:

$$H_2 + O^{2-} \rightarrow H_2O + 2e^-$$

$$CO + O^{2-} \rightarrow CO_2 + 2e^-$$

### Step 4: External Current Flow
- Electrons travel from the anode through the external load to the cathode, doing useful work.
- Mass balance and charge balance are perfectly maintained.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the "Why" Matters:** The electrolyte's selectivity (ions only, no electrons) is the fundamental reason the cell produces a voltage. Without this property, electrons would simply short-circuit internally and no current would flow externally.

---

## 2.2 Thermodynamic Foundation

The maximum electrical work obtainable from the fuel cell reaction equals the **Gibbs free energy change (ΔG)**:

$$\Delta G = -nFE$$

Where:
- **n** = number of electrons transferred per mole of fuel (**n = 2** for H₂)
- **F** = Faraday's constant = **96,485 C/mol**
- **E** = reversible cell potential (V)

For the standard hydrogen–oxygen reaction:

$$H_2 + \frac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow H_2O_{(l)}$$

At standard conditions (25°C, 1 atm):
- **ΔG° = −237.2 kJ/mol**
- **ΔH° = −285.8 kJ/mol**
- **E° = 1.229 V**

The **thermodynamic (reversible) efficiency** of the fuel cell is:

$$\eta_{rev} = \frac{\Delta G}{\Delta H} = \frac{237.2}{285.8} = 0.830 \;(83\%)$$

This is the **upper bound** — a Carnot cycle burning the same fuel at 1000°C can never exceed ~80% efficiency. The fuel cell approaches this limit without any moving parts.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are essential for solving KTU Part A (3-mark) and Part B (14-mark) questions on SOFC.**

| Formula / Equation | Description | Typical Use in KTU Problems |
|---|---|---|
| $E = -\dfrac{\Delta G}{nF}$ | Reversible cell potential from Gibbs energy | 3-mark / 14-mark derivations |
| $E^0 = \dfrac{-\Delta G^0}{nF}$ | Standard cell potential | Numerical: Compute E° from ΔG° |
| $E = E^0 + \dfrac{RT}{nF} \ln \left( \dfrac{P_{H_2} \cdot P_{O_2}^{1/2}}{P_{H_2O}} \right)$ | Nernst equation (SOFC, H₂ fuel) | 14-mark derivations, numerical |
| $E = E^0 + \dfrac{RT}{nF} \ln \left( \dfrac{P_{CO} \cdot P_{O_2}^{1/2}}{P_{CO_2}} \right)$ | Nernst equation (SOFC, CO fuel) | 14-mark |
| $\eta_{rev} = \dfrac{\Delta G}{\Delta H}$ | Thermodynamic efficiency | 3-mark conceptual |
| $\eta_{actual} = \dfrac{\text{Useful electrical output}}{\Delta H \cdot \dot{n}_{fuel}}$ | Practical cell efficiency | 14-mark |
| $V_{cell} = E - \eta_{act} - \eta_{ohm} - \eta_{conc}$ | Actual cell voltage (loss breakdown) | 14-mark |
| $\eta_{act} = \dfrac{RT}{\alpha n F} \ln \left( \dfrac{i}{i_0} \right)$ | Activation overpotential (Butler-Volmer, simplified Tafel form) | 14-mark |
| $\eta_{ohm} = i \cdot R_{ASR}$ | Ohmic overpotential (R<sub>ASR</sub> = area-specific resistance) | 14-mark |
| $\eta_{conc} = \dfrac{RT}{nF} \ln \left( \dfrac{i_L}{i_L - i} \right)$ | Concentration overpotential | 14-mark |
| $P = V_{cell} \cdot i \cdot A$ | Power output (A = active cell area) | Numerical |
| $\eta_{util,fuel} = \dfrac{i}{n F \dot{n}_{fuel}}$ | Fuel utilization factor | Numerical |
| $E_{T,SATP} = 1.253 - 0.000245(T - 298)$ | Empirical Nernst voltage for H₂/H₂O at 1 atm (T in K) | Short numerical |
| $F = 96485 \; C/mol$ | Faraday constant | Universal |
| $R = 8.314 \; J/(mol \cdot K)$ | Universal gas constant | Universal |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** In SOFC problems, the temperature term is dominant because of the high operating temperature. Always convert T to **Kelvin** (K = °C + 273) before substitution. Watch out for 800°C = **1073 K** — this is the most commonly tested value.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Stationary Power:** SOFCs are deployed in data centers, hospitals, and industrial CHP plants (1 kW to 50 MW).
- **Auxiliary Power Units (APU):** Heavy-duty truck APUs use SOFCs to power cabin electrics while the engine is off, reducing idling emissions.
- **Hybrid Systems:** SOFC + Gas Turbine (GT) bottoming cycles achieve **70%–80% LHV efficiency** in large installations.
- **Carbon Capture Compatibility:** The exhaust stream is concentrated CO₂ (when hydrocarbon fuels are used), making SOFCs attractive for near-zero-emission power with CO₂ sequestration.
- **Hydrogen Economy Bridge:** SOFCs can run on **blue hydrogen** (reformed natural gas with carbon capture) as a transition fuel.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Reversible Cell Potential from Gibbs Free Energy

**Goal:** Derive the relationship between the Gibbs free energy change and the cell EMF for an SOFC.

**Step 1 — Starting Point (First Law of Thermodynamics for an Electrochemical Cell):**
The maximum non-PV (non-expansion) work obtainable from a chemical reaction at constant T and P is equal to the change in Gibbs free energy:

$$W_{max} = -\Delta G$$

**Step 2 — Electrical Work Done by the Cell:**
When a current I flows for time t through a load at potential E, the electrical work is:

$$W_{elec} = Q \cdot E = (n \cdot F \cdot \text{moles}) \cdot E$$

**Step 3 — Equate the Two Expressions:**
For 1 mole of fuel undergoing the complete reaction with electron transfer n:

$$-\Delta G = nFE$$

**Step 4 — Solve for E:**

$$\boxed{E = -\frac{\Delta G}{nF}}$$

**Step 5 — Standard Conditions:**
At 25°C, 1 atm, for the H₂ + ½O₂ → H₂O(l) reaction:

$$E^0 = \frac{237200 \; J/mol}{(2)(96485 \; C/mol)} = 1.229 \; V$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Nernst Equation for SOFC (Hydrogen Fuel)

**Goal:** Derive the voltage of an SOFC as a function of partial pressures of reactants and products.

**Step 1 — Consider the General Reaction:**

$$H_2 + \frac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow H_2O$$

**Step 2 — Gibbs Free Energy as a Function of Activity:**
At non-standard conditions, the change in Gibbs free energy is:

$$\Delta G = \Delta G^0 + RT \ln \left( \frac{a_{H_2O}}{a_{H_2} \cdot a_{O_2}^{1/2}} \right)$$

**Step 3 — Replace Activities with Partial Pressures (Ideal Gas Assumption):**
For gases, activity aᵢ = Pᵢ / P° (where P° = 1 atm):

$$\Delta G = \Delta G^0 + RT \ln \left( \frac{P_{H_2O} / P^0}{(P_{H_2}/P^0)(P_{O_2}/P^0)^{1/2}} \right)$$

Since P° = 1 atm, this simplifies to:

$$\Delta G = \Delta G^0 + RT \ln \left( \frac{P_{H_2O}}{P_{H_2} \cdot P_{O_2}^{1/2}} \right)$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into the EMF Equation:**

$$E = -\frac{\Delta G}{nF} = -\frac{\Delta G^0}{nF} - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln \left( \frac{P_{H_2O}}{P_{H_2} \cdot P_{O_2}^{1/2}} \right)$$

**Step 5 — Recognize E° and Flip the Logarithm Argument:**

$$\boxed{E = E^0 + \frac{RT}{nF} \ln \left( \frac{P_{H_2} \cdot P_{O_2}^{1/2}}{P_{H_2O}} \right)}$$

**Step 6 — Special Case: Temperature Correction for SOFC:**
At the SOFC operating temperature, water is produced as **steam** (not liquid). The Nernst equation is therefore referenced to H₂O vapor, and the standard potential is temperature-dependent. The empirical form is:

$$E_T = 1.253 - 0.000245(T - 298) \; V \quad (T \; in \; K)$$

This linear relationship is widely used in KTU numerical problems.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Efficiency of an SOFC

**Goal:** Establish the theoretical and actual efficiency expressions.

**Step 1 — Thermodynamic (Reversible) Efficiency:**

$$\eta_{rev} = \frac{\text{Electrical energy out (max)}}{\text{Heat of combustion (input)}} = \frac{\Delta G}{\Delta H}$$

**Step 2 — Voltage-Based Efficiency:**
Since ΔG = −nFE and ΔH (per mole) = LHV (lower heating value) for the fuel:

$$\eta_{rev} = \frac{nFE^0}{LHV}$$

**Step 3 — Actual (Operating) Efficiency:**
The actual cell voltage is always less than E° due to irreversibilities. If the operating voltage is V_cell at current density i:

$$\eta_{actual} = \frac{nFV_{cell}}{LHV} = \eta_{rev} \cdot \frac{V_{cell}}{E^0}$$

**Step 4 — Voltage Efficiency:**
Define voltage efficiency as:

$$\eta_V = \frac{V_{cell}}{E} = 1 - \frac{\eta_{act} + \eta_{ohm} + \eta_{conc}}{E}$$

The actual efficiency is the product:

$$\boxed{\eta_{actual} = \eta_{rev} \cdot \eta_V \cdot \eta_{fuel}}$$

Where η_fuel is the **fuel utilization factor** (fraction of fuel actually consumed electrochemically).

---

## 3.4 Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** An SOFC operates at 1000°C with the following gas partial pressures at the electrodes:
- P_H₂ = 0.8 atm
- P_O₂ = 0.21 atm
- P_H₂O = 0.3 atm

Calculate: (a) the standard cell potential E°, (b) the actual reversible potential E at operating conditions, and (c) the cell efficiency based on LHV of H₂ = 241.8 kJ/mol.

### Solution:

**(a) Standard Cell Potential at 1000°C (1273 K):**

Using the empirical relation (valid for 800–1240 K):

$$E^0_{1273} = 1.253 - 0.000245(1273 - 298)$$

$$E^0_{1273} = 1.253 - 0.000245(975) = 1.253 - 0.239 = 1.014 \; V$$

**(b) Reversible Cell Potential at Operating Conditions:**

$$E = E^0 + \frac{RT}{2F} \ln \left( \frac{P_{H_2} \cdot P_{O_2}^{1/2}}{P_{H_2O}} \right)$$

Substituting values:

$$E = 1.014 + \frac{(8.314)(1273)}{2(96485)} \ln \left( \frac{0.8 \times (0.21)^{1/2}}{0.3} \right)$$

Compute each piece step by step:

$$\frac{RT}{2F} = \frac{(8.314)(1273)}{192970} = \frac{10584}{192970} = 0.05485 \; V$$

$$\sqrt{0.21} = 0.4583$$

$$\text{Argument} = \frac{0.8 \times 0.4583}{0.3} = \frac{0.3666}{0.3} = 1.222$$

$$\ln(1.222) = 0.2006$$

$$E = 1.014 + (0.05485)(0.2006) = 1.014 + 0.0110 = 1.025 \; V$$

**(c) Thermodynamic Efficiency:**

$$\eta_{rev} = \frac{nFE}{LHV} = \frac{(2)(96485)(1.025)}{241800} = \frac{197754}{241800} = 0.818 \; (81.8\%)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** The Nernst term (0.011 V) is small compared to E° at this condition. The slight gain in E is due to the higher partial pressure of reactants than products, favoring forward reaction.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 4: Cell Voltage Loss Breakdown

**Goal:** Show that the actual terminal voltage equals the reversible voltage minus all irreversibilities.

**Starting Equation:**

$$V_{cell} = E - \eta_{act} - \eta_{ohm} - \eta_{conc}$$

**Justification of Each Term:**

- **η_act (Activation Overpotential):** Energy barrier to be overcome for electrochemical reactions at the electrodes. Dominant at low current densities. Modeled by the Tafel equation (shown in formula sheet).

- **η_ohm (Ohmic Overpotential):** Resistive losses due to ionic conduction through the electrolyte and electronic conduction through electrodes and interconnects. Linear with current density:

$$\eta_{ohm} = i \cdot R_{ASR}$$

Where R<sub>ASR</sub> is the area-specific resistance (Ω·cm²). For a typical YSZ electrolyte at 1000°C: R<sub>ASR</sub> ≈ **0.3 Ω·cm²**.

- **η_conc (Concentration Overpotential):** Mass-transport limitation when reactant supply cannot keep up with reaction rate at high currents. Dominant at high current densities. Modeled by the limiting current density i_L:

$$\eta_{conc} = \frac{RT}{nF} \ln \left( \frac{i_L}{i_L - i} \right)$$

The actual cell power is then:

$$P_{cell} = V_{cell} \cdot i \cdot A$$

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 SOFC Layered Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

The diagram below maps the **functional architecture** of a single SOFC cell, showing material layers, flow streams, and electron/ion pathways. This is the most commonly expected schematic in KTU 14-mark answers.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1[Air Inlet: O2 plus N2] --> B1[Cathode: Porous Lanthanum Strontium Manganite LSM]
    B1 -->|Reduction half-reaction| C1[Electrolyte: Dense YSZ Yttria Stabilized Zirconia]
    C1 -->|O2- ion conduction 800 to 1000 C| D1[Anode: Porous Nickel YSZ Cermet]
    E1[Fuel Inlet: H2 or CO or CH4] --> D1
    D1 -->|Oxidation half-reaction produces H2O plus CO2| F1[Exhaust: H2O plus CO2]
    B1 -->|Electron flow e-| G1[External Circuit: Useful Electrical Load]
    G1 -->|Electron return e-| D1
    C1 -.->|Block electrons: forces them through external circuit| G1
    H1[Heat Exchanger: Recovers waste heat] -.->|Boiler or CHP| B1

    classDef gasFlow fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#0277BD,color:#000
    classDef electrode fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100,color:#000
    classDef electrolyte fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C,color:#000
    classDef circuit fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000
    classDef exhaust fill:#F5F5F5,stroke:#424242,color:#000

    class A1,E1,F1,H1 gasFlow
    class B1,D1 electrode
    class C1 electrolyte
    class G1 circuit
```

## 4.2 SOFC Electrochemical Process — Detailed Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Cathode TPB: O2 gains 2 electrons] -->|Step A: 0.5 O2 plus 2e- minus to O2-| A2[Electrolyte Bulk: O2- migrates via oxygen vacancies]
    A2 -->|Step B: Ion hopping through ZrO2 lattice| A3[Anode TPB: Fuel reacts with O2-]
    A3 -->|Step C: H2 plus O2- to H2O plus 2e-| A4[Electrons released to external circuit]
    A4 -->|Step D: Through load to cathode| A1

    classDef stepStyle fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17,color:#000
    class A1,A2,A3,A4 stepStyle
```

## 4.3 Tubular vs Planar SOFC Configurations

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TUB["TUBULAR SOFC Design"]
        T1[Air injected inside tube] --> T2[Porous cathode tube wall]
        T2 --> T3[YSZ electrolyte layer]
        T3 --> T4[Nickel cermet anode on outer surface]
        T4 --> T5[Fuel flows over outer tube]
    end

    subgraph PLAN["PLANAR SOFC Design"]
        P1[Air channel] --> P2[Ribbed bipolar plate cathode]
        P2 --> P3[YSZ electrolyte thin film 10 to 100 microns]
        P3 --> P4[Nickel cermet anode]
        P4 --> P5[Fuel channel]
    end

    classDef tubular fill:#FFCCBC,stroke:#BF360C,color:#000
    classDef planar fill:#B3E5FC,stroke:#01579B,color:#000
    class T1,T2,T3,T4,T5 tubular
    class P1,P2,P3,P4,P5 planar
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** The **planar design** is more compact and offers higher power density, but requires high-temperature gas-tight seals. The **tubular design** (developed by Westinghouse / Siemens) eliminates seals but has lower power density. Either is acceptable in descriptive answers.

## 4.4 Voltage Loss vs Current Density (Polarization Curve)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Low current region] -->|Activation loss dominates: steep initial drop| A2[Mid current region]
    A2 -->|Ohmic loss dominates: linear drop| A3[High current region]
    A3 -->|Concentration loss dominates: sharp drop to 0| A4[Limiting current density iL]

    classDef region fill:#E1BEE7,stroke:#4A148C,color:#000
    class A1,A2,A3,A4 region
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Implication:** The SOFC should be operated in the **mid-current linear region** to balance high power output (high i × V) against acceptable efficiency. Operating near i_L destroys efficiency rapidly.
